topics.title: Introduction to Design - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Design - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Design" in Engineering?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Engineering Design** is formally defined as the **iterative, decision-making process** of applying scientific principles, mathematical methods, empirical evidence, and engineering judgment to conceive, develop, and specify a device, system, or process that fulfils a defined functional need while satisfying a defined set of constraints.

Mathematically, the design problem can be expressed as an optimization statement:

$$
D^{*} = \arg\min_{D \in \mathcal{S}} \left[ C(D) \right] \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_{j}(D) \le 0, \; j = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

Where:
- $D^{*}$ = the **optimal design** vector
- $\mathcal{S}$ = the feasible design space
- $C(D)$ = the objective function (cost, weight, stress, etc.)
- $g_{j}(D)$ = the constraint functions (stress limits, deflection limits, manufacturing limits)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Design is not merely "drawing" or "drafting". It is a **cognitive, creative, and analytical activity** that converts a *need* into a *physical reality*. In automotive engineering, every component — from a piston to a chassis frame — is the output of an engineering design process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Designing a House vs. Buying a House"**
> Buying a ready-made house is like choosing a standard, off-the-shelf bolt from a catalogue — no design effort is needed by you. But **building a house** (deciding plot size, number of floors, room layout, materials, budget, weather resistance, future expansion) is exactly what an engineer does when designing an automotive component like a **crankshaft**. The engineer decides material, geometry, surface finish, tolerances, heat treatment, and manufacturing route — all to make the crankshaft do its job safely for millions of revolutions.

## 1.3 The "Definition" Triad in Automotive Design

Every automotive component design must answer three fundamental questions:

| Question | Engineering Term | Example (Brake Disc) |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **What must it do?** | Function | Dissipate kinetic energy via friction |
| **What must it NOT do?** | Constraint / Failure Avoidance | Crack under thermal shock, warp beyond $\le 0.05$ mm |
| **What does it cost?** | Objective / Economy | Target mass $\le 8$ kg, cost $\le \$25$ |

This triad forms the philosophical backbone of **PBAUT604 — Automotive Component Design**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes

## 2.1 The Four Recognized Types of Engineering Design

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) explicitly classifies design into **four canonical categories**. Examiners frequently test this classification for **3-mark Part A questions**.

### 2.1.1 Original (Creative / Innovative) Design
- A design produced from first principles for a **novel functional requirement**.
- Involves new principles, new materials, or new configurations.
- **Automotive example:** The transition from a conventional **sliding-vane steering rack** to an **electric power steering (EPS) column** in the 1990s.

### 2.1.2 Adaptive Design
- An existing proven design is **adapted / modified** to suit a new operating condition or new vehicle platform.
- Geometry, material, or load spectra are re-sized; core working principle is **retained**.
- **Automotive example:** Re-rating a passenger-car **connecting rod** for use in a heavier commercial-vehicle engine (same design, larger cross-section).

### 2.1.3 Development Design
- An existing design is **refined and improved** in performance, reliability, manufacturability, or cost — without changing its functional basis.
- **Automotive example:** Iterative redesign of a **multi-plate clutch** to improve heat dissipation and reduce pedal effort over successive model years.

### 2.1.4 Variant Design
- A **parametric / scaled version** of a standard design produced within a family of products.
- Only dimensions, ratings, or capacities change.
- **Automotive example:** A family of **disc brake calipers** (2-pot, 4-pot, 6-pot) sharing the same mounting interface but differing in clamping force.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook (Mnemonic): "O-A-D-V"**
> **O**riginal → **A**daptive → **D**evelopment → **V**ariant
> The cognitive load of "design effort" decreases as you move from O → V.

## 2.2 The Engineering Design Process — A Standardized Sequence

The widely accepted KTU-recommended sequence is:

1. **Recognition of Need** — Problem identification (market demand, customer complaint, regulatory push).
2. **Definition of Problem** — Functional specifications, performance targets, constraints.
3. **Synthesis** — Generation of alternative concepts (brainstorming, morphological chart, TRIZ).
4. **Analysis & Optimization** — Mathematical modelling, FEA, hand calculations, optimization.
5. **Evaluation** — Decision matrix, Pugh matrix, weighted scoring.
6. **Communication** — Engineering drawings, GD&T, BOM, CAD models, technical reports.
7. **Implementation / Manufacture** — Process planning, tooling, quality control.

## 2.3 Key Design Considerations in Automotive Component Design

The KTU 2024 module explicitly lists the following considerations. Every design solution must balance them:

- **Functionality** — Must satisfy the intended purpose under all service conditions.
- **Strength & Stiffness** — Must withstand static, dynamic, cyclic, and impact loads.
- **Manufacturability** — Must be producible by available processes (casting, forging, machining, MIM, 3D-printing).
- **Material Availability & Cost** — Use standard, readily available grades (e.g., SAE 1045, AISI 4140, AlSi9Cu3).
- **Weight / Lightweighting** — Critical for fuel efficiency and EV range.
- **Safety & Reliability** — Failure must not endanger occupants (ISO 26262 considerations).
- **Aesthetics & Ergonomics** — Especially for visible/interior parts (dashboard, steering wheel).
- **Maintainability & Serviceability** — Ease of assembly/disassembly, standard fasteners.
- **Environmental Compliance** — Recyclability, RoHS, end-of-life disposal.
- **Regulatory / Standards Compliance** — AIS, ECE, FMVSS, Indian Motor Vehicle Act norms.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Quantity / Concept | Symbol | Relationship / Formula | Unit |
|---|--------------------|--------|------------------------|------|
| 1 | Factor of Safety (static) | $F.S.$ | $F.S. = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{working}} = \dfrac{\tau_{y}}{\tau_{working}}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Design Stress (working) | $\sigma_{d}$ | $\sigma_{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{F.S.}$ | $\text{MPa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ |
| 3 | Tolerance (bilateral) | $t$ | $t = \dfrac{\pm \, \Delta x}{2}$ on nominal | $\text{mm}$ |
| 4 | Allowance (fit) | $A$ | $A = H - s$ (hole - shaft) | $\text{mm}$ |
| 5 | Volumetric Efficiency (compressor/IC) | $\eta_{v}$ | $\eta_{v} = \dfrac{\text{Actual volume}}{\text{Swept volume}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Power-to-Weight Ratio | $PWR$ | $PWR = \dfrac{P \, [\text{kW}]}{m \, [\text{kg}]}$ | $\text{kW/kg}$ |
| 7 | Specific Strength | $S_{sp}$ | $S_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}$ | $\text{N-m/kg}$ |
| 8 | Cost per Component | $C_{pc}$ | $C_{pc} = C_{mat} + C_{mfg} + C_{assy} + C_{OH}$ | $\text{₹}$ or $\text{\$}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** All the above quantities recur across **Modules 2 through 5** (shafts, springs, brakes, clutches, gears). Mastering them now is a **high-leverage study move**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Deriving the Design Equation

## 3.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Design Problem

The **fundamental design equation** the KTU examiner expects a student to be able to derive is the relation between **working stress, ultimate stress, and factor of safety**.

### Step 1 — State the design requirement
A component must carry a **service (working) load** $W$ without failure. The maximum internal stress produced by $W$ is the **working / design stress** $\sigma_{d}$.

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{W}{A}
$$

Where $A$ is the cross-sectional area resisting the load.

### Step 2 — Identify the failure-initiating property of the material
For ductile steels and cast irons used in automotive components (e.g., crankshaft, connecting rod, axle), failure typically initiates when stress reaches the **yield strength** $\sigma_{y}$ (static loading) or the **endurance limit** $\sigma_{e}$ (cyclic loading).

$$
\sigma_{yield} = \sigma_{y}, \qquad \sigma_{endurance} = \sigma_{e}
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Factor of Safety
By definition, the Factor of Safety is the **ratio of the failure strength to the working stress**.

$$
F.S. = \frac{\text{Failure Strength}}{\text{Working Stress}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute for static loading case
For static loading, failure strength $\rightarrow \sigma_{y}$ (yield strength). Therefore:

$$
F.S. = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{d}}
$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the design (working) stress
Solving the above equation for $\sigma_{d}$:

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{F.S.}
$$

### Step 6 — Extend to ultimate-strength basis
If the designer prefers to use the **ultimate tensile strength** $\sigma_{u}$ instead of yield strength (e.g., for brittle materials like cast iron brake drums):

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{\sigma_{u}}{F.S.}
$$

### Step 7 — Consolidate into a unified design equation

$$
\boxed{\, \sigma_{d} = \frac{\sigma_{u}}{F.S.} \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma_{d} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{F.S.} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Automotive Component Design (PBAUT604):**
> This single equation is the **gateway** to all subsequent modules. Whether you are sizing a connecting rod (Module 2), a clutch spring (Module 3), or a gear tooth (Module 4), the procedure begins with: *pick a material $\rightarrow$ know its $\sigma_{y}$ or $\sigma_{u}$ $\rightarrow$ choose $F.S.$ $\rightarrow$ compute allowable stress $\rightarrow$ solve geometry*.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Illustration (Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A connecting rod of a passenger car engine is to carry a peak tensile load of $W = 45{,}000 \text{ N}$. The rod is made of AISI 1045 steel with $\sigma_{y} = 530 \text{ MPa}$. If the recommended factor of safety is $F.S. = 3$, determine the minimum cross-sectional area required at the critical section.

### Solution — Step-by-step

**Step 1:** Identify known quantities.

$$
W = 45{,}000 \text{ N}, \qquad \sigma_{y} = 530 \text{ N/mm}^{2}, \qquad F.S. = 3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the design (working) stress using the derived formula.

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{F.S.} = \frac{530}{3}
$$

$$
\sigma_{d} = 176.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the required cross-sectional area from the stress definition.

$$
A_{req} = \frac{W}{\sigma_{d}} = \frac{45{,}000}{176.67}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{req} = 254.7 \text{ mm}^{2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Sanity-check with a standard rod cross-section. For a circular cross-section:

$$
A_{req} = \frac{\pi \, d^{2}}{4} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, A_{req}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 254.7}{\pi}}
$$

$$
d = 18.01 \text{ mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{adopt } d = 20 \text{ mm (standard)}
$$

**Step 5:** Check final actual stress with the adopted diameter.

$$
\sigma_{actual} = \frac{W}{\frac{\pi \, (20)^{2}}{4}} = \frac{45{,}000}{314.16} = 143.24 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

$$
F.S._{actual} = \frac{530}{143.24} = 3.70 \;>\; 3 \;\;\checkmark
$$

The design is safe.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Engineering Design Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the canonical **7-stage engineering design process** as required by the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus. It highlights the **iterative loops** between Analysis ↔ Synthesis ↔ Evaluation, which are the conceptual heart of design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stage1_Recognize_Need] --> B[stage2_Define_Problem]
    B --> C[stage3_Synthesis_Concepts]
    C --> D[stage4_Analysis_Optimization]
    D --> E{stage5_Evaluation_Decision}
    E -->|Accept| F[stage6_Communication_Drawings]
    E -->|Reject Iterate| C
    D -->|Convergence Failure Iterate| C
    F --> G[stage7_Implementation_Manufacture]
    G --> H[stage8_Testing_Validation]
    H --> I{Design_Acceptable}
    I -->|No Iterate| B
    I -->|Yes| J[stage9_Release_to_Production]

    classDef startend fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0b2545
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b2545

    class A,J startend
    class B,C,D,F,G,H process
    class E,I decision
```

## 4.2 Morphological Chart — Conceptual Design Aid

The **morphological chart** (also called a Zwicky box) is a structured way to generate design alternatives by combining subsystems and sub-functions. It is the most asked diagram from this topic for **7-mark Part B questions**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Function1_Brake_Torque_Generation] --> S1a[SolutionA_Hydraulic_Caliper]
    F1 --> S1b[SolutionB_Mechanical_Cable]
    F1 --> S1c[SolutionC_Electromagnetic_Park_Brake]

    F2[Function2_Force_Transmission] --> S2a[SolutionA_Fluid_Pressure]
    F2 --> S2b[SolutionB_Lever_Mechanism]
    F2 --> S2c[SolutionC_Electric_Motor]

    F3[Function3_Friction_Interface] --> S3a[SolutionA_Organic_Pad]
    F3 --> S3b[SolutionB_Sintered_Metallic_Pad]
    F3 --> S3c[SolutionC_Ceramic_Composite]

    S1a --> COMB[Combined_Design_Concept]
    S2a --> COMB
    S3a --> COMB
    S1b --> COMB2[Alt_Concept_Mech_Cable]
    S2b --> COMB2
    S3b --> COMB2
    S1c --> COMB3[Alt_Concept_Elec_Park]
    S2c --> COMB3
    S3c --> COMB3

    COMB --> EVAL[Evaluation_Pugh_Matrix]
    COMB2 --> EVAL
    COMB3 --> EVAL
    EVAL --> BEST[Best_Concept_Selected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The leftmost column lists **functions** (what the system must do). The middle column lists **alternative means** of achieving each function. The bottom row lists the **combined design concepts** formed by picking one alternative from each function. These combinations are then **scored against weighted criteria** (cost, weight, manufacturability, safety) to select the best concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define engineering design. List any four essential considerations in automotive component design.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Engineering Design** is the systematic, iterative process of applying scientific principles, mathematical methods, and engineering judgment to create a device, system, or process that fulfils a defined functional need while satisfying defined constraints (cost, weight, safety, reliability).
>
> Four essential considerations in automotive component design are:
> 1. **Functionality** — the component must perform its intended purpose.
> 2. **Manufacturability** — the design must be producible by available processes.
> 3. **Safety and reliability** — failure must not endanger occupants (ISO 26262).
> 4. **Weight and cost** — lightweighting for fuel economy; cost for market competitiveness.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Adaptive Design and Development Design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Adaptive Design | Development Design |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Definition** | An existing proven design is modified to suit a **new operating condition** or new vehicle platform. | An existing design is **refined and improved** in performance, quality, or cost. |
| **Core principle** | Retained (same) | Retained (same) |
| **Geometry** | Re-sized or re-rated | Re-optimised iteratively |
| **Automotive example** | Re-rating a passenger-car connecting rod for use in a commercial-vehicle engine. | Iterative redesign of a multi-plate clutch to improve heat dissipation across model years. |
| **Design effort** | Moderate | High (multiple iterations, prototyping, testing) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall**
> - Do **NOT** write only definitions in Part B. The examiner awards marks for *examples, applications, diagrams, and design steps*.
> - Always include a **neat flowchart or block diagram** — it carries **2 to 3 marks** by itself.
> - For design questions, **state assumptions clearly** before any calculation. Unstated assumptions = **−2 marks** in typical valuation.
> - In numericals, carry **units throughout** and box the **final answer**. A correct number without unit = **0 marks** for that part.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four types of engineering design** with suitable examples from automotive applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step engineering design process** with a neat flowchart. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four types are:

1. **Original Design** — A brand-new design produced from first principles for a novel requirement.
   - *Example:* Development of an **electric power steering (EPS)** system replacing the conventional hydraulic steering.

2. **Adaptive Design** — An existing design adapted for a new operating condition or platform.
   - *Example:* Upsizing a passenger-car **brake caliper** for a heavier SUV application.

3. **Development Design** — Iterative refinement of an existing design for improved performance, cost, or reliability.
   - *Example:* Year-on-year improvement of an **engine crankshaft** for higher specific output and NVH.

4. **Variant Design** — Parametric scaling of a standard design for a product family.
   - *Example:* A family of **disc brake rotors** (280 mm, 300 mm, 320 mm) sharing the same hub interface.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The step-by-step process (with reference to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1):

| Step | Activity | Output |
|------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Recognition of Need | Problem statement |
| 2 | Definition of Problem | Specifications, constraints |
| 3 | Synthesis | Concept alternatives (morphological chart) |
| 4 | Analysis & Optimization | Math model, FEA results |
| 5 | Evaluation | Pugh matrix, decision |
| 6 | Communication | Engineering drawings, GD&T, BOM |
| 7 | Implementation | Prototype, tooling, production |
| 8 | Testing & Validation | Test reports |
| 9 | Release | Production sign-off |

The process is **iterative** — feedback from testing and evaluation returns the designer to earlier stages until all constraints are met.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B]`

**(a)** What are the **essential considerations** an automotive design engineer must take into account while designing a component? Discuss any **eight** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **morphological chart** and its role in **conceptual design** of automotive components. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The eight essential considerations are:

1. **Functionality** — The component must perform its intended purpose under all service conditions.
2. **Strength & Stiffness** — Must resist static, dynamic, cyclic, and impact loads without yielding or failing in fatigue.
3. **Material selection** — Must match strength, corrosion resistance, machinability, and cost.
4. **Manufacturability** — Geometry must be producible by casting, forging, machining, or MIM with available tooling.
5. **Weight** — Lightweighting improves fuel economy (ICE) and range (EV); high specific strength is preferred.
6. **Cost** — Material + manufacturing + assembly + overhead must remain within the target.
7. **Safety & Reliability** — Failure must not endanger occupants; redundant features where required (ISO 26262 ASIL).
8. **Standards Compliance** — AIS, ECE, FMVSS, ISO, and Indian Motor Vehicle Act norms must be met.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **morphological chart** (Zwicky box) is a systematic design tool used to **generate and visualise alternative design concepts**.

**Structure:**
- The chart is a **matrix**.
- **Rows** represent **sub-functions** that the system must perform.
- **Columns** represent **alternative means** of achieving each sub-function.
- Each **complete row-to-column combination** forms one candidate design concept.

**Role in Conceptual Design of an Automotive Component (e.g., Brake System):**

1. **Identify sub-functions** of the brake system: torque generation, force transmission, friction interface, heat dissipation.
2. **List alternatives** for each sub-function (e.g., hydraulic, mechanical, electric for force transmission).
3. **Combine** one alternative from each sub-function to form candidate concepts (e.g., hydraulic caliper + organic pad = Conventional Hydraulic Disc Brake).
4. **Evaluate** each candidate concept against weighted criteria (cost, weight, NVH, reliability).
5. **Select** the highest-scoring concept for further detailed design.

**Advantages:**
- Encourages **systematic exploration** of design space.
- Reduces the risk of overlooking creative alternatives.
- Facilitates **team-based ideation** and **decision documentation**.

*(See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 for a visual representation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize**

- ☐ **Definition:** Design = iterative process converting a need into a physical reality, subject to constraints.
- ☐ **Four types:** Original, Adaptive, Development, Variant (Mnemonic: **O-A-D-V**).
- ☐ **Seven stages of the design process:** Recognize Need → Define Problem → Synthesize → Analyze & Optimize → Evaluate → Communicate → Implement.
- ☐ **Design is iterative**, not linear — feedback loops exist between Analysis ↔ Synthesis ↔ Evaluation.
- ☐ **Fundamental design equation:** $\sigma_{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{F.S.}$ (or use $\sigma_{u}$ for brittle materials).
- ☐ **Factor of Safety** is dimensionless and always $>$ 1.
- ☐ **Design considerations** (master at least 8): Function, Strength, Material, Manufacturability, Weight, Cost, Safety, Standards.
- ☐ **Morphological chart** = Zwicky box — a matrix of functions vs. alternative means.
- ☐ **Pugh matrix** is the standard tool for concept scoring (used after morphological chart).
- ☐ Always **state assumptions**, **carry units**, and **box final answers** in numerical problems.
- ☐ For numericals on $\sigma_{d}$, write **three things clearly**: knowns, formula, substituted value, result.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**
> *"Engineering design is the systematic conversion of a functional need into a documented, optimised, manufacturable, safe, and cost-effective physical component, achieved through the iterative O-A-D-V design taxonomy and the seven-stage design process, with all decisions governed by the fundamental equation $\sigma_{d} = \sigma_{y} / F.S.$."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steps in Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steps in Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automotive Component Design (PBAUT604)** as prescribed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the *Design Process* is defined as a **systematic, iterative, and multi-stage engineering methodology** that transforms an abstract functional need into a fully documented, manufacturable, and reliable automotive component or sub-assembly. The process integrates **customer requirements, engineering analysis, material selection, manufacturing constraints, regulatory compliance, and economic feasibility** into a coherent decision-making framework.

The classical engineering design process, as adopted by leading automotive OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) such as Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, and global firms like Toyota and Bosch, follows a well-established sequence of logical steps that ensure traceability, repeatability, and quality assurance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to enumerate, explain, and critically evaluate **each step of the design process** as it applies to real-world automotive components such as connecting rods, crankshafts, gears, brakes, and suspension systems. Examiners frequently test the *inter-relationship* and *iteration loops* between these steps.

### 1.2 The Seven Canonical Steps of the Design Process

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following canonical sequence, adapted from **Pahl & Beitz (Engineering Design: A Systematic Approach)** and **Shigley's Mechanical Engineering Design**:

1. **Recognition of Need** – Identifying a market, customer, or regulatory gap.
2. **Definition of Problem** – Translating the need into a precise design brief.
3. **Conceptual Design (Synthesis)** – Generating multiple solution principles.
4. **Preliminary Design (Analysis & Optimization)** – Sizing, material selection, and feasibility checks.
5. **Detailed Design** – Creating manufacturing-ready drawings, GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing), and BOM (Bill of Materials).
6. **Prototype, Testing & Validation** – Physical/virtual prototyping and verification against performance targets.
7. **Production & Continuous Improvement** – Handover to manufacturing with feedback loops for refinement.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Building a House

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition: "Designing a House"**
> Think of designing an automotive component as analogous to **building a custom house** for a client:
> - **Recognition of Need** → The client says, *"I need a bigger kitchen."*
> - **Definition of Problem** → The architect asks: How many people cook? What's the budget? What style?
> - **Conceptual Design** → Three different floor plans are sketched (L-shape, U-shape, island-style).
> - **Preliminary Design** → Load calculations, material choices (granite vs. marble), and ventilation analysis.
> - **Detailed Design** → Final blueprints with exact tile dimensions, electrical layouts, and plumbing.
> - **Prototype & Testing** → A 3D walkthrough or scaled model is reviewed.
> - **Production** → Actual construction begins, with feedback loops for any mid-construction changes.
>
> Just as no architect skips the foundation analysis, no automotive designer skips the **load and stress analysis** of a component.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following standard metrics govern decision-making throughout the design process:

- **Factor of Safety (FoS)**: Typically **1.5 to 3.0** for static automotive loads, **2.0 to 4.0** for dynamic/cyclic loads.
- **Design Life**: Passenger vehicles target **10 years / 200,000 km**.
- **Reliability Target**: **95% to 99%** confidence intervals for critical components.
- **Cost Reduction Goal**: **15% to 30%** per generation in mass-production programs.
- **Standard Reference Temperature**: **25°C (298 K)** for baseline material properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The design process is not a linear "waterfall" but rather an **iterative spiral** with continuous feedback. Below is a granular analysis of each step:

#### **Step 1: Recognition of Need**

- **Trigger Sources**: Market surveys, customer complaints, competitive benchmarking, regulatory changes (e.g., BS-VI, Euro 7 norms), safety incidents, and technology shifts (e.g., EV transition).
- **Output**: A preliminary *Need Statement* — a one-paragraph informal description of the problem.
- **Key Question**: *"What problem are we solving, and for whom?"*
- **Tools Used**: Voice of Customer (VoC), Quality Function Deployment (QFD), Kano Model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this step matters:** Skipping need recognition leads to **"engineering a feature in search of a problem"**, a classic pitfall observed in over-engineered automotive components that increase BOM cost without customer benefit.

#### **Step 2: Definition of Problem**

- The need statement is converted into a **formal Design Brief** containing:
  - Functional requirements (e.g., *"transmit 200 Nm torque at 4000 rpm"*).
  - Performance specifications (power, speed, efficiency, NVH targets).
  - Constraints (weight, cost, material, manufacturing process, regulatory).
  - Acceptance criteria.
- **Output**: A **Product Design Specification (PDS)** document.
- **Tools Used**: Morphological charts, requirements traceability matrix (RTM).

#### **Step 3: Conceptual Design (Synthesis)**

- **Goal**: Generate multiple *solution principles* without committing to dimensions or materials.
- **Approach**: Function-structure analysis → search for working principles → combine into concept variants.
- **Output**: 3–5 concept sketches or CAD models, each representing a fundamentally different solution approach.
- **Tools Used**: Brainstorming, TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving), morphological matrix, patent search.

#### **Step 4: Preliminary Design (Analysis & Optimization)**

- The most promising concept is selected (often via a **Pugh Matrix** or weighted scoring).
- Engineering analysis begins:
  - **Load estimation** (static, dynamic, fatigue).
  - **Stress analysis** (analytical + FEA).
  - **Material selection** (using Ashby charts or CES EduPack).
  - **Preliminary sizing** of cross-sections, diameters, lengths.
- **Output**: A *preliminary layout* with calculated dimensions and selected material grades.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In industry, this step consumes **~40% of total design time** and is where most engineering judgment is applied. The accuracy of preliminary calculations directly determines whether a component passes prototype testing.

#### **Step 5: Detailed Design**

- Final dimensions, tolerances, surface finishes, and material grades are locked.
- **GD&T (ASME Y14.5)** is applied to ensure manufacturability and assembly.
- **CAD model** is finalized (e.g., in CATIA, Siemens NX, SolidWorks).
- **CAE simulation** (FEA, CFD, modal analysis) validates performance.
- **Output**: Production-ready 2D drawings, 3D models, BOM, and assembly instructions.

#### **Step 6: Prototype, Testing & Validation**

- **Rapid Prototyping**: 3D printing (SLA, SLS, FDM) for form-fit models.
- **Functional Prototypes**: CNC-machined or cast prototypes for performance testing.
- **Testing Types**: Static load tests, fatigue tests (e.g., bench testing of connecting rods for **10^7 cycles**), NVH, crash, and durability runs.
- **Output**: Test reports, design verification (DV) sign-off.

#### **Step 7: Production & Continuous Improvement**

- **Pilot Run**: Small-batch production to validate manufacturing process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.33).
- **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)** reviews.
- **Post-launch monitoring**: Field failure data feeds back into the next design cycle.
- **Tools Used**: APQP (Advanced Product Quality Planning), PPAP (Production Part Approval Process), Six Sigma, Kaizen.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the key formulas and metrics that students must memorize and apply during the design process, particularly in **Step 4 (Preliminary Design)** and **Step 5 (Detailed Design)**.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit | Application Step |
|:------:|:---------|:---------------------|:----:|:----------------:|
| $\sigma$ | Normal Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Step 4 |
| $\tau$ | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa | Step 4 |
| $\sigma_{d}$ | Design Stress | $\sigma_{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{FoS}$ | Pa | Step 4 |
| $FoS$ | Factor of Safety | $FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | dimensionless | Step 4 |
| $\sigma_{b}$ | Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Pa | Step 4, 5 |
| $\sigma_{t}$ | Torsional Stress | $\sigma_{t} = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | Pa | Step 4, 5 |
| $J$ | Polar Moment of Inertia | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ (solid shaft) | m⁴ | Step 4, 5 |
| $I$ | Area Moment of Inertia | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$ (solid circular) | m⁴ | Step 4, 5 |
| $\sigma_{e}$ | Von Mises Stress | $\sigma_{e} = \sqrt{\sigma^{2} + 3\tau^{2}}$ | Pa | Step 5 |
| $S_{e}$ | Endurance Limit | $S_{e} = k_{a} k_{b} k_{c} \cdot S_{e}'$ | Pa | Step 4, 5 |
| $C_{p}$ | Process Capability | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Step 6, 7 |
| $N_{f}$ | Fatigue Life (Basquin) | $\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{f}' (2N_{f})^{b}$ | cycles | Step 4, 5 |
| $P_{cr}$ | Critical Buckling Load | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$ | N | Step 4 |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Step 3, 4 |
| $m$ | Mass (lightweighting) | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | kg | Step 4, 5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always carry forward **at least 4 significant figures** in intermediate calculations. Examiners deduct marks for premature rounding in stress and fatigue problems.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Iteration

The design process is fundamentally **non-linear**. After any step, the designer may **return to an earlier step** if:
- New customer requirements emerge (→ back to Step 1).
- Analysis reveals infeasibility (→ back to Step 3).
- Prototyping fails (→ back to Step 4 or 5).
- Field failures occur post-launch (→ restart the cycle).

This **spiral model** ensures continuous improvement and is the foundation of modern **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** and **APQP** frameworks used in Indian automotive industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow Within Each Design Step

Since the "Steps in Design Process" is a **methodological topic** rather than a pure derivations topic, the analytical depth is provided through **decision matrices and weighted scoring derivations** that examiners frequently test.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Weighted Decision Matrix Score

In **Step 3 (Concept Selection)**, automotive design teams use a **Pugh Matrix** to rank competing concepts. Let us derive the weighted scoring equation.

**Let:**
- $N$ = total number of evaluation criteria (e.g., cost, weight, manufacturability, performance).
- $C_{i}$ = weight of the $i$-th criterion (such that $\sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{i} = 1.0$).
- $S_{ij}$ = score of the $j$-th concept on the $i$-th criterion (typically on a scale of 1 to 5).

The **total weighted score** for concept $j$ is:

$$
T_{j} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{i} \cdot S_{ij}
$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Score } (T_{j}) &= C_{1} \cdot S_{1j} + C_{2} \cdot S_{2j} + C_{3} \cdot S_{3j} + \dots + C_{N} \cdot S_{Nj} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{i} \cdot S_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

The concept with the highest $T_{j}$ is selected for preliminary design.

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose we have **3 concepts (A, B, C)** and **4 criteria** as shown in the table below. Weights sum to 1.0.

| Criterion | Weight ($C_{i}$) | Concept A | Concept B | Concept C |
|:---------:|:----------------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|
| Cost | 0.35 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Weight | 0.25 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Manufacturability | 0.20 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Performance | 0.20 | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| **Total $T_{j}$** | **1.00** | **3.95** | **4.15** | **4.15** |

**Computation for Concept A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{A} &= (0.35 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 1.40 + 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.80 \\
&= 3.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation for Concept B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{B} &= (0.35 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 5) \\
&= 1.05 + 1.25 + 0.80 + 1.00 \\
&= 4.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation for Concept C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{C} &= (0.35 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 1.75 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.80 \\
&= 4.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Concept C wins with $T_{C} = 4.15$, marginally edging out Concept B. In case of a tie, a **sensitivity analysis** is performed by varying the weights $C_{i}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** The weights $C_{i}$ are determined by the design team based on **customer priorities, regulatory mandates, and corporate strategy**. For fuel-efficient passenger cars, weight criterion gets higher weight; for racing vehicles, performance gets higher weight.

### 3.3 Derivation of Fatigue Life Using Basquin's Equation (Used in Step 4 & 5)

For an automotive component subjected to cyclic loading (e.g., a connecting rod), the **Basquin's Equation** estimates the number of cycles to failure:

$$
\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{f}' \cdot (2N_{f})^{b}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{a}$ = alternating stress amplitude (Pa)
- $\sigma_{f}'$ = fatigue strength coefficient (≈ true fracture strength, Pa)
- $N_{f}$ = number of cycles to failure
- $b$ = fatigue strength exponent (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for steels)

**Solving for $N_{f}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{a} &= \sigma_{f}' \cdot (2N_{f})^{b} \\
\dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{\sigma_{f}'} &= (2N_{f})^{b} \\
\left( \dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{\sigma_{f}'} \right)^{1/b} &= 2N_{f} \\
N_{f} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{\sigma_{f}'} \right)^{1/b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A steel component has $\sigma_{f}' = 1000$ MPa and $b = -0.10$. If the alternating stress is $\sigma_{a} = 400$ MPa, find $N_{f}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{f} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{400}{1000} \right)^{1/(-0.10)} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \left( 0.4 \right)^{-10} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{0.4^{10}} \right) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{0.0001048576} \right) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 9536.74 \\
&\approx 4768 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

This low cycle count indicates **high-cycle fatigue risk**, and the designer must either reduce $\sigma_{a}$ (by increasing cross-section) or select a higher-strength material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often couple "Steps in Design Process" with a numerical on **shaft/rod design** where students must first *narrate the design steps* and then *execute the calculations* — a classic 14-mark integrated question.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary: Mapping Steps to Deliverables

| Design Step | Primary Deliverable | Key Analysis Type | Output Document |
|:-----------:|:-------------------:|:-----------------:|:---------------:|
| 1. Need Recognition | Need Statement | Market Research | Customer Voice Report |
| 2. Problem Definition | Design Brief | Requirements Engineering | Product Design Specification (PDS) |
| 3. Conceptual Design | Concept Sketches | Function-Structure | Morphological Chart |
| 4. Preliminary Design | Sized Layout | Stress, Fatigue, Thermal | Preliminary Drawing + Material Spec |
| 5. Detailed Design | Final CAD Model | FEA, CFD, GD&T | Production Drawing + BOM |
| 6. Prototype & Test | Test Report | DV Testing (DVP&R) | Validation Report |
| 7. Production | Pilot Run | Cpk, DFMA | PPAP Sign-off |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram maps the **interactions, decision gates, and feedback loops** between the seven steps of the design process, as mandated by the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Recognition of Need] --> A2[Step 2: Definition of Problem PDS Document]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Conceptual Design Synthesis and Ideation]
    A3 --> A4{Decision Gate 1: Concept Selection via Pugh Matrix}
    A4 -->|Selected Concept| A5[Step 4: Preliminary Design Stress and Sizing Analysis]
    A4 -->|Rejected| A3
    A5 --> A6[Step 5: Detailed Design CAD and GD&T]
    A6 --> A7[Step 6: Prototype Fabrication and Testing]
    A7 --> A8{Decision Gate 2: Design Verification}
    A8 -->|Pass| A9[Step 7: Pilot Production and APQP]
    A8 -->|Fail| A5
    A9 --> A10[Field Deployment and Monitoring]
    A10 -->|Field Failures or New Requirements| A1
    A10 -->|Continuous Improvement Kaizen| A5
```

### 4.2 Diagram Interpretation Guide

- **Rectangular nodes (A1–A10)** represent sequential design activities and deliverables.
- **Diamond nodes (A4, A8)** represent **decision gates** where the design may loop back to an earlier step.
- **Loopback arrows** (A4→A3, A8→A5, A10→A1) illustrate the **iterative nature** of engineering design.
- The terminal node **A10 (Field Deployment)** feeds back into **A1** if new customer needs or field failures arise, embodying the **closed-loop lifecycle** of automotive product development.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked to *"draw a flowchart of the design process"*, students must include **at least one feedback loop**. Marks are awarded for explicitly showing the **iteration**, not just linear arrows.

### 4.3 Additional Schematic: Concept Generation Funnel (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Multiple Need Statements] --> B2[Refined Problem Definition]
    B2 --> B3[Many Concept Variants 10 to 20]
    B3 --> B4[Screened Concepts 3 to 5]
    B4 --> B5[Selected Concept 1]
    B5 --> B6[Detailed Production Design]
```

This **funnel schematic** visually represents the **progressive convergence** of design options — a concept familiar to students from **DFMA (Design for Manufacturing and Assembly)** literature.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

*Define the term "Recognition of Need" as the first step in the engineering design process. List any two sources from which a design need may be identified in the automotive industry.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Recognition of Need* is the first and most critical step in the engineering design process, wherein an unmet customer, market, regulatory, or technological requirement is identified, triggering the design cycle. It culminates in a preliminary *Need Statement* that qualitatively describes the problem to be solved.

**Two sources of design need in the automotive industry:**

1. **Customer feedback and field failure data** — warranty claims, service center reports, and customer surveys often reveal design shortcomings in existing models (e.g., premature brake pad wear, dashboard rattles).

2. **Regulatory and safety mandates** — government regulations such as **AIS-010 (crashworthiness)**, **BS-VI emission norms**, and **AIS-052 (AIS for Electric Vehicles)** compel manufacturers to redesign components for compliance.

*(Award 1.5 marks for definition + 0.75 marks each for the two sources.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

*State the significance of the "Product Design Specification (PDS)" document in the design process. Mention any four elements typically included in a PDS.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Product Design Specification (PDS)** is a formal, structured document prepared in **Step 2 (Definition of Problem)** that translates the informal need statement into precise, measurable, and verifiable engineering requirements. It serves as the **single source of truth** for all subsequent design, analysis, and testing activities and forms the contractual basis for design verification.

**Four typical elements of a PDS:**

1. **Functional requirements** — what the component must do (e.g., transmit torque, absorb energy).
2. **Performance specifications** — quantitative targets (e.g., 250 Nm peak torque, 1,500 kg mass limit).
3. **Constraints** — material, manufacturing, cost, weight, and regulatory limitations.
4. **Acceptance criteria and test methods** — pass/fail thresholds and the standards used to verify them (e.g., AIS, ISO, SAE).

*(Award 1 mark for significance + 0.5 marks each for the four elements.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - When narrating the *Steps in Design Process*, students often write a **generic textbook list** without linking it to an **automotive component** (e.g., connecting rod, brake disc, gear). This loses 2–3 marks.
> - Marks are reserved for **mentioning iteration and feedback loops** explicitly. A linear-only answer is treated as incomplete.
> - For numerical sub-parts, **units must be carried throughout**, and **final answers must be boxed/rounded appropriately**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **seven steps of the engineering design process** as applied to the design of an **automotive connecting rod**. Highlight the iteration and feedback loops. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the connecting rod concept finalized in part (a), a preliminary **tensile load analysis** is to be performed. The maximum tensile force on the rod is $F = 45$ kN. If the rod cross-section is rectangular with width $b = 25$ mm and thickness $t = 12$ mm, calculate: **(i)** the maximum tensile stress, and **(ii)** the factor of safety if the material is **AISI 1045 steel** with yield strength $\sigma_{y} = 530$ MPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flowchart & Explanation (7 Marks):**

Refer to the **Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1** of these notes. The seven steps applied to a connecting rod are:

1. **Recognition of Need**: A need to redesign the existing connecting rod for higher specific output (kW/kg) in a turbocharged 1.5L petrol engine.

2. **Definition of Problem**: PDS specifies — material: forged steel; max cylinder pressure: 90 bar; engine speed: 6000 rpm; design life: 10⁷ cycles; weight target: ≤ 350 grams.

3. **Conceptual Design**: Three concepts generated:
   - I-section beam (lightweight).
   - H-section beam (higher stiffness).
   - Solid rectangular section (lowest cost).

4. **Preliminary Design**: H-section selected via Pugh Matrix ($T = 4.25$). Initial sizing: I-beam with web thickness 6 mm, flange width 22 mm.

5. **Detailed Design**: Final CAD model with GD&T, surface finish Ra 0.8 μm on big-end bore.

6. **Prototype & Test**: 3D-printed SLA model for fit-check; CNC-machined forged prototype for **endurance test on Schenck dynamometer** at 1.25× peak load for 10⁷ cycles.

7. **Production & Improvement**: DFMA review, Cpk validation, Kaizen feedback from field data.

**Iteration loops**: If fatigue test fails (Step 6), designer returns to Step 4 (resize) or Step 3 (re-select concept). *(Award 1 mark for each of 7 steps = 7 marks; deduct 1 mark if iteration not mentioned.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $F = 45$ kN $= 45{,}000$ N
- $b = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- $t = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m
- $\sigma_{y} = 530$ MPa $= 530 \times 10^{6}$ Pa

**Step (i): Maximum Tensile Stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= b \times t = 0.025 \times 0.012 \\
A &= 0.0003 \text{ m}^2 = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\
\sigma_{max} &= \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{45{,}000}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} \\
\sigma_{max} &= 1.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 150 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Factor of Safety**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FoS &= \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{max}} = \dfrac{530}{150} \\
FoS &= 3.53
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\sigma_{max} = 150$ MPa and $FoS = 3.53$.

**Interpretation:** Since $FoS = 3.53$ is in the recommended range of **2.0 to 4.0** for dynamic-loaded automotive components, the design is **safe for fatigue service**. *(Award 1 mark for area, 1 mark for stress, 1 mark for FoS formula, 1 mark for numerical value, and 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **conceptual design step** in detail. Discuss the role of **morphological analysis** and **TRIZ** in generating automotive component concepts. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A design team is evaluating **three brake disc concepts** (A, B, C) for a mid-size sedan. Using the weighted Pugh matrix data below, calculate the total score for each concept and recommend the best one. **(7 Marks)**

| Criterion | Weight | Concept A | Concept B | Concept C |
|:---------:|:------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|
| Cost | 0.30 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Weight | 0.20 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Thermal Performance | 0.30 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Manufacturability | 0.20 | 4 | 4 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Design Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Conceptual Design** is Step 3 of the design process, where the designer moves from a *defined problem* to *multiple solution principles* without committing to specific dimensions, materials, or manufacturing processes. The output is a set of 3–5 **concept variants** that represent fundamentally different approaches to solving the problem.

**Morphological Analysis** is a systematic concept-generation technique where the overall function of the component is broken into sub-functions, and for each sub-function, multiple *working principles* are listed. These are then combined into complete concept variants using a **morphological matrix**. For example, for a *brake caliper*, sub-functions may include "apply force," "transmit force," and "absorb heat," each with 3–4 alternative working principles (hydraulic, mechanical, electromagnetic, pneumatic). Combining one principle from each column yields a unique concept.

**TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)** is a Russian-developed methodology (by Genrich Altshuller) that uses **40 inventive principles** and **contradiction matrices** to resolve engineering conflicts. For example, the contradiction *"strong brake disc but lightweight"* is resolved using TRIZ Principle 7 ("Nested doll") — applying a hollow vented disc design. TRIZ helps automotive designers find proven, patent-avoiding solutions to recurring engineering problems.

*(Award 2 marks for conceptual design definition, 2.5 marks for morphological analysis, and 2.5 marks for TRIZ.)*

**(b) Pugh Matrix Calculation (7 Marks):**

**For Concept A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{A} &= (0.30 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.30 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.00 + 0.90 + 0.80 \\
&= 3.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Concept B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{B} &= (0.30 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 0.90 + 0.80 + 1.50 + 0.80 \\
&= 4.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Concept C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{C} &= (0.30 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.30 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 1.50 + 0.60 + 1.20 + 0.80 \\
&= 4.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $T_{A} = 3.90$, $T_{B} = 4.00$, $T_{C} = 4.10$.

**Recommendation:** Concept **C** is selected with the highest weighted score of **4.10**, as it offers the best balance of cost, weight, thermal performance, and manufacturability. *(Award 1.5 marks each for three calculations + 1 mark for correct comparison + 1 mark for recommendation with justification.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save Before Exam):**

- **7 Steps**: Recognition of Need → Problem Definition → Conceptual Design → Preliminary Design → Detailed Design → Prototype & Test → Production. **Mnemonic**: "**R**oyal **P**roblems **C**reate **P**ainful **D**esign **P**rocedures" → RPCDPDP.

- **Recognition of Need** is the *trigger*; it is *qualitative* and comes from market, customer, regulation, or technology push.

- **Problem Definition** is *quantitative* and produces the **PDS (Product Design Specification)** — the most important document in design.

- **Conceptual Design** uses **morphological analysis** and **TRIZ** to generate 3–5 solution variants. No dimensions are decided here.

- **Preliminary Design** uses the **Pugh Matrix** for concept selection, followed by **stress, fatigue, and material selection** analysis. Factor of Safety = **1.5 to 4.0** for automotive parts.

- **Detailed Design** locks in **GD&T (ASME Y14.5)**, tolerances, surface finish, and produces the final **CAD model + 2D drawing + BOM**.

- **Prototype & Test** validates the design using **DV (Design Verification)** testing, including static, fatigue (≥ 10⁷ cycles), NVH, and crash tests.

- **Production** uses **APQP, PPAP, DFMA, and Cpk ≥ 1.33** for process validation. **Kaizen** drives continuous improvement.

- **Iteration is mandatory** — every design step has a feedback loop to a previous step. Examiners reward this explicitly.

- **Basquin's Equation** for fatigue: $\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{f}'(2N_{f})^{b}$; always solve for $N_{f}$ in two-equation form.

- **Von Mises Stress** is the standard failure criterion for ductile automotive materials: $\sigma_{e} = \sqrt{\sigma^{2} + 3\tau^{2}}$.

- **Always carry units** (SI: Pa, m, N, kg) and **box the final answer** in numerical sub-parts.

- **Standard design life** target for passenger vehicles: **10 years or 200,000 km**.

- **Automotive-specific standards to remember**: AIS-010 (crash), AIS-052 (EV), BS-VI (emissions), ISO 26262 (functional safety), AIS-156 (battery safety).

- **Common exam pitfall**: Writing generic steps without applying to a specific automotive component. Always tie your answer to a real part (connecting rod, brake disc, gear, leaf spring, etc.) to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preferred Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preferred Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standardized Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preferred Numbers** are a set of standard, internationally recognized numerical values arranged in a **geometric progression**, adopted to rationalize the variety of sizes, ratings, and dimensions used in engineering components. They form the mathematical foundation of modular design, standardization, and interchangeability in automotive and mechanical engineering.

According to **ISO 3 (1973)** and **ISO 497 (1973)**, preferred numbers are defined as the rounded-off terms of geometrical series, calculated from the formula:

$$\phi_N = \sqrt[N]{10}$$

where **N** is the number of steps in one decade (i.e., the number of terms between **1** and **10**).

The two internationally accepted systems of preferred numbers are:
1. **Renard Series (R-Series)** — Governed by ISO 3.
2. **E-Series (E for Electronic)** — Governed by IEC 60063.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ladder of Standard Sizes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are designing a ladder where each rung must be a fixed distance apart. If you want **5 rungs between the floor (1 m) and the ceiling (10 m)**, each successive rung must multiply the previous height by the same constant factor. That constant is the **common ratio (r)**. If instead you want **10 rungs**, each step must be smaller — the ladder has finer steps. Preferred numbers work exactly this way: they are "evenly spaced on a logarithmic scale," giving a logical, economical spread of standardized values.

### Why Preferred Numbers Matter in Automotive Design

- **Reduce variety**: A car manufacturer does not need 1,000 different bolt diameters — only 5 or 10 standardized ones.
- **Interchangeability**: Spare parts across vehicle models can use the same fasteners, bearings, and pipes.
- **Economy of scale**: Mass production of standard sizes reduces cost.
- **Modular design**: Engines, transmissions, and chassis components can be combined from standard building blocks.
- **Logistics simplification**: Warehousing and supply chains are streamlined.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point for KTU 2024:** In automotive component design, preferred numbers are applied for selecting standard values of shaft diameters, bearing bore sizes, fastener sizes (M5, M6, M8, M10…), gear module values, sheet metal gauges, pipe diameters, and standard power ratings (e.g., engine displacements, electric motor kW ratings).

## 1.3 Two Major Series at a Glance

| Series Type | Step Count | Common Ratio | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Renard R5** | 5 steps/decade | ≈ **1.5849** | Coarse mechanical sizing |
| **Renard R10** | 10 steps/decade | ≈ **1.2589** | Standard mechanical sizing |
| **Renard R20** | 20 steps/decade | ≈ **1.1220** | Fine mechanical sizing |
| **Renard R40** | 40 steps/decade | ≈ **1.0593** | Precision components |
| **E-Series E6** | 6 steps/decade | ≈ **1.4678** | Coarse electronics (capacitors) |
| **E-Series E12** | 12 steps/decade | ≈ **1.2115** | Standard resistors |
| **E-Series E24** | 24 steps/decade | ≈ **1.1006** | Fine electronics |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Progression Foundation

A geometric progression (G.P.) is a sequence where each term after the first is obtained by multiplying the previous term by a fixed non-zero number called the **common ratio (r)**.

For preferred numbers in a single decade (typically **1 to 10**):

$$a_n = a_1 \cdot r^{(n-1)}$$

where:
- $a_1$ = first term in the decade (usually **1**)
- $a_n$ = nth term in the decade
- $r$ = common ratio
- $n$ = term index (1, 2, 3, …, N)

## 2.2 Derivation of the Common Ratio

The defining condition for any preferred-number series is that after **N** multiplicative steps starting from 1, we reach 10:

$$a_{N+1} = 1 \cdot r^{N} = 10$$

Solving for r:

$$r^{N} = 10 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad r = 10^{1/N} = \sqrt[N]{10}$$

This elegant relationship is the **heart of preferred number theory**. Each value of N yields a different "fineness" of series.

## 2.3 Renard Series — Detailed Step Values

| Series | Common Ratio $r = 10^{1/N}$ | Percentage Step | Total Terms in Decade |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R5** | $\sqrt[5]{10} \approx 1.5849$ | ≈ **58.49%** | 5 |
| **R10** | $\sqrt[10]{10} \approx 1.2589$ | ≈ **25.89%** | 10 |
| **R20** | $\sqrt[20]{10} \approx 1.1220$ | ≈ **12.20%** | 20 |
| **R40** | $\sqrt[40]{10} \approx 1.0593$ | ≈ **5.93%** | 40 |
| **R80** | $\sqrt[80]{10} \approx 1.0293$ | ≈ **2.93%** | 80 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note:** The R80 series is the most refined in the Renard family. Below R80, the steps become smaller than typical manufacturing tolerances, so further subdivision is rarely useful.

## 2.4 E-Series — Standard Values for Electronic Components

The **E-series** is specifically tailored for electronic components such as resistors, capacitors, and inductors, where the allowable tolerance dictates the granularity:

| E-Series | Tolerance | Common Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| E3 | ± 40% | $10^{1/3} \approx 1.4678$ |
| E6 | ± 20% | $10^{1/6} \approx 1.4678$ |
| E12 | ± 10% | $10^{1/12} \approx 1.2115$ |
| E24 | ± 5% (and ± 1%) | $10^{1/24} \approx 1.1006$ |
| E48 | ± 2% | $10^{1/48} \approx 1.0491$ |
| E96 | ± 1% | $10^{1/96} \approx 1.0243$ |
| E192 | ± 0.5% | $10^{1/192} \approx 1.0121$ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Geometric term | $a_n = a_1 \cdot r^{(n-1)}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | Common ratio | $r = 10^{1/N} = \sqrt[N]{10}$ | N = steps per decade |
| 3 | Percentage step | $\Delta\% = (r - 1) \times 100$ | Expressed in % |
| 4 | Decade product | $r^{N} = 10$ | Defining identity |
| 5 | Decade expansion | $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{N+1}$ | Always ends at 10× of start |
| 6 | E-series tolerance link | $r^N \cdot (1 - \text{tol}) \le r \cdot (1 + \text{tol})$ | Used to choose E-N |
| 7 | Total range coverage | $\log_{10}(a_{\max}/a_{\min})$ | In decades |
| 8 | Cumulative decade count | $N_{\text{total}} = N \times D$ | D = number of decades |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students often write $r = \sqrt[N+1]{10}$ or $r = 10^{1/(N-1)}$. The correct relation is $r = 10^{1/N}$ where **N is the number of preferred values in the decade (e.g., 5 for R5)**, not the index of the last term.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive engineering, preferred numbers govern:
- **Fasteners (bolts, nuts):** ISO metric thread sizes (M1.6, M2, M2.5, M3, M4, M5, M6, M8, M10…) follow R20-like progression.
- **Shaft diameters and bearing bores:** Standardized in mm.
- **Sheet metal thicknesses:** Follow R20/R40.
- **Engine displacements:** 800, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800, 2000 cc (R10-like).
- **Power ratings (kW):** 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100 kW (R5/R10).
- **Tire rim diameters:** 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20 inches.
- **Wire gauges and electric motor windings:** E-series values.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Common Ratio

We begin with the defining requirement: a geometric series of **N** terms in one decade must start at 1 and end at 10 (i.e., cover exactly one decade on a log scale).

**Step 1:** Write the general term of a geometric progression starting at $a_1 = 1$:

$$a_n = 1 \cdot r^{(n-1)} = r^{(n-1)}$$

**Step 2:** Impose the boundary condition that the (N+1)th term equals 10:

$$a_{N+1} = r^{N} = 10$$

**Step 3:** Take the Nth root of both sides to isolate r:

$$r = \sqrt[N]{10} = 10^{1/N}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to logarithmic form for percentage step analysis. The percentage step is defined as:

$$\Delta\% = \left(\frac{a_{n+1} - a_n}{a_n}\right) \times 100 = (r - 1) \times 100$$

For R5:

$$r = 10^{1/5} = 10^{0.2} = 1.58489$$

$$\Delta\% = (1.58489 - 1) \times 100 = 58.489\%$$

For R10:

$$r = 10^{1/10} = 10^{0.1} = 1.25893$$

$$\Delta\% = (1.25893 - 1) \times 100 = 25.893\%$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Generating the R5 Series Values

Starting from $a_1 = 1$ and using $r = 10^{1/5}$:

**Step 1:** $a_1 = 1.0000$ (base value)

**Step 2:** $a_2 = a_1 \cdot r = 1 \times 10^{1/5} = 10^{0.2} = 1.5849$

**Step 3:** $a_3 = a_2 \cdot r = 1.5849 \times 1.5849 = 10^{2/5} = 10^{0.4} = 2.5119$

**Step 4:** $a_4 = a_3 \cdot r = 10^{3/5} = 10^{0.6} = 3.9811$

**Step 5:** $a_5 = a_4 \cdot r = 10^{4/5} = 10^{0.8} = 6.3096$

**Step 6:** $a_6 = a_5 \cdot r = 10^{5/5} = 10^{1.0} = 10.0000$ (decade endpoint)

In summary, using the compact formula $a_n = 10^{(n-1)/5}$:

$$\boxed{a_n = 10^{(n-1)/N} \quad \text{where } N = 5 \text{ for R5}}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — R10 Series Full Decade

Using $r = 10^{1/10}$ and $a_n = 10^{(n-1)/10}$:

| n | $a_n = 10^{(n-1)/10}$ | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $10^{0.0}$ | **1.0000** |
| 2 | $10^{0.1}$ | **1.2589** |
| 3 | $10^{0.2}$ | **1.5849** |
| 4 | $10^{0.3}$ | **1.9953** |
| 5 | $10^{0.4}$ | **2.5119** |
| 6 | $10^{0.5}$ | **3.1623** |
| 7 | $10^{0.6}$ | **3.9811** |
| 8 | $10^{0.7}$ | **5.0119** |
| 9 | $10^{0.8}$ | **6.3096** |
| 10 | $10^{0.9}$ | **7.9433** |
| 11 | $10^{1.0}$ | **10.0000** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check:** The ratio between consecutive terms must be constant. For example, $1.5849 / 1.2589 = 1.2589$ ✓, and $3.1623 / 2.5119 = 1.2590$ ✓ (small rounding).

## 3.4 Worked Example — Generating Next Decade of R10

Suppose we need R10 values for the decade from 10 to 100. Each value is simply the R10 (1–10) values multiplied by 10.

**Step 1:** Take the standard R10 decade (1.0000 to 10.0000).

**Step 2:** Multiply every term by 10 to shift the decade:

$$a_n^{(2)} = 10 \times 10^{(n-1)/10} = 10^{(n-1)/10 + 1} = 10^{(n+9)/10}$$

**Step 3:** Numerical result: 10, 12.589, 15.849, 19.953, 25.119, 31.623, 39.811, 50.119, 63.096, 79.433, 100.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Choosing the Correct E-Series

A resistor manufacturer wants to make 12-ohm resistors with a tolerance of ±10%. Which E-series is appropriate?

**Step 1:** For a tolerance of ±10%, the standard recommendation is the **E12 series**.

**Step 2:** Verify using the criterion that the relative step between adjacent E-series values must be larger than twice the tolerance:

$$r = 10^{1/12} = 1.2115 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta\% = 21.15\%$$

$$2 \times \text{tolerance} = 2 \times 10\% = 20\%$$

Since $21.15\% > 20\%$, the E12 series guarantees that adjacent values do not overlap within tolerance. ✓

**Step 3:** For ±5% tolerance, E24 would be used (r ≈ 1.1006, Δ% ≈ 10.06% > 10% = 2×5% ✓).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Ratios

| Series | N | $r = 10^{1/N}$ Exact | $r$ Decimal | % Step | Half-Decade Spread |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R5 | 5 | $10^{0.2}$ | 1.5849 | 58.49% | 1 → 3.16 |
| R10 | 10 | $10^{0.1}$ | 1.2589 | 25.89% | 1 → 1.78 |
| R20 | 20 | $10^{0.05}$ | 1.1220 | 12.20% | 1 → 1.33 |
| R40 | 40 | $10^{0.025}$ | 1.0593 | 5.93% | 1 → 1.15 |
| R80 | 80 | $10^{0.0125}$ | 1.0293 | 2.93% | 1 → 1.07 |
| E6 | 6 | $10^{1/6}$ | 1.4678 | 46.78% | 1 → 2.15 |
| E12 | 12 | $10^{1/12}$ | 1.2115 | 21.15% | 1 → 1.47 |
| E24 | 24 | $10^{1/24}$ | 1.1006 | 10.06% | 1 → 1.21 |

## 3.7 Multi-Decade Extension — Formula

If you need to cover $D$ decades (e.g., 1 to $10^D$):

$$N_{\text{total}} = N \times D$$

For example, R10 over 4 decades (1 to 10,000):

$$N_{\text{total}} = 10 \times 4 = 40 \text{ values}$$

The general term in the dth decade (where d = 0, 1, 2, …, D−1) is:

$$a_{n,d} = 10^{d + (n-1)/N}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Deriving Preferred Numbers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need for Standardization] --> B[Define Decade Range 1 to 10]
    B --> C[Choose Number of Steps N per Decade]
    C --> D["Compute Common Ratio r = 10 to power 1 over N"]
    D --> E[Generate Geometric Series a sub n = 10 to power n minus 1 over N]
    E --> F{Renard or E-Series?}
    F -- Renard --> G[ISO 3 Standard: R5, R10, R20, R40, R80]
    F -- E-Series --> H[IEC 60063: E3, E6, E12, E24, E48, E96, E192]
    G --> I[Apply to Mechanical and Automotive Components]
    H --> J[Apply to Electronic Components]
    I --> K[Final Design with Standardized Sizes]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Selection Logic for Preferred Series

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Component Design Problem] --> Q1[Is the component mechanical or electronic?]
    Q1 -- Mechanical --> Q2[What is the required precision?]
    Q2 -- Coarse --> R5Choice["Use R5 Series - 5 steps per decade"]
    Q2 -- Standard --> R10Choice["Use R10 Series - 10 steps per decade"]
    Q2 -- Fine --> R20Choice["Use R20 Series - 20 steps per decade"]
    Q2 -- Precision --> R40Choice["Use R40 Series - 40 steps per decade"]
    Q1 -- Electronic --> Q3[What is the manufacturing tolerance?]
    Q3 -- 40 percent --> E3Choice["Use E3 Series"]
    Q3 -- 20 percent --> E6Choice["Use E6 Series"]
    Q3 -- 10 percent --> E12Choice["Use E12 Series"]
    Q3 -- 5 percent --> E24Choice["Use E24 Series"]
    Q3 -- 1 percent --> E96Choice["Use E96 Series"]
```

## 4.3 Architecture — Automotive Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerTrain
        A1[Engine Displacement cc] --> A2[Power Rating kW]
        A2 --> A3[Standardized Values]
    end
    subgraph Chassis
        B1[Shaft Diameter mm] --> B2[Bearing Bore mm]
        B2 --> B3[Standardized Sizes]
    end
    subgraph Body
        C1[Sheet Thickness mm] --> C2[Pipe Diameter mm]
        C2 --> C3[Standardized Gauges]
    end
    subgraph Electronics
        D1[Resistor Values ohm] --> D2[Capacitor Values F]
        D2 --> D3[Standardized E-Series]
    end
    PN[Preferred Number System R-series and E-series] --> A3
    PN --> B3
    PN --> C3
    PN --> D3
```

## 4.4 Decade-to-Decade Expansion Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Dec1["Decade 1: 1 to 10"] --> Dec2["Decade 2: 10 to 100"]
    Dec2 --> Dec3["Decade 3: 100 to 1000"]
    Dec3 --> Dec4["Decade 4: 1000 to 10000"]
    Dec1 -. Multiply all terms by 10 .-> Dec2
    Dec2 -. Multiply all terms by 10 .-> Dec3
    Dec3 -. Multiply all terms by 10 .-> Dec4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block-level functional architecture above maps how preferred number systems serve as the upstream design input for standardizing automotive subsystems — powertrain ratings, chassis dimensions, body gauges, and electronic components.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define preferred numbers. Why are they important in engineering design?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Preferred numbers are a standardized set of numerical values arranged in a geometric progression, used as a basis for selecting standard sizes, ratings, and dimensions in engineering components. They are governed by **ISO 3** and **ISO 497** standards.

**Importance (any 2 points for full marks):**
1. **Rationalization of variety** — They reduce the number of sizes a manufacturer must produce, lowering cost.
2. **Interchangeability** — Components of the same preferred size fit across different assemblies and product lines.
3. **Economy of scale** — Bulk production of standard sizes reduces unit cost.
4. **Logistics and inventory simplification** — Fewer SKUs in the supply chain.
5. **Design flexibility with modularity** — Combinations of standard modules cover a wide range of requirements.

---

### **Q2. State the general formula for the common ratio of a preferred number series and explain the significance of N.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The common ratio is given by:

$$r = 10^{1/N} = \sqrt[N]{10}$$

where **N** is the **number of preferred values in one decade** (i.e., the number of terms between 1 and 10).

**Significance of N:**
- **Larger N → smaller common ratio → finer steps** (e.g., R80 has N = 80, r ≈ 1.029, only 2.93% step).
- **Smaller N → larger common ratio → coarser steps** (e.g., R5 has N = 5, r ≈ 1.585, 58.49% step).
- The choice of N is dictated by the **required precision of components** and **manufacturing tolerances**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the common ratio of a Renard series. Show that the percentage step in R10 is approximately 25.89%. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Generate the R10 preferred numbers for the decade 10 to 100 and list the values. Justify why R10 is widely used in standard mechanical component design. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the defining condition.
A Renard series of N terms in a decade (1 to 10) must satisfy:

$$r^{N} = 10$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Solve for r.
Taking the Nth root:

$$r = \sqrt[N]{10} = 10^{1/N}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** For R10, substitute N = 10.

$$r = 10^{1/10} = 10^{0.1}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Compute the numerical value.

$$r = 10^{0.1} = 1.25893$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute the percentage step.

$$\Delta\% = (r - 1) \times 100 = (1.25893 - 1) \times 100 = 25.893\% \approx 25.89\%$$

Thus, the R10 series has a common ratio of **1.2589** and a step of **25.89%** per term. ✓

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Apply the formula. For the decade 10 to 100, the general term is:

$$a_n = 10 \times 10^{(n-1)/10} = 10^{(n+9)/10} \quad \text{for } n = 1, 2, \ldots, 11$$

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Tabulate the values:

| n | $a_n = 10^{(n+9)/10}$ | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $10^{1.0}$ | **10.00** |
| 2 | $10^{1.1}$ | **12.59** |
| 3 | $10^{1.2}$ | **15.85** |
| 4 | $10^{1.3}$ | **19.95** |
| 5 | $10^{1.4}$ | **25.12** |
| 6 | $10^{1.5}$ | **31.62** |
| 7 | $10^{1.6}$ | **39.81** |
| 8 | $10^{1.7}$ | **50.12** |
| 9 | $10^{1.8}$ | **63.10** |
| 10 | $10^{1.9}$ | **79.43** |
| 11 | $10^{2.0}$ | **100.00** |

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Justification of R10 use.
R10 is the most widely used mechanical preferred-number series because:
- **Moderate step size (25.89%)** balances variety reduction with adequate coverage.
- **10 sizes per decade** is sufficient for most mechanical components (shaft diameters, fastener sizes, sheet thicknesses).
- **Coarser than R20**, hence cheaper to manufacture and stock.
- **Finer than R5**, hence covers a wider design space.
- It aligns with **ISO metric fastener standards** (M5, M6, M8, M10, M12, M16, M20, M24, M30, M36).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the E-series of preferred numbers used in electronic components. State the relationship between the E-series and the manufacturing tolerance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A designer needs to select a preferred resistor value of 4.7 kΩ with a manufacturing tolerance of ±5%. Identify the correct E-series and verify your choice mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Definition of E-series.
The E-series (E for Electronic) is a system of preferred numbers published under **IEC 60063** for standardizing the values of electronic components such as resistors, capacitors, and inductors. The series are designated **E3, E6, E12, E24, E48, E96, and E192**, where the number indicates the count of preferred values per decade.

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Tolerance linkage.
The percentage step of the E-series must be **greater than twice the manufacturing tolerance** to ensure that two adjacent values do not overlap within their tolerance bands:

$$\Delta\% = (r - 1) \times 100 \quad \text{where} \quad r = 10^{1/N}$$

The standard tolerance assignments are:

| E-Series | Standard Tolerance |
|---|---|
| E3 | ± 40% |
| E6 | ± 20% |
| E12 | ± 10% |
| E24 | ± 5% (also ± 1%) |
| E48 | ± 2% |
| E96 | ± 1% |
| E192 | ± 0.5% |

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Example.
For E12 with N = 12, the step is $r = 10^{1/12} = 1.2115$, giving $\Delta\% = 21.15\%$. Since $21.15\% > 2 \times 10\% = 20\%$, adjacent E12 values do not overlap at ±10% tolerance. ✓

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the criterion. A ±5% tolerance corresponds to the **E24 series** (per IEC 60063).

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Verify the choice of E24.

For E24: $N = 24$, $r = 10^{1/24} = 10^{0.04167} = 1.1006$

Percentage step:

$$\Delta\% = (1.1006 - 1) \times 100 = 10.06\%$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply the overlap criterion.

$$2 \times \text{tolerance} = 2 \times 5\% = 10\%$$

Since $\Delta\% = 10.06\% > 10\%$, adjacent E24 values do not overlap within ±5% tolerance. ✓

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Locate 4.7 kΩ in E24.
The E24 decade (1 to 10) values around 4.7 are: …**3.9, 4.7, 5.6**… Multiplying by 1 kΩ gives **3.9 kΩ, 4.7 kΩ, 5.6 kΩ**. The value **4.7 kΩ** is indeed an E24 standard. ✓

A ±5% resistor of 4.7 kΩ has a range of 4.465 kΩ to 4.935 kΩ, which does not overlap with 3.9 kΩ (max 4.095 kΩ) or 5.6 kΩ (min 5.32 kΩ). ✓

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong formula for r:** Writing $r = 10^{1/(N+1)}$ or $r = 10^{(N-1)/N}$. The correct expression is $r = 10^{1/N}$.
> 2. **Confusing N with n:** N is the number of preferred values per decade (constant for the series). n is the running index of an individual term.
> 3. **Forgetting the percentage step:** The KTU examiner often awards a separate mark for explicitly computing $\Delta\% = (r-1) \times 100$. Do not skip this.
> 4. **Mixing Renard and E-series:** Do not state that E12 is governed by ISO 3 — E-series is governed by IEC 60063.
> 5. **Decimal truncation:** R10 values must be expressed to 4 significant digits (e.g., 1.259, 1.585, 1.995). Premature rounding to 2 digits loses accuracy marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the E-series tolerance link:** Always mention that the E-series is chosen so that the percentage step is greater than **twice the manufacturing tolerance** to avoid overlap.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Preferred numbers** are a geometric progression of standard values, used to rationalize component sizes.
- ✅ The **common ratio** is always $r = 10^{1/N} = \sqrt[N]{10}$ where N = number of terms per decade.
- ✅ **Renard series (R5, R10, R20, R40, R80)** is governed by **ISO 3** and is used for **mechanical** components.
- ✅ **E-series (E3 to E192)** is governed by **IEC 60063** and is used for **electronic** components.
- ✅ The **percentage step** of any series is $\Delta\% = (r - 1) \times 100$.
- ✅ **R10 step = 25.89%**, **R20 step = 12.20%**, **R40 step = 5.93%**, **R5 step = 58.49%**.
- ✅ E-series is chosen so that $\Delta\% \ge 2 \times$ manufacturing tolerance.
- ✅ E12 → ±10%, E24 → ±5%, E96 → ±1% (memorize this mapping for KTU).
- ✅ The general term of a preferred series is $a_n = 10^{(n-1)/N}$ (starting from 1).
- ✅ For multi-decade coverage, multiply the basic decade by $10^d$ for the dth decade.
- ✅ **Automotive applications:** fastener sizes, shaft diameters, bearing bores, engine displacements, power ratings, sheet metal gauges, tire rims, and electronic components.
- ✅ ISO metric threads (M5, M6, M8, M10, M12, M16, M20…) follow the **R20 series** in diameter progression.
- ✅ **Larger N → finer steps**; **Smaller N → coarser steps** — this is the key trade-off.
- ✅ Always express preferred values to **4 significant figures** in KTU exam answers.

---
