# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mohr's Circle — Complete Block Architecture (Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block maps the **complete logical flow** of Mohr's circle construction, used by KTU examiners as a **structured analysis method**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Given sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> A2[Compute Centre C_sigma = sigma_x + sigma_y / 2]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Radius R = sqrt half_diff_sq + tau_xy_sq]
    A3 --> A4[Draw Sigma Tau axes]
    A4 --> A5[Mark point X with sigma_x, minus tau_xy]
    A4 --> A6[Mark point Y with sigma_y, plus tau_xy]
    A5 --> A7[Join XY and bisect perpendicularly]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Verify centre lies on Sigma axis at C_sigma]
    A8 --> A9[Draw circle with centre C_sigma and radius R]
    A9 --> B1[Read Sigma_1 = C_sigma + R rightmost point]
    A9 --> B2[Read Sigma_2 = C_sigma minus R leftmost point]
    A9 --> B3[Read Tau_max = R topmost point]
    A1 --> C1[Compute tan 2theta_p = 2 tau_xy / sigma_x minus sigma_y]
    C1 --> C2[Find 2theta_p using quadrant rule]
    C2 --> C3[theta_p = 2theta_p / 2 gives principal plane]
    A1 --> D1[For inclined plane at angle theta]
    D1 --> D2[Compute sigma_n using transformation equation]
    D1 --> D3[Compute tau_n using transformation equation]
    B1 --> E1[Final result compilation]
    B2 --> E1
    B3 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1[End: Full stress state characterised]
```

## 4.2 Stress Element Transformation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Original 2D Element: sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> F2[Rotate element by angle theta CCW]
    F2 --> F3[Resolve forces using static equilibrium]
    F3 --> F4[Sum forces along outward normal direction]
    F3 --> F5[Sum forces along tangential direction]
    F4 --> F6[Apply double angle trigonometric identities]
    F5 --> F6
    F6 --> G1[Get sigma_n expression]
    F6 --> G2[Get tau_n expression]
    G1 --> G3[Set dsigma_n by dtheta = 0]
    G3 --> G4[Solve for principal angle theta_p]
    G4 --> G5[Substitute to get sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    G2 --> G6[Set dtau_n by dtheta = 0]
    G6 --> G7[Solve for theta_s = theta_p minus 45 degrees]
    G7 --> G8[Get tau_max]
    G5 --> H1[Final: Combined stress state fully determined]
    G8 --> H1
```

## 4.3 Application to Automotive Shaft (Bending + Torsion)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1[Automotive Shaft under Bending M and Torsion T] --> J2[Compute Bending Stress: sigma_b = 32 M over pi d cubed]
    J1 --> J3[Compute Torsional Shear Stress: tau_t = 16 T over pi d cubed]
    J2 --> J4[At a point on shaft surface, the element carries sigma_x = sigma_b and tau_xy = tau_t]
    J3 --> J4
    J4 --> J5[Substitute into principal stress equation]
    J5 --> J6[sigma_1,2 = sigma_b over 2 plus minus sqrt sigma_b over 2 squared plus tau_t squared]
    J6 --> J7[Equivalent von Mises stress: sigma_vm = sqrt sigma_b squared plus 3 tau_t squared]
    J6 --> J8[Equivalent max shear stress: sigma_eq = sqrt sigma_b squared plus 4 tau_t squared]
    J7 --> J9[Compare sigma_vm with sigma_allowable = Sy over FoS]
    J8 --> J9
    J9 --> J10[Design verdict: Safe or Redesign]
```

## 4.4 Notation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Symbol Legend] --> K2[sigma_x: Normal stress in x direction MPa]
    K1 --> K3[sigma_y: Normal stress in y direction MPa]
    K1 --> K4[tau_xy: Shear stress on x face in y direction MPa]
    K1 --> K5[theta: Inclination angle of plane in degrees or radians]
    K1 --> K6[sigma_n: Normal stress on inclined plane MPa]
    K1 --> K7[tau_n: Shear stress on inclined plane MPa]
    K1 --> K8[sigma_1, sigma_2: Principal stresses MPa]
    K1 --> K9[tau_max: Maximum in plane shear stress MPa]
    K1 --> K10[C_sigma: Centre of Mohr circle MPa]
    K1 --> K11[R: Radius of Mohr circle MPa]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the term "state of combined stress" at a point in a 2D element. List the three stress components that fully describe the in-plane state of stress at a point.

**Model Answer:**

The **state of combined stress at a point** refers to the simultaneous action of more than one type of stress (normal and/or shear) on an infinitesimal element in the vicinity of that point, such that the resultant stress depends on the orientation of the plane through the point.

For a **2D (plane stress) analysis**, the in-plane state of stress at a point is completely described by three independent components:

1. **Normal stress on the x-face:** $\sigma_x$
2. **Normal stress on the y-face:** $\sigma_y$
3. **Shear stress on the x-face (in the y-direction):** $\tau_{xy}$

By complementary shear, $\tau_{yx} = \tau_{xy}$, so there are only three independent components. The **state of stress** is represented by the symmetric stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** State and explain the **principle of stress invariance**. Why is the quantity $\sigma_x + \sigma_y$ constant for all orientations of the element?

**Model Answer:**

The **principle of stress invariance** states that certain combinations of stress components at a point remain **constant regardless of the orientation** of the coordinate system. For 2D plane stress, the two fundamental invariants are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_n + \sigma_t \quad \text{(on perpendicular planes)} \\
I_2 &= \sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \quad \text{(determinant of stress tensor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** When the element is rotated, the normal stresses on the two perpendicular faces **change** individually, but their **sum** remains constant. This can be verified by adding the normal stresses on any two perpendicular inclined planes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n + \sigma_t = \left(\frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta\right) + \left(\frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} - \frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta - \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta\right) = \sigma_x + \sigma_y
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\cos 2\theta$ and $\sin 2\theta$ terms cancel out, proving invariance. This is why the centre of Mohr's circle, $C_\sigma = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$, is a **fixed point** during element rotation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - **Sign error in transformation equation:** Students frequently forget that the third term is $+\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ in $\sigma_n$ but $-\dfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta$ in $\tau_n$. Cross-check the formula sheet before writing.
> - **Principal angle quadrant mistake:** The value of $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}$ gives $2\theta_p$, **not** $\theta_p$. Divide by 2 at the end.
> - **Mohr's circle sign convention:** Some textbooks plot $\tau_{xy}$ positive downwards; KTU follows the **standard convention** of plotting positive $\tau$ upwards. State the convention used at the start of your answer.
> - **Unit conversion:** $\pi d^3$ requires diameter in **mm** for the result in **MPa** when moment is in **N·mm**.
> - **Skipping Mohr's circle verification:** Even if the analytical answer is correct, drawing Mohr's circle and marking the principal points earns **easy 2 marks** in the KTU scheme.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (A):** At a critical point on an automotive **drive shaft**, the stresses on a 2D element are as follows:
- $\sigma_x = 120 \text{ MPa}$ (tensile, along the shaft axis)
- $\sigma_y = -30 \text{ MPa}$ (compressive, due to radial constraint)
- $\tau_{xy} = 50 \text{ MPa}$ (torsional shear)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine:
1. The **principal stresses** $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$.
2. The **principal angle** $\theta_p$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine:
1. The **maximum in-plane shear stress** $\tau_{max}$ and the corresponding plane orientation.
2. The **normal and shear stress on a plane inclined at $\theta = 25°$** (CCW) to the x-axis.
3. Draw the **Mohr's circle** showing all critical points.

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Centre of Mohr's circle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_\sigma = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{120 + (-30)}{2} = \frac{90}{2} = 45 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Half-difference of normal stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{120 - (-30)}{2} = \frac{150}{2} = 75 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Radius of Mohr's circle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{75^2 + 50^2} = \sqrt{5625 + 2500} = \sqrt{8125} \approx 90.14 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Principal stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= C_\sigma + R = 45 + 90.14 = 135.14 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2 &= C_\sigma - R = 45 - 90.14 = -45.14 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Principal angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 50}{120 - (-30)} = \frac{100}{150} = 0.6667 \\
2\theta_p &= \tan^{-1}(0.6667) = 33.69° \\
\theta_p &= 16.85° \text{ CCW from the x-axis}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Since $\sigma_x > \sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy} > 0$, the principal angle lies in the **first quadrant** of Mohr's circle (between 0° and 90°), confirming our answer.

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum in-plane shear stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} = R = 90.14 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Plane of maximum shear:**

The plane of $\tau_{max}$ is **45° away** from the principal plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_s = \theta_p - 45° = 16.85° - 45° = -28.15° \text{ (i.e., 28.15° CW from x-axis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Stresses on the plane at $\theta = 25°$:**

For $\theta = 25°$, $2\theta = 50°$, so $\cos 2\theta = \cos 50° = 0.6428$ and $\sin 2\theta = \sin 50° = 0.7660$.

**Normal stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \\
\sigma_n &= 45 + 75 \times 0.6428 + 50 \times 0.7660 \\
\sigma_n &= 45 + 48.21 + 38.30 = 131.51 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shear stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta \\
\tau_n &= -75 \times 0.7660 + 50 \times 0.6428 \\
\tau_n &= -57.45 + 32.14 = -25.31 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **negative sign** of $\tau_n$ indicates the shear acts **opposite** to the assumed positive direction on this plane.

**Step 4 — Mohr's Circle sketch (description for answer):**
- Centre on $\sigma$-axis at $(45, 0)$.
- Radius = 90.14 MPa.
- Point $X$ (x-face) at $(120, -50)$, Point $Y$ (y-face) at $(-30, +50)$.
- $\sigma_1$ at $(135.14, 0)$ — rightmost point.
- $\sigma_2$ at $(-45.14, 0)$ — leftmost point.
- $\tau_{max}$ at $(45, +90.14)$ — topmost point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (B):** A **solid circular shaft** of diameter $d = 60 \text{ mm}$ in an automobile gearbox is subjected to a **bending moment** $M = 1.5 \text{ kN·m}$ and a **torsional moment** $T = 2.0 \text{ kN·m}$ simultaneously.

**(a) [7 Marks]**
1. Calculate the **maximum bending stress** $\sigma_b$ and **maximum torsional shear stress** $\tau_t$ at the surface of the shaft.
2. Represent the stress state at the critical point using a **2D stress element** and identify $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]**
1. Determine the **principal stresses** $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ at the critical point.
2. Calculate the **maximum in-plane shear stress** $\tau_{max}$.
3. Using the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**, find the **factor of safety** if the yield strength of the shaft material is $S_y = 350 \text{ MPa}$.

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Section properties (for $d = 60$ mm):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (60)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 12{,}960{,}000}{64} = 636{,}172.5 \text{ mm}^4 \\
J &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = 2I = 1{,}272{,}345 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

Distance from neutral axis to outer fibre: $c = d/2 = 30$ mm.

**Step 2 — Maximum bending stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (60)^3} \\
\sigma_b &= \frac{48 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216{,}000} = \frac{48 \times 10^6}{678{,}584} \approx 70.74 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum torsional shear stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_t &= \frac{T \cdot c}{J} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2.0 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (60)^3} \\
\tau_t &= \frac{32 \times 10^6}{678{,}584} \approx 47.16 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — 2D stress element representation:**

At the topmost point of the shaft (fibre under maximum tension due to bending):
- $\sigma_x = \sigma_b = +70.74$ MPa (along the shaft axis)
- $\sigma_y = 0$ (no radial normal stress on the free surface)
- $\tau_{xy} = \tau_t = 47.16$ MPa (torsional shear on the cross-section)

At the bottommost point (fibre under maximum compression): $\sigma_x = -70.74$ MPa.

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Principal stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{70.74 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{70.74 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + (47.16)^2} \\
\sigma_{1,2} &= 35.37 \pm \sqrt{(35.37)^2 + (47.16)^2} \\
\sigma_{1,2} &= 35.37 \pm \sqrt{1251.04 + 2224.07} \\
\sigma_{1,2} &= 35.37 \pm \sqrt{3475.11} \\
\sigma_{1,2} &= 35.37 \pm 58.95
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= 35.37 + 58.95 = 94.32 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2 &= 35.37 - 58.95 = -23.58 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum in-plane shear stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = 58.95 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor of Safety using Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca):**

According to Tresca theory, yielding begins when $\tau_{max}$ in the element reaches the **shear yield strength** $S_{sy} = S_y/2$ in a simple tension test.

Equivalent maximum shear stress in combined loading:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max, equivalent} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{94.32 - (-23.58)}{2} = \frac{117.90}{2} = 58.95 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Allowable shear stress:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{allow} = \frac{S_y}{2} = \frac{350}{2} = 175 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Factor of safety:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{S_y / 2}{\tau_{max, equivalent}} = \frac{175}{58.95} \approx 2.97
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Design Verdict:** With a factor of safety of approximately **2.97**, the shaft is **safe** under the given combined loading. Since automotive design typically requires $N \geq 1.5$ to $2.0$ for static loading, the design is **acceptable** with a comfortable margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Bending + Torsion Formula:**
> A common error is to use $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \cdot \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}$ for **all** failure theories. This is **only** the **equivalent bending stress for the Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNST)**. For the **Distortion Energy (von Mises) Theory**, use $\sigma_{eq,vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2}$. For **Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca)**, use $\sigma_{eq,tr} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau_t^2}$. KTU paper **specifically** tests which theory the student applies. Always **state the theory** before writing the formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Combined stress** arises when more than one type of load acts on a component at the same time.
- [x] A **2D stress state** is fully described by three components: $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$.
- [x] The **stress transformation equation** (generalised) is:
      $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$
- [x] The **shear stress transformation** is:
      $\tau_n = -\dfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$
- [x] **Principal stresses** are the maximum and minimum normal stresses at a point and are obtained by setting $\dfrac{d\sigma_n}{d\theta} = 0$.
- [x] The **principal angle** satisfies $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$.
- [x] The **maximum in-plane shear stress** equals the radius of Mohr's circle:
      $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$
- [x] The plane of $\tau_{max}$ is at **45°** from the principal plane: $\theta_s = \theta_p - 45°$.
- [x] **Mohr's circle** is a graphical tool where $\sigma$ is plotted on the x-axis and $\tau$ on the y-axis, with centre at $C_\sigma$ and radius $R$.
- [x] On Mohr's circle, the **physical angle is doubled** (angle on circle = $2 \times$ physical angle).
- [x] The quantity $\sigma_x + \sigma_y$ is **invariant** under rotation — useful for verification.
- [x] For a **shaft under bending and torsion**, the equivalent stresses for different theories are:
      - **MNST:** $\sigma_{eq} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$
      - **DET (von Mises):** $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2}$
      - **Tresca (MSS):** $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau_t^2}$
- [x] Always **state the sign convention** before starting a problem — KTU awards 1 mark for clear conventions.
- [x] The **automotive applications** of this topic are drive shafts, axles, stub axles, connecting rods, crank pins, gudgeon pins, and gear shafts.

### Key Equations in One Glance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Centre of Mohr's circle:} \quad C_\sigma &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \\
\text{Radius of Mohr's circle:} \quad R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
\text{Principal stresses:} \quad \sigma_{1,2} &= C_\sigma \pm R \\
\text{Principal angle:} \quad \tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \\
\text{Maximum shear:} \quad \tau_{max} &= R = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Exam Strategy:**
> 1. **Always draw the 2D stress element** with arrows showing the directions of $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ — this earns **2 marks** just for the diagram.
> 2. **Identify $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$** with correct signs (tensile +ve, compressive –ve) before substitution.
> 3. **Compute centre and radius first**, then use them to find principal stresses — this is **faster** and **less error-prone** than direct substitution.
> 4. **Always verify** by checking $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ and the angle lies in the correct quadrant.
> 5. **Draw Mohr's circle** as the final step — it cross-checks the analytical answer and earns **2 additional marks** for presentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Concentration Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress Concentration Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress Concentration Factor (Kt or Kt)** is defined as the ratio of the **maximum (peak) stress** occurring at a localized geometric discontinuity to the **nominal (reference) stress** computed at the same cross-section in the absence of the discontinuity.

Mathematically:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{max}$ = Peak stress at the discontinuity (the true, highest local stress)
- $\sigma_{nom}$ = Nominal stress calculated from elementary strength of materials (ignoring the local disturbance)
- $K_t$ is a **dimensionless**, purely **geometric** factor (theoretical/elastic value)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River Analogy

Imagine a wide, calm river flowing smoothly. The water velocity is low and uniform. Now, place a **large boulder** (geometric discontinuity) in the middle of the stream. The water lines must suddenly squeeze around the boulder, and at the sides of the boulder, the water velocity (analogous to stress) shoots up dramatically. Far away from the boulder, the velocity returns to the original calm value. This localized "spike" in flow velocity at the obstacle is precisely what happens to stress lines (force flow) at a notch, fillet, or hole in a machine component.

In an automotive component (e.g., a crankshaft fillet or a drilled lug), the **theoretical uniform stress** calculated by $\sigma = P/A$ is the "calm river" value. The actual stress at the re-entrant corner can be **2 to 4 times higher** — this is the "rapids at the boulder."

### 1.3 Why Discontinuities Cause Stress Concentration

> [!NOTE]
> Stress flows along **force lines** (also called stress trajectories or isostatic lines). When a component cross-section is uniform, these lines remain parallel and evenly spaced. At a sudden change in geometry (a **discontinuity**), the lines are forced to **bunch up** to preserve continuity of force flow. Since stress is proportional to the density of force lines per unit area, the local stress rises sharply.

**Common Sources of Stress Concentration in Automotive Parts:**

| Component | Discontinuity Type |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Crankshaft | Fillet at journal-to-web transition |
| Connecting rod | Big-end fillet, small-end bore |
| Splined shaft | Tooth root |
| Gear tooth | Root fillet |
| Press-fitted hub | Abrupt change in diameter |
| Lug / bracket | Hole for bolt |
| Leaf spring | Eye-hole |
| Keyed shaft | Keyway termination |
| Riveted / bolted plate | Hole |

### 1.4 Two Related But Distinct Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kt (Theoretical Stress Concentration Factor):** Purely elastic/geometric. Used in static design and brittle materials.
>
> **Kf (Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor or Effective Stress Concentration Factor):** The *realized* amplification under cyclic/dynamic loading for ductile materials. It is always less than Kt for ductile metals because local yielding redistributes stress.
>
> $$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$
>
> where $q$ is the **notch sensitivity** of the material ($0 \le q \le 1$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — How Kt Arises

The derivation of $K_t$ fundamentally comes from the **theory of elasticity** (Airy stress functions, complex variable methods by Muskhelishvili, Kolosov, Inglis, Neuber). The conceptual step-by-step is:

1. **Step 1 — Define nominal stress** as if the geometry were *uniform* (no notch, no hole, no fillet). This is the "remote" or "far-field" stress computed by elementary mechanics: $\sigma_{nom} = P/A$ or $Mc/I$.
2. **Step 2 — Recognize that real stress fields** at a discontinuity are multi-axial and highly non-linear, even when the remote loading is simple uniaxial tension.
3. **Step 3 — Apply the principle of stress trajectory continuity.** Force lines cannot end inside the material. They must "turn" sharply at re-entrant corners, creating locally high triaxial stresses.
4. **Step 4 — Identify the critical point.** The point of maximum principal stress is almost always at the **root of a notch, the edge of a hole, or the fillet of a step**.
5. **Step 5 — Compute or look up Kt** from analytical solutions, photoelasticity, finite element analysis (FEA), or empirical design charts (Peterson, Pilkey, Shigley, Roark).

### 2.2 Why the "Why" Matters for KTU

KTU examiners routinely test *understanding* of why stress concentration occurs, not just formula recall. The **microscopic plastic flow** in ductile materials and the **lack thereof in brittle materials** is the key reason:
- **Ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminium alloys in cars) → local yielding blunts the peak → $K_f < K_t$
- **Brittle materials** (cast iron, ceramics) → no yielding → $K_f \approx K_t$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **master reference** for all KTU 2024 scheme questions on stress concentration. Memorize the geometry-to-formula mapping.

| # | Geometry & Loading | Formula for $K_t$ | Reference Notation | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inglis' Elliptical hole** in infinite plate (tension) | $K_t = 1 + 2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)$ | $a$ = semi-axis $\perp$ to load, $b$ = semi-axis $\parallel$ to load | Infinite plate, sharp ellipse |
| 2 | **Circular hole** in infinite plate (limit of Inglis as $a/b \to \infty$ is *not* taken; classical elasticity gives) | $K_t = 3.00$ | — | Infinite width plate |
| 3 | **Small circular hole** in finite-width plate | $K_t = 3.00 - 3.13\left(\frac{d}{w}\right) + 3.66\left(\frac{d}{w}\right)^{2} - 1.53\left(\frac{d}{w}\right)^{3}$ | $d$ = hole diameter, $w$ = plate width | Tension, $d/w < 0.5$ |
| 4 | **Opposite U-notches** in finite plate (tension) | $K_t = C_1 + C_2\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right) + C_3\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right)^{2} + C_4\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right)^{3}$ | $D$ = remaining ligament, $h$ = notch depth | $0.1 \le h/r \le 20$ |
| 5 | **Stepped flat bar with shoulder fillet** (tension) | $K_t$ from Neuber's chart/polynomial | $r$ = fillet radius, $h$ = step height, $D$ = smaller width | Axial tension |
| 6 | **Stepped round shaft with fillet** (torsion) | $K_{ts}$ from Neuber's chart | $r$ = fillet radius, $D$ = small dia, $d$ = large dia | Pure torsion |
| 7 | **Stepped round shaft with fillet** (bending) | $K_{tb}$ from Neuber's chart | Same geometry, bending moment | Pure bending |
| 8 | **Notch sensitivity** (Peterson) | $q = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{a}{r}}$ | $a$ = material constant (mm or in), $r$ = notch radius | $q \to 0$ for very small $r$; $q \to 1$ for large $r$ |
| 9 | **Effective fatigue factor** | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | — | Static equivalent of fatigue notch |
| 10 | **Neuber constant** (from Neuber's rule) | $K_f = 1 + \frac{K_t - 1}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{a}{r}}}$ | $a$ = Neuber material constant | Alternate form of Peterson |

### 2.4 Neuber's Equation (Most Important for Crankshafts)

For a **stepped flat bar with shoulder fillets under axial tension**, Neuber's classical empirical polynomial is:

$$K_t = C_1 + C_2\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right) + C_3\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right)^{2} + C_4\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right)^{3}$$

where $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$ are **functions of the radius ratio $r/h$** (or $r/d$ for round shafts) and are tabulated in **Neuber's "Theory of Notch Stresses"** (US Translation, 1958, JWA) — this is the **standard reference** in KTU automobile/mechanical design.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **round stepped shaft in bending** with $0.25 \le d/D \le 1$ and $0.25 \le r/d \le 4$, the polynomial coefficients are tabulated in **Shigley's Mechanical Engineering Design** (Chapter 3) and reproduced verbatim in Pilkey's *Peterson's Stress Concentration Factors*. The KTU syllabus cites both.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crankshaft design** — The fillet between the journal and the web has a $K_t$ of 2.0–2.8; design life of the entire engine depends on this.
- **Lug analysis** — Aircraft and suspension lugs are designed using lug efficiency charts derived from $K_t$.
- **Welded joints** — Toe of a fillet weld acts as a sharp notch; weld profiles are rated by toe angle.
- **Fatigue design** — All fatigue calculations under KTU syllabus use $K_f$, not $K_t$.
- **FEA validation** — Experimental stress concentration factors are used to verify mesh-converged FEA models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inglis' Classical Result for an Elliptical Hole

This is the **most important derivation** in the entire stress concentration chapter and a guaranteed KTU question.

**Problem Setup:**
Consider an infinite elastic plate under uniform uniaxial tension $\sigma$ along the x-axis. The plate contains an elliptical hole with semi-major axis $a$ perpendicular to the load and semi-minor axis $b$ parallel to the load.

**Inglis (1913) used the Airy stress function:**

$$\phi = \text{Re}\left[\overline{\overline{z}}\phi'(z) + \chi(z)\right]$$

For an elliptical boundary, the Muskhelishvili mapping function is:

$$z = \omega(\zeta) = \frac{a+b}{2}\zeta + \frac{a-b}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\zeta}$$

**Step 1 — Apply far-field boundary conditions** at infinity: $\sigma_{xx} \to \sigma$, $\sigma_{yy} \to 0$, $\tau_{xy} \to 0$.

**Step 2 — The mapped boundary of the ellipse** is the unit circle $\vert \zeta \vert = 1$, traction-free.

**Step 3 — After applying conformal mapping and satisfying the boundary conditions**, Inglis obtained the stress on the boundary of the ellipse. At the point where the ellipse intersects the x-axis (i.e., the **end of the major axis**), the hoop stress $\sigma_{\theta\theta}$ takes its maximum value.

**Step 4 — Maximum hoop stress** at the end of the major axis (point of highest curvature):

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma\left(1 + \frac{2a}{b}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Defining nominal stress** as the far-field applied stress $\sigma$, the stress concentration factor is:

$$\boxed{K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma} = 1 + 2\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)}$$

**Limit Checks (very important for KTU marks):**
- If $a = b$ (circular hole) → formula gives $K_t = 3$, matching the classical Kirsch (1898) result for a circular hole in an infinite plate. ✓
- If $a/b \to 0$ (very flat ellipse parallel to load) → $K_t \to 1$, meaning the "hole" becomes a thin slit parallel to the load, causing no concentration. ✓
- If $a/b \to \infty$ (sharp crack perpendicular to load) → $K_t \to \infty$, the classical precursor to **linear elastic fracture mechanics** (LEFM). ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Notch Sensitivity (Peterson's Equation)

**Physical Premise:**
In a ductile material, a sharp notch will have a small radius of plastic deformation at the root, which effectively **blunts** the theoretical sharpness. The notch sensitivity $q$ is defined as:

$$q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$$

The factor $q$ ranges from 0 (the material is so notch-sensitive that it does not benefit from local yielding, e.g., a very sharp crack in high-strength steel) to 1 (the material is notch-insensitive, i.e., it yields so readily that the peak stress is completely smoothed out, e.g., very low-strength mild steel).

**Peterson (1953) proposed an empirical relation:**

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{a}{r}}$$

where $a$ is a **material constant** (in mm or inches depending on unit system) and $r$ is the **notch radius**.

**Solving for $K_f$:**

$$K_f - 1 = q(K_t - 1) = \frac{K_t - 1}{1 + \frac{a}{r}}$$

Adding 1 to both sides:

$$K_f = 1 + \frac{K_t - 1}{1 + \frac{a}{r}}$$

**Equivalently**, multiplying numerator and denominator by $r$:

$$\boxed{K_f = 1 + \frac{r(K_t - 1)}{r + a}}$$

**Limit Checks:**
- $r \to \infty$ (large radius) → $K_f \to K_t$ (no effective blunting; the part behaves elastically). ✓
- $r \to 0$ (vanishingly sharp) → $K_f \to 1$ (sharp notch in very ductile material — the local yielding fully redistributes stress). ✓
- $a \to 0$ (very notch-sensitive material like ultra-high-strength steel) → $K_f \to K_t$. ✓

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (matches KTU 2024 Part B style):**
>
> A steel plate of width $w = 80$ mm and thickness $t = 10$ mm has a central transverse hole of diameter $d = 20$ mm. It is loaded in tension by a force $P = 50$ kN. The material is a ductile steel with notch sensitivity parameter $a = 0.25$ mm (in Peterson's equation). The notch radius at the hole edge is $r = 5$ mm. Determine the maximum stress and the effective fatigue stress concentration factor.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gross section area** (using full width):
$$A_g = w \cdot t = 80 \times 10 = 800 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Net section area** (subtracting the hole):
$$A_n = (w - d) \cdot t = (80 - 20) \times 10 = 600 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Choose the correct nominal stress definition.** KTU convention (and Shigley/Pilkey) typically uses the **net section** for plates with holes:
$$\sigma_{nom} = \frac{P}{A_n} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ N}}{600 \text{ mm}^2} = 83.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the geometric stress concentration factor** using Howland's polynomial for a finite-width plate:
$$\frac{d}{w} = \frac{20}{80} = 0.25$$

$$K_t = 3.00 - 3.13(0.25) + 3.66(0.25)^2 - 1.53(0.25)^3$$

Computing term-by-term:
- $3.13 \times 0.25 = 0.7825$
- $3.66 \times 0.0625 = 0.22875$
- $1.53 \times 0.015625 = 0.023906$

$$K_t = 3.00 - 0.7825 + 0.22875 - 0.023906 = 2.4223 \approx 2.42$$

**Step 5 — Notch sensitivity** (Peterson):
$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{a}{r}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{0.25}{5}} = \frac{1}{1.05} = 0.9524$$

**Step 6 — Effective fatigue stress concentration factor:**
$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.9524 \times (2.4223 - 1) = 1 + 0.9524 \times 1.4223 = 1 + 1.3546 = 2.3546$$

$$\boxed{K_f \approx 2.35}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum actual stress at the hole edge** (for fatigue design):
$$\sigma_{max} = K_f \cdot \sigma_{nom} = 2.35 \times 83.33 = 195.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Interpretation:** The peak stress at the hole boundary is about **2.35 times** the average stress on the net cross-section, reaching nearly 196 MPa. This single value dictates the entire fatigue life of the plate.

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Formula To Use When"

| Given Geometry | Loading | Formula to Apply | KTU Past Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Elliptical/circular hole in *wide* plate | Tension | Inglis: $K_t = 1 + 2a/b$ | Very High |
| Circular hole in *finite-width* plate | Tension | Howland polynomial (4th order) | High |
| Stepped round shaft with fillet | Bending | Neuber's polynomial (from chart/table) | Very High |
| Stepped round shaft with fillet | Torsion | Neuber's polynomial (from chart/table) | High |
| Stepped flat bar with shoulder fillet | Tension | Neuber's polynomial (from chart/table) | High |
| Plate with U-notches (both sides) | Tension | Neuber's notch polynomial | Medium |
| Any component with notch | Cyclic/fatigue | Peterson: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Very High |
| Material with unknown notch sensitivity | — | Use $q = 1$ (conservative, brittle assumption) | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stress Concentration Analysis

The flowchart below maps the **decision logic** a KTU-design engineer follows from loading to final design stress.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Geometry and Loading] --> B{Is there a geometric discontinuity?}
    B -- No --> C[Use simple strength of materials formula sigma equals P by A]
    B -- Yes --> D[Identify discontinuity type: hole, fillet, notch, keyway]
    D --> E[Compute nominal stress sigma_nom on reference section]
    E --> F[Apply appropriate Kt formula or chart lookup]
    F --> G[Determine material and notch sensitivity q]
    G --> H{Loading type?}
    H -- Static or brittle material --> I[Use Kt directly: sigma_max equals Kt times sigma_nom]
    H -- Cyclic or fatigue --> J[Compute Kf equals 1 plus q times Kt minus 1]
    I --> K[Apply factor of safety: design stress equals sigma_max by FS]
    J --> L[Apply Soderberg or Goodman fatigue criterion with Kf]
    K --> M[Final design stress check]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Accept or revise geometry to reduce Kt]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Kt, Kf, and Notch Sensitivity q

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Geometric Discontinuity] --> B[Kt theoretical elastic factor]
    A --> C[Material notch sensitivity q]
    C --> D[Peterson equation q equals 1 by 1 plus a by r]
    B --> E[Kf effective fatigue factor]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Kf equals 1 plus q times Kt minus 1]
    F --> G[Final fatigue life assessment]
    B --> H[Static design of brittle parts]
    F --> I[Design of ductile parts under cyclic load]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Force Line Behaviour at a Circular Hole

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Far_Field[Far field uniform tension sigma]
        A1[Stress lines parallel and evenly spaced]
    end
    subgraph Near_Hole[Near circular hole]
        B1[Stress lines bunch tightly at hole edges]
        B2[Maximum stress sigma_max at point perpendicular to load]
        B3[Minimum stress sigma_min equals zero at point parallel to load]
    end
    subgraph Plastic_Zone[Plastic zone if ductile]
        C1[Yielded region of radius rp blunts the peak]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    C1 --> B2
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Stepped Shaft with Fillet (Automotive Crankshaft Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Larger diameter D] --> B[Shoulder transition with fillet radius r]
    B --> C[Smaller diameter d]
    B --> D[Critical stress location: root of fillet]
    D --> E[Nominal stress sigma_nom on smaller diameter d]
    E --> F[Apply Neuber's polynomial for Kt]
    F --> G[Compute sigma_max equals Kt times sigma_nom]
    G --> H[Apply notch sensitivity q for fatigue]
    H --> I[Compute Kf and final design stress]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Inglis' Limiting Cases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inglis equation Kt equals 1 plus 2a by b] --> B{a by b ratio}
    B -- a equals b, circular hole --> C[Kt equals 3.0 Kirsch result]
    B -- a by b small, slit parallel to load --> D[Kt approaches 1.0, no concentration]
    B -- a by b large, sharp crack --> E[Kt approaches infinity, LEFM domain]
    B -- moderate a by b --> F[Typical Kt in range 2 to 4 for automotive]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define **Stress Concentration Factor (Kt)**. Mention its significance in the design of automotive components.

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Style):**
*Stress Concentration Factor (Kt) is defined as the ratio of the maximum local stress at a geometric discontinuity to the nominal stress computed on the reference cross-section:*

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$$

*It is a dimensionless, purely geometric factor that quantifies the amplification of stress at locations such as fillets, holes, keyways, and notches. In automotive component design (e.g., crankshaft fillet, lug holes, gear tooth roots), Kt plays a critical role because fatigue cracks almost always initiate at these stress-amplified locations. Accurate estimation of Kt is therefore essential for predicting fatigue life and ensuring safe design.*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Theoretical Stress Concentration Factor (Kt)** and **Fatigue/Effective Stress Concentration Factor (Kf)**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $K_t$ | $K_f$ |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Definition | Peak stress / nominal stress (elastic) | Effective factor used in fatigue design |
| Material dependence | None (purely geometric) | Depends on material notch sensitivity $q$ |
| Loading condition | Static / theoretical elastic | Cyclic / fatigue loading |
| Value range | Always $\ge 1$ | $1 \le K_f \le K_t$ |
| Brittle material | $K_f = K_t$ | $K_f = K_t$ |
| Ductile material | $K_t$ as computed | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ |

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q-B Choice 1, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the **stress concentration factor around an elliptical hole in an infinite plate under uniaxial tension**, using Inglis' classical elasticity approach. Clearly state all boundary conditions and limit cases.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A stepped round shaft in bending has $D = 60$ mm (larger diameter), $d = 40$ mm (smaller diameter), and fillet radius $r = 6$ mm. The bending moment is $M = 5$ kN·m. Using Neuber's polynomial for bending with $r/d = 0.15$ and $D/d = 1.5$, the relevant coefficients are: $C_1 = 0.945$, $C_2 = 0.560$, $C_3 = -0.045$, $C_4 = 0.030$. Compute the **maximum bending stress at the fillet root** and the **effective fatigue stress concentration factor**, given material notch sensitivity $q = 0.85$.

**Model Solution (a):**
- State the problem setup, the elliptical geometry, and the far-field boundary conditions (uniform $\sigma$ along x).
- Define the Airy stress function and apply Muskhelishvili's complex mapping $z = \omega(\zeta) = \frac{a+b}{2}\zeta + \frac{a-b}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\zeta}$.
- Walk through the four-step derivation culminating in $K_t = 1 + 2(a/b)$.
- Enumerate the three limit cases: $a/b = 1$ → $K_t = 3$ (Kirsch); $a/b \to 0$ → $K_t = 1$; $a/b \to \infty$ → $K_t \to \infty$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Section modulus at the smaller diameter:**
$$Z = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (40)^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 64{,}000}{32} = 6283.19 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Nominal bending stress:**
$$\sigma_{nom} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}}{6283.19 \text{ mm}^3} = 795.77 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Argument for Neuber's polynomial:**
$$\frac{2h}{D} = \frac{2 \times (D - d)/2}{d} = \frac{D - d}{d} = \frac{60 - 40}{40} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_t$:**
$$K_t = 0.945 + 0.560(0.5) + (-0.045)(0.5)^2 + 0.030(0.5)^3$$
$$K_t = 0.945 + 0.280 - 0.01125 + 0.00375 = 1.2175$$

**Step 5 — Maximum bending stress:**
$$\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom} = 1.2175 \times 795.77 = 968.97 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Effective fatigue factor:**
$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.85 \times (1.2175 - 1) = 1 + 0.85 \times 0.2175 = 1.1849$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\sigma_{max} \approx 969$ MPa
- $K_f \approx 1.185$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and derive **Peterson's equation for notch sensitivity**. Discuss its importance in fatigue design of automotive components.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An automotive **lug bracket** has a pin hole of diameter $d = 25$ mm. The lug width is $w = 50$ mm, and the lug is loaded in tension by $P = 80$ kN. Using Howland's polynomial for a finite-width plate with a central hole, compute the **stress concentration factor** and the **maximum stress at the hole boundary**. If the lug is made of ductile aluminium alloy with notch sensitivity parameter $a = 0.40$ mm and effective notch radius $r = 3$ mm, compute the **effective fatigue stress concentration factor $K_f$**.

**Model Solution (a):**
- Define notch sensitivity: $q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$
- Present Peterson's empirical relation: $q = \frac{1}{1 + a/r}$
- Solve for $K_f$: $K_f = 1 + \frac{r(K_t - 1)}{r + a}$
- Discuss the three limit cases ($r \to 0$, $r \to \infty$, $a \to 0$).
- Conclude with the practical significance: in cyclic-loaded automotive parts (e.g., connecting rods, suspension arms), $K_f$ is the actual factor to use; ignoring it over-estimates stress in ductile parts and wastes material, while ignoring $K_t$ under-estimates stress and causes fatigue failure.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Net section area** at the hole:
$$A_n = (w - d) \cdot t$$

(Assuming unit thickness $t = 1$ mm for the calculation; if thickness is not given, assume $t = 10$ mm standard.)
$$A_n = (50 - 25) \times 10 = 250 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Nominal stress on net section:**
$$\sigma_{nom} = \frac{P}{A_n} = \frac{80{,}000}{250} = 320 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K_t$ using Howland's polynomial** with $d/w = 25/50 = 0.5$:
$$K_t = 3.00 - 3.13(0.5) + 3.66(0.5)^2 - 1.53(0.5)^3$$
$$K_t = 3.00 - 1.565 + 0.915 - 0.19125 = 2.159$$

**Step 4 — Maximum stress at hole boundary:**
$$\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom} = 2.159 \times 320 = 690.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Notch sensitivity** (Peterson, $a = 0.40$ mm, $r = 3$ mm):
$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{0.40}{3}} = \frac{1}{1.1333} = 0.8824$$

**Step 6 — Effective fatigue factor:**
$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.8824 \times (2.159 - 1) = 1 + 0.8824 \times 1.159 = 2.0226$$

$$\boxed{K_f \approx 2.02}$$

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Stress Concentration Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong reference for nominal stress** — Always state clearly whether you are using the *net section* (with hole) or *gross section* (without hole). KTU convention is net section for plates with holes and the smaller diameter for stepped shafts.
> 2. **Using Kt for fatigue design of ductile parts** — This is the single most common error. Use $K_f$, not $K_t$, in Soderberg/Goodman fatigue equations.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — Peterson's $a$ is in mm (SI) or inches (US customary). Mixing units gives wrong $q$ values.
> 4. **Not validating limit cases** — Always check the formula at the boundary: e.g., for Inglis, $K_t = 3$ when $a = b$ (circular hole).
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" in theory questions** — KTU examiners award marks for *physical reasoning*, not just formula substitution. Always state "force lines bunch up at the discontinuity" or "local yielding redistributes stress in ductile materials."
> 6. **Confusing $a$ (semi-major axis) with $a$ (Peterson material constant)** — Use distinct notation: $a_{ellipse}$ and $a_{material}$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**
>
> - **Master the master equation**: $K_t = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_{nom}$ — always know your reference section.
> - **Inglis for ellipse**: $K_t = 1 + 2(a/b)$ — remember $a \perp$ load, $b \parallel$ load.
> - **Kirsch special case**: $K_t = 3$ for circular hole in *infinite* plate.
> - **Howland for finite plate with hole**: 4th-order polynomial in $d/w$.
> - **Neuber's polynomial**: $K_t = C_1 + C_2(2h/D) + C_3(2h/D)^2 + C_4(2h/D)^3$ — coefficients depend on $r/d$ and are read from charts/tables.
> - **Peterson's notch sensitivity**: $q = 1/(1 + a/r)$.
> - **Effective fatigue factor**: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$.
> - **$K_f$ is for fatigue/ductile**; **$K_t$ is for static/brittle**.
> - **Typical $K_t$ values** in automotive components: 1.5–2.5 (shafts with generous fillets), 2.0–3.0 (sharp fillets), 2.0–3.0 (lug holes), 2.5–3.0 (keyway terminations).
> - **Always check units** in Neuber's constants; British/American Shigley tables often use inches.
> - **FEA validation tip**: A converged FEA model should give $K_t$ within 5% of the analytical/chart value for a simple test case.
> - **Design mitigation strategies** (asked as application questions): increase fillet radius, add relief grooves, use generous blends, avoid sharp internal corners, and use undercuts in highly stressed regions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theories of Failure - Guest's Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Theories of Failure - Guest's Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Guest's Theory of Failure**, also known as the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST)** or **Tresca-Guest Yield Criterion**, was proposed by **W.J. Guest in 1900**. It is one of the earliest empirical theories of failure developed for predicting the yielding of ductile materials subjected to combined (multi-axial) loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Guest's Theory states that a ductile material under combined loading will yield (fail) when the maximum shear stress in the complex stress system equals the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tensile test conducted on the same material.*

Mathematically, the criterion is expressed as:

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_{max, \, yield}$$

where **τmax** is the absolute maximum shear stress in the combined stress state, and **τmax, yield = σyt / 2** is the shear yield stress obtained from a uniaxial tensile test (σyt = uniaxial tensile yield strength of the material).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Card Deck Shearing Test:** Imagine a deck of playing cards held firmly between your palms. If you slide your palms in opposite directions (shear), the cards slip past each other along parallel planes. The deck "fails" by sliding — exactly how a ductile metal slips along internal crystallographic planes (slip bands at ~45° to the loading axis). Guest's Theory says: *no matter how complicated the 3-D loading on a machine component, it will start to "slip" internally when the largest such sliding tendency matches that of a simple pulled-apart specimen in a tensile test.*

A simpler engineering analogy is **testing a chalk stick by twisting it** — it never snaps cleanly in tension; it shears off obliquely. That oblique fracture surface corresponds to the plane of maximum shear stress predicted by Guest.

### Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **σyt** = uniaxial tensile yield strength (in **MPa** or **N/mm²**) — determined experimentally.
- **τmax, yield** = **σyt / 2** (assumes the material yields simultaneously in tension and shear per the Tresca flow rule).
- **Factor of Safety (N or FOS)** = dimensionless ratio between the yield strength and the actual induced shear stress state.

### Historical Significance in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Guest's empirical work (1900) predates the more sophisticated **Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises, 1913)** and is therefore **historically the first rational yield criterion** to gain engineering acceptance. It is *still used today* because of its **simplicity and inherent conservatism**, especially when designing components where *safe-but-economical* designs are preferred over *minimum-weight* designs — typical in **automotive chassis, suspension arms, brake levers, and steering linkages**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Underlying Physical Reasoning

1. **Crystallographic Slip Initiation:** Ductile metals (e.g., mild steel, aluminum alloys) deform plastically when atomic planes slide over one another. This sliding is governed purely by **shear stress** on the slip plane.
2. **Critical Plane Hypothesis:** Guest postulated that there exists *one* plane in the stressed body on which the shear stress is maximum; failure begins on that plane when the shear stress reaches a critical value.
3. **Experimental Basis:** From a uniaxial tensile test, at the moment of yielding, σ = σyt and the complementary shear stresses on planes at 45° equal σyt / 2. This is the **calibration value**.
4. **Equivalence Principle:** For any complex 3-D stress state, *compute* the absolute maximum shear stress (from principal stresses σ₁, σ₃) and *compare* it to σyt / 2.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes every formula the KTU examiner expects you to know for Guest's Theory. Memorize this — it constitutes ~70 % of all 3-mark and 14-mark question stems in Module 1.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Principal stresses (2-D plane stress) | First step in **all** Guest's theory problems |
| 2 | $\sigma_{1,2,3} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3} + \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_y - \sigma_z}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_z - \sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2} \cdot \cos\theta$ | Principal stresses (3-D, full cubic) | Only for thick-walled components like brake drums, flywheels |
| 3 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | Absolute maximum shear stress | Governing formula for Guest |
| 4 | $\tau_{max,\, yield} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{2}$ | Shear yield stress in tension | Calibration value |
| 5 | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}$ | Guest's failure equation with FOS = N | Direct design equation |
| 6 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{2 N}$ | Equivalent shear-stress formulation | Often easier to recall |
| 7 | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$ | Factor of Safety from known loading | Verification of existing designs |
| 8 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}$ | Equivalent allowable normal stress (for design) | When the problem states σ_x, σ_y, τ_xy and asks for member thickness |

### Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Equation

- **Equation 1** arises from solving the characteristic equation of the 2-D stress tensor — its derivation is required in SECTION_3.
- **Equation 3** uses **Mohr's Circle** geometry: the radius of the largest Mohr's circle (constructed from σ₁ and σ₃) is the absolute maximum shear stress.
- **Equation 5** is the *operational form*: the *difference* between the algebraically largest and algebraically smallest principal stresses governs yielding.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Automotive Component | Stress State | Why Guest's Theory is Used |
|---|---|---|
| Connecting rod (combined bending + axial) | Biaxial (σ_x, σ_y) | Simple, conservative, no σ₂ data needed |
| Steering knuckle | Biaxial bending + torsion | τmax directly measurable from torque |
| Drive shaft under combined torsion + bending | Biaxial | Direct τmax formula application |
| Brake pedal (cantilever beam) | Bending + shear | Quick FOS estimation |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When a KTU problem gives you *only* σ_x, σ_y and τ_xy (without specifying σ_z), assume **plane stress** (σ_z = 0) and compute two in-plane principal stresses; then identify σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃ where σ₃ could be **0** (or **negative** if both in-plane stresses are tensile but one principal stress is negative when one stress component is compressive).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Principal Stresses in 2-D Plane Stress

**Starting Point — The Stress Transformation Equation:**

For a plane element subjected to σ_x, σ_y and τ_xy, the normal stress on a plane inclined at angle θ from the x-axis is given by:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

**Step 1 — Identify the extremum condition.** Principal stresses occur where dσ_n / dθ = 0:

$$\frac{d\sigma_n}{d\theta} = -\left(\sigma_x - \sigma_y\right)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the principal angle.** Rearranging:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the principal-stress magnitudes.** Substituting the optimum angle back into σ_n and using trigonometric identities (sin 2θ = 2τ_xy / R, cos 2θ = (σ_x − σ_y)/R where R is the radius):

$$\boxed{\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

**Logical Narrative:** The term under the square root, *R*, is the radius of Mohr's circle for the in-plane stress state. Adding it to the centre coordinate gives σ₁; subtracting gives σ₂. The third principal stress (σ₃) for plane stress is **0** (out-of-plane direction is stress-free).

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress from Mohr's Circle

Consider three principal stresses ordered as: **σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃**.

**Step 1 — Construct three Mohr's circles.** The first circle (largest) connects σ₁ and σ₃, the second connects σ₁ and σ₂, the third connects σ₂ and σ₃.

**Step 2 — Identify absolute maximum shear stress.** By inspection, the radius of the *largest* circle governs:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

**Logical Narrative:** Even though σ₂ may be large, the shear between σ₁ and σ₃ — i.e., on the plane containing the σ₂ axis — is always the largest possible shear in the body. This is the **central insight of Guest's Theory**: σ₂ does *not* appear in the governing equation.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Guest Yield Criterion

**Step 1 — Calibration experiment (simple tension).** In a uniaxial tensile test, at the instant of yielding:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_{yt}, \quad \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$$

The maximum shear stress in this calibration test is:

$$\tau_{max,\, yield} = \frac{\sigma_{yt} - 0}{2} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the failure postulate.** Guest asserted:

$$\tau_{max,\, complex} = \tau_{max,\, yield}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Generalise for safety factor N.** Introducing the factor of safety:

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{2N} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}}$$

**Logical Narrative:** The factor of safety N is *applied* to the yield strength (resistance side), not to the load — this is the **Load and Resistance Factor Design (LRFD)** convention used throughout KTU.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem Statement:** A steel shaft is subjected to the following stresses at a critical point:
σ_x = 80 MPa (tensile), σ_y = 40 MPa (compressive), τ_xy = 30 MPa.
The material has σ_yt = 250 MPa. Using Guest's Theory, find the **factor of safety**.

**Step 1 — Compute the in-plane principal stresses:**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{40}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{120}{2}\right)^2 + 900}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{(60)^2 + 900} = 20 \pm \sqrt{3600 + 900}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{4500} = 20 \pm 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

So: σ₁ = +87.08 MPa, σ₂ = −47.08 MPa.

**Step 2 — Identify σ₃ (plane stress):** For plane stress, σ₃ = 0. However, we must order:
σ₁ = 87.08 MPa > σ₂ = 0 MPa > σ₃ = −47.08 MPa.

**Step 3 — Compute τ_max:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{87.08 - (-47.08)}{2} = \frac{134.16}{2} = 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Guest's equation:**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}$$

$$134.16 = \frac{250}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{250}{134.16} = 1.863$$

**Final Answer:** **N ≈ 1.86** — the component is safe with a Factor of Safety of 1.86.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Apply Guest's Theory

| Stress State | σ₂ known? | Recommended Criterion | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plane stress, ductile | No (assume σ₂ = 0) | **Guest's Theory** | Simple, conservative |
| Biaxial tension, σ₂ known | Yes | Both Guest & DE give similar results | DE ≈ 1.155 × Guest |
| Pure torsion | N/A (only shear) | **Guest's Theory** | Identical to DE in pure shear |
| Hydrostatic pressure dominant | All σ_i ≈ equal | **Neither** (deformation is volumetric, not distortional) | Use Rankine or special criteria |
| Brittle material (cast iron, ceramics) | N/A | **NOT Guest's** | Use Rankine / Modified Mohr |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Guest's Theory Problem-Solving Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block maps the **engineering decision flow** for solving any Guest's Theory problem in the KTU Automotive Component Design examination. Memorize this flow — it has appeared as a 7-mark sub-question in past papers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Stress State sigma_x sigma_y tau_xy] --> B{Plane Stress or 3D State}
    B -->|Plane Stress| C[Compute in-plane principal stresses sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    B -->|3D State| D[Compute all 3 principal stresses via stress invariants]
    C --> E[Set sigma_3 = 0]
    D --> F[Order: sigma_1 greater than sigma_2 greater than sigma_3]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute tau_max equals sigma_1 minus sigma_3 divided by 2]
    G --> H{Find FOS or Design Thickness}
    H -->|Find FOS| I[N equals sigma_yt divided by sigma_1 minus sigma_3]
    H -->|Design Thickness| J[Set N equals design FOS, solve for unknown dimension]
    I --> K[Compare with allowable FOS, conclude SAFE or UNSAFE]
    J --> K
    K --> L[End]
```

### 4.2 Mohr's Circle Schematic — Conceptual Visualization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below is a **text-based Mohr's Circle** representation. In KTU exams, you are expected to *draw* this circle to scale and label σ₁, σ₂ (or σ₃), the centre C, the radius R = τ_max, and the principal planes (2θ_p apart).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> B[Compute Center: C equals sigma_x plus sigma_y divided by 2]
    A --> C[Compute Radius: R equals sqrt of bracket sigma_x minus sigma_y over 2 squared plus tau_xy squared]
    B --> D[Plot sigma_axis horizontal, tau_axis vertical]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Mark Point P1 sigma_x negative tau_xy]
    D --> F[Mark Point P2 sigma_y positive tau_xy]
    E --> G[Draw circle with center C and radius R]
    F --> G
    G --> H[sigma_1 equals C plus R right extreme]
    G --> I[sigma_3 or sigma_2 equals C minus R left extreme]
    H --> J[tau_max equals R equals sigma_1 minus sigma_3 divided by 2]
    I --> J
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Guest vs. Other Theories of Failure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Theories of Failure for Ductile Materials] --> B[Maximum Normal Stress Theory - Rankine]
    A --> C[Maximum Shear Stress Theory - Guest]
    A --> D[Maximum Principal Strain Theory - Saint Venant]
    A --> E[Distortion Energy Theory - von Mises Hencky]
    C --> F[Criterion: sigma_1 minus sigma_3 equals sigma_yt divided by N]
    E --> G[Criterion: complex second invariant formula]
    F --> H[Conservative, simple, ignores sigma_2]
    G --> I[More accurate, considers sigma_2, preferred for design]
    H --> J[Best for: shafts, axles, torsion dominant members]
    I --> K[Best for: general 3D components, FEA validation]
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Failure Mapping (Automotive Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Automotive Component] --> B{Loading Type}
    B -->|Bending plus Axial| C[Connecting Rod, Piston Pin]
    B -->|Torsion plus Bending| D[Drive Shaft, Axle]
    B -->|Pure Bending| E[Leaf Spring, Stabilizer Bar]
    C --> F[Apply Guest's Theory with 2D principal stresses]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Apply Guest's Theory with combined bending plus torsion equivalence]
    F --> H[Compute FOS and check against KTU design code]
    G --> H
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*State Guest's Theory of Failure. Why is it preferred for ductile materials subjected to combined loading?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Guest's Theory (Maximum Shear Stress Theory) states that a ductile material under combined loading yields when the maximum shear stress in the complex stress system equals the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tension test: **(σ₁ − σ₃)/2 = σyt/(2N)**.
2. **Justification (1.5 marks):** It is preferred because:
   - Ductile materials fail by **shear (slip along crystallographic planes)**, not by brittle fracture.
   - It is **mathematically simple** — requires only σ₁ and σ₃ (intermediate σ₂ is ignored).
   - It is **conservative**, providing an inherent safety margin.
   - It is particularly suited for **torsion-dominated** automotive components (drive shafts, axles).

---

#### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*List any three limitations of Guest's Theory of Failure.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**
1. It **ignores the effect of the intermediate principal stress σ₂**, even though σ₂ influences yielding in real materials.
2. It gives **identical predictions for tension and compression**, which is inaccurate for materials with different tensile and compressive strengths (e.g., cast iron).
3. It is **not suitable for brittle materials** like cast iron, ceramics, or concrete — for these, Rankine's Maximum Normal Stress Theory is preferred.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Order the principal stresses** before applying the formula. Many students lose 2 marks by using (σ₁ − σ₂) instead of (σ₁ − σ₃). Always sort: σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃.
> 2. **For plane stress**, remember σ₃ = 0, but σ₂ may be negative (compressive), which then becomes the new σ₃ after reordering.
> 3. **Do not confuse Guest with Rankine** — Rankine uses max(σ₁, |σ₃|), Guest uses (σ₁ − σ₃). They give very different answers.
> 4. **Unit consistency**: Use **MPa** throughout; do not mix N/mm² and Pa.
> 5. Show all intermediate steps — examiners award **step marks** even if the final answer is incorrect.

---

#### **Question B-A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 — Internal Choice Pattern]
A point on a critical section of an automotive connecting rod is subjected to the following stresses:
σ_x = 120 MPa (tensile), σ_y = 60 MPa (compressive), τ_xy = 45 MPa.

The connecting rod is made of **EN8 steel** with σ_yt = 380 MPa and σ_uc = 700 MPa.

**(a)** Determine the **principal stresses** at the critical point and identify the planes on which they act. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Guest's Theory**, calculate the **factor of safety** of the connecting rod at this point and comment on its safety. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
σ_x = 120 MPa, σ_y = −60 MPa, τ_xy = 45 MPa.

**Step 2 — Compute centre of Mohr's circle:**

$$C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{120 + (-60)}{2} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute radius:**

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - (-60)}{2}\right)^2 + (45)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(90)^2 + (45)^2} = \sqrt{8100 + 2025} = \sqrt{10125} = 100.62 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute principal stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = C + R = 30 + 100.62 = 130.62 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = C - R = 30 - 100.62 = -70.62 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

For plane stress, σ₃ = 0.

**Step 5 — Reorder the three principal stresses:**
σ₁ = 130.62 MPa, σ₂ = 0 MPa, σ₃ = −70.62 MPa.

**Step 6 — Compute the principal plane angle:**

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 45}{120 - (-60)} = \frac{90}{180} = 0.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_p = 13.28°}$$

The σ₁ plane is at θ₁ = 13.28° and σ₃ plane is at θ₃ = 13.28° + 90° = 103.28°.

---

**Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Apply Guest's failure equation:**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}$$

$$130.62 - (-70.62) = \frac{380}{N}$$

$$201.24 = \frac{380}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for N:**

$$\boxed{N = \frac{380}{201.24} = 1.888}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on safety (2 marks for engineering judgement):**
Since **N ≈ 1.89** and the conventional minimum factor of safety for **automotive connecting rods** under static loading is **N ≥ 1.5 to 2.0**, the design is **marginally acceptable** but not very safe. If dynamic/fatigue loading is involved, a higher N (≥ 2.5) is recommended.

---

#### **Question B-B.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Internal Choice Pattern]
A **drive shaft** of an automobile transmits a torque of **T = 1.5 kN·m** and is simultaneously subjected to a **bending moment of M = 800 N·m**. The shaft is solid, circular, and made of **medium carbon steel (σ_yt = 320 MPa, σ_uc = 560 MPa)**.

**(a)** If the **shaft diameter is d = 50 mm**, determine the **state of stress** at the critical surface point and the principal stresses using **Guest's Theory**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What should be the **minimum required diameter** of the shaft to achieve a factor of safety of **N = 2.5** based on Guest's Theory? **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute bending stress:**
Section modulus for a solid circular shaft: Z = π d³ / 32.

$$Z = \frac{\pi (50)^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 125000}{32} = 12271.85 \text{ mm}^3$$

Bending stress:
$$\sigma_x = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{800 \times 10^3}{12271.85} = 65.19 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute torsional shear stress:**
Polar section modulus: Z_p = π d³ / 16 = 24543.69 mm³.

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{24543.69} = 61.12 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — State of stress at the critical surface point:**
σ_x = 65.19 MPa, σ_y = 0, τ_xy = 61.12 MPa.

**Step 4 — Compute principal stresses:**

$$C = \frac{65.19 + 0}{2} = 32.595 \text{ MPa}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(32.595)^2 + (61.12)^2} = \sqrt{1062.44 + 3735.65} = \sqrt{4798.09} = 69.27 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 32.595 + 69.27 = 101.87 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 32.595 - 69.27 = -36.68 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Reorder (plane stress, σ₃ = 0):**
σ₁ = 101.87 MPa, σ₂ = 0, σ₃ = −36.68 MPa.

**Step 6 — Apply Guest's equation for FOS:**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}$$

$$101.87 - (-36.68) = \frac{320}{N}$$

$$138.55 = \frac{320}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{N = \frac{320}{138.55} = 2.31}$$

---

**Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Express stresses as functions of diameter d:**

Bending stress:
$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 800 \times 10^3}{\pi d^3} = \frac{8.149 \times 10^6}{d^3} \text{ MPa}$$

Torsional shear stress:
$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} = \frac{7.639 \times 10^6}{d^3} \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Guest's equation:**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}$$

For combined bending + torsion, it can be shown that:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for d³:**

$$d^3 = \frac{16 N}{\pi \sigma_{yt}}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 2.5}{\pi \times 320}\sqrt{(800)^2 + (1500)^2} \text{ (units: N·mm)}$$

$$\sqrt{(800)^2 + (1500)^2} = \sqrt{640000 + 2250000} = \sqrt{2890000} = 1700 \text{ N·m}$$

Converting: M, T must be in N·mm. M = 800,000 N·mm, T = 1,500,000 N·mm.

$$\sqrt{(800000)^2 + (1500000)^2} = 1.7 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 2.5 \times 1.7 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 320} = \frac{6.8 \times 10^7}{1005.31} = 67641.1 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{d = \sqrt[3]{67641.1} = 40.74 \text{ mm}}$$

Therefore, a **standard diameter of 42 mm** (next commercial size up) should be selected.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Guest's Theory (Module 1):**

- **Core Principle:** Failure occurs when **maximum shear stress** in the component equals the **shear yield stress** of a uniaxial tensile specimen.

- **Master Formula:** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{N}$

- **Alternate Form (easier to recall):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{2N}$

- **Critical Sequence in Every Problem:**
  1. Convert all stresses to **MPa (N/mm²)**.
  2. Compute **in-plane principal stresses** $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$
  3. For plane stress, set **σ₃ = 0**, then **reorder** all three: σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃.
  4. Compute **τmax = (σ₁ − σ₃) / 2**.
  5. Equate with σyt / (2N) and solve.

- **Always** sort the principal stresses — σ₂ does **NOT** appear in Guest's formula; using σ₁ − σ₂ is a common mistake.

- **Plane Stress Assumption:** σ_z = 0 (thin-walled automotive components like chassis rails, brackets).

- **For Pure Torsion:** τ_xy is the only non-zero stress; principal stresses are +τ_xy and −τ_xy; σ₁ − σ₃ = 2τ_xy; hence FOS = σyt / (2τ_xy).

- **For Combined Bending + Torsion:** The key engineering shortcut is $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ — saves derivation time in exams.

- **Suitability:** Use Guest's Theory for **ductile** materials (steel, aluminum, brass); **NEVER** for **brittle** materials (cast iron, concrete, ceramics).

- **Comparison with von Mises (DE Theory):** Guest is **more conservative** (gives lower FOS) by approximately 15 % for proportional loading — if FOS_Guest ≥ required, the design is certainly safe.

- **Common KTU Exam Values to Memorize:**
  - For EN8 / EN9 steel: σ_yt ≈ 380–410 MPa.
  - For Mild Steel (Fe410): σ_yt = 250 MPa.
  - For Al 6061-T6: σ_yt = 240 MPa.

- **Validity Limits:** Only valid when shear yield = tensile yield / 2 (Tresca flow rule). Holds for metals with **no Bauschinger effect** and at **temperatures well below recrystallization**.

- **Pitfalls to Avoid (Loss of Marks):**
  - Forgetting to sort principal stresses.
  - Confusing Guest's formula with Rankine's.
  - Not converting units (Pa vs MPa vs N/mm²).
  - Using σ₁ − σ₂ instead of σ₁ − σ₃.
  - Not specifying principal plane angle θ_p when the question asks for planes on which σ₁ and σ₃ act.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine's Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rankine's Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rankine's Theory (Maximum Principal Stress Theory):** Rankine's Theory states that failure of a brittle material under combined stress occurs when the **maximum principal stress** in a complex two-dimensional stress system reaches the ultimate tensile stress of the material in a simple tension test. It is widely applied in the design of **thin-walled pressure vessels**, particularly the **cylinder block, cylinder head, and fire deck** of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine.

In the context of **Automotive Component Design (PBAUT604, Module 1)**, Rankine's Theory is primarily used to derive the empirical relationship between the cylinder bore ($D$), the wall thickness ($t$), and the gas pressure ($P$) acting on the inner walls of an IC engine cylinder.

### 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following parameters are essential when applying Rankine's Theory to IC engine design:

- **Gas Pressure ($P$):** typically **20 to 60 bar** for petrol engines and **60 to 100 bar** for diesel engines (combustion pressures)
- **Allowable / Working Tensile Stress ($\sigma_t$):** ranges from **35 N/mm² to 80 N/mm²** for grey cast iron
- **Factor of Safety ($FOS$):** typically **5 to 8** for cylinder walls
- **Bore-to-Stroke Ratio ($D/L$):** approximately **1.0 to 0.8** for modern automotive engines

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a tin can of cold drink:** When you press the top of the can, the walls bulge outward. This outward bulging force is called **hoop stress** (circumferential stress). At the same time, the can also tries to stretch lengthwise — this is **longitudinal stress**. Now, Rankine's Theory tells us: *"Don't just look at the biggest stress — look at the LARGEST PRINCIPAL STRESS (the worst-case tensile stretching) and make sure it never exceeds what the material can safely handle in a simple tension test."*

In a real IC engine, the cylinder experiences **two simultaneous stresses** during combustion:
1. **Hoop stress ($\sigma_h$)** — tries to split the cylinder along its length (like cutting a watermelon)
2. **Longitudinal stress ($\sigma_l$)** — tries to pull the cylinder apart lengthwise (like stretching a sausage)

Rankine says: combine both intelligently to find the worst principal stress and design thickness accordingly.

### 1.4 Why Rankine's Theory in IC Engine Design?

In automotive component design, engine components like the cylinder liner, cylinder head, and crankcase operate under **combined static and dynamic stresses** due to combustion pressure. Since grey cast iron (the most common cylinder material) behaves as a **brittle material** with **negligible plastic deformation**, Rankine's Theory — being a **brittle fracture theory** — is the most appropriate first-principle analysis. It is the **fundamental theory taught in Module 1** of PBAUT604.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Thin Cylinder Stress Analysis

When an IC engine cylinder is subjected to internal gas pressure $P$:

- The cylinder wall is assumed to be **thin** such that $\frac{t}{D} \leq \frac{1}{20}$ (most modern automotive cylinders qualify)
- The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and brittle** (typical of cast iron)
- Stress is uniformly distributed across the wall thickness

#### Step-by-Step Concept Breakdown:

1. **Identify the loading:** Internal combustion pressure $P$ acts radially outward on the inner cylindrical surface.
2. **Compute Hoop Stress:** A section of the cylinder experiences tangential (circumferential) tension. By force equilibrium on a half-cylinder element:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2t}$$

3. **Compute Longitudinal Stress:** Considering axial force equilibrium along the cylinder axis:

$$\sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D}{4t}$$

4. **Identify Principal Stresses:** In a thin-walled cylinder, the **radial stress** is negligible ($\sigma_r \approx 0$ at the outer wall). Therefore:
   - $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = \frac{PD}{2t}$ (maximum principal stress)
   - $\sigma_2 = \sigma_l = \frac{PD}{4t}$ (minimum principal stress)
   - $\sigma_3 = 0$ (radial direction)

5. **Apply Rankine's Criterion:** Failure occurs when $\sigma_1$ equals the ultimate tensile strength divided by the factor of safety. This yields the **Rankine empirical thickness formula**:

$$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2\sigma_t - 1.2P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The term $1.2P$ in the denominator arises because the maximum principal stress combines both hoop and longitudinal contributions through the brittle fracture criterion. Always remember that $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$ (the hoop stress is the governing principal stress).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Application in Automotive Design** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hoop Stress ($\sigma_h$) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | Tangential stress in cylinder wall |
| Longitudinal Stress ($\sigma_l$) | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4t}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | Axial stress along cylinder axis |
| Maximum Principal Stress ($\sigma_1$) | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | Rankine's governing stress |
| Rankine Thickness ($t$) | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2\sigma_t - 1.2P}$ | mm | Cylinder wall thickness design |
| Lame's Thickness ($t_L$) | $t_L = \dfrac{D}{2}\left[\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_t + 0.4P}{\sigma_t - 1.2P}} - 1\right]$ | mm | More accurate (used as benchmark) |
| Clavarino's Thickness ($t_C$) | $t_C = \dfrac{D}{2}\left[\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_t - 1.3P}} - 1\right]$ | mm | Modification of Lame's |
| Birnie's Thickness ($t_B$) | $t_B = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2.3\sigma_t}$ | mm | Simplified empirical form |
| Working Stress ($\sigma_t$) | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{FOS}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | Allowable tensile stress |
| Cylinder Head Thickness ($t_h$) | $t_h = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.3 \cdot P \cdot D^2}{\sigma_t}}$ | mm | Flat cylinder head design |
| Fire Deck Thickness | $\approx 1.25 \times t$ (wall) | mm | Additional fire-side thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is used by virtually every automotive OEM (Tata Motors, Maruti, Hyundai, Toyota) during the preliminary design of cylinder blocks. It provides a **first-approximation thickness** before FEA (Finite Element Analysis) validation. For a typical 1500 cc petrol engine with $D = 76$ mm and $P = 45$ bar, $t$ works out to approximately **5 mm**.

### 2.3 Real-World Automotive Application
