**Step 1 — Add the steady torsional contribution as a mean shear stress $\tau_m = 40$ MPa.**

For a **bending-dominant** loading under Von Mises, the equivalent normal mean stress is:

$$\sigma_{m,eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_m^2 + 3\tau_m^2} = \sqrt{(100)^2 + 3 \times (40)^2} = \sqrt{10000 + 4800} = \sqrt{14800} = 121.66 \text{ MPa}$$

The equivalent alternating stress is unchanged (steady torque has no alternating component):

$$\sigma_{a,eq} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Goodman on equivalent stresses:**

$$\frac{1}{N} = \frac{\sigma_{a,eq}}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_{m,eq}}{\sigma_{ut}}$$

$$\frac{1}{N} = \frac{150}{280} + \frac{121.66}{600} = 0.5357 + 0.2028 = 0.7385$$

$$N = \frac{1}{0.7385} = 1.354$$

**Answer:** With the steady torque, the factor of safety reduces to **$N \approx 1.35$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The Von Mises combination multiplies the shear term by **3** (not 4, not 1). Writing $4\tau^2$ instead of $3\tau^2$ is a common KTU marking trap. Recall that this 3 comes from $3\tau^2 = (\sqrt{3}\tau)^2$ where $\sqrt{3}$ is the conversion factor from shear to equivalent uniaxial stress.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Exams on "Design Based on Strength":**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Using kN·m for torque and mm for diameter without converting to N·mm. The shaft formula demands N·mm throughout.
> 2. **Confusing Soderberg and Goodman:** Soderberg uses $\sigma_y$ in the denominator of the mean term; Goodman uses $\sigma_{ut}$. Many students interchange them.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of safety $N$ in the shaft diameter formula:** Writing $d^3 = (16/\pi\sigma_y)\sqrt{4M^2+3T^2}$ without the $N$ — a 3-mark penalty.
> 4. **Not reading the failure theory specified:** The question stem almost always says "Von Mises" or "Maximum Shear". Students who use the wrong theory lose 4+ marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to take cube root and standardise:** Writing $d^3$ as the final answer instead of $d = 53.55$ mm and rounding to 54 mm.
> 6. **Mixing up $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$:** Not distinguishing fully-reversed ($R = -1$) from pulsating ($R = 0$) loading.
> 7. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ in Von Mises combination:** For a $K_t$ chart, forgetting to multiply by 1.73 (≈ $\sqrt{3}$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Design Based on Strength":**

- **Design Philosophy:** Size the part so that $\sigma_{induced} \le \sigma_{allowable} = \sigma_{strength} / N$.
- **Factor of Safety (N):** Empirical number that absorbs uncertainty in loads, materials, and environment. Common KTU values: $N = 2$ (well-known loads), $N = 3$ (less certain), $N = 4$ (public safety).
- **Failure Theories:**
  * **Ductile** → Von Mises (most accurate) or Tresca (conservative).
  * **Brittle** → Rankine (simple) or Coulomb-Mohr (refined).
- **Shaft Diameter Formula (DE/Von Mises):**

$$d = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 N}{\pi \sigma_y}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}}$$

- **Shaft Diameter Formula (ASME/Tresca):**

$$d = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 N}{\pi \sigma_y}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}$$

- **Stress Amplitude and Mean Stress:**

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}, \quad \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

- **Three Mean-Stress Correction Criteria:**
  * **Soderberg** (most conservative, line) → uses $\sigma_y$.
  * **Goodman** (industry standard, line) → uses $\sigma_{ut}$.
  * **Gerber** (least conservative, parabola) → uses $\sigma_{ut}$.
- **Safety Factor Equations:**

$$N_{Soderberg} = \left[\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}\right]^{-1}, \quad N_{Goodman} = \left[\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right]^{-1}$$

- **Empirical Endurance Limit:** $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \sigma_{ut}$ for steels only.
- **Marin's Modified Endurance Limit:** $\sigma_e' = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \sigma_e$ — multi-factor correction.
- **Stress Concentration:** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$; remember to use $K_f$ in fatigue and $K_t$ in static analysis.
- **Endurance Limit for Variable Loading:** Combine $K_f$ and Marin factors **before** applying Soderberg/Goodman.
- **KTU Number-1 Mistake:** Writing N in the wrong position of the equation or omitting it altogether. Always include $N$ in shaft design.
- **KTU Number-2 Mistake:** Using $\sigma_y$ in the Goodman equation (should be $\sigma_{ut}$). Always match the formula to the criterion.

> **End of Topic: Design Based on Strength | Module 1, PBAUT604**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rigidity and Critical Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rigidity and Critical Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Rigidity of Shafts (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rigidity** is defined as the ability of a machine component (specifically a shaft) to resist deformation (bending deflection and angular twist) under the action of external loads, **transmitted torques**, and **self-weight** of mounted components (gears, pulleys, rotors).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, rigidity is treated as a **serviceability criterion** — distinct from strength (which prevents yielding/failure). A shaft may be strong enough to carry the load yet too flexible, causing:
- Misalignment of gears, bearings, and couplings
- Excessive vibration and noise
- Premature failure of bearings and seals

**Permissible Rigidity Limits (KTU Design Data Handbook):**
- **Linear deflection (y):** $y \le 0.01 \text{ mm per metre length of shaft}$
- **Slope of deflection (dy/dx):** $\frac{dy}{dx} \le 0.001 \text{ radian}$
- **Angular twist (θ):** $\theta \le 0.25^\circ \text{ per metre length of shaft}$

### 1.2 Critical Speed (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical speed (Nc)** is the rotational speed of a shaft at which the lateral (transverse) vibration frequency of the shaft coincides with the **forcing frequency** due to unbalance of the rotating masses, leading to resonance and a theoretically infinite amplitude of whirling.

When the operating speed approaches the critical speed, the shaft undergoes **whirling** — a violent lateral vibration in which the shaft bows out into a whirling orbit, potentially causing catastrophic failure.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \text{ RPM}$$

where $\delta_{st}$ is the **static deflection** (in metres) of the shaft at the centre of gravity of the rotating mass.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 – Rigidity (The Diving Board):** Imagine a thin diving board (flexible) versus a thick concrete plank (rigid). Both can support a diver's weight without breaking, but the diving board bends visibly. A machine shaft behaves the same way — it must be **stiff enough** so that gears and pulleys stay aligned.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 – Critical Speed (The Push on a Swing):** A child on a swing will swing higher if you push at exactly the right rhythm. Push at the wrong rhythm and the swing barely moves. The "right rhythm" is the natural frequency. **Critical speed is the engine RPM that matches the shaft's natural swaying frequency** — pushing at this speed is what causes the dangerous "whirling."

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** = **9810 mm/s²** |
| $\pi$ | $\pi$ | **3.14159** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rigidity — Theoretical Background

A shaft loaded by transverse forces (e.g., weight of a gear, belt tension) deflects. The slope of the elastic curve is:

$$\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{M(x)}{EI}$$

where:
- $M(x)$ = Bending moment at section $x$
- $E$ = Young's modulus of shaft material (**210 GPa** for steel)
- $I$ = Second moment of area of shaft cross-section, $I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$ (solid shaft)

**Why it matters in automobiles:** Propeller shafts, transmission shafts, and half shafts in passenger cars and trucks must be rigid enough to keep driveline angles within narrow limits — otherwise vibrations reach the cabin and U-joints wear out prematurely.

### 2.2 Torsional Rigidity

The angle of twist over length $L$ under torque $T$ is:

$$\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ}$$

where:
- $G$ = Modulus of rigidity (**80 GPa** for steel)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia, $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ (solid shaft)

### 2.3 Critical Speed — Underlying Theory

When a shaft of mass $m$ with a rotor of mass $W/g$ mounted at its centre is rotated:
- Centrifugal force on the whirling shaft: $F_c = m \omega^2 y$
- Restoring force from shaft elasticity: $F_r = k \cdot y$ (where $k$ is shaft stiffness)

At equilibrium during whirling: $m \omega^2 y = k y$

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{k}{m} = \frac{g}{\delta_{st}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The static deflection $\delta_{st}$ is the deflection produced when the rotor weight $W$ is applied **statically** at the centre of the shaft, given by $\delta_{st} = W/k$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Critical angular speed | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Critical speed (RPM) | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | RPM |
| 3 | Critical speed (δ in mm) | $N_c = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{st}}}$ | RPM, $\delta$ in mm |
| 4 | Rayleigh's method (multiple rotors) | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g\sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}}$ | $y_i$ in metres |
| 5 | Dunkerley's method | $\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \dfrac{1}{N_{c1}^2} + \dfrac{1}{N_{c2}^2} + \dfrac{1}{N_{c3}^2} + \dots$ | Combined effect |
| 6 | Rigidity — Slope criterion | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \le 0.001 \text{ rad}$ | KTU design data |
| 7 | Rigidity — Deflection criterion | $y \le 0.01 \text{ mm/m}$ | KTU design data |
| 8 | Torsional twist criterion | $\theta \le 0.25^\circ/\text{m}$ | KTU design data |
| 9 | Shaft stiffness (point load at centre) | $k = \dfrac{48EI}{L^3}$ | For simply-supported shaft |
| 10 | Deflection (point load $W$ at centre) | $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$ | metres |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rayleigh's method gives an UPPER bound** of the true critical speed, while **Dunkerley's method gives a LOWER bound**. The true critical speed lies between the two. In KTU examinations, the **lower bound (Dunkerley)** is preferred for design safety.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive propeller shafts** in trucks operate at 1500–3000 RPM — must be designed well below critical speed.
- **Turbocharger shafts** spin at 100,000+ RPM with very small diameters — multi-stage critical speed analysis essential.
- **Crankshafts** of engines undergo torsional vibration — proximity to critical speeds is monitored.
- **Aerospace rotorcraft drive shafts** use Dunkerley's method to account for multiple gear/pulley stations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Critical Speed Formula (Simple Shaft with Single Rotor)

**Step 1 — System Setup**

Consider a uniform simply-supported shaft of length $L$ carrying a rotor of mass $m$ (weight $W = mg$) at its mid-span. Let $y$ be the lateral deflection of the shaft centre during rotation.

**Step 2 — Static Deflection**

When $W$ is applied statically at the centre of a simply-supported shaft, the deflection is:

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{WL^3}{48EI} \quad \text{(standard strength of materials result)}$$

This gives the shaft stiffness:

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta_{st}} = \frac{48EI}{L^3}$$

**Step 3 — Forces During Whirling**

When the shaft rotates at angular speed $\omega$, the rotor experiences a centrifugal force acting outward, proportional to the whirling radius $y$:

$$F_c = m \omega^2 y = \frac{W}{g} \omega^2 y$$

The shaft exerts an inward elastic restoring force (Hooke's law in bending):

$$F_r = k \cdot y = \frac{W}{\delta_{st}} \cdot y$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium at Critical Speed**

At critical speed, the shaft whirls with a constant whirling radius — i.e., centrifugal and restoring forces balance:

$$\frac{W}{g} \omega_c^2 y = \frac{W}{\delta_{st}} y$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Critical Angular Speed**

Cancelling $W$ and $y$ from both sides:

$$\frac{\omega_c^2}{g} = \frac{1}{\delta_{st}}$$

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{g}{\delta_{st}}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to RPM**

Using $N_c = \dfrac{60 \, \omega_c}{2\pi}$:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

**Step 7 — Special Form for δ in mm**

Substituting $g = 9810 \text{ mm/s}^2$ and $\delta$ in mm:

$$N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9810}{\delta}} = 9.5493 \times \frac{99.05}{\sqrt{\delta}} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{st}(\text{in mm})}} \text{ RPM}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Rayleigh's Formula (Energy Method)

**Step 1 — Energy Balance**

At critical speed, the rotating shaft is in a state of steady whirling. Apply the principle of conservation of energy:
- Work done by centrifugal forces = Strain energy stored in bending

**Step 2 — Work Done by Gravity-Equivalent Centrifugal Forces**

For a system with multiple rotors $W_i$ at deflections $y_i$, the work done by the effective gravity-like force during deflection is:

$$U_{work} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i y_i$$

**Step 3 — Strain Energy in Bending**

The strain energy stored in the shaft under the deflected shape is equal to the work done by the equivalent static loads at each rotor station:

$$U_{strain} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i y_i$$

**Step 4 — Apply Equivalence with Centrifugal Effect**

For whirling, replace $g$ by $\omega_c^2 y_i$ at each station. The effective work is:

$$U_{work,whirl} = \frac{1}{2}\sum W_i \cdot \omega_c^2 y_i \cdot y_i = \frac{\omega_c^2}{2}\sum W_i y_i^2$$

**Step 5 — Equate Energies**

$$\frac{\omega_c^2}{2}\sum W_i y_i^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum W_i y_i$$

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{\sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2} \cdot g$$

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g \sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}} \text{ rad/s}}$$

In RPM form:

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g \sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}} \text{ RPM}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Dunkerley's Equation

**Step 1 — Concept**

Dunkerley treats each rotor as if it were the only source of unbalance, finds the corresponding critical speed $N_{ci}$, and then combines them.

**Step 2 — Effect of Single Rotor**

If rotor $i$ alone is present, the critical speed satisfies:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_{ci}^2} = \frac{y_i \, m_i}{g \, m_i} = \frac{y_i}{g}$$

So $\omega_{ci}^2 = \dfrac{g}{y_i}$ (deflection at point $i$ caused by weight $W_i$ at point $i$).

**Step 3 — Effect of All Rotors Combined**

For all rotors acting together, the deflection at point $i$ is (by superposition) the sum of deflections from each rotor:

$$y_i^{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} W_j$$

where $a_{ij}$ is the flexibility coefficient (deflection at $i$ per unit load at $j$).

**Step 4 — Substitute and Sum**

The combined critical speed condition gives:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{W_i y_i^{total}}{g \sum_j W_j y_j}$$

Carrying through the algebra with flexibility coefficients $a_{ii}$ (assuming $a_{ij}$ cross terms contribute via individual $N_{ci}$'s):

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c2}^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{\omega_{cn}^2}$$

In RPM:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{N_{ci}^2}}$$

This is **Dunkerley's empirical lower-bound formula**, widely used in KTU examinations for shafts with multiple rotors.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Worked Problem)

**Problem:** A simply-supported steel shaft of length $L = 1.2$ m and diameter $d = 50$ mm carries a rotor of weight $W = 500$ N at its centre. $E = 210$ GPa. Calculate the critical speed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moment of Inertia**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^4}{64} = 3.068 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Static Deflection**

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{WL^3}{48EI} = \frac{500 \times (1.2)^3}{48 \times 210 \times 10^9 \times 3.068 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{500 \times 1.728}{48 \times 210 \times 10^9 \times 3.068 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{864}{3.092 \times 10^6} = 2.794 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\delta_{st} = 0.2794 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Critical Speed**

$$N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{0.2794}} = \frac{946}{0.5286} = 1789.6 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{N_c \approx 1790 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Step 4 — Validity Check**

Operating RPM should be kept at $N < 0.8 \, N_c$ or $N > 1.2 \, N_c$ (subcritical or supercritical operation).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Shaft with Rotor — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Simply Supported Shaft\nLength L, Diameter d] --> B[Elastic Restoring Force\nF = k × y]
    C[Rotor of Mass m\nWeight W at Centre] --> D[Centrifugal Force\nF = m × omega squared × y]
    D --> E{Equilibrium at Whirling}
    B --> E
    E -->|Balanced| F[Critical Speed Nc\n= 946 / sqrt delta mm]
    E -->|Unbalanced| G[Resonance / Whirling\nFailure Mode]
    F --> H[Operating Speed N\nMust lie in 0 to 0.8 Nc]
    H --> I{Design Check}
    I -->|N < 0.8 Nc| J[SAFE - Subcritical Operation]
    I -->|0.8 Nc to 1.2 Nc| K[UNSAFE - Avoid Resonance]
    I -->|N > 1.2 Nc| L[SAFE - Supercritical Operation]
```

### 4.2 Multi-Rotor Shaft Analysis Methodology (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Multi-Rotor Shaft Data\n- Number of rotors\n- Weights Wi\n- Positions xi\n- Shaft EI and L] --> A1[Step 1: Compute Static\nDeflection yi at each\nrotor station]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Apply Rayleigh Method\nNc_Rayleigh = 30 by pi times\nsqrt g sum Wi yi by sum Wi yi squared]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Compute Nc_i for each\nrotor acting alone]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Apply Dunkerley Method\n1 by Nc squared = sum 1 by Nc_i squared]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: True Critical Speed\nlies between Dunkerley and Rayleigh]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Compare with\nOperating RPM]
    A6 --> A7{Design Safe?}
    A7 -->|Yes| A8[Accept Design]
    A7 -->|No| A9[Increase Diameter d\nor Change Material\nor Add Bearing]
    A9 --> A1
```

### 4.3 Rigid vs Flexible Shaft — Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Calculate y and theta] --> Q2{y less than 0.01 mm per m?\nAND slope less than 0.001 rad?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Check Torsional Twist\ntheta less than 0.25 deg per m]
    R1 -->|Yes| R2[Shaft is RIGID\nDesign Acceptable]
    R1 -->|No| R3[Increase Diameter\nfor Torsional Rigidity]
    Q2 -->|No| R4[Shaft LACKS Rigidity\nIncrease d or use Stiffer Material]
    R3 --> S1[Re-check Critical Speed\nNc = 946 / sqrt delta]
    R4 --> S1
    S1 --> S2{Nc greater than 1.2 times N?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[DESIGN ACCEPTED]
    S2 -->|No| S4[REDESIGN: Larger d,\nShorter L, or Stiffer E]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the term "critical speed" of a shaft. What is whirling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Critical speed of a shaft is the speed at which the shaft tends to vibrate violently in the transverse (lateral) direction due to resonance, i.e., when the natural frequency of lateral vibration of the shaft coincides with the frequency of the disturbing force caused by the eccentricity of the rotating mass mounted on the shaft. Mathematically, $N_c = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{st}}}$ RPM, where $\delta_{st}$ is the static deflection in mm.

Whirling is the phenomenon of a shaft bowing out transversely and rotating about its geometric axis while describing a whirling orbit. The shaft appears to "whirl" like a jump rope, with the whirling radius becoming dangerously large at critical speed, leading to possible seizure or fracture.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. List any three permissible limits of rigidity used in shaft design as per KTU data handbook.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Linear deflection:** $y \le 0.01$ mm per metre length of shaft.
2. **Slope of the deflected shaft:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \le 0.001$ radian.
3. **Angular twist (torsional rigidity):** $\theta \le 0.25^\circ$ per metre length of shaft.

These limits ensure that the shaft does not deflect excessively, maintains alignment of gears and pulleys, and avoids premature bearing failure.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the critical speed of a shaft carrying a single rotor at its centre. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transmission shaft of length $L = 1.5$ m, diameter $d = 60$ mm, simply supported, carries a gear of weight $1200$ N at its mid-span. Take $E = 210$ GPa. Find the critical speed. Is the design safe if the operating speed is 1500 RPM? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Critical Speed (7 marks):**

> Full step-by-step derivation as in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Assumptions:**
1. The shaft is uniform, homogeneous, and isotropic.
2. The rotor mass is concentrated at the centre of the shaft.
3. Damping and internal friction in the shaft material are neglected.
4. The shaft is simply supported (deflection formula $\delta = WL^3/48EI$ applies).
5. The whirling is steady-state with constant whirling radius $y$.

**Final Formula:**
$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{st}(\text{mm})}} \text{ RPM}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Moment of Inertia**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.060)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{64} = 6.362 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Static Deflection**

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{WL^3}{48EI} = \frac{1200 \times (1.5)^3}{48 \times 210 \times 10^9 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{1200 \times 3.375}{6.413 \times 10^6} = \frac{4050}{6.413 \times 10^6} = 6.316 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.6316 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Critical Speed**

$$N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{0.6316}} = \frac{946}{0.7947} = 1190.4 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Safety Check**

- Operating speed $N = 1500$ RPM
- $1.2 \times N_c = 1.2 \times 1190.4 = 1428.5$ RPM
- Since $N = 1500 > 1.2 N_c = 1428.5$ RPM → **Supercritical operation**

> [!NOTE]
> Although 1500 RPM > 1.2 Nc, the margin is only $\sim 5\%$. The operating speed is in the supercritical range, but a typical design rule requires a safety margin of at least 20%, so the design is **marginally unsafe**. Recommended: increase shaft diameter to bring Nc well above 1500 RPM, OR add an intermediate bearing to reduce L.

**Required Nc for 20% margin:** $N_c \ge \dfrac{1500}{1.2} = 1250$ RPM. Current Nc = 1190 RPM — slightly below. Recommended to increase $d$ to ~62 mm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain Dunkerley's method for finding the critical speed of a shaft carrying multiple rotors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft carries three rotors at positions such that when each is loaded alone, the static deflections at their respective stations are $0.5$ mm, $0.8$ mm, and $0.4$ mm. The corresponding weights are $W_1 = 200$ N, $W_2 = 300$ N, and $W_3 = 150$ N. Find the critical speed of the shaft using (i) Rayleigh's method, and (ii) Dunkerley's method. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Dunkerley's Method (7 marks):**

Refer to the **flow diagram in Section 4.2** and the derivation in **Section 3.3** of these notes.

**Key points for full marks:**
- Statement: For a shaft with multiple rotors, the combined critical speed is lower than that computed for any single rotor.
- Formula: $\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \dfrac{1}{N_{c1}^2} + \dfrac{1}{N_{c2}^2} + \dfrac{1}{N_{c3}^2} + \dots$
- Each $N_{ci}$ is the critical speed computed assuming only the $i^{\text{th}}$ rotor is present.
- Dunkerley's method gives a **lower bound** to the true critical speed → used for safe design.
- Assumption: The whirling of the shaft in any one normal mode may be approximately treated as if it were the only mode present, with the rest treated as additional static loads.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Individual Critical Speeds**

For each rotor acting alone, the critical speed is:

$$N_{ci} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_i \text{ (mm)}}}$$

- $N_{c1} = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{0.5}} = \dfrac{946}{0.7071} = 1337.8$ RPM
- $N_{c2} = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{0.8}} = \dfrac{946}{0.8944} = 1057.7$ RPM
- $N_{c3} = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{0.4}} = \dfrac{946}{0.6325} = 1495.8$ RPM

**Step 2 — Dunkerley's Critical Speed**

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \frac{1}{1337.8^2} + \frac{1}{1057.7^2} + \frac{1}{1495.8^2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{1{,}789{,}709} + \frac{1}{1{,}118{,}729} + \frac{1}{2{,}237{,}418}$$

$$= 5.587 \times 10^{-7} + 8.939 \times 10^{-7} + 4.469 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$= 1.8995 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$N_c^2 = 5.265 \times 10^5$$

$$\boxed{N_c^{Dunk} = 725.6 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Step 3 — Rayleigh's Critical Speed**

The deflections at the rotor stations when all weights are present must be computed (or assumed as given; for KTU problems, deflections are given as the static deflections at each station under the combined loading).

Using $y_1 = 0.5$ mm, $y_2 = 0.8$ mm, $y_3 = 0.4$ mm:

**Numerator** ($\sum W_i y_i$):

$$\sum W_i y_i = (200 \times 0.5) + (300 \times 0.8) + (150 \times 0.4)$$
$$= 100 + 240 + 60 = 400 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Denominator** ($\sum W_i y_i^2$):

$$\sum W_i y_i^2 = (200 \times 0.25) + (300 \times 0.64) + (150 \times 0.16)$$
$$= 50 + 192 + 24 = 266 \text{ N·mm}^2$$

**Critical speed (Rayleigh):**

$$N_c = 946 \sqrt{\frac{\sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}} = 946 \sqrt{\frac{400}{266}}$$

$$= 946 \sqrt{1.5038} = 946 \times 1.2263$$

$$\boxed{N_c^{Ray} = 1160.1 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Step 4 — True Critical Speed Range**

$$725.6 \text{ RPM} \le N_c^{true} \le 1160.1 \text{ RPM}$$

For safe design, use $N_c = 725.6$ RPM (Dunkerley lower bound).

> Operating speed must satisfy: $N \le 0.8 \times 725.6 = 580.5$ RPM (subcritical) **OR** $N \ge 1.2 \times 725.6 = 870.7$ RPM (supercritical).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Most students use $g = 9.81$ m/s² but keep $\delta$ in mm, leading to a wildly wrong answer. Always convert $\delta$ to **metres** when using $g = 9.81$ m/s², OR use the special formula $N_c = 946/\sqrt{\delta(\text{mm})}$.
> 2. **Dunkerley vs Rayleigh confusion:** Students often invert the formula in Dunkerley's method. Remember: $\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{N_{ci}^2}$ — it is the **inverse squares that add**, not the RPMs themselves.
> 3. **Mixing up $L^3$ and $L^4$:** The static deflection formula is $WL^3/(48EI)$, not $WL^4$. The $d^4$ appears in $I$, not in the load term.
> 4. **Forgetting to comment on design safety:** A numerical answer without stating "Safe / Unsafe" loses at least 1 mark. Always compare with $0.8 N_c$ and $1.2 N_c$.
> 5. **Using the wrong safety criterion:** KTU accepts $N < 0.8 N_c$ (subcritical) **OR** $N > 1.2 N_c$ (supercritical). Marks are lost when students say "always operate below critical speed" — supercritical operation is also valid for certain machinery (e.g., turbochargers).

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Rigidity** = stiffness criterion; distinct from strength. KTU limits: $y \le 0.01$ mm/m, slope $\le 0.001$ rad, twist $\le 0.25°$/m.
- **Critical speed** $N_c$ is the speed of **resonance** leading to whirling failure.
- Master formula: $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$; in RPM: $N_c = 946/\sqrt{\delta_{st}(\text{mm})}$.
- **Operating Rule:** Keep $N < 0.8 N_c$ (subcritical) **OR** $N > 1.2 N_c$ (supercritical). **Never** between $0.8 N_c$ and $1.2 N_c$.
- **Rayleigh's method** → **Upper bound** of $N_c$; uses combined static deflections $y_i$ and weights $W_i$ at each rotor.
- **Dunkerley's method** → **Lower bound** of $N_c$; uses individual rotor critical speeds: $\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{N_{ci}^2}$.
- True $N_c$ lies between Dunkerley and Rayleigh values.
- For shafts with **multiple rotors**, always apply **Dunkerley** for safety-critical designs.
- The static deflection for a centre-loaded simply-supported shaft: $\delta_{st} = WL^3/(48EI)$.
- $I = \pi d^4/64$ (solid circular shaft); $J = \pi d^4/32$.
- Material constants: $E = 210$ GPa, $G = 80$ GPa for steel.
- **Units discipline:** Keep $W$ in N, $L$ in m, $I$ in m⁴, $E$ in Pa → $\delta$ in m. Or use the 946/√δ shortcut with δ in mm.
- Always provide a **design safety statement** in your KTU answer.
- Auto applications: propeller shafts, transmission shafts, half-shafts, turbocharger shafts, crankshafts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Static and Fatigue Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design for Static and Fatigue Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Design for Static Loads — Formal Definition

**Static Load Design** is the engineering methodology of proportioning a machine component such that the **maximum induced stress** under steady (time-invariant) or slowly applied loading remains within the **allowable stress** derived from the material's yield or ultimate strength, multiplied by an appropriate **Factor of Safety (FoS)**. In the KTU 2024 automotive design context, this involves applying **failure theories** (Maximum Normal Stress, Maximum Shear Stress, and **Distortion Energy Theory / Von Mises Criterion**) to translate multi-axial stress states into a single design check.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Static failure occurs when the load-induced stress either exceeds the yield strength (yielding) or reaches the ultimate strength (fracture) of the material under steady loading conditions."*

---

### 1.2 Design for Fatigue Loads — Formal Definition

**Fatigue Design** is the systematic procedure of ensuring a component can withstand a specified number of stress cycles (often **N = 10⁶ to 10⁸** for automotive components like crankshafts, connecting rods, leaf springs, and axle shafts) without crack initiation or failure, even though the maximum cyclic stress is well below the material's static yield strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Remember:**
> - Standard reference cycle count: **N = 10⁶ cycles** (Steel endurance limit baseline)
> - Aluminum alloys: No true endurance limit; design for **N = 5 × 10⁸** cycles

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Paper Clip:**
> Imagine bending a paper clip slowly once — it may not break (static load). But if you bend it back and forth 20 times, it snaps. This is **fatigue failure**. The stress was never high enough to yield the material in one cycle, yet repeated sub-yield stress cycles progressively damage the crystal lattice until sudden fracture occurs.
>
> **Automotive Example:** A car **leaf spring** experiences ~10⁶ to 10⁸ load cycles per year. Static analysis says it is "safe" at 300 MPa, but without fatigue design, it cracks at the eye-hole within 2 years on Indian roads.

---

### 1.4 Why Both Are Critical in Automotive Design

- **Static loads** dominate in components like **gudgeon pins during combustion peak pressure** and **bolts during pre-tensioning**.
- **Fatigue loads** dominate in **rotating shafts, springs, connecting rods, and gears** because of cyclic stresses from engine firing, road undulations, and torque fluctuations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often design only for static yield and ignore fatigue. In KTU valuation, a fatigue analysis question that ignores the **endurance limit modification (Marin factors)** is considered incomplete and attracts significant mark deductions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Static Failure Theories (Step-by-Step Logic)

When a 2D/3D stress state exists ($\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$, etc.), we cannot directly compare it to the uniaxial yield strength $S_{yt}$. Failure theories provide the bridge.

#### Theory 1: Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's)
- **Why:** Assumes failure occurs when the principal stress reaches the uniaxial strength.
- **How:** Compare $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ (algebraically largest and smallest principal stresses) with $S_{yt}$ or $S_{ut}$.

#### Theory 2: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca's)
- **Why:** Assumes failure when max shear in element reaches max shear in tension test.
- **How:** Compare $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$ with $S_{yt}/2$.

#### Theory 3: Distortion Energy Theory (Von Mises) — *Industry Preferred*
- **Why:** Most accurate for ductile materials; considers only the shape-changing (deviatoric) strain energy.
- **How:** Compute Von Mises stress and compare with $S_y$.

---

### 2.2 Fatigue Loading — Core Building Blocks

**Cyclic Stress Characterization (Fig. 2.1 conceptual):**

A sinusoidal stress cycle is decomposed into:

- **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$):** The static (DC) component
- **Alternating Stress ($\sigma_a$):** The dynamic (AC) amplitude

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

**Key Ratios:**
- **Stress Ratio:** $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$
- **Amplitude Ratio:** $A = \sigma_a / \sigma_m$

**Common Loading Types (KTU favorite exam question):**

| Loading Type | $\sigma_{max}$ | $\sigma_{min}$ | R | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fully Reversed | $\sigma_a$ | $-\sigma_a$ | $-1$ | Rotating shafts |
| Pulsating (Zero to Max) | $\sigma_{max}$ | $0$ | $0$ | Leaf springs |
| Static | $\sigma_m$ | $\sigma_m$ | $+1$ | Bolts, static brackets |

---

### 2.3 S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) & Endurance Limit

The S-N (Stress vs. Number of cycles) curve for **ferrous steels** becomes horizontal beyond **10⁶ cycles**, defining the **Endurance Limit ($S_e'$ or $S_e$)**.

- For **non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu alloys):** No true endurance limit; use **Fatigue Strength at N = 5×10⁸**.
- For **Steels:** $S_e' \approx 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ (for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa).

---

### 2.4 Marin Factors — Modifying the Endurance Limit

Theoretical endurance limit is reduced by real-world factors:

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$

| Factor | Symbol | Accounts For | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Factor | $k_a$ | Surface roughness | $0.5 - 1.0$ |
| Size Factor | $k_b$ | Component size | $0.6 - 1.0$ |
| Load Factor | $k_c$ | Type of loading (axial, bending, torsion) | $0.59 - 1.0$ |
| Temperature Factor | $k_d$ | Operating temperature | $0 - 1.0$ |
| Reliability Factor | $k_e$ | Statistical reliability (90%, 99%, 99.9%) | $0.75 - 1.0$ |
| Miscellaneous Factor | $k_f$ | Residual stresses, corrosion, etc. | $< 1.0$ |

---

### 2.5 Combined Mean and Alternating Stress Failure Criteria

For a component with combined static + cyclic loading, the **high-cycle fatigue** design equations are:

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Classic Fatigue Failure Criteria (KTB-Mandatory):**

**Soderberg Criterion (Most Conservative — Yield + Fatigue Safety):**

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Goodman Criterion (Most Widely Used in Automotive Design):**

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Gerber Criterion (Least Conservative — Parabolic):**

$$\frac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$$

where $n$ = Factor of Safety.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Von Mises Stress (2D) | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | MPa |
| 2 | Von Mises Stress (Principal) | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)^2}{2}}$ | MPa |
| 3 | Factor of Safety (Static) | $n = S_{yt} / \sigma_{VM}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}) / 2$ | MPa |
| 5 | Alternating Stress | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}) / 2$ | MPa |
| 6 | Stress Ratio | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Endurance Limit (Steel) | $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ | MPa |
| 8 | Marin Endurance Limit | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, S_e'$ | MPa |
| 9 | Surface Factor (Shigley) | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Size Factor (Shigley) | $k_b = 0.879 \cdot d^{-0.107}$ (for 2.79 ≤ d ≤ 51 mm) | dimensionless |
| 11 | Soderberg | $\sigma_a / S_e + \sigma_m / S_{yt} = 1/n$ | — |
| 12 | Goodman | $\sigma_a / S_e + \sigma_m / S_{ut} = 1/n$ | — |
| 13 | Gerber | $n\sigma_a / S_e + (n\sigma_m / S_{ut})^2 = 1$ | — |
| 14 | Stress Concentration | $K_t = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_{nom}$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Notch Sensitivity | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Fatigue Stress Conc. | $K_{fs} = 1 + q_{shear}(K_{ts} - 1)$ | dimensionless |
| 17 | Fatigue Factor of Safety (Goodman) | $n_f = \frac{1}{\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}}$ | — |
| 18 | Yield Factor of Safety | $n_y = S_{yt} / (\sigma_a + \sigma_m)$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations form the backbone of crankshaft design (combined bending + torsion, reversed loading), leaf spring design (pulsating tension), and connecting rod design (combined static + cyclic). The **Goodman line** is the most commonly used diagram in Indian automotive industries (TATA, Mahindra, Ashok Leyland) for component approval.

---

### 2.7 Stress Concentration Factors (SCF)

Real components have **fillets, keyways, holes, and shoulders** that cause local stress amplification.

$$\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom}$$

For fatigue design, the **notch sensitivity** ($q$, typically 0 to 1) modifies this:

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) \quad \text{(for normal stress)}$$

$$K_{fs} = 1 + q_{s}(K_{ts} - 1) \quad \text{(for shear stress)}$$

| Material | Notch Sensitivity (q) |
|---|---|
| $\text{S}_{ut} = 550$ MPa | $\approx 0.70$ |
| $\text{S}_{ut} = 1000$ MPa | $\approx 0.87$ |
| $\text{S}_{ut} = 1500$ MPa | $\approx 0.95$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Von Mises Stress (Distortion Energy Theory)

The **Total Strain Energy per unit volume** in a 3D stress state is:

$$U_{total} = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 \right] - \frac{\nu}{E} \left[ \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_1 \sigma_3 \right] + \frac{\tau_{xy}^2}{2G} + ...$$

The **Volumetric (Dilatation) Energy** is associated with uniform volume change and does not cause yielding:

$$U_{vol} = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E} \left[ \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3 \right]^2$$

**Distortion Energy** (responsible for yielding) = Total - Volumetric:

$$U_{dist} = U_{total} - U_{vol}$$

After algebraic simplification (subtracting volumetric part from total), the Von Mises equivalent stress emerges as:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)^2}{2}}$$

**Application:** A component fails (yields) when $\sigma_{VM} = S_{yt}$. The **Factor of Safety** becomes:

$$n = \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_{VM}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Mean and Alternating Stress from a Sinusoidal Wave

A general cyclic stress can be expressed as:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t)$$

At the **maximum** of the cycle ($t = T/4$, where sine = 1):

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$$

At the **minimum** of the cycle ($t = 3T/4$, where sine = -1):

$$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$$

**Adding the two equations:**

$$\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

**Subtracting the two equations:**

$$\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

This fundamental decomposition is the basis of **all fatigue calculations**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Goodman Line Equation

Consider a component with combined mean stress $\sigma_m$ and alternating stress $\sigma_a$. For the **critical failure point**:

- At $\sigma_m = 0$ (fully reversed): $\sigma_a = S_e$ (endurance limit)
- At $\sigma_a = 0$ (pure static): $\sigma_m = S_{ut}$ (ultimate strength)

The **Goodman line** is a straight line connecting these two points on a $\sigma_m$-$\sigma_a$ plot.

Using the **two-point form of a line equation**:

$$\frac{\sigma_a - S_e}{0 - S_e} = \frac{\sigma_m - 0}{S_{ut} - 0}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\frac{\sigma_a - S_e}{-S_e} = \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$$

$$S_{ut}(\sigma_a - S_e) = -S_e \cdot \sigma_m$$

$$S_{ut} \cdot \sigma_a - S_{ut} \cdot S_e = -S_e \cdot \sigma_m$$

Dividing throughout by $S_{ut} \cdot S_e$:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} - 1 = -\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1}$$

For a **Factor of Safety $n$**, all stresses are scaled by $n$:

$$\boxed{\frac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1}$$

Solving for the **Fatigue Factor of Safety**:

$$n_f = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Soderberg Equation (Same Logic, Yield Strength)

Soderberg replaces $S_{ut}$ with $S_{yt}$ for a more conservative (safer) design:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Yield Factor of Safety:**

$$n_y = \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_a + \sigma_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Rule of Thumb:** Always choose $n = \min(n_f, n_y)$ as the governing factor of safety. A safe design satisfies BOTH fatigue and yielding.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style

**Problem:** A steel shaft ($S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_{yt} = 380$ MPa) is subjected to a bending moment that fluctuates between $+2.0$ kN·m and $-0.5$ kN·m. The shaft diameter is 50 mm, surface ground, operating at 25°C with 90% reliability. Determine the factor of safety using the **Goodman criterion**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Bending Stresses**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 M_{max}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 2000 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (50)^3} = 162.97 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{32 M_{min}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times (-500) \times 10^3}{\pi \times (50)^3} = -40.74 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Mean and Alternating Stresses**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{162.97 + (-40.74)}{2} = 61.11 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{162.97 - (-40.74)}{2} = 101.86 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Endurance Limit (Unmodified)**

$$S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 600 = 300 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4: Marin Factors**

For ground surface ($a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$ for $S_{ut}$ in MPa):

$$k_a = 1.58 \times (600)^{-0.085} = 1.58 \times 0.594 = 0.938$$

For $d = 50$ mm (between 2.79 and 51 mm):

$$k_b = 0.879 \times (50)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.738 = 0.649$$

For bending: $k_c = 1.0$, for room temperature: $k_d = 1.0$, for 90% reliability: $k_e = 1.0$.

**Step 5: Modified Endurance Limit**

$$S_e = 0.938 \times 0.649 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 300 = 182.6 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6: Factor of Safety (Goodman)**

$$n_f = \frac{1}{\dfrac{101.86}{182.6} + \dfrac{61.11}{600}} = \frac{1}{0.5578 + 0.1018} = \frac{1}{0.6596} = 1.516$$

$$\boxed{n_f \approx 1.52}$$

The design is **safe** (typical $n_f \ge 1.5$ is acceptable for automotive shafts).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Criterion to Use?

| Criterion | Line Shape | Use When | Safety Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soderberg** | Straight line to $S_{yt}$ | Yielding is critical concern | Most conservative |
| **Goodman** | Straight line to $S_{ut}$ | Brittle materials, general automotive | Moderate-conservative |
| **Gerber** | Parabola through $S_{ut}$ | Ductile materials, less conservative | Least conservative |
| **Yield Check** | $\sigma_a + \sigma_m \le S_{yt}/n$ | First cycle yielding check | Independent check |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Static and Fatigue Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Loading] --> B{Is Load Steady or Cyclic?}
    B -- Steady --> C[Static Analysis]
    B -- Cyclic --> D[Fatigue Analysis]
    C --> C1[Identify Stress State: sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy]
    C1 --> C2[Apply Failure Theory: Von Mises / Tresca]
    C2 --> C3[Compute Factor of Safety n = Syt / sigma_VM]
    C3 --> C4{n >= n_required?}
    C4 -- No --> C5[Resize / Change Material]
    C4 -- Yes --> C6[Static Design Approved]
    D --> D1[Decompose into sigma_m and sigma_a]
    D1 --> D2[Apply Marin Factors: S_e = k_a k_b k_c S_e']
    D2 --> D3[Apply SCF: K_f = 1 + q Kt - 1]
    D3 --> D4[Select Criterion: Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber]
    D4 --> D5[Compute Fatigue FoS n_f]
    D5 --> D6{Also check Yield: n_y >= n_req?}
    D6 -- No --> D7[Resize / Use Better Material]
    D6 -- Yes --> D8[Fatigue Design Approved]
    C5 --> A
    D7 --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Marin Factor Application Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sut Given] --> B[Se Prime equals 0.5 Sut]
    B --> C[Apply ka Surface]
    C --> D[Apply kb Size]
    D --> E[Apply kc Load Type]
    E --> F[Apply kd Temperature]
    F --> G[Apply ke Reliability]
    G --> H[Apply kf Misc]
    H --> I[Modified Se Achieved]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Goodman, Soderberg, Gerber Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combined Stress State] --> B[sigma_m and sigma_a]
    B --> C{Failure Criterion}
    C -- Conservative --> D[Soderberg Line: hits Syt]
    C -- Moderate --> E[Goodman Line: hits Sut]
    C -- Liberal --> F[Gerber Parabola: hits Sut]
    D --> G[Factor of Safety n_f]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Also Verify Yield}
    H --> I[Final n = min n_f, n_y]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Cyclic Stress Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time-Varying Load P t] --> B[Sinusoidal Stress sigma t]
    B --> C[Maximum sigma_max]
    B --> D[Minimum sigma_min]
    C --> E[Mean Stress sigma_m]
    D --> E
    C --> F[Alternating Stress sigma_a]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Static Component]
    F --> H[Cyclic Component]
    G --> I[Goodman / Soderberg Check]
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** These block-level flow diagrams represent the **functional design logic** required by KTU Module 1. The actual S-N curve (Wöhler curve) and Goodman diagram are typically reproduced in exam answer sheets as hand-drawn schematics with proper axes and labelling.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the term "Endurance Limit" and state its significance in fatigue design of automotive components.**

**Model Answer:**
The **endurance limit ($S_e$)** is the maximum completely reversed stress that a material (typically ferrous steel) can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles (≥ 10⁶ cycles)** without failure. Its significance in automotive design is profound — components like **crankshafts, leaf springs, and transmission gears** undergo millions of stress cycles during their service life. Designing the working stress below the endurance limit ensures the component has **infinite life** (i.e., it will not fail due to fatigue), which is critical for vehicle safety and reliability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the Distortion Energy Theory and the Maximum Shear Stress Theory for static failure.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Distortion Energy (Von Mises) | Max Shear Stress (Tresca) |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Basis | Distortion (shape change) energy causes yielding | Max shear stress causes yielding |
| Accuracy for ductile materials | High (within ~15% of experiment) | Conservative (over-designs by 15-20%) |
| Equation | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{0.5[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)^2]}$ | $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$ |
| Industrial Preference | **Widely used in automotive and aerospace** | Used rarely; simple hand calculation |
| Safety | Moderately conservative | Over-conservative |

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Skipping Marin Factors** → 2-3 marks lost. Always show $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \cdot S_e'$.
> - **Confusing Stress Concentration ($K_t$) vs. Fatigue Stress Concentration ($K_f$)** → 1-2 marks lost. $K_t$ is geometric, $K_f$ accounts for material notch sensitivity.
> - **Forgetting to include $K_f$ in the alternating stress** → 1 mark lost. The corrected $\sigma_a = K_f \cdot \sigma_{a,nominal}$.
> - **Wrong choice of failure criterion** for the given material → 1 mark lost. Use **Goodman for ductile steels**, **Soderberg for critical yielding** scenarios.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Fatigue Design

**[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]**

A **drive shaft** of an automobile is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa and $S_{yt} = 490$ MPa. The shaft is subjected to a fluctuating torque that varies from $+1500$ N·m to $+500$ N·m, and a fluctuating bending moment from $+2000$ N·m to $-500$ N·m. The shaft has a **fillet radius of 3 mm** at a shoulder where the diameter changes from 75 mm to 60 mm. The surface is machined, the component is to be designed for **99.9% reliability**, and operates at **50°C**. Use the **Goodman criterion** with a factor of safety of 2.

**Calculate the factor of safety against fatigue failure.**

**Part (a) — Static and Cyclic Stress Decomposition (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Torsional Stresses**
Polar section modulus for the smaller diameter (governing):

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^3}{16} = 4.241 \times 10^4 \, \text{mm}^3$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T_{max}}{Z_p} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{4.241 \times 10^4} = 35.37 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\tau_{min} = \frac{T_{min}}{Z_p} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{4.241 \times 10^4} = 11.79 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\tau_m = \frac{35.37 + 11.79}{2} = 23.58 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\tau_a = \frac{35.37 - 11.79}{2} = 11.79 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Bending Stresses**
Section modulus for bending:

$$Z_b = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^3}{32} = 2.121 \times 10^4 \, \text{mm}^3$$

$$\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z_b} = \frac{2000 \times 10^3}{2.121 \times 10^4} = 94.30 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{b,min} = \frac{-500 \times 10^3}{2.121 \times 10^4} = -23.58 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_m = \frac{94.30 + (-23.58)}{2} = 35.36 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{94.30 - (-23.58)}{2} = 58.94 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Part (b) — Marin Factors and Stress Concentration (7 Marks)**

**Step 3: Endurance Limit (Unmodified)**

$$S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 700 = 350 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4: Marin Factors**

For machined surface ($a = 2.70$, $b = -0.265$ for $S_{ut}$ in kpsi; convert or use $a = 4.51$, $b = -0.265$ for MPa):

$$k_a = 4.51 \times (700)^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1317 = 0.594$$

For $d = 60$ mm (using $k_b = 0.91 d^{-0.157}$ for non-rotating):

$$k_b = 0.91 \times (60)^{-0.157} = 0.91 \times 0.529 = 0.481$$

Loading factor (combined bending + torsion, use $k_c = 0.85$ for torsion, but for combined it's complex; use $k_c = 0.85$ as conservative for torsion dominance):

Actually, for **Von Mises equivalent** combined loading, $k_c = 1.0$ (treated as a single equivalent stress): Let $k_c = 1.0$ for DE-theory-based analysis.

Temperature factor: $k_d = 1.0$ (50°C is near room temp).
Reliability factor for 99.9%: $k_e = 0.75$.
Miscellaneous: $k_f = 1.0$.

**Step 5: Modified Endurance Limit**

$$S_e = 0.594 \times 0.481 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.75 \times 1.0 \times 350 = 75.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6: Stress Concentration**
For $D/d = 75/60 = 1.25$ and $r/d = 3/60 = 0.05$ (bending): $K_t \approx 2.0$ (from charts).
Notch sensitivity for $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa: $q \approx 0.82$.

$$K_f = 1 + 0.82(2.0 - 1) = 1.82$$

**Step 7: Von Mises Equivalent Stresses**

$$\sigma_{a,VM} = \sqrt{(K_f \cdot \sigma_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} \cdot \tau_a)^2} = \sqrt{(1.82 \times 58.94)^2 + 3(1.82 \times 11.79)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(107.27)^2 + 3(21.46)^2} = \sqrt{11506.95 + 1381.79} = \sqrt{12888.74} = 113.53 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{m,VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_m^2 + 3\tau_m^2} = \sqrt{(35.36)^2 + 3(23.58)^2} = \sqrt{1250.33 + 1668.49} = \sqrt{2918.82} = 54.03 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 8: Factor of Safety (Goodman)**

$$n_f = \frac{1}{\dfrac{113.53}{75.0} + \dfrac{54.03}{700}} = \frac{1}{1.5137 + 0.0772} = \frac{1}{1.5909} = 0.629$$

Since $n_f < 1$, the shaft will fail. **The shaft diameter must be increased or a higher-strength material used.**

**Required diameter for $n = 2$:** Iterate; result is approximately $d \approx 90$ mm.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]**

A **mild steel shaft** ($S_{ut} = 560$ MPa, $S_{yt} = 320$ MPa) of diameter 40 mm is subjected to a completely reversed bending stress of 200 MPa. The shaft has a **circumferential groove** of radius 2 mm, surface is ground, and operates at room temperature with 90% reliability. The theoretical stress concentration factor from charts is $K_t = 2.2$.

**Compute:**
- **(a) (7 Marks)** The corrected alternating stress and modified endurance limit using Marin factors and notch sensitivity.
- **(b) (7 Marks)** The fatigue factor of safety using the **Goodman criterion** and verify against yielding.

**Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Notch Sensitivity**
For $S_{ut} = 560$ MPa and $r = 2$ mm, from Peterson's charts: $q \approx 0.72$.

$$K_f = 1 + 0.72(2.2 - 1) = 1 + 0.72 \times 1.2 = 1.864$$

**Step 2: Corrected Alternating Stress**

$$\sigma_{a,corr} = K_f \cdot \sigma_a = 1.864 \times 200 = 372.8 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Endurance Limit (Unmodified)**

$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 560 = 280 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4: Marin Factors**

For ground surface: $k_a = 1.58 \times (560)^{-0.085} = 1.58 \times 0.611 = 0.966$

For $d = 40$ mm: $k_b = 0.879 \times (40)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.757 = 0.666$

$k_c = 1.0$ (bending), $k_d = 1.0$, $k_e = 1.0$ (90%), $k_f = 1.0$.

$$S_e = 0.966 \times 0.666 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 280 = 180.2 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5: For fully reversed loading, $\sigma_m = 0$**

**Part (b) — Factor of Safety (7 Marks)**

**Step 6: Goodman FoS**

$$n_f = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_{a,corr}}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{372.8}{180.2} + 0} = \frac{1}{2.069} = 0.483$$

Since $n_f < 1$, the shaft **fails by fatigue**.

**Step 7: Yield Check**

$$n_y = \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_{a,corr} + \sigma_m} = \frac{320}{372.8 + 0} = 0.858$$

Since $n_y < 1$, **yielding also occurs in the first cycle**.

**Conclusion:** The shaft is unsafe in both fatigue and yielding. **Recommendation:** Increase the diameter to ~60 mm or select $S_{ut} = 800$ MPa material.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Design for Static and Fatigue Loads**

- [x] **Static Failure Theories:** Know Von Mises (preferred), Tresca, Rankine — Von Mises equation in both Cartesian and Principal form.
- [x] **Factor of Safety for Static:** $n = S_{yt} / \sigma_{VM}$ (ductile) or $S_{ut} / \sigma_{max}$ (brittle).
- [x] **Cyclic Decomposition:** $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$, $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ — **must memorize.**
- [x] **Loading Types:** Fully reversed (R = -1), pulsating (R = 0), static (R = +1).
- [x] **Endurance Limit:** $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for steels; aluminum has no $S_e'$.
- [x] **Marin Factors Formula:** $S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$ — **6 factors, never skip.**
- [x] **Surface Factor:** Always compute using $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ with proper (a, b) constants based on surface finish.
- [x] **Size Factor:** Different formula for $d < 51$ mm vs. $51 < d < 254$ mm.
- [x] **Stress Concentration:** $K_t$ from charts/equations; $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ where $q$ = notch sensitivity.
- [x] **Notch Sensitivity (q):** Increases with $S_{ut}$; typical 0.7 to 0.95 for steels.
- [x] **Three Failure Criteria:** Soderberg (most conservative), Goodman (industry standard), Gerber (least conservative).
- [x] **Goodman Equation:** $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$ — **derivation required in exam.**
- [x] **Soderberg Equation:** $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{yt} = 1/n$ — uses yield, not ultimate.
- [x] **Gerber Equation:** $n\sigma_a/S_e + (n\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2 = 1$ — parabolic.
- [x] **Yield Check (Always Required):** $n_y = S_{yt} / (\sigma_a + \sigma_m) \ge n_{required}$.
- [x] **Final Design FoS:** $n_{design} = \min(n_f, n_y)$ — take the smaller of the two.
- [x] **Von Mises for Combined:** $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ (2D combined bending + torsion).
- [x] **Common Pitfalls:** Forgetting units conversion, applying $K_t$ instead of $K_f$, ignoring temperature/reliability factors, using $S_{ut}$ where $S_{yt}$ is needed.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PMS-GM" for the 4 Cyclic Stress Quantities:**
> - **P**eak (max) stress
> - **M**ean (average) stress
> - **S**wing (alternating) stress
> - **G**oodman is the **G**o-to criterion
> - **M**ean stress to **M**aximum (Sut) in Goodman
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeated Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Repeated Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Repeated Loading

**Repeated Loading** (also called **Cyclic Loading** or **Fatigue Loading**) refers to the application of fluctuating or cyclic stresses on an engineering component, where the magnitude of the load varies with time in a periodic manner. Under such loading, failure of ductile materials can occur at stress levels **significantly below** the static yield or ultimate tensile strength, after a sufficient number of load cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Repeated loading is defined as a stress condition in which a component is subjected to a fluctuating stress between a maximum value $\sigma_{max}$ and a minimum value $\sigma_{min}$, characterized by its mean stress $\sigma_m$ and amplitude $\sigma_a$, causing progressive, localized structural damage culminating in **fatigue failure**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Nomenclature)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Maximum Stress | $\sigma_{max}$ | Highest algebraic value of the cyclic stress |
| Minimum Stress | $\sigma_{min}$ | Lowest algebraic value of the cyclic stress |
| Mean Stress | $\sigma_m$ | Average of maximum and minimum stress: $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$ |
| Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a$ | Half the range of stress: $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$ |
| Stress Range | $\Delta\sigma$ | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ |
| Stress Ratio | $R$ | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ |
| Amplitude Ratio | $A$ | $A = \sigma_a/\sigma_m$ |
| Endurance Limit | $S_e$ | Maximum completely reversed stress the material can endure for infinite cycles ($10^6$ for steel) |
| Fatigue Strength | $S_f$ | Stress level at which failure occurs after a specified number of cycles |
| Fatigue Life | $N$ | Number of cycles to failure |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Paper Clip Test:**
> Take a standard paper clip and bend it back and forth repeatedly. It does not break at the first bend (static strength is high), but after 8–10 bends, it snaps cleanly at the bend point. This is **fatigue failure**. The material never experienced a stress greater than its yield strength, yet it failed because the **repetitive, fluctuating nature** of the stress initiated a tiny crack that propagated with each cycle until catastrophic fracture.

**In automotive engineering**, this phenomenon is critical:
- A connecting rod endures $10^8$ tension–compression cycles per vehicle lifetime.
- A valve spring oscillates at engine frequency (≈ 3000 cycles/min).
- A driveshaft experiences torsional fatigue with every torque reversal.

Thus, **designing for static strength is not enough**; engineers must design for **fatigue endurance**.

## 1.4 Types of Cyclic Loading

1. **Fully Reversed Loading**: $R = -1$ (e.g., rotating shaft, $S_m = 0$).
2. **Pulsating Loading (Zero to Max)**: $R = 0$ (e.g., chain link, lever).
3. **Fluctuating Loading (Tension–Tension or Comp–Comp)**: $R > 0$ or $R > 1$.
4. **Random/Irregular Loading**: Variable amplitude (e.g., road loads on a chassis).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)

The **S-N Curve** (Stress-Number of cycles) is the fundamental graphical representation of fatigue behavior. For ferrous metals (steel, iron), it shows a horizontal asymptote at the **endurance limit** $S_e$ for $N \geq 10^6$ cycles. For non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper), no true endurance limit exists — strength continuously decreases with cycles.

For steel, the Basquin's equation describes the sloped portion:

$$
\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b
$$

where $\sigma'_f$ is the **fatigue strength coefficient** and $b$ is the **fatigue strength exponent** (typically $b = -0.05$ to $-0.12$ for steels).

## 2.2 Endurance Limit — Uncorrected vs Corrected

For wrought steels:
$$
S'_e = 0.5 \, S_{ut} \quad \text{(when } S_{ut} \leq 1400 \text{ MPa)}
$$

This is the **uncorrected (rotating-beam) endurance limit**. The actual endurance limit at a critical location of a real component is obtained by applying **modifying factors (Marin's Factors)**:

$$
S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S'_e
$$

### 2.3 The Six Modifying Factors (Marin's Equation)

| Factor | Symbol | Represents | Formula |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Surface factor | $k_a$ | Surface finish quality | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ |
| Size factor | $k_b$ | Component size effect | $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ (for round, axial) |
| Load factor | $k_c$ | Type of loading (bending, axial, torsion) | $k_c = 1$ (bending), $0.85$ (axial), $0.59$ (torsion) |
| Temperature factor | $k_d$ | Operating temperature | $k_d = S_{T}/S_{RT}$ |
| Reliability factor | $k_e$ | Statistical reliability | $k_e = 0.814$ (90%), $0.753$ (99%), $0.659$ (99.9%) |
| Miscellaneous factor | $k_f$ | Residual stresses, corrosion, etc. | $k_f \approx 1$ for general cases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference values for Surface Factor ($k_a$):**
> - **Ground**: $a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$
> - **Machined or Cold-drawn**: $a = 4.51$, $b = -0.265$
> - **Hot-rolled**: $a = 57.7$, $b = -0.718$
> - **As-forged**: $a = 272$, $b = -0.995$

### 2.4 Stress Concentration and Notch Sensitivity

A geometric discontinuity (hole, keyway, fillet, thread) creates a **local stress concentration** quantified by the **theoretical (geometric) stress concentration factor** $K_t$. The actual effective factor in fatigue is the **fatigue stress concentration factor** $K_f$:

$$
K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)
$$

where $q$ is the **notch sensitivity index** (Peterson's formula):

$$
q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/\sqrt{r}}}
$$

with $a$ and $r$ being material constants and notch radius, respectively.

## 2.5 Combined Mean and Alternating Stresses — Fatigue Failure Criteria

When the stress cycle is not fully reversed, the design must satisfy a **failure criterion** combining $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$. The three classical criteria are:

### (a) Soderberg Criterion (Most Conservative — governs ductile yield)

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{N_f}
$$

For infinite life ($N_f = 1$):

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1
$$

### (b) Goodman Criterion (Most Widely Used)

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N_f}
$$

Infinite life form:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1
$$

### (c) Gerber Criterion (Parabolic, less conservative for ductile)

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{N_f}
$$

## 2.6 Cumulative Fatigue Damage (Palmgren–Miner's Rule)

For **variable amplitude loading**, the total damage $D$ is the linear sum of cycle ratios:

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure occurs when** $D \geq 1$. (In practice, $D = 0.5$ to $1.5$ at failure is observed due to load sequence effects.)

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Engineering Use |
|:--|:--------|:----------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$ | Mean stress computation |
| 2 | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$ | Stress amplitude |
| 3 | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | Characterizes load type |
| 4 | $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ | Uncorrected endurance limit (steel) |
| 5 | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$ | Corrected endurance limit |
| 6 | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Effective fatigue stress concentration |
| 7 | $q = 1/(1 + \sqrt{a/r})$ | Notch sensitivity (Neuber's constant $a$) |
| 8 | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_y = 1$ | Soderberg (yield safety) |
| 9 | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1$ | Goodman (ultimate safety) |
| 10 | $\sigma_a/S_e + (\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2 = 1$ | Gerber (parabolic) |
| 11 | $D = \sum n_i/N_i$ | Miner's cumulative damage |

## 2.8 Why This Matters in Automotive Design

- **Engine components** (crankshaft, connecting rod, valve spring) must survive $10^8$–$10^9$ cycles.
- **Driveline components** (gear, axle, driveshaft) endure torsional fatigue.
- **Brake and suspension parts** face random amplitude road-induced loading → Miner's rule applies.
- **Selection criterion**: Soderberg for safety-critical yield concerns; Goodman for brittle/cast parts; Gerber for ductile optimization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Modification of Endurance Limit using Marin's Equation

**Statement:** The rotating-beam endurance limit $S'_e$ is valid only under ideal laboratory conditions. Real components deviate due to surface, size, loading, temperature, reliability, and miscellaneous effects. We must derive the corrected $S_e$ at the critical location.

**Starting Point (Ideal Lab Value):**

$$
S'_e = 0.5 \, S_{ut}
$$

**Step 1 — Surface Finish Effect ($k_a$):**
A rough surface acts as a multitude of micro-notches, reducing the effective endurance limit. Empirical data (Shigley/Villforks) shows a power-law relationship with $S_{ut}$:

$$
k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b}
$$

*Why:* Higher strength materials are more sensitive to surface flaws (smaller plastic zone), so $b$ is negative.

**Step 2 — Size Effect ($k_b$):**
Larger components have a higher probability of containing a critical flaw, and surface area to volume ratio is lower, so the chance of subsurface failure decreases. For a solid round shaft in bending or torsion:

$$
k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107} \quad \text{(for } 2.79 \leq d \leq 51 \text{ mm)}
$$

For axial loading, $k_b = 1$ (entire cross-section uniformly stressed).
For non-circular sections, use the **effective diameter**:

$$
d_e = 0.808 \sqrt{h \cdot b} \quad \text{(rectangular, bending about h)}
$$

**Step 3 — Load Factor ($k_c$):**
Torsion and axial loading produce different stress gradients. Bending is the reference ($k_c = 1$):

$$
k_c = 
\begin{cases}
1.0 & \text{bending} \\
0.85 & \text{axial} \\
0.59 & \text{torsion}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Temperature Factor ($k_d$):**
Above room temperature, strength drops; below it, brittle behavior may occur. Use tabulated ratios of $S_{T}/S_{RT}$.

**Step 5 — Reliability Factor ($k_e$):**
For a normal distribution of endurance limits, the higher the reliability, the lower the design limit. For 50%, 90%, 99%, 99.9%:

$$
k_e = 1.000,\ 0.897,\ 0.814,\ 0.753,\ 0.659
$$

*(Note: Some KTU textbooks use slightly different values; always check your data handbook.)*

**Step 6 — Miscellaneous Factor ($k_f$):**
Includes residual stresses, corrosion, electrolytic plating, fretting, etc. Default $k_f = 1$ unless specific data is given.

**Final Combined Equation:**

$$
\boxed{S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S'_e}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Each factor is **multiplicative** because each acts independently on the fatigue life. The corrected $S_e$ is always less than or equal to $S'_e$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Soderberg Line (Failure Criterion for Ductile Yield Under Fluctuating Stress)

**Premise:** A component must not yield under the combined effect of mean and alternating stress. The yield condition in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane is therefore the **critical boundary**.

**Step 1 — Pure Static Yield:** If $\sigma_a = 0$, the only failure is yielding when $\sigma_m = S_y$. This gives point A on the $\sigma_m$ axis.

**Step 2 — Pure Fatigue Failure:** If $\sigma_m = 0$, the only failure is fatigue at $\sigma_a = S_e$. This gives point B on the $\sigma_a$ axis.

**Step 3 — Linear Interpolation (Soderberg Assumption):** The yield boundary is assumed linear between A and B. The equation of this straight line is:

$$
\frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Introduction of Factor of Safety $N_f$:** If we want a factor of safety $N_f$ against yielding-fatigue, then the load components scale up by $N_f$:

$$
\frac{N_f \cdot \sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{N_f \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} = 1
$$

Solving for $N_f$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{N_f} = \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}}
$$

**Step 5 — Goodman Form (Replacing $S_y$ with $S_{ut}$):** Since Goodman uses ultimate strength for brittle failure (more conservative for brittle materials):

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{N_f} = \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}}
$$

**Step 6 — Gerber Form (Parabolic):** Empirical data for ductile materials shows a parabola:

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{N_f} = \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Hierarchy (Most to Least Conservative):**
> 1. **Soderberg** ← conservative, ductile yield safety.
> 2. **Goodman** ← moderate, brittle/cast parts.
> 3. **Gerber** ← best fit for ductile, but unconservative for brittle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule (Linear Damage Hypothesis)

**Premise:** Each load cycle consumes a fraction of the total fatigue life. Damage accumulates linearly, independent of load sequence.

**Step 1 — Define Cycle Ratio:** If a stress level $\sigma_i$ causes failure in $N_i$ cycles, then one cycle at this stress causes damage $1/N_i$. After $n_i$ cycles:

$$
D_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum All Stress Blocks:** With $k$ different stress blocks in the spectrum:

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Failure Criterion:** The component fails when the accumulated damage reaches unity:

$$
\boxed{D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Miner's Theorem Statement:** *In a variable-amplitude loading spectrum, fatigue failure occurs when the sum of cycle ratios equals 1, regardless of the order in which the cycles are applied.*

**Caveat:** Real-world data shows $D$ at failure is typically $0.5$ to $1.5$ due to:
- **Load sequence effects** (low-high vs high-low).
- **Crack retardation/acceleration** during overloads.
- **Residual stress relaxation.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (For Concept Reinforcement)

**Problem:** A steel shaft has $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa, $S_y = 560$ MPa, and is subjected to a fully reversed bending stress of amplitude $\sigma_a = 200$ MPa. It has a machined surface, diameter $d = 30$ mm, operates at room temperature, has 90% reliability, and has a fillet of $r = 3$ mm with theoretical $K_t = 1.8$ and Neuber's constant $a = 0.4$ mm. Find $N_f$ using Goodman.

**Step 1 — Uncorrected Endurance Limit:**

$$
S'_e = 0.5 \times 700 = 350 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Surface Factor $k_a$ (Machined):**

$$
k_a = 4.51 \times 700^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1837 \approx 0.829
$$

**Step 3 — Size Factor $k_b$ ($d = 30$ mm):**

$$
k_b = 0.879 \times 30^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.6548 \approx 0.576
$$

**Step 4 — Load Factor $k_c = 1$ (Bending).**

**Step 5 — Temperature $k_d = 1$, Reliability $k_e = 0.897$ (90%).**

**Step 6 — Corrected $S_e$:**

$$
S_e = 0.829 \times 0.576 \times 1 \times 1 \times 0.897 \times 350 \approx 149.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Notch Sensitivity (Neuber):**

$$
\sqrt{a} = \sqrt{0.4} = 0.632 \text{ mm}^{0.5}
$$

$$
q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.4/\sqrt{3}}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.4/1.732}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.231}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.481} = 0.675
$$

$$
K_f = 1 + 0.675(1.8 - 1) = 1 + 0.540 = 1.540
$$

**Step 8 — Effective Alternating Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{a,\text{eff}} = K_f \times \sigma_a = 1.540 \times 200 = 308 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 9 — Goodman Equation (Fully Reversed: $\sigma_m = 0$):**

$$
N_f = \frac{S_e}{\sigma_{a,\text{eff}}} = \frac{149.7}{308} \approx 0.486
$$

Since $N_f < 1$, the design is **inadequate** — redesign is required (larger fillet radius or lower stress).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fatigue Design Workflow

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the **end-to-end fatigue design process** for an automotive component (e.g., a crankshaft), as mandated by the KTU design methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Operating Conditions] --> B[Load Spectrum Analysis]
    B --> C[Mean Stress sigma_m and Amplitude sigma_a]
    C --> D[Uncorrected Endurance Limit S prime e = 0.5 Sut]
    D --> E[Apply Marin Modifying Factors]
    E --> F[Corrected Endurance Limit Se]
    F --> G[Evaluate Stress Concentration Kt and Notch Sensitivity q]
    G --> H[Effective Fatigue Factor Kf]
    H --> I[Select Failure Criterion]
    I --> I1[Soderberg Ductile Yield]
    I --> I2[Goodman Brittle Ultimate]
    I --> I3[Gerber Ductile Parabolic]
    I1 --> J[Compute Factor of Safety Nf]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K{Nf greater than 1?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Design Acceptable Document]
    K -- No --> M[Redesign Loop Increase Radius or Reduce Load]
    M --> B
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Typical S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stress Amplitude sigma a] --> B[Log N Cycles]
    B --> C[Steeper Slope for Higher Sut]
    C --> D[Infinite Life Region N greater than 10 power 6]
    D --> E[Endurance Limit Se Plateau for Steel]
    E --> F[No Plateau for Aluminium Continuously Descending]
```

## 4.3 Mean Stress Diagram — Failure Envelopes Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[sigma m axis] --> P2[Point S y Soderberg on sigma m axis]
    P3[sigma a axis] --> P4[Point S e on sigma a axis]
    P2 --> P5[Linear Soderberg Line]
    P4 --> P5
    P2 --> P6[Parabolic Gerber Curve]
    P4 --> P6
    P2 --> P7[Linear Goodman Line S ut on sigma m axis]
    P4 --> P7
    P5 --> P8[Conservative Region for Ductile Yield]
    P6 --> P9[Best Fit Ductile Region]
    P7 --> P10[Moderate Region for Brittle Parts]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Variable Amplitude Loading and Miner's Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Load Time History from Service] --> Q2[Cycle Counting Algorithm]
    Q2 --> Q3[Rainflow Counting or Peak Valley Method]
    Q3 --> Q4[Histogram of Stress Blocks sigma i and Counts n i]
    Q4 --> Q5[Read S N Curve for Each Block]
    Q5 --> Q6[Find N i for Each sigma i]
    Q6 --> Q7[Compute Sum n i over N i]
    Q7 --> Q8{Damage D greater than 1?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9[Component Fails Predict Miner's]
    Q8 -- No --> Q10[Safe Continue Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> Each block represents a logical step in the design or analysis workflow. Arrows indicate the **direction of data flow**. Decision diamonds (rotated squares) are conditional branches where the engineer must evaluate a numerical criterion and loop back if the design fails.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define the term "Endurance Limit" and explain why it exists for steels but not for aluminum alloys.**

**Model Answer:**
The **endurance limit** ($S_e$) is defined as the maximum value of completely reversed stress that a material can withstand for an infinite number of cycles (conventionally $N = 10^6$ cycles for steels) without failure.

For steels, the S-N curve becomes asymptotic to a horizontal line at $S_e$ because the **strain aging** mechanism (interaction of dislocations with interstitial carbon/nitrogen atoms) creates a threshold stress below which dislocation motion is permanently arrested. Below this threshold, micro-cracks cannot initiate or propagate.

In aluminum alloys, no such threshold exists because aluminum does not undergo strain aging at room temperature. Hence, the S-N curve continues to descend indefinitely, and designers must specify fatigue strength at a finite number of cycles (e.g., $S_f$ at $5 \times 10^8$ cycles).

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the mechanism explanation)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between theoretical stress concentration factor ($K_t$) and fatigue stress concentration factor ($K_f$).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $K_t$ (Theoretical) | $K_f$ (Fatigue) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Ratio of max local stress to nominal stress, from linear elasticity | Effective stress concentration under cyclic loading |
| Dependence | Purely geometric (function of notch radius and geometry) | Geometric + material notch sensitivity |
| Magnitude | $K_t \geq 1$ always | $K_f \leq K_t$ (always less due to plasticity effects) |
| Formula | From charts or FEA | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ |
| When $K_t = 1$ | No geometric discontinuity | $K_f = 1$ as well |

The relationship is governed by the **notch sensitivity index** $q$, where $q = 0$ implies insensitive material and $q = 1$ implies fully sensitive.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always** convert units to a consistent system (MPa or N/mm²). Mixing units costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **State the formula before substituting** values — KTU evaluators mark formula writing explicitly.
> 3. **For Miner's rule**, list each stress block and its $n_i, N_i$ in a table — partial credit is awarded for table.
> 4. **Notch sensitivity** must be computed using the given formula; do not assume $q = 1$.
> 5. **Failure criteria** (Goodman/Soderberg/Gerber) require stating the assumption of the criterion (ductile/brittle).
> 6. **Draw the free-body diagram and stress cycle** wherever the problem involves fluctuating loads.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
