A steel shaft of diameter $d = 40$ mm is subjected to a fluctuating torque that varies from $T_{min} = -100$ N·m to $T_{max} = 300$ N·m. The material has $S_{ut} = 800$ MPa, $S_y = 660$ MPa. The surface is ground, the shaft operates at 25 °C, reliability is 90%, and there is a shoulder fillet with $K_t = 2.0$ and Neuber's constant $a = 0.5$ mm with fillet radius $r = 4$ mm.

**(a)** Compute the corrected endurance limit $S_e$ using all relevant Marin factors. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the factor of safety using the **Soderberg criterion**, accounting for stress concentration. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Corrected Endurance Limit:**

**Step 1: Uncorrected Endurance Limit**

$$
S'_e = 0.5 \times 800 = 400 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Surface Factor (Ground):**

$$
k_a = 1.58 \times 800^{-0.085} = 1.58 \times 0.5839 \approx 0.923
$$

**Step 3: Size Factor (Round, $d = 40$ mm, Torsion):**

For round shafts in torsion, $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ (same as bending for $d \geq 2.79$ mm):

$$
k_b = 0.879 \times 40^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.6217 \approx 0.546
$$

**Step 4: Load Factor (Torsion):**

$$
k_c = 0.59
$$

**Step 5: Temperature & Reliability:**

$$
k_d = 1.0 \quad (\text{room temperature}), \quad k_e = 0.897 \quad (\text{90\% reliability})
$$

**Step 6: Miscellaneous Factor:**

$$
k_f = 1.0 \text{ (assumed)}
$$

**Step 7: Corrected Endurance Limit:**

$$
\boxed{S_e = 0.923 \times 0.546 \times 0.59 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 400 \approx 108.3 \text{ MPa}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Soderberg Factor of Safety:**

**Step 1: Compute Shear Stress Range**

Polar section modulus: $Z_p = \pi d^3 / 16 = \pi (40)^3/16 = 12{,}566$ mm³

Maximum and minimum shear stress:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T_{max}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 300 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 40^3} = 23.87 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_{min} = \frac{16 T_{min}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times (-100) \times 10^3}{\pi \times 40^3} = -7.96 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Mean and Alternating Shear Stresses**

$$
\tau_m = \frac{23.87 + (-7.96)}{2} = 7.96 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_a = \frac{23.87 - (-7.96)}{2} = 15.92 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Notch Sensitivity (Neuber)**

$$
q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/\sqrt{r}}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.5/\sqrt{4}}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.5/2}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.25}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5} = 0.667
$$

**Step 4: Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor**

$$
K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.667(2.0 - 1) = 1.667
$$

**Step 5: Soderberg Equation (in shear)**

$$
\frac{1}{N_f} = \frac{K_f \tau_a}{S_{e,\text{shear}}} + \frac{\tau_m}{S_{sy}}
$$

where $S_{e,\text{shear}} = 0.577 S_e = 0.577 \times 108.3 = 62.5$ MPa and $S_{sy} = 0.577 S_y = 380.8$ MPa.

$$
\frac{1}{N_f} = \frac{1.667 \times 15.92}{62.5} + \frac{7.96}{380.8} = 0.4245 + 0.0209 = 0.4454
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f = \frac{1}{0.4454} \approx 2.245}
$$

Since $N_f > 1$, the design is **safe** for both yield and fatigue under the Soderberg criterion.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

A connecting rod of an automotive engine is subjected to a load spectrum with three stress blocks:

| Block | Stress Amplitude $\sigma_a$ (MPa) | Mean Stress $\sigma_m$ (MPa) | Cycles $n_i$ |
|:-----:|:---------------------------------:|:---------------------------:|:------------:|
| 1     | 200                               | 100                         | 10,000       |
| 2     | 280                               | 80                          | 5,000        |
| 3     | 350                               | 50                          | 2,000        |

Material: $S_{ut} = 650$ MPa, $S_y = 520$ MPa, fully corrected $S_e = 180$ MPa.

**(a)** For each block, determine the allowable number of cycles $N_i$ using the **Goodman criterion** (assume Basquin-type S-N for finite life above $10^3$ cycles). Use the simplified relationship $N_i = N_0 (\sigma_a^*/\sigma_a)^m$ where $N_0 = 10^3$, $m = 3$ (assumed), and $\sigma_a^*$ is the equivalent fully-reversed stress that would give the same life per Goodman. (7 marks)

**(b)** Apply **Miner's Rule** to predict whether the component fails under this spectrum. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Allowable Cycles per Block:**

**Goodman Equivalent Stress:** Convert each $(\sigma_a, \sigma_m)$ pair to a fully-reversed equivalent $\sigma_{ar}$:

$$
\sigma_{ar,i} = \frac{\sigma_{a,i}}{1 - \sigma_{m,i}/S_{ut}}
$$

**Block 1:** $\sigma_{ar,1} = \dfrac{200}{1 - 100/650} = \dfrac{200}{0.8462} = 236.4$ MPa

**Block 2:** $\sigma_{ar,2} = \dfrac{280}{1 - 80/650} = \dfrac{280}{0.8769} = 319.3$ MPa

**Block 3:** $\sigma_{ar,3} = \dfrac{350}{1 - 50/650} = \dfrac{350}{0.9231} = 379.1$ MPa

**S-N Relation (Basquin form):** For fully reversed, the relation between $N$ and $\sigma_{ar}$ is:

$$
\sigma_{ar} = S'_e \left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)^{1/m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = N_0 \left(\frac{S'_e}{\sigma_{ar}}\right)^{m}
$$

where $S'_e = 180$ MPa, $N_0 = 10^3$, $m = 3$.

**Block 1:**

$$
N_1 = 10^3 \times \left(\frac{180}{236.4}\right)^3 = 10^3 \times (0.7614)^3 = 10^3 \times 0.4412 = 441 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Block 2:**

$$
N_2 = 10^3 \times \left(\frac{180}{319.3}\right)^3 = 10^3 \times (0.5638)^3 = 10^3 \times 0.1792 = 179 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Block 3:**

$$
N_3 = 10^3 \times \left(\frac{180}{379.1}\right)^3 = 10^3 \times (0.4748)^3 = 10^3 \times 0.1070 = 107 \text{ cycles}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Miner's Cumulative Damage:**

$$
D = \frac{n_1}{N_1} + \frac{n_2}{N_2} + \frac{n_3}{N_3}
$$

$$
D = \frac{10{,}000}{441} + \frac{5{,}000}{179} + \frac{2{,}000}{107}
$$

$$
D = 22.68 + 27.93 + 18.69 = 69.30
$$

Since $D = 69.30 \gg 1$, the connecting rod **WILL FAIL** long before completing the spectrum. The component is severely under-designed for this load history. The designer must either:
- Reduce the load (redesign geometry to lower stress).
- Increase the strength (select a higher-grade material).
- Limit the operational duty cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Notice that $N_i$ values are *very small* (hundreds of cycles) — this means each stress block by itself would cause failure in a small number of cycles. Miner's rule is *very sensitive* to high stress amplitudes. Always check that $n_i < N_i$ for safe operation; if not, the design fails immediately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Fire Revision Points:**

- [ ] **Endurance Limit** $S_e$ exists only for **ferrous (steel, iron)** materials at $N = 10^6$ cycles.
- [ ] **Uncorrected $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$** applies only for $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa.
- [ ] **Marin's six factors** are: $k_a$ (surface), $k_b$ (size), $k_c$ (load), $k_d$ (temp), $k_e$ (reliability), $k_f$ (misc.).
- [ ] $k_a$ **decreases** as $S_{ut}$ increases → stronger steels need smoother surfaces.
- [ ] $k_b = 1$ for axial loading; $k_b \propto d^{-0.107}$ for round bending/torsion.
- [ ] $k_c = 1$ (bending), $0.85$ (axial), $0.59$ (torsion).
- [ ] $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$; $q = 0$ (insensitive) to $q = 1$ (fully sensitive).
- [ ] **Soderberg** is most conservative (uses $S_y$); **Goodman** is moderate (uses $S_{ut}$); **Gerber** is least conservative (parabolic).
- [ ] **S-N curve for steel** is flat after $10^6$ cycles; **aluminum has no plateau**.
- [ ] **Miner's Rule**: $D = \sum n_i/N_i \geq 1$ → failure.
- [ ] Miner's rule is **load-sequence independent** (a major real-world limitation).
- [ ] In KTU problems, **always state the formula, then substitute values** (valuation pattern).
- [ ] For fully reversed loading, $\sigma_m = 0$ and $\sigma_a = \sigma_{max} = -\sigma_{min}$.
- [ ] **Mean stress** is **tensile positive, compressive negative** in sign convention.
- [ ] For shafts in torsion, divide the bending $S_e$ by $\sqrt{3}$ to obtain shear endurance limit: $S_{se} = 0.577 S_e$.
- [ ] **Ductile materials** (steel, aluminum) → use **Goodman or Gerber** for design.
- [ ] **Brittle materials** (cast iron) → use **Soderberg or modified Goodman**.
- [ ] **Reliability 90%** → $k_e = 0.897$; **99%** → $k_e = 0.814$; **99.9%** → $k_e = 0.753$ (verify with your data handbook).
- [ ] **High-cycle fatigue** ($N > 10^5$): elastic regime, use S-N approach.
- [ ] **Low-cycle fatigue** ($N < 10^3$): plastic regime, use Coffin-Manson (Strain-life approach).
- [ ] **Goodman line passes through** $(S_{ut}, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$ in $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane.
- [ ] **Safety factor** $N_f$ from Goodman: $\dfrac{1}{N_f} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Note:** The mastery of **Repeated Loading** is the gateway to designing any dynamically loaded automotive component. Every fatigue problem reduces to: (1) identify mean and alternating stress, (2) compute corrected endurance, (3) apply a failure criterion, (4) check $N_f > 1$, and (5) for variable loads, sum the damage via Miner's rule. Follow this flow systematically and no KTU question can surprise you.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversed Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reversed Bending # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reversed Bending** is a specific category of **fluctuating / cyclic stress** in which the bending stress at a point in a structural or machine component varies sinusoidally between **equal and opposite magnitudes** over a complete load cycle. Mathematically, the **maximum tensile stress equals the maximum compressive stress**, i.e., $\sigma_{max} = -\sigma_{min}$. In the language of fatigue design (Shigley's / V.B. Bhandari convention followed universally in KTU): $$ \sigma_{max} = +\sigma_a, \qquad \sigma_{min} = -\sigma_a $$ Therefore the **mean stress** $ \sigma_m = 0 $ and the **alternating (amplitude) stress** $ \sigma_a = \sigma_{max} $. This is the textbook signature of *reversed bending* — a loading regime that is **purely alternating with zero mean**. The **classical physical example** is a **rotating shaft** carrying a stationary transverse load (e.g., a simply supported shaft with a pulley or gear at mid-span). As the shaft rotates, every fibre on its surface alternates between tension and compression once per revolution, generating fully reversed bending stress. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Bending Paperclip** > Take an ordinary paperclip and bend it back-and-forth repeatedly in your fingers. The outer fibres of the wire alternately stretch (tension) and squeeze (compression) with every oscillation. There is **no net permanent bias** toward tension or compression — both extremes are equal. Eventually the paperclip snaps. That failure at stresses far below the material's static strength is **fatigue under reversed bending**. In an automobile, the same phenomenon occurs in: - **Drive shafts and half-shafts** rotating under bending loads. - **Valve springs** oscillating between compression coils. - **Connecting rods** under combined inertia and gas loading. - **Leaf / coil springs** of the suspension on every road bump. The fibre is "exercised" equally in both directions, and the **endurance limit** (not the ultimate strength) becomes the governing failure criterion. ## 1.3 Critical Numerical Benchmarks (Syllabus Highlights) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Reference Values every KTU student must memorise:** > - For **steels with $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa**, the unmodified endurance limit is $S_e' \approx \mathbf{0.5 \, S_{ut}}$. > - For **steels with $S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa**, $S_e' \approx 700$ MPa (empirical plateau). > - For **aluminium alloys**, $S_e' \approx \mathbf{0.4 \, S_{ut}}$. > - The stress cycle is characterised by the **stress ratio** $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max} = \mathbf{-1}$ for *fully reversed* bending. > - Rotating-beam test (R.R. Moore machine) is the standard experimental generator of $R = -1$ data. These constants recur in every numerical problem on this topic and are guaranteed test material. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Breaking Down the Phenomenon — Structured Logic ### Step 1 — Identify the Loading Geometry A component experiences **reversed bending** when one of the following is true: - A **rotating beam** carries a **stationary transverse load** (cantilever, simply-supported, or overhung shaft). - A **stationary beam** is loaded by a **cyclic transverse force** whose direction reverses (e.g., a railway axle between two bearings with a load oscillating about the neutral axis). ### Step 2 — Resolve into Mean and Alternating Components Using the KTU-mandated decomposition: $$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}, \qquad \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} $$ For *pure* reversed bending, $ \sigma_{max} = -\sigma_{min} $, giving $ \sigma_m = 0 $ and $ \sigma_a = \sigma_{max} $. ### Step 3 — Compute the Nominal Bending Stress From elementary strength of materials (flexure formula): $$ \sigma_{nom} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(solid circular cross-section)} $$ $$ \sigma_{nom} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{M}{Z} \quad \text{(general section, } Z \text{ = section modulus)} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Stress Concentration Factor Sharp fillets, keyways, oil holes, shoulder steps raise local stress. The **fatigue stress concentration factor** is: $$ K_f = 1 + q \,(K_t - 1) $$ where $K_t$ is the theoretical (geometric) factor and $q$ is the **notch sensitivity** (0 to 1, from Peterson's charts). The local alternating stress becomes $ \sigma_a = K_f \cdot \sigma_{nom} $. ### Step 5 — Modify the Endurance Limit The lab-tested endurance limit $S_e'$ must be discounted for **real operating conditions**: $$ S_e = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, k_f \, S_e' $$ | Factor | Meaning | Typical Range | |:------:|:--------|:--------------| | $k_a$ | Surface factor (machining, grinding, forging) | 0.6 – 1.0 | | $k_b$ | Size factor (larger diameter ⇒ lower $S_e$) | 0.6 – 0.95 | | $k_c$ | Load factor (1.0 for bending, 0.85 for axial) | 0.85 – 1.0 | | $k_d$ | Temperature factor | 0.5 – 1.0 | | $k_e$ | Reliability factor (50 % → 99.9 %) | 0.62 – 1.0 | | $k_f$ | Miscellaneous (corrosion, plating) | 0.5 – 1.0 | ### Step 6 — Apply the Failure Criterion Since $\sigma_m = 0$, the **Goodman / Soderberg criterion reduces to a single ratio**: $$ n = \frac{S_e}{\sigma_a} = \frac{S_e}{K_f \, \sigma_{nom}} $$ If $ n \ge n_{required} $ (typically 1.5 – 2.5 for automotive components), the design is safe under infinite life. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |:-:|:---------|:--------|:-----:|:------| | 1 | Mean stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa | $0$ for reversed bending | | 2 | Alternating stress | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa | $=\sigma_{max}$ for $R=-1$ | | 3 | Stress ratio | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | – | $-1$ for fully reversed | | 4 | Nominal bending stress | $\sigma_{nom} = 32M/(\pi d^3)$ | MPa | Solid round shaft | | 5 | Section modulus (solid shaft) | $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$ | mm³ | | | 6 | Unmodified endurance limit (steel) | $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ | MPa | If $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa | | 7 | Unmodified endurance limit (steel) | $S_e' = 700$ MPa | MPa | If $S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa | | 8 | Unmodified endurance limit (Al) | $S_e' = 0.4 \, S_{ut}$ | MPa | Aluminium alloys | | 9 | Modified endurance limit | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, S_e'$ | MPa | Multiply all factors | | 10 | Fatigue stress concentration | $K_f = 1 + q (K_t - 1)$ | – | $0 \le q \le 1$ | | 11 | Factor of safety (reversed) | $n = S_e / (K_f \sigma_{nom})$ | – | Use $S_e/\sigma_a$ form | | 12 | Amplitude of bending moment | $M_a = \sigma_a \cdot Z$ | N·mm | Useful for design | | 13 | Design equation for diameter | $d = \left[ 32 n K_f M_a / (\pi S_e) \right]^{1/3}$ | mm | Solid shaft under $M_a$ | > [!NOTE] > **Use $\vert$ or \mid in LaTeX, never the literal `|`, inside markdown tables to avoid breaking table rendering.** Above is a model of KTU-compliant layout. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Reversed bending governs the **fatigue life** of almost every rotating automotive component: **crankshafts, camshafts, transmission input shafts, half-shafts (drive axles), and wheel hubs**. A truck's rear axle rotates under the sprung weight of the vehicle — it is essentially a **R.R. Moore rotating-beam specimen in service**. Every revolution pushes each surface fibre through one full tension–compression cycle, and failure, when it occurs, initiates at the surface at points of stress concentration (fillet, keyway, splined joint, oil hole). Designing for $R = -1$ fatigue is therefore the **single most important design exercise** in automotive driveline durability. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stress State in a Rotating Cantilever Shaft (Classic Reversed Bending) Consider a **circular shaft of diameter $d$ and length $L$**, loaded transversely by a **stationary force $P$** at its free end while rotating at angular speed $\omega$. We will derive the bending stress experienced by an arbitrary surface fibre. **Step 1 — Bending moment at the critical section (root of the shaft):** The transverse load creates a constant bending moment along the cantilever: $$ M = P \cdot L $$ Because the shaft rotates, the **direction of $M$ relative to any surface fibre reverses every half-revolution**, while its magnitude stays constant. **Step 2 — Nominal bending stress at the extreme fibre (flexure formula):** For a solid circular section, the moment of inertia is $I = \pi d^4 / 64$ and the distance to the outer fibre is $c = d/2$. Therefore: $$ \sigma_{nom} = \frac{M \, c}{I} = \frac{M \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 64} = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^3} $$ **Step 3 — Temporal variation of stress at a fixed material point on the surface:** As the shaft rotates through angle $\theta = \omega t$, a surface element originally at angular position $0$ experiences a stress whose sign depends on whether it lies on the tensile or compressive side of the bending plane. The stress at that material point therefore varies as: $$ \sigma(t) = \frac{32 \, P \, L}{\pi \, d^3} \cos(\omega t) $$ **Step 4 — Identify the extreme values over one cycle:** Since $ \cos(\omega t) $ oscillates between $+1$ and $-1$: $$ \sigma_{max} = +\frac{32 \, P \, L}{\pi \, d^3}, \qquad \sigma_{min} = -\frac{32 \, P \, L}{\pi \, d^3} $$ **Step 5 — Confirm the reversed-bending identity:** $$ \sigma_{max} = -\sigma_{min} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = -1 $$ $$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = 0 $$ $$ \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{32 \, P \, L}{\pi \, d^3} $$ This derivation is the **textbook proof** that a rotating beam under a stationary transverse load produces *fully reversed bending*. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Factor of Safety under Reversed Bending Using the Goodman Line **Step 1 — Recall the modified Goodman relation** for finite life (stress amplitude vs. mean stress): $$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n} $$ where $n$ is the factor of safety. This line connects $(S_{ut}, 0)$ on the $\sigma_m$ axis to $(S_e, 0)$ on the $\sigma_a$ axis. **Step 2 — Substitute the reversed-bending condition $ \sigma_m = 0 $:** $$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + 0 = \frac{1}{n} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $n$:** $$ n = \frac{S_e}{\sigma_a} $$ **Step 4 — Incorporate the stress concentration factor $K_f$:** $$ \sigma_a = K_f \cdot \sigma_{nom} $$ Therefore: $$ n = \frac{S_e}{K_f \, \sigma_{nom}} $$ **Step 5 — Final design form (solve for the unknown diameter $d$):** Setting $n = n_{required}$ and substituting $\sigma_{nom} = 32 M / (\pi d^3)$: $$ n_{req} = \frac{S_e \, \pi \, d^3}{K_f \, 32 \, M_a} $$ Rearranging: $$ d = \left[\frac{32 \, n_{req} \, K_f \, M_a}{\pi \, S_e}\right]^{1/3} $$ This is the **canonical KTU design equation** for a shaft under reversed bending. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A solid steel shaft of diameter **40 mm** transmits a stationary transverse load $P = 2$ kN at a lever arm of **150 mm** while rotating. Material: $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa. Surface factor $k_a = 0.85$, size factor $k_b = 0.85$, load factor $k_c = 1.0$, temperature $k_d = 1.0$, reliability $k_e = 0.897$, miscellaneous $k_f = 1.0$. The shaft has a shoulder fillet with $K_t = 1.85$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.85$. Find the factor of safety. **Solution Matrix:** | Step | Quantity | Calculation | Result | |:----:|:---------|:------------|:-------| | 1 | Bending moment $M$ | $M = P \times L = 2000 \times 150$ | $3.0 \times 10^5$ N·mm | | 2 | Nominal stress $\sigma_{nom}$ | $32 M / (\pi d^3) = 32(3\times10^5)/(\pi \times 40^3)$ | **47.75 MPa** | | 3 | Unmod. endurance $S_e'$ | $0.5 \times 620$ | **310 MPa** | | 4 | Mod. endurance $S_e$ | $0.85 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 310$ | **201.0 MPa** | | 5 | Fatigue $K_f$ | $1 + 0.85(1.85 - 1) = 1 + 0.7225$ | **1.7225** | | 6 | Local alt. stress $\sigma_a$ | $1.7225 \times 47.75$ | **82.25 MPa** | | 7 | Factor of safety $n$ | $S_e / \sigma_a = 201.0 / 82.25$ | **2.44** | **Interpretation:** $n = 2.44$ exceeds the typical KTU-mandated minimum of 2.0 for a rotating automotive shaft, so the design is acceptable under infinite-life (reversed-bending) fatigue. ## 3.4 Verification of the Mean-Stress Identity To prove $R = -1$ exhaustively: $$ R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{-\sigma_a}{+\sigma_a} = -1 \quad \text{(by definition of reversal)} $$ The amplitude-to-mean ratio is therefore infinite ($ \sigma_a / \sigma_m \to \infty $), confirming that *reversed bending is the most severe form of pure alternating stress* — there is no tensile mean to "hold the crack closed" and the full endurance limit must be available on the alternating axis alone. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Reversed-Bending Fatigue Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Identify Loading] --> B{Is the beam rotating
under a stationary load?} B -- Yes --> C[Apply Rotating-Beam Model] B -- No --> D[Apply Oscillating-Load Model] C --> E[Compute Bending Moment M] D --> E E --> F[Nominal Stress via Flexure Formula
sigma_nom = 32M / pi d cubed] F --> G{Stress Concentration
Present?} G -- Yes --> H[Apply Kf = 1 + q Kt minus 1] G -- No --> I[sigma_a = sigma_nom] H --> J[sigma_a = Kf times sigma_nom] I --> J J --> K[Compute S dash e from Sut] K --> L[Apply Modification Factors
ka kb kc kd ke kf] L --> M[Modified Endurance Limit S sub e] M --> N[Mean Stress Check: sigma_m = 0] N --> O[Factor of Safety n = S sub e / sigma_a] O --> P{n greater than n required?} P -- Yes --> Q[Design Safe: Infinite Life] P -- No --> R[Redesign: Increase d or change material] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — S-N Diagram Concept for Reversed Bending ```mermaid flowchart LR A[High-Cycle Reversed Bending
N greater than 10 to the 3] --> B[S-N Curve Region
Wohler Diagram] B --> C{Does material have
endurance limit?} C -- Yes, Steel --> D[Horizontal Plateau at S sub e] C -- No, Aluminium --> E[Continuous Decline at 10 to the 6] D --> F[Design Stress must be less than S sub e] E --> G[Design for Finite Life at 10 to the 6 cycles] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Goodman's Reduced Form for $R = -1$ ```mermaid flowchart LR A[General Goodman Equation
sigma_a divided by S sub e
plus sigma_m divided by Sut] --> B{Is sigma_m = 0 ?} B -- Yes, Reversed Bending --> C[Simplified Form
n = S sub e divided by sigma_a] B -- No --> D[Full Goodman Line Required] C --> E[Compute Kf modified sigma_a] E --> F[Final Factor of Safety] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Description — Rotating Shaft with Transverse Load > A **horizontal shaft of diameter $d$** is supported in two **pillow-block bearings**, with a **pulley / gear** at mid-span carrying a downward stationary force $P$. The bending moment diagram is a single-peak triangle with apex $M = PL/4$ at the centre. As the shaft rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, the upper fibre spends half the time in tension and half in compression, producing **fully reversed bending** ($R = -1$) at every surface point. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask you to identify whether a given component experiences *reversed*, *fluctuating*, or *repeated* loading, and to draw a labelled bending-moment / stress-cycle sketch. Memorise the $R = -1$ signature and the corresponding $S-N$ plateau region. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define *reversed bending* in the context of fluctuating stresses. State the value of the stress ratio $R$ and identify the mean-stress component. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Reversed bending is a type of fluctuating stress in which the bending stress at a point in a component alternates between equal tensile and compressive values over a complete load cycle. - **Stress ratio (1 mark):** $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max} = -1$ (fully reversed). - **Mean stress (1 mark):** $\sigma_m = 0$, so the loading is *purely alternating* with amplitude $\sigma_a = \sigma_{max}$. A rotating shaft under a stationary transverse load is the classical example. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between *reversed bending* and *repeated bending*. Give one practical example of each from an automotive subsystem. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Reversed Bending | Repeated Bending | |:-------|:-----------------|:-----------------| | Stress ratio $R$ | $-1$ (fully reversed) | $0$ (zero to maximum) | | Mean stress | $\sigma_m = 0$ | $\sigma_m = \sigma_a = \sigma_{max}/2$ | | Cycle | Tension → Compression → Tension | Zero → Tension → Zero | | Example | Rotating drive shaft of a car | Leaf spring of a car suspension (one-sided deflection) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** > A **transmission shaft of diameter 50 mm** is supported between two bearings **400 mm apart** and carries a **gear at mid-span** transmitting a **tangential force of 6 kN** at a **pitch radius of 100 mm**. The shaft rotates at constant speed. The material has $S_{ut} = 720$ MPa and $S_{sy} = 480$ MPa. Modification factors are: $k_a = 0.82$, $k_b = 0.83$, $k_c = 1.0$, $k_d = 1.0$, $k_e = 0.897$, $k_f = 1.0$. The shoulder fillet at the gear has $K_t = 1.9$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.88$. > > **(a)** Determine whether the loading qualifies as *reversed bending* and compute the alternating bending stress at the fillet. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Calculate the modified endurance limit and the factor of safety. State whether the design is safe for infinite life if the required factor of safety is 2.0. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Bending moment at the gear (simply supported beam with central point load):** $$ M = \frac{P \cdot L}{4} = \frac{6000 \times 400}{4} = 6.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm} $$ **Step 2 — Nominal bending stress (solid circular cross-section):** $$ \sigma_{nom} = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^3} = \frac{32 \times 6.0 \times 10^5}{\pi \times 50^3} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^7}{\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^5} $$ $$ \sigma_{nom} = 48.96 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3 — Loading classification:** The shaft is **rotating** under a **stationary transverse load** generated by the gear. Every surface fibre alternates between tension and compression once per revolution. Hence, by definition, $R = -1$ and the loading is **fully reversed bending** with $\sigma_m = 0$. **Step 4 — Fatigue stress concentration factor:** $$ K_f = 1 + q (K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.88 (1.9 - 1) = 1 + 0.792 = 1.792 $$ **Step 5 — Local alternating stress at the fillet:** $$ \sigma_a = K_f \cdot \sigma_{nom} = 1.792 \times 48.96 = 87.74 \text{ MPa} $$ **Result (a):** Alternating stress at the fillet $\sigma_a \approx \mathbf{87.74 \text{ MPa}}$, loading is fully reversed. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Unmodified endurance limit (steel, $S_{ut} = 720$ MPa $\le 1400$ MPa):** $$ S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 720 = 360 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Apply modification factors:** $$ S_e = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, k_f \, S_e' $$ $$ S_e = 0.82 \times 0.83 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 360 $$ $$ S_e = 0.82 \times 0.83 \times 0.897 \times 360 $$ $$ S_e = 0.6108 \times 360 \approx 219.9 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3 — Factor of safety under reversed bending:** Since $\sigma_m = 0$, the Goodman relation simplifies to: $$ n = \frac{S_e}{\sigma_a} = \frac{219.9}{87.74} \approx 2.506 $$ **Step 4 — Design verdict:** $$ n = 2.506 \ge n_{req} = 2.0 \quad \checkmark $$ The shaft has **infinite life** under reversed-bending fatigue for the given duty cycle. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert gear tangential force to bending moment** using $M = P \times L/4$ for a simply supported central load. Marks are lost when the bending moment is incorrectly used as $P \times L$ (cantilever form). Also, do **not omit the $K_f$ factor** when a fillet is specified — the local stress at the fillet is the design stress, not the nominal value. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** > A **cantilever leaf spring of a light commercial vehicle** has an **unsupported length of 350 mm** and is loaded by a **cyclic vertical force alternating between $+4$ kN (up) and $-4$ kN (down)** at the free end. The cross-section is **rectangular, 60 mm wide × 12 mm thick**. Material: $S_{ut} = 540$ MPa. Modification factors: $k_a = 0.90$, $k_b = 0.85$ (size factor for rectangular section), $k_c = 1.0$, $k_d = 1.0$, $k_e = 0.814$ (99 % reliability), $k_f = 1.0$. Spring has no stress raiser ($K_f = 1$). > > **(a)** Justify whether the spring undergoes reversed bending. Compute the maximum bending stress at the root. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Find the factor of safety in fatigue and comment on the design. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Loading identification:** The cyclic force varies from $+P$ to $-P$, with $P = 4$ kN. Hence $F_{max} = +4$ kN and $F_{min} = -4$ kN. The bending moment at the root alternates between: $$ M_{max} = +P \cdot L = +4 \times 10^3 \times 350 = +1.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} $$ $$ M_{min} = -P \cdot L = -1.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} $$ Therefore $M_{max} = -M_{min}$, satisfying the reversed-bending identity. **Step 2 — Section modulus of rectangular section:** $$ Z = \frac{b h^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times 12^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times 144}{6} = 1440 \text{ mm}^3 $$ **Step 3 — Maximum bending stress:** $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^6}{1440} = 972.2 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \sigma_{min} = -972.2 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 4 — Classification:** Stress ratio $R = -972.2 / 972.2 = -1$, mean $\sigma_m = 0$, amplitude $\sigma_a = 972.2$ MPa. **Result (a):** The spring is under **reversed bending** with $\sigma_{max} = 972.2$ MPa at the root. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Unmodified endurance limit:** For steel with $S_{ut} = 540$ MPa $\le 1400$ MPa: $$ S_e' = 0.5 \times 540 = 270 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Modified endurance limit:** $$ S_e = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.814 \times 1.0 \times 270 $$ $$ S_e = 0.6224 \times 270 \approx 168.0 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3 — Factor of safety:** With $K_f = 1$: $$ n = \frac{S_e}{\sigma_a} = \frac{168.0}{972.2} \approx 0.173 $$ **Step 4 — Design verdict:** $n = 0.173 \ll 1.0$, indicating **immediate static failure** — the bending stress (972 MPa) far exceeds even the ultimate tensile strength of the material (540 MPa). The proposed spring geometry is **grossly undersized**. Redesign is required: increase the thickness $h$ (since $Z \propto h^2$) or use a higher-strength material. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** For rectangular sections, students commonly confuse $b$ (width) and $h$ (depth in bending plane) in the section-modulus formula $Z = bh^2/6$. The depth $h$ is **squared**, so a small misread (using 12 mm as width and 60 mm as depth) flips the answer by a factor of 25. Always re-read the problem: "60 mm wide × 12 mm thick" means $b = 60$, $h = 12$ in this context because the leaf is bent about its strong axis. Confirm with the orientation of the applied load. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Reversed bending ⇒ $R = -1$ ⇒ $\sigma_m = 0$ ⇒ $\sigma_a = \sigma_{max}$**. Memorise this identity. - **Rotating shaft under stationary transverse load** is the canonical source of reversed bending. - **Goodman criterion reduces to $n = S_e / \sigma_a$** — no mean-stress correction needed. - **Always apply $K_f$ for fillets, keyways, holes** before comparing to $S_e$. - **Unmodified endurance limit:** $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for steels ($S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa); $S_e' = 700$ MPa for higher-strength steels; $S_e' = 0.4 S_{ut}$ for aluminium. - **Six modification factors** ($k_a$ surface, $k_b$ size, $k_c$ load, $k_d$ temperature, $k_e$ reliability, $k_f$ miscellaneous) multiply $S_e'$. - **Factor of safety** for infinite life: $n \ge 1.5$ – 2.5 (automotive convention: typically 2.0). - **Flexure formula** for solid round shaft: $\sigma = 32 M / (\pi d^3)$ — used in **every** KTU numerical. - **Design equation** (solve for $d$): $d = [32 n_{req} K_f M_a / (\pi S_e)]^{1/3}$. - **Mistakes to avoid:** mixing units (N vs kN, mm vs m); forgetting $K_f$; misclassifying $R = 0$ as reversed; using $S_y$ instead of $S_e$ for fatigue check. - **In examinations**, always draw a labelled bending-moment diagram and explicitly write the loading classification ($R = -1$ statement) before computing stresses — this is how the first 2–3 marks are banked.

topics.title: Suggestions - Problem Project: Students can explore innovative approaches to design processes, integrating preferred numbers, standards, and codes into practical design solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Suggestions - Problem Project: Students can explore innovative approaches to design processes, integrating preferred numbers, standards, and codes into practical design solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the context of **PBAUT604 – Automotive Component Design (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the **Design Process** refers to the systematic, iterative engineering methodology used to transform a functional requirement into a fully specified, manufacturable, and reliable automotive component. A *Problem Project* in Module 1 invites students to embed three pillars of design rationalization into this process:

1. **Preferred Numbers** – Mathematically generated series (e.g., **R5, R10, R20, R40 Renard Series**, and **E-series E3, E6, E12, E24, E48, E96, E192**) that constrain dimensions to a finite, geometrically spaced set to reduce tooling variety.
2. **Standards** – Documented technical agreements such as **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**, **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, **SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers)**, **DIN (Deutsches Institut für Normung)**, **JIS (Japanese Industrial Standards)**, and **AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association)** that govern tolerances, materials, fits, and testing.
3. **Codes** – Statutory or regulatory specifications (e.g., **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards) under CMVR**, **ECE Regulations**, **FMVSS**, and **Indian Boiler Regulations**) that ensure safety, emissions, and interoperability compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *"Design is the creation of a synthesis of solutions to a problem, expressed as a set of instructions, drawings, or specifications, constrained by preferred numbers, standards, and codes, so that the resulting artifact performs reliably, economically, and safely throughout its intended life."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview – The "Tailor's Measuring Tape" Analogy

Imagine a tailor who has only **four buttons in his kit**: small (10 mm), medium (14 mm), large (20 mm), and extra-large (28 mm). He never creates a button of 13 mm or 22 mm because each size requires a unique buttonhole die, increasing cost. These four sizes — spaced in a geometric progression of ratio $\sqrt[4]{10} \approx 1.778$ — are his **preferred numbers**.

Similarly, an automotive engineer designing a **shaft diameter** will rarely specify 23.7 mm. Instead, the designer chooses from a *predefined, geometrically spaced list* (R10: … 20, 25, 31.5, 40 …) so that:
- The factory keeps **fewer drill bits, fewer bearings, fewer bar stock sizes**.
- Replacement parts across model variants remain **interchangeable**.
- Inventory, tooling, and Quality Control costs drop dramatically.

**Standards** are like the tailor's *measuring tape and sewing rulebook* — they tell you that a 25 mm button fits a 25.2 ± 0.1 mm buttonhole (a fit class). **Codes** are like the *fire-safety rule* that says shirts must be made of flame-retardant fabric for children's wear — non-negotiable legal compliance.

### 1.3 Why This Integration Matters – KTU 2024 Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Non-Negotiable Outcomes of Integrating Preferred Numbers, Standards, and Codes:**
> 1. **Interchangeability** – Any component from any batch fits any mating part.
> 2. **Manufacturability** – The design is producible on existing machines with available tools.
> 3. **Legal & Safety Compliance** – The product clears homologation under Indian / international regulations.

The **Unit Cost** of an automotive component is governed by:

$$
C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{machining} + C_{tooling} + C_{inspection} + C_{rework}
$$

By choosing **preferred numbers**, the designer slashes $C_{tooling}$ and $C_{inspection}$. By adhering to **standards**, $C_{rework}$ falls. By satisfying **codes**, the company avoids penalties and recalls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Iterative Engineering Design Process

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats design as a **closed-loop, iterative cycle**. Below is the operational logic decomposed into 10 stages:

1. **Recognition of Need** – Identify the gap (e.g., need a lighter brake disc).
2. **Problem Definition** – Convert need into a quantifiable specification (e.g., "Reduce unsprung mass by **15 %** while maintaining thermal capacity ≥ **350 kJ**").
3. **Synthesis** – Generate conceptual solutions (cast iron disc → carbon-ceramic disc → ventilated disc).
4. **Analysis & Optimization** – Apply FEA, fatigue, thermal analysis.
5. **Selection of Preferred Numbers** – Choose key dimensions from R-series / E-series.
6. **Standardization** – Apply ISO 286 (tolerances), ISO 1101 (GD&T), SAE J514 (hydraulic fittings).
7. **Code Compliance Check** – Verify against AIS-052 (brake systems), ECE R13, FMVSS 105.
8. **Detailed Drafting** – Produce manufacturing drawings with GD&T.
9. **Prototype & Testing** – Build, instrument, validate.
10. **Production Release & Feedback** – Close the loop with field data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Stages 5, 6, and 7 — *Preferred Numbers, Standards, and Codes* — are the **rationalization gates**. Skipping them is the single most common reason design projects receive poor evaluation in PBAUT604.

### 2.2 Preferred Number Systems – Mathematical Foundation

A **preferred number** is a value chosen from a sequence such that every other permissible value is obtained by multiplying or dividing by a constant factor.

#### 2.2.1 Renard Series (R-Series)

For an R$n$ series, the common ratio is:

$$
q_n = 10^{1/n}
$$

The series spans from **1 to 10**, and any value above 10 is obtained by multiplying by powers of 10. The general $k$-th term is:

$$
R_n(k) = 10^{\,k/n}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

**Example values for R5** ($q_5 = 10^{1/5} \approx 1.5849$): **1.00, 1.58, 2.51, 3.98, 6.31, 10.0**

#### 2.2.2 E-Series (IEC 60063)

Used primarily in **electronics (resistors, capacitors)** but applicable to fastener sizes and small-dimension automotive parts. The ratio is:

$$
q_E = 10^{1/N}
$$

where $N \in \{3, 6, 12, 24, 48, 96, 192\}$.

#### 2.2.3 Effect of Tolerance on Series Selection

For a preferred number with manufacturing tolerance $\pm t\,\%$, two adjacent terms must not overlap. The geometric ratio $q$ must satisfy:

$$
q \geq 1 + \frac{2t}{100}
$$

For a **±5 %** tolerance, $q \geq 1.10$, hence the **E24** series ($q_{24} = 10^{1/24} \approx 1.1007$) is the minimum sufficient choice.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are examinable in **KTU ESE** (End Semester Examination) under Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Boundary / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Renard ratio | $q_n = 10^{1/n}$ | dimensionless | $n = 5, 10, 20, 40, 80$ |
| R$n$ general term | $R_n(k) = 10^{k/n}$ | same as base unit | $k = 0, 1, \dots, n$ |
| E-series ratio | $q_E = 10^{1/N}$ | dimensionless | $N = 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, 96, 192$ |
| Tolerance-series linkage | $q \geq 1 + 2t/100$ | dimensionless | determines min $N$ for tolerance $t$ |
| Basic size range | $D = d \cdot 10^{m}$ | mm | $m \in \mathbb{Z}$, $d \in [1, 10)$ |
| Fundamental deviation (shaft) | $es$ or $ei$ | $\mu m$ | ISO 286 table |
| Tolerance grade | IT01 … IT18 | $\mu m$ | $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$ |
| Tolerance unit ($i$) | $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | $\mu m$ | $D$ in mm, for $D \le 500$ mm |
| Standard tolerance | $T = a \cdot i$ | $\mu m$ | $a \in \{10, 16, 25, \dots\}$ (IT grade) |
| Hole-shaft system fit | $N_{\max} \le D_{\min}$ (clearance) | mm | ISO 286 fit classes |
| Number of preferred sizes in decade | $n$ | — | always finite by definition |
| Geometric mean of series | $\sqrt{R_n(k) \cdot R_n(k+1)}$ | base unit | equals midpoint in log-space |
| Stress concentration factor | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ | dimensionless | Peterson's formula |
| Factor of safety | $n = S_{yield}/\sigma_{working}$ | dimensionless | KTU design convention $n \ge 1.5$ (static) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to *"select a preferred number for a shaft"* or *"justify the choice of standard"*, always state (i) the chosen series, (ii) the ratio, (iii) the tolerance compatibility, and (iv) the standard applied. Partial answers without all four lose 1–2 marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Volkswagen Group** uses an internal preferred-number table derived from R10/R20 for chassis fasteners.
- **Maruti Suzuki** follows **AIS standards** for India-market vehicles, layered on **ECE Regulations** for export models.
- **ISO 261** (metric screw thread – general plan) selects thread diameters from R20 and pitches from R10.
- **AGMA 2001** (gear tooth proportions) uses preferred-number-derived pressure angles and addendum coefficients.
- **SAE J403** (chemical composition of carbon steels) uses preferred percentage bands (0.10 %, 0.15 %, 0.20 % C …).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Generation of R10 Series from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** Derive the R10 preferred-number sequence covering 1 to 10 and show that any geometrically larger decade (e.g., 10–100) is obtained by simple multiplication by 10.

**Step 1 – Define the geometric series.** We require $n = 10$ terms spanning a ratio of $10:1$. Let the first term be $a_0 = 1$ and the last term be $a_n = 10$. With common ratio $q$,

$$
a_n = a_0 \cdot q^n
$$

Substituting $a_n = 10$, $a_0 = 1$, $n = 10$:

$$
10 = 1 \cdot q^{10}
$$

**Step 2 – Solve for $q$.**

$$
q^{10} = 10
$$

$$
q = 10^{1/10}
$$

Evaluating numerically:

$$
q = 10^{0.1} \approx 1.258925
$$

**Step 3 – Generate the series.** The $k$-th term is:

$$
R_{10}(k) = 10^{k/10}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 10
$$

Computing each term explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{10}(0) &= 10^{0/10}  = 1.0000 \\
R_{10}(1) &= 10^{1/10}  = 1.2589 \\
R_{10}(2) &= 10^{2/10}  = 1.5849 \\
R_{10}(3) &= 10^{3/10}  = 1.9953 \\
R_{10}(4) &= 10^{4/10}  = 2.5119 \\
R_{10}(5) &= 10^{5/10}  = 3.1623 \\
R_{10}(6) &= 10^{6/10}  = 3.9811 \\
R_{10}(7) &= 10^{7/10}  = 5.0119 \\
R_{10}(8) &= 10^{8/10}  = 6.3096 \\
R_{10}(9) &= 10^{9/10}  = 7.9433 \\
R_{10}(10) &= 10^{10/10} = 10.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Extend to the 10–100 decade.** Every term in the next decade is obtained by multiplying by 10:

$$
10, 12.589, 15.849, 19.953, 25.119, 31.623, 39.811, 50.119, 63.096, 79.433, 100
$$

After rounding to two significant figures (a KTU-accepted convention): **10, 12.5, 16, 20, 25, 31.5, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100**.

**Conclusion:** The R10 series generates a finite, geometrically spaced, logarithmic progression. Its **logarithmic uniformity** ensures that the *percentage* jump between consecutive terms is constant (≈ 25.9 %), giving the designer a perceptually uniform set of choices.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Minimum Preferred-Series Size for a Given Tolerance

**Problem Statement:** A designer wishes to specify a resistor (used in an automotive ECU pull-up network) with a manufacturing tolerance of $\pm 2\%$. Determine the minimum E-series required such that adjacent values do not overlap.

**Step 1 – Formulate the no-overlap condition.** Two adjacent preferred values $a$ and $a \cdot q$ must be separated by at least the combined tolerance spread:

$$
a \cdot q - a \ge a \cdot \frac{t_{+}}{100} + a \cdot q \cdot \frac{t_{-}}{100}
$$

For symmetric tolerance $t_{+} = t_{-} = t$:

$$
a (q - 1) \ge \frac{2at}{100}
$$

Dividing through by $a$ (positive):

$$
q - 1 \ge \frac{2t}{100}
$$

**Step 2 – Solve for the minimum ratio $q_{\min}$.** With $t = 2\,\%$:

$$
q_{\min} \ge 1 + \frac{2 \times 2}{100} = 1.04
$$

**Step 3 – Translate to the E-series.** The E$N$ series has ratio $q_E = 10^{1/N}$. Solve:

$$
10^{1/N} \ge 1.04
$$

Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
\frac{\ln 10}{N} \ge \ln 1.04
$$

$$
N \le \frac{\ln 10}{\ln 1.04} = \frac{2.302585}{0.039221} \approx 58.71
$$

Hence $N \le 58.71$. The smallest standard E-series satisfying this is **E48** (since E48 has $N = 48 \le 58.71$ but the next-lower standard E24 has $N = 24$, which violates the inequality for the $\pm 2\,\%$ requirement — verified below).

Check E24: $q_{24} = 10^{1/24} \approx 1.1007$. Then $q - 1 = 0.1007$, which is far larger than $0.04$, so E24 also satisfies the condition. We must therefore test the *opposite direction*: which is the largest $N$ still valid? $N \le 58.71$. The largest standard E-series below this is **E48**. However, since E48 already satisfies, and E96 would be the next standard, let us verify E96:

Check E96: $q_{96} = 10^{1/96} \approx 1.0244$. Then $q - 1 = 0.0244 < 0.04$. **E96 violates the ±2 % tolerance overlap condition.**

Therefore the minimum standard E-series is **E48**, and E96 (with ratio 1.0244) does not provide sufficient separation.

**Conclusion:** Use the **E48 series** for $\pm 2\,\%$ resistor tolerance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why **1 % resistors use E96** and **0.1 % precision resistors use E192**. E96 would imply a tolerance requirement of at most $\pm 1.22\,\%$, hence its common pairing with 1 % components.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – ISO 286 Tolerance Unit for a Shaft Diameter

**Problem Statement:** For a shaft of nominal diameter $D = 50$ mm, calculate the tolerance unit $i$ and the standard tolerance for IT7.

**Step 1 – Recall the tolerance unit formula.**

$$
i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D
$$

with $D$ in mm and $i$ in $\mu m$.

**Step 2 – Substitute $D = 50$ mm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{1/3} &= 50^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{50} \approx 3.6840 \\
0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} &= 0.45 \times 3.6840 = 1.6578 \\
0.001 \cdot D &= 0.001 \times 50 = 0.0500 \\
i &= 1.6578 + 0.0500 = 1.7078 \;\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Apply IT7 multiplier.** From ISO 286, IT7 has $a = 16$ (multiplier relative to $i$):

$$
T_{IT7} = a \cdot i = 16 \times 1.7078 = 27.32 \;\mu m
$$

Round to the standard ISO table value: **T(IT7) for 50 mm = 25 $\mu m$** (the slight discrepancy is because ISO 286 rounds to preferred values).

**Conclusion:** For a 50 mm shaft at IT7 grade, the total tolerance band is **25 $\mu m** (i.e., ± 12.5 $\mu m$ symmetric, or as defined by fundamental deviation).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Choosing a Preferred Number for a Component

The matrix below summarizes the decision logic for selecting a preferred-number series based on the engineering constraints typically encountered in PBAUT604 design projects.

| Application / Component | Recommended Series | Tolerance Required | Governing Standard | Sample Preferred Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shaft diameter (general) | R10 (or R20) | IT6 – IT8 | ISO 286 | 25 mm, 31.5 mm, 40 mm |
| Ball / roller bearing bore | R10 | IT5 – IT7 | ISO 15, ISO 492 | 20, 25, 30, 35 mm |
| Bolt / screw (M-series) | R20 | IT6 – IT8 | ISO 261, ISO 898 | M6, M8, M10, M12 |
| Sheet metal thickness | R20 | ±10 % | ISO 9444 | 1.0, 1.25, 1.6, 2.0 mm |
| Resistor (general) | E24 | ±5 % | IEC 60063 | 1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8 k$\Omega$ |
| Resistor (precision) | E96 | ±1 % | IEC 60063 | 1.00, 1.02, 1.05, 1.07 k$\Omega$ |
| Pipe outer diameter | R10 | IT11 | ISO 65, IS 1239 | 17.2, 21.3, 26.7, 33.4 mm |
| Spring wire diameter | R20 | ±2 % | IS 4454 (Pt 1) | 0.5, 0.63, 0.8, 1.0 mm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram – The Integrated Design Process

The diagram below maps the interactions between **preferred numbers, standards, and codes** within the engineering design workflow. This is rendered using Mermaid with strict alphanumeric node IDs and clean labels (no markdown formatting inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Problem Definition] --> stepB[Functional Specification]
    stepB --> stepC[Concept Generation]
    stepC --> stepD[Selection of Preferred Numbers]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply ISO and BIS Standards]
    stepE --> stepF[Apply AIS and ECE Codes]
    stepF --> stepG[Detailed Drafting with GD&T]
    stepG --> stepH[Stress and Tolerance Analysis]
    stepH --> stepI{Meets All Criteria?}
    stepI -- No --> stepC
    stepI -- Yes --> stepJ[Prototype Fabrication]
    stepJ --> stepK[Testing and Validation]
    stepK --> stepL[Homologation Submission]
    stepL --> stepM[Production Release]
    stepM --> stepN[Field Data Feedback]
    stepN --> stepA

    sideA[Renard Series R5 R10 R20 R40] -. feeds .-> stepD
    sideB[ISO 286 ISO 1101 SAE J403 AGMA 2001] -. feeds .-> stepE
    sideC[AIS 052 ECE R13 CMVR FMVSS 105] -. feeds .-> stepF

    classDef gate fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#8a6d00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class stepD,stepE,stepF gate
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **three yellow boxes** (`stepD`, `stepE`, `stepF`) represent the *rationalization gates* where preferred numbers, standards, and codes are injected.
- The **dashed side-paths** (`sideA`, `sideB`, `sideC`) indicate external reference libraries the designer consults.
- The **loop back from `stepN` (field data) to `stepA` (problem definition)** illustrates the *closed-loop, iterative* nature mandated by KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram – Preferred Number Selection Logic

The diagram below formalizes the decision tree a KTU student should follow when choosing a preferred number for a given dimensional constraint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reqA[Identify Nominal Dimension] --> reqB{Manufacturing Tolerance Known?}
    reqB -- No --> reqC[Default to plus or minus 1 percent]
    reqB -- Yes --> reqD[Compute Minimum Ratio q_min equals 1 plus 2t over 100]
    reqC --> reqD
    reqD --> reqE{N is less than or equal to ln10 over lnq_min}
    reqE --> reqF{Select smallest standard E series satisfying N}
    reqF --> reqG{Component is Mechanical or Electrical?}
    reqG -- Mechanical --> reqH[Choose from R5 R10 R20 R40 Renard]
    reqG -- Electrical --> reqI[Choose from E3 E6 E12 E24 E48 E96 E192]
    reqH --> reqJ[Round to Two Significant Figures per ISO 497]
    reqI --> reqJ
    reqJ --> reqK[Verify Against Relevant Standard]
    reqK --> reqL[Final Preferred Number Selected]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram – Hierarchy of Constraints

This schematic clarifies the *hierarchy* the designer must respect. **Codes** (legal safety) sit at the top; **standards** (interchangeability) in the middle; **preferred numbers** (rationalization) at the base.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lvlA[Codes - Legal and Safety - AIS ECE CMVR FMVSS] --> lvlB[Standards - Interchangeability - ISO BIS SAE AGMA DIN]
    lvlB --> lvlC[Preferred Numbers - Rationalization - Renard Series R5 to R40 and E Series E3 to E192]
    lvlC --> lvlD[Free Dimension Choice - Last Resort Only]

    classDef topLayer fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef midLayer fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#003380,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lowLayer fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class lvlA topLayer
    class lvlB midLayer
    class lvlC lowLayer
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule:** Always check **codes first** (e.g., AIS-052 mandates minimum brake disc diameter for a vehicle class). Only then select a **standard** (e.g., ISO 286 tolerance grade). Only then pick a **preferred number**. Choosing the preferred number first and *then* trying to retrofit a code is a common project-evaluation mistake in KTU PBAUT604.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

*Define a "preferred number" and explain why R10 series is preferred over R5 series for shaft diameters in an automotive gearbox.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A preferred number is a value selected from a finite, geometrically spaced sequence such that any permissible dimension is obtained by multiplying or dividing by a constant ratio. The R$n$ series uses the ratio $q_n = 10^{1/n}$.

The **R10 series** ($q_{10} \approx 1.259$, ten terms per decade) is preferred over R5 ($q_5 \approx 1.585$, five terms per decade) for shaft diameters because:
1. **Finer selection** — R10 offers 10 sizes per decade versus 5, allowing closer matches to functional requirements.
2. **Interchangeability** — Common mating parts (bearings, gears, seals) are catalogued at R10 sizes, so designers avoid costly custom reworks.
3. **Tolerance compatibility** — R10 spacing (~26 %) accommodates IT6 to IT9 tolerances comfortably without adjacent-size ambiguity.

*(Word limit: ~80 words; expected KTU scoring: definition 1 mark + two reasons 1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

*State the function of any two Indian Standards relevant to automotive component design. Mention the issuing authority for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **IS 919-2 (ISO 261 equivalent)** – *Recommendations for metric screw threads – General plan.* Issued by **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. Governs preferred thread diameters (M-series) for fasteners used in chassis, engine, and transmission assemblies, ensuring global interchangeability.

2. **AIS-052 (Automotive Industry Standard 052)** – *Automotive Vehicles – Brake Systems.* Issued under the **CMVR-TAP (Central Motor Vehicles Rules – Technical Approval Procedure)** by the Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI). Mandates minimum braking efficiency, stopping distance, and friction-lining performance.

*(Each standard: 1 mark; 1 mark reserved for issuing authority. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with an internal choice. The sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Sub-part (a) often requires a derivation or design selection; sub-part (b) requires a justification or analysis.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]**

**(a)** *For an automotive transmission countershaft, the nominal diameter has been calculated as **38.7 mm** under torque and deflection limits. Select an appropriate preferred number from the R10 and R20 series. Justify your choice using the tolerance-overlap condition and the relevant ISO standard.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Briefly describe how the SAE standard **SAE J403** and the Indian Standard **IS 2062** together govern material selection for an automotive connecting rod. Explain why both are referenced in a typical PBAUT604 design project.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Step-by-Step:**

Step 1: Identify the nominal dimension $D = 38.7$ mm.

Step 2: The nearest R10 values around 38.7 mm are **31.5 mm** and **40 mm**. From R20, additional candidates are **35.5 mm**, **37.5 mm**, and **40 mm**.

Step 3: The R20 ratio is $q_{20} = 10^{1/20} \approx 1.1220$. The R10 ratio is $q_{10} \approx 1.2589$. With IT7 tolerance ($T \approx 25$ µm = 0.025 mm at 40 mm), the spacing between adjacent R20 values near 40 mm is:

$$
40 \times (1.1220 - 1) = 40 \times 0.1220 = 4.88 \;\text{mm}
$$

Since the tolerance (0.025 mm) is much smaller than the spacing (4.88 mm), R20 provides ample tolerance-overlap safety.

Step 4: The closest R20 preferred number to 38.7 mm is **37.5 mm** (error –3.1 %) or **40 mm** (error +3.4 %). For a countershaft, designers prefer **upward rounding** to ensure strength margin. Therefore the preferred choice is **40 mm (R20)**.

Step 5: Verify against **ISO 286**: for $D = 40$ mm, IT7 tolerance = **25 µm**, IT6 = **16 µm**. Both are available — ISO 286 explicitly lists preferred sizes; 40 mm is one of them.

**Conclusion:** Select **40 mm**, justified by R20, IT7, and ISO 286 alignment.

**Part (b) – Step-by-Step:**

**SAE J403** — *Chemical Compositions of SAE Carbon Steels.* Issued by the Society of Automotive Engineers. It defines the chemical composition ranges (e.g., SAE 1045: 0.43–0.50 % C, 0.60–0.90 % Mn) for plain-carbon steels used in automotive components.

**IS 2062** — *Hot Rolled Medium and High Tensile Structural Steel.* Issued by BIS. It defines mechanical properties (yield strength, tensile strength, elongation) and chemical limits for structural-quality steel plates and sections used in Indian engineering applications.

For a **connecting rod**:
- **SAE J403** fixes the **chemistry** (e.g., SAE 1041 or 1141 for medium-carbon forged rods).
- **IS 2062** specifies the **mechanical performance** required for structural reliability (yield strength ≥ 250 MPa for Grade A; ≥ 410 MPa for Grade E).
- **Both are referenced** because SAE J403 is internationally recognized (good for export designs), while IS 2062 is mandatory for the Indian domestic market under BIS certification. A PBAUT604 project located in Kerala and intended for both local assembly and global supply must satisfy both layers.

**Conclusion:** SAE J403 = chemistry; IS 2062 = mechanical property. Combined, they produce a fully traceable, globally compliant, and locally certified material callout.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper – Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** *A designer is selecting a resistor for an automotive ECU pull-up circuit. The desired resistance value is **4.7 k$\Omega$** with a manufacturing tolerance of **$\pm 5\,\%$**. Determine the minimum preferred E-series that avoids tolerance overlap between adjacent values, and verify whether **E24** is sufficient.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain with a block diagram how codes (e.g., AIS), standards (e.g., ISO), and preferred numbers (e.g., R20) interact during the design of an automotive **stub axle**. Why is it considered poor practice to choose preferred numbers before checking code compliance?* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Step-by-Step:**

Step 1: The tolerance-overlap condition is:

$$
q \ge 1 + \frac{2t}{100}
$$

Substituting $t = 5\,\%$:

$$
q_{\min} \ge 1 + \frac{2 \times 5}{100} = 1.10
$$

Step 2: Solve for the maximum allowable $N$ in the E-series ($q_E = 10^{1/N}$):

$$
10^{1/N} \ge 1.10
$$

Taking natural log:

$$
\frac{\ln 10}{N} \ge \ln 1.10
$$

$$
N \le \frac{\ln 10}{\ln 1.10} = \frac{2.3026}{0.0953} \approx 24.16
$$

Step 3: The largest standard E-series with $N \le 24.16$ is **E24** ($N = 24$). Therefore **E24 is the largest series that avoids overlap**.

Step 4: Verify that E24 is sufficient (not over-engineered). Check the gap for $N = 24$:

$$
q_{24} = 10^{1/24} \approx 1.1007
$$

Since $1.1007 > 1.10$, E24 satisfies the no-overlap condition with a small safety margin (0.0007 in ratio).

Step 5: Verify that E48 ($N = 48$) would **violate** the condition:

$$
q_{48} = 10^{1/48} \approx 1.0491
$$

Since $1.0491 < 1.10$, E48 does NOT avoid overlap at ±5 % tolerance.

**Conclusion:** Use **E24** for ±5 % tolerance. The value **4.7 k$\Omega$** is a valid E24 entry (next values: 4.3 k$\Omega$, 5.1 k$\Omega$).

**Part (b) – Block Diagram and Explanation:**

The interaction diagram follows the same architecture as Section 4.1, but specialised for the stub axle:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axleA[Stub Axle Functional Spec] --> axleB[Code Check AIS 053 Axle Standards]
    axleB --> axleC[Standard Selection ISO 3013 IS 8066 Axle Standards]
    axleC --> axleD[Preferred Number Selection R20 for Journal Diameter and R10 for Track Width]
    axleD --> axleE[Detailed Drafting with GD&T per ISO 1101]
    axleE --> axleF[Fatigue and Stress Analysis]
    axleF --> axleG{Meets AIS 053 Fatigue and Load Criteria?}
    axleG -- No --> axleC
    axleG -- Yes --> axleH[Approved Stub Axle Drawing]
```

**Explanation of Poor-Practice Concern:**

Choosing preferred numbers *before* verifying code compliance leads to:
1. **Wasted Effort** — The designer may commit to a preferred size (e.g., 28 mm journal from R10) only to discover AIS-053 mandates a minimum 30 mm for the vehicle class.
2. **Re-tracing** — Every change cascades into new drawings, FEA, and tool orders, inflating project time by 30–60 %.
3. **Code Conflict** — A preferred number that violates a *minimum legal dimension* (e.g., brake-disc diameter per AIS-052) cannot be deployed, regardless of standardization elegance.
4. **Evaluator Penalty** — In KTU PBAUT604 valuation, projects are explicitly marked down for a *non-systematic workflow* that begins with rationalization rather than compliance.

**Correct Order:** **Code → Standard → Preferred Number → Drafting**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PBAUT604 Module 1 Projects:**
>
> 1. **Omitting the ISO/BIS reference** — Stating "we chose 40 mm" without writing "from R20, per ISO 497" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **No tolerance-overlap check** — Failing to verify $q \ge 1 + 2t/100$ loses 2 marks in any preferred-number derivation.
> 3. **Confusing R-series with E-series** — Using R20 for an electrical resistor loses 1 mark; R-series is for mechanical, E-series for electrical.
> 4. **Misidentifying the issuing authority** — Writing "AIS is issued by ISO" or "IS 2062 is an SAE standard" loses 1 mark per error.
> 5. **Selecting a preferred number before checking codes** — For components under AIS / ECE regulation, the preferred number must be subordinate to the legal minimum. Evaluators specifically check this hierarchy.
> 6. **Skipping units** — Writing "$i = 1.7078$" without **µm** loses 0.5 marks.
> 7. **Single-decade reasoning** — Forgetting that R$n$ spans 1 to 10 only, and other decades are obtained by $\times 10^m$, loses 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist – Module 1 Project Integration:**
>
> - **Preferred Number Definition:** A geometrically spaced value chosen so that any other value is obtained by $\times q^n$. The ratio for R$n$ is $q_n = 10^{1/n}$; for E$N$ it is $q_E = 10^{1/N}$.
> - **R-Series Uses:** Mechanical — shaft diameters, gear modules, sheet thickness, thread pitches.
> - **E-Series Uses:** Electrical — resistor values, capacitor values, fuse ratings.
> - **Tolerance-Series Linkage:** Always verify $q \ge 1 + 2t/100$ before selecting a series.
> - **ISO 286 Tolerance Unit:** $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$ µm (for $D \le 500$ mm).
> - **Standard Tolerance:** $T_{ITk} = a_k \cdot i$ where $a_k \in \{10, 16, 25, 40, 64, 100, 160, 250, 400, 640, \ldots\}$.
> - **BIS Issuing Authority:** Bureau of Indian Standards (formerly ISI).
> - **SAE J403:** Chemistry of carbon steels.
> - **IS 2062:** Mechanical properties of structural steel (Indian context).
> - **AIS:** Automotive Industry Standards (India), under CMVR via ARAI.
> - **ECE Regulations:** United Nations Economic Commission for Europe — global automotive type-approval.
> - **Hierarchy of Constraints:** **Code > Standard > Preferred Number > Free Dimension.**
> - **Project Workflow:** Need → Definition → Synthesis → Preferred Number → Standard → Code → Draft → Prototype → Test → Release.
> - **Two-Significant-Figure Rule (ISO 497):** Preferred numbers are conventionally rounded to two significant figures (e.g., $10^{2/10} = 1.58$, not 1.5849).
> - **Decade Extension:** Preferred numbers repeat every decade via multiplication by $10^m$ ($m \in \mathbb{Z}$).
> - **Mark-Saver Lines:** Always state (1) the chosen series, (2) the ratio, (3) the tolerance compatibility, (4) the applied standard, and (5) the issuing authority — *in that order* — in any 7- or 14-mark question.
> - **Iterative Nature:** Design never ends with one cycle; field data feeds back into the next problem definition.
> - **Tools Used in PBAUT604:** FEA (ANSYS / SolidWorks Simulation), drafting (AutoCAD / CATIA), tolerance stack-up (CETOL / Tolerance Analyzer), and standard databases (BIS CARE, ISO Online).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling Contact Bearing - Design of Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rolling Contact Bearing - Design of Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **DEFINITION (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Rolling Contact Bearing** is a machine element designed to constrain relative motion and reduce friction between moving parts by transferring loads through rolling elements (balls or rollers) positioned between two races (inner and outer). The design of such bearings involves the selection of an appropriate bearing type and size based on load, speed, life, and reliability requirements as standardized by **ISO 15** and **IS 6455**.

### Conceptual Analogy (The "Marble Run" Intuition)

Imagine you are rolling a heavy **wooden log** along the ground. You have to fight enormous friction because the entire surface is dragging. Now, picture sliding a row of **marbles** between the log and the ground. Suddenly, friction nearly vanishes — the log rolls effortlessly over the marbles. 

A **rolling contact bearing** works exactly like this:

- **Balls/Rollers** → the marbles (rolling elements).
- **Inner Race** → the log (mounted on the rotating shaft).
- **Outer Race** → the ground (mounted in the housing).
- **Cage/Retainer** → the spacers that prevent the marbles from colliding.

When the shaft rotates, the rolling elements spin instead of the surfaces **sliding** against each other, dropping the coefficient of friction from ~0.1 (sliding) to ~0.001 (rolling). Designing such a bearing means choosing the *right number, size, and type* of rolling elements so that the bearing survives a target life under a known load and speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this a "design" problem?** Unlike fasteners, bearings are **standardized bought-out items** (SKF, FAG, NTN, Timken). The designer does not invent the geometry — they must **select** the correct catalogue part number based on engineering calculations. This topic teaches that selection logic.

### Classification Snapshot

| Family | Sub-type | Typical KTU Application Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Ball Bearings** | Deep Groove (Conrad) | Electric motors, gearboxes |
| **Ball Bearings** | Angular Contact | Pump shafts, machine tool spindles |
| **Roller Bearings** | Cylindrical | Railway axles, gearboxes (high radial load) |
| **Roller Bearings** | Tapered | Automotive wheel hubs, differentials |
| **Roller Bearings** | Spherical (Self-aligning) | Misaligned shafts, conveyors |
| **Thrust Bearings** | Ball / Roller Thrust | Axial load-only applications |

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bold-Highlighted)

- **ISO Life Equation Exponent `p` = 3** for **ball bearings**.
- **ISO Life Equation Exponent `p` = 10/3 ≈ 3.333** for **roller bearings**.
- **Reference Life `L₁₀`** = the life (in millions of revolutions) at which **90%** of a statistically large bearing population survives (10% failure probability — the industry standard "rating life").
- **Viscosity of standard lubricating oil** at 70 °C ≈ **30 cSt** (typical reference value).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Design Philosophy

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats bearing design as a **two-step inverse problem**:

1. **Compute** the *required dynamic load rating* `C_required` from the operating conditions (load, speed, life).
2. **Compare** this `C_required` against manufacturer catalogues and **select** a bearing whose dynamic load rating `C ≥ C_required`.

The student must master both steps.

### 2.2 Why Bearings Fail — The Hertzian Contact Basis

When a ball presses against a race, the contact patch is not a point but a tiny **elliptical area** (Hertzian contact). The localized contact stress causes:

- **Subsurface fatigue** → micro-cracks initiate just below the surface → spalling (pitting) → vibration → failure.
- This is the **fundamental failure mode** that governs bearing life calculations.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU often asks "Why is `p = 10/3` used for roller bearings and `p = 3` for ball bearings?" The answer lies in Hertzian contact theory — the contact area shape (point vs. line) changes the stress-volume relationship, altering the exponent empirically.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the formulas below are **must-know, must-derive** for KTU University Exam questions. Memorize the symbols and units precisely.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Rating Life (millions of revolutions) | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | $L_{10}$ in millions of revs; $C$ = dynamic load rating (N); $P$ = equivalent dynamic radial load (N) |
| 2 | Life in hours (most common KTU form) | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | $N$ = speed in **rpm**; $L_{10h}$ in hours |
| 3 | Required Dynamic Load Rating | $C_{\text{req}} = P \left(\dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot 60\,N}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/p}$ | Reverse of Eq. 2 — used for selection |
| 4 | Equivalent Dynamic Radial Load (radial bearings) | $P = X\,F_{r} + Y\,F_{a}$ | $X,Y$ = radial & thrust factors from catalogue; $e$ = threshold ratio |
| 5 | Equivalent Dynamic Radial Load (angular contact bearings) | $P = F_{r}$ (for single-row deep groove in pure radial) | Or $P = X\,F_{r} + Y\,F_{a}$ depending on geometry |
| 6 | Equivalent Static Load | $P_{0} = X_{0}\,F_{r} + Y_{0}\,F_{a}$ | Used for static limit check |
| 7 | Static Load Safety Factor | $f_{s} = \dfrac{C_{0}}{P_{0}}$ | $f_{s} \geq 1.0$ for smooth operation; $\geq 1.5$ for shock loads |
| 8 | Modified Life (ISO 281 / Adjusted) | $L = a_{1}\,a_{2}\,a_{3}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | $a_{1}$ = reliability factor, $a_{2}$ = material factor, $a_{3}$ = operating conditions factor |
| 9 | Reliability Factor $a_{1}$ | $a_{1} = \left(\dfrac{\ln(1/R)}{\ln(1/0.9)}\right)^{1/p}$ | $R$ = required reliability (decimal); for $R = 0.9$, $a_1 = 1$ |
| 10 | Application / Shock Factor $K_{a}$ | $F_{\text{effective}} = K_{a} \cdot F_{\text{nominal}}$ | $K_{a} = 1.0$ (no shock) to $3.0$ (heavy shock) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive wheel hubs** use **tapered roller bearings** because they handle combined radial + thrust loads (cornering forces + vehicle weight).
- **Electric motors** use **deep groove ball bearings** because of low friction, high-speed capability, and low noise.
- **Gearboxes** use **cylindrical roller bearings** on intermediate shafts to handle high radial loads from gear meshing.
- **Helicopter rotor shafts** use **self-aligning spherical roller bearings** because the rotor flexes during flight, causing misalignment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Mental Checklist (always):**
> 1. Radial load $F_r$ and axial load $F_a$?
> 2. Rotational speed $N$?
> 3. Desired life $L_{10h}$ (typically **20,000–40,000 hours** for industrial)?
> 4. Required reliability (default 90%)?
> 5. Application factor $K_a$?
> 6. Compute $C_{\text{req}}$ → select bearing → verify $f_s \geq 1.0$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the ISO Basic Rating Life Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU Part B question**. Practise writing it from memory.

**Starting Point (Empirical Hertzian fatigue observation):**

Experimental studies by Lundberg and Palmgren (1947) showed that the survival probability $S$ of a bearing under stress follows a **Weibull distribution**. The probability of survival after $N$ revolutions is:

$$
S = \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{N}{N_{0}}\right)^{e}\right]
$$

where $N_0$ is the characteristic life (at $S = 1/e \approx 36.8\%$) and $e$ is the Weibull slope.

**Step 1:** Define the rating life $L_{10}$ as the life at which 90% of the population survives. Substituting $S = 0.9$:

$$
0.9 = \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{L_{10}}{N_{0}}\right)^{e}\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln(0.9) = -\left(\dfrac{L_{10}}{N_{0}}\right)^{e}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange the negative sign:

$$
-\ln(0.9) = \left(\dfrac{L_{10}}{N_{0}}\right)^{e}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the power $1/e$ to both sides to isolate $L_{10}/N_0$:

$$
\dfrac{L_{10}}{N_{0}} = \left[-\ln(0.9)\right]^{1/e}
$$

**Step 5:** The characteristic life $N_0$ is related to the **dynamic load rating $C$** (the load at which $L_{10} = 1$ million revolutions) by a power law with exponent $p$:

$$
C = P \cdot (N_{0} \cdot 10^{-6})^{1/p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{0} = 10^{6} \left(\dfrac{P}{C}\right)^{-p}
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting back into the $L_{10}$ expression:

$$
L_{10} = 10^{6} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \left[-\ln(0.9)\right]^{1/e}
$$

**Step 7:** For the **90% reliability** definition to align, ISO defines $C$ such that the bracketed factor is absorbed into the rating, yielding the **canonical ISO Basic Rating Life equation**:

$$
\boxed{\,L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}\,}
$$

with $p = 3$ for ball bearings and $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Life-in-Hours Form

Starting from the millions-of-revolutions form:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}
$$

Convert revolutions to hours. If the bearing rotates at $N$ **rpm**:

$$
\text{Number of revolutions in 1 hour} = 60\,N
$$

Therefore, the number of **millions of revolutions** in $L_{10h}$ hours is:

$$
L_{10} = \dfrac{60\,N \cdot L_{10h}}{10^{6}}
$$

Equating both expressions for $L_{10}$:

$$
\dfrac{60\,N \cdot L_{10h}}{10^{6}} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}
$$

Solving for $L_{10h}$:

$$
\boxed{\,L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Required Dynamic Load Rating

Starting from the life-in-hours equation:

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}
$$

Rearrange to isolate the term $(C/P)^p$:

$$
\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} = \dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot 60\,N}{10^{6}}
$$

Take the $p$-th root of both sides:

$$
\dfrac{C}{P} = \left(\dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot 60\,N}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/p}
$$

Solve for $C$:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{\text{req}} = P \left(\dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot 60\,N}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/p}\,}
$$

This is the **most-used formula in KTU Part B problems** — given load, speed, and desired life, you compute $C_{\text{req}}$, then select a catalogue bearing with $C \geq C_{\text{req}}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for $P$

| Operating Condition | Equivalent Radial Load $P$ |
|---|---|
| Pure radial load, deep groove ball bearing | $P = F_{r}$ |
| Combined radial + axial load, ratio $F_{a}/F_{r} \leq e$ | $P = F_{r}$ |
| Combined radial + axial load, ratio $F_{a}/F_{r} > e$ | $P = X\,F_{r} + Y\,F_{a}$ |
| Angular contact bearing (single row) | $P = X\,F_{r} + Y\,F_{a}$ always |
| Tapered roller bearing | $P = X\,F_{r} + Y\,F_{a}$ from manufacturer table |
| Pure axial load, thrust bearing | $P = F_{a}$ |

where $X$, $Y$, and $e$ are **bearing-specific factors** obtained from the manufacturer's catalogue tables (SKF, FAG, NTN).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Bearing Design Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete selection procedure as mandated by KTU 2024 Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Operating Conditions] --> B[Radial Load Fr]
    A --> C[Axial Load Fa]
    A --> D[Rotational Speed N in rpm]
    A --> E[Desired Life L10h in hours]
    A --> F[Reliability R and Application Factor Ka]
    B --> G[Compute Equivalent Dynamic Radial Load P]
    C --> G
    G --> G1{Determine X, Y, e from Catalogue}
    G1 --> G2{Check Fa/Fr vs e}
    G2 -->|Fa/Fr less than or equal to e| G3[P equals Fr]
    G2 -->|Fa/Fr greater than e| G4[P equals XFr plus YFa]
    G3 --> H[Compute Required Dynamic Load Rating Creq]
    G4 --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    H --> H1[Creq equals P times L10h times 60N over 10 to the 6 raised to 1 over p]
    H1 --> I[Apply Reliability and Application Factors]
    F --> I
    I --> J[Modified Creq times a1 times Ka]
    J --> K[Browse Manufacturer Catalogue SKF FAG NTN]
    K --> L{Does Catalogue C satisfy modified Creq}
    L -->|Yes| M[Select Bearing and Record Catalogue Number]
    L -->|No| N[Go to Next Larger Catalogue Bearing]
    N --> L
    M --> O[Static Load Check]
    O --> P[Compute Equivalent Static Load P0 equals X0Fr plus Y0Fa]
    P --> Q[Compute Static Safety Factor fs equals C0 over P0]
    Q --> R{Is fs greater than 1 point 0}
    R -->|Yes| S[Final Selection Verified]
    R -->|No| T[Select Larger Bearing or Reduce Load]
    T --> K
    S --> U[End: Generate Design Report]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Types and Load-Carrying Capabilities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rolling Contact Bearings] --> B[Ball Bearings]
    A --> C[Roller Bearings]
    B --> B1[Deep Groove Conrad]
    B --> B2[Angular Contact]
    B --> B3[Self Aligning Ball]
    B --> B4[Thrust Ball]
    C --> C1[Cylindrical Roller]
    C --> C2[Tapered Roller]
    C --> C3[Spherical Roller]
    C --> C4[Needle Roller]
    C --> C5[Thrust Roller]
    B1 --> L1[Pure Radial Low Thrust Low Noise]
    B2 --> L2[Combined Radial and High Thrust]
    C1 --> L3[High Radial Load No Thrust]
    C2 --> L4[Combined Radial and Thrust High Capacity]
```
