Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Rating Life Equation **Starting Premise (Lundberg–Palmgren):** The probability of survival at $N$ revolutions is governed by the survival probability of a sub-surface stressed volume $V$. **Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Stress Model** For a ball on a race under load $Q$, the maximum Hertzian contact stress is: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3Q}{2\pi a b} \end{aligned} $$ where $a, b$ are the contact ellipse semi-axes. Through dimensional analysis, the *contact ellipse area* scales as $Q^{2/3}$. **Step 2 — Sub-Surface Shear Stress** The maximum alternating shear stress at depth $z$ below the contact centre is: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{0} \propto Q^{1/3} \end{aligned} $$ It is **independent of curvature** to first order, depending only on load magnitude. **Step 3 — Stress Cycles to Failure** For a stressed material volume $V$ subject to shear stress $\tau_0$, the fatigue life of that volume follows a power law: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{f} = A \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{c}^{\,-c} \cdot \Delta V^{1/c}}{\tau_{0}^{\,c}} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $c$ ≈ 31/3 ≈ 10.33 (empirical Weibull exponent for bearing steel) - $\tau_{c}$ is the fatigue limit of the steel - $\Delta V$ is the stressed volume per unit load **Step 4 — Survival Probability** The bearing has many stressed volumes (one per ball/roller, one per inner race, one per outer race). Treating each as a *weakest-link* in series, the **Weibull cumulative failure function** gives survival probability: $$ \begin{aligned} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{S}\right) \propto \tau_{0}^{\,c} \cdot N^{e} \cdot V \end{aligned} $$ Setting $S = 0.90$ (i.e., 10% failure at $L_{10}$) defines the rated load $C$ and the rated life. **Step 5 — Eliminate Material Constant** Since $C$ is by definition the load giving $L_{10} = 1$ million revolutions, we have: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{0}(C) &\propto C^{1/3} \\ \tau_{0}(P) &\propto P^{1/3} \end{aligned} $$ Equating the survival conditions and solving for $L_{10}$: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &\propto \left[\tau_{0}(C)\right]^{c} \cdot \left[10^{6}\right]^{e} \\ L_{10} &\propto \left[\tau_{0}(P)\right]^{-c/e} \cdot 1 \\ \therefore \quad L_{10} &= \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{c/e} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Identification of the Life Exponent** For **ball bearings**: $c/e = 3$ → $p = 3$ For **roller bearings**: line contact → the stress-volume integral gives $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The final Lundberg–Palmgren equation**: > $$ > \begin{aligned} > L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \;\; \text{million revolutions} > \end{aligned} > $$ ### 3.2 Conversion from Revolutions to Hours Operating hours are the *practical* metric used by automotive designers. **Step-by-step conversion:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Revolutions per minute} &= n \;\;[\text{rpm}] \\ \text{Revolutions per hour} &= 60 \, n \\ \text{Hours to reach } L_{10} \text{ million revolutions} &= \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^{6}}{60 \, n} \\ L_{10h} &= \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \, n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \;\; \text{hours} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Modified Life per ISO 281 The modern ISO 281 expression incorporates *real-environment* corrections: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{nm} = a_{1} \, a_{23} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \end{aligned} $$ Derivation logic: - $a_1$ adjusts **reliability** through the Weibull slope (Section 2.4). - $a_{23}$ adjusts **load-zone fatigue limit** through lubrication and contamination. - $L_{nm}$ therefore represents the *adjusted* life at a chosen reliability with realistic lubrication. > [!NOTE] > **Important Engineering Insight:** $a_{23}$ can be **less than 1** (poor lubrication → shorter life) or **greater than 1** (clean premium lubrication → longer life). Modern hybrid ceramic bearings with high-grade grease can achieve $a_{23} > 3$. ### 3.4 Variable Load Cumulative Damage (Palmgren–Miner) Automotive bearings rarely see a single constant load. **Palmgren–Miner linear damage hypothesis** gives: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{N_{i}}{L_{10,i}} \leq 1 \end{aligned} $$ where $N_i$ is the actual revolutions at load $P_i$, and $L_{10,i}$ is the rating life at $P_i$. For a 2-step load spectrum: $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{N_{1}}{L_{10,1}} + \dfrac{N_{2}}{L_{10,2}} \leq 1 \end{aligned} $$ This is highly relevant for **wheel bearings** which experience cornering, braking, and straight-line driving as distinct load regimes. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Bearing Life Calculation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step 1: Identify Operating Conditions
Loads, Speed, Temperature, Lubrication"] --> B["Step 2: Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load P
P = X*Fr + Y*Fa"] B --> C["Step 3: Apply Application Factor fd
P_corrected = fd * P"] C --> D["Step 4: Select Bearing Type
Ball p=3 or Roller p=10/3"] D --> E["Step 5: Read Catalogue Ratings
C and C0 from Manufacturer"] E --> F["Step 6: Compute Basic Rating Life
L10 = C/P raised to p million rev"] F --> G["Step 7: Apply Reliability Factor a1
per Weibull distribution"] G --> H["Step 8: Apply Life Modification Factor a23
per ISO 281 lubrication curve"] H --> I["Step 9: Compute Modified Life Lnm
Lnm = a1 * a23 * L10"] I --> J{"Step 10: Life Check
Lnm greater than Required?"} J -- "YES" --> K["Step 11: Verify Static Load
P0 less than C0 by S0"] K --> L["ACCEPT DESIGN"] J -- "NO" --> M["INCREASE C
Choose larger bearing
or reduce applied load"] M --> D ``` ### 4.2 Reliability Curve (Weibull Failure Distribution) ```mermaid graph LR A["Reliability R = 90 percent
a1 = 1.00
Standard Rating"] --> B["Reliability R = 95 percent
a1 = 0.84"] B --> C["Reliability R = 99 percent
a1 = 0.62"] C --> D["Reliability R = 99.9 percent
a1 = 0.48"] D --> E["Higher reliability
= Lower a1
= Shorter Lnm"] ``` ### 4.3 Load-Life Relationship (p = 3 Ball Bearing) ```mermaid graph LR L1["P = C
L10 = 1 million rev"] --> L2["P = 0.5 C
L10 = 8 million rev"] L2 --> L3["P = 0.25 C
L10 = 64 million rev"] L3 --> L4["P = 0.1 C
L10 = 1000 million rev"] L4 --> L5["Observation:
Halving load multiplies life by 8"] ``` ### 4.4 Subsurface Stress Mechanism (Functional Block) ```mermaid graph TD A["External Load Q"] --> B["Hertzian Contact
Sub-surface Stress Field"] B --> C["Maximum Shear at Depth z"] C --> D["Each Revolution = 1 Stress Cycle"] D --> E["Inclusions / Defects act as Crack Initiators"] E --> F["Sub-surface Crack Propagation"] F --> G["Crack Reaches Surface"] G --> H["Spalling / Pitting Visible"] H --> I["BEARING LIFE EXPIRED"] ``` ### 4.5 Modified Life Block Architecture (ISO 281) ```mermaid graph LR A["L10 Basic Life
from Lundberg-Palmgren"] --> B["Multiply by a1
Reliability Factor"] B --> C["Multiply by a23
Lubrication plus Contamination"] C --> D["Final Modified Life Lnm"] D --> E["Compare with Design Target
Example: 5000 hours for wheel bearing"] E -- "Pass" --> F["Design Accepted"] E -- "Fail" --> G["Re-select Bearing
Larger C required"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define the term **basic rating life (L₁₀)** of a rolling-contact bearing. Why is it expressed statistically at 90% reliability rather than 100%? > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **basic rating life L₁₀** is the number of revolutions (or hours at constant speed) that **90% of a large group of apparently identical bearings** will attain or exceed before the first evidence of rolling-contact fatigue develops. > It is statistical because: (i) material heterogeneity (inclusions, voids) means each bearing has slightly different endurance; (ii) rolling-contact fatigue is governed by the **weakest-link** statistical model of sub-surface stressed volumes; (iii) giving a 100% guarantee would require extremely conservative ratings, increasing cost and size. The 90% benchmark represents an industry-standard compromise between reliability and economy. > **[3 marks: 1.5 definition + 1.5 statistical reasoning]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** Distinguish between **basic dynamic load rating (C)** and **basic static load rating (C₀)**. In which operating condition is each used? > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > | Aspect | C (Dynamic) | C₀ (Static) | > |--------|-------------|-------------| > | Definition | Constant radial load at which L₁₀ = 1 million revolutions | Static load producing a permanent deformation of 0.0001 × ball diameter at the most heavily loaded contact | > | Use | Predicting fatigue life under **rotating** load | Verifying that **non-rotating** or **slow oscillating** bearings do not plastically indent | > | Standard | ISO 15 / ABMA 9 | ISO 76 / ABMA 11 | > **[3 marks]** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.** --- #### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** A deep-groove ball bearing has the following data: Basic dynamic load rating $C = 35.2$ kN, Equivalent dynamic load $P = 12.5$ kN, speed $n = 1450$ rpm. The application requires a **99% reliability**. Calculate: (i) Basic rating life $L_{10}$ in million revolutions. (ii) Life in operating hours at the given speed. (iii) Modified rating life $L_{nm}$ assuming $a_{23} = 0.85$ and reliability = 99%. **(b)** Briefly explain how **lubrication viscosity ratio $\kappa$** and **contamination coefficient $\eta_c$** influence the life modification factor $a_{23}$. --- #### Model Solution **(a)(i) Basic Rating Life in million revolutions:** Ball bearing → $p = 3$ $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10} &= \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \\ &= \left(\dfrac{35.2}{12.5}\right)^{3} \\ &= (2.816)^{3} \\ L_{10} &= 22.32 \text{ million revolutions} \end{aligned} $$ **Full marks:** ✅ **(a)(ii) Life in operating hours:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10h} &= \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \, n} \times L_{10} \\ &= \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1450} \times 22.32 \\ &= \dfrac{22.32 \times 10^{6}}{87{,}000} \\ L_{10h} &= 256.55 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Full marks:** ✅ **(a)(iii) Modified Life for 99% reliability:** From the KTU standard table (Section 2.4), at $R = 99\%$, $a_1 = 0.62$. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{nm} &= a_{1} \cdot a_{23} \cdot L_{10} \\ &= 0.62 \times 0.85 \times 22.32 \\ L_{nm} &= 11.76 \text{ million revolutions} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to hours: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{nmh} &= \dfrac{11.76 \times 10^{6}}{87{,}000} = 135.17 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Full marks (7):** ✅ --- **(b) Influence of $\kappa$ and $\eta_c$ on $a_{23}$:** **Viscosity Ratio $\kappa$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \kappa = \dfrac{\nu}{\nu_{1}} \end{aligned} $$ where $\nu$ = actual operating kinematic viscosity of lubricant, $\nu_{1}$ = reference viscosity required for adequate EHL film thickness at 70°C. - If $\kappa < 1$: lubricant film too thin → metal-to-metal contact → fatigue accelerated → **$a_{23} < 1$**. - If $\kappa = 1$: nominal film → $a_{23} \approx 1$. - If $\kappa > 1$: thicker film → rolling fatigue reduced → **$a_{23} > 1$**, plateau at high $\kappa$ values. **Contamination Coefficient $\eta_c$:** Reflects the level of particulate contamination. Per ISO 281 Annex B, $\eta_c$ ranges from very clean (high cleanliness) to heavily contaminated. - Cleanliness $\uparrow$ (low $\eta_c$ level value) → longer life → $a_{23}$ rises. - Severe contamination → dents act as stress raisers → accelerated spalling → $a_{23}$ collapses to **0.1 – 0.3**. In ISO 281, both effects are combined into the single factor $a_{23}$ via tabulated/curve data (Fig-2 of ISO 281). **Full marks (7):** ✅ --- #### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** A cylindrical roller bearing is subjected to the following two-stage load spectrum: - **Stage 1:** $P_1 = 18$ kN for $N_1 = 600$ million revolutions - **Stage 2:** $P_2 = 9$ kN for $N_2 = 900$ million revolutions The bearing's basic dynamic load rating $C = 60$ kN. Using the **Palmgren–Miner cumulative damage hypothesis**, determine whether the bearing will survive the load spectrum. Use $p = 10/3$. **(b)** Explain why the **modified life equation (ISO 281)** is preferred over the basic Lundberg–Palmgren equation in modern automotive applications. --- #### Model Solution **(a) Cumulative Damage Calculation:** Roller bearing → $p = 10/3$. **Step 1: Compute $L_{10}$ at each load level.** Stage 1: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{10,1} &= \left(\dfrac{C}{P_1}\right)^{p} = \left(\dfrac{60}{18}\right)^{10/3} \\ &= (3.333)^{3.333} \end{aligned} $$ Using logarithmic evaluation: $$ \begin{aligned} \ln L_{10,1} &= 3.333 \times \ln(3.333) \\ &= 3.333 \times 1.2040 \\ &= 4.013 \\ L_{10,1} &= e^{4.013} = 55.27 \text{ million revolutions} \end{aligned} $$ Stage 2: $$ \begin{aligned} \ln L_{10,2} &= 3.333 \times \ln(60/9) \\ &= 3.333 \times \ln(6.667) \\ &= 3.333 \times 1.8971 \\ &= 6.324 \\ L_{10,2} &= e^{6.324} = 558.6 \text{ million revolutions} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Apply Palmgren–Miner.** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{N_1}{L_{10,1}} + \dfrac{N_2}{L_{10,2}} &= \dfrac{600}{55.27} + \dfrac{900}{558.6} \\ &= 10.86 + 1.611 \\ &= 12.47 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Interpretation.** Since the cumulative damage ratio **greatly exceeds 1.0** (12.47 ≫ 1), the bearing will **NOT survive** the spectrum. The Stage 1 load dominates the damage. **Step 4: Required redesign.** To bring the damage ratio to ≤ 1, increase $C$ by a factor of $(12.47)^{1/p}$ for roller bearings, or limit operating cycles. $$ \begin{aligned} C_{new} &\approx 60 \times (12.47)^{0.3} \\ &\approx 60 \times 2.07 \\ C_{new} &\approx 124 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Full marks (7):** ✅ --- **(b) Why Modified Life (ISO 281) is preferred:** | # | Limitation of Basic L₁₀ | Improvement by ISO 281 | |---|--------------------------|------------------------| | 1 | Assumes 90% reliability only | $a_1$ allows any reliability 90–99.9% | | 2 | Ideal lubrication | $a_{23}$ captures real viscosity and contamination | | 3 | No material-grade effect | $a_{23}$ incorporates vacuum-degassed steel benefits | | 4 | Constant load | Combined with Palmgren–Miner for variable spectra | | 5 | No thermal penalty | $a_{23}$ includes thermal-viscosity degradation | | 6 | Modern EV/hybrid demands high reliability | $a_1 a_{23}$ product captures engineering reality | For automotive applications such as **EV motor bearings, wheel hub bearings, and gearbox bearings**, the ISO 281 modified life can be **2–5 times longer** than basic L₁₀ under favourable conditions, allowing either smaller bearings (cost savings) or higher reliability for the same size. **Full marks (7):** ✅ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:** > 1. **Wrong exponent $p$** — Using $p=3$ for roller bearings (loss: 2 marks). Always check bearing type first. > 2. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing kN with N in $C/P$ ratio (loss: 3 marks). Verify catalogue units. > 3. **Forgetting $a_1$** for non-90% reliability (loss: 2 marks). > 4. **In Palmgren–Miner problems**, computing $L_{10,i}$ in hours instead of revolutions — these must be **in the same units** as $N_i$ (loss: 3 marks). > 5. **Omitting application factor $f_d$** when explicitly mentioned in problem statement. > 6. **Static check skipped** — even after a successful dynamic life check, examiners expect verification of $P_0 \le C_0 / S_0$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Lundberg–Palmgren equation**: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ million revolutions, with **p = 3 (ball)** and **p = 10/3 (roller)**. - ✅ **Operating hours**: $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60\,n} (C/P)^p$. - ✅ **Modified life (ISO 281)**: $L_{nm} = a_1 a_{23} (C/P)^p$. - ✅ **Reliability factor**: $a_1 = 1.0$ at 90%, drops to 0.62 at 99%, 0.48 at 99.9%. - ✅ **Life modification factor $a_{23}$** combines **lubrication viscosity ratio $\kappa$** and **contamination level**. - ✅ **Equivalent dynamic load**: $P = X F_r + Y F_a$; **Application factor** $f_d$ for shock loads. - ✅ **Static load rating $C_0$** must be checked via $P_0 \le C_0 / S_0$ for non-rotating/slowly oscillating bearings. - ✅ **Palmgren–Miner cumulative damage**: $\sum N_i / L_{10,i} \le 1$ for variable loads. - ✅ **Halving the load increases life 8×** for ball bearings, **≈ 9.85×** for roller bearings — load has a dramatic effect on life. - ✅ **Doubling speed halves life** (in hours) — always consider speed sensitivity in automotive design. - ✅ Modern EV and passenger-car applications demand **L₁₀h ≥ 1500–3000 hours** with reliability ≥ 95–99%. ---

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Load Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Load Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Static Load Capacity (C₀) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Load Rating (C₀):** The static load rating is defined as the load acting on a non-rotating, slowly oscillating, or slowly rotating bearing, at which the permanent deformation of the rolling element (ball or roller) at the most heavily loaded contact point reaches **0.0001 of the rolling element diameter (d)**. This threshold is universally accepted by **ISO 76:2006** and **IS 6455** standards, and forms the basis for all static capacity calculations in KTU board examinations.

**Units:** The static load capacity is expressed in **kilonewtons (kN)** in SI practice, or in **Newton (N)** for small precision bearings.

## 1.2 Dynamic Load Capacity (C) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Load Rating (C):** The dynamic load rating is the constant, steady load which a rolling bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **1 million revolutions (10⁶ revolutions)** before the first evidence of fatigue failure (pitting/spalling) develops on the raceway. This is governed by the **ISO 281:2007** and **IS 6455** standards.

**Units:** Expressed in **kilonewtons (kN)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stacking **identical iron bricks** on top of a wooden plank:

- **Static Load Capacity (C₀)** — Think of placing a heavy object on a **stationary** table. The table doesn't break from fatigue; it either survives the weight (elastic recovery) or deforms permanently (dents in the wood). The maximum weight the table can bear before it visibly **dents** is its static capacity.

- **Dynamic Load Capacity (C)** — Now imagine a **swinging bridge** that opens and closes 50 times a day. Even though each cycle applies a moderate load (well within the static capacity), after **1 million open-close cycles**, the metal fatigues and cracks. The load that gives exactly 1 million cycles of life before fatigue is the dynamic capacity.

In automotive engineering:
- **Static (C₀)** governs **parked, sudden shock, or reversing loads** — e.g., vehicle jacked up, brake caliper loads, crash loads.
- **Dynamic (C)** governs **continuous running loads** — e.g., wheel bearings at highway speeds, transmission gears, crankshaft journals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask "Why is C₀ higher than C?" Answer: Because fatigue (C) limits life much earlier than plastic indentation (C₀). A bearing can carry 3–5× more load statically than it can dynamically for the same life.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Source |
|-----------|--------|-----------------------|--------|
| Fatigue Life Constant | e | **3** for ball bearings, **10/3** for roller bearings | ISO 281 |
| Indentation Factor | f₀ | **0** to **50** (depending on contact geometry) | ISO 76 |
| Reference Life (dynamic) | L₁₀ | **1,000,000 revolutions** | ISO 281 |
| Static Safety Factor | S₀ | **1.0 to 4.0** (depending on application) | IS 6455 |
| EHL Film Parameter | κ | **1 to 2** | Hamrock-Dowson |
| Hertzian Contact Pressure Limit | σ_lim | **4000 MPa** (ball) / **4200 MPa** (roller) | ISO/TR 1281 |

---

## 1.5 Fundamental Relationship Between C and C₀

The ratio **C / C₀** for a given bearing series is a fixed geometric property:

$$\frac{C}{C_0} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \quad \text{(for ball bearings)}$$

$$\frac{C}{C_0} \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 3.0 \quad \text{(for roller bearings)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The dynamic capacity is **always numerically smaller** than the static capacity for the same bearing, because dynamic failure (fatigue) precedes static failure (indentation) under repetitive loading.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Two Capacities Exist

Rolling element bearings experience two distinct failure modes:

1. **Fatigue Failure (Dynamic)**: Progressive subsurface cracks initiate below the raceway surface due to repeated Hertzian shear stress cycling. Cracks propagate to the surface and produce **pitting** or **spalling**.

2. **Plastic Indentation (Static)**: When the load is so high (or rotation so slow) that fatigue does not have time to occur, the asperities permanently deform at the contact patch, leaving **brinelling** marks.

The bearing industry chose **two separate rating numbers** so that designers can apply two independent safety criteria.

## 2.2 Rating Life Equation (ISO 281)

The most fundamental equation in bearing design is the **basic rating life** (also called **L₁₀ life**, since 90% of bearings will exceed this life):

$$L_{10} = \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^p \quad \text{[million revolutions]}$$

Where:
- **L₁₀** = basic rating life in millions of revolutions
- **C** = dynamic load rating (kN)
- **P** = equivalent dynamic load (kN)
- **p** = exponent of the life equation = **3** for ball bearings, **10/3** for roller bearings

### 2.2.1 Conversion of L₁₀ to Hours

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6 \cdot L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{16667 \cdot L_{10}}{n} \quad \text{[hours]}$$

Where **n** = rotational speed in **rpm**.

### 2.2.2 Modified Rating Life (ISO 281:2007)

Modern ISO standards include **lubrication cleanliness** and **a₁, a_ISO** factors:

$$L_{10m} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

Where:
- **a₁** = reliability factor (e.g., a₁ = **1.00** for 90% reliability, **0.62** for 99%)
- **a_ISO** = combined factor for lubrication, contamination, and operating conditions

## 2.3 Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)

When a bearing carries both radial (Fr) and axial (Fa) loads simultaneously, we compute an **equivalent load** that produces the same fatigue life:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \quad \text{[kN]}$$

Where:
- **X** = radial factor
- **Y** = axial factor
- These values depend on the **ratio Fa/Fr** and the bearing geometry; they are tabulated in SKF, FAG, and NTN catalogues.

For **pure radial loading** (Fa = 0): **P = Fr**.

## 2.4 Equivalent Static Load (P₀)

The static equivalent load is the static load that would produce the same permanent deformation as the actual combined static load:

$$P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a \quad \text{[kN]}$$

Where **X₀** and **Y₀** are the static radial and axial factors from the manufacturer catalogue.

## 2.5 Static Safety Factor Check

After computing P₀, the bearing is acceptable if:

$$S_0 = \frac{C_0}{P_0} \geq S_{0,min}$$

| Application | S₀,min |
|-------------|--------|
| Smooth, vibration-free operation | **1.0** |
| Normal operation (most automotive) | **1.5 – 2.0** |
| Shock loads, gearboxes | **2.0 – 3.0** |
| Crane bearings, crusher bearings | **3.0 – 4.0** |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU 2024 numerical question on this topic tests one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 | $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ | Basic rating life | Million revs |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = 16667 \cdot L_{10} / n$ | Convert to hours | hours |
| 3 | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load | kN |
| 4 | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | Equivalent static load | kN |
| 5 | $S_0 = C_0 / P_0$ | Static safety factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $L_{10m} = a_1 a_{ISO} (C/P)^p$ | Modified rating life | Million revs |
| 7 | $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{1/p}$ | Required dynamic capacity | kN |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max} = (3F / (2\pi a b))$ | Hertzian contact pressure | MPa |
| 9 | $Q / Q_{max} = \cos(\psi)^{1/2}$ | Load distribution on rolling elements | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\delta / \delta_{max} = (1 - 1/\epsilon)\cos(\psi)$ | Elastic deflection (Stribeck) | dimensionless |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Automotive Engineering

- **Wheel hub bearings**: Selected primarily by **dynamic capacity** because they rotate continuously at vehicle speed. A typical passenger car wheel bearing operates at ~1000 rpm at 100 km/h.
- **Clutch release bearings**: Selected primarily by **static capacity** because they are loaded only momentarily during gear shifts and remain stationary otherwise.
- **Transmission output shaft bearings**: Both criteria are checked; the **dynamic life** governs, but **static safety** is checked against gear-shock loads.
- **Steering column bearings**: Static-dominant, but with low S₀ values acceptable because loads are well-defined and impact-free.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Note:** Tesla Model 3 rear motor bearings operate at ~17,000 rpm, requiring careful use of **modified L₁₀m** with a_ISO > 0.8 for EHL lubricant regimes. This is why automotive OEMs use **synthetic PAO oils with extreme pressure (EP) additives**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Lundberg-Palmgren Life Equation

This is the foundational analytical work behind the **dynamic load rating (C)** used in all modern catalogues.

### 3.1.1 Starting Postulate

For a rolling element subjected to cyclic Hertzian shear stress τ₀ at depth z₀ below the contact surface, the probability of survival after N stress cycles is:

$$S(N) = \exp\left(-\frac{N^a \tau_0^a V}{z_0^h}\right)$$

Where:
- **V** = stressed volume
- **a** = Weibull slope (typically **9/8 for ball** and **10/9 for roller**)
- **h** = depth exponent (≈ **31/3**)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Integrate survival probability over the **raceway** (load distribution varies around the raceway from Q_max to 0).

Setting survival probability = 0.9 (i.e., 10% failure for L₁₀ life):

$$0.1 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{N^a \tau_0^a V_0}{z_0^h}\right) \cos(\psi)\, d\psi$$

Where the **load-deflection relationship** (Stribeck) is:

$$Q = K \delta^{3/2}$$

**Step 2** — Express τ₀ in terms of Q:

From Hertz contact theory for point contact (ball-on-raceway):

$$\tau_0 = \tau_{max} \cdot f(Q) \propto Q^{2/3}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute and integrate. After laborious algebra (Lundberg & Palmgren, 1947):

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{with} \quad p = 3 \text{ (ball)}, \quad p = 10/3 \text{ (roller)}$$

**Step 4** — Definition of C (dynamic load rating):

By definition, when **P = C**, **L₁₀ = 1 million revolutions**. This convention **fixes** the numerical value of C as the load corresponding to L₁₀ = 10⁶.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Board Style — 14 Mark Problem Setup)

> **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:**
> A deep groove ball bearing 6308 is subjected to a radial load Fr = 5 kN and an axial load Fa = 2 kN at a speed of n = 1450 rpm. The bearing is required to give 5000 hours of life with 90% reliability. From the manufacturer's catalogue:
> - C = **41 kN**, C₀ = **24 kN**
> - X = 0.56, Y = 1.15 (for Fa/Fr > e = 0.26)
> - X₀ = 0.6, Y₀ = 0.5
> 
> Determine: (a) The actual rating life in hours; (b) Whether the bearing is statically safe for S₀,min = 1.5.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio and select X, Y**

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

Check whether this exceeds **e**:

$$F_a / F_r = 0.4 > e = 0.26 \implies \text{Use } X = 0.56, \; Y = 1.15$$

**Step 2 — Compute the equivalent dynamic load P**

$$P = X F_r + Y F_a$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= (0.56)(5) + (1.15)(2) \\
  &= 2.80 + 2.30 \\
  &= 5.10 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the basic rating life L₁₀ (million revs)**

For a **ball bearing**, p = 3:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \left(\frac{41}{5.10}\right)^3$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{P} &= \frac{41}{5.10} = 8.039 \\
L_{10} &= (8.039)^3 \\
       &= 519.5 \; \text{million revolutions}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours**

$$L_{10h} = \frac{16667 \times L_{10}}{n} = \frac{16667 \times 519.5}{1450}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{8{,}657{,}837}{1450} \\
        &= 5971 \; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verify against the requirement**

$$L_{10h,actual} = 5971 \; \text{hours} > L_{10h,required} = 5000 \; \text{hours}$$

✅ **The bearing is dynamically adequate.**

**Step 6 — Compute the equivalent static load P₀**

$$P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= (0.6)(5) + (0.5)(2) \\
    &= 3.0 + 1.0 \\
    &= 4.0 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the static safety factor S₀**

$$S_0 = \frac{C_0}{P_0} = \frac{24}{4.0} = 6.0$$

**Step 8 — Compare against the minimum**

$$S_0 = 6.0 > S_{0,min} = 1.5$$

✅ **The bearing is statically safe — actually over-designed for static load.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode vs. Load Type

| Loading Condition | Operating State | Governing Capacity | KTU Equation |
|-------------------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Bearing stationary, heavy load | At rest / parked | **Static (C₀)** | $S_0 = C_0 / P_0 \geq S_{0,min}$ |
| Bearing rotating, constant load | Continuous duty | **Dynamic (C)** | $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ |
| Bearing rotating, variable load | Automotive duty cycle | **Dynamic (C)** with spectrum factor | $L_{10} = (C/P_{eq})^p$ |
| Sudden shock, no rotation | Crash / impact | **Static (C₀)** | Use **f₀** contact factor |
| Slow oscillation | Steering / slewing | **Static (C₀)** | $S_0 \geq 2.0$ recommended |

## 3.4 Derivation of Equivalent Load Spectrum (Cumulative)

For automotive duty cycles with **N₁ cycles at load P₁**, **N₂ at P₂**, ..., the **equivalent constant load** is:

$$P_{eq} = \left(\frac{N_1 P_1^p + N_2 P_2^p + \cdots + N_k P_k^p}{\sum N_i}\right)^{1/p}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Each load Pi contributes $N_i P_i^p$ damage (Miner's rule).
2. Total damage = sum of all $N_i P_i^p$.
3. An equivalent constant load P_eq over the same total cycles gives the same damage:

$$P_{eq}^p \sum N_i = \sum N_i P_i^p$$

$$P_{eq} = \left(\frac{\sum N_i P_i^p}{\sum N_i}\right)^{1/p}$$

Then **L₁₀ = (C / P_eq)^p**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Bearing Capacity Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application] --> B{Operating Mode?}
    B -->|Rotating| C[Compute Dynamic Life L10]
    B -->|Stationary| D[Compute Static Safety S0]
    B -->|Both| E[Run BOTH Checks]
    C --> C1[Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    C1 --> C2[Apply L10 = C by P cubed p]
    C2 --> C3{L10h exceeds Required?}
    C3 -->|Yes| F[Select Bearing]
    C3 -->|No| G[Upsize Bearing Series]
    D --> D1[Compute Equivalent Static Load P0]
    D1 --> D2[Apply S0 = C0 by P0]
    D2 --> D3{S0 exceeds S0min?}
    D3 -->|Yes| F
    D3 -->|No| G
    E --> E1[Dynamic Check]
    E --> E2[Static Check]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    G --> C
    F --> H[Verify Lubrication aISO]
    H --> I[Final Selection]
```

## 4.2 Load Distribution on Rolling Elements (Stribeck Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outer Raceway] --> B[Load Zone = 180 deg]
    B --> C[Qmax at Bottom]
    B --> D[Q decreases to 0 at Edges]
    C --> E[Load Distribution Q of psi]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Stribeck: Q = Qmax x cos to power 1/2 psi]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dynamic vs Static Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Loads]
        FR[Radial Force Fr]
        FA[Axial Force Fa]
        N[Rotational Speed n rpm]
    end
    subgraph EQUIV[Equivalent Load Calculation]
        PEQ[P = X Fr + Y Fa]
        P0EQ[P0 = X0 Fr + Y0 Fa]
    end
    subgraph CAP[Capacity Domains]
        CDYN[Dynamic Capacity C]
        CSTA[Static Capacity C0]
    end
    subgraph LIFE[Life and Safety]
        LLIFE[L10 = C by P cubed p]
        SSAF[S0 = C0 by P0]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision Output]
        DEC1{Dynamic OK?}
        DEC2{Static OK?}
        OUTFIN[Selected Bearing]
    end
    FR --> PEQ
    FA --> PEQ
    FR --> P0EQ
    FA --> P0EQ
    PEQ --> LLIFE
    P0EQ --> SSAF
    CDYN --> LLIFE
    CSTA --> SSAF
    N --> LLIFE
    LLIFE --> DEC1
    SSAF --> DEC2
    DEC1 -->|Yes| OUTFIN
    DEC2 -->|Yes| OUTFIN
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Safeguard Verification

All node IDs are alphanumeric (PEQ, P0EQ, CDYN, CSTA, LLIFE, SSAF, DEC1, DEC2, OUTFIN, etc.) and contain **no reserved keywords** (end, subgraph, graph, style). All labels use **clean uppercase text** with no markdown formatting tags inside double quotes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define dynamic load capacity of a rolling contact bearing and state the standard life used to define it.**

**Model Answer:**
The dynamic load capacity (C) of a rolling contact bearing is defined as the constant radial load which a bearing can theoretically withstand for a **rating life of 1 million revolutions (10⁶ revs)** before the first evidence of material fatigue (pitting) develops on the raceway. This definition is standardized in **ISO 281:2007** and **IS 6455**. It is used to compute the rating life using the equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$, where p = 3 for ball bearings and 10/3 for roller bearings.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Distinguish between static load capacity (C₀) and dynamic load capacity (C).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static Load Capacity (C₀) | Dynamic Load Capacity (C) |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Definition | Load that produces 0.0001 d permanent indentation | Load for 10⁶ rev rating life |
| Failure mode | Plastic deformation / brinelling | Material fatigue / pitting |
| Application | Stationary, slow oscillation | Continuous rotation |
| Standard | ISO 76:2006 | ISO 281:2007 |
| Numerical value | Higher (1.5–3× C) | Lower |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

A 6310 deep groove ball bearing in an automotive gearbox is subjected to:
- Radial load **Fr = 8 kN**
- Axial load **Fa = 3 kN**
- Speed **n = 1000 rpm**
- Required life **L₁₀h = 8000 hours** at 90% reliability

From the catalogue:
- C = **62 kN**, C₀ = **38 kN**
- X = 0.56, Y = 1.15 (when Fa/Fr > e = 0.28)
- X₀ = 0.6, Y₀ = 0.5

**Solve:**
**(a)** Compute the equivalent dynamic load P and verify the bearing's life in hours.
**(b)** Compute the equivalent static load P₀ and check whether S₀ ≥ 2.0 is satisfied.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Compute load ratio:

$$F_a / F_r = 3 / 8 = 0.375 > e = 0.28$$

So use **X = 0.56, Y = 1.15**.

**Step 2:** Equivalent dynamic load:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= X F_r + Y F_a \\
  &= (0.56)(8) + (1.15)(3) \\
  &= 4.48 + 3.45 \\
  &= 7.93 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Basic rating life (millions of revolutions) with p = 3 for ball:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= (C / P)^3 \\
       &= (62 / 7.93)^3 \\
       &= (7.818)^3 \\
       &= 477.8 \; \text{million revs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to hours:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= 16667 \times L_{10} / n \\
        &= 16667 \times 477.8 / 1000 \\
        &= 7964 \; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compare:

$$L_{10h} = 7964 \; \text{hours} < L_{10h,required} = 8000 \; \text{hours}$$

❌ **The bearing is marginally under-designed** — select the next larger series (6312).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Equivalent static load:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a \\
    &= (0.6)(8) + (0.5)(3) \\
    &= 4.8 + 1.5 \\
    &= 6.3 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Static safety factor:

$$S_0 = C_0 / P_0 = 38 / 6.3 = 6.03$$

**Step 3:** Compare:

$$S_0 = 6.03 > S_{0,min} = 2.0$$

✅ **The bearing is statically safe.**

**Conclusion:** The bearing is statically safe but dynamically marginal — recommend **upsizing to 6312**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

A cylindrical roller bearing NU 2210 in a transmission countershaft operates under a varying duty cycle:

| Stage | Radial Load (kN) | Duration (revs) |
|-------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 (urban driving) | 4.5 | 4 × 10⁶ |
| 2 (highway cruise) | 2.0 | 18 × 10⁶ |
| 3 (hill climb) | 7.0 | 3 × 10⁶ |

Speed **n = 2500 rpm**, dynamic capacity **C = 95 kN**, exponent **p = 10/3** (roller bearing). Pure radial loading (Fa = 0).

**Solve:**
**(a)** Compute the equivalent constant load P_eq and the rating life L₁₀ in revolutions and hours.
**(b)** If the application requires **L₁₀h ≥ 10,000 hours**, decide whether the bearing is suitable or recommend a larger series.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** For pure radial loading, **P_i = F_ri**:

$$P_1 = 4.5 \; \text{kN}, \quad P_2 = 2.0 \; \text{kN}, \quad P_3 = 7.0 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 2:** Equivalent constant load using Miner's rule with p = 10/3:

$$P_{eq} = \left(\frac{\sum N_i P_i^{10/3}}{\sum N_i}\right)^{3/10}$$

**Numerator calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_1 P_1^{10/3} &= 4 \times 10^6 \times (4.5)^{10/3} \\
              &= 4 \times 10^6 \times 64.43 \\
              &= 257.7 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_2 P_2^{10/3} &= 18 \times 10^6 \times (2.0)^{10/3} \\
              &= 18 \times 10^6 \times 10.08 \\
              &= 181.4 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_3 P_3^{10/3} &= 3 \times 10^6 \times (7.0)^{10/3} \\
              &= 3 \times 10^6 \times 184.2 \\
              &= 552.6 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum N_i P_i^{10/3} = 991.7 \times 10^6$$

$$\sum N_i = (4 + 18 + 3) \times 10^6 = 25 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3:** P_eq:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} &= (991.7 / 25)^{3/10} \\
       &= (39.668)^{0.3} \\
       &= 2.99 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Rating life:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= (C / P_{eq})^{10/3} \\
       &= (95 / 2.99)^{10/3} \\
       &= (31.77)^{3.333} \\
       &= 31.77^3 \times 31.77^{0.333} \\
       &= 32068 \times 3.158 \\
       &= 101{,}270 \; \text{million revolutions}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to hours:

$$L_{10h} = 16667 \times 101{,}270 / 2500 = 675{,}142 \; \text{hours}$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison:**

$$L_{10h} = 675{,}142 \; \text{hours} \gg L_{10h,required} = 10{,}000 \; \text{hours}$$

✅ **The bearing is heavily over-designed.** This is because automotive transmission countershafts typically run at low-to-moderate loads relative to bearing capacity. The current NU 2210 is acceptable, and you could potentially **downsize** to NU 2208 if weight/cost reduction is critical, but only after re-running the calculation with the smaller C value.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Using p = 3 for roller bearings** ❌ — Roller bearings use **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.333**. Using 3 gives a life **3–5% too low**, losing 1 mark on average.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion** — Using L₁₀ in revs without converting to hours via **16667/n** loses **2 marks** in the final answer.
>
> 3. **Selecting X, Y based on default values** ❌ — X and Y depend on **Fa/Fr > e or ≤ e**. Always check the ratio against e first.
>
> 4. **Confusing static and dynamic** — Some students compute P and then divide by C₀ instead of using P₀. Remember: **C₀ goes with P₀, C goes with P**.
>
> 5. **Not reporting units** — KTU expects **kN** in final answers, not Newtons. Wrong unit → 0.5 mark deducted.
>
> 6. **Skipping the conclusion statement** — Always end with a clear ✅ or ❌ and a brief engineering recommendation. This is worth **1–2 marks** for "engineering judgment."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Exam:**

- **Dynamic Load Rating (C):** Load for L₁₀ = 10⁶ revs; failure mode is **fatigue pitting**.
- **Static Load Rating (C₀):** Load producing 0.0001 d indentation; failure mode is **brinelling**.
- **Dynamic Life Equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ in **million revolutions**.
- **Roller Bearing Exponent:** Always use **p = 10/3**, never 3.
- **Hours Conversion:** Divide by **n × 60 / 10⁶** = multiply by **16667 / n**.
- **Equivalent Load (Dynamic):** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$.
- **Equivalent Load (Static):** $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$.
- **Static Safety Factor:** $S_0 = C_0 / P_0 \geq S_{0,min}$ (typically 1.5–2.0 for automotive).
- **Modified Life:** $L_{10m} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}$ for reliability > 90%.
- **Equivalent Variable Load (Miner's Rule):** $P_{eq} = \left(\sum N_i P_i^p / \sum N_i\right)^{1/p}$.
- **Automotive Examples:**
  * Wheel bearings → Dynamic dominant.
  * Clutch release → Static dominant.
  * Gearbox shafts → Both, dynamic usually governs.
- **Required Capacity Rule:** $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10,req}^{1/p}$ (used when you start from required life and find needed C).
- **Common Bearing Series:** 6308 = Deep groove ball, NU 2210 = Cylindrical roller, 32208 = Tapered roller.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Static and Dynamic Load Capacity**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axial and Radial Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Axial and Radial Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Automotive Component Design**, loads acting on rotating machine elements (shafts, bearings, gears, pulleys) are resolved into two principal orthogonal components relative to the **axis of rotation** of the shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Axial Load ($F_a$ or $A$):** A force whose line of action is **parallel** to the axis of rotation of the shaft. It is also called a **thrust load**. It tends to push or pull the shaft along its longitudinal direction.
>
> **Radial Load ($F_r$ or $R$):** A force whose line of action is **perpendicular** to the axis of rotation of the shaft. It is also called a **transverse load**. It tends to bend the shaft or press the bearing raceways radially.

The **resultant load** on a bearing or shaft is the vector sum of these two components:
$$F = \sqrt{F_r^2 + F_a^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction Convention Used in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - The **Z-axis** is conventionally taken along the axis of the shaft.
> - **Axial direction (X-direction in some texts):** along the shaft axis.
> - **Radial direction (Y and Z planes):** perpendicular to the shaft axis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rotating bicycle wheel mounted on a fixed axle**:

- **Radial Load Analogy:** If you press your hand on the **side of the wheel hub** (perpendicular to the axle), the load tries to **bend the axle** and presses the bearings sideways. This is a radial load. The weight of the rider acting downward on a vehicle axle is a classic radial load.

- **Axial Load Analogy:** If you push the **end face of the axle** inward (like pushing a rod into a hole), the load tries to **slide the shaft longitudinally** through its bearings. The thrust produced by a car's transmission propeller shaft during acceleration, or the helical gear's separating force component, produces axial loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in automotive design?**
> - Wheel hub bearings handle **radial loads** (vehicle weight) and **axial loads** (cornering forces, braking torque).
> - Clutch release bearings experience pure **axial load**.
> - Transmission shafts and differential pinions must be designed for **combined radial + axial loading** from helical and bevel gears.

### 1.3 Sources of Loads in Automotive Components

| Component | Typical Radial Load Source | Typical Axial Load Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Spur Gear** | Tangential + Radial mesh forces | None (zero thrust) |
| **Helical Gear** | Tangential + Radial mesh forces | Axial mesh force (thrust) |
| **Bevel Gear** | Tangential + Radial mesh forces | Axial mesh force (thrust) |
| **V-Belt / Pulley** | Belt tension difference | None (with proper alignment) |
| **Chain / Sprocket** | Chain tension | None (slight from slack side) |
| **Vehicle Wheel Bearing** | Vehicle weight | Cornering + braking force |
| **Propeller Shaft** | Weight of shaft itself | Clutch/driving thrust |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Radial Load Analysis

A **radial load** on a shaft creates:
1. **Bending moment** along the length of the shaft.
2. **Shear force** at the support sections.
3. **Radial reaction forces** at the bearing supports.

The bending moment diagram (BMD) is constructed by treating the shaft as a simply supported (or overhung) beam, and the maximum bending moment is used in design calculations.

For a shaft supported on two bearings with a central load:
$$M_{max} = \frac{F_r \cdot L}{4}$$
where:
- $F_r$ = total radial load (N)
- $L$ = distance between bearing centers (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an automotive transmission countershaft, the radial loads are the **tangential ($F_t$)** and **radial ($F_r$)** gear mesh forces. The combined radial force on the shaft is:
> $$F_{r,total} = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2}$$

### 2.2 Axial Load Analysis

A pure **axial load** creates **uniform tensile or compressive stress** across the cross-section:
$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{F_a}{A} = \frac{4F_a}{\pi d^2}$$

However, when combined with a **bending moment**, the maximum bending stress occurs at the extreme fiber:
$$\sigma_{bending} = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$

### 2.3 Combined Loading – The Von Mises / Equivalent Moment Approach

When a shaft is subjected to **simultaneous bending and torsion** (the most common case in automotive drivelines), an **equivalent twisting moment** is used:
$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m \cdot T)^2}$$

An **equivalent bending moment** is also defined as:
$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m \cdot T)^2}\right)$$

where:
- $M$ = bending moment (N·mm)
- $T$ = torsional moment (N·mm)
- $K_m$ = combined shock and fatigue factor for load application

The shaft diameter is then sized using the **maximum shear stress theory** (or equivalent Von Mises criterion).

### 2.4 Equivalent Dynamic Bearing Load

This is the **single most important equation** in KTU Module 2 for bearing selection:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

where:
- $P$ = equivalent dynamic bearing load (N)
- $F_r$ = actual radial load on the bearing (N)
- $F_a$ = actual axial (thrust) load on the bearing (N)
- $X$ = **radial load factor** (dimensionless, from manufacturer's catalog)
- $Y$ = **thrust load factor** (dimensionless, from manufacturer's catalog)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factors $X$ and $Y$ depend on:
> 1. The **type of bearing** (ball, cylindrical roller, tapered roller, spherical roller).
> 2. The **ratio** $\frac{F_a}{F_r}$ compared to a parameter $e$ (also from catalog).
>
> **Rule of Selection:**
> - If $\frac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$ : use $X = 1$, $Y = 0$, so $P = F_r$
> - If $\frac{F_a}{F_r} > e$ : use $X$ and $Y$ as given in the catalog table for that $F_a/C_0$ ratio.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \sqrt{F_r^2 + F_a^2}$ | Resultant of radial and axial load | N |
| 2 | $M_{max} = \dfrac{F_r \cdot L}{4}$ | Max bending moment (central load, simply supported) | N·mm |
| 3 | $M_{max} = \dfrac{F_a \cdot a \cdot (L-a)}{L}$ | Max bending moment (overhung load) | N·mm |
| 4 | $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}$ | Equivalent twisting moment | N·mm |
| 5 | $M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[M + \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}\right]$ | Equivalent bending moment | N·mm |
| 6 | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic bearing load | N |
| 7 | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | Equivalent static bearing load | N |
| 8 | $C = P \cdot L_{10}^{1/a}$ | Basic dynamic load rating relation | N |
| 9 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Bearing life in million revolutions | — |
| 10 | $C_0 \geq S_0 \cdot P_0$ | Static load rating check (with safety factor $S_0$) | N |
| 11 | $\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{4 F_a}{\pi d^2}$ | Axial stress in shaft | N/mm² |
| 12 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ | Bending stress in shaft | N/mm² |

**Exponent values ($p$ or $a$):**
- **Ball bearings:** $p = 3$ (million revs)
- **Roller bearings:** $p = \dfrac{10}{3}$ (million revs)

**Typical combined shock & fatigue factors ($K_m$):**

| Type of Load | $K_m$ (Uniform) | $K_m$ (Light Shock) | $K_m$ (Heavy Shock) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Combined bending + torsion | 1.0 | 1.25 – 1.5 | 1.75 – 2.5 |
| Pure torsion | 1.0 | 1.0 – 1.25 | 1.5 – 2.0 |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bearing Catalog Selection:** SKF, NTN, FAG publish $X$, $Y$, $e$ tables. KTU exam questions give these values.
- **Vehicle Driveline Design:** The propeller shaft under acceleration carries combined bending (shaft weight) and torsion (engine torque) — directly applicable to design problems.
- **Wheel Hub Bearing:** Subjected to vertical (radial) load = vehicle weight share, and axial load from cornering = $F_{a,cornering} = \dfrac{m \cdot v^2}{R_{turn}}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Twisting Moment under Combined Bending and Torsion

**Starting Point (Maximum Shear Stress Theory):**

A shaft of diameter $d$ under combined bending moment $M$ and torque $T$ has:
- Maximum bending (normal) stress: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$
- Maximum torsional (shear) stress: $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$

**Step 1:** According to the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** (also called the Guest's theory, which is the basis for the ASME shaft design code), the equivalent shear stress is:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expressions for $\sigma_b$ and $\tau$:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 4:** For design with shock factor $K_m$ applied to torque only (KTU convention for shafts):

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Define the **Equivalent Twisting Moment** $T_e$ as the torque that would produce the same shear stress on a shaft under pure torsion:

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 6:** Equate and solve for $T_e$:

$$\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}}$$

Similarly for $M_e$:

$$\boxed{M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[M + \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}\right]}$$

**Physical Meaning:** $T_e$ is a fictitious torque that, acting alone, would produce the same maximum shear stress as the actual combination of $M$ and $T$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Shaft under Combined Loading

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**

A transmission countershaft in an automobile carries a **helical gear** at its midspan. The gear mesh produces the following forces on the shaft:
- Tangential force: $F_t = 4500$ N
- Radial force: $F_r = 1650$ N
- Axial force: $F_a = 1900$ N

The shaft is supported on two bearings **300 mm apart**, with the gear centered between them. The shaft transmits a **torque of 180 N·m**. Assume light shock loading.

**Step 1: Identify load type for shaft bending**

The gear forces act on the shaft in three directions. For shaft bending, only the components **perpendicular to the shaft axis** matter — i.e., the tangential and radial forces.
$$F_{r,total} = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2} = \sqrt{4500^2 + 1650^2}$$
$$F_{r,total} = \sqrt{20{,}250{,}000 + 2{,}722{,}500} = \sqrt{22{,}972{,}500}$$
$$F_{r,total} \approx 4792.96 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the maximum bending moment**

For a simply supported shaft with a central concentrated load:
$$M = \dfrac{F_{r,total} \cdot L}{4} = \dfrac{4792.96 \times 300}{4}$$
$$M = \dfrac{1{,}437{,}888}{4} = 359{,}472 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Identify the torsional moment**
$$T = 180 \text{ N·m} = 180{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4: Apply shock factor**

For light shock with combined bending and torsion: $K_m = 1.5$
$$K_m \cdot T = 1.5 \times 180{,}000 = 270{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5: Calculate equivalent twisting moment**
$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2} = \sqrt{359{,}472^2 + 270{,}000^2}$$
$$T_e = \sqrt{1.292 \times 10^{11} + 7.29 \times 10^{10}}$$
$$T_e = \sqrt{2.021 \times 10^{11}}$$
$$T_e \approx 449{,}557 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 6: Calculate equivalent bending moment**
$$M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[M + \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}\right]$$
$$M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[359{,}472 + 449{,}557\right] = \dfrac{1}{2}(809{,}029)$$
$$M_e \approx 404{,}514 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 7: Compare $M_e$ and $T_e$ for design**

The design is governed by the **larger** of the two. Here $T_e > M_e$, so use $T_e = 449{,}557$ N·mm for shaft diameter calculation.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Equivalent Dynamic Bearing Load

**Problem Statement:**

A **deep-groove ball bearing** supports a radial load of $F_r = 4000$ N and an axial load of $F_a = 1500$ N. The manufacturer's catalog gives: $e = 0.30$, $Y = 1.65$ (when $F_a/F_r > e$), $X = 0.56$.

**Step 1: Calculate the load ratio**
$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1500}{4000} = 0.375$$

**Step 2: Compare with parameter $e$**
$$0.375 > 0.30 \implies \text{Use } X = 0.56, \ Y = 1.65$$

**Step 3: Calculate equivalent dynamic load**
$$P = X F_r + Y F_a = 0.56 \times 4000 + 1.65 \times 1500$$
$$P = 2240 + 2475$$
$$\boxed{P = 4715 \text{ N}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the comparison gave $F_a/F_r \leq e$, then the axial component would have been **ignored** in the equivalent load, since the bearing can absorb that thrust without affecting fatigue life. This is a critical KTU exam trap.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Approach

| Loading Condition | Governing Equation | Design Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Pure radial load on shaft | $M = F_r L / 4$ | Bending stress only |
| Pure axial load on shaft | $\sigma_a = 4F_a/(\pi d^2)$ | Direct stress only |
| Bending + Torsion | $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}$ | Use in shaft diameter |
| Pure radial load on bearing | $P = F_r$ | $X=1, Y=0$ |
| Radial + Axial on bearing | $P = XF_r + YF_a$ | If $F_a/F_r > e$ |
| Pure axial on bearing (axial contact) | $P = F_a$ | Thrust bearing |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Shaft Loading Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Driving Force] --> B[Force Decomposition]
    B --> C[Tangential Ft]
    B --> D[Radial Fr]
    B --> E[Axial Fa]
    
    C --> F[Combined Shaft Load Calculator]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    
    F --> G[Bending Moment M]
    F --> H[Torsional Moment T]
    F --> I[Axial Stress]
    
    G --> J[Equivalent Moment Me]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    
    J --> K{Select Governing Parameter}
    K -->|Me > Te| L[Design Shaft for Bending]
    K -->|Te > Me| M[Design Shaft for Torsion]
    K -->|Both| N[Design with Larger Value]
    
    L --> O[Shaft Diameter d]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style O fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Bearing Load Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Radial Load Fr and Axial Load Fa] --> B{Fa / Fr compared to e}
    B -->|Less than or equal to e| C[P equals Fr]
    B -->|Greater than e| D[Read X and Y from catalog]
    D --> E[P equals X times Fr plus Y times Fa]
    
    C --> F[Use P in Life Equation]
    E --> F
    
    F --> G[L10 equals C by P raised to p]
    G --> H{Required life met?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Select Bearing]
    H -->|No| J[Choose larger C]
    J --> F
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Direction Convention Diagram (Force Resolution on Shaft)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Axial Direction - along shaft axis] -->|Fa| B[Thrust on bearings]
    C[Radial Direction - perpendicular] -->|Fr| D[Bending of shaft]
    E[Tangential Direction - perpendicular] -->|Ft| F[Torsion of shaft]
    
    A -.-> G[No bending]
    A -.-> H[No torsion]
    C -.-> I[Bending only]
    E -.-> F
    
    style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#FFAAAA,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#FFAAAA,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#AAFFAA,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFFFAA,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Combined Loading on Helical Gear Shaft (Text Schematic)

```
                       AXIAL DIRECTION (along shaft)
                              ↑ Fa
                              |
        Bearing A =================================================== Bearing B
        |                |                    |                        |
       Ra (radial        |       GEAR          |                       Rb (radial
        reaction)        |     (midspan)       |                       reaction)
        |                |                    |                        |
        |        ←————— Ft (tangential, horizontal, perpendicular)      |
        |                ↓ Fr (radial, vertical)                         |
        |                |                                               |
        ==================|===============================================
                              ↓
                       RADIAL DIRECTION (perpendicular to axis)
                       
   - Fa creates axial stress + push on bearings
   - Fr creates bending in vertical plane
   - Ft creates bending in horizontal plane + torsion about shaft axis
```

### 4.5 Vehicle Wheel Bearing Loading (Application Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Weight W] -->|Vertical Down| B[Wheel Hub]
    C[Braking Force Fb] -->|Horizontal| B
    D[Cornering Force Fc] -->|Lateral| B
    
    B --> E[Wheel Bearing]
    
    E -->|Radial Reaction| F[Inner Race Contact]
    E -->|Axial Thrust| G[Bearing Thrust Capacity]
    
    F --> H{Combined load case}
    G --> H
    H -->|Fa/Fr less than e| I[P equals Fr]
    H -->|Fa/Fr greater than e| J[P equals XFr + YFa]
    
    I --> K[Bearing Life Check]
    J --> K
    
    style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Distinguish clearly between radial load and axial load as applied to a rolling contact bearing, with one automotive example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Radial Load (1 mark):** A radial load acts **perpendicular to the axis of rotation** of the shaft. It causes the inner race of the bearing to press against the outer race in a direction normal to the shaft axis. *Example:* The **vertical load on a wheel bearing** due to the vehicle's weight acting downward through the tyre contact patch.

- **Axial Load (1 mark):** An axial load acts **parallel to the axis of rotation** of the shaft. It tends to displace the shaft longitudinally along its axis. *Example:* The **thrust on a helical gear shaft** due to the axial component of the gear mesh force, or the **axial load on a clutch release (throw-out) bearing** when the clutch pedal is pressed.

- **Effect on bearing life (1 mark):** When the ratio $F_a/F_r$ exceeds the manufacturer's value $e$, the axial load **reduces the bearing's effective load-carrying capacity**, so the equivalent dynamic load becomes $P = XF_r + YF_a > F_r$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**State the equivalent dynamic load equation for a deep-groove ball bearing. Under what condition is the axial component neglected?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Equation (2 marks):** The equivalent dynamic load is:
$$P = X F_r + Y F_a$$
where $X$ is the radial load factor and $Y$ is the axial (thrust) load factor, both obtained from the manufacturer's catalog based on the ratio $F_a/C_0$ (axial load to static load rating).

- **Neglecting the axial component (1 mark):** The axial component is **neglected** (i.e., $X=1, Y=0$) when the ratio $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$, where $e$ is a dimensionless parameter from the catalog. In this regime, the bearing's internal geometry can sustain the thrust without affecting its fatigue life, and the equivalent load reduces to $P = F_r$.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

## **Choice A: Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

A **countershaft of an automotive gearbox** carries a **helical pinion** at the center between two bearings spaced **280 mm apart**. The pinion transmits **7.5 kW at 1200 rpm** and has a **normal pressure angle of 20°**, **helix angle of 25°**, and **pitch circle diameter of 80 mm**. The pinion has **20 teeth** and is mounted between bearings with **light shock conditions**. Determine:

**(a)** The gear forces (tangential, radial, axial) acting on the shaft, and the maximum bending moment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The required shaft diameter using an allowable shear stress of **45 MPa** for the material. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Q.A.**

#### **Part (a) — Gear Forces and Bending Moment (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Torque transmitted (1 mark)**

$$T = \dfrac{60 \times P}{2 \pi N} = \dfrac{60 \times 7500}{2 \pi \times 1200} = \dfrac{450{,}000}{7539.82}$$
$$T \approx 59.68 \text{ N·m} = 59{,}683 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Tangential force on the pinion (1 mark)**

Tangential force is given by:
$$F_t = \dfrac{T}{r} = \dfrac{2T}{d} = \dfrac{2 \times 59{,}683}{80} = 1492.07 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Radial and Axial forces (2 marks)**

For a helical gear:
$$F_r = F_t \cdot \dfrac{\tan \alpha_n}{\cos \beta}$$
$$F_a = F_t \cdot \tan \beta$$

where $\alpha_n = 20°$ (normal pressure angle) and $\beta = 25°$ (helix angle).

$$F_r = 1492.07 \times \dfrac{\tan 20°}{\cos 25°} = 1492.07 \times \dfrac{0.3640}{0.9063}$$
$$F_r = 1492.07 \times 0.4015 = 599.07 \text{ N}$$

$$F_a = 1492.07 \times \tan 25° = 1492.07 \times 0.4663 = 695.85 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Resultant radial load on the shaft (1 mark)**

$$F_{r,total} = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2} = \sqrt{1492.07^2 + 599.07^2}$$
$$F_{r,total} = \sqrt{2{,}226{,}273 + 358{,}885} = \sqrt{2{,}585{,}158} = 1607.84 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Maximum bending moment for a central load (1 mark)**

$$M = \dfrac{F_{r,total} \cdot L}{4} = \dfrac{1607.84 \times 280}{4} = 112{,}548.8 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 6: Torsional moment (1 mark)**

$$T = 59{,}683 \text{ N·mm}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Shaft Diameter Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Apply shock factor (1 mark)**

For light shock with combined bending and torsion: $K_m = 1.5$

$$K_m \cdot T = 1.5 \times 59{,}683 = 89{,}524.5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent twisting moment (1 mark)**

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2} = \sqrt{112{,}548.8^2 + 89{,}524.5^2}$$
$$T_e = \sqrt{1.267 \times 10^{10} + 8.014 \times 10^9}$$
$$T_e = \sqrt{2.068 \times 10^{10}}$$
$$T_e \approx 143{,}820 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Equivalent bending moment (1 mark)**

$$M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[M + T_e\right] = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[112{,}548.8 + 143{,}820\right]$$
$$M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}(256{,}368.8) = 128{,}184.4 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4: Identify the governing moment (1 mark)**

Since $T_e = 143{,}820$ N·mm > $M_e = 128{,}184$ N·mm, the design is governed by **$T_e$**.

**Step 5: Apply the maximum shear stress (Guest's) theory (1 mark)**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi d^3} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

**Step 6: Solve for diameter (2 marks)**

$$d^3 \geq \dfrac{16 \times T_e}{\pi \times \tau_{allow}} = \dfrac{16 \times 143{,}820}{\pi \times 45}$$

$$d^3 \geq \dfrac{2{,}301{,}120}{141.37} = 16{,}277.5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d \geq \sqrt[3]{16{,}277.5} = 25.37 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d_{required} \approx 25.4 \text{ mm (adopt standard } d = 30 \text{ mm)}}$$

---

## **Choice B: Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

A **rolling mill of an automotive steel plant** uses a **double-row spherical roller bearing** to support a roll shaft. The bearing carries a **radial load of 25,000 N** and an **axial load of 8,000 N**. The manufacturer specifies the following data for the bearing: static load rating $C_0 = 200$ kN, dynamic load rating $C = 320$ kN, parameter $e = 0.30$, $Y = 1.85$ (for $F_a/F_r > e$), and $X = 0.67$.

**(a)** Calculate the **equivalent dynamic load** on the bearing and verify if the bearing can carry the load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **expected bearing life in hours** if the operating speed is **1450 rpm**, and state whether the bearing is suitable for a **design life of 10,000 hours**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Q.B.**

#### **Part (a) — Equivalent Dynamic Load (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate the load ratio (1 mark)**

$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{8000}{25{,}000} = 0.32$$

**Step 2: Compare with the catalog parameter $e$ (1 mark)**

Since $0.32 > e = 0.30$, the axial load affects the equivalent load.

We use $X = 0.67$ and $Y = 1.85$ as given.

**Step 3: Calculate equivalent dynamic load (1 mark)**

$$P = X F_r + Y F_a = 0.67 \times 25{,}000 + 1.85 \times 8{,}000$$
$$P = 16{,}750 + 14{,}800 = 31{,}550 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Check the dynamic load rating (1 mark)**

Since $C = 320{,}000$ N, and $P = 31{,}550$ N, we have $C > P$, so the bearing can carry the load under static considerations.

**Step 5: Compute the load ratio $C/P$ (1 mark)**

$$\dfrac{C}{P} = \dfrac{320{,}000}{31{,}550} \approx 10.14$$

**Step 6: Initial assessment (2 marks)**

For a roller bearing, the life exponent is $p = 10/3 \approx 3.333$. The life will be calculated in part (b).

$$\boxed{P = 31{,}550 \text{ N} = 31.55 \text{ kN}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Bearing Life in Hours (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Life in million revolutions (1 mark)**

For a roller bearing, $p = \dfrac{10}{3}$:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p = (10.14)^{10/3}$$

**Step 2: Compute $(10.14)^{10/3}$ (2 marks)**

$$10.14^{10/3} = 10.14^{3.333}$$

Let $x = \log_{10}(10.14) = 1.00604$

$$3.333 \times 1.00604 = 3.3535$$

$$10^{3.3535} \approx 2255.4 \text{ million revolutions}$$

$$L_{10} \approx 2255.4 \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 3: Convert to hours using the speed (1 mark)**

$$L_h = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \times N} = \dfrac{2255.4 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1450}$$

$$L_h = \dfrac{2.2554 \times 10^9}{87{,}000}$$

$$L_h \approx 25{,}924 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4: Compare with design life (1 mark)**

Design life required: $L_{design} = 10{,}000$ hours.
Calculated life: $L_h = 25{,}924$ hours.

Since $25{,}924 > 10{,}000$, the bearing **meets the design life requirement** with a safety margin of $\approx 2.59\times$.

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{\text{Bearing life} \approx 25{,}924 \text{ hours, which is adequate for the 10,000-hour design requirement.}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Axial/Radial Load Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the shock factor $K_m$** to the torque only — not to bending. Applying it to both loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Incorrectly combining the gear forces:** The bending moment must be calculated using the **resultant of tangential and radial forces**, not just one. The axial force is NOT included in the bending moment but contributes to the bearing thrust.
> 3. **Forgetting the comparison $F_a/F_r$ vs $e$:** If the ratio is less than $e$, students must show $P = F_r$ (i.e., $X=1, Y=0$). Defaulting to $X = 0.56, Y = 1.65$ blindly loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Unit conversion:** Power is in kW, torque comes out in N·m — must convert to N·mm before plugging into shaft diameter formula. Forgetting this gives an off-by-1000 error.
> 5. **Standard diameter adoption:** Always round up the calculated diameter to the nearest **standard value** (e.g., 25, 28, 30, 32, 35, 40 mm for shafts). Reporting $d = 25.37$ mm is acceptable only if you clearly say "adopt $d = 28$ mm."
> 6. **Bearing life exponent confusion:** $p = 3$ for **ball bearings** and $p = 10/3$ for **roller bearings**. Mixing these up gives an error of over 60% in calculated life.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Radial Load** = perpendicular to shaft axis → causes **bending**.
- **Axial Load** = parallel to shaft axis → causes **tension/compression** in shaft; **thrust** on bearings.
- **Resultant** of two perpendicular loads: $F = \sqrt{F_r^2 + F_a^2}$ (N).
- For a **shaft under combined loading**:
  - Equivalent twisting moment: $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}$
  - Equivalent bending moment: $M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[M + \sqrt{M^2 + (K_m T)^2}\right]$
  - Use the **larger** of $M_e$ and $T_e$ to size the shaft using $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \leq \tau_{allow}$.
- For a **bearing under combined loading**:
  - Equivalent dynamic load: $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (if $F_a/F_r > e$, else $P = F_r$).
  - Life equation: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ million revolutions.
  - $p = 3$ for ball bearings; $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings.
- **Helical gear forces** on shaft: $F_t$ (tangential), $F_r = F_t \tan \alpha_n / \cos \beta$, $F_a = F_t \tan \beta$.
- **Standard shock factors $K_m$:** 1.0 (uniform) → 1.5 (light shock) → 2.0+ (heavy shock) for combined bending + torsion.
- **Direction convention:** Z-axis along shaft; bending occurs in X-Y plane; torsion about Z.
- **Always** check $F_a / F_r$ against $e$ before using the full equivalent load equation.
- **Always** convert torque from N·m to N·mm in shaft diameter formulas.
- **Always** adopt the **next higher standard** shaft diameter from the calculated value.
- **High-yield tip:** 80% of KTU Module 2 numerical problems involve either (i) gear force decomposition + shaft diameter, or (ii) bearing equivalent load + life check. Master these two templates.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selection of Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bearing Selection** is the systematic engineering procedure of choosing the most appropriate rolling-element or plain bearing for a given mechanical application, based on quantitative evaluation of **load magnitude**, **load direction**, **rotational speed**, **desired operating life**, **lubrication regime**, **alignment tolerance**, **temperature**, and **environmental contamination**.

In the context of automotive component design, the selection of bearings is the process of determining the geometry, size, series, and type of bearing required to support a rotating shaft (or sliding member) within the constraints imposed by the host subsystem — such as the crankshaft main journals, gearbox shafts, wheel hubs, differential pinions, and suspension links.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Shoulder of the Machine"

Imagine a person continuously spinning a heavy grinding stone on top of a wooden pole.

- If the stone spins **slowly** but is **very heavy** — you only need a **strong, steady post** (a sliding/journal bearing).
- If the stone spins **very fast** and you need **precision and low friction** — you would prefer **steel balls in a raceway** so that the stone "floats" on tiny rolling contacts (a ball bearing).

Bearings are the **mechanical shoulders** of every rotating part in an automobile. Choosing the wrong one is like asking a child to carry a refrigerator — the geometry, capacity, and conditions must all match.

## 1.3 Why is Bearing Selection Critical in Automobiles?

> [!NOTE]
> According to industry surveys, **bearings account for nearly 30% of all mechanical failures** in rotating machinery. In an automobile, a failed bearing can lead to wheel seizure, gearbox seizure, or catastrophic engine damage.

| Automotive Subsystem | Typical Bearing Used | Selection Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Crankshaft (Main & Big-end) | Plain (Hydrodynamic) journal bearing | High load, moderate speed, oil-lubricated |
| Wheel Hub | Tapered roller bearing / Ball bearing | Combined radial + axial thrust |
| Transmission Gearbox | Cylindrical / Needle / Tapered roller | High speed, moderate-to-high radial load |
| Differential | Tapered roller bearing | Heavy combined load, misalignment tolerance |
| Alternator / Water Pump | Deep-groove ball bearing | High speed, moderate radial load |
| Steering Column | Needle / Ball bearing | Compact, light, precise |

## 1.4 KTU 2024 — Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For PBAUT604 Module 2, the KTU board expects students to master:
> - Distinguishing **static** vs. **dynamic** load behaviour.
> - Application of the **ISO 281 Life Equation** and equivalent load concepts.
> - The **step-by-step selection flowchart** used by SKF, FAG, NTN, and Timken.
> - Numerical problems combining radial + axial load and desired L10 life.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Hierarchy of Bearing Selection

The selection of a bearing is never a single equation — it is a **layered decision process**. The engineer moves through these stages sequentially:

1. **Identify the load regime**
   - Pure radial? Pure axial (thrust)? Combined?
   - Constant magnitude or fluctuating? Shock loads?

2. **Quantify the operating conditions**
   - Rotational speed $n$ [rpm]
   - Operating temperature $T$ [°C]
   - Contamination level (clean / dusty / wet)

3. **Decide the bearing family**
   - Ball bearings → higher speeds, lower loads, less friction.
   - Roller bearings → higher loads, lower speeds, better shock absorption.

4. **Compute the Equivalent Dynamic Load $P$**
   - Converts the actual mixed load into a single equivalent radial load that produces the same fatigue life.

5. **Compute the Required Basic Dynamic Load Rating $C_{req}$**
   - Back-calculated from desired L10 life.

6. **Select the catalogue reference**
   - Pick a bearing whose catalogue $C \geq C_{req}$.

7. **Verify static load, lubrication, and thermal limits.**

8. **Confirm with modified life equation (ISO 281:2007).**

## 2.2 Static vs. Dynamic Load Selection

| Aspect | Static Selection | Dynamic Selection |
|---|---|---|
| Used when | Shaft does **not rotate**, or rotates very slowly ($n < 10$ rpm) | Shaft rotates continuously at significant speed |
| Failure mode | Permanent brinelling of raceway | Subsurface-originated fatigue spalling |
| Governing parameter | **Basic static load rating $C_0$** | **Basic dynamic load rating $C$** |
| Check | $P_0 \leq C_0$ | $C_{req} \leq C_{cat}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these two regimes different?** Under static load, the ball/roller deforms the raceway plastically (a small permanent dent). Under dynamic load, repeated sub-surface shear stresses initiate micro-cracks that propagate into spalls.

## 2.3 Equivalent Dynamic Bearing Load $P$

For a bearing subjected simultaneously to a radial load $F_r$ and an axial (thrust) load $F_a$, the **equivalent dynamic load** is the single hypothetical radial load that would give the same fatigue life as the actual combined loading.

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

The factors $X$ and $Y$ depend on the ratio $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$ and a catalogue constant $e$:

$$P = \begin{cases} F_r, & \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e \\[6pt] X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a, & \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e \end{cases}$$

> **Why $e$ exists**: Below a certain thrust fraction, the contact ellipse stays within the inner ring shoulder, and the thrust contributes little to fatigue. Beyond it, the contact climbs up the shoulder and the load capacity rises, so we add the $Y \cdot F_a$ contribution.

## 2.4 Equivalent Static Load $P_0$

$$P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$$

where $X_0$ and $Y_0$ are catalogue constants (different from dynamic $X$, $Y$).

## 2.5 The ISO Life Equation (The Heart of Bearing Selection)

The **basic rating life** $L_{10}$ (in millions of revolutions) of a bearing subjected to a constant equivalent dynamic load $P$ is:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

with the exponent $p$:

$$p = \begin{cases} 3, & \text{Ball bearings} \\[4pt] \dfrac{10}{3}, & \text{Roller bearings} \end{cases}$$

Expressed in **operating hours** at speed $n$ rpm:

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad [\text{hours}]$$

## 2.6 Required Basic Dynamic Load Rating

If the design demands a life $L_{10h}^*$ hours, the required rating is:

$$C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}^*}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$

> **Engineering utility**: This is the **reverse engineering** of the life equation — it is the equation every design engineer uses to size a bearing against a target service life (e.g., 5,000 hours for an automotive gearbox).

## 2.7 Modified Rating Life (ISO 281:2007)

For modern reliability-aware design:

$$L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}$$

where:
- $a_1$ = **reliability factor** (e.g., $a_1 = 1$ for 90 %, $a_1 \approx 0.62$ for 99 %, $a_1 \approx 0.21$ for 99.9 %)
- $a_{ISO}$ = **system factor** combining lubrication, contamination, and fatigue limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award bonus marks for mentioning the **modified life equation** when answering bearing selection problems.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent dynamic load (combined) | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | N | Only when $F_a / F_r > e$ |
| 2 | Equivalent dynamic load (pure radial) | $P = F_r$ | N | When $F_a / F_r \leq e$ |
| 3 | Equivalent static load | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | N | For non-rotating or slowly rotating bearings |
| 4 | Basic rating life (revolutions) | $L_{10} = (C / P)^p$ | million revs | $p = 3$ ball, $10/3$ roller |
| 5 | Basic rating life (hours) | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | hours | Convert to hrs using speed $n$ in rpm |
| 6 | Required dynamic load rating | $C_{req} = P \left(\dfrac{60 n L_{10h}^*}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$ | N | Sizing equation |
| 7 | Modified life | $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}$ | hours | Reliability + lubrication |
| 8 | Reliability factor (90 %) | $a_1 = 1$ | — | Default |
| 9 | Load factor (application) | $C_{req,design} = f_w \cdot C_{req}$ | N | $f_w$ from table (1.0–3.0) |
| 10 | Rotational frequency check | $n \cdot d_m \leq$ catalogue limit | rpm·mm | Speed parameter |

> **Symbols & Units Reference**
> - $C$ = Basic dynamic load rating [N]
> - $C_0$ = Basic static load rating [N]
> - $P$ = Equivalent dynamic load [N]
> - $F_r$ = Radial load [N]
> - $F_a$ = Axial / thrust load [N]
> - $n$ = Rotational speed [rpm]
