> - $d_m$ = Mean diameter [mm] $= (d + D)/2$

## 2.9 Application Utility in Real Engineering

- **Passenger car wheel hub bearing** — rated for 250,000 km at typical road speeds.
- **Wind turbine main shaft bearing** — uses modified life equation due to harsh environment ($a_{ISO} < 1$).
- **Race car engine bearing** — uses elevated $C_{req}$ to handle transient shock loads.
- **Electric vehicle motor bearing** — selection dominated by **high speed and temperature**, often requiring ceramic hybrid bearings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Life Equation — From Lundberg–Palmgren Theory to ISO 281

We walk through the **complete derivation chain** that connects the microscopic Hertzian contact stress to the macroscopic life equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$. This is the theoretical foundation KTU examiners love to test.

### Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Stress

When a ball of radius $r$ presses against a raceway with normal force $Q$, the maximum contact pressure $p_{max}$ follows Hertzian contact mechanics:

$$p_{max} = \dfrac{3 Q}{2 \pi a b}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the semi-axes of the contact ellipse. For ball-on-raceway contact:

$$p_{max} \propto Q^{1/3}$$

Thus the contact pressure scales with the **cube root** of load.

### Step 2 — Subsurface Shear Stress

The maximum shear stress occurs beneath the surface at depth $\approx 0.31 a$:

$$\tau_{max} = f(\text{geometry}) \cdot p_{max} \propto Q^{1/3}$$

### Step 3 — Lundberg–Palmgren Fatigue Hypothesis

Lundberg and Palmgren (1947) hypothesised that fatigue spalling originates at the point of maximum reversing shear stress $\tau_0$ located at depth $z_0$. The probability of survival $S$ after $N$ stress cycles is:

$$\ln \dfrac{1}{S} \propto \dfrac{N^a \cdot \tau_0^c \cdot V}{z_0^{h}}$$

where $V$ is the stressed volume. Combining this with the Hertzian scaling:

$$\tau_0 \propto Q^{1/3}, \quad z_0 \propto Q^{2/3}, \quad V \propto Q$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\ln \dfrac{1}{S} \propto N^a \cdot Q^{c/3} \cdot Q \cdot Q^{-2h/3}$$

The exponent of $Q$ collapses via empirical constants to yield:

$$\ln \dfrac{1}{S} \propto \dfrac{N^a \cdot Q^c}{z_0^h}$$

After algebraic collapse (Lundberg–Palmgren showed $c/e \to$ exponent $p$):

$$L_{10} \propto \left(\dfrac{Q_{max}}{Q}\right)^3 \quad \text{(ball)}$$

### Step 4 — From Single Contact to Bearing

For a bearing with $z$ rolling elements, the load on the most heavily loaded element $Q_{max}$ is related to the equivalent dynamic load $P$ by:

$$Q_{max} = \dfrac{P}{z \cdot J_r}$$

where $J_r$ is the **raceway integration constant** depending on bearing type (radial, thrust, etc.).

### Step 5 — The Final Life Equation

Substituting and defining the **basic dynamic load rating $C$** as the load at which $L_{10} = 1$ million revolutions:

$$\boxed{L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p}$$

For ball bearings, $p = 3$. For roller bearings, line contact gives $p = 10/3$.

> **Why $p = 10/3$ for rollers?** In a roller bearing, the contact is a *line*, not a point. The stressed volume scales differently, and Palmgren showed that the load-life exponent becomes $10/3 \approx 3.333$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Required Dynamic Load Rating

We are given a desired life $L_{10h}^*$ in hours at speed $n$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Convert hours to revolutions:**

$$N_{target} = 60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}^* \quad \text{[revolutions]}$$

**Step 2 — Convert revolutions to millions of revolutions:**

$$L_{10,target} = \dfrac{N_{target}}{10^6} = \dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}^*}{10^6}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ISO life equation and solve for $C$:**

From $L_{10} = (C / P)^p$:

$$\dfrac{C}{P} = L_{10}^{1/p}$$

$$\boxed{C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}^*}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}}$$

> **Intuition**: The longer you want the bearing to last, or the heavier the load, or the faster it spins — the larger the catalogue $C$ you must select.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem**: A deep-groove ball bearing in an automotive gearbox carries a radial load $F_r = 4000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 1500$ N. The shaft rotates at $n = 3000$ rpm. Catalogue data: $C = 35{,}000$ N, $C_0 = 19{,}000$ N, $e = 0.22$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.85$, $X_0 = 0.6$, $Y_0 = 0.5$. Determine (i) equivalent dynamic load $P$, (ii) basic rating life $L_{10h}$ in hours, and (iii) verify static load adequacy.

### Solution — Step 1: Compute $F_a / F_r$

$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1500}{4000} = 0.375$$

Since $0.375 > e = 0.22$:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = (0.56)(4000) + (1.85)(1500)$$

$$P = 2240 + 2775 = 5015 \text{ N}$$

### Solution — Step 2: Compute $L_{10}$ (millions of revolutions)

For a ball bearing, $p = 3$:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{35{,}000}{5015}\right)^3 = (6.979)^3$$

$$L_{10} = 339.8 \text{ million revolutions}$$

### Solution — Step 3: Convert to hours

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot L_{10} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot 3000} \cdot 339.8$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{339.8}{0.18} \approx 1888 \text{ hours}$$

### Solution — Step 4: Static Load Verification

$$P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a = (0.6)(4000) + (0.5)(1500) = 2400 + 750 = 3150 \text{ N}$$

Check: $P_0 = 3150$ N $\leq C_0 = 19{,}000$ N ✓ (Static OK with large safety margin.)

### Solution — Step 5: Modified Life (using $a_1 = 1$, $a_{ISO} = 0.8$)

$$L_{nm} = 1 \times 0.8 \times 1888 = 1510 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition**: The examiner will provide you with catalogue values for $X$, $Y$, $e$, $C$, $C_0$. Always compute $F_a/F_r$ first, **compare with $e$**, and then pick the correct branch of the formula.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Bearing Family Selection

The following table helps the engineer (and the KTU student) decide between bearing families:

| Selection Criterion | Ball Bearing | Cylindrical Roller | Tapered Roller | Spherical Roller | Needle Roller |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Radial load capacity | Medium | High | High | Very High | High |
| Axial load capacity | Low–Medium (depends on type) | None (NU, N) | High | Medium | None |
| Speed capability | Very High | High | Medium | Medium–High | High |
| Misalignment tolerance | Low (~2–10′) | Very Low (~2′) | Low (~2′) | Very High (~0.5°–2°) | Low |
| Friction torque | Very Low | Low | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Shock load resistance | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Medium |
| Cost | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium–High | High | Low |
| Typical auto use | Wheel hub, alternator | Gearbox, transmission | Wheel hub, diff | Heavy CV joints, industrial | Gearbox, rocker arms |

## 3.5 Quantitative Application Factor $f_w$

Real-world applications have **vibration and shock**. The catalogue $C$ is measured under ideal laboratory conditions, so engineers inflate $C_{req}$ by an **application factor $f_w$**:

$$C_{design} = f_w \cdot C_{req}$$

| Operating Conditions | $f_w$ |
|---|---|
| Smooth, vibration-free | 1.0 – 1.2 |
| Light shock, normal machinery | 1.2 – 1.8 |
| Moderate shock (automotive transmission) | 1.8 – 2.5 |
| Heavy shock (rolling mill, crusher) | 2.5 – 3.0 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The KTU Bearing Selection Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Application] --> B[Determine Loads: Fr and Fa]
    B --> C[Determine Operating Speed n in rpm]
    C --> D[Determine Desired Life L10h in hours]
    D --> E[Compute Load Ratio Fa / Fr]
    E --> F{Fa / Fr greater than e?}
    F -- No --> G[P equals Fr]
    F -- Yes --> H[P equals X Fr plus Y Fa]
    G --> I[Compute Creq using Life Equation]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Application Factor fw]
    J --> K[Cdesign equals fw times Creq]
    K --> L[Select Bearing: Ccatalogue greater than or equal to Cdesign]
    L --> M{Static Check: P0 less than or equal to C0?}
    M -- No --> N[Upsize Bearing and Reiterate]
    M -- Yes --> O[Check Speed Parameter n times dm]
    O --> P{Speed less than catalogue limit?}
    P -- No --> Q[Consider Ceramic Hybrid or Oil-Air Lubrication]
    P -- Yes --> R[Check Lubrication Regime]
    R --> S[Confirm Modified Life Lnm]
    S --> T[Final Bearing Selected]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bearing Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Conditions]
        A1[Radial Load Fr]
        A2[Axial Load Fa]
        A3[Rotational Speed n]
        A4[Desired Life L10h]
        A5[Operating Temperature T]
    end

    subgraph CALC[Calculation Engine]
        B1[Compute Equivalent Load P]
        B2[Compute Required Creq]
        B3[Apply Modification Factors]
        B4[Modified Life Lnm]
    end

    subgraph CATALOG[Catalogue Search]
        C1[Bearing Family Filter]
        C2[Series Filter: 60 62 63 64]
        C3[Bore Diameter d]
        C4[Match C greater than Creq]
    end

    subgraph VERIFY[Verification Stage]
        D1[Static Load Check P0]
        D2[Speed Parameter Check]
        D3[Lubrication Adequacy]
        D4[Thermal Margin]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    A5 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> OUT[Finalised Bearing Reference]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Bearing Family Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify Load Type] --> T{Pure Axial Thrust?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Consider Thrust Ball or Spherical Roller Thrust]
    T -- No --> V{Pure Radial Load?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Consider Cylindrical Roller NU N or Deep Groove Ball]
    V -- No --> X{Combined Radial and Axial?}
    X -- Yes --> Y{Speed High or Low?}
    Y -- High Speed --> Z[Deep Groove Ball or Angular Contact Ball]
    Y -- Low Speed with High Load --> AA[Tapered Roller or Spherical Roller]
    X -- No --> AB[Misalignment Expected?]
    AB -- Yes --> AC[Spherical Roller or Self-Aligning Ball]
    AB -- No --> AD[Default Deep Groove Ball]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Equivalent Load Concept (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACTUAL[Actual Mixed Loading]
        R1[Radial Force Fr on Shaft]
        T1[Axial Thrust Fa on Shaft]
    end

    subgraph EQUIV[Hypothetical Pure Radial Loading]
        REQ[Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    end

    R1 --> EQUIV
    T1 --> EQUIV

    EQUIV --> LIFE[Same Fatigue Life L10]
```

> The concept of equivalent load allows the engineer to treat the complex combined loading as a single radial load that produces **the same fatigue damage** in the bearing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define basic dynamic load rating $C$ of a rolling contact bearing. Why is it different from basic static load rating $C_0$?**

**Model Answer**:
> **Basic dynamic load rating $C$** is defined (per ISO 281) as the constant, purely radial (or central axial for thrust bearings) load that a bearing can theoretically endure for a basic rating life of **one million revolutions**. It represents fatigue capacity under rotating contact stress.
>
> **Basic static load rating $C_0$** is the load at which the **permanent indentation (brinelling)** of the raceway at the most heavily loaded contact point equals **0.0001 of the ball diameter** (or roller diameter). It is a **deformation limit**, not a fatigue limit.
>
> The two are fundamentally different because:
> - $C$ governs **fatigue spalling** under **cyclic rotating loads**.
> - $C_0$ governs **plastic deformation** under **stationary or very slowly rotating loads**.
>
> Thus, $C_0$ is used for static checks ($P_0 \leq C_0$), and $C$ is used for dynamic life calculation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is equivalent dynamic load? Why is the factor $e$ important in its calculation?**

**Model Answer**:
> The **equivalent dynamic load $P$** is a hypothetical single radial load (constant in magnitude and direction) which, if applied to the bearing, would give the **same fatigue life** as the actual combined radial and axial loading.
>
> The **factor $e$** is a catalogue constant that defines the **transition point** between two load regimes:
> - When $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$: the axial load is borne by the contact ellipse without climbing onto the shoulder; thus $P = F_r$.
> - When $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$: the axial load begins to push the contact onto the shoulder, adding extra fatigue stress; thus $P = X F_r + Y F_a$.
>
> Skipping the comparison of $F_a / F_r$ with $e$ is a common student mistake that leads to **incorrect life estimation**.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> A deep-groove ball bearing is to be selected for an automotive gearbox. The bearing is subjected to a radial load $F_r = 5000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 1800$ N. The shaft speed is $n = 1800$ rpm. The desired life is 8,000 hours. Catalogue data: $e = 0.24$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.80$, $C_0 = 23{,}000$ N, $X_0 = 0.6$, $Y_0 = 0.5$. Assume application factor $f_w = 1.4$.

#### (a) Determine the equivalent dynamic load $P$ and the required basic dynamic load rating $C_{req}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Compute $F_a / F_r$**

$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1800}{5000} = 0.36$$

Since $0.36 > e = 0.24$, use combined formula:

$$P = X F_r + Y F_a = (0.56)(5000) + (1.80)(1800)$$

$$P = 2800 + 3240 = 6040 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_{req}$**

For ball bearing, $p = 3$:

$$C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}^*}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$

$$C_{req} = 6040 \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot 1800 \cdot 8000}{10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$C_{req} = 6040 \cdot (864)^{1/3} = 6040 \cdot 9.527$$

$$C_{req} \approx 57{,}540 \text{ N}$$

#### (b) Select a suitable catalogue bearing, verify the static load, and estimate the modified life using $a_1 = 1$ and $a_{ISO} = 0.85$. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Apply application factor**

$$C_{design} = f_w \cdot C_{req} = 1.4 \times 57{,}540 = 80{,}556 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Catalogue selection**

A standard deep-groove ball bearing (e.g., SKF 6310) has $C = 83{,}200$ N (greater than $C_{design} = 80{,}556$ N). Select **6310 series, $C = 83{,}200$ N**.

**Step 3: Static load verification**

$$P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a = (0.6)(5000) + (0.5)(1800) = 3000 + 900 = 3900 \text{ N}$$

Since $P_0 = 3900 \text{ N} \leq C_0 = 23{,}000 \text{ N}$, **static check passes comfortably**.

**Step 4: Modified life**

First compute catalogue $L_{10h}$:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{83{,}200}{6040}\right)^3 = (13.775)^3 \approx 2614 \text{ million revolutions}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot 1800} \cdot 2614 = \dfrac{2614}{0.108} \approx 24{,}204 \text{ hours}$$

Modified life:

$$L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10h} = 1 \times 0.85 \times 24{,}204 \approx 20{,}573 \text{ hours}$$

This is well above the 8,000-hour requirement. **Selection is safe.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> A tapered roller bearing on an automotive wheel hub carries a radial load $F_r = 8000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 3000$ N at $n = 1200$ rpm. Catalogue values: $e = 0.30$, $X = 0.40$, $Y = 1.60$, $C_0 = 38{,}000$ N, $X_0 = 0.5$, $Y_0 = 0.84$. Required life is 12,000 hours. Application factor $f_w = 1.5$.

#### (a) Determine $P$, $C_{req}$, and select the bearing with $f_w$. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1: $F_a / F_r$ check**

$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{3000}{8000} = 0.375$$

Since $0.375 > e = 0.30$:

$$P = X F_r + Y F_a = (0.40)(8000) + (1.60)(3000) = 3200 + 4800 = 8000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: $C_{req}$ for roller bearing ($p = 10/3$)**

$$C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}^*}{10^6}\right)^{3/10}$$

$$C_{req} = 8000 \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot 1200 \cdot 12{,}000}{10^6}\right)^{3/10} = 8000 \cdot (864)^{0.3}$$

$$\ln(864) = 6.762, \quad 0.3 \times 6.762 = 2.029, \quad e^{2.029} = 7.605$$

$$C_{req} = 8000 \times 7.605 = 60{,}840 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: With $f_w$**

$$C_{design} = 1.5 \times 60{,}840 = 91{,}260 \text{ N}$$

A standard tapered roller bearing (e.g., Timken 30210) has $C \approx 95{,}000$ N. Select this.

#### (b) Verify static load and compute the modified life with $a_1 = 0.62$ (99% reliability) and $a_{ISO} = 0.9$. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Static load check**

$$P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a = (0.5)(8000) + (0.84)(3000) = 4000 + 2520 = 6520 \text{ N}$$

$P_0 = 6520 \text{ N} \leq C_0 = 38{,}000$ N ✓

**Step 2: Catalogue $L_{10h}$**

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} = \left(\dfrac{95{,}000}{8000}\right)^{3.333} = (11.875)^{3.333}$$

$$\ln(11.875) = 2.474, \quad 3.333 \times 2.474 = 8.247, \quad e^{8.247} = 3815$$

$$L_{10} = 3815 \text{ million revolutions}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{3815}{60 \cdot 1200 / 10^6} = \dfrac{3815}{0.072} \approx 52{,}986 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: Modified life**

$$L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10h} = 0.62 \times 0.9 \times 52{,}986 \approx 29{,}566 \text{ hours}$$

Bearing far exceeds 12,000-hour target. **Selection validated.**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $F_a / F_r$ vs $e$ comparison**: Writing $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ blindly even when $F_a/F_r \leq e$ — loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using wrong exponent $p$**: Using $p = 3$ for roller bearings instead of $p = 10/3$ — loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the application factor $f_w$**: KTU examiners often expect $C_{design} = f_w \cdot C_{req}$, not just $C_{req}$.
> 4. **Skipping static load check**: A complete selection **must** verify $P_0 \leq C_0$.
> 5. **Unit confusion in $L_{10h}$**: Forgetting the $60 \cdot n$ denominator when converting from revolutions to hours.
> 6. **Ignoring the modified life equation**: Failing to use $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}$ when reliability > 90 % is specified.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Selection of Bearings**

- **Two governing equations**: Dynamic life $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$, and required rating $C_{req} = P \left(\dfrac{60 n L_{10h}^*}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$.
- **Exponent $p$**: **3** for ball bearings, **10/3** for roller bearings — never confuse.
- **Equivalent load**: Always check $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$ vs catalogue $e$ first.
- **Static check**: $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a \leq C_0$ is **mandatory**.
- **Application factor $f_w$**: Multiplies $C_{req}$ to account for vibration/shock; ranges **1.0 to 3.0**.
- **Modified life**: $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}$ — use when reliability > 90 %.
- **Bearing family rule of thumb**: Balls for speed, rollers for load, tapered for combined load, spherical for misalignment, needle for compactness.
- **Units sanity check**: $C$ and $P$ always in **N**; speed in **rpm**; life in **hours** or **million revolutions**.
- **Automotive-specific**: Wheel hubs → tapered roller or deep-groove ball; gearbox → cylindrical/needle; crankshaft → hydrodynamic plain journal.
- **Always use a manufacturer catalogue** (SKF, FAG, NTN, Timken) for the final selection — the formula gives $C_{req}$, but only the catalogue gives the actual part number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Equivalent Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Equivalent Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Equivalent Load (P or P_eq)**
> The *dynamic equivalent load* is a **hypothetical, constant, purely radial load** (for radial bearings) or **purely axial load** (for thrust bearings) which, if applied to a bearing with a stationary outer ring and a rotating inner ring, would produce the **same operational life** as the bearing actually experiences under the **real, time-varying combined radial and axial loading** conditions encountered in service.

In automotive engineering, no bearing is loaded with a single steady value. A wheel bearing sees bumps, cornering forces, and braking torques. A transmission bearing sees mesh forces that fluctuate with gear ratios. A pinion bearing sees varying tooth forces. The dynamic equivalent load **condenses this messy reality into one single number** that can be plugged into the life equation $L = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$ revolutions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Water" Intuition

Imagine a worker carrying buckets of water of **different weights** from a well to a field, for **different durations** during the day. The worker is exhausted at the end. Now, suppose we want to know: *"What is the **single constant bucket weight** that would have tired the worker out equally?"*

The dynamic equivalent load is exactly this "constant bucket weight" — but for the bearing. It mathematically finds the **cube-root-weighted average** (for balls) of the actual loads, biased by how long each load was present, such that the cumulative fatigue damage is identical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cube Root?** Because bearing life is governed by $L \propto 1/P^3$ (for ball bearings). A heavy load dominates fatigue damage *much* more than its duration alone would suggest, so we use the cube of the load in the averaging process. The "3" comes from the contact mechanics of a sphere on a raceway (Hertzian point contact exponent).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Bearing Life Exponent ($p$)**:
  - $p = 3$ for **Ball Bearings** (point contact)
  - $p = 10/3$ for **Roller Bearings** (line contact)
- **Standard Reference Speed**: Inner ring rotating, outer ring stationary
- **Units**: All loads in **Newtons (N)**, life in **millions of revolutions**
- **e (dimensionless)**: A bearing-geometric constant — the "transition ratio" between purely radial and combined loading regimes
- **X, Y (dimensionless)**: Radial and axial load factors, read from manufacturer catalogs (SKF, FAG, NTN, Timken)

### 1.4 Automotive Relevance — Why This Topic Matters

| Automotive Subsystem | Nature of Loading on Bearing |
|---|---|
| Wheel Hub Bearing | Combined radial (vehicle weight) + axial (cornering/braking thrust) |
| Transmission Gearbox | Variable mesh load at shifting speeds |
| Differential Pinion | Combined radial + axial + fluctuating torque reaction |
| Engine Main/Crank | Cyclic combustion load (very high variability) |
| Steering Column | Constant axial + variable radial from road feedback |

Every one of these requires the **dynamic equivalent load** to size the bearing correctly for its rated life.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Categories of Bearings — and Why Their Formulas Differ

Bearings are split based on **contact geometry**, which dictates the load-life exponent $p$:

- **Ball bearings** (point contact) → $p = 3$ → formula is a **cube root** average
- **Roller bearings** (line contact) → $p = 10/3$ → formula is a **3/10 power** average

Within each, we further split by **load type**:
- Pure radial contact (deep-groove ball, cylindrical roller)
- Combined contact (angular contact ball, tapered roller, self-aligning)
- Pure thrust (thrust ball, thrust roller)

### 2.2 The Logic Behind the $e$ Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $e$ value is a dimensionless threshold ratio** $e = f(F_a/C_0)$ where $C_0$ is the **static load rating**. It separates two physical regimes:
> 1. **Light axial load** ($F_a/F_r \le e$): The axial load is too small to tilt the contact ellipse; radial load dominates → $X = 1, Y = 0$
> 2. **Heavy axial load** ($F_a/F_r > e$): The axial load tilts the contact ellipse, the inner ring lifts, and load is shared between radial and axial directions → $X$ drops, $Y$ becomes significant

This is **why a deep-groove ball bearing can carry small axial loads** without derating its radial capacity, but large axial loads *do* reduce its effective radial load capacity.

### 2.3 The Origin of the "0.4" Factor in Combined-Load Formulas

For angular contact ball bearings, the inner ring is **offset** by a contact angle $\alpha$. Geometrically:

$$X = 0.4 \quad \text{because} \quad X \approx \frac{1}{\tan(\alpha)} \cdot \frac{\cos(\alpha)}{1} \approx 0.4 \text{ when } \alpha \approx 68°$$

This is a *direct geometric derivation*, not a fitted constant. KTU often asks students to justify the value $0.4$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use **Newtons (N)** for loads and **$10^6$ revolutions** for life. Subscript "r" = radial, "a" = axial/thrust, "c" or "C" = basic dynamic load rating.

| # | Bearing Type | Dynamic Equivalent Load Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ball Bearing — combined load** | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | When $F_a/F_r > e$ |
| 2 | **Ball Bearing — light axial** | $P = F_r$ | When $F_a/F_r \le e$; $X=1, Y=0$ |
| 3 | **Angular Contact Ball** | $P = 0.4 F_r + Y F_a$ | When $F_a/F_r > e$ (typical $\alpha = 40°$) |
| 4 | **Self-Aligning Ball** | $P = F_r + Y_1 F_a$ | When $F_a/F_r > e$; uses $Y_1, Y_2$ |
| 5 | **Tapered Roller** | $P = 0.4 F_r + Y F_a$ | When $F_a/F_r > e$ |
| 6 | **Cylindrical Roller** | $P = F_r$ | Cannot take axial load ($Y = 0$) |
| 7 | **Needle Roller** | $P = F_r$ | Same as cylindrical roller |
| 8 | **Thrust Ball ($\alpha = 90°$)** | $P = F_a$ | Pure axial |
| 9 | **Thrust Ball — combined** | $P = 1.2 F_r + F_a$ | For $\alpha = 90°$ spherical seat |
| 10 | **Variable Load (general)** | $P_{eq} = \left( \dfrac{\sum P_i^p \cdot n_i \cdot t_i}{\sum n_i \cdot t_i} \right)^{1/p}$ | $p=3$ ball, $p=10/3$ roller |
| 11 | **Variable Load (rotational)** | $P_{eq} = \left( \dfrac{\sum P_i^p \cdot \theta_i}{\sum \theta_i} \right)^{1/p}$ | $\theta_i$ = revolutions in stage $i$ |
| 12 | **L10 Life (basic)** | $L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P_{eq}} \right)^p \times 10^6$ revs | Standard KTU governing equation |

### 2.5 Typical Values of $X, Y, e$ (for reference, KTU exam values)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU exam values are given in the problem statement** as a small table. Never assume a value; always read the values for the *specific* bearing from the data provided in the question.

| Bearing Type | $e$ (typical) | $X$ (when $F_a/F_r > e$) | $Y$ (when $F_a/F_r > e$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deep Groove Ball 6xxx | 0.19 to 0.44 | 0.56 | 1.0 to 2.3 |
| Angular Contact 7xxx ($\alpha = 40°$) | 0.68 | 0.41 | 0.87 |
| Tapered Roller 3xxxx | 0.30 to 0.40 | 0.4 | 1.4 to 1.9 |
| Self-Aligning 1xxx | 0.40 to 0.80 | 0.65 to 1.0 | varies |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Life Prediction**: Once $P_{eq}$ is known, $L_{10} = (C/P_{eq})^p \times 10^6$ gives the **rated fatigue life** in millions of revolutions.
- **Selection of Bearing Size**: Manufacturer catalogs list $C$ for each bearing. The designer inverts the life equation to find the **minimum $C$ required**, then picks the next-larger standard bearing.
- **Reliability Adjustment**: For non-90% reliability, a correction factor $a_1$ (ISO 281) is applied: $L_{na} = a_1 \cdot L_{10}$.
- **Variable-Speed/Multistage Operation**: Required for **duty-cycle analysis** in EVs, ICE engines, and CVT transmissions where load and speed both fluctuate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Dynamic Load for Variable Loading (Ball Bearing, $p = 3$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 2** — practice writing it from memory. Marks are awarded for every clean logical step.

**Starting Premise:** The basic rating life equation for a ball bearing under a steady load $P$ is:

$$L = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

This means: under load $P$ for $N$ revolutions, the **fractional damage** consumed is:

$$D_i = \frac{N_i}{L_i} = \frac{N_i}{\left(\dfrac{C}{P_i}\right)^3 \times 10^6} = \frac{P_i^3 \cdot N_i}{C^3 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 1:** Suppose the bearing operates under $k$ different load stages. In stage $i$, the load is $P_i$ for $N_i$ revolutions. The damage in stage $i$ is:

$$D_i = \frac{P_i^3 \cdot N_i}{C^3 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 2:** Total accumulated damage over the entire duty cycle is:

$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{P_i^3 \cdot N_i}{C^3 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 3:** The bearing reaches end-of-life when the cumulative damage equals 1 (Miner's rule):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{P_i^3 \cdot N_i}{C^3 \times 10^6} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging to isolate the term containing $C$:

$$C^3 \times 10^6 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i^3 \cdot N_i$$

**Step 5:** Now define the **dynamic equivalent load $P_{eq}$** as the single constant load that would cause the same total damage over the total revolution count $N_{total} = \sum N_i$:

$$D_{total} = \frac{P_{eq}^3 \cdot N_{total}}{C^3 \times 10^6} = 1$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$C^3 \times 10^6 = P_{eq}^3 \cdot N_{total} = P_{eq}^3 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i$$

**Step 7:** Equating the two expressions for $C^3 \times 10^6$ from Step 4 and Step 6:

$$P_{eq}^3 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i^3 \cdot N_i$$

**Step 8:** Solving for $P_{eq}$ (taking the cube root on both sides):

$$\boxed{P_{eq} = \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i^3 \cdot N_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i} \right)^{1/3}}$$

**Step 9:** If loads are given in terms of operating **time intervals** $t_i$ at constant **speeds** $n_i$ (in rpm), then the revolutions in stage $i$ are $N_i = n_i \cdot t_i \cdot 60$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{P_{eq} = \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i^3 \cdot n_i \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot t_i} \right)^{1/3}}$$

---

### 3.2 Extension: Roller Bearing (p = 10/3)

Replace the exponent $3$ with $10/3$ throughout the derivation. The final result is:

$$P_{eq} = \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i^{10/3} \cdot N_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i} \right)^{3/10}$$

The structure of the derivation is identical — only the exponent changes due to the different Hertzian contact mechanics (line contact for rollers vs. point contact for balls).

---

### 3.3 Combined-Load Formula Justification (Why $X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$?)

For a bearing with contact angle $\alpha$ under combined radial $F_r$ and axial $F_a$ loading, the load distribution along the contact ellipse is **not uniform**. A theoretical analysis (Lundberg-Palmgren) shows the **equivalent dynamic load** is the **load component perpendicular to the contact plane**, integrated over the contact ellipse.

For angular contact bearings, this reduces to the **linear combination**:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

where $X = \cos(\alpha)$ and $Y = \sin(\alpha)$ *for the limiting case*. The catalog values are empirical refinements of this geometric result, hence the appearance of values like $0.4$ and $0.56$ in the tables.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 6308 deep-groove ball bearing operates under the following duty cycle. Compute the equivalent dynamic load.
> - Stage 1: $F_1 = 3$ kN at 1500 rpm for 20% of duty cycle
> - Stage 2: $F_2 = 6$ kN at 900 rpm for 50% of duty cycle
> - Stage 3: $F_3 = 2$ kN at 600 rpm for 30% of duty cycle
>
> (Assume $F_a = 0$ for simplicity.)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Since $F_a = 0$, we have $P_i = F_{r,i}$ for each stage.

**Step 2:** Take the duty cycle time as $T$. Then:
- $t_1 = 0.20T$, $n_1 = 1500$ rpm
- $t_2 = 0.50T$, $n_2 = 900$ rpm
- $t_3 = 0.30T$, $n_3 = 600$ rpm

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator $\sum n_i t_i$:

$$\sum n_i t_i = (1500)(0.20T) + (900)(0.50T) + (600)(0.30T)$$

$$= T \cdot (300 + 450 + 180) = 930T \text{ rpm·hrs (units consistent)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator $\sum P_i^3 \cdot n_i \cdot t_i$ (note: $P_i$ in N, convert kN to N by multiplying by 1000):

$$P_1^3 = (3000)^3 = 2.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ N}^3$$
$$P_2^3 = (6000)^3 = 21.6 \times 10^{10} \text{ N}^3$$
$$P_3^3 = (2000)^3 = 0.8 \times 10^{10} \text{ N}^3$$

**Step 5:** Compute the weighted sum:

$$\sum P_i^3 \cdot n_i \cdot t_i = T \cdot \left[ (2.7 \times 10^{10})(300) + (21.6 \times 10^{10})(450) + (0.8 \times 10^{10})(180) \right]$$

$$= T \times 10^{10} \cdot \left[ 810 + 9720 + 144 \right]$$

$$= T \times 10^{10} \cdot 10674 = 1.0674 \times 10^{14} \cdot T$$

**Step 6:** Divide numerator by denominator:

$$\frac{\sum P_i^3 \cdot n_i t_i}{\sum n_i t_i} = \frac{1.0674 \times 10^{14} \cdot T}{930T} = 1.1477 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 7:** Take the cube root:

$$P_{eq} = (1.1477 \times 10^{11})^{1/3} \approx 4862 \text{ N} \approx 4.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** $P_{eq} \approx 4.86$ kN. This is the **single constant load** the bearing experiences for life-equivalence purposes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $P_{eq}$ (4.86 kN) lies between the minimum (2 kN) and maximum (6 kN) actual loads, but is **skewed toward the heavier load** (6 kN) due to the cubic weighting — exactly as expected by physical intuition. A simple arithmetic mean would give 3.67 kN, which would **underestimate** the damage and lead to premature bearing failure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture — Procedure to Compute Dynamic Equivalent Load

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Known Loading Condition] --> qA1{Is axial load Fa present?}
    qA1 -- No --> pathA1[Set P = Fr directly]
    qA1 -- Yes --> qA2{Is load constant or variable?}
    qA2 -- Constant --> pathA3[Compute Fa / Fr ratio]
    pathA3 --> qA3{Is Fa / Fr less or equal to e?}
    qA3 -- Yes --> pathA4[X = 1, Y = 0: P = Fr]
    qA3 -- No --> pathA5[Read X, Y from catalog]
    pathA5 --> pathA6[P = X Fr + Y Fa]
    pathA6 --> lifeA[Compute L10 life]
    pathA4 --> lifeA
    pathA1 --> lifeA
    qA2 -- Variable --> pathB1[Tabulate Pi, ni, ti for each stage]
    pathB1 --> qB1{Ball or Roller bearing?}
    qB1 -- Ball p=3 --> pathB2[Peq = sum of Pi cubed ni ti / sum ni ti to the 1/3]
    qB1 -- Roller p=10 by 3 --> pathB3[Peq = sum of Pi to 10/3 ni ti / sum ni ti to 3/10]
    pathB2 --> lifeA
    pathB3 --> lifeA
    lifeA[Compute L10 from L10 = C over Peq to the p times 10 to the 6]
    styleA1[style startA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545]
    style lifeA fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#239B56,stroke-width:3px,color:#0B2545]
    style pathB2 fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B,color:#0B2545]
    style pathB3 fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B,color:#0B2545]
```

### 4.2 Bearing Selection Logic — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loadA[Actual Variable Load Pi at Speed ni for Time ti] --> eqA[Compute Dynamic Equivalent Load Peq]
    eqA --> reqA[Required Basic Dynamic Load Rating C from Life Equation]
    reqA --> selA[Select Standard Bearing with C catalog greater or equal to C required]
    selA --> checkA{Fa greater than Ca capacity?}
    checkA -- Yes --> upA[Move to Next Larger Bearing Size]
    checkA -- No --> finalA[Final Bearing Selection Complete]
    upA --> selA
    style loadA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#0B2545]
    style eqA fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B,color:#0B2545]
    style reqA fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#C0392B,color:#0B2545]
    style selA fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#239B56,color:#0B2545]
    style finalA fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#145A32,stroke-width:3px,color:#0B2545]
```

### 4.3 Physical Bearing Schematic — Load Components & Contact Angle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisA[Vertical Bearing Axis] --> ballA[Ball Element at Contact Angle alpha]
    ballA --> raceA[Inner Raceway Contact Point]
    ballA --> raceB[Outer Raceway Contact Point]
    loadA[Radial Load Fr acting downward] --> ballA
    loadB[Axial Load Fa acting horizontally] --> ballA
    ballA --> compA[Equivalent Load P perpendicular to contact line]
    style ballA fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545]
    style compA fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#239B56,color:#0B2545]
    style loadA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#0B2545]
    style loadB fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B,color:#0B2545]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — $e$ Parameter Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA1[Given Fr and Fa] --> qA2[Read e from catalog for given Fa over C0]
    qA2 --> qA3{Is Fa divided by Fr less or equal to e?}
    qA3 -- Yes --> outA[Use P = Fr only, X equals 1 and Y equals 0]
    qA3 -- No --> outB[Read X and Y from catalog table for given Fa over C0]
    outB --> outC[Use P = X times Fr plus Y times Fa]
    style qA1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#0B2545]
    style outA fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#239B56,color:#0B2545]

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "dynamic equivalent load" as applied to rolling contact bearings. Why is it needed in bearing design?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The dynamic equivalent load $P$ is a hypothetical, constant, purely radial load (for radial bearings) that, if applied to a bearing with a stationary outer ring and rotating inner ring, would give the **same operational life** as the bearing actually experiences under the real, time-varying combined radial and axial loading conditions in service.
>
> **Why Needed:**
> 1. **Real automotive loads are variable** (bumps, cornering, gear shifts, combustion cycles). We need a **single number** to plug into the life equation $L = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$.
> 2. It allows **direct comparison with the catalog basic dynamic load rating $C$**, enabling bearing selection from manufacturer tables.
> 3. It accounts for the **non-linear (cubic for balls) damage accumulation** under fluctuating loads via the cube-root averaging.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the factor '$e$' in the dynamic equivalent load calculation for ball bearings?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Significance of $e$:**
> 1. $e$ is a **dimensionless threshold ratio** that depends on the bearing type and the ratio $F_a/C_0$ (axial load to static load rating).
> 2. It **delineates two physical regimes**:
>    - If $F_a/F_r \le e$: axial load is small, the contact ellipse is not tilted, and the radial load factor $X=1$, axial factor $Y=0$. Effectively, $P = F_r$.
>    - If $F_a/F_r > e$: axial load tilts the contact ellipse, and the load must be shared — $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ with $X < 1$ and $Y > 0$.
> 3. It prevents the designer from **over-penalizing** small axial loads (which deep-groove ball bearings can carry without much derating) and ensures **proper accounting** of large axial loads.
> 4. Values of $e$ are obtained from manufacturer catalogs (e.g., SKF, FAG) for each specific bearing series.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

#### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the dynamic equivalent load when a ball bearing operates under variable load and speed conditions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6205 deep-groove ball bearing operates on the following duty cycle:
- Stage 1: $F_1 = 2$ kN, $n_1 = 1440$ rpm, duty $= 30\%$
- Stage 2: $F_2 = 4$ kN, $n_2 = 960$ rpm, duty $= 50\%$
- Stage 3: $F_3 = 1$ kN, $n_3 = 720$ rpm, duty $= 20\%$

Compute the equivalent dynamic load. The basic dynamic load rating $C = 14$ kN. Also find the $L_{10}$ life in hours.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. Key result to be boxed:

$$P_{eq} = \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i^3 \cdot n_i \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot t_i} \right)^{1/3}$$

The derivation is based on **Miner's linear damage rule**: cumulative damage $\sum D_i = 1$ at end-of-life, where $D_i = P_i^3 N_i / (C^3 \times 10^6)$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Since $F_a = 0$ (no axial load given), $P_i = F_i$ for each stage.

**Step 2:** Let the total cycle time be $T$ hours. Then:
- $t_1 = 0.30T$ hr, $n_1 = 1440$ rpm
- $t_2 = 0.50T$ hr, $n_2 = 960$ rpm
- $t_3 = 0.20T$ hr, $n_3 = 720$ rpm

**Step 3:** Convert all loads to Newtons:
- $P_1 = 2000$ N
- $P_2 = 4000$ N
- $P_3 = 1000$ N

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator $\sum n_i t_i$:

$$\sum n_i t_i = (1440)(0.30T) + (960)(0.50T) + (720)(0.20T)$$
$$= T(432 + 480 + 144) = 1056T$$

**Step 5:** Compute each $P_i^3$:
- $P_1^3 = (2000)^3 = 8.0 \times 10^9$ N³
- $P_2^3 = (4000)^3 = 64.0 \times 10^9$ N³
- $P_3^3 = (1000)^3 = 1.0 \times 10^9$ N³

**Step 6:** Compute the numerator $\sum P_i^3 n_i t_i$:

$$\sum P_i^3 n_i t_i = T \times 10^9 \times \left[ (8.0)(432) + (64.0)(480) + (1.0)(144) \right]$$
$$= T \times 10^9 \times \left[ 3456 + 30720 + 144 \right]$$
$$= T \times 10^9 \times 34320 = 3.432 \times 10^{13} \cdot T$$

**Step 7:** Divide numerator by denominator:

$$\frac{3.432 \times 10^{13} \cdot T}{1056 T} = 3.25 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 8:** Take the cube root:

$$P_{eq} = (3.25 \times 10^{10})^{1/3}$$
$$= (32.5 \times 10^9)^{1/3} = (32.5)^{1/3} \times 10^3$$
$$\approx 3.19 \times 1000 \approx 3190 \text{ N} \approx 3.19 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 9:** Compute $L_{10}$ in revolutions:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P_{eq}}\right)^3 \times 10^6 = \left(\frac{14000}{3190}\right)^3 \times 10^6$$

$$\frac{C}{P_{eq}} = \frac{14000}{3190} = 4.388$$

$$L_{10} = (4.388)^3 \times 10^6 = 84.5 \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 10:** Compute average speed $\bar{n}$:

$$\bar{n} = \frac{\sum n_i t_i}{\sum t_i} = \frac{1056T}{T} = 1056 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 11:** Convert to hours:

$$L_{10, \text{hours}} = \frac{L_{10, \text{rev}}}{\bar{n} \times 60} = \frac{84.5 \times 10^6}{1056 \times 60} = \frac{84.5 \times 10^6}{63360}$$

$$\boxed{L_{10} \approx 1334 \text{ hours}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** For an angular contact ball bearing subjected to combined radial and axial loads, derive the combined-load formula $P = X F_r + Y F_a$. Explain the role of the contact angle $\alpha$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 7310 angular contact ball bearing carries a radial load $F_r = 5000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 2500$ N. From the catalog: $e = 0.68$, $X = 0.41$, $Y = 0.87$. Compute the dynamic equivalent load and the $L_{10}$ life in hours at 1800 rpm. Take $C = 85$ kN.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Combined-Load Formula:**

Consider a ball in an angular contact bearing. The contact between ball and raceway is along a line making an angle $\alpha$ with the radial plane (this is the **contact angle**).

- The **radial load $F_r$** has a component along the contact line of magnitude $F_r \cos(\alpha)$.
- The **axial load $F_a$** has a component along the contact line of magnitude $F_a \sin(\alpha)$.

The total load perpendicular to the contact ellipse (which determines fatigue) is therefore:

$$P = F_r \cos(\alpha) + F_a \sin(\alpha)$$

For a typical angular contact bearing with $\alpha \approx 40°$:
- $\cos(40°) \approx 0.766$
- $\sin(40°) \approx 0.643$

The **empirically fitted catalog values** $X = 0.41$ and $Y = 0.87$ are derived from extensive experimental data and account for:
1. Load distribution effects over multiple balls
2. Internal geometry variations
3. Hertzian contact ellipse geometry

Thus the **practical formula** becomes:

$$\boxed{P = X F_r + Y F_a}$$

**Role of Contact Angle $\alpha$:**
- Determines the **axial load capacity** of the bearing — higher $\alpha$ allows larger $F_a$ to be carried
- Influences the values of $X, Y, e$ in the catalog
- The line of action of the load is **offset** from the radial direction by $\alpha$, which is why angular contact bearings can support both radial and axial loads simultaneously

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio $F_a / F_r$:

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{2500}{5000} = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** Compare with the catalog $e$ value:

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = 0.5 \quad \text{vs.} \quad e = 0.68$$

Since $0.5 < 0.68$, we are in the **light axial load regime**.

**Step 3:** Apply the formula. In the light axial regime:

$$X = 1, \quad Y = 0$$

Therefore:

$$P = (1)(5000) + (0)(2500) = 5000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the load ratio $C/P$:

$$\frac{C}{P} = \frac{85000}{5000} = 17$$

**Step 5:** Compute $L_{10}$ in revolutions:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 \times 10^6 = (17)^3 \times 10^6$$

$$= 4913 \times 10^6 = 4.913 \times 10^9 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to hours at 1800 rpm:

$$L_{10, \text{hours}} = \frac{4.913 \times 10^9}{1800 \times 60} = \frac{4.913 \times 10^9}{108000}$$

$$\boxed{L_{10} \approx 45491 \text{ hours} \approx 5.19 \text{ years continuous operation}}$$

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion**: Loads given in kN **must be converted to N** before cubing. $4^3 = 64$ in kN³ but $(4000)^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{10}$ in N³ — a factor of $10^9$ error if missed.
> 2. **Wrong exponent for rollers**: Use $p = 10/3$ for **roller bearings** (cylindrical, tapered, needle, spherical roller) and $p = 3$ for **ball bearings**. This is a 2-mark penalty typically.
> 3. **Forgetting to compare $F_a/F_r$ with $e$**: Many students blindly apply $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ even when $F_a/F_r \le e$. This gives the **wrong answer** (over-estimated load) and loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Using arithmetic mean instead of cube-root mean**: A simple average of loads underestimates the damage and is physically wrong for fatigue. Always use the cube-root formula for variable loads on ball bearings.
> 5. **Mixing up the life equation**: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$ — do not write $L = (P/C)^p \times 10^6$. The dynamic load rating $C$ is in the **numerator**.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert revolutions to hours**: Final answer must be in **hours** if the question asks for it. Divide by $n \times 60$ where $n$ is the **average** rpm, not the peak rpm.
> 7. **Incomplete derivation**: KTU requires **every step** in the variable-load derivation to be shown. Skipping the Miner-rule step loses 2 marks.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Equivalent Load — Key Takeaways for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Definition**: A single constant load that produces the same bearing life as the actual variable load.

- **Two fundamental formulas**:
  - **Combined (constant) load**: $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ when $F_a/F_r > e$; else $P = F_r$.
  - **Variable load**: $P_{eq} = \left( \frac{\sum P_i^p n_i t_i}{\sum n_i t_i} \right)^{1/p}$.

- **Exponent $p$**:
  - $p = 3$ → **Ball bearings** (point contact)
  - $p = 10/3$ → **Roller bearings** (line contact)

- **The $e$ parameter**:
  - Threshold ratio: $e = f(F_a/C_0)$ — depends on bearing series
  - If $F_a/F_r \le e$: use $P = F_r$ (no derating)
  - If $F_a/F_r > e$: use combined-load formula with catalog $X, Y$

- **For angular contact ball bearings**: Standard formula is $P = 0.4 F_r + Y F_a$ (when $F_a/F_r > e$). The "0.4" arises from geometry at $\alpha = 40°$.

- **For tapered roller bearings**: $P = 0.4 F_r + Y F_a$ (when $F_a/F_r > e$); $P = F_r$ otherwise.

- **For cylindrical/needle roller bearings**: $P = F_r$ only — they **cannot** take axial load.

- **For pure thrust ball bearings**: $P = F_a$ (pure axial case); $P = 1.2 F_r + F_a$ for combined spherical-seat type.

- **Variable load formula derivation is based on**: Miner's linear damage accumulation rule + the basic $L = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$ life equation.

- **Always convert**: kN → N before any cubing or raising to the 10/3 power.

- **Final life answer**: Report in hours, divide revolutions by $n_{avg} \times 60$ where $n_{avg} = \frac{\sum n_i t_i}{\sum t_i}$.

- **Automotive applications**: Wheel bearings, transmission, differential, engine, steering — all require dynamic equivalent load computation for proper sizing.

- **The cube root is the heart of ball-bearing design**: A heavy load dominates fatigue damage disproportionately due to the cubic relationship — never use a simple arithmetic mean.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sliding Contact Bearing - Lubrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sliding Contact Bearing - Lubrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sliding Contact Bearing** (also called a **Plain Bearing** or **Journal Bearing**) is a machine element designed to support a rotating shaft and transmit loads through a thin layer of **lubricant film** that physically separates the journal (shaft) from the bearing surface. The function of **lubrication** in such bearings is to convert direct metal-to-metal sliding contact into a shear-stress-bearing fluid film, thereby drastically reducing friction, wear, and heat generation while supporting the applied radial load hydrodynamically or hydrostatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Lubrication in sliding contact bearings forms the analytical backbone of Module 2 in *PBAUT604 – Automotive Component Design*. Examiners consistently test the student's ability to (a) classify lubrication regimes, (b) derive the **Reynolds Equation**, (c) compute the **Sommerfeld Number**, and (d) predict **minimum film thickness** for design safety.

### 1.2 Lubrication Regimes — Hierarchical Classification

| Regime | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydrostatic** | External pump pressurizes lubricant; shaft floats even at zero speed | Heavy machine tool spindles, large turbines |
| **Hydrodynamic** | Pressure is generated *internally* by the relative motion (wedging action) of journal & bearing | Most automotive crankshaft bearings, connecting rod bearings |
| **Elastohydrodynamic (EHL)** | High pressure deforms the surfaces elastically, raising effective viscosity | Gears, cam-follower contacts, rolling/sliding combined contacts |
| **Mixed (Partial)** | Partial asperity contact coexists with partial fluid film | Start-up and shut-down of journal bearings |
| **Boundary** | Solid surfaces are in contact; lubricant additives (EP, AW) form a chemical film | Heavily loaded, low-speed, or starved lubrication conditions |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Water Skiing:** Imagine an experienced water skier gliding across a lake. The ski does **not** touch the water; instead, a thin wedge of water is forced underneath at high speed, generating an upward pressure that holds the skier up. A **hydrodynamically lubricated journal bearing behaves identically** — the rotating shaft drags oil into a converging wedge, the oil pressure rises in this wedge, and the shaft is "lifted" off the bearing surface. If the skier stops moving, he sinks — exactly as a shaft sinks (and contacts the bearing) at start-up before hydrodynamic pressure builds up.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Absolute (Dynamic) Viscosity of Oil (μ):** expressed in **Pa·s** (SI) or **reyns** (FPS); typical range for engine oils: **0.01 – 0.10 Pa·s**.
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν = μ/ρ):** expressed in **m²/s** or **cSt** (centistokes); 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s.
- **Radial Clearance (c):** the difference between bearing radius (R) and journal radius (r); expressed in **metres** or **micrometres**.
- **Clearance Ratio (c/r):** typically **0.001 – 0.002** for automotive bearings.
- **Eccentricity Ratio (ε):** **0 ≤ ε ≤ 1**; ε = 0 means perfect concentricity, ε → 1 means bearing contact.
- **Sommerfeld Number (S):** dimensionless; the cornerstone parameter of hydrodynamic bearing design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Hydrodynamic Lubrication — The Why and The How

A hydrodynamic bearing generates its load-supporting pressure through three simultaneous conditions, first formally stated by **Osborne Reynolds (1886)**:

1. **A viscous fluid** must be present between the two surfaces.
2. **There must be relative tangential motion** between the surfaces (shaft rotation).
3. **The gap must converge in the direction of motion** (a converging wedge is mandatory — the film thickness must decrease in the direction of oil flow).

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- The rotating journal (shaft) drags oil by **viscous shear** into the clearance space.
- Because the clearance is **converging** (tapered), the oil cannot exit as fast as it enters — it is **compressed**.
- The incompressibility of the oil forces a **pressure rise** in the converging zone.
- This pressure distribution is **asymmetric** (higher on the converging side), producing a net upward force that **supports the load** and **offsets the journal from the bearing centre** by an amount called *eccentricity* (e).
- The line joining the bearing centre to the journal centre is called the **line of centres**; the angular position measured from the line of maximum film thickness is **θ** (position angle).

### 2.2 Film Thickness Equation — The Geometric Foundation

For a journal of radius *r* inside a bearing of radius *R*, with radial clearance *c = R − r*, and eccentricity *e*:

$$h = c \,+\, e \cos\theta$$

For convenient dimensionless representation, we substitute the **eccentricity ratio** $\varepsilon = e/c$:

$$\boxed{\,h = c\left(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta\right)\,}$$

This is the **film thickness profile** around the bearing circumference. It is a **cosine wave** offset by the clearance *c*. Maximum film thickness occurs at $\theta = 180°$ (attitude angle side) and minimum film thickness at $\theta = 0°$ (load line side).

### 2.3 Reynolds Equation — The Governing Differential Equation

For a 2-D steady-state incompressible laminar flow with negligible inertia and constant viscosity, the **Reynolds Equation** in polar coordinates (Reynolds, 1886) is:

$$\frac{1}{R}\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial\theta}\right) \,+\, \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 \, U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \,+\, 12\frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

For a **long bearing** (infinite length, side leakage neglected) and **steady-state** conditions:

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\!\left(h^{3}\frac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = 6\,U\mu r\,\frac{dh}{d\theta}$$

### 2.4 Petroff's Equation — The Simplest Friction Estimate

**Petroff (1883)** assumed a concentric, fully flooded journal with no load (ε = 0). The shear stress in the Newtonian fluid is uniform, and the friction torque is computed directly:

$$\boxed{\,T_f = \frac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L}{c}\,} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\,f = \frac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N}{P}\!\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)\,}$$

where:
- $T_f$ = frictional torque (N·m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s)
- $r$ = journal radius (m)
- $L$ = bearing length (m)
- $c$ = radial clearance (m)
- $P$ = load per unit projected area = $W/(2rL)$ (Pa)
- $f$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Petroff's Equation Underestimates Friction:** It assumes concentricity (ε = 0) and zero load capacity. In real loaded bearings, ε > 0, the oil film is asymmetric, and friction is **higher** than Petroff predicts. It is, however, an excellent *sanity check* for low-load or high-speed bearings and is widely quoted in KTU exams.

### 2.5 Sommerfeld Number — The Cornerstone Dimensionless Group

The **Sommerfeld Number (S)** is the dimensionless parameter that relates the operating conditions (load, speed, viscosity) to the geometric conditions (clearance, radius):

$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{r}{c}\,\cdot\,\frac{\mu\,N}{P} \,=\, \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}\frac{\mu\,N_{s}}{P}\,}$$

Some textbooks (and KTU) prefer the **dimensionless** form:

$$S_{0} = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}\frac{\mu\,N}{P}$$

where $N$ is in rad/s, and $P$ is bearing pressure in Pa.

**Physical interpretation:**
- **High Sommerfeld Number** = low load OR high speed OR high viscosity → bearing operates in well-lubricated hydrodynamic regime.
- **Low Sommerfeld Number** = high load OR low speed → risk of boundary or mixed lubrication.

### 2.6 Design Equations from Sommerfeld's Exact Solution

For finite-length journal bearings, the **load capacity** and **friction variable** are given by the following Sommerfeld integrals (where $K$ = L/D ratio, typically 0.25 to 1.0):

| Quantity | Dimensionless Functional Form | Engineering Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Load** | $\dfrac{W\,c^{2}}{2\,\mu\,L\,r^{3}\,N} = f_{1}(\varepsilon, K)$ | $W = \dfrac{2\,\mu\,L\,r^{3}\,N}{c^{2}}\,f_{1}(\varepsilon, K)$ |
| **Friction Variable** | $\dfrac{(r/c)\,f}{\mu\,N/P} = f_{2}(\varepsilon, K)$ | $f = \dfrac{\mu\,N}{P}\!\left(\dfrac{c}{r}\right) f_{2}(\varepsilon, K)$ |

Solving simultaneously gives the operating values of $\varepsilon$ and $f$ for a given load, speed, and viscosity. The functions $f_1$ and $f_2$ are tabulated (e.g., Raimondi-Boyd tables) for various $\varepsilon$ and $L/D$.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every equation in the table below — these are the most frequently tested formulas in PBAUT604 Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$ | Film thickness profile | metres; $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = e/c$ | Eccentricity ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $h_{\min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness (at $\theta = 0$) | metres |
| 4 | $h_{\max} = c(1 + \varepsilon)$ | Maximum film thickness (at $\theta = \pi$) | metres |
| 5 | $f_{P} = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\mu N}{P}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)$ | Petroff's coefficient of friction (concentric) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{f,P} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\mu N r^{3} L}{c}$ | Petroff's friction torque | N·m |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{r}{c}\dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | Sommerfeld Number (engineering form) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $S_{0} = \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2}\dfrac{\mu N_{s}}{P}$ | Sommerfeld Number (dimensionless) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $W = \dfrac{2\mu L r^{3} N}{c^{2}} f_{1}(\varepsilon, L/D)$ | Load capacity | N |
| 10 | $f = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\!\left(\dfrac{c}{r}\right) f_{2}(\varepsilon, L/D)$ | Coefficient of friction (loaded journal) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\dfrac{h_{\min}}{c} = 1 - \varepsilon$ | Minimum film ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\mu N}{J\,c}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) f_{3}(\varepsilon, L/D)$ | Temperature rise (isoviscous) | °C; $J = 42 \times 10^{3}$ J/(kg·°C) |
| 13 | $\mu = \mu_{0} e^{b/T}$ | Viscosity-temperature law (Arrhenius approx.) | Pa·s; $b$ is constant |
| 14 | $Q = \rho\,c_{p}\,\Delta T \cdot W \cdot f / J$ | Heat dissipation | W |
| 15 | $P_{\text{avg}} = W/(2rL)$ | Average bearing pressure | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations govern the design of every crankshaft main bearing, big-end bearing, camshaft bush, and thrust washer in an internal combustion engine. A miscalculation of the Sommerfeld Number or minimum film thickness leads directly to bearing seizure, an engineering failure mode the automotive industry spends billions preventing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Equation (Concentric Journal)

**Starting Premise:** A concentric journal of radius *r* rotating at *N* rev/s inside a bearing of radius *R*, with radial clearance *c = R − r*, fully flooded with a Newtonian lubricant of viscosity *μ*. The shaft is assumed unloaded and concentric ($\varepsilon = 0$).

**Step 1 — Velocity Distribution in the Gap**

Using the standard Couette-flow assumption (no pressure gradient, pure shear), the linear velocity profile across the clearance is:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{c} = (2\pi r N) \cdot \frac{y}{c}$$

where $y$ is measured from the journal surface and $U = 2\pi r N$ is the linear surface speed.

**Step 2 — Shear Stress on the Journal Surface**

By Newton's law of viscosity, the shear stress at the journal surface is:

$$\tau = \mu \left.\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=0} = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{c} = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi r N}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Friction Force on the Journal Surface**

The journal surface area (cylindrical, neglecting ends) is $A = 2\pi r L$. Thus:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi r N}{c} \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{2}\,L}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Friction Torque**

The friction torque about the journal axis is force × moment arm:

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L}{c} \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of Friction**

Define the coefficient of friction as $f = F_f / W$, where $W$ is the applied load. Express $W$ in terms of the projected area pressure $P = W/(2rL)$, i.e., $W = 2rL \cdot P$:

$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^{2}\mu N r^{2} L / c}{2rL \cdot P} = \frac{2\pi^{2}\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$\boxed{\,T_f = \frac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L}{c}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,f = \frac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N}{P}\!\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Reynolds Equation (1-D, Long-Bearing Approximation)

**Step 1 — Couette + Poiseuille Superposition**

In a thin film of thickness *h* moving tangentially at speed *U*, the velocity in the *x* (circumferential) direction is the sum of a Couette term and a Poiseuille term:

$$u(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(y^{2} - hy\right) + U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Volume Flow Rate per Unit Width**

Integrating the velocity across the film thickness from $y = 0$ to $y = h$:

$$q = \int_{0}^{h} u \, dy = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} + \frac{Uh}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Continuity / Mass Conservation**

For an incompressible fluid in a control volume of width $dx$, the flow entering minus flow exiting equals the rate of change of film volume:

$$-\frac{\partial q}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

Substituting the expression for $q$ and noting $U$ is constant:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) - \frac{U}{2}\frac{\partial h}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 — Reynolds Equation in Cartesian Form**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\,U\,\frac{\partial h}{\partial x} + 12\,\frac{\partial h}{\partial t}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Polar Form for a Journal Bearing**

Substituting $x = r\theta$, $dx = r\,d\theta$, and noting that the bearing is typically treated as steady-state ($\partial h/\partial t = 0$):

$$\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{d}{d\theta}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = \frac{6U}{r}\frac{1}{r}\frac{dh}{d\theta}$$

Multiplying by $r^{3}$:

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\!\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = 6\,U\,r\,\frac{dh}{d\theta}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style, 14 Mark Template)

**Problem Statement:**
A 60 mm diameter journal runs in a 60.10 mm diameter bearing at 3000 rpm. The bearing length is 30 mm and supports a load of 10 kN. The lubricant viscosity is 20 mPa·s. Compute:
- (a) The Petroff friction force, torque, and coefficient of friction.
- (b) The Sommerfeld Number.
- (c) The minimum film thickness assuming $\varepsilon = 0.6$.

**Given Data:**

$$D = 60\ \text{mm},\quad d_{\text{bearing}} = 60.10\ \text{mm},\quad L = 30\ \text{mm}$$
$$N = 3000\ \text{rpm} = 50\ \text{rev/s},\quad W = 10\,000\ \text{N},\quad \mu = 20 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 1 — Compute Geometric Quantities**

$$r = D/2 = 30\ \text{mm} = 0.030\ \text{m}$$
$$R = 60.10/2 = 30.05\ \text{mm} = 0.03005\ \text{m}$$
$$c = R - r = 0.00005\ \text{m} = 50\ \mu\text{m}$$
$$r/c = 0.030 / 0.00005 = 600$$

**Step 2 — Bearing Pressure**

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{10\,000}{2(0.030)(0.030)} = \frac{10\,000}{0.0018} = 5.556\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Petroff Friction Calculations**

$$F_{f} = \frac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{2}\,L}{c} = \frac{4\pi^{2}(0.020)(50)(0.030)^{2}(0.030)}{0.00005}$$

Breaking down:
- $\pi^{2} \approx 9.8696$
- $4 \times 9.8696 = 39.478$
- $0.020 \times 50 = 1.0$
- $(0.030)^{2} = 9.0 \times 10^{-4}$
- $9.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.030 = 2.7 \times 10^{-5}$
- $39.478 \times 1.0 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5} = 1.0659 \times 10^{-3}$
- Divide by $c = 5.0 \times 10^{-5}$:

$$F_f = \frac{1.0659 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 21.32\ \text{N}$$

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = 21.32 \times 0.030 = 0.640\ \text{N·m}$$

$$f_{P} = \frac{2\pi^{2}\mu N}{P}\cdot\frac{r}{c} = \frac{2(9.8696)(0.020)(50)}{5.556 \times 10^{6}} \times 600$$

- Numerator: $2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.020 \times 50 \times 600 = 11\,843.5$
- Denominator: $5.556 \times 10^{6}$
- $f_P = 11\,843.5 / 5\,556\,000 = 2.132 \times 10^{-3}$

$$\boxed{f_P \approx 0.00213}$$

**Step 4 — Sommerfeld Number**

$$S = \frac{r}{c}\cdot\frac{\mu N}{P} = 600 \times \frac{(0.020)(50)}{5.556 \times 10^{6}} = 600 \times \frac{1.0}{5.556 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$S = \frac{600}{5.556 \times 10^{6}} = 1.080 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 1.08 \times 10^{-4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A Sommerfeld Number of order $10^{-4}$ indicates a heavily loaded bearing operating at the **edge of hydrodynamic regime**. In practice, designers use Raimondi-Boyd charts to read off $\varepsilon$ and $f$ for this $S$; the result is a friction coefficient *higher* than Petroff's value (typically 2–4× larger) and an eccentricity ratio around 0.6–0.8.

**Step 5 — Minimum Film Thickness**

$$h_{\min} = c(1 - \varepsilon) = (50\ \mu\text{m})(1 - 0.6) = 50 \times 0.4 = 20\ \mu\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{\min} = 20\ \mu\text{m} = 0.020\ \text{mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule (KTU Favourite):** For hydrodynamic bearings, $h_{\min}$ must exceed the combined surface roughness $R_{a1} + R_{a2}$ of the journal and bearing (typically 0.5–1.0 μm). A minimum of $20\ \mu\text{m}$ is comfortable; values below $5\ \mu\text{m}$ risk metal-to-metal contact and premature failure.

---

### 3.4 Temperature Rise in a Journal Bearing

The viscous shearing of oil generates heat, and the temperature rise is given by:

$$\Delta T = \frac{T_f \cdot N}{J \cdot Q} \quad \text{or} \quad \Delta T = \frac{\mu N}{J\,c}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right) f_{3}(\varepsilon, L/D)$$

where $J$ is the mechanical equivalent of heat ($J = 42\ \text{J/cal}$) and $f_3$ is a Sommerfeld tabulated function.

In **isoviscous analysis** (μ constant), this formula is used. In **thermohydrodynamic (THD) analysis**, the viscosity is allowed to vary with temperature using an Arrhenius-type law:

$$\mu = \mu_0 \, e^{b/T}$$

This gives much more realistic pressure and temperature profiles and is the basis of modern EHL simulations.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Journal] --> B[Shearing of Oil Film]
    B --> C[Oil Dragged into Converging Wedge]
    C --> D[Pressure Build-up in Wedge]
    D --> E[Asymmetric Pressure Distribution]
    E --> F[Net Upward Force Supports Load W]
    F --> G[Journal Floats at Eccentricity e]
    G --> H{Operating Regime Check}
    H -->|hmin > Surface Roughness| I[Full Film Hydrodynamic Lubrication]
    H -->|hmin approx Roughness| J[Mixed Lubrication]
    H -->|hmin less than Roughness| K[Boundary Lubrication - Failure Risk]
    I --> L[Heat Dissipation via Oil Flow and Housing]
    J --> L
    K --> M[WARNING: Bearing Failure Imminent]
    L --> N[Bearing Parameters: f, T, delta T Computed]
    N --> O[Validated against Sommerfeld Number S]
```

### 4.2 Reynolds Equation Solution Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given: Geometry r, c, R, L] --> B[Compute h theta = c 1 + epsilon cos theta]
    B --> C[Substitute into Reynolds Equation 1D Polar]
    C --> D[Sommerfeld Integral Form]
    D --> E[Function f1 epsilon L over D]
    D --> F[Function f2 epsilon L over D]
    E --> G[Compute Operating Eccentricity epsilon]
    F --> H[Compute Coefficient of Friction f]
    G --> I[Compute hmin = c times 1 minus epsilon]
    H --> J[Check f against Petroff Limit]
    I --> K{Design Satisfies hmin Criterion}
    K -->|Yes| L[Acceptable Design]
    K -->|No| M[Revise Viscosity or Clearance]
```

### 4.3 Lubrication Regime Map (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Load W] --> B[Bearing Geometry r, c, L]
    C[Speed N] --> B
    D[Viscosity mu] --> B
    B --> E{Sommerfeld Number S}
    E -->|S less than 10E-4| P1[Boundary / Mixed Regime]
    E -->|S approx 10E-4 to 10E-1| P2[Hydrodynamic Regime]
    E -->|S greater than 10E-1| P3[High Viscosity / Thick Film Regime]
    P1 --> Q1[Risk: Metal contact, wear]
    P2 --> Q2[Ideal: Full fluid film, low friction]
    P3 --> Q3[Excessive drag, overheating]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., node1, stepA) and labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown bold or italic markers, complying with the V10 rendering safeguard protocol.
