## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1.** Define **hydrodynamic lubrication** in a journal bearing. State the three essential conditions required for its formation.

**Model Answer:**

> Hydrodynamic lubrication is a regime of lubrication in which the load between two relatively moving surfaces is supported entirely by a fluid film generated by the motion of the surfaces themselves, without the aid of an external pressure source.
>
> The three essential conditions (Reynolds, 1886) are:
> 1. A **viscous fluid** must be present between the journal and the bearing.
> 2. There must be **relative tangential motion** between the surfaces (shaft rotation).
> 3. The clearance must **converge in the direction of motion**, producing a wedge-shaped film that traps and pressurises the lubricant.

---

**Q2.** What is the **Sommerfeld Number**? Write its expression and explain its physical significance.

**Model Answer:**

> The Sommerfeld Number (S) is a dimensionless parameter that characterises the operating condition of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. It is given by:
>
> $S = \dfrac{r}{c}\dfrac{\mu N}{P} = \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2}\dfrac{\mu N_s}{P}$
>
> where *r/c* is the inverse clearance ratio, *μ* is the dynamic viscosity, *N* is the rotational speed, and *P* is the bearing pressure.
>
> **Significance:** A **high S** indicates well-lubricated conditions (low load, high speed, or high viscosity), while a **low S** indicates heavily loaded, slow, or starved conditions approaching boundary lubrication. The Sommerfeld Number allows engineers to predict friction, eccentricity ratio, and minimum film thickness via standardised design charts (Raimondi-Boyd).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries 14 marks. Each sub-part below is 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** A full journal bearing has the following specifications: diameter $D = 80\ \text{mm}$, length $L = 80\ \text{mm}$, radial clearance $c = 0.08\ \text{mm}$, radial load $W = 25\ \text{kN}$, speed $N = 1800\ \text{rpm}$, viscosity $\mu = 25\ \text{mPa·s}$. Determine the **Petroff friction torque**, the **Petroff coefficient of friction**, and the **Sommerfeld Number**.

**(b)** Explain the **difference between hydrostatic and hydrodynamic lubrication** with neat sketches and mention two practical applications of each.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Given Data:**
$$D = 80\ \text{mm} = 0.080\ \text{m},\quad L = 80\ \text{mm} = 0.080\ \text{m},\quad c = 0.08\ \text{mm} = 8.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$
$$W = 25\,000\ \text{N},\quad N = 1800/60 = 30\ \text{rev/s},\quad \mu = 25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 1 — Geometric & Pressure Quantities**

$$r = 0.040\ \text{m},\quad r/c = 0.040 / (8.0 \times 10^{-5}) = 500$$

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{25\,000}{2(0.040)(0.080)} = \frac{25\,000}{6.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.906\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Petroff Friction Torque**

$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^{2}\mu N r^{3} L}{c} = \frac{4(9.8696)(0.025)(30)(0.040)^{3}(0.080)}{8.0 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Compute step-by-step:
- $4 \times 9.8696 = 39.478$
- $0.025 \times 30 = 0.75$
- $(0.040)^{3} = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$
- $6.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.080 = 5.12 \times 10^{-6}$
- Numerator: $39.478 \times 0.75 \times 5.12 \times 10^{-6} = 1.516 \times 10^{-4}$
- Divide by $c = 8.0 \times 10^{-5}$:

$$T_f = \frac{1.516 \times 10^{-4}}{8.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.895\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_f \approx 1.90\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Step 3 — Petroff Coefficient of Friction**

$$f_P = \frac{2\pi^{2}\mu N}{P}\cdot\frac{r}{c} = \frac{2(9.8696)(0.025)(30)}{3.906 \times 10^{6}} \times 500$$

- Numerator: $2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 30 \times 500 = 7\,402.2$
- Denominator: $3.906 \times 10^{6}$
- $f_P = 7\,402.2 / 3\,906\,000 = 1.895 \times 10^{-3}$

$$\boxed{f_P \approx 0.00190}$$

**Step 4 — Sommerfeld Number**

$$S = \frac{r}{c}\cdot\frac{\mu N}{P} = 500 \times \frac{(0.025)(30)}{3.906 \times 10^{6}} = 500 \times \frac{0.75}{3.906 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$S = \frac{375}{3.906 \times 10^{6}} = 9.60 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 9.6 \times 10^{-5}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A sub-part a):** Students commonly forget to **convert rpm to rev/s** (divide by 60). A value of $N = 1800$ instead of 30 rev/s will produce a Sommerfeld Number 60× too large, shifting the bearing into an entirely wrong design regime on the Raimondi-Boyd chart. **Always double-check units before substitution.**

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

| Feature | **Hydrostatic Lubrication** | **Hydrodynamic Lubrication** |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Source** | External high-pressure pump | Self-generated by journal rotation |
| **Speed Required** | Works at **zero speed** | Requires **minimum threshold speed** |
| **Load Capacity** | Independent of speed | Strongly **speed-dependent** |
| **Viscosity Required** | Any fluid (even water) | Requires adequate viscosity |
| **Eccentricity** | Very small (ε ≈ 0) | Significant (ε = 0.6–0.95 typical) |
| **Applications** | Machine tool spindles, large turbines, heavy thrust bearings, hydraulic lift pads | IC engine crankshaft bearings, gearbox bushes, electric motor bearings |
| **Disadvantage** | Requires pump, filter, cooler, and control system | Vulnerable at start-up and shut-down |

**Sketch Description (for answer script):**

- **Hydrostatic:** Draw a bearing with a high-pressure pump connected through an inlet orifice, oil entering uniformly around the journal, and a thin film of uniform thickness supporting the shaft at all speeds.
- **Hydrodynamic:** Draw a journal offset from the bearing centre, with arrows showing oil being dragged into a converging wedge, generating a hump-shaped pressure distribution, and the shaft "lifted" off the bearing on a thin film.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled diagram, derive the **Petroff's equation** for friction torque in a concentric journal bearing. State clearly the assumptions made in the derivation.

**(b)** A short journal bearing has the following parameters: $L/D = 0.5$, eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = 0.7$, and the Sommerfeld tabulated functions yield $f_1 = 1.42$ and $f_2 = 3.20$. If the bearing pressure is $P = 3.5\ \text{MPa}$, viscosity $\mu = 0.025\ \text{Pa·s}$, $N = 3000\ \text{rpm}$, and the diameter is $D = 100\ \text{mm}$, compute the **load capacity** *W* and the **coefficient of friction** *f*. Assume $r/c = 1000$.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> A complete derivation of Petroff's equation is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. For the answer script, students should reproduce the 5-step derivation with the following **assumptions** clearly stated:

**Assumptions:**
1. The journal is **concentric** with the bearing (eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = 0$).
2. The bearing is **fully flooded** with a continuous supply of lubricant.
3. The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** (viscosity independent of shear rate).
4. The flow is **laminar** and **steady-state**.
5. **Inertia and body forces** are negligible.
6. The pressure variation in the film is **negligible** (no load carrying capacity; pure Couette shear flow).
7. The bearing is **infinitely long** (no end-leakage effects).

**Final Result (to be boxed in answer script):**

$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L}{c},\qquad f = \frac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N}{P}\!\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)$$

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$D = 100\ \text{mm} = 0.100\ \text{m},\quad L = 0.5D = 0.050\ \text{m}$$
$$P = 3.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa},\quad \mu = 0.025\ \text{Pa·s},\quad N = 3000/60 = 50\ \text{rev/s}$$
$$r = 0.050\ \text{m},\quad r/c = 1000\ \Rightarrow c = 5.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m},\quad \varepsilon = 0.7$$
$$f_1 = 1.42,\quad f_2 = 3.20$$

**Step 1 — Load Capacity**

The load capacity in the Sommerfeld formulation is:

$$W = \frac{2\mu L r^{3} N}{c^{2}} \cdot f_{1}(\varepsilon, L/D)$$

Substituting values:

$$\frac{r^{3}}{c^{2}} = \frac{(0.050)^{3}}{(5.0 \times 10^{-5})^{2}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.0 \times 10^{4}\ \text{m}$$

$$\frac{2\mu L N}{1} = 2(0.025)(0.050)(50) = 0.125$$

$$W = 0.125 \times 5.0 \times 10^{4} \times 1.42 = 8\,875\ \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 8.88\ \text{kN}}$$

**Step 2 — Coefficient of Friction**

$$f = \frac{\mu N}{P}\cdot\frac{c}{r}\cdot f_{2}(\varepsilon, L/D)$$

$$\frac{\mu N}{P} = \frac{(0.025)(50)}{3.5 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.25}{3.5 \times 10^{6}} = 3.571 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\frac{c}{r} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$f = (3.571 \times 10^{-7})(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(3.20) = 1.143 \times 10^{-9}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Examiner's Pitfall (Part B sub-part b):** Watch the **unit system**. The expression $\dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ requires *N* in rev/s, *μ* in Pa·s, and *P* in Pa — the dimensionless Sommerfeld formulation demands SI throughout. A common mistake is to mix cgs or fps units, yielding friction coefficients that are wrong by 5–6 orders of magnitude. Additionally, students often confuse the *load capacity equation* with the *friction equation*, using $f_2$ in place of $f_1$ or vice versa. **Memorise the structure: $f_1$ goes with $W$, $f_2$ goes with $f$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Solution Check (Highly Recommended in Exams):** The answer can be sanity-checked by computing $W$ from $P \cdot 2rL$ and comparing. $W_{\text{pressure}} = 3.5 \times 10^{6} \times 2(0.050)(0.050) = 17\,500\ \text{N}$. The discrepancy (8.88 kN vs 17.5 kN) arises because the bearing as specified has *higher* load capacity (per Sommerfeld) than the applied pressure would suggest — this is consistent with $\varepsilon = 0.7$ being a moderate-to-high eccentricity. In a real exam, mention this interpretation to earn the **full 7 marks** for analytical depth.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — PBAUT604 Module 2**

- **Lubrication Regimes (in order of increasing severity):** Hydrostatic → Hydrodynamic → Elastohydrodynamic (EHL) → Mixed → Boundary.
- **Three Reynolds Conditions:** Viscous fluid + relative motion + converging gap. Memorise these — KTU loves them.
- **Film Thickness Profile:** $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$; $h_{\min} = c(1-\varepsilon)$ at $\theta = 0°$; $h_{\max} = c(1+\varepsilon)$ at $\theta = 180°$.
- **Petroff's Equation (concentric, no load):** $T_f = 4\pi^{2}\mu N r^{3} L / c$ and $f = (2\pi^{2}\mu N / P)(r/c)$. **Always use *N* in rev/s.**
- **Sommerfeld Number (Dimensionless):** $S = (r/c)^{2} \cdot \mu N / P$ (with $N$ in rev/s) — the single most important parameter in hydrodynamic design.
- **Design Chart:** Raimondi-Boyd charts give $\varepsilon$, $f$, and $\Delta T$ for any $S$ and $L/D$ — no analytical closed form exists for finite-length bearings.
- **Design Rule of Thumb:** $h_{\min}$ must exceed combined surface roughness; typical safe values: $h_{\min}/c \ge 0.4$ for hydrodynamic regime.
- **Heat Generation:** $H = f W U$; must equal heat dissipation $H = C \Delta T$ where *C* is a heat transfer coefficient.
- **Viscosity-Temperature Law:** $\mu = \mu_0 e^{b/T}$ (Arrhenius) for thermohydrodynamic analysis; this is *more accurate* than constant-viscosity (isoviscous) analysis.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Forgetting to convert rpm → rev/s; mixing units in the Sommerfeld equation; using $f_1$ and $f_2$ interchangeably.
- **Automotive Context:** Crankshaft main bearings, big-end (connecting rod) bearings, and camshaft bushes are all **hydrodynamic journal bearings**; their design directly determines engine reliability, fuel efficiency, and emissions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubricant:** A substance (solid, liquid, semi-solid, or gaseous) introduced between two sliding/rolling surfaces to reduce friction, wear, heat generation, and to provide protection against corrosion — thereby extending the operational life and efficiency of automotive components.

In automotive engineering, the term **Tribology** governs the study of interacting surfaces in relative motion — encompassing **friction, wear, and lubrication**. Lubricants are the engineering countermeasure applied to manage these tribological interactions.

### 1.2 Broad Classification of Lubricants

| Physical State | Examples | Typical Automotive Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquid Lubricants** | Mineral oils (mono-grade, multi-grade), Synthetic oils (PAO, esters), Vegetable oils | Engine crankcase, gearboxes, automatic transmissions, hydraulic systems |
| **Semi-Solid Lubricants** | Greases (Calcium-based, Sodium-based, Lithium-based, Bentone) | Wheel bearings, chassis points, constant-velocity (CV) joints, water pumps |
| **Solid Lubricants** | Graphite, Molybdenum Disulphide (MoS₂), PTFE (Teflon), Boron Nitride, Tungsten Disulphide | High-temperature bearings (oven conveyors), slow-moving heavy gears, dry-film situations |
| **Gaseous Lubricants** | Air, Nitrogen, Helium | High-speed spindles, gas bearings in turbines, instrument bearings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classification matters:** The selection of a lubricant for a specific automotive component depends on operating **temperature range, load, speed, environment, and contamination exposure**. For instance, the lubricant used in a wheel bearing (a sealed environment, oscillating motion) is **grease**, while the engine crankcase (continuous splash and pressurized circulation) uses **liquid engine oil**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two rough wooden planks being dragged against each other. You can hear squeaking, feel resistance, and see dust fly off (that's **wear**). Now pour some honey on the joint. The honey fills the microscopic valleys and ridges, separating the surfaces with a slippery film. Friction drops dramatically. This is exactly what engine oil does inside a bearing — except the "honey" is a precisely engineered petroleum or synthetic fluid.

Now imagine doing the same thing in a hot oven (engine bay temperatures exceeding 150°C). The honey would burn. So we switch to a specially formulated high-temperature grease or synthetic oil that resists thermal breakdown. **The choice of lubricant is dictated by the operating envelope of the machine element.**

### 1.4 The Three Fundamental Lubrication Regimes

This is a critical concept appearing frequently in KTU examinations:

1. **Boundary Lubrication** — The surfaces are only partially separated by a thin adsorbed film. Friction is governed by surface properties and additives. Occurs during engine cold-start.

2. **Mixed Lubrication** — A transition state where partial fluid-film support exists alongside asperity contact. Dominant in heavily loaded gear teeth.

3. **Hydrodynamic (Full-Film) Lubrication** — Surfaces are completely separated by a fluid pressure wedge. Friction depends purely on the fluid's viscosity. Operating regime during steady-state cruising.

### 1.5 Key Lubrication Terms in Automotive Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stribeck Curve** — The classical curve plotting the friction coefficient ($\mu$) against the Hersey number ($\eta N / P$, where $\eta$ is dynamic viscosity, $N$ is rotational speed, and $P$ is load). It identifies the three lubrication regimes and the optimal operating zone for bearings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sommerfeld Number ($So$)** — A dimensionless quantity that characterizes the operating regime of a fluid-film bearing. Used to compute the position of minimum film thickness in journal bearings.

$$
So = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}
$$

where $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $N$ = speed (rev/s), $P$ = bearing pressure, $r$ = journal radius, $c$ = radial clearance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Liquid Lubricants — The Engineering Checklist

A lubricant's fitness for automotive service is judged by the following measurable properties:

### 2.1.1 Viscosity
The **single most important property**. Defined as the resistance of a fluid to deformation or flow.

- **Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity ($\mu$ or $\eta$):** Units: **Pa·s** (SI) or **Poise (P)** in CGS, with **centipoise (cP)** = 0.001 Pa·s being the common unit.
- **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** $\nu = \mu / \rho$. Units: **m²/s** or **Stokes (St)**; commonly **centistokes (cSt)**.
- **Saybolt Universal Viscosity (SUS)** — measured in seconds for a fixed volume to flow through a calibrated orifice at a specified temperature (typically 38°C / 100°F or 99°C / 210°F).

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity** — the foundational constitutive equation:
>
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$
>
> where $\tau$ is shear stress (Pa), $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $du/dy$ is the velocity gradient (shear rate).

### 2.1.2 Viscosity Index (VI)
A **dimensionless empirical number** (proposed by Dean & Davis, 1929) that quantifies how much a lubricant's viscosity changes with temperature. Higher VI = less viscosity variation = better multi-temperature performance.

| Lubricant Base Stock | Typical VI Range |
|---|---|
| Paraffinic base oils (Naphthenic) | 95 – 105 |
| Solvent-refined paraffinic | 90 – 100 |
| High VI Hydrocracked | 110 – 125 |
| Polyalphaolefin (PAO) synthetic | 135 – 150 |
| Polymethacrylate (PMA) blends | 150 – 200+ |

### 2.1.3 Flash Point & Fire Point
- **Flash Point** — The lowest temperature at which the oil vapor forms an ignitable mixture with air (a brief flame flashes across the surface but does not sustain combustion).
- **Fire Point** — The temperature at which the oil vapor burns **continuously** for at least 5 seconds.

For automotive engine oils, a high flash point (typically **200°C – 250°C**) is essential for safety.

### 2.1.4 Pour Point
The **lowest temperature** at which the oil will flow under standardized test conditions (ASTM D97). Critical for cold-start and winter-grade lubricants.

### 2.1.5 Cloud Point
The temperature at which dissolved wax begins to crystallize, making the oil appear hazy.

### 2.1.6 Aniline Point
The temperature at which equal volumes of aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) and oil become completely miscible. **Low aniline point → high aromatic content → low VI but good solvency.** Inverse relationship to solvency power.

### 2.1.7 Neutralization Number (Acid Number)
Mass of KOH (in mg) required to neutralize 1 g of oil. Indicates oxidation and degradation. A **Total Acid Number (TAN)** above threshold signals oil change.

### 2.1.8 Carbon Residue (Conradson Number)
The percentage of carbon deposited when oil is evaporated under standardized conditions. Indicates the **coking tendency** — lower is better.

### 2.1.9 Emulsification, Demulsibility & Foaming
- **Emulsification** — ability to mix with water.
- **Demulsibility** — ability to separate from water (preferred for turbine/hydraulic oils).
- **Foaming** — air entrainment tendency, controlled by **anti-foam additives** (typically silicone).

## 2.2 Grease — Composition & Thickener Chemistry

Grease = **Base Oil (~75–95%) + Thickener (~5–25%) + Additives (0–10%)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thickener Type → Operating Temperature Range** — This is a high-yield KTU table:

| Thickener | Type | Drop Point (°C) | Water Resistance | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Calcium (Hydrated)** | Simple soap | 80 – 100 | Excellent | Chassis, water pumps |
| **Sodium** | Simple soap | 150 – 170 | Poor | Wheel bearings (high-temp, dry) |
| **Lithium** | Simple soap | 180 – 200 | Good | **Multi-purpose automotive** (most common) |
| **Calcium Complex** | Complex soap | 260+ | Excellent | High-temperature wheel bearings |
| **Lithium Complex** | Complex soap | 260+ | Good | Disc-brake wheel bearings |
| **Aluminum Complex** | Complex soap | 230 – 260 | Excellent | Industrial, food-grade |
| **Polyurea (non-soap)** | Organic | 240+ | Excellent | Sealed-for-life bearings |
| **Bentone (Clay)** | Non-soap inorganic | 270+ | Good | High-temperature, no melting |

**Drop Point** — The temperature at which grease loses its semi-solid structure and becomes liquid.

## 2.3 Additives — The Performance Enhancers

> [!NOTE]
> **Engine oil without additives is just a base oil.** Modern performance is largely an additive engineering story.

| Additive Class | Function | Typical Chemistry |
|---|---|---|
| **Anti-wear (AW)** | Form sacrificial film on metal | Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate (ZDDP) |
| **Extreme Pressure (EP)** | React with surface under load to prevent scuffing | Sulfur-phosphorus compounds, chlorinated paraffins |
| **Detergent** | Neutralize acids, suspend soot | Calcium/Magnesium sulfonates, phenates |
| **Dispersant** | Keep contaminants suspended | Succinimide-based polymers |
| **Antioxidant** | Slow oxidation | ZDDP, hindered phenols, aromatic amines |
| **Viscosity Index Improver (VII)** | Reduce viscosity-temperature sensitivity | Polymethacrylates (PMA), OCP (olefin copolymers) |
| **Pour Point Depressant** | Improve cold flow | Polymethacrylates, wax-modifiers |
| **Anti-foam** | Prevent air entrainment | Silicone polymers |
| **Rust & Corrosion Inhibitor** | Protect metal surfaces | Fatty acid esters, amine phosphates |
| **Friction Modifier (FM)** | Reduce boundary friction | Organic fatty acids, MoDTC |

## 2.4 Viscosity Classification Systems

### 2.4.1 SAE Viscosity Grades (J 300 — Engine Oils)
The **Society of Automotive Engineers** viscosity grading is the global standard. Two sets:

- **"W" grades (Winter):** 0W, 5W, 10W, 15W, 20W, 25W — defined by **maximum low-temperature cranking viscosity** and **maximum low-temperature pumping viscosity**.
- **Non-W grades (Hot):** 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 — defined by **minimum and maximum kinematic viscosity at 100°C** and **minimum High-Temperature High-Shear (HTHS) viscosity at 150°C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-grade oil:** Bears two grades (e.g., **SAE 5W-30**). The polymer VII additives cause the oil to behave like a 5W oil at -30°C but retain the high-temperature viscosity of a 30-grade at 100°C. This is the dominant choice in modern engines.

### 2.4.2 SAE Viscosity Grades for Transmission & Gear Oils (J 306)
- **75W, 80W, 85W, 90, 110, 140, 190, 250**

### 2.4.3 API Service Categories
- **"S" Service** (Spark-ignition / petrol): SA (obsolete) → SB → … → SN, SN+, SP.
- **"C" Service** (Compression-ignition / diesel): CA (obsolete) → … → CK-4, FA-4.
- Two-letter designations like **SN/CF** indicate dual suitability.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in KTU numericals and derivations.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity — basic shear-stress relation |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity from dynamic viscosity & density |
| 3 | $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | Viscosity Index from Dean–Davis chart (L, U, H reference viscosities at 40°C for unknown oil U and reference oils L & H both at 100°C target) |
| 4 | $So = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2}$ | Sommerfeld Number — bearing operating regime |
| 5 | $h_o = c \cdot f(So)$ | Minimum film thickness from Sommerfeld number & eccentricity ratio |
| 6 | $P_{\text{viscous}} = \dfrac{\mu \, \omega \, r^{3} \, L}{c^{2}}$ | Viscous friction power loss in journal bearing |
| 7 | $r_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{r \, N \, L}{P}$ | Hersey number — used on Stribeck curve |
| 8 | $F = \dfrac{\mu A \, v}{h}$ | Viscous drag force in a thin film of thickness $h$ |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r \, c^{3} \, \omega}{2}$ (short bearing approx.) | Side-flow leakage rate from journal bearing |
| 10 | $k = \dfrac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}$ at 40°C and 100°C | Viscosity ratio used for VI calculation |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of Lubrication Engineering

- **Fuel economy** is directly influenced by the HTHS viscosity of engine oil. Lowering HTHS from 3.5 to 2.6 mPa·s can yield **1–2% fuel savings**.
- **Bearing life** scales as $L \propto (h_o)^{-n}$ with $n \approx 2$–$3$ — so a 50% increase in minimum film thickness roughly quadruples bearing fatigue life.
- **Gear scuffing prevention** is achieved with EP additives that form iron-sulfide sacrificial films at asperity temperatures.
- **Electric vehicle (EV) transmission fluids** require low-viscosity, high VI, low foaming, and electrical insulation — driving new lubricant specifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Newton's Law of Viscosity — From Kinetic Theory to Shear Stress

### Statement
For a Newtonian fluid in laminar flow, the shear stress at a point is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (shear rate) at that point.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider two parallel plates separated by a thin fluid film of thickness $h$. The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate moves at velocity $U$.

**Step 1 — Velocity Profile Assumption**

We assume laminar, incompressible flow with no-slip at both surfaces. For small gap, the velocity profile is linear:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Shear Rate**

Differentiate the velocity profile with respect to the transverse coordinate $y$:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity**

By definition, the shear stress at the fluid layer is:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Linear Velocity Gradient**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 5 — Express the Viscous Drag Force on the Moving Plate**

For a plate of contact area $A$:

$$\boxed{F = \tau \cdot A = \frac{\mu \, A \, U}{h}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The required force to drag the upper plate is proportional to viscosity, contact area, and plate velocity — but **inversely proportional** to the fluid film thickness. Doubling the gap halves the friction force.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Viscosity Index (VI) Calculation — Dean–Davis Method

### Problem Setup

A lubricating oil has kinematic viscosities:
- At 40°C: $\nu_{40} = 110 \text{ cSt}$
- At 100°C: $\nu_{100} = 11 \text{ cSt}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the VI Definition**

The VI is computed by comparing the unknown oil to two reference oils:
- **L-oil:** A low-VI reference (Penn. naphthenic, VI = 0) with $\nu_{100,L}$.
- **H-oil:** A high-VI reference (Penn. paraffinic, VI = 100) with $\nu_{100,H}$.

Both reference oils have the **same kinematic viscosity at 100°C** as the unknown oil ($\nu_{100,U}$), but different viscosities at 40°C: $\nu_{40,L} > \nu_{40,U} > \nu_{40,H}$.

**Step 2 — Read Reference Viscosities from Dean–Davis Tables**

For $\nu_{100,U} = 11 \text{ cSt}$, from standard VI tables:

$$\nu_{40,L} = 120.0 \text{ cSt} \quad \text{(VI = 0 reference)}$$

$$\nu_{40,H} = 70.0 \text{ cSt} \quad \text{(VI = 100 reference)}$$

*(Reference values taken from standard kinematic VI tables; rounded for illustration.)*

**Step 3 — Apply the VI Formula**

$$VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

where $L = \nu_{40,L}$, $U = \nu_{40,U}$, $H = \nu_{40,H}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VI &= \frac{120.0 - 110.0}{120.0 - 70.0} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{10.0}{50.0} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

A VI of **20** indicates this oil has a strong temperature dependence — typical of **naphthenic base stocks**. For modern automotive engine oils, a VI of **95–120 (mineral)** or **130–200 (synthetic)** is desired.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Petroff's Equation — Friction Torque in a Concentric Journal Bearing

### Setup

A journal of radius $r$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ inside a bearing of length $L$ with radial clearance $c$, completely submerged in lubricant of dynamic viscosity $\mu$. The journal is concentric (no eccentricity) — the simplest hydrodynamic case.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Geometry**

The film thickness at every angular location equals the radial clearance $c$:

$$h(\theta) = c \quad \text{(concentric case)}$$

**Step 2 — Shear Stress from Newton's Law**

The surface velocity of the journal is $U = r\omega$. With a linear velocity profile across the film (Couette approximation):

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{r\omega}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Friction Force on the Journal Surface**

The differential surface area on the journal is $dA = r \, d\theta \cdot L$. The tangential friction force element:

$$dF = \tau \cdot dA = \mu \cdot \frac{r\omega}{c} \cdot r \, d\theta \, L$$

**Step 4 — Friction Torque**

The torque contribution of each elemental area is $dF \cdot r$:

$$dT = dF \cdot r = \mu \cdot \frac{r^{3} \, \omega \, L}{c} \, d\theta$$

Integrate around the full circumference from $\theta = 0$ to $2\pi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{\mu \, r^{3} \, \omega \, L}{c} \, d\theta \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\mu \, r^{3} \, \omega \, L}{c} \cdot (2\pi) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, r^{3} \, N \, L}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N = \omega / (2\pi)$ in rev/s.

**Step 5 — Power Loss Due to Viscous Friction**

$$
\boxed{P = T \, \omega = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, r^{3} \, N^{2} \, L}{c}}
$$

> **Key insight:** Petroff's equation gives an **upper-bound** for friction in real bearings (which have eccentricity and converging wedges). However, it is invaluable because it directly shows that power loss scales with $\mu$, $N^{2}$, and the cube of radius — justifying the design drive toward **smaller, faster, lower-viscosity systems**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sommerfeld Number for a Loaded Journal Bearing

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Load Capacity**

For a short journal bearing, the hydrodynamic load capacity $W$ is given by:

$$W = \frac{\mu \, \omega \, r^{3} \, L}{c^{2}} \cdot C$$

where $C$ is a dimensionless function of eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon$.

**Step 2 — Express the Bearing Pressure**

The projected bearing area is $A = 2rL$. Thus the bearing pressure:

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{\mu \, \omega \, r^{2}}{2c^{2}} \cdot C$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for the Sommerfeld Group**

By definition:

$$So = \frac{\mu \, \omega}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}$$

Substitute $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
So &= \frac{\mu \, \omega}{\dfrac{\mu \, \omega \, r^{2}}{2c^{2}} \cdot C} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2c^{2}}{r^{2} \cdot C} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{c^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The Sommerfeld number is **inversely proportional to the load capacity coefficient** $C(\varepsilon)$. Larger $\varepsilon$ (more eccentric) → larger $C$ → smaller $So$ → tighter film.

**Step 4 — Practical Use**

For a design target $\varepsilon$:

$$\mu_{\text{required}} = \frac{So \cdot P \cdot c^{2}}{\omega \cdot r^{2}}$$

This is used to **select lubricant grade** to achieve a specific eccentricity and minimum film thickness $h_o = c(1 - \varepsilon)$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Multi-Grade Oil Viscosity Behavior

**Problem:** An engine oil is graded SAE **5W-30**. The kinematic viscosity at 100°C is 9.3 cSt (within the SAE 30 grade range of 9.3 – 12.5 cSt). The viscosity index improver (VII) gives a VI of **170**. Estimate the **dynamic viscosity at -30°C** (cold-start) and at **150°C** (HTHS).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Use the Walther equation (ASTM D341) for viscosity-temperature**

$$\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log T$$

where $\nu$ is in cSt and $T$ in Kelvin.

**Step 2 — From known data points, find constants A and B**

- $T_1 = 373 \text{ K}$, $\nu_1 = 9.3 \text{ cSt}$
- $T_2 = 313 \text{ K}$, take $\nu_2$ from table interpolation at VI = 170... assume $\nu_2 = 65 \text{ cSt}$ (illustrative).

**Step 3 — Plug into the Walther equation**

At $T = 243 \text{ K}$ (-30°C):

$$\nu \approx \text{(consulted from charts)} = 4500 \text{ cSt}$$

This is below the **SAE 5W maximum of 6600 cSt at -30°C** ✓ — confirming cold-start pumpability.

**Step 4 — Dynamic viscosity at -30°C**

With oil density $\rho \approx 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho = 4500 \times 10^{-6} \times 880 \approx 3.96 \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Conclusion:** The multi-grade 5W-30 oil is pumpable at -30°C and retains its 100°C viscosity grade — a tribological achievement only possible with carefully engineered VII polymers.

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — Lubricant Selection Decision Tree

| Operating Condition | Recommended Lubricant Type | Key Justification |
|---|---|---|
| High speed + light load + steady temperature | Low-viscosity mineral oil | Hydrodynamic film forms easily; minimal viscous loss |
| High load + slow speed | High-viscosity oil + EP additives | Boundary regime dominates; EP film prevents scuffing |
| Oscillating motion (e.g., steering linkage) | Lithium grease | Grease retained by seals; resists squeeze-out |
| High temperature (oven, exhaust bearing) | Bentone or polyurea grease | No melting — thermally stable thickener |
| Cryogenic environment | PTFE or PFPE solid lubricant | Liquid oils solidify; solids maintain low shear strength |
| Wet/contaminated environment | Calcium-complex grease | Excellent water resistance |
| Precision instrument bearing | Gas bearing (air) | Near-zero contamination, very low viscosity → very low drag |
| EV transmission | Low-viscosity synthetic (PAO) + electrical-grade additives | Efficiency + insulation against arcing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]`
**Differentiate between Flash Point and Fire Point of a lubricating oil. Why is a high flash point essential for engine crankcase oils?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flash Point | Fire Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Lowest temperature at which oil vapors momentarily ignite (flash) but **do not sustain** combustion | Lowest temperature at which oil vapors **sustain combustion** for at least 5 seconds |
| **Value** | Always lower than fire point | Always higher than flash point (typically 5–30°C above) |
| **Test Standard** | ASTM D93 (Pensky–Martens closed cup), D92 (Cleveland open cup) | ASTM D92 (Cleveland open cup) |

**Engineering Significance for Engine Oils:**
- Engine bay temperatures can reach 120–150°C near exhaust manifolds.
- If the flash point is too low, oil vapors may ignite inside the crankcase ventilation system → **crankcase explosion risk**.
- High flash point (**>200°C**) ensures oil remains in liquid phase under all normal engine operating temperatures, providing safety margin against accidental ignition.

*(3 marks — Award 1 mark each for correct definitions and 1 mark for engine-specific justification.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is Viscosity Index? Explain its significance in the context of multi-grade engine oils like SAE 15W-40.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is a dimensionless, empirical number that quantifies the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature. It was developed by Dean and Davis in 1929.

**Standard Reference:**
- A Pennsylvanian paraffinic oil (low temperature sensitivity) → **VI = 100**
- A Gulf Coast naphthenic oil (high temperature sensitivity) → **VI = 0**

**Formula:**
$$VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

**Significance for SAE 15W-40:**
- A high VI (**>120**) is essential to make multi-grade behavior possible.
- The **"15W"** portion means the oil must not exceed 7000 cP at -20°C (cranking) and must have a maximum pumping viscosity of 60,000 cP at -25°C.
- The **"40"** portion requires kinematic viscosity between 12.5–16.3 cSt at 100°C.
- A high VI ensures the oil is **thin enough at low temperature** (easy cold start, fast oil circulation) yet **thick enough at high temperature** (sustains hydrodynamic film at 100°C).
- Without high VI, a 15W-40 grade would not be achievable — the same oil would either be too thick cold or too thin hot.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula/significance, 1 mark multi-grade link.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches and derivation, explain the phenomenon of **hydrodynamic lubrication in a journal bearing**. Define and derive the **Sommerfeld Number**, and discuss its utility in bearing design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A full journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Journal radius $r = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Bearing length $L = 100 \text{ mm}$
- Radial clearance $c = 0.1 \text{ mm}$
- Speed $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$
- Applied load $W = 25{,}000 \text{ N}$

The lubricant used has dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.025 \text{ Pa·s}$ at operating temperature. Calculate:
1. The **Sommerfeld number**
2. The **Petroff friction torque**
3. The **viscous power loss**

---

### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**1. Hydrodynamic Lubrication — Concept:**
When a journal rotates inside a bearing with sufficient speed, viscous oil is dragged into the converging wedge region. The resulting **pressure build-up** supports the load, completely separating the two surfaces.

> **Reynolds Equation (1886)** governs this pressure generation:
>
> $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6U\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

**2. Sommerfeld Number Derivation (refer to Section 3.4 above for full derivation):**

$$So = \frac{\mu \, \omega}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}$$

**3. Utility in Bearing Design:**
- Determines the **operating regime** (boundary / mixed / hydrodynamic).
- Maps to **eccentricity ratio** $\varepsilon$ via design charts.
- Used to compute **minimum film thickness** $h_o = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ — critical for fatigue life prediction.
- Higher $So$ → lower eccentricity → thicker film → longer bearing life.

---

### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:**
- $r = 0.050 \text{ m}$, $L = 0.100 \text{ m}$, $c = 0.0001 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1500 \text{ rpm} = 25 \text{ rev/s}$, so $\omega = 2\pi N = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$
- $W = 25{,}000 \text{ N}$, $\mu = 0.025 \text{ Pa·s}$

**Bearing Pressure:**
$$P = \frac{W}{2 r L} = \frac{25{,}000}{2 \times 0.050 \times 0.100} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.010} = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

---

**(1) Sommerfeld Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
So &= \frac{\mu \, \omega}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.025 \times 157.08}{2.5 \times 10^{6}} \cdot \left(\frac{0.050}{0.0001}\right)^{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.927}{2.5 \times 10^{6}} \cdot (500)^{2} \\[6pt]
&= 1.5708 \times 10^{-6} \times 250{,}000 \\[6pt]
&= 0.3927
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{So \approx 0.393}$$

---

**(2) Petroff Friction Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{4 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, r^{3} \, N \, L}{c} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4 \pi^{2} \times 0.025 \times (0.050)^{3} \times 25 \times 0.100}{0.0001} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 \times 0.100}{0.0001} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.5}{1 \times 10^{-4}} \\[6pt]
&= 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 2.5 \\[6pt]
&\approx 2.467 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 2.47 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

**(3) Viscous Power Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{loss}} &= T \cdot \omega = 2.467 \times 157.08 \\[6pt]
&\approx 387.5 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{loss}} \approx 387.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.388 \text{ kW}}$$

> **Engineering note:** This is the energy dissipated purely as heat by the lubricant — the engine cooling system must remove this plus combustion heat.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Alternate Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of lubricants** based on physical state. Compare the properties, advantages, and limitations of **solid vs. liquid lubricants** for high-temperature automotive applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **manufacturing process of lithium-based multipurpose grease** (saponification + finishing). List its typical **operating temperature range**, **drop point**, and **applications** in automobiles.

---

### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Classification based on Physical State** (refer to Section 1.2 table).

**Detailed Comparison: Solid vs. Liquid Lubricants at High Temperatures**

| Property | Solid Lubricant (e.g., MoS₂, Graphite) | Liquid Lubricant (e.g., Synthetic PAO) |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature ceiling** | 350–450°C in air (MoS₂); 500–600°C (Graphite in inert) | Typically 200–250°C before rapid oxidation |
| **Load capacity** | High — physically layered structure | Depends on viscosity — degrades above VI breakdown temperature |
| **Speed** | Limited — heat dissipation is poor | Excellent — fluid flow carries heat away |
| **Contamination tolerance** | Excellent — tolerates dust | Sensitive — particulates catalyze oxidation |
| **Volatility** | Negligible | Significant at high T → oil consumption, smoke |
| **Examples in automobiles** | Clutch release bearing (MoS₂ coating), some piston ring coatings | Engine oil, transmission fluid, gear oil |

**Conclusion for high-T automotive use:** Solid lubricants excel where liquid cannot survive (oven conveyor bearings, exhaust turbocharger bearings in early designs). However, **synthetic liquids + advanced additives** dominate most modern high-T automotive zones because of their superior heat-removal capability.

*(7 marks — 3 marks classification, 4 marks comparison table with conclusion.)*

---

### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Lithium Grease Manufacturing Process:**

**Step 1 — Saponification Reaction (in an open or pressure kettle):**
- Raw materials: **12-hydroxystearic acid** + **lithium hydroxide monohydrate (LiOH·H₂O)** + base oil.
- Temperature: 80–100°C.
- Reaction:
$$\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}(\text{OH})\text{COOH} + \text{LiOH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}(\text{OH})\text{COOLi} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Heating/Crystallization:**
- Temperature raised to ~190–210°C to dehydrate and grow long, fibrous lithium-soap crystals.
- Crystal morphology (fiber length, aspect ratio) directly determines the grease's mechanical stability and oil-retention capacity.

**Step 3 — Cooling:**
- Slow controlled cooling. The soap fibers form a **3-D sponge matrix** that traps the base oil via capillary forces.

**Step 4 — Milling/Finishing:**
- Pass through colloid mill or homogenizer to break down aggregates, achieving smooth texture and uniform consistency (NLGI grade 2 typical).

**Step 5 — Additive Incorporation:**
- Add antioxidants (e.g., hindered phenols), rust inhibitors, EP additives as required.

**Specifications of Lithium Multi-Purpose Grease:**
- **Drop point:** 180–200°C
- **Operating temperature range:** −30°C to +150°C (continuous), 180°C (intermittent)
- **NLGI consistency:** Grade 2 (most common), Grade 3 for wheel bearings
- **Water resistance:** Good (but inferior to calcium)
- **Mechanical stability:** Excellent — retains consistency under shear

**Automotive Applications:**
1. Wheel bearings (front non-driven wheels)
2. Chassis grease points (ball joints, tie-rod ends, king pins)
3. Water pump bearings
4. Universal joints (older designs)
5. Alternator and starter motor bearings

*(7 marks — 3 marks process, 2 marks specifications, 2 marks applications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Lubricants Module:**
> 1. **Confusing Flash Point with Fire Point** — Students often swap definitions; remember Fire > Flash by 5–30°C, and Fire is *continuous* ignition.
> 2. **Wrong unit in Sommerfeld calculations** — $\mu$ must be in **Pa·s**, not cP. Always convert: $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.
> 3. **Mixing up Petroff vs. Raimondi-Boyd** — Petroff gives a *theoretical upper-bound* for concentric bearing only; for real eccentric bearings, use **Raimondi-Boyd charts** for accurate friction and flow.
> 4. **Viscosity Index calculation error** — $L$ and $H$ are *40°C* viscosities of reference oils that share the *same 100°C viscosity* as the unknown — students often invert the formula.
> 5. **Drop Point vs. Melting Point** — Greases don't have a true melting point; the drop point indicates when the soap matrix collapses and oil separates out.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Lubricants**

### Conceptual Pillars
- **Lubricant** = any substance that reduces friction and wear between contacting surfaces.
- **Tribology** = the umbrella science: friction + wear + lubrication.
- **Petroldatum 1:** Multi-grade engine oils are an **additive engineering** breakthrough (VII polymers).
- **Petroldatum 2:** Grease = base oil + thickener + additives; the thickener type dictates the temperature ceiling.
- **Petroldatum 3:** Hydrodynamic lubrication creates load-supporting pressure wedges without external pressure source — this is the foundation of journal and thrust bearing design.

### Numerical Mastery
- Newton's Law: $\tau = \mu \cdot du/dy$.
- Kinematic conversion: $\nu = \mu/\rho$ (watch units — kg/m³ for $\rho$).
- Petroff torque: $T = 4\pi^{2}\mu r^{3} N L / c$.
- Petroff power loss: $P = 4\pi^{2}\mu r^{3} N^{2} L / c$.
- Sommerfeld: $So = (\mu \omega / P)(r/c)^{2}$.
- VI formula: $VI = (L-U)/(L-H) \times 100$.

### Property Memory Hooks
- **High VI = less temperature sensitivity = better**.
- **Low pour point = better cold flow**.
- **High flash point = safer at high temperature**.
- **Low carbon residue = cleaner combustion / less deposit**.

### Grease Selection Logic
1. Identify temperature range → choose thickener family.
2. Identify load & speed → choose base oil viscosity & EP additives.
3. Identify contamination (water/dust) → choose water-resistant thickener.
4. Match NLGI grade to the sealing method and dispensing system.

### Modern Trends (Bonus Knowledge for KTU)
- **EV transmission fluids** — ultra-low viscosity, electrical-grade.
- **Bio-based lubricants** — from vegetable oils (high VI, low toxicity, renewable).
- **Nano-lubricants** — MoS₂, hBN, graphene nanoparticles dispersed in base oil — show 10–30% friction reduction.
- **Group III/IV/V base oils** — hydrocracked and GTL (gas-to-liquid) base stocks replacing conventional Group I/II.

### Cross-Connections to Other Modules
- **Module 1 (Gears):** Gear oils require EP additives for hypoid gears; viscosity grades follow SAE J306.
- **Module 2 (Bearings — current sub-topic):** Hydrodynamic analysis via Sommerfeld & Petroff.
- **Module 3 (Engine Components):** Crankcase lubrication system, splash vs. pressurized feed.
- **Module 4 (Braking System):** Brake fluid (DOT 3, 4, 5.1) — high boiling point, hygroscopic behavior, glycol-ether / silicone chemistry.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscosity** is the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to deformation or flow. It quantifies the internal frictional force between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities. In the context of **Automotive Component Design**, viscosity is the single most important physical property of lubricants (engine oils, gear lubricants, automatic transmission fluids, hydraulic oils, and greases) because it governs hydrodynamic film formation in bearings, gears, and pistons.

Mathematically, viscosity is defined through **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, which states that the shear stress ($\tau$) between two adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$) normal to the direction of flow.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹)

The proportionality constant $\mu$ is termed the **Dynamic Viscosity** (also called absolute viscosity or coefficient of viscosity).

## 1.2 Types of Viscosity

There are two principal forms of viscosity that automotive engineers routinely handle:

1. **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$)** — measured in **Pa·s** (Pascal-seconds) or in older CGS units, **Poise (P)** where 1 Pa·s = 10 P. Common automotive sub-unit: **centipoise (cP)** where 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s.

2. **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)** — defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

- Units: **m²/s** (SI) or **Stokes (St)** in CGS, where 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s. Common sub-unit: **centistokes (cSt)** where 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Kinematic Viscosity?** In automotive practice, kinematic viscosity is reported on lubricant datasheets because it is measured directly and conveniently using a **capillary viscometer** (e.g., the Redwood, Saybolt, or Cannon-Fenske apparatus) without needing to determine fluid density separately.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Honey vs. Water Picture**
> Imagine pouring **honey** and **water** down the same inclined glass plate. Water slides quickly and forms a thin, fast-moving stream, while honey creeps sluggishly. The honey has *higher viscosity* because its molecules cling more strongly to one another, resisting relative motion between layers.
>
> In an **engine**, this internal "stickiness" is what allows oil to be dragged into the contact zone between the crankshaft journal and the bearing, where it generates a pressurized hydrodynamic film that prevents metal-to-metal contact. Without adequate viscosity, the film collapses, leading to **boundary lubrication**, wear, and eventual seizure.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Automotive Lubricants

- **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)** — empirical time-based viscosity unit, used in older American standards.
- **Engler degrees (°E)** — European empirical viscosity.
- **ISO Viscosity Grade (VG)** — industrial lubricant grading (e.g., ISO VG 32, VG 46, VG 68) based on kinematic viscosity at 40 °C.
- **SAE Viscosity Grade** — automotive engine and gear oil grading (e.g., SAE 10W-30, SAE 75W-90) covering both low- and high-temperature performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAE Grading Context (KTU Favourite):** Multigrade oils such as **SAE 20W-50** carry a "W" (winter) suffix followed by a high-temperature grade. The **W number** relates to low-temperature pumpability (cold cranking), while the **second number** reflects high-temperature kinematic viscosity (typically at 100 °C). This dual-numbering is governed by **SAE J300** and **SAE J306** standards.

## 1.5 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Physical Interpretation

When two parallel plates are separated by a fluid film of thickness $h$, and the top plate moves with velocity $U$ while the bottom plate is stationary, the fluid layers deform in a linear fashion (Couette flow). The shear stress at any fluid element is given by:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

This relation assumes **laminar flow**, **no-slip at the walls**, and **steady state**.

---

## 1.6 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Property | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity behaviour | Constant $\mu$ regardless of shear rate | $\mu$ varies with applied shear rate |
| Shear stress vs. shear rate | Linear | Non-linear |
| Examples | Water, air, thin mineral oils | Gear oils (at high pressure), greases, multigrade oils with VI improvers, blood, ketchup |
| Engineering treatment | Simple linear math | Requires empirical models (Bingham, Ostwald-de Waele, Carreau) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Many automotive oils, particularly **multigrade engine oils containing viscosity index (VI) improvers** and **lithium greases**, exhibit **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behaviour at high shear rates found in gear contacts. Students often wrongly assume all fluids are Newtonian. In bearing analysis under **elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)**, viscosity must be evaluated at the local pressure and temperature using the **Roelands equation**, not assumed constant.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Shear in a Lubricant Film

When a journal rotates inside a bearing, the lubricant is dragged by the rotating surface and develops a velocity profile across the clearance. The shear stress arises from intermolecular cohesive forces and momentum transfer between fluid laminae. These microscopic interactions collectively produce a macroscopic tangential resistance.

The fundamental assumption of **Newtonian fluid mechanics** is that the shear stress depends only on the **rate of strain**, not on the strain itself. For one-dimensional Couette flow:

$$\tau_{yx} = \mu \frac{du_x}{dy}$$

This is a constitutive equation — it is an experimentally observed law, not a derived result.

## 2.2 Variations of Viscosity with Temperature

Viscosity is highly sensitive to temperature. As temperature rises, molecular spacing increases, cohesive forces weaken, and viscosity drops dramatically. The empirical relationships most frequently tested in KTU exams are:

### 2.2.1 Reynolds Equation for Viscosity (1886)
$$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_0}\left[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)\right]$$

Where:
- $\mu_0$ = Viscosity at reference temperature $T_0$
- $\alpha$ = Temperature coefficient of viscosity (K⁻¹)
- Valid only for limited temperature ranges

### 2.2.2 ASTM Standard D341 — Walther Equation (MacCoull-Walther)
$$\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$$

Where $\nu$ is in centistokes and $T$ in Kelvin; $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants. This equation gives an excellent straight-line fit over wide temperature ranges and is used to generate **viscosity-temperature charts (ASTM D341 charts)**.

### 2.2.3 Vogel Equation (Cameron Equation)
$$\log \eta = A + \frac{B}{T + C}$$

Three-parameter model (more accurate than the simple Reynolds equation), commonly used for hydraulic fluids.

### 2.2.4 Viscosity Index (VI)
The **Viscosity Index** is an empirical number indicating the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature. A higher VI means the oil thins less as temperature rises.

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

Where:
- $U$ = Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of the test oil
- $L$ = Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of an oil with VI = 0 having the same viscosity at 100 °C
- $H$ = Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of an oil with VI = 100 having the same viscosity at 100 °C

## 2.3 Variation of Viscosity with Pressure

In heavily loaded contacts like **gears, cam-followers, and rolling-element bearings**, local pressures can exceed 1–3 GPa, causing viscosity to rise by several orders of magnitude. This is captured by the **Barus equation** (exponential model):

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 \, e^{\alpha p}$$

Where $\alpha$ = pressure-viscosity coefficient (typically 1–3 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹ for mineral oils).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** The pressure-viscosity exponent is the dominant reason why **elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)** films can support enormous loads in gears — the lubricant effectively "solidifies" under pressure, providing a high-viscosity (almost glassy) separating film.

The more accurate **Roelands equation** is used in advanced EHL analysis:

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 \, \exp\!\left\{\left[\ln(\mu_0) + 9.67\right]\!\left[\left(1 + \frac{p}{p_r}\right)^{z} - 1\right]\right\}$$

Where $p_r = 1.98 \times 10^8$ Pa and $z = \alpha / \left[5.1 \times 10^{-9} (\ln \mu_0 + 9.67)\right]$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following consolidated table forms the **single most important reference** for solving viscosity-related problems in KTU Module 2.

| # | Formula / Relation | Expression | Key Variables / Units | Application in Automotive Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \,\dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau$ [Pa], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $du/dy$ [s⁻¹] | Foundation of hydrodynamic lubrication |
| 2 | Couette Flow Shear Stress | $\tau = \mu \,\dfrac{U}{h}$ | $U$ [m/s], $h$ [m] | Slider bearing analysis |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | $\nu$ [m²/s], $\rho$ [kg/m³] | Lubricant datasheets, capillary viscometers |
| 4 | Reynolds Temperature Relation | $1/\mu = (1/\mu_0)\!\left[1 + \alpha(T-T_0)\right]$ | $\alpha$ [K⁻¹] | Approximate viscosity at operating temperature |
| 5 | Walther / ASTM D341 | $\log\!\log(\nu+0.7) = A - B\log T$ | $A,B$ empirical, $T$ in K | Viscosity-temperature charts |
| 6 | Vogel Equation | $\log \eta = A + B/(T+C)$ | $A,B,C$ fitted | Hydraulic fluids over wide $T$ |
| 7 | Barus Pressure-Viscosity | $\mu_p = \mu_0 e^{\alpha p}$ | $\alpha$ ≈ 1–3 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹ | EHL film in gears & bearings |
| 8 | Roelands Equation | $\mu_p = \mu_0 \exp\!\left\{[\ln\mu_0 + 9.67]\!\left[(1+p/p_r)^z - 1\right]\right\}$ | $p_r = 1.98 \times 10^8$ Pa | Accurate EHL analysis |
| 9 | Viscosity Index (VI) | $\text{VI} = (L-U)/(L-H) \times 100$ | $U,L,H$ in cSt at 40 °C | Oil quality classification |
| 10 | Poiseuille Flow Viscosity | $\mu = \dfrac{\pi \, r^4 \, \Delta p}{8 \, Q \, L}$ | $r$ [m], $\Delta p$ [Pa], $Q$ [m³/s], $L$ [m] | Capillary viscometer calibration |
| 11 | Saybolt to Kinematic Conversion | $\nu\,[\text{cSt}] = 0.226\,t - \dfrac{1.95}{t}$ | $t$ [SUS, Saybolt seconds] | Older datasheet interpretation |
| 12 | Stoke's Viscosity for Sphere | $F_d = 6\pi\mu r v$ | $r$ [m], $v$ [m/s] | Falling-ball viscometer |
| 13 | Hagen-Poiseuille Equation | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^4 \, \Delta p}{8\mu L}$ | Used to derive item 10 | Flow through capillary tubes |
| 14 | Sommerfeld Number (uses $\mu$) | $S_o = \dfrac{r \mu N}{P}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | $r,c$ [m], $N$ [rps], $P$ [N] | Journal bearing design — links $\mu$ directly to load capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of Viscosity in Automotive Engineering:**
> 1. **Engine Bearings** — Determines minimum oil pressure at start-up and film thickness under load.
> 2. **Gear Lubrication** — Influences EHL film formation and scoring/bending fatigue life.
> 3. **Hydraulic Systems** — Brake fluid, power-steering fluid, and ATF depend on stable viscosity across −40 °C to +150 °C.
> 4. **Fuel Injection** — Diesel fuel viscosity affects injector spray atomization and pump lubrication.
> 5. **Coolant Pumps** — Coolant viscosity governs NPSH and pump efficiency.
> 6. **Chassis Grease** — Base oil viscosity of grease dictates low-temperature torque and high-temperature dropping point.

---

## 2.5 Worked Example of Hagen-Poiseuille Capillary Viscometer

The Cannon-Fenske capillary viscometer measures kinematic viscosity directly using the relation:

$$\nu = C \cdot t$$

Where $C$ is the calibrated viscometer constant (mm²/s²) and $t$ is the efflux time (seconds).

From Poiseuille's law, the constant $C$ is given by:

$$C = \frac{\pi \, g \, r^4 \, h}{8 \, L \, V}$$

Where:
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (m/s²)
- $h$ = average effective head (m)
- $r$ = capillary radius (m)
- $L$ = capillary length (m)
- $V$ = efflux volume (m³)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Trick:** If a problem gives efflux time $t$ and viscometer constant $C$, do not bother re-deriving Poiseuille's law — simply multiply $C \times t$ to get $\nu$ in cSt. Use **$\mu = \nu \rho$** to obtain dynamic viscosity.

---

## 2.6 Viscosity in Journal Bearing Hydrodynamics

In a full journal bearing, the **Reynolds equation** in two dimensions is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

The **Sommerfeld number** $S_o$ (dimensionless) governs bearing performance:

$$S_o = \frac{r \mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{\mu \, N}{P/A}$$

Where $r$ = journal radius, $c$ = radial clearance, $N$ = rotational speed, $P$ = load, $A$ = projected area.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **viscosity $\mu$ appears in the numerator of $S_o$**: higher viscosity → higher load-carrying capacity, but also higher friction and lower operating temperature. This trade-off is the central paradox of lubrication engineering, and KTU regularly tests it.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Couette Flow Viscosity from Newton's Law

**Problem Statement:** Two parallel plates separated by oil of film thickness $h = 0.5$ mm. The top plate moves at $U = 2$ m/s while the bottom is stationary. The oil's dynamic viscosity is $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s. Calculate the shear stress and the shear force per unit area.

**Solution:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

For linear (Couette) velocity profile from $u = 0$ at $y = 0$ to $u = U$ at $y = h$:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{h - 0} = \frac{U}{h}$$

Substituting:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

$$\tau = 0.1 \times \frac{2}{0.0005}$$

$$\tau = 0.1 \times 4000$$

$$\tau = 400 \text{ Pa}$$

**Shear force per unit area = 400 N/m².**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hagen-Poiseuille Law (Step-by-Step)

Consider steady, laminar, incompressible flow through a horizontal circular pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$. Pressure drop $\Delta p$ exists from inlet to outlet.

### Step 1 — Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$

The pressure force acting on the end caps is balanced by the shear force on the cylindrical surface:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's law of viscosity

For purely viscous flow with no-slip at the wall, the velocity gradient is negative (velocity decreases as $r$ increases toward the wall):

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

### Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $\tau$

$$\frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

### Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \, r$$

$$du = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \, r \, dr$$

Integrate from centre ($r = 0$, $u = u_{\max}$) to radius $r$:

$$\int_{u_{\max}}^{u} du = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \int_0^r r \, dr$$

$$u - u_{\max} = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{r^2}{2}$$

$$u = u_{\max} - \frac{\Delta p \, r^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 5 — Apply boundary condition $u = 0$ at $r = R$

$$0 = u_{\max} - \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{4\mu L}$$

$$u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 6 — Velocity profile (parabolic)

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

### Step 7 — Volumetric flow rate by integration

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L} \int_0^R \left(R^2 r - r^3\right) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 \cdot R^2}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 \mu L}}$$

### Step 8 — Solve for viscosity

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 Q L}}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**, the cornerstone of capillary viscometer calibration.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Falling-Sphere Viscometer (Stokes' Method)

A sphere of radius $r$ and density $\rho_s$ falls at terminal velocity $v_t$ through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$. At terminal velocity, the gravitational force is balanced by buoyancy and Stokes drag.

### Step 1 — Force balance

$$\text{Weight} - \text{Buoyancy} = \text{Drag Force}$$

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g = 6\pi \mu r v_t$$

### Step 2 — Factor common terms

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6\pi \mu r v_t$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\mu$

$$\mu = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** This formula is used to measure the viscosity of **transparent new oils** in laboratories. The terminal velocity is observed by timing the sphere through two marked lines a known distance apart.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Viscosity Index (VI) Computation

**Problem:** An oil has kinematic viscosity 73 cSt at 40 °C and 9.3 cSt at 100 °C. For an oil of VI = 0 having 9.3 cSt at 100 °C, the viscosity at 40 °C is $L = 113$ cSt. For an oil of VI = 100 with the same 100 °C viscosity, the value at 40 °C is $H = 70$ cSt. Calculate VI.

**Solution:**

Apply the definition:

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

Substituting $L = 113$, $U = 73$, $H = 70$:

$$\text{VI} = \frac{113 - 73}{113 - 70} \times 100$$

$$\text{VI} = \frac{40}{43} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{VI} = 93.0}$$

A VI of 93 indicates a **high-quality multigrade oil** with excellent viscosity-temperature stability — typical of a **synthetic 5W-30 engine oil**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Barus Pressure-Viscosity Application to Gear Tooth Contact

**Problem:** A spur gear pitch point has a Hertzian contact pressure $p = 1.5$ GPa. The oil at atmospheric pressure has $\mu_0 = 0.08$ Pa·s, and its pressure-viscosity coefficient is $\alpha = 2.0 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹. Calculate the viscosity at the contact pressure.

**Solution:**

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 e^{\alpha p}$$

$$\mu_p = 0.08 \times e^{(2.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$\mu_p = 0.08 \times e^{30}$$

$$\mu_p = 0.08 \times 1.0686 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_p \approx 8.55 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Reality Check:** This astronomically high value is why the EHL film in gears behaves almost like a **solid glass-like layer** under load — preventing direct asperity contact and enabling modern gearboxes to transmit gigapascal-level stresses without surface welding.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Problem Solutions

| Problem | Given | Find | Key Formula | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = 0.5$ Pa·s, $U = 3$ m/s, $h = 1$ mm | Shear stress | $\tau = \mu U/h$ | $\tau = 1500$ Pa |
| 2 | $R = 2$ mm, $L = 0.5$ m, $\Delta p = 50$ kPa, $\mu = 0.2$ Pa·s | Volumetric flow $Q$ | $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta p/(8\mu L)$ | $Q = 7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s |
| 3 | $r = 1.5$ mm sphere, $\rho_s = 7800$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 900$ kg/m³, $v_t = 0.05$ m/s | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu = 2r^2 g(\rho_s-\rho_f)/(9v_t)$ | $\mu = 0.69$ Pa·s |
| 4 | $\nu = 32$ cSt, $\rho = 870$ kg/m³ | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu = \nu \rho$ | $\mu = 0.02784$ Pa·s |
| 5 | $\mu_0 = 0.05$ Pa·s, $\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹, $p = 2$ GPa | $\mu_p$ | Barus | $\mu_p = 1.21 \times 10^{17}$ Pa·s |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** Define **dynamic viscosity** and **kinematic viscosity**. State their SI units and the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

> **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the ratio of shear stress to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) across a fluid layer, given by Newton's law $\tau = \mu \, \frac{du}{dy}$. **SI unit: Pa·s** (also N·s/m²).
>
> **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid, $\nu = \mu / \rho$. **SI unit: m²/s.**
>
> **Relationship:** $\mu = \rho \, \nu$ and $\nu = \mu / \rho$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q:** Explain the significance of the **Viscosity Index (VI)** in selecting lubricants for automotive engines.

**Model Answer:**

> The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an empirical dimensionless number that quantifies the rate at which an oil's viscosity changes with temperature.
>
> - **High VI** (>95) → viscosity changes slowly with temperature → oil remains effective over a wide temperature range → suitable for **multigrade engine oils** (e.g., 5W-30 synthetic).
> - **Low VI** (<40) → viscosity drops rapidly with temperature → unsuitable for engines operating across wide temperature swings.
>
> VI guides the formulator in selecting **base oils** (mineral vs. synthetic) and **VI improver additives** to achieve the desired SAE grade. Higher VI gives better cold-start protection (film persistence) and high-temperature load-bearing capacity simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — Choice A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Q:** Derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for steady laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a circular pipe, starting from Newton's law of viscosity. Use the result to obtain an expression for the **dynamic viscosity** in terms of measurable quantities.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$, length $L$:**

Pressure force balances viscous shear force on the curved surface:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2 — Newton's law of viscosity:**

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Equating (1) and (2):**

$$\frac{\Delta p \, r}{2L} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta p \, r}{2 \mu L}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate (boundary condition $u = u_{\max}$ at $r = 0$, $u = 0$ at $r = R$):**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \, 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8\mu L}$$

**Step 6 — Final expression for viscosity:**

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 Q L}}$$

This is used in **capillary viscometers** (Cannon-Fenske, Ubbelohde) where $Q = V/t$, $V$ being the efflux volume and $t$ the efflux time.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Q:** A **Cannon-Fenske viscometer** has a capillary radius $R = 0.5$ mm, capillary length $L = 100$ mm, and an efflux volume $V = 5$ cm³. The efflux time for an oil sample is $t = 250$ s under a pressure head equivalent to $h = 0.1$ m of oil. The oil density is $\rho = 880$ kg/m³. Calculate the **kinematic viscosity** and **dynamic viscosity** of the oil.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pressure drop (gauge pressure due to oil column):**

$$\Delta p = \rho g h = 880 \times 9.81 \times 0.1$$

$$\Delta p = 863.28 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{250} = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic viscosity from Hagen-Poiseuille:**

$$\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 Q L}$$

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^4 \times 863.28}{8 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times (100 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-11} \times 863.28}{1.6 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\mu = \frac{1.6944 \times 10^{-7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\mu = 10.59 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{10.59}{880}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.01203 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 12\,034 \text{ cSt}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert cm³ to m³ or mm to m, leading to errors of 10⁶ or 10⁹. **Always convert all units to SI before substituting.** Also note that efflux time alone gives kinematic viscosity only if the viscometer constant $C$ is supplied — here, since $C$ is not given, you must compute $\mu$ from Poiseuille and then divide by $\rho$.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — Choice B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Q:** Using the **Barus equation**, explain the effect of pressure on the viscosity of a lubricant. A gear tooth contact experiences a Hertzian pressure of 1.2 GPa. If the lubricant viscosity at atmospheric pressure is $\mu_0 = 0.05$ Pa·s and the pressure-viscosity coefficient is $\alpha = 2.2 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹, calculate the effective viscosity at the contact zone.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Barus equation:**

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 \, e^{\alpha p}$$

This empirical relation shows that viscosity grows **exponentially** with pressure. In heavily loaded contacts (gears, rolling-element bearings, cam-followers), local pressures are 1–3 GPa, so viscosity can rise by 5–10 orders of magnitude — effectively turning the oil into a glassy solid that supports load elastically.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\mu_p = 0.05 \times e^{(2.2 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.2 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$\mu_p = 0.05 \times e^{26.4}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate exponential:**

$$e^{26.4} \approx 2.86 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$\mu_p = 0.05 \times 2.86 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_p \approx 1.43 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

This is the basis of **elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)**, which enables modern gearboxes to operate at extreme loads without surface welding or scuffing.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Q:** A journal bearing has journal radius $r = 50$ mm, radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm, rotational speed $N = 1500$ rpm, and load $P = 20$ kN. The lubricant viscosity at operating temperature is $\mu = 0.02$ Pa·s. Compute the **Sommerfeld number** and comment on its significance.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$N = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rps}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Sommerfeld number:**

$$S_o = \frac{r \, \mu \, N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

$$S_o = \frac{0.05 \times 0.02 \times 25}{20\,000} \times \left(\frac{0.05}{0.0001}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate each term:**

Numerator: $0.05 \times 0.02 \times 25 = 0.025$

Ratio term: $\left(\frac{0.05}{0.0001}\right)^2 = (500)^2 = 250\,000$

$$S_o = \frac{0.025 \times 250\,000}{20\,000}$$

$$S_o = \frac{6250}{20\,000}$$

$$\boxed{S_o = 0.3125}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on significance:**

The Sommerfeld number is a **dimensionless group** that uniquely determines the operating eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$ and the minimum film thickness $h_{\min}$ in a journal bearing. A value of $S_o \approx 0.31$ places the bearing in the moderately loaded regime where the eccentricity is moderate and a stable hydrodynamic film exists.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Student Errors:**
> 1. **rpm vs. rps confusion:** Failing to divide $N$ by 60 will produce $S_o$ off by 60× — a guaranteed zero.
> 2. **Units of clearance:** Always express $c$ in metres to match $r$.
> 3. **Using $N$ in rev/s** in some formulas and **rad/s** in others. In the Sommerfeld equation above, use **rps**.
> 4. **Neglecting $(\frac{r}{c})^2$:** This term amplifies $S_o$ by 10⁵ to 10⁶; forgetting it collapses the entire result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Viscosity (KTU Module 2)**
>
> ✅ **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ — fundamental constitutive equation for Newtonian fluids.
>
> ✅ **Two principal measures:** Dynamic ($\mu$, Pa·s) and Kinematic ($\nu = \mu/\rho$, m²/s). Lubricant datasheets use kinematic.
>
> ✅ **Temperature sensitivity:** Viscosity drops with temperature. Use **Reynolds**, **Vogel**, or **Walther (ASTM D341)** equations depending on the required accuracy range.
>
> ✅ **Pressure sensitivity:** Viscosity rises exponentially with pressure — captured by **Barus** (simple) and **Roelands** (advanced). Drives EHL film formation in gears and rolling bearings.
>
> ✅ **Viscosity Index (VI):** Empirical measure of viscosity-temperature stability; higher VI = better lubricant. Modern synthetics exceed VI = 130.
>
> ✅ **SAE Grade:** "W" suffix = low-temperature pumpability; second number = 100 °C kinematic viscosity. Multigrade oils (e.g., 5W-30) cover wide temperature ranges.
>
> ✅ **Capillary viscometry:** Cannon-Fenske & Ubbelohde use $\nu = C \cdot t$; underlying law is **Hagen-Poiseuille** ($Q = \pi R^4 \Delta p / 8\mu L$).
>
> ✅ **Falling-sphere method:** $\mu = 2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) / (9 v_t)$.
>
> ✅ **Sommerfeld number:** $S_o = \frac{r \mu N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$ — directly incorporates $\mu$; governs journal bearing performance.
>
> ✅ **Non-Newtonian caveat:** Many real automotive fluids (gear oils, greases, multigrades with VI improvers) are shear-thinning; never assume constant $\mu$ in EHL analysis.
>
> ✅ **Standard units recap:** 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP; 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt.
>
> ✅ **Design consequence:** Selecting lubricant viscosity balances **load capacity** (high $\mu$ good) against **friction and heat generation** (low $\mu$ good) — central trade-off in bearing/gear design.
>
> ✅ **Engineered adjuvants:** Viscosity Index Improvers (VIIs) are long-chain polymers (PMA, OCP, PIB, styrene esters) that make oil behave as if it has higher VI without raising baseline viscosity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
