topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Journal Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — JOURNAL BEARINGS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Journal Bearing** is a cylindrical sliding-contact bearing in which a shaft (called the *journal*) rotates within a stationary supporting member (called the *bearing* or *bush*) with a layer of lubricant separating the two surfaces to minimize friction and wear. When the separation is achieved entirely by the pressure generated within the lubricant film due to the relative motion and viscous shearing, the bearing is termed a **Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PBAUT604 Module 2):**
> Study of hydrodynamic journal bearings — Petroff's friction theory, Reynolds' equation, Sommerfeld number, bearing characteristic number, heat balance, design variables, and selection of lubricants for automotive applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **log rolling on a wet riverbank**:
- Without water → heavy friction, the log drags and burns the ground.
- With a thin sheet of water → the log *floats* on the water film, almost no resistance.
- The water gets "pumped" into the wedge-shaped gap because the log is rotating and dragging water with it — this is the **hydrodynamic wedge**.

A journal bearing works on exactly this principle: a rotating shaft drags lubricant into a converging wedge, and the pressure generated in this wedge *lifts* the shaft off the bearing surface. The shaft essentially **floats on a thin oil film** of thickness typically **10 µm to 100 µm**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Minimum oil film thickness ($h_0$) | **10 – 100 µm** |
| Absolute viscosity of lubricant ($\mu$) | **0.001 – 0.1 Pa·s** |
| L/D ratio of journal | **0.5 – 2.0** (commonly 1.0) |
| Clearance ratio ($c/r$) | **0.001 – 0.002** |
| Sommerfeld number ($S_o$) | **0.001 – 1.0** (dimensionless) |
| Petroff's friction coefficient | **0.001 – 0.01** (extremely low) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Even a paper-thin oil film (≈ 20 µm — about 1/5 the thickness of a human hair) is enough to support tons of load in a hydrodynamic journal bearing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Journal Bearings

- **Based on Lubrication Mechanism:**
  1. *Hydrostatic* — external pump supplies pressure
  2. *Hydrodynamic* — pressure generated by motion of journal (most common in automobiles)
  3. *Boundary / Mixed / Full-film* — based on $\lambda$ ratio
- **Based on Load Direction:**
  - *Radial* (load ⟂ to shaft axis) — I.C. engine crankshaft main bearings
  - *Thrust* (load ∥ to shaft axis) — less common as pure journal
- **Based on Geometry:** Full journal, partial journal, fitted (fillet) bearing.

## 2.2 Petroff's Friction Theory (Frictionless Ideal Bearing)

Petroff assumed a **concentric journal** (no load) with lubricant behaving as a Newtonian fluid. The torque and friction power loss are derived purely from viscous shear in a uniform clearance gap.

### Assumptions of Petroff's Theory
1. Journal is concentric with the bearing (no eccentricity).
2. Lubricant is Newtonian (viscosity constant).
3. Flow is laminar and the film is continuous.
4. No side leakage (infinite length assumption).
5. Bearing is lightly loaded → no pressure generation.

### Petroff's Equations
- **Friction force:** $F = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$
- **Friction torque:** $T = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N^2 L r^4}{c}$
- **Power loss:** $P = \dfrac{4 \pi^3 \mu N^3 L r^5}{c \times 10^6}$ (Watts, when SI units)
- **Coefficient of friction:** $f = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{P_a}$, where $P_a$ is load per unit projected area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Petroff's theory **underestimates friction** in real loaded bearings because it ignores the converging wedge and pressure build-up. It is, however, the **foundation** for understanding viscous friction and the concept of viscosity.

## 2.3 Reynolds' Equation — The Heart of Hydrodynamic Lubrication

Osborne Reynolds (1886) derived the governing differential equation for pressure in a thin lubricant film. In its **2D form for journal bearings**, it is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( \frac{h^3}{\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( \frac{h^3}{\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

Where:
- $h$ = film thickness at any angular position $\theta$
- $U$ = surface velocity of journal = $r\omega$
- $x$ = coordinate along direction of motion, $z$ = along axis
- $p$ = film pressure
- $\mu$ = absolute dynamic viscosity

> [!NOTE]
> The term $6U \partial h/\partial x$ on the RHS is the **squeeze + wedge term** — it represents the volumetric flow rate generated by the moving surface over a varying gap. **No wedge → no hydrodynamic pressure.** This is why a bearing cannot generate load-supporting pressure if the journal is concentric.

## 2.4 Sommerfeld Number — The Dimensionless Design Parameter

The **Sommerfeld Number** is the most important dimensionless group in journal bearing design. It correlates load, speed, viscosity, and geometry.

$$S_o = \dfrac{\mu \, N}{P_a} \left( \dfrac{r}{c} \right)^2 = \dfrac{\mu N}{P_a} \left( \dfrac{D}{c} \right)^2 \times \dfrac{1}{4}$$

Where:
- $N$ = journal speed in rps
- $P_a$ = load per unit **projected area** $= W / (L \cdot D)$
- $r/c$ or $D/c$ = diameter-to-clearance ratio
- $W$ = applied load (N)
- $L$ = bearing length, $D$ = journal diameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Sommerfeld number is **inversely proportional to load**: lighter loads (or higher speeds / viscosities) → higher $S_o$ → more eccentric position → **thicker** minimum film.

## 2.5 Eccentricity, Attitude Angle, and Film Geometry

- **Eccentricity** $e$ = radial displacement of journal center from bearing center.
- **Attitude angle** $\phi$ = angle between the line of centers and the line of load.
- **Minimum film thickness** $h_0 = r - e = c(1 - \epsilon)$ where $\epsilon = e/c$ is the **eccentricity ratio**.
- **Location of minimum film thickness** is approximately at $\theta \approx 90° + \phi$ (just past the load line).

## 2.6 Heat Balance & Bearing Temperature Rise

Power lost to friction $P_f$ must be dissipated by:
1. Conduction through housing → $Q_{cond} = h_s A_s (T_b - T_a)$
2. Oil circulation (sump cooling) → $Q_{oil} = \dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i)$

The operating temperature $T_b$ is found by **equating heat generated to heat dissipated** (i.e., plotting the bearing characteristic curve and the bearing temperature curve on a Sommerfeld vs. temperature plot, and finding their intersection — McKee's solution).

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_P = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N^2 L r^4}{c}$ | Petroff's friction torque | N·m |
| 2 | $P_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^3 \mu N^3 L r^5}{c \times 10^6}$ | Petroff's power loss | W |
| 3 | $f_P = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P_a}$ | Petroff's coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\!\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\!\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\!\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\!\right) = 6U\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | Reynolds equation (2D) | Pa/m³ etc. |
| 5 | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P_a}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | Sommerfeld number | dimensionless |
| 6 | $h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness | m |
| 7 | $P_a = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | Projected bearing pressure | Pa (N/m²) or N/mm² |
| 8 | $h_s = \dfrac{P_f}{A_s (T_b - T_a)}$ | Heat dissipation coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 9 | $\dot{Q}_{oil} = \rho \, Q \, c_p (T_o - T_i)$ | Oil cooling capacity | W |
| 10 | $\dfrac{h_0}{c} = f(S_o, L/D)$ | Design chart equation | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world automotive utility:** These equations directly govern the design of crankshaft main bearings, connecting rod big-end bearings, camshaft bearings, and balance-shaft bearings in passenger cars, trucks, and two-wheelers. A $20\%$ reduction in minimum film thickness can reduce bearing life by **5× to 10×**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Friction Torque (Exhaustive)

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a journal of radius $r$ rotating at $N$ rps inside a concentric bearing of length $L$, with radial clearance $c$. The lubricant film thickness is uniform ($h = c$).

- Surface (linear) velocity of journal: $U = 2\pi r N$
- Velocity gradient in the film (Newtonian, linear): $\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{U}{c}$
- Shear stress at the journal surface: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{\mu U}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi \mu r N}{c}$

### 3.1.2 Friction Force
The shear acts over the projected cylindrical area of the journal: $A = 2\pi r L$.

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \frac{2\pi \mu r N}{c} \times 2\pi r L = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{c}$$

### 3.1.3 Friction Torque
The friction force acts at the journal radius, so torque:

$$T = F \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{c} \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$$

### 3.1.4 Power Loss
$$P_f = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot (2\pi N) = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c} \times 2\pi N = \frac{8\pi^3 \mu N^2 L r^3}{c}$$

⚠ **Note:** Some textbooks use $P_f = T \cdot 2\pi N$ where $N$ is rps, giving $P_f = \frac{8\pi^3 \mu N^2 L r^3}{c}$ in SI. The standard form **$P = \dfrac{4\pi^3 \mu N^3 L r^5}{c \times 10^6}$ in watts** is the one adopted by Shigley/Mott when $N$ is in rpm and dimensions in mm — keep an eye on units.

### 3.1.5 Coefficient of Friction
Projected bearing pressure $P_a = W / (L \cdot D)$. Then:

$$f = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{F}{P_a \cdot L \cdot 2r} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P_a}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Reynolds' Equation (1-D, Short Bearing Approximation)

### 3.2.1 Starting Point: Navier-Stokes + Continuity
For an incompressible Newtonian fluid in a thin film of thickness $h(x)$:
- $u = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}(y^2 - yh) + U\!\left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)$
- $v = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y}(y^2 - yh)$
- $w = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z}(y^2 - yh)$

### 3.2.2 Integrate Continuity Over Film Thickness
Apply $\int_0^h (\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z)\, dy = 0$:

$$\int_0^h \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\, dy + \int_0^h \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\, dy + \int_0^h \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\, dy = 0$$

The last integral gives $[v]_0^h = 0$ (no flow through surfaces). Hence:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\int_0^h u\, dy + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\int_0^h w\, dy = 0$$

### 3.2.3 Substitute $u$ and $w$ and Integrate
After performing the integration term-by-term and simplifying:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( \frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left( \frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6U\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

This is the **Reynolds equation** for a journal bearing. The right-hand side is non-zero only when there is a **wedge** in the gap ($\partial h/\partial x \neq 0$).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Sommerfeld Number

### 3.3.1 Load Carrying Capacity
Integrating the pressure distribution over the bearing surface gives total load $W$:

$$W = 2 r L \int_0^{\pi} p \cos\theta\, d\theta \quad \text{(in polar form)}$$

### 3.3.2 Pressure Distribution (Approximate Closed Form)
For a long or short bearing, the analytical pressure integral yields, after considerable manipulation:

$$W = \dfrac{6 \mu \omega r^3 L}{c^2} \cdot \dfrac{\epsilon (1 - \epsilon^2)}{\sqrt{1 - \epsilon^2 + (2 + \epsilon^2)\pi^2/(4L^{*2})}}$$

where $L^{*} = L/D$ is the length-to-diameter ratio.

### 3.3.3 Rearranging
Defining the load per unit projected area $P_a = W/(2rL)$ and $\omega = 2\pi N$, then algebra:

$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P_a}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{2\pi \epsilon(1 - \epsilon^2)}{3 \sqrt{1 - \epsilon^2 + (2 + \epsilon^2)\pi^2/(4L^{*2})}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This transcendental relation is what links the **Sommerfeld number** (a measurable engineering quantity) to the **eccentricity ratio** $\epsilon$ (a geometric quantity) and is the basis of every design chart in journal bearing design.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A journal bearing has the following data: $D = 100$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $c = 0.1$ mm, $N = 1500$ rpm, $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s, radial load $W = 50$ kN. Find (a) Petroff's power loss, (b) Petroff's coefficient of friction, (c) Sommerfeld number.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Projected pressure:**
$$P_a = \frac{W}{L D} = \frac{50\,000}{0.1 \times 0.1} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Petroff's friction force:**
$$F = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{c} = \frac{4\pi^2 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 0.1 \times (0.05)^2}{1 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$F = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 0.1 \times 0.0025}{10^{-4}} = \frac{0.12337}{10^{-4}} = 1233.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Petroff's friction torque:**
$$T = F \times r = 1233.7 \times 0.05 = 61.685 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Petroff's power loss:**
$$P_f = T \cdot 2\pi N = 61.685 \times 2\pi \times 25 = 9690 \text{ W} \approx 9.69 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Petroff's coefficient of friction:**
$$f_P = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P_a} = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 0.05}{5 \times 10^6}$$
$$f_P = \frac{1.2337}{5 \times 10^6} = 2.467 \times 10^{-7} \;\;(\text{Too small — confirms Petroff under-predicts friction!})$$

**Step 6 — Sommerfeld number:**
$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P_a}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{0.05 \times 25}{5 \times 10^6} \times \left(\frac{0.05}{10^{-4}}\right)^2$$
$$S_o = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 2500^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 6.25 \times 10^6 = 1.5625$$

> [!TIP]
> With $S_o \approx 1.56$ and $L/D = 1$, reading from Raimondi-Boyd charts gives $\epsilon \approx 0.6$, hence $h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon) = 0.1(0.4) = 0.04$ mm = 40 µm — a healthy hydrodynamic film!

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Journal Bearing System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Journal Shaft] --> B[Sliding Contact Interface]
    C[Lubricant Supply System] --> D[Oil Pump and Filter]
    D --> E[Oil Distribution Grooves]
    E --> F[Wedge-Shaped Film Gap h theta]
    B --> F
    F --> G{Hydrodynamic Pressure Generation}
    G --> H[Load Carrying Pressure p theta]
    H --> I[Radial Load Support W]
    F --> J[Shear Stress tau in Oil]
    J --> K[Friction Torque T_f]
    K --> L[Heat Generation Q_gen]
    F --> M[Minimum Film Thickness h0]
    M --> N{Safety Check: h0 vs Allowable}
    N -->|OK| O[Stable Hydrodynamic Operation]
    N -->|NOT OK| P[Bearing Failure Boundary Lubrication]
    L --> Q[Heat Dissipation Paths]
    Q --> R[Housing Conduction]
    Q --> S[Oil Sump Convection]
    R --> T[Equilibrium Operating Temperature T_b]
    S --> T
    T --> G
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Reynolds Equation Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Journal Velocity U] --> C[6U dh/dx term - Wedge Action]
    B[Film Thickness h theta] --> C
    B --> D[h cubed term - Convective Inertia]
    A --> E[Reynolds Equation PDE]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    F[Boundary Conditions: p0 and p2pi is 0] --> E
    E --> G[Pressure Field p theta z]
    G --> H[Load W by integration]
    G --> I[Friction Torque T_f by integration]
    H --> J[Sommerfeld Number S_o]
    I --> K[Heat Generation Rate]
    J --> L[Design Chart: epsilon vs S_o]
    L --> M[Minimum Film Thickness h0]
    M --> N[Bearing Life Prediction]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Bearing Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Input Data: W, N, L, D, mu, T_in] --> S2[Step 2: Compute P_a equals W over L D]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Petroff Estimates for T_f and Q_gen]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Sommerfeld Number S_o]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Use Raimondi-Boyd Charts to find epsilon and phi]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute h0 equals c times 1 minus epsilon]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Heat Balance Iteration to find T_b]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Recompute mu at T_b and Iterate]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Final Check: h0 greater than 10 micrometer]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Select Lubricant Grade and Finalize Design]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** What is a hydrodynamic journal bearing? State any two assumptions of Petroff's theory.

**Model Answer:**
A hydrodynamic journal bearing is one in which the **pressure required to support the load is generated within the lubricant film** by the relative motion of the journal and the bearing — no external pressure source is needed.

Two assumptions of Petroff's theory:
1. The journal is **concentric** with the bearing (eccentricity = 0).
2. The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** with constant viscosity.
*(Any two of: laminar flow, no side leakage, continuous film, no pressure variation in axial direction.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Define the Sommerfeld number. What is its physical significance?

**Model Answer:**
The Sommerfeld number is a dimensionless parameter given by:

$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P_a}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

**Physical significance:** It represents the ratio of viscous forces (which build the supporting pressure) to the load-carrying requirement. Higher $S_o$ means the journal runs at higher eccentricity ratio and produces a **thicker oil film**, hence longer bearing life.

---

## PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice: A or B)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Sommerfeld-Number-Based Design]

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
> A hydrodynamic journal bearing has a diameter of $80$ mm, length $80$ mm, and runs at $1440$ rpm. The radial load is $30$ kN. The lubricant used has a viscosity of $20$ mPa·s. The radial clearance is $0.08$ mm. The bearing is cooled by a simple oil bath at $40°$C.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Calculate the Sommerfeld number and the coefficient of friction using Petroff's equation. Comment on the adequacy of Petroff's theory.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Using Raimondi-Boyd charts (assume L/D = 1), determine the eccentricity ratio, attitude angle, and minimum oil-film thickness. State whether the bearing operates in safe hydrodynamic lubrication.

#### Model Solution

**Given:**
$D = 80$ mm = 0.08 m, $L = 80$ mm = 0.08 m, $N = 1440$ rpm = 24 rps, $W = 30$ kN, $\mu = 20$ mPa·s = 0.02 Pa·s, $c = 0.08$ mm = $8 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $r = 0.04$ m.

**(a) Sommerfeld number and Petroff's friction:**

Projected pressure:
$$P_a = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{30\,000}{0.08 \times 0.08} = 4.6875 \text{ MPa}$$

Sommerfeld number:
$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P_a}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{0.02 \times 24}{4.6875 \times 10^6} \times \left(\frac{0.04}{8 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^2$$
$$S_o = 1.024 \times 10^{-7} \times (500)^2 = 1.024 \times 10^{-7} \times 2.5 \times 10^5 = 0.0256$$

Petroff's friction force:
$$F_P = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{c} = \frac{4\pi^2 \times 0.02 \times 24 \times 0.08 \times (0.04)^2}{8 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$F_P = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.02 \times 24 \times 0.08 \times 0.0016}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.002424}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 30.3 \text{ N}$$

Petroff's coefficient of friction:
$$f_P = \frac{F_P}{W} = \frac{30.3}{30\,000} = 1.01 \times 10^{-3} = 0.00101$$

> **Comment:** Petroff's value is a *lower bound*. The real coefficient of friction (from Raimondi-Boyd) is typically 2 to 4 times higher. Petroff's theory ignores the wedge effect and hence under-predicts friction.

**(b) Reading Raimondi-Boyd charts at $S_o = 0.0256$ and $L/D = 1$:**

Standard chart values (typical readings):
- Eccentricity ratio $\epsilon \approx 0.6$
- Attitude angle $\phi \approx 50°$
- Variable $(r/c)f \approx 2.2$ → $f \approx 2.2 \times (8 \times 10^{-5}/0.04) = 0.0044$
- Variable $Q/(rc\omega L) \approx 0.45$ (side flow rate)
- Variable $h_0/c \approx 0.4$

Minimum film thickness:
$$h_0 = 0.4 \times c = 0.4 \times 0.08 = 0.032 \text{ mm} = 32 \text{ \mu m}$$

> **Verdict:** Since $h_0 = 32$ µm is well above the 10 µm safety threshold, the bearing operates in **safe full-film hydrodynamic lubrication**. The film is thick enough to prevent metal-to-metal contact and to ensure long service life.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Heat Balance & Operating Temperature]

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
> A hydrodynamic journal bearing for an I.C. engine crankshaft has the following data:
> - Journal diameter $D = 60$ mm, Length $L = 60$ mm
> - Speed $N = 3000$ rpm
> - Radial load $W = 20$ kN
> - Radial clearance $c = 0.05$ mm
> - Oil inlet temperature $T_i = 50°$C, oil outlet $T_o = 70°$C
> - Oil flow rate $Q = 6$ litres/min, $\rho = 880$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1900$ J/kg·K
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Calculate the friction power loss and the coefficient of friction. Assume the lubricant viscosity at operating temperature is $0.025$ Pa·s.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Perform a heat balance check. Compute the heat dissipation rate through oil. Comment on whether the bearing is in thermal equilibrium.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Friction power loss and coefficient of friction:**

$N$ in rps: $N = 3000/60 = 50$ rps, $r = 0.03$ m, $L = 0.06$ m, $c = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s.

Sommerfeld number:
$$P_a = \frac{W}{L D} = \frac{20\,000}{0.06 \times 0.06} = 5.556 \text{ MPa}$$
$$S_o = \frac{0.025 \times 50}{5.556 \times 10^6} \times \left(\frac{0.03}{5 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-7} \times 600^2 = 0.081$$

From Raimondi-Boyd at $L/D = 1$ and $S_o = 0.081$:
- $(r/c)f \approx 3.2$ → $f = 3.2 \times (5 \times 10^{-5}/0.03) = 5.33 \times 10^{-3}$

Friction force: $F = f \cdot W = 5.33 \times 10^{-3} \times 20\,000 = 106.7$ N

Friction torque: $T_f = F \cdot r = 106.7 \times 0.03 = 3.2$ N·m

Friction power loss:
$$P_f = T_f \cdot 2\pi N = 3.2 \times 2\pi \times 50 = 1005 \text{ W} \approx 1.0 \text{ kW}$$

**(b) Heat balance check:**

Heat removed by oil:
$$\dot{Q}_{oil} = \rho Q c_p (T_o - T_i) = 880 \times \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \times 1900 \times (70 - 50)$$
$$\dot{Q}_{oil} = 880 \times 10^{-4} \times 1900 \times 20 = 880 \times 10^{-4} \times 38\,000 = 3344 \text{ W}$$

> **Verdict:** Heat generated $= 1005$ W, heat removed by oil $= 3344$ W. Since $\dot{Q}_{oil} \gg P_f$, the bearing is in **safe thermal equilibrium with significant margin** (≈ 70% spare cooling capacity). The bearing can tolerate slight load or speed increases without overheating.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing $N$ in rps vs. rpm without conversion (every 4 marks will be docked for this).
> 2. **Using $W = P_a \cdot L$** (cylindrical area) instead of $W = P_a \cdot L \cdot D$ (projected area) — the **projected area $L \cdot D$** is KTU standard.
> 3. **Forgetting to iterate viscosity** with temperature in heat-balance problems — viscosity at $T_b$ is required, not the inlet value.
> 4. **Petroff vs. Raimondi-Boyd** — students write "Petroff's coefficient of friction" but the question asks for the *real* friction (use charts when eccentricity is given).
> 5. **Not checking the safe $h_0$ criterion** — examiners always award the last 1–2 marks for the concluding safety statement.
> 6. **Sign error in Reynolds' equation** — the wedge term sign convention must follow the direction of motion; reversing it changes load support from positive to negative.

---

## 5.X TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Journal Bearings (Module 2)**

- **Hydrodynamic bearing** = load support entirely from film pressure generated by motion (no external pump).
- **Petroff's theory** → concentric, no wedge, gives a *lower bound* on friction.
- **Reynolds' equation** → governing PDE; the wedge term $6U\,\partial h/\partial x$ is the *only* source of load-supporting pressure.
- **Sommerfeld number** $S_o = (\mu N / P_a) (r/c)^2$ — the master design parameter; high $S_o$ → thick film.
- **Eccentricity ratio** $\epsilon = e/c$ from Raimondi-Boyd charts; **attitude angle** $\phi$ is the angle between the line of centers and the line of action of the load.
- **Minimum film thickness** $h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon)$ → must be **≥ 10 µm** for safe hydrodynamic operation.
- **L/D ratio** typically **0.5 to 2.0**; **1.0** is most common for automotive bearings.
- **Heat balance** → $P_f = \dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i) + h_s A_s (T_b - T_a)$ must be satisfied for steady-state temperature.
- **Lubricant selection** depends on viscosity grade (SAE 20, 30, 50 etc.), which itself depends on operating temperature and load.
- **Automotive applications**: crankshaft main and big-end bearings, camshaft bearings, balance-shaft bearings, rocker-arm shafts, transmission shafts.
- **Common failure modes** (last topic, FYI): fatigue spalling, scoring, smearing, cavitation erosion, and electric-erosion pitting (due to stray currents in turbocharged engines).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrodynamic Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrodynamic Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrodynamic Theory** is the analytical framework that describes the generation of a load-supporting fluid film in machine elements (especially journal and thrust bearings) purely through the relative motion and viscosity of the lubricating fluid — *without* any externally applied pressure. The pressure required to separate the two contacting surfaces is generated *internally* within the converging wedge of the lubricant as it is dragged into the narrowing gap.

In the context of an automotive **sleeve (journal) bearing**, the theory establishes the mathematical relationships between:
- The applied load $W$
- The rotational speed $N$
- The lubricant viscosity $\mu$
- The geometric clearances (radial clearance $c$, radius $r$, length $L$)
- The resulting eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Hydrodynamic theory of lubrication deals with the formation of a continuous, self-sustaining pressurized oil film between two relatively moving surfaces, where the pressure is generated by the viscosity of the oil and the wedging action caused by the geometry and eccentricity of the bearing."*

The foundational governing equation is the **Reynolds Equation** (1886), which is a reduced form of the Navier-Stokes equations for thin-film, incompressible, isoviscous flow.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water-Skating" Analogy:**
> Imagine pushing a flat wooden plank across a thin film of water on a smooth floor. The plank **does not slide on the wood** — it **skates on the water**. The water is dragged *into* the gap, and because the water molecules resist being squeezed (viscosity), they push back *upward*, lifting the plank. 

> This is exactly what happens inside a journal bearing:
> - The **rotating journal** drags oil into the **converging wedge** between the journal and the bearing.
> - Because the oil *cannot escape fast enough* through the narrow gap, **pressure builds up**.
> - This pressure **lifts the journal** slightly off the bearing surface, preventing metal-to-metal contact.

### Why this matters in an automobile:
| Component | Where Hydrodynamic Film Exists |
|---|---|
| **Crankshaft main bearings** | Between crankshaft journal and bearing shell |
| **Connecting rod bearings** | Between crankpin and big-end bearing |
| **Camshaft bearings** | Between cam journal and bearing housing |
| **Transmission gears (sleeve)** | Between gear shaft and bush |
| **Wheel hub bearings** | Inside hub bearing assembly |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If a surface is rotating at high speed inside a close-fitting sleeve with oil present — *it is almost certainly supported by a hydrodynamic film.*

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit | Typical Automotive Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of oil | Pa·s (or N·s/m²) | 0.01 – 0.10 Pa·s |
| $\rho$ | Density of oil | kg/m³ | 850 – 900 kg/m³ |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | rps (rev/s) | 30 – 300 rps (crankshaft) |
| $P$ | Bearing pressure (projected) | N/mm² (MPa) | 7 – 35 MPa |
| $c$ | Radial clearance (D - d)/2 | mm | 0.02 – 0.10 mm |
| $\epsilon$ | Eccentricity ratio (e/c) | dimensionless | 0.1 – 0.9 |
| $\eta$ (Z) | Absolute viscosity | cP or Pa·s | 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity | cSt or m²/s | 10 – 100 cSt at 100°C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize:** The viscosity $\mu$ is the **single most temperature-sensitive parameter** in hydrodynamic theory. Engine oil loses roughly **50% of its viscosity for every 20°C rise**. This is why KTU problems always specify the oil temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The formation of a hydrodynamic oil film follows a strict, *non-negotiable* sequence of conditions (the **Petrov-Gümbel conditions**):

1. **Surface Relative Motion:** The journal must rotate relative to the bearing (sliding velocity $U = \pi d N$).
2. **Wedge-Shaped Clearance:** The clearance between journal and bearing must be *non-uniform* (achieved naturally by eccentricity $e$).
3. **Sufficient Viscosity:** The lubricant must have adequate viscosity at operating temperature.
4. **Adhesion to Surfaces:** Oil must adhere to both surfaces (no slip).
5. **Continuous Supply:** Oil must be supplied continuously to the high-pressure zone.
6. **Load Support Pressure:** The integrated pressure must equal the applied load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Hydrodynamic pressure is generated **only in the converging region** of the wedge (where film thickness is *decreasing* in the direction of motion). In the *diverging* region, sub-atmospheric pressure occurs, but cavitation prevents negative pressure from contributing to load support.

---

## 2.2 Governing Equation — The Reynolds Equation

For a thin, incompressible, Newtonian lubricant film, the **Reynolds Equation** in two dimensions (axial $z$ and circumferential $x$) is:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} $$

where:
- $h$ = film thickness at any point
- $p$ = hydrodynamic pressure
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity
- $U$ = surface velocity of the journal
- $x$ = direction of motion (circumferential)
- $z$ = axial direction

---

## 2.3 Film Thickness Equation

For a journal bearing, the film thickness at any angular position $\phi$ (measured from the line of centers) is:

$$ h = c\left(1 + \epsilon \cos\phi\right) $$

where:
- $c = (D - d)/2$ = radial clearance
- $\epsilon = e/c$ = eccentricity ratio
- $e$ = eccentricity (offset between journal and bearing centers)
- $\phi$ = angle measured from maximum film thickness

> [!TIP]
> **At $\phi = 0$:** $h_{max} = c(1 + \epsilon)$ (thickest part)
> **At $\phi = \pi$:** $h_{min} = c(1 - \epsilon)$ (thinnest part)

---

## 2.4 Sommerfeld's Pressure Distribution (Infinite Bearing)

By integrating the Reynolds equation for an *infinitely long* bearing (neglecting axial pressure gradient $\partial p/\partial z = 0$), **Sommerfeld** obtained the closed-form pressure distribution:

$$ p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi (2 + \epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2 (1 + \epsilon\cos\phi)^2 (2 + \epsilon^2)} $$

This equation gives the hydrodynamic pressure as a function of angular position $\phi$.

---

## 2.5 Petroff's Equation (Concentric, No-Load Friction)

For a **concentric** ($\epsilon = 0$) journal bearing under no load, the frictional torque is given by **Petroff's equation**:

$$ T_f = \frac{4\pi^3 \mu N r^3 L}{c} $$

and the coefficient of friction:

$$ f = \frac{T_f}{W \cdot r} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} \quad \text{when} \ \epsilon \to 0 $$

> [!WARNING]
> Petroff's equation is **only valid for concentric or near-concentric bearings** (very lightly loaded). For practical $\epsilon > 0.1$, use Raimondi-Boyd charts.

---

## 2.6 The Sommerfeld Number (Dimensionless Load Parameter)

The **Sommerfeld Number** is the most important dimensionless group in hydrodynamic bearing design. It relates load, speed, viscosity, and geometry:

$$ S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot 2\pi \quad \text{[or in alternate form: } S = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \text{]} $$

where:
- $N$ = rotational speed in **revolutions per second (rps)**
- $P = W/(2rL)$ = projected bearing pressure
- $r/c$ = clearance ratio (reciprocal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Sommerfeld number is the **designer's "master dial"** — once you know $S$, you can read off $\epsilon$, $f$, $Q/(rcN L)$, and $h_{min}/c$ directly from the Raimondi-Boyd design charts (Module 2 next sub-topic).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Commit to memory for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface velocity | $U = \pi d N$ | m/s | $N$ in rps |
| 2 | Radial clearance | $c = (D - d)/2$ | m | |
| 3 | Clearance ratio | $\psi = c/r$ | dimensionless | usually 0.001–0.002 |
| 4 | Film thickness | $h = c(1 + \epsilon\cos\phi)$ | m | |
| 5 | Reynolds equation | $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}) = 6\mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | Pa/m | Thin-film form |
| 6 | Sommerfeld pressure | $p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi (2 + \epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2 (1 + \epsilon\cos\phi)^2 (2 + \epsilon^2)}$ | Pa | Infinite bearing |
| 7 | Petroff friction torque | $T_f = 4\pi^3 \mu N r^3 L / c$ | N·m | Concentric only |
| 8 | Petroff coefficient of friction | $f = 2\pi^2 \mu N r / (P c)$ | dimensionless | $\epsilon \to 0$ |
| 9 | Sommerfeld number | $S = (\mu N / P)(r/c)^2$ | dimensionless | rps, **not rpm** |
| 10 | Projected bearing pressure | $P = W / (L \cdot d) = W / (2rL)$ | Pa or N/mm² | |
| 11 | Load capacity (infinite bearing) | $W = \frac{6\pi\mu U L r^3 \epsilon}{c^2(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)} \cdot 2$ | N | From integration |
| 12 | Friction variable $\bar{f}$ | $\bar{f} = (r/c) f$ | dimensionless | Read from Raimondi-Boyd |
| 13 | Side flow ratio | $\bar{Q} = Q / (rcNL)$ | dimensionless | Read from Raimondi-Boyd |
| 14 | Min film ratio | $\bar{h} = h_{min}/c = 1 - \epsilon$ | dimensionless | Read from Raimondi-Boyd |
| 15 | Temperature rise | $\Delta T = \frac{8.3 \times 10^6 \cdot f \cdot P}{1 + (\bar{Q}/2)}$ | °C | Empirical |

> [!WARNING]
> **UNIT ALERT:** The Sommerfeld number **MUST** use $N$ in **rps (rev/s)**, not rpm. A common KTU error is using rpm directly, which gives a Sommerfeld number off by a factor of 60.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Why Hydrodynamic Theory Matters |
|---|---|
| **IC Engine Crankshaft** | Determines bearing size, oil grade, and minimum oil pump pressure |
| **Turbogenerators** | High-speed rotor stability depends on Sommerfeld number design |
| **Gearboxes** | Sleeve bearings on layshaft, counter-shaft |
| **Jet Engines** | High-temperature, high-speed bearings operate purely hydrodynamically |
| **Compressors** | Continuous-duty reliability requires full-film lubrication |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** In hydrodynamic design, we **always design for minimum film thickness** $h_{min} \geq 0.025$ mm (for rough surfaces) to ensure the surface asperities do not touch — this is called the **Stribeck criterion**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **complete, non-truncated derivations**. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. Pay close attention to boundary conditions — KTU examiners award marks specifically for stating them.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Equation (Thin-Film Form)

### Starting Point — Navier-Stokes Equations

For an incompressible, Newtonian fluid with negligible body forces and steady flow, the Navier-Stokes equations in Cartesian coordinates reduce for a thin film ($z$ very small) to:

$$ \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial z^2} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = 0 $$

**Step 1: Integrate pressure equation in z**

Since $\partial p/\partial z = 0$, pressure $p$ is a function of $x$ and $y$ only:

$$ p = p(x, y) $$

**Step 2: Integrate velocity profile in z**

For the $x$-direction, integrate twice with boundary conditions $u = 0$ at $z = 0$ (stationary surface) and $u = U$ at $z = h$ (moving surface):

$$ \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} $$

Integrating once:

$$ \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} z + C_1 $$

Integrating again:

$$ u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} z^2 + C_1 z + C_2 $$

**Step 3: Apply boundary conditions**

At $z = 0$: $u = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$

At $z = h$: $u = U \Rightarrow C_1 = \frac{U}{h} - \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h$

**Step 4: Final velocity profile**

$$ u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(z^2 - zh) + \frac{Uz}{h} $$

$$ u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} z(z - h) + \frac{Uz}{h} $$

**Step 5: Apply continuity (mass conservation)**

For an incompressible fluid in a control volume of width $dx$, depth $dy$, and height $h$:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\int_0^h u \, dz\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\int_0^h v \, dz\right) = 0 $$

**Step 6: Carry out the integration**

After integration and simplification (assuming $h$ varies slowly in $x$ — the *Reynolds* or *lubrication approximation*):

$$ \int_0^h u \, dz = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2} $$

$$ \int_0^h v \, dz = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} $$

**Step 7: Substitute back into continuity**

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 0 $$

**Step 8: Rearrange to obtain Reynolds Equation**

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6U\frac{\partial h}{\partial x} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions Used in the Derivation:**
> 1. **No-slip at surfaces:** $u = 0$ at $z = 0$, $u = U$ at $z = h$
> 2. **Thin film:** $h \ll L$ (lubrication approximation)
> 3. **Incompressible flow:** $\rho$ = constant
> 4. **No body forces:** gravity neglected
> 5. **Laminar flow:** $Re$ very low
> 6. **No cavitation:** fluid remains continuous

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Film Thickness Equation

**Given:** A journal of radius $r$ rotates inside a bearing of radius $R$ with an offset (eccentricity) $e$ between their centers.

**Step 1: Define radial clearance**

$$ c = R - r $$

**Step 2: Express film thickness in vector form**

From the geometry (law of cosines on the triangle formed by the two centers and the point of minimum film thickness):

$$ h^2 = R^2 - (e + r\cos\phi)^2 - (r\sin\phi)^2 + 2 \cdot r\sin\phi \cdot 0 $$

After trigonometric simplification (using $R = r + c$ and neglecting $c^2$ since $c \ll r$):

$$ h = c + e\cos\phi $$

**Step 3: Introduce eccentricity ratio**

$$ \epsilon = e/c $$

**Step 4: Final form**

$$ h = c(1 + \epsilon\cos\phi) $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Sommerfeld's Pressure Distribution (Infinite Bearing)

**Assumption:** $\partial p/\partial z = 0$ (infinite length), so Reynolds equation becomes 1D:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(h^3 \frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{dh}{dx} $$

**Step 1: First integration**

$$ h^3 \frac{dp}{dx} = 6\mu U h + C_1 $$

**Step 2: Apply boundary condition at $\phi = 0$**

At the **point of maximum film thickness** ($\phi = 0$), the pressure gradient is zero (symmetry):

$$ h = c(1+\epsilon), \quad \frac{dp}{dx} = 0 $$

Substituting:

$$ 0 = 6\mu U c(1+\epsilon) + C_1 $$

$$ C_1 = -6\mu U c(1+\epsilon) $$

**Step 3: Solve for pressure gradient**

$$ \frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{6\mu U}{h^2} - \frac{6\mu U c(1+\epsilon)}{h^3} $$

$$ \frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{6\mu U}{h^2}\left[1 - \frac{c(1+\epsilon)}{h}\right] $$

**Step 4: Convert to angular coordinate**

Since $x = r\phi$, then $dx = r \, d\phi$:

$$ \frac{dp}{d\phi} = \frac{6\mu U r}{h^2}\left[1 - \frac{c(1+\epsilon)}{h}\right] $$

**Step 5: Substitute film thickness**

$$ \frac{c(1+\epsilon)}{h} = \frac{1+\epsilon}{1+\epsilon\cos\phi} $$

$$ 1 - \frac{1+\epsilon}{1+\epsilon\cos\phi} = \frac{(1+\epsilon\cos\phi) - (1+\epsilon)}{1+\epsilon\cos\phi} = \frac{\epsilon(\cos\phi - 1)}{1+\epsilon\cos\phi} $$

**Step 6: Combine**

$$ \frac{dp}{d\phi} = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon (\cos\phi - 1)}{c^2(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^3} $$

**Step 7: Integrate from 0 to $\phi$ with $p(0) = 0$**

$$ p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon}{c^2} \int_0^\phi \frac{\cos\phi' - 1}{(1+\epsilon\cos\phi')^3} d\phi' $$

**Step 8: Evaluate the integral** (using standard integral tables / partial fractions)

$$ p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi (2 + \epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2 (1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2 (2+\epsilon^2)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Sommerfeld Integration:**
> 1. $p = 0$ at $\phi = 0$ (start of pressure zone)
> 2. $p = 0$ at $\phi = \pi$ (end of pressure zone, due to cavitation — Gümbel condition)
> 3. $dp/d\phi = 0$ at $\phi = 0$ (maximum pressure symmetry)

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Petroff's Equation (Concentric Bearing Friction)

**Assumption:** $\epsilon = 0$ (concentric), $h = c$ (constant film thickness)

**Step 1: Velocity profile (Couette flow)**

With $\partial p/\partial x = 0$ (no pressure-driven flow), the velocity profile is linear:

$$ u = U \cdot \frac{z}{c} $$

**Step 2: Shear stress at the journal surface ($z = 0$)**

$$ \tau_0 = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0} = \frac{\mu U}{c} $$

**Step 3: Substitute $U = \pi d N = 2\pi r N$**

$$ \tau_0 = \frac{2\pi \mu r N}{c} $$

**Step 4: Total frictional force** (acting on journal surface area $2\pi r L$):

$$ F_f = \tau_0 \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu r^2 N L}{c} $$

**Step 5: Friction torque**

$$ T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^3 \mu N r^3 L}{c} $$

**Step 6: Coefficient of friction** (using $W = P \cdot 2rL$):

$$ f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu r^2 N L / c}{P \cdot 2rL} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative form using $Z$ (centipoise):** Engineers often write Petroff's equation as:
> $$ f = \frac{ZN}{44700 \, P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} $$ 
> where $Z$ is in centipoise and $N$ is in rpm.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Load Capacity (Infinite Bearing)

**Step 1: Express the load from pressure**

The vertical component of pressure force supports the load:

$$ W = \int_0^{2\pi} p \cos\phi \cdot Lr \, d\phi $$

**Step 2: Substitute Sommerfeld's $p(\phi)$**

$$ W = \frac{6\mu U L r^3 \epsilon}{c^2(2+\epsilon^2)} \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{\sin\phi \cos\phi (2+\epsilon\cos\phi)}{(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2} d\phi $$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral** (using residue calculus or table lookup):

$$ I = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{\sin\phi \cos\phi (2+\epsilon\cos\phi)}{(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2} d\phi = \frac{2\pi \epsilon}{(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)} $$

**Step 4: Substitute back**

$$ W = \frac{6\mu U L r^3 \epsilon}{c^2(2+\epsilon^2)} \cdot \frac{2\pi \epsilon}{(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)} $$

$$ W = \frac{12\pi \mu U L r^3 \epsilon^2}{c^2 (1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)^2} $$

**Step 5: Express in terms of Sommerfeld number**

Substituting $U = 2\pi r N$ and $P = W/(2rL)$:

$$ S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{2\pi \mu N r L}{W} \cdot \frac{r^2}{c^2} = \frac{2\pi \mu N r^3 L}{W c^2} $$

Therefore:

$$ S = \frac{2\pi \mu N r^3 L}{W c^2} = \frac{(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)^2}{6 \epsilon^2} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master design equation:** For a given Sommerfeld number $S$, the eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$ is uniquely determined (and vice versa). It is the foundation of the Raimondi-Boyd design charts.

---

## 3.6 Derivation of Frictional Torque (Eccentric Bearing)

**Step 1: Shear stress at journal surface**

The total shear at $z = 0$ is the sum of **Couette** (drag) and **Poiseuille** (pressure-driven) components:

$$ \tau_0 = \frac{\mu U}{h} \pm \frac{h}{2}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \bigg|_{z=0} $$

**Step 2: Integrate shear over journal surface**

$$ F_f = \int_0^{2\pi} \tau_0 \cdot Lr \, d\phi $$

**Step 3: Solve** (the integration is lengthy but tractable):

$$ F_f = \frac{2\pi \mu U L r}{c} \left[\frac{1 + 2\epsilon^2}{(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\epsilon^2}} $$

**Step 4: Final dimensionless friction**

Dividing by the Petroff value $F_0 = 4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L / c$:

$$ \frac{F_f}{F_0} = \frac{(1 + 2\epsilon^2)}{(1-\epsilon^2)} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \text{(chart factor)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, this is read directly from the **Raimondi-Boyd charts** as the variable $\bar{f} = (r/c) f$.

---

## 3.7 Summary of Derived Equations

| Derived Result | Final Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds equation | $\partial_x(h^3 \partial_x p) + \partial_z(h^3 \partial_z p) = 6\mu U \partial_x h$ | Foundation of all HD analysis |
| Film thickness | $h = c(1 + \epsilon\cos\phi)$ | Geometry |
| Pressure | $p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi(2+\epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2(2+\epsilon^2)}$ | Infinite bearing |
| Load capacity | $W = \frac{12\pi\mu U L r^3\epsilon^2}{c^2(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)^2}$ | Infinite bearing |
| Petroff torque | $T_f = 4\pi^3\mu N r^3 L / c$ | Concentric only |
| Sommerfeld number | $S = \frac{\mu N}{P}(r/c)^2$ | Design parameter |
