# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice). Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year marker and a target Course Outcome / Bloom's level.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** | **CO2 | Bloom: Remember**

> **Q: Define hydrodynamic lubrication. State the Petrov-Gümbel conditions necessary for the formation of a hydrodynamic oil film in a journal bearing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Hydrodynamic lubrication is a regime of lubrication in which the load between two relatively moving surfaces is supported entirely by a self-generated pressurized fluid film. The pressure is generated internally by the **viscous drag** of the lubricant being forced through a **converging wedge** formed by the relative motion and geometry of the surfaces — *without any external pressurization*.

**Petrov-Gümbel Conditions (1 mark — list any three):**
1. There must be relative motion between the surfaces (sliding velocity $U$).
2. The clearance between surfaces must be wedge-shaped (varying gap).
3. The lubricant must have sufficient viscosity $\mu$.
4. The lubricant must adhere to both surfaces (no-slip condition).
5. There must be a continuous supply of lubricant to the wedge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** Examiners award 2 marks for the definition and 1 mark for listing the conditions. Memorize at least 3 conditions by name.

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** | **CO2 | Bloom: Understand**

> **Q: Derive Petroff's equation for the frictional torque in a concentric journal bearing. State clearly all assumptions used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The journal is concentric with the bearing ($\epsilon = 0$).
2. No load (or negligible load) — the surfaces do not touch.
3. The film thickness is constant ($h = c$).
4. Flow is laminar, steady, and isoviscous.
5. The lubricant is a Newtonian fluid.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
With no pressure gradient in the film ($\partial p/\partial x = 0$), the velocity is linear Couette flow:

$$ u = U \cdot \frac{z}{c} $$

Shear stress at the journal surface:

$$ \tau_0 = \mu \frac{U}{c} = \frac{2\pi \mu r N}{c} $$

Total frictional force on journal area $A = 2\pi r L$:

$$ F_f = \tau_0 \cdot A = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu r^2 N L}{c} $$

**Friction torque:**

$$ T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^3 \mu N r^3 L}{c} $$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A: Choice 1 (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** | **CO2 | Bloom: Apply / Analyze**

> **Q (a)** A full journal bearing has the following specifications:
> - Journal diameter: $d = 100$ mm
> - Bearing length: $L = 80$ mm
> - Radial clearance: $c = 0.08$ mm
> - Load: $W = 25$ kN
> - Speed: $N = 1500$ rpm
> - Lubricant viscosity: $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s
>
> **Calculate:**
> 1. The Sommerfeld number of the bearing. (3 marks)
> 2. The coefficient of friction using Petroff's equation. (4 marks)

> **Q (b)** Explain the significance of the Sommerfeld number in hydrodynamic bearing design. State the four dimensionless design variables that are typically read from the Raimondi-Boyd design charts. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q (a):**

**Given Data:**
- $d = 100$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $L = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- $c = 0.08$ mm $= 0.00008$ m
- $W = 25$ kN $= 25{,}000$ N
- $N = 1500$ rpm $= 1500/60 = 25$ rps
- $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s

**Step 1: Projected bearing pressure (P)**

$$ P = \frac{W}{L \cdot d} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.08 \times 0.1} $$

$$ P = \frac{25{,}000}{0.008} = 3.125 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 3.125 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 2: Clearance ratio**

$$ \frac{r}{c} = \frac{50}{0.08} = 625 $$

$$ \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = 390{,}625 $$

**Step 3: Sommerfeld number**

$$ S = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 $$

$$ S = \frac{0.025 \times 25}{3.125 \times 10^6} \times 390{,}625 $$

$$ S = \frac{0.625}{3.125 \times 10^6} \times 390{,}625 $$

$$ S = 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 390{,}625 = 0.0781 $$

**Result:** $S \approx 0.078$ (dimensionless)

**Step 4: Petroff's coefficient of friction**

$$ f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c} $$

$$ f = \frac{2 \times \pi^2 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times 0.05}{3.125 \times 10^6 \times 0.00008} $$

**Numerator:** $2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times 0.05 = 0.6168$

**Denominator:** $3.125 \times 10^6 \times 0.00008 = 250$

$$ f = \frac{0.6168}{250} = 2.47 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Result:** $f \approx 0.00247$

> [!TIP]
> **Mark allocation (Q a):** 1 mark for $P$, 1 mark for $r/c$, 1 mark for $S$, 1 mark for $f$ formula, 2 marks for substitution and final value, 1 mark for unit check.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q (b):**

**Significance of the Sommerfeld Number (4 marks):**

1. **Dimensionless Design Parameter:** The Sommerfeld number $S$ consolidates the four primary bearing variables (load, speed, viscosity, clearance) into a single dimensionless parameter, enabling universal design analysis independent of bearing size.

2. **Uniqueness of Eccentricity:** For a given $S$ and $L/D$ ratio, the eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$ is uniquely determined — making it the *primary design dial* for hydrodynamic bearings.

3. **Enables Use of Design Charts:** Once $S$ is computed, all other performance variables — friction $\bar{f}$, side flow $\bar{Q}$, temperature rise $\Delta T$, and minimum film thickness $\bar{h}_{min}$ — can be read directly from the Raimondi-Boyd charts.

4. **Operating Regime Indicator:** The value of $S$ indicates the operating regime:
   - **$S < 0.01$:** Heavily loaded, high $\epsilon$ (close to metal contact)
   - **$S \approx 0.1$:** Normal operation
   - **$S > 1$:** Lightly loaded, low friction

**Four Dimensionless Raimondi-Boyd Variables (3 marks):**

| # | Variable | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\epsilon$ | Eccentricity ratio $= e/c$ | Journal position |
| 2 | $\bar{f} = (r/c)f$ | Friction variable | Total frictional resistance |
| 3 | $\bar{Q} = Q/(rcNL)$ | Side flow ratio | Oil flow rate required |
| 4 | $\bar{h}_{min} = h_{min}/c$ | Minimum film ratio | Film thickness for safe operation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $S$ as a function of $N$ in rpm. **Always convert to rps first.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing "Petroff's $f$" (valid only for $\epsilon \to 0$) with "Raimondi-Boyd $\bar{f}$" (valid for any $\epsilon$).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to express $P$ as *projected* pressure $W/(L \cdot d)$ — not the actual contact area pressure.
> - **Mark Loss:** Not stating the Sommerfeld number formula explicitly costs 1 mark in most KTU answer sheets.

---

### Question B-B: Choice 2 (Alternative to B-A, 14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** | **CO2 | Bloom: Apply / Analyze**

> **Q (a)** A short journal bearing is subjected to a load of $W = 12$ kN at a speed of $N = 1800$ rpm. The bearing has a diameter of $80$ mm, length $40$ mm, and radial clearance $0.05$ mm. The lubricant viscosity is $\mu = 0.020$ Pa·s. Determine the **coefficient of friction** and the **frictional power loss** using Petroff's equation. (7 marks)

> **Q (b)** With neat sketches, describe the **variation of hydrodynamic pressure** along the circumference of a journal bearing. Show that the load-carrying capacity is generated only in the **converging region** of the wedge. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q (a):**

**Given Data:**
- $W = 12$ kN $= 12{,}000$ N
- $N = 1800$ rpm $= 30$ rps
- $d = 80$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 0.04$ m
- $L = 40$ mm $= 0.04$ m
- $c = 0.05$ mm $= 0.00005$ m
- $\mu = 0.020$ Pa·s

**Step 1: Projected bearing pressure**

$$ P = \frac{W}{L \cdot d} = \frac{12{,}000}{0.04 \times 0.08} = \frac{12{,}000}{0.0032} = 3.75 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 2: Petroff's coefficient of friction**

$$ f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c} $$

**Numerator:** $2 \times \pi^2 \times 0.020 \times 30 \times 0.04 = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.024 = 0.4737$

**Denominator:** $3.75 \times 10^6 \times 0.00005 = 187.5$

$$ f = \frac{0.4737}{187.5} = 2.526 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Result:** $f \approx 0.00253$

**Step 3: Frictional force**

$$ F_f = f \cdot W = 0.00253 \times 12{,}000 = 30.36 \text{ N} $$

**Step 4: Friction torque**

$$ T_f = F_f \cdot r = 30.36 \times 0.04 = 1.214 \text{ N·m} $$

**Step 5: Angular velocity**

$$ \omega = 2\pi N = 2\pi \times 30 = 188.5 \text{ rad/s} $$

**Step 6: Frictional power loss**

$$ P_{loss} = T_f \cdot \omega = 1.214 \times 188.5 = 228.8 \text{ W} \approx 0.229 \text{ kW} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer (Q a):** $f \approx 0.00253$, $P_{loss} \approx 0.23$ kW

---

#### **Model Solution to Q (b):**

**Description of Pressure Variation (4 marks):**

For an eccentrically loaded journal bearing, the film thickness varies as $h = c(1 + \epsilon \cos\phi)$. The hydrodynamic pressure, given by the Sommerfeld equation, varies along the circumference as follows:

**Region 1: Converging Wedge** (from $\phi = 0$ to $\phi \approx \pi$ where positive pressure exists)

- Maximum positive pressure occurs near $\phi \approx 60°$ to $90°$ (depending on $\epsilon$).
- Pressure rises from **zero at $\phi = 0$**, reaches a **peak $p_{max}$**, and falls back to **zero at the cavitation boundary** (typically $\phi \approx \pi$).
- This is the **load-supporting region**.

**Region 2: Diverging Wedge** (from $\phi = \pi$ to $\phi = 2\pi$)

- Mathematically, the Sommerfeld equation predicts *negative* pressure here.
- In reality, **cavitation** occurs — dissolved gases and vapor form bubbles, preventing sub-atmospheric pressure.
- The **Gümbel boundary condition** is applied: $p = 0$ throughout this region.

**Mathematical Justification (3 marks):**

The load capacity is obtained by integrating the **vertical component** of pressure around the bearing:

$$ W = \int_0^{2\pi} p(\phi) \cos\phi \cdot Lr \, d\phi $$

For pressure in the diverging region ($p = 0$ by cavitation), only the **converging region** contributes:

$$ W = \int_0^{\pi_{cav}} p(\phi) \cos\phi \cdot Lr \, d\phi $$

The integral of $\cos\phi$ in the diverging region ($\pi$ to $2\pi$) is *negative*, but since $p = 0$ there, no negative contribution exists. This mathematically proves that the **converging region alone supports the load**.

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**
- A circular bearing with journal offset.
- Pressure curve: starts at 0, peaks at ~60-90°, falls to 0 at $\sim 180°$, remains at 0 thereafter.
- Label: converging wedge, diverging wedge, maximum pressure zone, cavitation zone.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Drawing the pressure curve as a *symmetric sine wave* — this is **wrong**. The pressure is *asymmetric* and terminates at the cavitation boundary.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention the **Gümbel cavitation condition** — KTU examiners specifically look for this in any hydrodynamic theory answer.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the location of maximum pressure with the location of maximum film thickness.
> - **Mark Loss:** Failing to label the *converging* and *diverging* regions on the sketch costs 1-2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Pin this to your wall before the exam.**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ Hydrodynamic lubrication = self-generated pressurized film from viscous drag in a converging wedge.
- ☐ Hydrostatic lubrication = external pump creates the film (NOT covered in this module).
- ☐ Elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL) = for gear and rolling contact (covered in Module 3).

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- ☐ Film thickness: $h = c(1 + \epsilon \cos\phi)$
- ☐ Sommerfeld pressure: $p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi(2+\epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2(2+\epsilon^2)}$
- ☐ Petroff torque: $T_f = 4\pi^3 \mu N r^3 L / c$
- ☐ Petroff friction coefficient: $f = 2\pi^2 \mu N r / (Pc)$
- ☐ Sommerfeld number: $S = (\mu N / P)(r/c)^2$ — **with $N$ in rps**
- ☐ Projected pressure: $P = W/(L \cdot d)$

### Boundary Conditions to Always State
- ☐ $p = 0$ at $\phi = 0$ (start of pressure zone)
- ☐ $p = 0$ at $\phi = \pi$ (cavitation boundary, Gümbel condition)
- ☐ $dp/d\phi = 0$ at $\phi = 0$ (symmetry)
- ☐ No-slip at surfaces: $u = 0$ at $z=0$, $u = U$ at $z=h$

### Physical Insights
- ☐ Pressure is generated **only in the converging region** (0 to π).
- ☐ Cavitation prevents negative pressure — Gümbel boundary condition applies.
- ☐ Higher $\epsilon$ → higher load capacity, higher friction, lower minimum film thickness.
- ☐ Higher viscosity $\mu$ → higher load capacity (use this for heavy loads).
- ☐ Higher speed $N$ → higher load capacity (use this for high-speed machines).
- ☐ Petroff's equation is **only for $\epsilon \to 0$** (concentric). For real bearings, use Raimondi-Boyd.

### Common KTU Exam Pitfalls
- ☐ Using $N$ in rpm instead of rps in the Sommerfeld number (off by factor 60).
- ☐ Confusing actual contact area $Ld$ with projected area $Ld$ (they are the same — projected area is $L \times d$, since $d$ is the journal diameter).
- ☐ Forgetting to convert $c$ from mm to m in SI units.
- ☐ Stating Reynolds equation without mentioning the **lubrication approximation** ($h \ll L$).

### Real-World Automotive Applications
- ☐ Crankshaft main and big-end bearings
- ☐ Camshaft bearings
- ☐ Transmission layshaft bearings
- ☐ Turbocharger rotor bearings
- ☐ Wheel hub bearings (sleeve type)

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid for the exam:**
> *"Reynolds generates pressure → Pressure supports load → Load sets eccentricity → Eccentricity creates the wedge → Wedge creates the next pressure cycle."* This closed loop is the essence of hydrodynamic theory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sommerfeld Number
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sommerfeld Number

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Sommerfeld Number (So)** is a **dimensionless quantity** that mathematically characterizes the operating regime of a **hydrodynamically lubricated journal bearing** (sliding contact bearing). It establishes a non-dimensional relationship between the bearing's load-carrying capacity, the lubricant's dynamic viscosity, the rotational speed of the journal, and the geometric clearances of the bearing assembly.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (Automotive Component Design), the Sommerfeld Number is the *primary design index* used to select lubricants, determine eccentricity, and predict the **minimum oil film thickness (h₀)** in crankshaft main bearings, connecting rod bearings, and camshaft bearings of internal combustion engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The Sommerfeld Number serves as the bridge between the **Reynolds Equation** (which describes hydrodynamic pressure generation) and the practical **bearing design charts** (used to read off the coefficient of friction, eccentricity ratio, and side leakage factor). It is *the* governing parameter for all hydrodynamic bearing analysis.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Skier & The Ski Boat"

Imagine a water skier being pulled by a boat. For the skier to stay afloat on the water surface (analogous to the journal "floating" on an oil film), three things must be true:

| Real-World Element | Bearing Equivalent | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Boat's pulling speed | Journal rotational speed | $N$ or $\omega$ |
| Water's resistance to flow | Lubricant dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ |
| Skier's body weight | Load on the bearing | $W$ or $P$ |
| Gap between ski & water | Radial clearance | $c$ |

If the **boat moves too slowly** (low speed) or the **skier is too heavy** (high load), the skier sinks — the bearing will experience **boundary lubrication / metal-to-metal contact**. If the boat moves fast enough, hydrodynamic pressure builds up in the wedge-shaped water film, lifting the skier. The **Sommerfeld Number mathematically captures this "lift-to-sink" balance**.

- A **High Sommerfeld Number** ⇒ Strong hydrodynamic film ⇒ Safe operation.
- A **Low Sommerfeld Number** ⇒ Weak film ⇒ Risk of seizure/wear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ of typical engine oil (SAE 20W-50 at 100°C) ≈ **0.011 Pa·s**
> - Standard bearing clearance ratio $c/r$ for automotive bearings ≈ **0.001 to 0.002**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Principle

When a journal rotates inside a bearing at speed $\omega$, the viscous oil dragged into the converging wedge (between the journal and the bearing) generates a hydrodynamic pressure $p$ as governed by the **Reynolds Equation**. This pressure pushes the journal upward, offsetting the external load $W$ and causing the journal center to **eccentrically displace** from the bearing center by an amount $e$.

The **dimensionless eccentricity ratio** is:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{e}{c}$$
where $c = R - r$ is the **radial clearance** (bearing inner radius $R$ minus journal radius $r$).

The Sommerfeld Number is derived by **non-dimensionalizing** the Reynolds equation with respect to the bearing geometry, lubricant properties, and operating conditions.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Step 1 — Identify the load-bearing film:** Oil is dragged by the rotating journal into the converging gap.
2. **Step 2 — Pressure builds hydrodynamically:** Viscosity resists the shearing, generating pressure $p(\theta)$.
3. **Step 3 — Pressure acts on the journal:** The integrated pressure over the bearing arc must equal the applied load $W$.
4. **Step 4 — Force balance is non-dimensionalized:** All variables (load, speed, viscosity, geometry) are grouped into a single dimensionless number — the Sommerfeld Number.
5. **Step 5 — Charts are consulted:** The calculated $S_o$ is entered into published design charts (e.g., Raimondi-Boyd charts) to extract $\varepsilon$, friction variable, and flow variable.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Convention:** Always state the units before substitution. KTU examiners deduct marks for unit ambiguity.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N_s}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | Sommerfeld Number (with $N_s$ in rev/s) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{60\, P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | Sommerfeld Number (with $N$ in **rpm**) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | In terms of angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N_s$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{r}{c}\dfrac{\mu N_s}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)$ | Sommerfeld **Constant** $S$ (bearing-specific) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | Bearing pressure (projected area) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 6 | $c = R - r$ | Radial clearance | m |
| 7 | $\dfrac{L}{D}$ | Length-to-diameter ratio (aspect ratio) | — |
| 8 | $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness | m |
| 9 | $h_0 = c(1 + \varepsilon)$ | Maximum film thickness | m |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{e}{c}$ | Eccentricity ratio | — |
| 11 | $\mu_h = \mu_0 \, e^{\beta(T_0 - T)}$ | Viscosity-temperature relation (Vogel/Slotter) | Pa·s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Warning:** Some textbooks (e.g., Khurmi & Gupta) use $P$ for **load per unit projected area** (Pa) while others use $P$ for **total load** (N). The KTU 2024 convention aligns with **bearing pressure $P$ in N/m²**, computed as $P = W/(L \cdot D)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine bearing selection:** Helps in choosing the right grade of bearing material (white metal, copper-lead, aluminum-tin) for given load and speed.
- **Lubricant design:** Enables formulation of oils with specific viscosity grades (SAE 5W-30, 10W-40, etc.) to keep $S_o$ in the safe hydrodynamic regime at all operating temperatures.
- **Electric motor bearings:** Critical in selecting greases and oil-mist lubrication systems.
- **Turbomachinery:** Used in designing large steam/gas turbine journal bearings where $S_o$ values of 0.1–1.0 are typical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sommerfeld Number (Petroff's Starting Point)

The classical derivation begins with **Petroff's Equation** — the friction torque for a concentric journal (zero load, full concentricity).

### Step 1: Shear Stress in a Viscous Fluid
For a thin Newtonian fluid film of thickness $h$, the shear stress due to viscosity is given by Newton's law of viscosity:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

The velocity gradient across the film is $\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{U}{h}$, where $U = r\omega$ is the linear surface speed of the journal. Hence:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{r\omega}{h}$$

### Step 2: Friction Force on the Journal
The frictional force $F_f$ acting on the journal surface (assumed concentric, $h = c$ everywhere) is the shear stress times the bearing contact area $2\pi r L$:
$$F_f = \tau \cdot (2\pi r L) = \mu \frac{r\omega}{c} \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega r^2 L}{c}$$

### Step 3: Petroff's Friction Power
The power lost to friction is:
$$\mathcal{P}_{f} = F_f \cdot U = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega r^2 L}{c} \cdot r\omega = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega^2 r^3 L}{c}$$

### Step 4: Non-Dimensionalization
The total load $W$ is related to bearing pressure $P$ through the projected area $L \cdot D = 2rL$:
$$W = P \cdot (2rL)$$

To make the analysis dimensionless, we form the ratio of the **load-carrying tendency** to the **friction forces**, scaled by the clearance:
$$S_o = \frac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

This is obtained by:
$$\frac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu \omega}{P} \cdot \frac{r^2}{c^2} = \frac{\mu \omega \cdot r^2}{P \cdot c^2} = \frac{\mu \omega r^2 L}{P c^2 L} = \frac{F_f / 2\pi}{W/2r} \cdot \frac{r^2}{c^2}$$

Substituting $F_f = 2\pi \mu \omega r^2 L/c$ and $W = 2rLP$:
$$S_o = \frac{F_f}{W}\cdot\frac{r}{2\pi c} = \frac{\mu \omega r^2 L / c}{2rLP}\cdot\frac{r}{2\pi c} = \frac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

### Step 5: Final Form (KTU Standard Notation)
$$\boxed{\,S_o = \frac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Note for KTU 2024:** Examiners expect students to explicitly state the assumptions:
> 1. Inertia of the lubricant is negligible.
> 2. The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** (viscosity independent of shear rate).
> 3. The flow is **laminar** in the bearing clearance gap.
> 4. The pressure is constant across the film thickness.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Variation of Design Parameters with $S_o$

| Sommerfeld Number $S_o$ | Eccentricity Ratio $\varepsilon$ | Film Thickness $h_0$ | Operating Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_o \to 0$ (no load, high speed) | $\varepsilon \to 0$ | $h_0 \to c$ (maximum) | **Full film hydrodynamic** (Petroff regime) |
| $S_o \approx 0.001$ | $\varepsilon \approx 0.1$ | $h_0 \approx 0.9c$ | Thick film, near-concentric |
| $S_o \approx 0.1$ | $\varepsilon \approx 0.5$ | $h_0 \approx 0.5c$ | Typical operating point |
| $S_o \approx 1.0$ | $\varepsilon \approx 0.9$ | $h_0 \approx 0.1c$ | Thin film, near-edge loading |
| $S_o \to \infty$ (no speed) | $\varepsilon \to 1$ | $h_0 \to 0$ (metal contact) | **Boundary lubrication** (seizure) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A journal bearing has the following specifications:
> - Journal diameter $D = 100$ mm, $L = 60$ mm
> - Radial clearance $c = 0.05$ mm
> - Load $W = 25$ kN
> - Speed $N = 1500$ rpm
> - Lubricant viscosity $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Bearing pressure $P$, (b) Sommerfeld Number $S_o$, (c) State the operating regime.

**Solution:**

**(a) Bearing Pressure:**
$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{25 \times 10^3}{0.060 \times 0.100} = \frac{25\,000}{0.006} = 4.167 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} \approx 4.17\ \text{MPa}$$

**(b) Angular Velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Journal Radius:**
$$r = \frac{D}{2} = 0.050\ \text{m}$$

**Clearance Ratio:**
$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.050}{0.00005} = 1000$$

**Sommerfeld Number:**
$$S_o = \frac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{0.025 \times 157.08}{4.167 \times 10^6} \times (1000)^2$$
$$S_o = \frac{3.927}{4.167 \times 10^6} \times 10^6 = \frac{3.927}{4.167} \approx 0.943$$

**(c) Operating Regime:**
Since $S_o \approx 0.943$ (close to 1.0), the bearing operates in the **thin-film / near-edge-loading regime**. Eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon \approx 0.9$, indicating that the journal center has shifted significantly toward the bearing surface. The minimum oil film thickness would be:
$$h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon) \approx 0.05\,(1 - 0.9) = 0.005\ \text{mm} = 5\ \mu\text{m}$$

This is dangerously close to surface roughness values, suggesting the need for either lower viscosity oil or improved surface finishing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Role of the Sommerfeld Number in Bearing Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs: Load W, Speed N, Viscosity mu, Geometry D L c] --> B[Compute Angular Velocity omega = 2pi N/60]
    B --> C[Compute Bearing Pressure P = W / L*D]
    C --> D[Compute Clearance Ratio r/c]
    D --> E[Compute Sommerfeld Number So = mu*omega/P * r/c^2]
    E --> F{Database Lookup: Raimondi-Boyd Charts}
    F --> G[Read Eccentricity Ratio epsilon]
    F --> H[Read Friction Variable r mu Ns / P]
    F --> I[Read Flow Variable Q / r c Ns L]
    G --> J[Compute Minimum Film Thickness h0 = c1 - epsilon]
    H --> K[Compute Friction Force Ff]
    I --> L[Compute Side Leakage Flow Q]
    J --> M[Compare h0 with Surface Roughness]
    M -->|h0 greater than Ra| N[SAFE: Full Film Hydrodynamic]
    M -->|h0 less than Ra| O[UNSAFE: Boundary / Mixed Lubrication]
    N --> P[Final Bearing Design Confirmed]
    O --> Q[Redesign: Reduce mu, Increase c, or use better surface finish]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Physical Setup of a Journal Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Bearing_Cross_Section
        J1[Journal Center O_j] -->|radius r| JM[Journal Surface]
        B1[Bearing Center O_b] -->|radius R| BM[Bearing Inner Surface]
        JM -.->|minimum film h0| BM
        JM -.->|maximum film h_max| BM2[Bearing Inner Surface Opposite Side]
    end
    LO[Applied Load W] -->|acts vertically down| B1
    DISP[Eccentricity e = O_b - O_j] --> B1
    DISP --> J1
    WET[Lubricant Supply] -->|inlet at clearance gap| BM
    DR[Rotational Drive omega] -->|rotates journal| J1
    HFRES[Hydrodynamic Pressure p theta] -->|integrated over arc| B1
    HFRES -.->|balances| LO
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reynolds Equation for Journal Bearing] --> B[Non-Dimensionalization]
    B --> C[Sommerfeld Number So]
    C --> D[Design Chart Lookup: Raimondi-Boyd 1958]
    D --> E1[Eccentricity Ratio epsilon]
    D --> E2[Friction Coefficient f]
    D --> E3[Side Flow Ratio Qs]
    E1 --> F1[Minimum Film Thickness h0]
    F1 --> G{Design Verification}
    G -->|h0 greater than k_combined| H[Approved Design]
    G -->|h0 less than k_combined| I[Iterate: Re-select mu, c, or material]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Q: Define the Sommerfeld Number. State its significance in the design of a journal bearing.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sommerfeld Number $S_o$ is a **dimensionless parameter** used to characterize the operating conditions of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. It is defined as:
$$S_o = \frac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$
where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, $\omega$ is the angular velocity, $P$ is the bearing pressure, $r$ is the journal radius, and $c$ is the radial clearance.

**Significance:**
1. It non-dimensionalizes the load, speed, viscosity, and geometric parameters of a bearing into a single number.
2. It is used as the entry parameter in design charts (e.g., Raimondi-Boyd) to obtain the eccentricity ratio, friction variable, and side flow variable.
3. It determines the **operating regime** — whether the bearing operates in safe hydrodynamic lubrication or unsafe boundary lubrication.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: What is the effect of a high Sommerfeld Number on the operating characteristics of a journal bearing?**

**Model Answer:**
A **high Sommerfeld Number** indicates a relatively low bearing pressure (load) or a high rotational speed, or a large clearance ratio $r/c$. Its effects on operating characteristics are:

1. **Low eccentricity ratio** $\varepsilon$ — the journal center stays close to the bearing center, providing a thick lubricant film.
2. **Larger minimum film thickness** $h_0 = c(1-\varepsilon)$ — better protection against metal-to-metal contact.
3. **Lower coefficient of friction** — reduced frictional losses and heat generation.
4. **Safe hydrodynamic lubrication regime** — desirable for long bearing life.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: A full journal bearing has the following specifications:**
- Journal diameter: $D = 80$ mm
- Bearing length: $L = 80$ mm (square bearing, $L/D = 1$)
- Radial clearance: $c = 0.04$ mm
- Load on bearing: $W = 20$ kN
- Speed: $N = 1440$ rpm
- Lubricant viscosity: $\mu = 0.020$ Pa·s
- Specific gravity of lubricant: $0.86$

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The Sommerfeld Number and hence the eccentricity ratio (assume $\varepsilon = 0.6$ for first iteration).
**(b)** The coefficient of friction and the heat generated due to friction per minute.
**(7 + 7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Compute the Sommerfeld Number.

Step 1 — Bearing pressure:
$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{20\,000}{0.080 \times 0.080} = \frac{20\,000}{0.0064} = 3.125 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} = 3.125\ \text{MPa}$$

Step 2 — Angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440}{60} = 48\pi \approx 150.80\ \text{rad/s}$$

Step 3 — Clearance ratio:
$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.040}{0.00004} = 1000 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = 10^6$$

Step 4 — Sommerfeld Number:
$$S_o = \frac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{0.020 \times 150.80}{3.125 \times 10^6} \times 10^6$$
$$S_o = \frac{3.016}{3.125} \approx 0.965$$

For $L/D = 1$ and $S_o = 0.965$, from the **Raimondi-Boyd design chart** the eccentricity ratio is:
$$\varepsilon \approx 0.85 \quad \text{(re-iterate if required)}$$

Step 5 — Minimum film thickness:
$$h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 0.04(1 - 0.85) = 0.006\ \text{mm} = 6\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Part (b):** Coefficient of friction and heat dissipation.

Step 6 — For $L/D = 1$ and $\varepsilon = 0.85$, the **friction variable** from Raimondi-Boyd charts:
$$\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)\mu\frac{N_s}{P} \approx 4.0 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

Step 7 — Speed in rev/s:
$$N_s = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{1440}{60} = 24\ \text{rev/s}$$

Step 8 — Solve for the friction force $F_f$:
$$\frac{F_f}{r\,c\,L\,N_s\,\mu} = 4.0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad F_f = 4.0 \cdot r \cdot c \cdot L \cdot N_s \cdot \mu$$
$$F_f = 4.0 \times 0.040 \times 0.00004 \times 0.080 \times 24 \times 0.020$$
$$F_f = 4.0 \times 6.144 \times 10^{-8} = 2.458 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{N}$$
*(Note: Numerical value here is illustrative; the correct procedure uses published chart values.)*

Step 9 — Coefficient of friction:
$$\mu_f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{2.458 \times 10^{-7}}{20\,000} \approx 1.23 \times 10^{-11}$$

Step 10 — Heat generated per minute:
$$H = F_f \cdot v \cdot 60 = F_f \cdot (\pi D N) \cdot 60$$
*(Use the journal surface velocity $v = \pi D N / 60$.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** or **rev/s** costs 1 mark. Always show $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up clearance $c$ and diameter $D$** in the projected area formula $P = W/(LD)$ is a common error.
> 3. **Not stating the chart values used** for $\varepsilon$ and the friction variable — examiners want to see which chart value you are interpolating.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** in viscosity (Pa·s vs. cP) and pressure (Pa vs. MPa) is a frequent mark-deduction reason.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Set) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: A short journal bearing has the following operating data:**
- Diameter: $D = 100$ mm
- Length: $L = 50$ mm
- Radial clearance: $c = 0.05$ mm
- Load: $W = 30$ kN
- Speed: $N = 1000$ rpm
- Viscosity: $\mu = 0.030$ Pa·s

**Determine:**
**(a)** The Sommerfeld Number, the bearing pressure, and the Sommerfeld Constant.
**(b)** Using Raimondi-Boyd charts (assume $\varepsilon = 0.75$), determine the coefficient of friction and the power lost in friction.
**(7 + 7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a):**
- $P = W/(LD) = 30\,000 / (0.050 \times 0.100) = 6.0 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $\omega = 2\pi \times 1000/60 = 104.72$ rad/s
- $(r/c)^2 = (0.050/0.00005)^2 = 10^6$
- $S_o = (0.030 \times 104.72 / 6.0 \times 10^6) \times 10^6 = 3.142/6.0 = 0.524$
- Sommerfeld Constant $S = S_o \cdot L/(2r) = 0.524 \times 0.050/0.100 = 0.262$ (for $L/D = 0.5$)

**Part (b):**
- From Raimondi-Boyd charts for $L/D = 0.5$ and $\varepsilon = 0.75$, read the friction variable.
- Compute friction force $F_f$ from the friction variable.
- Compute power loss $\mathcal{P} = F_f \times \pi D N / 60$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Part B:** Students forget that for **finite-length bearings**, the Sommerfeld Number is a function of $L/D$. Always use the *Sommerfeld Constant* $S = S_o \cdot (L/D)$ when consulting the charts. Using the infinite-length assumption ($L/D \to \infty$) for short bearings leads to a 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Sommerfeld Number**

- [ ] **Definition:** $S_o = \dfrac{\mu \omega}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ — **dimensionless**.
- [ ] **Three input groups:** Viscosity $\mu$, Angular velocity $\omega$, Bearing pressure $P$ — combine with **clearance ratio** $r/c$.
- [ ] **High $S_o$** ⇒ Thin-film regime risky; **Low $S_o$** ⇒ Safe hydrodynamic regime.
- [ ] **Typical range for automotive bearings:** $S_o = 0.001$ to $1.0$.
- [ ] **Always convert $N$** (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) or $N_s$ (rev/s) before substituting.
- [ ] **Sommerfeld Constant** $S = S_o \cdot (L/D)$ — used for finite-length bearing design chart lookups.
- [ ] **Minimum film thickness** $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ is the design safety criterion.
- [ ] **Raimondi-Boyd charts** (1958) are the standard KTU-referenced design charts.
- [ ] **Petroff's equation** is the starting point for deriving $S_o$ assuming concentric motion.
- [ ] **Assumptions:** Newtonian fluid, laminar flow, negligible inertia, isoviscous — must be stated.
- [ ] **Real-world application:** Crankcase bearings in IC engines, turbine bearings, electric motor bearings.
- [ ] **Unit vigilance:** $\mu$ in **Pa·s**, $P$ in **Pa**, $r$ and $c$ in **m** — never mix mm, cm, m arbitrarily.
- [ ] **Physical meaning:** $S_o$ is essentially the ratio of viscous shearing forces (friction tendency) to the bearing load.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Considerations** in the context of automotive component design refer to the comprehensive set of engineering, manufacturing, economic, ergonomic, and regulatory factors that a designer must systematically evaluate and integrate during the conceptualization, analysis, and detailed design phases of any automotive part or sub-assembly.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature aligned with **NEP 2020** and **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** frameworks, design considerations constitute the *pre-design and concurrent design intelligence* that governs every subsequent decision — from material selection and geometric form to manufacturing route and end-of-life recyclability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *Design considerations* encompass the qualitative and quantitative criteria — including strength, stiffness, wear resistance, manufacturability, cost, weight, safety, reliability, ergonomics, environmental impact, and standardization — that collectively constrain the design space and define an acceptable engineering solution for an automotive component.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing an automotive component like **planning a long-distance road trip in a family car**:

- **The Destination (Function):** Where are you going? (The component must *do* something — transmit torque, support load, seal fluid.)
- **The Passengers (Constraints):** Safety, comfort, budget, time. (Strength, ergonomics, cost, durability.)
- **The Vehicle Choice (Material & Process):** SUV vs. Sedan vs. Hatchback. (Cast iron vs. aluminum vs. composite; forging vs. casting vs. machining.)
- **The Road Conditions (Operating Environment):** Mountains, deserts, monsoon. (Temperature, vibration, corrosion, shock loads.)
- **The Fuel Stops (Manufacturing & Assembly):** Where and how you refuel affects route. (Machinability, weldability, assembly sequence.)

> [!NOTE]
> Just as no rational person plans a trip considering *only* the destination, no rational engineer designs a brake disc, connecting rod, or crankshaft considering *only* strength. Every **design consideration** is one such "trip-planning variable," and the *art of design* lies in balancing them.

---

## 1.3 The Holistic Triad of Automotive Design

Every design consideration ultimately rolls up into one of **three master categories**:

| Master Category | Core Question | Typical Metrics |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Performance** | *Will it work?* | Stress (MPa), deflection (mm), fatigue life (cycles), NVH (dB) |
| **Manufacturability & Cost** | *Can we make it affordably?* | Cycle time (s), tooling cost (₹), tolerance (μm), yield (%) |
| **Sustainability & Safety** | *Is it safe and responsible?* | CO₂ footprint (kg), recyclability (%), failure rate (ppm), ISO/ECE compliance |

> [!TIP]
> In the **KTU University Exam**, examiners often award marks to students who explicitly **categorize** their design considerations rather than presenting them as a flat list. Always signal your answer with this triad when relevant.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following standard metrics are **universally referenced** in KTU board exams for automotive design:

- **Factor of Safety (FOS or n):** typically **1.5 to 4.0** for static automotive components; **1.2 to 2.5** for fatigue-loaded components.
- **Density of Mild Steel:** **7850 kg/m³** (baseline reference for weight calculations).
- **Young's Modulus of Steel:** **E = 200 GPa** (standard for stiffness deflection).
- **Endurance Limit of Steel:** **σ_e ≈ 0.5 × σ_ut** for ferrous materials (Rotating-beam baseline).
- **Standard ambient design temperature for under-hood components:** **−40 °C to +150 °C**.
- **Standard acceleration envelope (road vehicles):** **up to 8 g** (transient shock on rough terrain).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Design Considerations — Structured Breakdown

Design considerations in PBAUT604 are best understood as a **hierarchical decision tree**, evaluated from broad (system-level) to narrow (feature-level).

### Level 1 — Functional Requirements
- **Primary Function:** Load path, energy transmission, motion conversion.
- **Operating Conditions:** Static, dynamic, fatigue, impact, thermal.
- **Performance Envelope:** Torque, RPM, pressure, displacement.

### Level 2 — Mechanical Design Considerations
- **Strength:** Resistance to yielding and fracture under load.
- **Stiffness:** Resistance to elastic deformation.
- **Fatigue Life:** Endurance under cyclic loading (S-N approach, strain-life).
- **Wear Resistance:** Surface durability under sliding/rolling contact.
- **Creep & Stress Relaxation:** High-temperature behaviour.
- **Buckling Resistance:** For slender components (drive shafts, columns).

### Level 3 — Material Selection Considerations
- **Mechanical Properties:** σ_y, σ_ut, E, G, hardness, toughness.
- **Physical Properties:** Density, thermal conductivity, CTE, corrosion resistance.
- **Manufacturing Compatibility:** Castability, forgeability, machinability, weldability.
- **Cost & Availability:** Per-kg cost, supply chain stability.

### Level 4 — Manufacturing & Process Considerations
- **Process Selection:** Casting, forging, machining, sheet metal, MIM, additive.
- **Tolerance Capability:** Achievable ± tolerances per ISO 2768.
- **Surface Finish:** Ra values, coating requirements.
- **Joining Methods:** Welding, riveting, adhesive bonding, snap-fits.

### Level 5 — Economic & Lifecycle Considerations
- **Initial Cost:** Material + process + tooling.
- **Operating Cost:** Fuel economy impact (weight-driven).
- **Maintenance Cost:** Serviceability, parts commonality.
- **End-of-Life:** Disassembly, recyclability, ELV directive compliance.

### Level 6 — Regulatory & Ergonomic Considerations
- **Safety Standards:** AIS, ECE R-series, FMVSS, ISO 26262 (functional safety).
- **Ergonomics:** Driver reach, control force, H-point.
- **Aesthetics & Brand:** Styling constraints, perceived quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the hierarchy matters in KTU exams:**
> Examiners test whether students understand that **Level 1 constraints drive Level 2 calculations**, which in turn restrict Level 3 choices. A 14-mark question typically expects you to walk through at least three levels explicitly.

---

## 2.2 The Design Logic Flow — The "Why" and "How"

A logical sequence that mirrors how professional design engineers and KTU examiners think:

1. **Identify Function** → *Why* does this component exist? (e.g., crankshaft converts reciprocating → rotary motion.)
2. **Quantify Loads** → *How* much force/torque? (use engine torque, firing pressure, inertia.)
3. **Select Material** → *Why this alloy?* (strength-to-weight ratio, fatigue, cost.)
4. **Choose Manufacturing Process** → *How will it be made?* (forging for strength-critical, casting for complex geometry.)
5. **Apply Sizing Equations** → *How big?* (bending, torsion, combined loading equations.)
6. **Apply Factor of Safety** → *How confident are we?* (uncertainty in loads, materials, environment.)
7. **Check Secondary Modes** → *What else can fail?* (fatigue, buckling, wear, corrosion, NVH.)
8. **Optimize** → *Can we do better?* (topology optimization, weight reduction, cost reduction.)
9. **Validate** → *Does it actually work?* (FEA, prototype testing, homologation.)

> [!TIP]
> Memorizing this **9-step logic flow** and applying it even in a 3-mark conceptual question demonstrates the *systems thinking* that KTU examiners reward with full marks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **core mathematical backbone** of Module 2 — Design Considerations. They cover factor of safety, stress concentration, fatigue, and combined loading checks.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Factor of Safety (yielding) | $n_y = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress (MPa), $\sigma_{max}$ = max working stress (MPa) | $n_y \geq 1.5$ (static ductile) |
| 2 | Factor of Safety (ultimate) | $n_u = \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | $\sigma_{ut}$ = ultimate tensile stress (MPa) | $n_u \geq 2.5$ (static brittle) |
| 3 | Stress Concentration Factor | $K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$ | $\sigma_{max}$ = peak stress at discontinuity (MPa), $\sigma_{nom}$ = nominal stress (MPa) | $K_t \geq 1.0$; from charts/tables |
| 4 | Notch Sensitivity (Peterson) | $q = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{r}}}$ | $a$ = material constant (mm), $r$ = notch radius (mm) | $0 \leq q \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Fatigue Stress Concentration | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | $K_t$ = geometric, $q$ = notch sensitivity | $K_f \geq 1.0$ |
| 6 | Modified Endurance Limit (Marin) | $\sigma_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, \sigma_e'$ | Each $k$ is a modifying factor (dimensionless) | $\sigma_e \leq \sigma_e'$ |
| 7 | Surface Factor (Marin) | $k_a = a \sigma_{ut}^b$ | $a,b$ from table (e.g., $a=4.51$, $b=-0.265$ for ground) | $\sigma_{ut}$ in MPa |
| 8 | Size Factor (for $d$ in mm) | $k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$ | $d$ = effective diameter (mm), $2.79 \leq d \leq 51$ mm | Round/square sections |
| 9 | Goodman Mean-Stress Correction | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | $\sigma_a$ = alternating, $\sigma_m$ = mean, $n$ = FOS | Conservative (most used) |
| 10 | Soderberg Criterion | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress | Most conservative criterion |
| 11 | Gerber Parabolic Criterion | $\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\dfrac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | — | Less conservative than Goodman |
| 12 | von Mises Equivalent Stress | $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + 3 \tau_{xy}^2}$ | For 2D plane stress | Ductile failure (most used) |
| 13 | Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | $\sigma_1,\sigma_3$ = max, min principal | Conservative vs. von Mises |
| 14 | Shaft Torsional Shear | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | $T$ = torque (N·m), $d$ = diameter (m) | Solid circular shaft |
| 15 | Shaft Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ | $M$ = bending moment (N·m) | Solid circular shaft |
| 16 | Column Buckling (Euler) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $I$ = 2nd moment, $L$ = length | Slenderness ratio $\geq$ critical |
| 17 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{K L}{r}$ where $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ | $r$ = radius of gyration (m) | $\lambda > \lambda_c$ for Euler |
| 18 | Hertzian Contact Stress | $\sigma_H = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 F}{\ell} \cdot \dfrac{(R_2 - R_1)}{R_1 R_2}}$ | $F$ = normal load, $R$ = radii, $\ell$ = line contact length | Line contact (gears, bearings) |
| 19 | Steady Thermal Stress | $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = CTE (per °C), $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (°C) | Constrained expansion |
| 20 | Specific Strength | $S_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) | Used for weight-critical choice |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Never confuse **$K_t$** (geometric/theoretical, from charts) with **$K_f$** (fatigue, modified by notch sensitivity). Examiners *frequently* test this distinction.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These design considerations govern every major automotive component:

- **Crankshaft:** Bending fatigue (Goodman) + Torsional fatigue + Bearing wear (Hertz).
- **Connecting Rod:** Buckling check + Fatigue (Goodman) + Lightweighting (Specific Strength).
- **Brake Disc:** Thermal stress + Wear resistance + Vibration (NVH).
- **Helical Spring (Suspension/Valve):** Fatigue (Soderberg/Goodman) + Buckling + Surge frequency.
- **Gears:** Hertzian contact stress + Bending fatigue (Lewis equation) + Scuffing.
- **Frame/Chassis:** Buckling (Euler) + Crashworthiness + NVH.

In professional design offices (Tata Motors, Mahindra, Maruti, Bosch, Continental), the **selection and prioritization** of these considerations — not just the formulas — is what differentiates a senior design engineer from a junior one.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section performs **complete, line-by-line derivations** of the three most heavily tested equations from Module 2's design considerations syllabus: **Marin's Modified Endurance Limit, Goodman Line derivation, and the Equivalent Bending Moment for Combined Shaft Loading.** No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Marin's Modified Endurance Limit

The unmodified endurance limit $\sigma_e'$ is the rotating-beam fatigue strength of a small, polished specimen. Real components differ — they are larger, rougher, loaded in various modes, and operate in corrosive or elevated-temperature environments. **Marin's equation** systematically accounts for these deviations.

### Step 1: Surface Finish Factor ($k_a$)

A rougher surface introduces micro-notches that initiate cracks earlier. The empirical fit (Shigley):

$$
k_a = a \, \sigma_{ut}^{b}
$$

where $a$ and $b$ are tabulated constants. For example:
- **Ground:** $a = 4.51$, $b = -0.265$
- **Machined or Cold-Drawn:** $a = 3.04$, $b = -0.217$
- **Hot-Rolled:** $a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$
- **As-Forged:** $a = 1.00$, $b = 0$ (baseline reference)

The **logic**: As $\sigma_{ut}$ increases, the surface becomes relatively more sensitive to roughness → $k_a$ decreases.

### Step 2: Size Factor ($k_b$)

Larger cross-sections have a higher probability of containing a critical flaw (statistical size effect). For a round, rotating, bending-loaded specimen with diameter $d$ (mm) in $2.79 \leq d \leq 51$ mm:

$$
k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}
$$

> For axial loading (no size gradient), $k_b = 1$.

### Step 3: Load Factor ($k_c$)

$$
k_c = 
\begin{cases}
1.0 & \text{(pure bending)} \\
0.85 & \text{(pure axial)} \\
0.59 & \text{(pure torsion)}
\end{cases}
$$

This is **experimentally determined**, not derived — captures that torsion and axial loads are less damaging per unit stress than bending.

### Step 4: Temperature Factor ($k_d$)

$$
k_d = 
\begin{cases}
1.0 & T \leq 450^\circ C \\
0.85 & 450 < T \leq 550^\circ C \\
\text{lower} & T > 550^\circ C
\end{cases}
$$

Creep and oxidation reduce endurance at elevated temperature.

### Step 5: Reliability Factor ($k_e$)

$$
k_e = 
\begin{cases}
1.000 & 50\% \text{ reliability} \\
0.897 & 90\% \\
0.814 & 95\% \\
0.753 & 99\% \\
0.659 & 99.9\%
\end{cases}
$$

Derived from the standard normal distribution (z-scores): $k_e = 1 - z \cdot s_{log}$, where $s_{log}$ is the standard deviation of $\log(\text{life})$.

### Step 6: Miscellaneous Factor ($k_f$)

$$
k_f \approx 0.7 \text{ to } 1.0
$$

Captures residual stresses, corrosion, plating, fretting, and other unmodeled effects. Often conservatively taken as **0.814** for general use.

### Step 7: Final Assembly

$$
\sigma_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot \sigma_e'
$$

> The factors are **multiplicative** because each represents an *independent* deviation from the baseline rotating-beam test condition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Goodman Line (Mean-Stress Correction)

### Premise
Under fully reversed loading ($\sigma_m = 0$), the endurance limit is $\sigma_e$.
Under static loading ($\sigma_a = 0$), the failure stress is $\sigma_{ut}$.
We need a linear relationship between these two extremes — the **Goodman line**.

### Step 1: Define the Two Anchor Points
- **Point A:** $(\sigma_a, \sigma_m) = (\sigma_e, 0)$ — fully reversed fatigue.
- **Point B:** $(\sigma_a, \sigma_m) = (0, \sigma_{ut})$ — static rupture.

### Step 2: Equation of a Straight Line Through Two Points

$$
\frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{0 - \sigma_e} = \frac{\sigma_m - 0}{\sigma_{ut} - 0}
$$

### Step 3: Algebraic Simplification

$$
\frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{-\sigma_e} = \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}
$$

Multiply both sides by $-\sigma_e$:

$$
\sigma_a - \sigma_e = -\frac{\sigma_e \, \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\sigma_a + \frac{\sigma_e \, \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = \sigma_e
$$

### Step 4: Introduce Factor of Safety $n$

For design, we require the actual alternating stress $\sigma_a$ and mean stress $\sigma_m$ to lie **inside** the safe region. Define $n$ such that:

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{a,act}}{n}, \quad \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{m,act}}{n}
$$

where $\sigma_{a,act}, \sigma_{m,act}$ are actual operating stresses. Substituting and dividing by $\sigma_e$:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

> This is the **Goodman criterion in design form**, the most commonly tested mean-stress correction equation in KTU examinations.

### Step 5: Soderberg Variant (Derivation Same Structure, Different Anchor)

Replace $\sigma_{ut}$ with $\sigma_y$ (yield, not rupture):

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

Soderberg is **more conservative** because $\sigma_y < \sigma_{ut}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Bending Moment for a Shaft under Combined Loading

A transmission shaft is typically subjected to **bending moment $M$ and torque $T$ simultaneously**. KTU examiners *frequently* ask for the equivalent moment formulation.

### Step 1: Maximum Bending Stress

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}
$$

### Step 2: Maximum Torsional Shear Stress

$$
\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Distortion-Energy (von Mises) Failure Theory for Combined Stress

The von Mises equivalent stress for a state with $\sigma_b$ (normal) and $\tau$ (shear):

$$
\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^{2} + 3\tau^{2}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2} + 3\left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2}}
$$

### Step 4: Factor Out the Common Term

$$
\sigma_{vm} = \frac{32}{\pi d^{3}} \sqrt{M^{2} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{4} T^{2}}
$$

> Note: $\left(\dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^2 \cdot 3 = \dfrac{3 \cdot 256 T^2}{\pi^2 d^6} = \dfrac{768 T^2}{\pi^2 d^6}$
>
> And: $\left(\dfrac{32}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{3}{4} T^2 = \dfrac{1024}{\pi^2 d^6} \cdot \frac{3}{4} T^2 = \dfrac{768 T^2}{\pi^2 d^6}$. ✓ Algebraic consistency verified.

### Step 5: Define the Equivalent Bending Moment

By analogy with $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$, define $M_e$ such that:

$$
\sigma_{vm} = \frac{32 M_e}{\pi d^3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + \frac{3}{4} T^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $M_e$ formula is **extremely high-yield** for KTU board exam problems involving crankshafts, drive shafts, and axles. Memorize the $\sqrt{M^2 + 0.75\,T^2}$ form verbatim.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Theory Selection Guide

| Loading Condition | Material Type | Recommended Failure Theory | Governing Equation | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniaxial tension/compression | Ductile | Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) or Distortion Energy (DE) | $\tau_{max} = \sigma_y/2$ or DE | Both work; DE gives ~15% higher allowable |
| Combined bending + torsion | Ductile | Distortion Energy (von Mises) | $M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2}$ | Most accurate for ductiles |
| Pure shear | Ductile | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau = \tau_y = 0.5 \sigma_y$ | Direct from Tresca |
| Static, brittle (CI, ceramics) | Brittle | Maximum Normal Stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{ut}$ | Cracks propagate from max principal |
| Cyclic, any material | Any | Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber | See Section 3.2 | Captures mean-stress effects |
| Contact (gears, bearings) | Any | Hertzian contact theory | $\sigma_H \leq \sigma_{H,allow}$ | Subsurface shear, not bulk yield |
| Slender column (axle in bending) | Any | Euler buckling | $P < P_{cr}/n$ | Geometric instability |
| High temperature | Any | Creep laws (Norton-Bailey) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^n e^{-Q/RT}$ | Time-dependent deformation |

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Design Considerations Decision Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid **flowchart** maps the sequential and iterative decision-making process that a designer follows when applying Module 2's design considerations to an automotive component. It is the *mental model* examiners expect in a 14-mark design question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
