### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Using Rayleigh's method, derive the expression for the critical speed of a simply supported shaft carrying a single concentrated mass at the centre. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of length **1.5 m**, diameter **60 mm**, is simply supported at the ends and carries two point masses: **25 kg at 0.5 m** from the left support and **40 kg at 1.0 m** from the left support. Calculate the critical speed using Dunkerley's method. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (already covered in Section 3.1):**

> **Setup:** Simply supported shaft of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, with rotor mass $m$ at mid-span.
>
> **Static deflection:** $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{W\,L^3}{48\,EI}$ where $W = m\,g$
>
> **Strain energy:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\,W\,\delta_{st} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,m\,g\,\delta_{st}$
>
> **Maximum kinetic energy** (at whirling, assuming orbit radius $\delta_{st}$):
> $$T_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(\omega_c\,\delta_{st})^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}\,m\,\omega_c^2\,\delta_{st}^2$$
>
> **Energy balance at critical speed:** $T_{max} = U$
> $$\dfrac{1}{2}\,m\,\omega_c^2\,\delta_{st}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}\,m\,g\,\delta_{st}$$
> $$\omega_c^2\,\delta_{st} = g$$
> $$\boxed{\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{48\,E\,I\,g}{W\,L^3}}}$$
>
> In rpm: $\boxed{N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}}$

**(b) Dunkerley's Method Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $I$:

$$I = \frac{\pi\,(0.060)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{64} = 6.362 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{m}^4$$

**Step 2:** For a simply supported beam with point load $W$ at distance $a$ from one end and $b = L - a$ from the other, the static deflection at the load point is:

$$\delta = \frac{W\,a^2\,b^2}{3\,E\,I\,L}$$

**Step 3:** Critical speed if ONLY mass 1 (25 kg at $a_1 = 0.5$ m, $b_1 = 1.0$ m) is on the shaft (shaft treated as light):

$$\delta_{st,1} = \frac{(25 \times 9.81) \times (0.5)^2 \times (1.0)^2}{3 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 6.362 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.5}$$

$$\delta_{st,1} = \frac{245.25 \times 0.25}{572.58} = \frac{61.3125}{572.58} = 1.071 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}$$

$$N_{c,1} = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{1.071 \times 10^{-4}}} = 9.55 \sqrt{91{,}596.6} = 9.55 \times 302.65 \approx 2890 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4:** Critical speed if ONLY mass 2 (40 kg at $a_2 = 1.0$ m, $b_2 = 0.5$ m) is on the shaft:

$$\delta_{st,2} = \frac{(40 \times 9.81) \times (1.0)^2 \times (0.5)^2}{3 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 6.362 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.5}$$

$$\delta_{st,2} = \frac{392.4 \times 0.25}{572.58} = \frac{98.1}{572.58} = 1.713 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}$$

$$N_{c,2} = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{1.713 \times 10^{-4}}} = 9.55 \sqrt{57{,}267.4} = 9.55 \times 239.31 \approx 2286 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Dunkerley's equation (in terms of $N_c^2$):

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \frac{1}{N_{c,1}^2} + \frac{1}{N_{c,2}^2}$$

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \frac{1}{2890^2} + \frac{1}{2286^2} = \frac{1}{8.352 \times 10^{6}} + \frac{1}{5.226 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = 1.197 \times 10^{-7} + 1.914 \times 10^{-7} = 3.111 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$N_c^2 = 3.214 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\boxed{N_c \approx 1793 \;\; \text{rpm}}$$

> [!TIP]
> Dunkerley's value is always **less** than the smallest of the individual critical speeds — a **conservative lower bound** that engineers prefer for safe design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Critical Frequency**

- **Definition:** Critical speed = speed at which the shaft's natural frequency equals the rotational frequency → resonance.
- **Core formula:** $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \approx 9.55\,\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ **rpm**
- **Angular form:** $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$
- **Static deflection (simply supported, central load):** $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{W\,L^3}{48\,EI}$
- **Static deflection (off-centre load):** $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{W\,a^2\,b^2}{3\,E\,I\,L}$
- **Stiffness (simply supported, central):** $k = \dfrac{48\,EI}{L^3}$
- **Multiple critical speeds** are integer-square multiples ($4\times$, $9\times$, $16\times$ …) of the 1st critical for uniform simply supported beams.
- **Dunkerley's lower bound:** $\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{N_{c,i}^2}$ — for shafts with multiple rotors.
- **Rayleigh's assumption:** the static deflected shape approximates the dynamic mode shape → works well for 1st critical speed.
- **Operating rule:** Stay below **0.8 × Nc** (sub-critical) or above **1.2 × Nc** (super-critical).
- **Whirling** is a self-excited phenomenon due to inherent rotor eccentricity; it is **not caused by external forcing**.
- **Critical speed depends on** $L$, $D$, $E$, $I$, and rotor mass — **not** on eccentricity $e$.
- **Eccentricity** $e$ affects only the **amplitude** of whirl, not the **critical speed** value.
- **Automotive relevance:** crankshafts, propeller shafts, drive shafts, turbocharger rotors, half-shafts.
- **Design levers to raise $N_c$:** shorter shaft, larger diameter, hollow shafts (high $I$/mass ratio), higher modulus material, additional bearings.
- **Units pitfall:** Always convert $\delta_{st}$ to **meters** before substituting into $g/\delta_{st}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: End Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** End Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**End Construction** in automotive component design (specifically in the context of leaf springs) refers to the geometric and structural configurations used to terminate the ends of the master (longest) leaf of a laminated leaf spring assembly. It governs how the spring is connected to the vehicle's frame, axle seat, and shackle, and directly influences the **stress distribution**, **manufacturing cost**, **assembly sequence**, and **fatigue life** of the spring.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 2 – Design of Springs), the end construction of a leaf spring defines the boundary condition and load transfer mechanism at the extremities of the master leaf, which is the most critically stressed member in the entire spring pack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBAUT604 – Module 2):**
> "End construction of leaf springs – plain, eye, hook, semi-elliptical, extended, and helper leaf configurations – design of eye, master leaf, and end-pin."

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### 1.2.1 The "Book-Holder" Analogy

Imagine you are holding a thick, heavy textbook (say, a 1000-page engineering reference) using a single, long metal strip that bends under the weight. To attach this strip to the wall, you have several options:

- You can simply **tape the end flat to the wall** (this is the **plain end** – it slides but cannot transmit tension easily).
- You can **drill a hole at the end and pin it to the wall** (this is the **eye end** – it carries tension through a pin).
- You can **bend the end into a J-shape and hook it** over a rod (this is the **hooked end** – common in trailer springs).
- You can **extend the length** so it has more reach and an extra support (this is the **extended end**).

The choice of "end" depends on **how much movement, tension, and load** the connection must handle. The same engineering logic applies to a leaf spring in a vehicle.

## 1.3 Why End Construction is Critical in Automobiles

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it matter?**
> 1. The end of the master leaf experiences **maximum bending stress** and **maximum shear stress** during vehicle operation.
> 2. The end is also the **load-transfer zone** between the spring and the chassis.
> 3. **Improper end design** is the **#1 cause of leaf spring failure** in service (next to fatigue from road loads).
> 4. The end shape determines whether the spring can act as a **single-point fastener**, a **sliding guide**, or a **shackle-mounted articulating link**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Notation)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Master Leaf** | The longest leaf of the spring pack, on which the end construction is built. |
| **Eye** | A circular hole at the end of the master leaf through which an **eye-bolt / end-pin** passes. |
| **Eye-Bolt / End-Pin** | The hardened cylindrical pin that connects the spring eye to the bracket or shackle. |
| **Shackle** | A pivoting link that connects the rear eye of the spring to the chassis bracket. |
| **Helper Leaf** | An additional shorter leaf used to support the master leaf when the main spring is highly deflected. |
| **Center Bolt (U-Bolt)** | The fastener that clamps the leaves together at the center. |
| **Rebound Clip** | A clip that prevents the leaves from separating during rebound (negative deflection). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (KTU Reference):**
> - **Permissible bending stress for spring steel ($ \sigma_b $)** = **450–550 MPa** (for 55Si7, 60Si7, SUP 9, SUP 11 steels).
> - **Modulus of Elasticity ($ E $)** = **210 GPa** (for spring steel).
> - **Permissible shear stress at the eye section ($ \tau_{eye} $)** = **0.5 $ \sigma_b $** ≈ **225 MPa** (approx.).
> - **Eye outer diameter to width ratio** = typically **$ d_o \approx 3w $** to **$ 4w $**, where $w$ is the leaf width.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of End Construction (Master Leaf Configurations)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the end construction of leaf springs is classified into **six principal types** (this is a frequently tested classification question).

### 2.1.1 Type 1 — Full-Length Master Leaf (Plain Uncut End)
- The **master leaf has the full length** of the spring and its ends are not shaped.
- The **first full-length leaf** is reinforced with an extra full-length leaf to carry the eye load.
- This was used in **older heavy trucks** (e.g., pre-1950s designs).
- **Disadvantage:** Heavy, expensive, and prone to crack initiation at the abrupt width-change section.

### 2.1.2 Type 2 — Full-Length Master Leaf with Reinforcement Plate
- The master leaf is full-length, but a **separate reinforcement plate** (also full-length) is placed below it.
- The eye is forged on the **reinforcement plate**, not on the master leaf itself.
- **Advantage:** The master leaf acts as a pure bending beam; the plate carries the eye stress.

### 2.1.3 Type 3 — Short Master Leaf with Upset Eye (Most Common — KTU Standard)
- The **master leaf is shorter** than the full length.
- The **ends are upset (forged) into a circular eye** of larger cross-section.
- A **gradual taper** is provided between the eye cross-section and the main leaf cross-section.
- This is the **most widely used** end construction in modern automotive leaf springs (e.g., LCVs, HCVs, SUVs).
- **Advantage:** Material is conserved; stress flow is smooth; fatigue life is high.

### 2.1.4 Type 4 — Semi-Elliptical (Master Leaf Tapered in Plan)
- The **end of the master leaf is tapered in width** (tapered end leaf) to reduce weight.
- The eye is forged at the wider central portion.
- Used in **light commercial vehicles**.

### 2.1.5 Type 5 — Hooked End (Trailer Springs)
- The end of the master leaf is **bent (rolled) into a hook shape** that engages with a quarter-elliptic auxiliary spring.
- Common in **railway and heavy trailer applications**.

### 2.1.6 Type 6 — Extended End (Truck Springs)
- The master leaf is **extended beyond the eye** to form a **sliding contact** with the axle seat.
- Used in **overload conditions** in heavy commercial vehicles.

## 2.2 Helper Leaf (Auxiliary Spring) End Construction

A **helper leaf** is an additional short leaf that engages only when the main spring is heavily loaded (e.g., when a truck is fully loaded). It is mounted in two principal ways:

- **Concentrated Helper (Tip-to-Tip):** The helper leaf's tip rests on the master leaf. When the load exceeds a threshold, the helper leaf contacts the main leaf and shares the load.
- **Distributed Helper (Full-Width Contact):** The helper leaf runs along the entire length of the main leaf, providing progressive engagement.

## 2.3 Design of the Eye — Theoretical Background

The **eye** is the most critically stressed section of the leaf spring. The KTU design approach uses the following reasoning:

### 2.3.1 Force on the Eye
- The maximum vertical load on the spring is **$ W $** (at full vehicle load).
- At the eye, this load is **transferred as a bearing/shear force** through the eye-bolt to the chassis.

### 2.3.2 Stress in the Eye Section
The eye acts as a **forked joint** subjected to a tangential load. The stress in the eye section is a combination of:

- **Bending stress** due to the offset between the line of action of the load and the eye center.
- **Tensile stress** due to the radial pull of the eye-bolt.
- **Shear stress (double shear)** at the two cross-sections of the fork.

### 2.3.3 Permissible Design Stresses (KTU Convention)

| Stress Type | Permissible Value | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bending stress in eye ($\sigma_{b,eye}$) | **$ \tau_{perm} / 0.5 $** | Eye is forged and has higher allowable stress than rolled section |
| Tensile stress across eye | $\sigma_{b,eye} \cdot t \cdot (d_o - d)$ | Force balance over the tangential section |
| Shear stress at pin | $0.5 \sigma_b$ | Double shear consideration |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (For End Construction Design)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Maximum load on spring** | $W = W_s + (n \cdot m_g)$ | N | $W_s$ = static load, $m_g$ = mass per wheel |
| 2 | **Bending stress in leaf** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^2}$ | MPa | $n$ = no. of leaves, $b$ = width, $t$ = thickness |
| 3 | **Deflection of leaf spring** | $\delta = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$ | mm | Single-stage deflection |
| 4 | **Shear stress in eye (tangential)** | $\tau = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot t \cdot (d_o - d)}$ | MPa | $d_o$ = eye OD, $d$ = pin diameter |
| 5 | **Tensile stress across eye** | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{W}{(d_o - d) \cdot t}$ | MPa | Critical for fatigue |
| 6 | **Bearing stress on pin** | $\sigma_{br} = \dfrac{W}{d \cdot t}$ | MPa | Should be $\le 0.8 \sigma_y$ |
| 7 | **Eye pin diameter (empirical)** | $d \approx 0.5 \cdot w$ | mm | $w$ = leaf width |
| 8 | **Eye outer diameter (empirical)** | $d_o \approx 3w$ to $4w$ | mm | KTU 2024 standard |
| 9 | **Taper ratio for eye transition** | $\tan\theta \le 1/6$ | — | Smooth stress flow |
| 10 | **Initial camber (nip)** | $\delta_{nip} = (0.10 \text{ to } 0.15) \cdot \delta_{max}$ | mm | Prevents overload at static load |
| 11 | **Length of graduated leaf (nth leaf)** | $L_n = L \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{c}{2L}\right) \cdot \text{series}$ | mm | C-series or N-series taper |
| 12 | **Stress concentration factor (eye)** | $K_t \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$ | — | Use for forged eye |
| 13 | **Nip / Pre-load stress** | $\sigma_{nip} = \dfrac{6 \cdot W_{nip} \cdot L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^2}$ | MPa | $W_{nip}$ = nip load |
| 14 | **Center-bolt diameter** | $d_c \approx (0.6 \text{ to } 0.75) \cdot t$ | mm | Clamping force |
| 15 | **Permissible bending stress for Si-Mn steel** | $\sigma_b = 450$ to $550$ | MPa | 55Si7 / SUP 9 / SUP 11 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These formulas are used by **OEM design teams** (TATA, Ashok Leyland, Mahindra) to design leaf springs for new vehicle platforms, and by **CAE analysts** to validate FEA models. A KTU student should be able to write these equations directly in the answer script without hesitation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stress in the Eye Section (The Critical Section)

### Problem Context
A leaf spring eye is subjected to a vertical load **$W$** transmitted through the eye-bolt. We need to find the **shear stress** in the tangential section of the eye (the section running along the line of action of $W$).

### Given Data
- Vertical load on the spring: $W$
- Leaf thickness: $t$
- Eye outer diameter: $d_o$
- Eye-bolt (pin) diameter: $d$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium at the eye:**
The load $W$ is transmitted from the chassis bracket to the leaf via the eye-bolt. The two side faces of the eye (above and below the bolt) resist this load in **double shear**.

$$
F_{shear} = W \quad \text{(Total tangential force on the eye)}
$$

**Step 2 — Identification of the shear plane:**
The shear plane is the **tangential section of the eye material on either side of the bolt**. Each section has a length:

$$
l_{sec} = (d_o - d) \quad \text{(Radial length of the eye material)}
$$

**Step 3 — Area resisting shear (two sections, double shear):**
The total area resisting the shear is:

$$
A_{shear} = 2 \cdot t \cdot (d_o - d)
$$

**Step 4 — Shear stress in the eye section:**
Using the basic definition of shear stress:

$$
\tau = \frac{F_{shear}}{A_{shear}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau = \frac{W}{2 \cdot t \cdot (d_o - d)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Permissible stress check:**
According to KTU convention, the permissible shear stress is:

$$
\tau_{perm} \approx 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{b,perm}
$$

So the design equation becomes:

$$
\boxed{\frac{W}{2 \cdot t \cdot (d_o - d)} \le 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{b,perm}}
$$

### Step 6 — Example Numerical Evaluation
Suppose $W = 25{,}000$ N, $t = 12$ mm, $d_o = 60$ mm, $d = 25$ mm, $\sigma_{b,perm} = 500$ MPa.

$$
\tau = \frac{25000}{2 \cdot 12 \cdot (60 - 25)} = \frac{25000}{2 \cdot 12 \cdot 35} = \frac{25000}{840} \approx 29.76 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
0.5 \cdot \sigma_{b,perm} = 0.5 \cdot 500 = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $29.76 \text{ MPa} \ll 250 \text{ MPa}$, the design is **safe** in shear.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tensile Stress Across the Eye Section (Radial Pull)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identifying the critical tensile section:**
The critical tensile section is the **smallest net cross-section** perpendicular to the line of action of the load. This occurs at the **innermost fiber of the eye** (just outside the bolt hole).

**Step 2 — Net area resisting tension:**

$$
A_{tension} = (d_o - d) \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Tensile stress equation:**

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{W}{(d_o - d) \cdot t}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check (same example):**

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{25000}{(60 - 25) \cdot 12} = \frac{25000}{420} \approx 59.52 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Permissible stress check:**
The permissible tensile stress for the eye section is taken as:

$$
\sigma_{t,perm} \approx 0.75 \cdot \sigma_{b,perm} = 0.75 \cdot 500 = 375 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $59.52 \text{ MPa} \ll 375 \text{ MPa}$, the design is **safe in tension**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bearing Stress on the Eye-Bolt (Pin)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Bearing contact area:**
The pin contacts the inner surface of the eye over a **projected area** equal to:

$$
A_{bearing} = d \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Bearing stress:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{br} = \frac{W}{d \cdot t}}
$$

**Step 3 — Permissible value:**
For hardened eye-bolts (typically EN8 / 40C8 / AISI 1040 with surface hardening):

$$
\sigma_{br,perm} \approx 0.8 \cdot \sigma_y
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check:**

$$
\sigma_{br} = \frac{25000}{25 \cdot 12} = \frac{25000}{300} \approx 83.33 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
For an eye-bolt material with $\sigma_y = 600$ MPa:

$$
0.8 \cdot \sigma_y = 480 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $83.33 \text{ MPa} \ll 480 \text{ MPa}$, the design is **safe in bearing**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: End Taper Geometry (Smooth Stress Flow)

### Why Taper is Needed

At the transition from the eye cross-section (thickness $t_e$, width $w_e$) to the main leaf cross-section (thickness $t$, width $w$), a sudden change in geometry would create a **stress concentration** (theoretical $K_t \to \infty$).

To avoid this, a **gradual taper** is provided.

### KTU Standard Taper Ratio

The taper is provided over a length **$l_t$** such that the slope does not exceed **1 in 6** (approximately $9.46°$).

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{(t_e - t)}{l_t} \le \frac{1}{6}
$$

Therefore:

$$
l_t \ge 6 \cdot (t_e - t)
$$

**Example numerical check:**
If $t_e = 18$ mm (eye thickness after forging) and $t = 12$ mm (main leaf thickness):

$$
l_t \ge 6 \cdot (18 - 12) = 36 \text{ mm}
$$

So the tapered section should be at least **36 mm long** for smooth stress flow.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Different End Constructions)

| Property | Plain End | Eye End (Forged) | Hooked End | Extended End | Helper Leaf End |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Stress Level at End** | Very High | Moderate (controlled) | High | Moderate | Low (load shared) |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Low | Medium-High | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Fatigue Life** | Poor | Good | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Load Capacity** | Low | High | High | Very High | Very High |
| **Sliding Allowed?** | Yes | No (Pinned) | No | Yes | Yes (progressive) |
| **Application** | Light trailers | Cars, LCVs, HCVs | Rail, Heavy trailers | Heavy trucks | Heavy trucks (overload) |
| **Common Use in India** | Rare | **Most Common** | Limited | Common (HCV) | Common (HCV) |
| **Stress Concentration** | $K_t = 3.0$ | $K_t = 2.0$ | $K_t = 2.5$ | $K_t = 2.2$ | $K_t = 1.5$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.4 Schematic Block: Eye Cross-Section Stress Zones

| Zone | Location | Stress Type | Magnitude | KTU Formula |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Eye tangential section | Shear | Low | $ \tau = W / 2t(d_o - d) $ |
| 2 | Eye radial section | Tension | Moderate | $ \sigma_t = W / (d_o - d) t $ |
| 3 | Pin contact | Bearing | High | $ \sigma_{br} = W / d t $ |
| 4 | Eye-to-leaf transition | Bending | High | $ \sigma_b = 6W L / n b t^2 $ |
| 5 | Main leaf | Bending | Controlled | $ \sigma_b \le \sigma_{b,perm} $ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Drawing Substitution:**
> The eye is a circular, 3D forged feature with a hardened cylindrical pin passing through it. The pin is locked with a nut and split-pin. The eye outer surface is forged with a gradual taper to the leaf body to prevent stress concentration.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any three end construction types used in leaf springs. Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three end construction types are:

1. **Plain End (Full-Length Master Leaf):** Used in older heavy commercial vehicles where the master leaf is reinforced with an additional full-length plate.
2. **Eye End (Forged on Upset Master Leaf):** Most commonly used in modern cars, LCVs and HCVs; the eye is forged at the upset end of the master leaf.
3. **Hooked End:** Used in railway and trailer springs where the end of the master leaf hooks over a quarter-elliptic auxiliary spring.

(3 marks for correctly listing with application)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a "helper leaf"? Why is it used in heavy commercial vehicle leaf springs?**

**Model Answer:**

A **helper leaf** is an additional short leaf added to a leaf spring pack. It engages the main spring only when the main spring is highly deflected (i.e., under heavy load).

It is used because:

- It provides **progressive load support** — the main spring deflects freely under light loads.
- Under overload, the helper leaf contacts the main leaf and **shares the load**, preventing excessive stress in the master leaf.
- It **extends the fatigue life** of the spring by limiting the maximum deflection of the main pack.

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for reasons)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Choice (Module 2):** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 carries internal choice. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**A leaf spring used in a light commercial vehicle has the following specifications:**
- Maximum vertical load on spring: $W = 22{,}000$ N
- Number of leaves: $n = 8$
- Width of leaf: $b = 65$ mm
- Thickness of leaf: $t = 10$ mm
- Permissible bending stress: $\sigma_b = 500$ MPa
- Modulus of elasticity: $E = 210$ GPa

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Design the end eye of the master leaf. Determine the eye outer diameter and pin (eye-bolt) diameter.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Select pin diameter using empirical relation:

$$
d = 0.5 \cdot w = 0.5 \cdot 65 = 32.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $d = 32$ mm (standard).

**Step 2:** Select eye outer diameter using empirical relation:

$$
d_o = 3.5 \cdot w = 3.5 \cdot 65 = 227.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $d_o = 225$ mm (standard).

**Step 3:** Check shear stress in the eye tangential section:

$$
\tau = \frac{W}{2 \cdot t \cdot (d_o - d)} = \frac{22000}{2 \cdot 10 \cdot (225 - 32)} = \frac{22000}{3860} \approx 5.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Check permissible shear stress:

$$
\tau_{perm} = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{b,perm} = 0.5 \cdot 500 = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $5.7 \text{ MPa} \ll 250 \text{ MPa}$ → **SAFE in shear**. ✅

**Step 5:** Check tensile stress in the eye:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{W}{(d_o - d) \cdot t} = \frac{22000}{(225 - 32) \cdot 10} = \frac{22000}{1930} \approx 11.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6:** Check permissible tensile stress:

$$
\sigma_{t,perm} = 0.75 \cdot 500 = 375 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $11.4 \text{ MPa} \ll 375 \text{ MPa}$ → **SAFE in tension**. ✅

**Step 7:** Check bearing stress on the pin:

$$
\sigma_{br} = \frac{W}{d \cdot t} = \frac{22000}{32 \cdot 10} = 68.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 8:** Check permissible bearing stress (assuming pin material $\sigma_y = 600$ MPa):

$$
\sigma_{br,perm} = 0.8 \cdot 600 = 480 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $68.75 \text{ MPa} \ll 480 \text{ MPa}$ → **SAFE in bearing**. ✅

**Conclusion (7 marks distributed as):**
- Empirical selection of d and d_o → 2 marks
- Shear stress check → 2 marks
- Tensile stress check → 1 mark
- Bearing stress check → 1 mark
- Final conclusion → 1 mark

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Determine the length of the tapered transition from the eye cross-section to the main leaf cross-section. Also, calculate the deflection of the spring at maximum load, assuming the half-length of the spring is L = 600 mm.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Eye thickness (after upset forging) is typically $1.5 \cdot t$:

$$
t_e = 1.5 \cdot t = 1.5 \cdot 10 = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Difference in thickness:

$$
\Delta t = t_e - t = 15 - 10 = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply KTU taper ratio $\tan\theta \le 1/6$:

$$
l_t \ge 6 \cdot \Delta t = 6 \cdot 5 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $l_t = 35$ mm for additional safety.

**Step 4:** Deflection of the leaf spring at maximum load:

$$
\delta = \frac{6 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values (convert to N and mm):

$$
\delta = \frac{6 \cdot 22000 \cdot (600)^3}{8 \cdot (210 \times 10^3) \cdot 65 \cdot (10)^3}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute step by step:

- Numerator: $6 \cdot 22000 \cdot 216 \times 10^6 = 6 \cdot 22000 \cdot 216000000 = 2.8512 \times 10^{13}$
- Denominator: $8 \cdot 210000 \cdot 65 \cdot 1000 = 8 \cdot 210000 \cdot 65000 = 1.092 \times 10^{11}$

$$
\delta = \frac{2.8512 \times 10^{13}}{1.092 \times 10^{11}} \approx 261.1 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7:** Adopt initial camber / nip = 15% of max deflection:

$$
\delta_{nip} = 0.15 \cdot 261.1 \approx 39.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion (7 marks):**
- Taper length calculation → 3 marks
- Deflection formula and substitution → 3 marks
- Nip calculation → 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With neat sketches, explain any FOUR types of end construction used in leaf springs. State one advantage of each type.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Full-Length Master Leaf (Plain End):**
   - The master leaf is full-length and is reinforced with an additional full-length leaf.
   - *Advantage:* Simple manufacturing, high load capacity.
   - *Disadvantage:* Heavy and prone to crack initiation at width-change section.

2. **Short Master Leaf with Forged Eye:**
   - The master leaf is shorter; ends are upset (forged) to form circular eyes.
   - *Advantage:* Best balance between strength, weight, and fatigue life — most widely used.
   - *Disadvantage:* Forging process increases cost.

3. **Hooked End:**
   - The end of the master leaf is rolled into a J-shape hook engaging with an auxiliary spring.
   - *Advantage:* Provides progressive load transfer in trailer and railway applications.
   - *Disadvantage:* High stress concentration at the bend radius.

4. **Extended End:**
   - The master leaf is extended beyond the eye to slide on the axle seat.
   - *Advantage:* Distributes load over a larger contact area under overload conditions.
   - *Disadvantage:* Requires precise sliding surface finish and lubrication.

(7 marks: 1.5 marks per type + 0.5 mark for each advantage)

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A leaf spring for a heavy truck carries a maximum load of $W = 50{,}000$ N. The spring has $n = 10$ leaves, each of width $b = 90$ mm and thickness $t = 14$ mm. The eye outer diameter is $d_o = 280$ mm and the pin diameter is $d = 45$ mm. Verify the design for shear, tension, and bearing stresses. Take $\sigma_{b,perm} = 500$ MPa and pin yield strength $\sigma_y = 600$ MPa.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Shear stress in the eye:**

$$
\tau = \frac{W}{2 \cdot t \cdot (d_o - d)} = \frac{50000}{2 \cdot 14 \cdot (280 - 45)} = \frac{50000}{2 \cdot 14 \cdot 235} = \frac{50000}{6580} \approx 7.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Permissible shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{perm} = 0.5 \cdot 500 = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $7.6 \text{ MPa} \ll 250 \text{ MPa}$ → **SAFE**. ✅

**Step 3 — Tensile stress across the eye:**

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{W}{(d_o - d) \cdot t} = \frac{50000}{(280 - 45) \cdot 14} = \frac{50000}{3290} \approx 15.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Permissible tensile stress:**

$$
\sigma_{t,perm} = 0.75 \cdot 500 = 375 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $15.2 \text{ MPa} \ll 375 \text{ MPa}$ → **SAFE**. ✅

**Step 5 — Bearing stress on pin:**

$$
\sigma_{br} = \frac{W}{d \cdot t} = \frac{50000}{45 \cdot 14} = \frac{50000}{630} \approx 79.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Permissible bearing stress:**

$$
\sigma_{br,perm} = 0.8 \cdot 600 = 480 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $79.4 \text{ MPa} \ll 480 \text{ MPa}$ → **SAFE**. ✅

**Conclusion:** The eye design is safe under all three stress modes.

(7 marks: 2 marks per stress check + 1 mark for final conclusion)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for End Construction Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in shear stress:** Students often write $ \tau = W / t(d_o - d) $ instead of $W / 2t(d_o - d)$. The factor 2 is for **double shear** at the two tangential sections of the eye — this is the most common mistake and costs 2–3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing "tangential" and "radial" sections of the eye:** Tangential section → shear; Radial section → tension. Mixing these up leads to wrong formulas.
> 
> 3. **Not stating the empirical relations for $d$ and $d_o$:** When the problem does not provide $d$ and $d_o$, you MUST use $d \approx 0.5w$ and $d_o \approx 3w$ to $4w$. Skipping this step loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Not mentioning the taper ratio:** If the question asks for "smooth stress flow at the eye," the standard KTU answer is $\tan\theta \le 1/6$.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring nip/pre-load:** A spring without camber (nip) will have high stress at static load. Always include $\delta_{nip} = 0.10$ to $0.15 \cdot \delta_{max}$ in your answer.
> 
> 6. **Wrong units in stress calculation:** Ensure $W$ is in N, $t$, $d$, $d_o$ are in mm. Result will then be in MPa directly (since N/mm² = MPa).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Construction — Key Points (One-Page Summary)**

- **Definition:** End construction = the geometry at the extremity of the master leaf of a laminated leaf spring.

- **Six Standard Types (KTU):**
   1. Full-length master leaf (plain)
   2. Full-length master leaf + reinforcement plate
   3. Short master leaf with **forged upset eye** ← **most common**
   4. Semi-elliptical end (tapered in width)
   5. Hooked end (railway/trailer)
   6. Extended end (heavy truck)

- **Three Critical Stresses in the Eye:**
   - **Shear** $ \tau = W / [2t(d_o - d)] $
   - **Tension** $ \sigma_t = W / [(d_o - d) t] $
   - **Bearing** $ \sigma_{br} = W / (d t) $

- **Empirical Sizing Rules:**
   - Pin diameter $d \approx 0.5 \cdot w$
   - Eye OD $d_o \approx 3w$ to $4w$

- **Taper Rule:** $\tan\theta \le 1/6$ → $l_t \ge 6(t_e - t)$

- **Nip / Camber:** $\delta_{nip} = (0.10 \text{ to } 0.15) \cdot \delta_{max}$

- **Permissible Stresses (Spring Steel 55Si7 / SUP 9):**
   - Bending: 450–550 MPa
   - Shear (eye): 0.5 × bending ≈ 225–275 MPa
   - Tension (eye): 0.75 × bending ≈ 340–410 MPa
   - Bearing (pin): 0.8 × pin yield strength

- **Helper Leaf:** Used for progressive load support in HCVs; engages only under heavy load.

- **Most Frequently Asked KTU Questions:**
   1. Classify end construction types (3–4 marks)
   2. Derive shear/tension/bearing stress in the eye (7 marks)
   3. Design the eye given $W$, $b$, $t$, $\sigma_b$ (7–14 marks)
   4. Explain helper leaf arrangement (3 marks)

- **Mnemonic — "STEBS":**
   - **S**hear in eye (2 tangential sections)
   - **T**ension in eye (1 radial section)
   - **E**ye-bolt bearing
   - **B**ending in main leaf
   - **S**tress flow taper (1:6)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leaf Springs - Flat Springs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Leaf Springs - Flat Springs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Leaf Spring** is a simple form of spring made from flat, rectangular cross-section plates (leaves) of spring steel, stacked together and clamped at the center (or ends) to support the chassis of an automobile. The flat leaf spring is a classic example of a **mechanical energy storage device** that absorbs shock loads and stores strain energy through **bending deformation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBAUT604 - Module 2):**
> A leaf spring is a long, flat, curved or tapered piece of steel of rectangular cross-section that deflects under the action of a transverse load, primarily sustaining **bending stress** while transmitting the load between the axle and the chassis frame of a vehicle.

The term **"Flat Spring"** specifically refers to springs manufactured from flat stock material (as opposed to helical/coiled springs made from round wire or rod). Leaf springs belong to the **flexure-type** or **bending-type** spring family and are classified as **single-leaf** (mono-leaf) or **multi-leaf** (laminated) springs.

### 1.2 Types of Leaf Spring Mounting Configurations

| Configuration | Description | Common Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Full-elliptic (Full-length)** | Two eyes at both ends, both ends pivoted to the frame | Heavy commercial vehicles, railway wagons |
| **Half-elliptic (Semi-elliptic)** | Eye at one end, shackle joint at the other end | **Most common** – Light/medium commercial vehicles, cars |
| **Three-quarter elliptic** | Eye at one end, half spring plus a quarter auxiliary | Older heavy trucks |
| **Quarter-elliptic** | One end fixed, other end pivoted – acts as a cantilever | Front suspension of older cars |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Diving Board**
> Imagine a diver standing at the end of a long, flexible wooden diving board. The board bends downward under the diver's weight and returns to its original position when the diver jumps off. The diving board **stores** the diver's energy as it bends and **releases** it as the diver springs up.
> 
> Now imagine the board is not one piece but made of **several thinner boards stacked and bolted together** (like a thick sandwich). The combination makes the board **stiffer** (carries more load) but still bends elastically. This stacked-board arrangement is exactly how a **multi-leaf spring** works in your car's suspension — every time the wheel hits a pothole, the spring flexes, absorbs the shock, and pushes the wheel back down smoothly.

### 1.4 Constructional Anatomy of a Laminated Leaf Spring

A standard **multi-leaf spring** consists of the following components:

1. **Master Leaf (Main Leaf)**: The longest leaf, with both ends curved into eyes. It is the uppermost leaf in a standard assembly.
2. **Graduated Leaves**: Progressively shorter leaves stacked beneath the master leaf. Their cross-section is usually **tapered** (reduced in thickness or width) toward the ends.
3. **Eye (End Cylinder)**: A circular hole at the end of the master leaf through which a pivot pin is inserted. A **steel bushing** (bronze or rubber) is pressed in to prevent wear.
4. **Shackle**: A swinging link that connects the rear eye of the spring to the frame; it permits length change of the spring during deflection.
5. **Centre Bolt (Rebound Clip)**: A U-bolt or central bolt that clamps all leaves together at the middle and also attaches the spring to the axle seat.
6. **U-Bolts and Clips (Rebound Clips)**: Hold the leaves in alignment and prevent lateral shifting.

### 1.5 Terminology of Vital Importance

- **Length of Spring (L)** = *Effective length*, i.e., the distance between the centre of the eye (or pivot point) and the centre bolt. Also called the **working span** of the spring.
- **Width (b)** of the leaf = constant across most leaves for simplicity.
- **Thickness (t)** of the leaf = constant for full-length leaves; varies (tapered) in modern designs.
- **Camber** = Initial curvature given to the spring so that the load-deflection curve starts at zero and the master leaf straightens under full load.
- **Pitch (p)** = The distance between the centres of the bolts clamping the leaves; typically **2 × width of leaf** for the centre clip.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Note:** The length **L** used in formulas is the *effective* (half) length of a semi-elliptic spring — i.e., distance from the eye centre to the centre-bolt centre. Always use this definition in numerical problems to avoid losing marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions Used in the Classical Leaf Spring Analysis

The standard KTU 2024 design analysis of a laminated flat leaf spring is based on the following assumptions:

1. The leaves are **initially stressed** due to the **nipping** process (the centre bolt pulls them tightly together), so the initial radius of curvature differs from leaf to leaf.
2. The **frictional forces** between leaves are **neglected** — this is a simplifying assumption that gives a slightly optimistic (lower) value of deflection.
3. The leaves are **freely supported** (simply supported), i.e., treated as **simply supported beams** of span *L* with a central point load.
4. The **tensile and compressive stresses** in the leaves are uniform across the width *b*.
5. The **elastic modulus E** is constant for all leaves.
6. The stress in any leaf is **maximum at the centre** (where bending moment is maximum) and is given by the simple bending formula.

### 2.2 Theory of Flat Leaf Spring (Detailed Working Logic)

**Step 1 — Load Distribution Among Leaves**
At the centre of the spring, the load is shared by *n* leaves. By symmetry, if all leaves were identical, each would carry an equal share of the central load:

$$W_{\text{per leaf}} = \frac{W}{n}$$

> But the master leaf (top) and the bottom leaf carry **half** of this share because they are not clamped on both faces. In KTU theory, we still treat the *n* leaves as carrying equal shares, leading to the standard formulas.

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment**
For a simply supported beam of span *L* with a central point load *W*:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Stress**
For a rectangular cross-section (width *b*, thickness *t*), the section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6}$$

Therefore, the **maximum bending stress** in the leaves is:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{W L / 4}{b t^{2} / 6} = \frac{6 W L}{4 \, b \, t^{2}} = \frac{3 W L}{2 \, b \, t^{2}}$$

When *n* leaves share the load *W*, the load per leaf becomes *W/n*, giving:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 \, W \, L}{2 \, n \, b \, t^{2}} = \frac{6 W L}{4 n b t^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Deflection of a Cantilever-Treated Leaf**
For a beam of length *L* (treated as cantilever from end to centre) loaded by *W/2* at the free end (or equivalently, *W/n* over *n* leaves), the maximum deflection is:

$$\delta = \frac{W L^{3}}{3 E I} \quad \text{(Cantilever form)}$$

For *n* leaves in parallel, each carrying *W/n*:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{6 \, W \, L^{3}}{n \, E \, b \, t^{3}}}$$

> The factor 6 appears because the standard cantilever formula *WL³/(3EI)* is rewritten using $I = bt^{3}/12$, giving $12/(bt^{3}) \times 1/3 = 4/(bt^{3})$. Multiplied by the factor 2 (to convert cantilever to simply-supported behaviour) and divided by *n*, the coefficient becomes **6**.

**Step 5 — Stiffness of the Spring**
The spring stiffness (force per unit deflection) is:

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{n \, E \, b \, t^{3}}{6 \, L^{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Strain Energy Stored**
The strain energy stored in the spring is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} W \delta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W^{2}}{k} = \frac{3 W^{2} L^{3}}{n \, E \, b \, t^{3}}$$

**Step 7 — Number of Leaves Required**
Rearranging the stress equation to find the required number of leaves:

$$\boxed{n = \frac{6 W L}{2 \, \sigma_{\text{per}} \, b \, t^{2}} = \frac{3 W L}{2 \, \sigma_{\text{per}} \, b \, t^{2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In most KTU textbooks and solutions, the *master leaf* is counted as the **first leaf**. The bottom-most (shortest) leaf is the **n-th leaf**. The total number of leaves (including master leaf) is denoted by *n* in the formula $\sigma = 3WL/(2nbt^{2})$.

### 2.3 The "Nipping" Process (Initial Stressing)

Because the leaves have different natural radii of curvature, the longer leaves are **stretched** and the shorter leaves are **compressed** when the centre bolt is tightened. This gives every leaf an **initial pre-stress** of opposite sign to its working stress.

**Why is nipping done?**
- Equalises the working stress in all leaves.
- Prevents the leaves from separating under the rebound load (impact).
- Ensures that all leaves deflect together as a single unit.

The required nip *C* is given by:

$$C = \frac{L^{2}}{2} \left( \frac{1}{R_{1}} - \frac{1}{R_{n}} \right)$$

where $R_{1}$ and $R_{n}$ are the radii of curvature of the **first** and **n-th** leaves respectively.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **all critical formulas** for KTU 2024 Module 2 examination problems on flat leaf springs. Units in **SI** (N, mm, MPa = N/mm², N·mm).

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max Bending Moment | $M_{\max} = \dfrac{W L}{4}$ | N·mm |
| 2 | Max Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{3 W L}{2 n b t^{2}}$ | MPa (N/mm²) |
| 3 | Max Deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{6 W L^{3}}{n E b t^{3}}$ | mm |
| 4 | Spring Stiffness | $k = \dfrac{n E b t^{3}}{6 L^{3}}$ | N/mm |
| 5 | Strain Energy | $U = \dfrac{3 W^{2} L^{3}}{n E b t^{3}}$ | N·mm (= mJ) |
| 6 | Number of Leaves | $n = \dfrac{3 W L}{2 \sigma_{b} b t^{2}}$ | dimensionless, round up to integer |
| 7 | Camber | $h = \dfrac{3}{2} \, \delta$ | mm |
| 8 | Section Modulus (Rectangle) | $Z = \dfrac{b t^{2}}{6}$ | mm³ |
| 9 | Moment of Inertia (Rectangle) | $I = \dfrac{b t^{3}}{12}$ | mm⁴ |
| 10 | Initial Nip Stress (Master Leaf) | $\sigma_{i} = \dfrac{E \cdot t \cdot C}{L^{2}}$ | MPa |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Suspension**: Primary shock-absorbing element between axle and chassis.
- **Railway Bogies**: Used as the main suspension spring in goods wagons.
- **Agricultural Tractors**: Acts as a combined spring-and-leveling device.
- **Heavy Trailers and Trucks**: Full-elliptic configurations are used in 40-tonne capacity trailers.
- **Industrial Presses and Buffers**: Where linear energy absorption under shock load is required.

The leaf spring also serves a **dual purpose** in automotive applications: (i) spring action, and (ii) **location/guidance** of the axle — replacing the need for a separate suspension link.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Bending Stress in a Laminated Flat Leaf Spring

**Given:** A simply supported semi-elliptic leaf spring of effective length *L*, width *b*, leaf thickness *t*, with *n* leaves, subjected to a central point load *W* at the centre bolt.

**Step 1 — Free Body Analysis**

By symmetry of the simply supported beam, each support carries half the load:
- Reaction at each end = $W/2$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment**

The bending moment at the centre (where the load is applied) is:

$$M_{\max} = R \times \frac{L}{2} = \frac{W}{2} \times \frac{L}{2} = \frac{W L}{4}$$

This is the maximum moment because the moment diagram is triangular and peaks at the centre.

**Step 3 — Section Modulus of a Rectangular Leaf**

For a leaf of width *b* and thickness *t*, the second moment of area about the neutral axis is:

$$I = \frac{b \, t^{3}}{12}$$

The section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{b t^{3}/12}{t/2} = \frac{b t^{2}}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Bending Stress in a Single Leaf**

By the simple flexure formula, the maximum bending stress in **one** leaf at the centre is:

$$\sigma_{1} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{W L / 4}{b t^{2} / 6} = \frac{6 W L}{4 b t^{2}} = \frac{3 W L}{2 b t^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Load Sharing Among *n* Leaves**

When *n* identical leaves are stacked and clamped, the total load *W* is shared equally (assuming uniform thickness) among all leaves. Therefore, the load resisted by each leaf is $W/n$. The bending moment at the centre for **each** leaf becomes:

$$M_{\text{per leaf}} = \frac{(W/n) \cdot L}{4} = \frac{W L}{4 n}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Bending Stress in a Laminated Spring**

Substituting back into the flexure formula:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\text{per leaf}}}{Z} = \frac{W L / (4 n)}{b t^{2} / 6} = \frac{6 W L}{4 n b t^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 \, W \, L}{2 \, n \, b \, t^{2}}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Stress is **inversely proportional** to the number of leaves *n* → more leaves = lower stress.
- Stress is **inversely proportional to the square of thickness** → doubling the thickness reduces stress to one-quarter.
- Stress is **linearly proportional to span L** → longer springs experience higher stress.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Deflection of a Laminated Flat Leaf Spring

**Given:** Same spring as above, with Young's modulus *E*.

**Step 1 — Deflection of a Single Cantilever Leaf**

Consider one leaf treated as a **cantilever** of length *L*, fixed at the eye and free at the centre. The half-load $W/2$ acts at the free end.

The maximum deflection of a cantilever of length *L* with end load *P* is:

$$\delta_{\text{cantilever}} = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 E I}$$

**Step 2 — Conversion from Cantilever to Simply-Supported**

A simply supported beam of length *L* with central load *W* is geometrically equivalent to **two cantilevers** of length *L/2* each carrying load *W/2* at the free end. Therefore:

$$\delta_{\text{SSB}} = 2 \times \frac{(W/2) (L/2)^{3}}{3 E I} = 2 \times \frac{W L^{3}}{48 E I} = \frac{W L^{3}}{24 E I}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $I = bt^{3}/12$**

$$\delta_{\text{SSB}} = \frac{W L^{3}}{24 E \cdot b t^{3}/12} = \frac{12 W L^{3}}{24 E b t^{3}} = \frac{W L^{3}}{2 E b t^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Deflection with *n* Leaves**

With *n* leaves in parallel, each carries $W/n$, so the deflection per leaf is *1/n* of the single-leaf deflection:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n} \times \frac{W L^{3}}{2 E b t^{3}} = \frac{W L^{3}}{2 n E b t^{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiplying by 3 to Account for the Real Semi-Elliptic Geometry**

In a real semi-elliptic spring, the moment distribution and clamping conditions differ from the simple SSB case. The empirical factor used in KTU design codes is **3**, giving:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{6 \, W \, L^{3}}{n \, E \, b \, t^{3}}}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for maximum deflection of a semi-elliptic laminated leaf spring.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Camber of the Leaf Spring

**Why camber is needed:**
The camber is the initial vertical "rise" given to the spring so that under maximum load, the master leaf becomes almost straight. This ensures the load-deflection curve passes through the origin (i.e., no residual deflection when fully loaded).

**Geometric relation between camber and deflection:**

For a leaf bent into a circular arc of radius *R* and subtending a small angle $\theta$ at the centre, the rise (sagitta) is:

$$h = R - R \cos\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \approx \frac{R \theta^{2}}{8} \quad \text{(for small $\theta$)}$$

The arc length equals $L$:

$$L = R \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{L}{R}$$

For a small angle, the maximum strain at the outer fibre is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{t/2}{R} = \frac{t \theta}{2 L}$$

The corresponding maximum stress is:

$$\sigma = E \varepsilon = \frac{E t \theta}{2 L}$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \frac{2 \sigma L}{E t}$$

Substituting into the camber equation:

$$h = \frac{R \theta^{2}}{8} = \frac{L \theta^{2}}{8} = \frac{L}{8} \left( \frac{2 \sigma L}{E t} \right)^{2} = \frac{L \cdot 4 \sigma^{2} L^{2}}{8 E^{2} t^{2}} = \frac{\sigma^{2} L^{3}}{2 E^{2} t^{2}}$$

Substituting the bending stress $\sigma = 3WL/(2nbt^{2})$:

$$h = \frac{1}{2 E^{2} t^{2}} \cdot \left( \frac{3 W L}{2 n b t^{2}} \right)^{2} \cdot L^{3}$$

After simplification:

$$h = \frac{9 W^{2} L^{5}}{8 n^{2} b^{2} t^{6} E^{2}}$$

Dividing this by the deflection formula $\delta = 6WL^{3}/(nEbt^{3})$ gives the classic ratio:

$$\boxed{\frac{h}{\delta} = \frac{3}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac{3}{2} \delta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** This $h = (3/2)\delta$ relation is **universally tested** in the KTU ESE and is considered a high-priority result. Always quote it as a stand-alone point in the theory section.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Design Parameter** | **Proportionality to Load (W)** | **Proportionality to Length (L)** | **Proportionality to Thickness (t)** | **Proportionality to No. of Leaves (n)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bending Stress $\sigma$ | $W^{1}$ | $L^{1}$ | $t^{-2}$ | $n^{-1}$ |
| Deflection $\delta$ | $W^{1}$ | $L^{3}$ | $t^{-3}$ | $n^{-1}$ |
| Spring Stiffness $k$ | $W^{0}$ | $L^{-3}$ | $t^{3}$ | $n^{1}$ |
| Camber $h$ | $W^{2}$ | $L^{5}$ | $t^{-6}$ | $n^{-2}$ |
| Strain Energy $U$ | $W^{2}$ | $L^{3}$ | $t^{-3}$ | $n^{-1}$ |

> **Engineering Takeaway:** From this matrix, observe that **thickness *t*** has the most dramatic effect on every parameter. Doubling *t* reduces stress by 4×, deflection by 8×, and camber by 64×. This is why modern leaf springs use **parabolic (tapered) leaves** to achieve uniform stress while keeping camber low.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A laminated semi-elliptic leaf spring has 10 leaves, each of width 60 mm and thickness 8 mm. The effective length of the spring is 1000 mm. The central load is 25 kN. Find:
1. The maximum bending stress
2. The maximum deflection
3. The required number of leaves if the allowable stress is 400 MPa

**Given:**
- $n = 10$, $b = 60$ mm, $t = 8$ mm, $L = 1000$ mm, $W = 25{,}000$ N
- $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² (for spring steel)

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Maximum bending stress

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 W L}{2 n b t^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 25{,}000 \times 1000}{2 \times 10 \times 60 \times 8^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{75{,}000{,}000}{76{,}800} = 976.56 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b):** Maximum deflection

$$\delta = \frac{6 W L^{3}}{n E b t^{3}} = \frac{6 \times 25{,}000 \times 1000^{3}}{10 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 60 \times 8^{3}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{14}}{6.144 \times 10^{11}} = 244.14 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (c):** Number of leaves for allowable stress = 400 MPa

$$n = \frac{3 W L}{2 \sigma_{b} b t^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 25{,}000 \times 1000}{2 \times 400 \times 60 \times 64} = \frac{75{,}000{,}000}{3{,}072{,}000} = 24.41$$

Round up → **n = 25 leaves**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Leaf Spring Assembly

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **inter-relationship of components** in a laminated flat leaf spring, mapping the **load path** from the road surface to the vehicle chassis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Road Surface Excitation] --> B[Wheel and Tyre Assembly]
    B --> C[Axle Housing]
    C --> D[Centre Bolt Clamp U-Bolts]
    D --> E[Master Leaf Top of Stack]
    E --> F[Graduated Leaf 2]
    F --> G[Graduated Leaf 3]
    G --> H[Graduated Leaf n-1]
    H --> I[Shortest Leaf Bottom of Stack]
    E --> J[Front Eye Pivot Pin]
    I --> K[Shackle Joint Rear]
    J --> L[Chassis Frame Front Mount]
    K --> M[Chassis Frame Rear Mount]
    L --> N[Vehicle Body Chassis]
    M --> N
    D --> N
    N --> O[Vibration Dampened Smooth Ride]
```

### 4.2 Force Flow Schematic of a Semi-Elliptic Leaf Spring

This diagram explains the **internal load transfer mechanism** — how the load is distributed from the central bolt to the two pivot eyes through bending of the leaves.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Load W Applied at Centre Bolt] --> R1[Reaction W/2 at Front Eye]
    P1 --> R2[Reaction W/2 at Rear Shackle]
    R1 --> BM1[Bending Moment WL/4 max at Centre]
    R2 --> BM1
    BM1 --> SIG1[Bending Stress sigma = 3WL / 2nbt squared]
    BM1 --> DEF1[Deflection delta = 6WL cubed / nEbt cubed]
    SIG1 --> OUT1[Material must be within Elastic Limit]
    DEF1 --> OUT2[Camber = 1.5 times delta]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow for Design of a Laminated Leaf Spring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Load W from vehicle data] --> S2[Step 2 Assume leaf thickness t and width b]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Assume allowable stress sigma permissible]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Compute number of leaves n]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Verify bending stress is within limit]
    S5 --> S6{Stress OK?}
    S6 -- No --> S2
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Step 6 Compute deflection delta]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7 Compute camber h = 1.5 delta]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8 Compute strain energy U]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9 Standardize n to next integer and Finalize Design]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Nipping Process (Initial Pre-Stress)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Free State Different Radii of Curvature] --> N2[Clamping with Centre Bolt]
    N2 --> N3[Longer Leaves Stretched Tension Pre-Stress]
    N2 --> N4[Shorter Leaves Compressed Compression Pre-Stress]
    N3 --> N5[All Leaves Have Same Radius Under Load]
    N4 --> N5
    N5 --> N6[Equal Stress Distribution Working Condition]
    N6 --> OUT[Nipping Achieved Stress Equalisation]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Reading Tip:** In the first diagram (Section 4.1), the **vertical cascade** (A → B → C → D → E → F → …) shows the *load path*; the **bottom-up arrangement** (K → M, J → L) shows how the spring's two ends anchor to the chassis. This is the **typical KTU 14-mark question** for "Explain with sketch the construction of a leaf spring."
