topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Suggestion - Problem Project: Students will have the autonomy to explore and design bearing and spring systems based on specified performance criteria and real-world applications. The project will integrate concepts from rolling contact bearings, sliding contact bearings, and springs, focusing on design considerations, material selection, and performance analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Design Project on Bearing and Spring Systems** is an integrative, open-ended capstone-style problem statement within Module 2 of PBAUT604 that mandates the student to perform a **complete design cycle** — from problem formulation, conceptual synthesis, analytical sizing, material selection, performance verification, to a final design recommendation — of an automotive (or automotive-adjacent) mechanical subsystem in which **rolling contact bearings**, **sliding contact bearings**, and **mechanical springs** must be co-designed to satisfy simultaneously stated performance criteria.

In the language of the **2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework of KTU, this project is mapped to the higher cognitive levels of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**, specifically **Analyse (L4)**, **Evaluate (L5)**, and **Create (L6)**, because the student is expected not merely to recall formulae but to **synthesise** a working design from a loose performance brief.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Project Scope (Verbatim Theme):** *"Students will have the autonomy to explore and design bearing and spring systems based on specified performance criteria and real-world applications. The project will integrate concepts from rolling contact bearings, sliding contact bearings, and springs, focusing on design considerations, material selection, and performance analysis."*

The three design pillars that the project expects the student to demonstrate competence in are:

1. **Design Considerations** — Load capacity, speed, fatigue life, deflections, buckling, misalignment tolerance, lubrication strategy, mounting constraints, NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness), thermal behaviour, manufacturability, cost, weight, reliability, and safety factor.
2. **Material Selection** — Use of standard material indices (e.g., the **Ashby method**, **CES EduPack**-type screening, or traditional experience-based selection) for bearing races/balls, journal/bush, and spring wires.
3. **Performance Analysis** — Application of the standard equations of rolling bearing life (ISO 281 / L10), PV-limit for sliding bearings, and helical / leaf spring stress-deflection analysis to *verify* that the design meets the specification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine that you have been asked by a small electric-vehicle start-up in Kochi to design the **rear-axle assembly** of a three-wheeler. The axle must:

- **Spin** smoothly at, say, 1500 rpm while carrying a radial load of 6 kN (this is your **rolling/sliding bearing** job).
- **Cushion** road bumps so that the chassis sees a maximum acceleration of 4 g (this is your **spring** job).
- **Last** for at least 1.5 lakh km in Kerala's humid, coastal-corrosive climate (this is your **material + lubrication** job).

Just as a chef does not cook the rice, the dal, and the sabzi in three separate rooms and then expects them to combine into a meal, you cannot design the bearing in Chapter 1, the spring in Chapter 2, and the housing in Chapter 3 in isolation. The **chef tastes as he cooks**; the designer must **iterate** between bearing stiffness, spring stiffness, and shaft stiffness until the system response (natural frequency, deflection, life) meets the brief.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for the Student:** The "problem project" is essentially a **mini-thesis**. The marking scheme rewards (a) clarity of assumptions, (b) correctness of governing equations used, (c) a logical design table, and (d) a justified final material-and-size selection — *not* the prettiest CAD render.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics Used in the Project

The following are the **canonical performance metrics** a student must compute for any integrated bearing-spring design problem under the KTU 2024 scheme. Every metric has a standard symbol, a standard SI unit, and a standard governing equation (covered in Section 2).

- **Dynamic Load Rating ($C$)** of rolling bearing, in **N**.
- **Basic Rating Life ($L_{10}$ or $L_{10h}$)**, in **million revolutions** or **hours**.
- **Static Load Rating ($C_0$)**, in **N**.
- **Equivalent Dynamic Bearing Load ($P$)**, in **N**.
- **Bearing Bore Diameter ($d$) and Outer Diameter ($D$)**, in **mm**.
- **PV Product** (Pressure × Sliding Velocity) for journal bearings, in **MPa · m/s**.
- **Mean Coil Diameter ($D_m$) and Wire Diameter ($d_w$)** of helical spring, in **mm**.
- **Spring Index ($C_s$ or $K$)** — dimensionless, normally 4 to 12.
- **Spring Rate ($k$)**, in **N/mm**.
- **Maximum Shear Stress in Spring ($\tau_{max}$)**, in **MPa**.
- **Deflection ($\delta$) of Spring**, in **mm**.
- **Factor of Safety (FoS)** — dimensionless.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners explicitly look for the *interaction* between these metrics. For example, a stiffer spring (higher $k$) may shift the system's natural frequency, which in turn may force you to re-select the bearing's internal clearance class. Always close this feedback loop in your design report.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Design Logic (Structured)

The integrated design project, although free-form, can always be decomposed into **three nested design loops**. The student is advised to structure the report along these three loops to score full marks.

### Loop A — Requirements & Constraint Definition
- **Step 1:** Translate the problem statement into a numerical brief — radial load $F_r$, axial load $F_a$, rotational speed $N$, life target $L_{10h}^{req}$, maximum deflection $\delta_{max}$, operating temperature $T$, environment (corrosive / dusty / clean).
- **Step 2:** Identify the **design variables** that are *free* (e.g., bearing bore $d$, spring wire $d_w$) and the variables that are *fixed* (e.g., shaft diameter imposed by the automotive chassis).
- **Step 3:** Identify **interaction variables** that couple the bearing design to the spring design — most commonly **shaft stiffness**, **shaft deflection at the bearing seat**, and **system natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_{eq}/m_{eq}}$.

### Loop B — Component Sizing
- **Step 4:** Select the **bearing type** (deep-groove ball, cylindrical roller, tapered roller, spherical roller, angular contact) based on the load-speed-lubrication triad.
- **Step 5:** Compute the **equivalent dynamic load** $P$ using the standard ISO formula $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ for radial + axial combined loading.
- **Step 6:** Compute the **required dynamic load rating** $C_{req}$ from the target life using $C_{req} = P \left( L_{10}^{req} \right)^{1/p}$, where $p = 3$ for ball bearings and $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings.
- **Step 7:** Choose the **sliding (plain) bearing** material and compute the **PV product** to ensure it is within the manufacturer's limit.
- **Step 8:** Compute the **spring wire diameter** $d_w$ from the Wahl-corrected stress equation, and the **spring index** $C_s = D_m / d_w$.
- **Step 9:** Compute the **spring rate** $k = G d_w^4 / (8 D_m^3 N_a)$ and check whether the deflection $\delta = F/k$ lies within $\delta_{max}$.
- **Step 10:** Verify **buckling** for compression springs and **clash allowance** for extension springs.

### Loop C — Verification, Material Selection & Iteration
- **Step 11:** Compare computed life to the required life; if $L_{10}^{comp} < L_{10}^{req}$, **iterate** by increasing $C$ (select next-larger bore) or by improving lubrication (lower $a_{ISO}$).
- **Step 12:** Apply the **Ashby material index** $\rho^{-1} \cdot E^{-1}$ (or stiffness-specific strength indices for springs) to shortlist 2–3 candidate materials and justify the final choice.
- **Step 13:** Run a **deflection check** on the shaft under the combined bearing reaction and spring reaction to ensure shaft slope at the bearing seat is within the manufacturer's tilt limit (typically $< 2$ to $10$ arc-minutes).
- **Step 14:** Compute **Factor of Safety (FoS)** on both bearing static load and spring maximum stress, ensuring FoS $\geq 1.5$ for static and FoS $\geq 1.0$ (with $a_{ISO} < 1$) for fatigue.
- **Step 15:** Document the design in a **standard design table** and provide a 2D annotated sketch.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols follow **ISO 15 / ISO 281** for bearings and **Shigley's / Design Data Book** conventions for springs. The vertical-bar symbol is written as `\vert` to keep this table parseable.

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Boundary |
|---|----------|--------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Equivalent dynamic bearing load | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | $F_r$ radial [N], $F_a$ axial [N], $X,Y$ factors [-] | When $F_a / F_0 \le e$ use $X=1, Y=0$ |
| 2 | Static equivalent load | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | $X_0, Y_0$ from catalogue | $P_0 \le C_0$ required |
| 3 | Basic rating life [rev] | $L_{10} = \left( C / P \right)^{p}$ | $C$ dynamic rating [N], $p = 3$ (ball), $10/3$ (roller) | Million-revolution basis |
| 4 | Life in hours | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \, N} \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{p}$ | $N$ [rpm] | Convert $L_{10}$ to hours |
| 5 | Required dynamic rating | $C_{req} = P \left( L_{10}^{req} \right)^{1/p}$ | $L_{10}^{req}$ in million rev | Used to size bearing |
| 6 | Modified life (ISO 281) | $L_{nm} = a_1 a_{ISO} L_{10}$ | $a_1$ reliability, $a_{ISO}$ lubrication | Modern formulation |
| 7 | Sliding bearing PV product | $PV = p \cdot v$ | $p = F/(L \cdot d)$ [MPa], $v = \pi d N / 60$ [m/s] | $PV \le PV_{lim}$ from mat. chart |
| 8 | Sommerfeld number | $S_o = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | $r$ radius, $c$ clearance, $\mu$ viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Helical spring index | $C_s = D_m / d_w$ | $D_m$ mean coil dia, $d_w$ wire dia | $4 \le C_s \le 12$ |
| 10 | Wahl correction factor | $K_w = \dfrac{4 C_s - 1}{4 C_s - 4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C_s}$ | — | For $C_s \ge 4$ |
| 11 | Max shear stress in helical spring | $\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot \dfrac{8 F D_m}{\pi d_w^{3}}$ | $F$ axial load [N] | $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{allow}$ |
| 12 | Deflection of helical spring | $\delta = \dfrac{8 F D_m^{3} N_a}{G d_w^{4}}$ | $G$ shear modulus [MPa], $N_a$ active coils | Active coils only |
| 13 | Spring rate | $k = \dfrac{G d_w^{4}}{8 D_m^{3} N_a}$ | — | $k > 0$ |
| 14 | Buckling check (compression) | $\ell_f / D_m \le$ catalogue value | $\ell_f$ free length | Avoid Euler buckling |
| 15 | Natural frequency (system) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_{eq}/m_{eq}}$ | $k_{eq}$ equiv. stiffness, $m_{eq}$ mass | $\omega_n$ must avoid resonance with $N$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the automotive industry, this exact three-loop workflow is what is performed by a **CAE (Computer-Aided Engineering) sub-system lead** in a Tier-1 supplier (e.g., Bosch, ZF, Schaeffler, SKF, Timken). The student is essentially being trained to perform the **first-pass analytical sizing** that is later refined using FEA (e.g., ANSYS Mechanical) and MBS (Multi-Body Simulation, e.g., ADAMS / CarSim). The performance metrics in the formula sheet are the **KPIs (Key Performance Indicators)** of every automotive drivetrain design review.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Dynamic Bearing Load $P$

The equivalent dynamic bearing load is **defined** as that constant, purely radial (for radial bearings) load which would give the same rating life as the actual combined radial + axial load. For most modern ISO 15 deep-groove and angular-contact ball bearings, the empirical catalogue relation is

$$
P = X F_r + Y F_a \quad \text{when} \quad F_a / F_r > e
$$

When the axial load is small enough that $F_a / F_r \le e$, the axial term drops out and we use

$$
P = F_r \quad \text{when} \quad F_a / F_r \le e
$$

**The "Why" behind the threshold $e$:** The $e$ value demarcates the load regime in which the contact ellipse of the ball with the inner race migrates from the bottom of the groove toward the shoulder, unlocking the axial-load-carrying capacity of the bearing. The values of $X$, $Y$, and $e$ are **empirically derived** from manufacturer endurance tests and tabulated in the bearing catalogue (SKF, NSK, NTN).

## 3.2 Derivation of Basic Rating Life Equation

Consider a single ball under a Hertzian contact stress $\sigma_0$. The maximum ortho-radial stress at the centre of the contact circle is given by

$$
\sigma_0 = \left( \frac{6 F_E}{\pi^{3} \Sigma \rho} \right)^{1/3}
$$

where $F_E$ is the equivalent load on the ball and $\Sigma \rho$ is the sum of curvatures. Lundberg and Palmgren (1947, 1952) showed that the **fatigue life** of a stressed volume element below the contact is given by a Weibull survival integral that ultimately collapses to the empirical power law

$$
L_{10} = \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{p}
$$

Let us expand this with the **limit-load normalisation** of the dynamic load rating $C$:

By definition, $C$ is the load at which a bearing achieves a rating life of exactly **1 million revolutions**:

$$
1 = \left( \frac{C}{C} \right)^{p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{10} = 1 \text{ million rev at } P = C
$$

For any other operating load $P$, equating the same survival probability gives the **inverse power law** in load:

$$
L_{10} = \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{p}
$$

Converting from million revolutions to **hours** using $L_{10h} = L_{10} \cdot 10^{6} / (60 N)$:

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \, N} \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{p}
$$

Rearranging to get the **required dynamic load rating** $C_{req}$ for a target life $L_{10h}^{req}$ (expressed in million revolutions as $L_{10}^{req}$):

$$
C_{req} = P \left( L_{10}^{req} \right)^{1/p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponent $p = 3$ for **ball bearings** and $p = 10/3$ for **roller bearings**. This is one of the most frequently mis-quoted constants in the KTU answer script — a single digit here can flip the bearing size class by one or two bore increments.

## 3.3 Derivation of Wahl Correction Factor for Helical Spring

For a close-coiled helical spring loaded by an axial force $F$, the **direct shear stress** (torsion in the wire, converted to a shear stress) is

$$
\tau_{direct} = \frac{8 F D_m}{\pi d_w^{3}}
$$

However, the wire is also bent because the load line is offset from the wire centroid by a radius $D_m/2$. This produces an **additional direct shear** of magnitude

$$
\tau_{bend} = \frac{4 F}{\pi d_w^{2}}
$$

The maximum shear occurs on the **inside of the coil** (fibre nearest to the load line) where both components are in the same direction. The total maximum shear stress is therefore

$$
\tau_{max} = \tau_{direct} + \tau_{bend} = \frac{8 F D_m}{\pi d_w^{3}} + \frac{4 F}{\pi d_w^{2}}
$$

Factoring out $8 F D_m / (\pi d_w^{3})$:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{8 F D_m}{\pi d_w^{3}} \left( 1 + \frac{d_w}{2 D_m} \right) = \frac{8 F D_m}{\pi d_w^{3}} \left( 1 + \frac{0.5}{C_s} \right)
$$

Empirically, A.M. Wahl refined this linear-elastic result by incorporating stress-concentration effects at the coil curvature and recommended a slightly larger factor:

$$
\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot \frac{8 F D_m}{\pi d_w^{3}}, \quad K_w = \frac{4 C_s - 1}{4 C_s - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C_s}
$$

For $C_s = 8$ (a typical mid-range index), $K_w \approx 1.184$, i.e. the direct-stress value is bumped up by **~18 %** for fatigue safety. KTU examiners love this number — write the $K_w$ substitution explicitly.

## 3.4 Derivation of Helical Spring Rate and Deflection

The strain energy stored in a close-coiled helical spring of $N_a$ active coils subjected to torque $T = F D_m / 2$ in the wire is

$$
U = \frac{T^{2} L_t}{2 G J_p}
$$

where $L_t = \pi D_m N_a$ is the active length of wire, $G$ is the shear modulus, and $J_p = \pi d_w^{4} / 32$ is the polar second moment of area. Substituting:

$$
U = \frac{(F D_m / 2)^{2} \cdot \pi D_m N_a}{2 G \cdot \pi d_w^{4} / 32}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
U = \frac{8 F^{2} D_m^{3} N_a}{G d_w^{4}}
$$

By **Castigliano's theorem**, the deflection is

$$
\delta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial F} = \frac{16 F D_m^{3} N_a}{G d_w^{4}}
$$

Hmm — let me re-check the factor. The classical Shigley result is

$$
\delta = \frac{8 F D_m^{3} N_a}{G d_w^{4}}
$$

This corresponds to a slightly different (and standard) derivation using $U = F^{2}/(2k)$ directly with the angular twist, not Castigliano on a free torque. Re-deriving cleanly:

The angular twist of the wire under torque $T = F D_m/2$ over its active length $L_t = \pi D_m N_a$ is

$$
\phi = \frac{T L_t}{G J_p} = \frac{(F D_m/2) \cdot \pi D_m N_a}{G \cdot \pi d_w^{4}/32} = \frac{16 F D_m^{2} N_a}{G d_w^{4}}
$$

Since the axial deflection is $\delta = \phi \cdot D_m / 2$:

$$
\delta = \frac{16 F D_m^{2} N_a}{G d_w^{4}} \cdot \frac{D_m}{2} = \frac{8 F D_m^{3} N_a}{G d_w^{4}}
$$

Good — this matches Shigley. Therefore the **spring rate** is

$$
k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{G d_w^{4}}{8 D_m^{3} N_a}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked example demonstrates how all three sub-systems (rolling bearing + sliding bearing + spring) are sized for a single **shaft–bracket assembly** of a small electric two-wheeler. The student is expected to attempt this *before* reading the solution to build confidence.

**Brief (translated into KTU standard):**

- Radial load on shaft: $F_r = 4.5$ kN
- Axial load on shaft: $F_a = 1.2$ kN
- Speed: $N = 1750$ rpm
- Required life: $L_{10h}^{req} = 20{,}000$ h
- Reliability: 90 % (so $a_1 = 1.0$)
- Operating temperature: 110 °C
- Suspension spring: must support static load $W = 1800$ N with deflection $\delta_{st} \le 90$ mm
- Spring material: chrome-vanadium oil-tempered wire, $G = 77$ GPa, $\tau_{allow} = 550$ MPa
- Allowed spring index: $5 \le C_s \le 10$

**Step 1 — Compute $L_{10}^{req}$ in million revolutions:**

$$
L_{10}^{req} = \frac{60 \cdot N \cdot L_{10h}^{req}}{10^{6}} = \frac{60 \cdot 1750 \cdot 20{,}000}{10^{6}} = 2100 \text{ million rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose bearing type and compute $P$:**

We tentatively choose a **single-row deep-groove ball bearing**. From the catalogue, with $F_a / F_r = 1.2 / 4.5 = 0.267$ and assuming $e \approx 0.22$, we have $F_a / F_r > e$, so we need the factors. For a 30-series deep-groove ball bearing at this load ratio, typical values are $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.85$. Then:

$$
P = X F_r + Y F_a = 0.56 \cdot 4500 + 1.85 \cdot 1200 = 2520 + 2220 = 4740 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{req}$:**

$$
C_{req} = P \left( L_{10}^{req} \right)^{1/3} = 4740 \cdot (2100)^{1/3} \approx 4740 \cdot 12.78 = 60{,}580 \text{ N} \approx 60.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Select catalogue bearing:**

From a standard 62-series table, a **6208** bearing has $C = 65.5$ kN, $C_0 = 38$ kN, bore $d = 40$ mm, OD $D = 80$ mm. Since $C_{actual} = 65.5$ kN $> C_{req} = 60.6$ kN, the bearing is adequate. The achieved life is

$$
L_{10h}^{comp} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot 1750} \left( \frac{65{,}500}{4740} \right)^{3} = 9.524 \cdot (13.82)^{3} = 9.524 \cdot 2638 = 25{,}128 \text{ h}
$$

This exceeds the 20,000 h requirement. **Design of bearing complete.**

**Step 5 — Sliding bearing (thrust washer at the spring seat):**

For a thrust-collar plain bearing, projected area $A = 50 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm} = 2500 \text{ mm}^{2}$. Sliding velocity $v = \pi d_m N / 60$ with $d_m = 60$ mm = 0.06 m:

$$
v = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.06 \cdot 1750}{60} = 5.50 \text{ m/s}
$$

Pressure: $p = W / A = 1800 / 2500 = 0.72$ MPa. PV product:

$$
PV = 0.72 \cdot 5.50 = 3.96 \text{ MPa} \cdot \text{m/s}
$$

For a PTFE-bronze backed thrust washer the catalogue PV limit is ~5 MPa·m/s at 1 m/s and degrades with $v^{0.5}$, giving ~2.1 MPa·m/s at 5.5 m/s. **PV exceeds the limit → choose sintered bronze with PV limit ~7 MPa·m/s at 5.5 m/s.** Acceptable.

**Step 6 — Spring sizing:**

Choose a target spring index $C_s = 7$ (mid-range). The Wahl factor:

$$
K_w = \frac{4 \cdot 7 - 1}{4 \cdot 7 - 4} + \frac{0.615}{7} = \frac{27}{24} + 0.0879 = 1.125 + 0.0879 = 1.213
$$

Required minimum wire diameter (from $\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot 8 F D_m / (\pi d_w^3) \le \tau_{allow}$):

Substitute $D_m = C_s \cdot d_w = 7 d_w$:

$$
\tau_{allow} = K_w \cdot \frac{8 \cdot 1800 \cdot 7 d_w}{\pi d_w^{3}} = K_w \cdot \frac{100{,}800}{\pi d_w^{2}}
$$

$$
d_w^{2} \ge \frac{K_w \cdot 100{,}800}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}} = \frac{1.213 \cdot 100{,}800}{\pi \cdot 550} = \frac{122{,}270}{1727.9} = 70.78 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
d_w \ge \sqrt{70.78} = 8.41 \text{ mm}
$$

Choose next-standard $d_w = 9$ mm (SWG or SWP standard). Therefore $D_m = 7 \cdot 9 = 63$ mm. Recompute $K_w$ with $C_s = 7$ → $K_w = 1.213$ (no change). Verify:

$$
\tau_{max} = 1.213 \cdot \frac{8 \cdot 1800 \cdot 63}{\pi \cdot 9^{3}} = 1.213 \cdot \frac{907{,}200}{2290.2} = 1.213 \cdot 396.1 = 480.5 \text{ MPa} < 550 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Spring rate and number of coils:**

Required spring rate to limit static deflection to 90 mm:

$$
k_{req} = W / \delta_{st} = 1800 / 90 = 20 \text{ N/mm}
$$

Rearrange $k = G d_w^4 / (8 D_m^3 N_a)$ to solve for $N_a$:

$$
N_a = \frac{G d_w^{4}}{8 D_m^{3} k_{req}} = \frac{77{,}000 \cdot 9^{4}}{8 \cdot 63^{3} \cdot 20}
$$

$$
N_a = \frac{77{,}000 \cdot 6561}{8 \cdot 250{,}047 \cdot 20} = \frac{505{,}197{,}000}{40{,}007{,}520} = 12.63
$$

Choose $N_a = 13$ active coils, total coils $N_t = N_a + 2 = 15$ (assuming squared-and-ground ends). Recompute $k$:

$$
k = \frac{77{,}000 \cdot 9^{4}}{8 \cdot 63^{3} \cdot 13} = \frac{505{,}197{,}000}{2{,}600{,}489} = 194.3 \text{ N/mm}
$$

Deflection:

$$
\delta_{st} = W / k = 1800 / 194.3 = 9.27 \text{ mm} \ll 90 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 8 — Buckling check (slenderness):**

Free length $\ell_f \approx (N_t + 1) d_w = 16 \cdot 9 = 144$ mm (for compression spring with closed ends). Ratio $\ell_f / D_m = 144 / 63 = 2.29$, well below the critical buckling ratio of 5.3 for fixed-free ends. **No buckling.** ✓

**Step 9 — Final design summary table (the "deliverable" expected by KTU):**

| Sub-System | Selected Part / Geometry | Material | Key Checked Quantity | Required | Achieved | Status |
|------------|--------------------------|----------|---------------------|----------|----------|--------|
| Shaft | Ø 40 mm, length 240 mm | EN8 / 45 C8 | Torsional rigidity | 1.5 × 10⁵ N·mm/rad | 1.8 × 10⁵ | ✓ |
| Rolling bearing | 6208 deep-groove ball | Through-hard. Cr-steel | $L_{10h}$ | 20,000 h | 25,128 h | ✓ |
| Sliding bearing | Ø 60 mm thrust collar | Sintered bronze | PV | $\le 5.5$ MPa·m/s | 3.96 | ✓ |
| Spring | Helical, $d_w = 9$, $D_m = 63$, $N_a = 13$ | Chrome-vanadium | $\tau_{max}$ | $\le 550$ MPa | 480.5 | ✓ |
| Spring | Same | Same | $\delta_{st}$ | $\le 90$ mm | 9.27 | ✓ |

> [!TIP]
> The **summary table** above is what differentiates a top-scoring KTU project from an average one. The examiner wants to see, in one glance, that every spec was *checked* and *met*. Always end the report with such a table.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix for Bearing Type Selection

The following table is a useful **analytical matrix** that the student can copy into the project report to justify bearing selection.

| Criterion | Deep-Groove Ball | Cylindrical Roller | Tapered Roller | Spherical Roller | Angular Contact |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|----------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Radial load capacity | Low–Med | High | High | Very High | Med |
| Axial load capacity | Low (both dirs) | None (N type) / Some (NJ) | High (one dir) | Some | High (one dir) |
| Speed limit | Very High | Very High | Med | Med | High |
| Misalignment tolerance | 2–10 arc-min | 2–4 arc-min | 1–2 arc-min | 0.5–2° | 1–2 arc-min |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium–High | Highest | Medium |
| Best fit in our project | Backup option | Good if pure radial | **Selected** — combined load | Heavy-duty alt. | Backup option |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: In the worked example we *initially* chose a deep-groove ball bearing for simplicity. In a real KTU project, the student should re-evaluate and may switch to a tapered roller bearing (TRB) once the combined load analysis shows that the axial component is non-negligible. The student is expected to document this *switch* with the equivalent-load equation applied to the TRB catalogue's $X$ and $Y$ factors.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Integrated Design Workflow

The following **Mermaid functional-architecture flow** captures the iterative design procedure for the project, including the **feedback loop** between bearing sizing, spring sizing, and system-level verification. (This is the *block-level* rendition — the physical drawings of the assembly are typically produced by the student in AutoCAD / SolidWorks and submitted as an annexure.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 0: Receive Performance Brief from Customer] --> A1[Step 1: Define Loads, Speeds, Life, Deflection]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Classify Loads - Radial vs Axial]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Tentative Bearing Type Selection]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Compute Required Dynamic Load Rating C_req]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Pick Catalogue Bearing - Confirm Bore d and OD D]
    A6 --> A7{Step 7: Check Life L10h computed vs required}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Step 8: Iterate - Increase Bore or Improve Lubrication]
    A8 --> A3
    A7 -- Yes --> B1[Step 9: Sliding Bearing Material Selection - PV Check]
    B1 --> B2{Step 10: PV within limit?}
    B2 -- No --> B3[Step 11: Re-select Material or Increase Footprint Area]
    B3 --> B1
    B2 -- Yes --> C1[Step 12: Spring Type Selection - Helical vs Leaf]
    C1 --> C2[Step 13: Pick Spring Index Cs in 4 to 12]
    C2 --> C3[Step 14: Compute Wire Diameter d_w from Wahl-corrected Stress]
    C3 --> C4[Step 15: Compute Spring Rate k and Required Active Coils N_a]
    C4 --> C5{Step 16: Deflection and Buckling Checks Pass?}
    C5 -- No --> C6[Step 17: Iterate - Adjust d_w, D_m, N_a]
    C6 --> C2
    C5 -- Yes --> D1[Step 18: System Level - Shaft Stiffness and Natural Frequency Check]
    D1 --> D2{Step 19: Natural Frequency off the Operating Range?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[Step 20: Re-balance k_eq via Spring Stiffness Change]
    D3 --> C1
    D2 -- No --> E1[Step 21: Material Selection via Ashby Indices]
    E1 --> E2[Step 22: Cost and Manufacturability Check]
    E2 --> F1[Step 23: Final Design Table and 2D Drawing]
    F1 --> G1[Step 24: Design Report Submission]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram:** Every rectangular node is a *design step*; every diamond node is a *decision gate*. The arrows that loop back to a previous step (e.g., from Step 8 back to Step 3) are **iteration loops** — they are exactly what the KTU evaluator expects the student to *mention* in the report. A report that goes linearly from A0 to G1 without acknowledging any iteration is a red flag.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Customer Brief: Load, Speed, Life, Environment] --> M1[Rolling Bearing Subsystem]
    L0 --> M2[Sliding Bearing Subsystem]
    L0 --> M3[Spring Subsystem]
    M1 -- shaft stiffness k_shaft --> M2
    M1 -- shaft stiffness k_shaft --> M3
    M2 -- reaction force --> M1
    M3 -- reaction force --> M1

    M3 --> O1

```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BriefReceived
    BriefReceived --> ComponentSized
    ComponentSized --> LifeCheck
    LifeCheck --> Iterating : L10h computed less than L10h required
    LifeCheck --> StressCheck : L10h check passed
    Iterating --> ComponentSized
    StressCheck --> DeflectionCheck

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for the KTU project:** The KTU 2024 scheme **explicitly rewards** process documentation in addition to the final numerical answer. Even a single process flow diagram, hand-drawn or Mermaid-rendered, can lift a project from a "B+" grade to an "A" grade, because it demonstrates that the student *thought* about the design as an iterative system rather than a one-shot calculation.
