# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the following terms with reference to spur gears: (i) Pitch circle, (ii) Addendum, (iii) Pressure angle.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Pitch Circle:** The pitch circle is an imaginary circle on a gear on which two meshing gears effectively roll upon one another without slipping. All geometric calculations relating to speed ratio and center distance are based on this circle. Its diameter is denoted by $d = mT$, where $m$ is the module and $T$ is the number of teeth.

**(ii) Addendum:** The addendum is the radial distance from the pitch circle to the tip (outside circle) of a tooth. For a standard 20° full-depth involute gear, $a = m$ (one module).

**(iii) Pressure Angle:** The pressure angle $\phi$ is the angle between the line of action (common normal to the two meshing tooth profiles) and the common tangent to the pitch circles at the pitch point. Standard value: $\phi = 20°$.

**Mark distribution (per KTU pattern):** Definition 1 mark, formula/symbol 1 mark, standard value/practical note 1 mark.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between **circular pitch** and **diametral pitch** for a spur gear. State the relation between module and diametral pitch.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Circular Pitch ($p$) | Diametral Pitch ($P$) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Arc length measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points on adjacent teeth | Number of teeth per unit length of pitch diameter |
| Symbol | $p$ | $P$ |
| Unit | mm (linear) | teeth per inch (1/in) |
| Formula | $p = \pi m$ | $P = T/d$ |
| System | SI / ISO / KTU standard | Imperial / American standard |

**Relation between module and diametral pitch:**

Since module $m$ (in mm) and diametral pitch $P$ (in 1/inch) are reciprocal:

$$
P = \frac{25.4}{m} \quad \text{or} \quad m = \frac{25.4}{P}
$$

If both were in identical units (theoretical case): $P = 1/m$ exactly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Each Module typically offers an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Both choices are given below with sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Two meshing spur gears have a module of 4 mm and a pressure angle of 20°. The pinion has 18 teeth and the gear has 72 teeth. Calculate:

**(a)** Pitch diameters of both gears, center distance, and base circle diameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Circular pitch, addendum, dedendum, whole depth, working depth, tooth thickness, and outside/root diameters of the pinion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given:** $m = 4$ mm, $\phi = 20°$, $T_1 = 18$ (pinion), $T_2 = 72$ (gear), standard 20° full-depth involute system.

#### Part (a) Solution

**Pitch diameters:**
$$
d_1 = mT_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
d_2 = mT_2 = 4 \times 72 = 288 \text{ mm}
$$

**Center distance:**
$$
C = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{72 + 288}{2} = 180 \text{ mm}
$$

**Base circle diameters:**
$$
\cos 20° = 0.9397
$$
$$
d_{b1} = d_1 \cos\phi = 72 \times 0.9397 = 67.66 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
d_{b2} = d_2 \cos\phi = 288 \times 0.9397 = 270.63 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Circular pitch:**
$$
p = \pi m = \pi \times 4 = 12.566 \text{ mm}
$$

**Standard proportions (for 20° full-depth involute):**

$$
a = m = 4 \text{ mm (addendum)}
$$
$$
d = 1.25m = 5 \text{ mm (dedendum)}
$$
$$
h_t = a + d = 4 + 5 = 9 \text{ mm (whole depth)}
$$
$$
h_w = 2a = 8 \text{ mm (working depth)}
$$
$$
t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 4}{2} = 6.283 \text{ mm (tooth thickness)}
$$

**Pinion outside and root diameters:**
$$
d_{o1} = m(T_1 + 2) = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
d_{r1} = m(T_1 - 2.5) = 4 \times 15.5 = 62 \text{ mm}
$$

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A pair of meshing spur gears with 20° full-depth involute teeth have a velocity ratio of 3:1. The pinion rotates at 1440 rpm and the center distance is 240 mm. Calculate:

**(a)** Number of teeth of both gears and module of the gears. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Pitch line velocity, circular pitch, tooth thickness, and addendum of the gear. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given:** Velocity ratio $i = 3:1$, $N_1 = 1440$ rpm, $C = 240$ mm, $\phi = 20°$, standard full-depth involute.

#### Part (a) Solution

Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the number of teeth on pinion and gear respectively.

**From velocity ratio:**
$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = 3T_1
$$

**From center distance:**
$$
C = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2} = \frac{m(T_1 + 3T_1)}{2} = 2mT_1
$$
$$
240 = 2mT_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad mT_1 = 120
$$

**Trial 1:** Let $T_1 = 30$, then $m = 120/30 = 4$ mm. Check: $T_2 = 90$. (No common factor preventing meshing; 30 and 90 are valid.) — **Acceptable.**

(Other valid combinations: $T_1 = 24, m = 5$; $T_1 = 20, m = 6$; $T_1 = 40, m = 3$; $T_1 = 15, m = 8$, etc.)

**Selecting $T_1 = 30$, $m = 4$ mm:**

$$
\boxed{T_1 = 30, \quad T_2 = 90, \quad m = 4 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Check no interference** (rule of thumb): $T_1 \geq 18$ for 20° pressure angle (minimum without undercutting). $T_1 = 30$ > 18. ✓

#### Part (b) Solution

**Pitch diameters:**
$$
d_1 = mT_1 = 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
d_2 = mT_2 = 4 \times 90 = 360 \text{ mm}
$$

**Pitch line velocity of pinion:**
$$
v = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.120 \times 1440}{60} = 9.048 \text{ m/s}
$$

(Same for gear: $v = \pi d_2 N_2 / 60 = \pi \times 0.360 \times 480/60 = 9.048$ m/s — pitch line velocity is identical for both, as expected.)

**Circular pitch:**
$$
p = \pi m = \pi \times 4 = 12.566 \text{ mm}
$$

**Tooth thickness:**
$$
t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 4}{2} = 6.283 \text{ mm}
$$

**Addendum:**
$$
a = m = 4 \text{ mm}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Per Question**
>
> 1. **Confusing symbols:** Writing $d$ for both *pitch diameter* and *dedendum* without disambiguation. Always write *"pitch diameter, $d = mT$"* first, then *"dedendum, $d = 1.25m$"* with explicit notation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting "standard 20° full-depth"** assumption: If the problem doesn't state it explicitly, **state the assumption yourself** at the start of the answer. Without this, your addendum/dedendum values are unverified.
>
> 3. **Mixing module and diametral pitch:** KTU follows SI system. **Never** compute $P = 1/m$ directly; use $P = 25.4/m$ only if explicitly asked in imperial units.
>
> 4. **Skipping the unit:** Every numerical answer must carry the unit (mm, m/s). An answer without a unit is *incomplete* in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Missing the minimum tooth count check:** For 20° full-depth teeth, minimum $T$ without undercutting is **17** (some texts: 18). Mentioning this check in design problems earns bonus impression marks.
>
> 6. **Assuming base circle = pitch circle:** This is a classic misconception. The base circle is always *smaller* than the pitch circle for $\phi > 0°$. Their ratio is $\cos\phi$ (≈ 0.9397 for 20°).
>
> 7. **Forgetting to verify "modules of meshing gears must be equal":** A common conceptual trap question asks "Can a 3-module gear mesh with a 5-module gear?" The answer is **NO**, and the explanation must mention that tooth sizes would not match.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Gear Nomenclature**

### Core Vocabulary (Memorize the Definitions)
- **Pitch circle**: imaginary rolling circle; $d = mT$.
- **Pitch point**: point of tangency of two pitch circles; lies on the line of centers.
- **Addendum (a)**: tip extension = $m$.
- **Dedendum (d)**: root depth = $1.25m$.
- **Whole depth ($h_t$)**: $2.25m$ — tip to root.
- **Working depth ($h_w$)**: $2m$ — actual engagement depth.
- **Clearance (c)**: $0.25m$ — non-engaging gap.
- **Tooth thickness (t)**: $\pi m / 2 = 1.5708\,m$.
- **Space width (s)**: equal to tooth thickness.
- **Outside diameter ($d_o$)**: $m(T+2)$.
- **Root diameter ($d_r$)**: $m(T-2.5)$.
- **Base diameter ($d_b$)**: $d \cos\phi$.
- **Pressure angle ($\phi$)**: standard 20°.

### Formulas at a Glance
- $m = d/T$
- $p = \pi m$
- $P = 25.4/m$
- $C = m(T_1+T_2)/2$
- $v = \pi d N / 60$
- $i = T_2/T_1 = N_1/N_2$
- $\cos\phi' = (C/C')\cos\phi$

### Critical Principles (For Conceptual Questions)
- For meshing gears, **module must be identical**.
- **Circular pitch** is identical for both meshing gears.
- Base circle diameter is **always less than** pitch circle diameter.
- Increasing center distance → **increases** operating pressure angle.
- Standard 20° full-depth involute: addendum = $m$, dedendum = $1.25m$.
- Minimum tooth count for 20° full-depth without undercutting: **$T_{min} = 17$**.

### Automotive Connections
- **Manual transmission**: spur + helical gears.
- **Differential**: spiral bevel gears (uses similar nomenclature extended for bevels).
- **Steering**: rack and pinion (rack has linear teeth — same addendum/dedendum concepts).
- **Starter motor**: planetary spur gears.
- **Electric vehicles (EVs)**: single-speed reducers often use helical gears — same module-based nomenclature.

### Quick Mnemonics
- **"ADAM"** for top-down tooth parts: **A**ddendum → **D**edendum → **A**ddendum (mirror), **M**odule is the unit.
- **"Pitch is the heart"** — every gear calculation begins with $d = mT$.
- **"20° is the friendly angle"** — default unless stated otherwise.

### Final Board Strategy
1. Read the problem twice — note $m$, $T$, $\phi$, $i$, $C$ as given.
2. State "Standard 20° full-depth involute system assumed" if not given.
3. List the formulas you will use (mark scheme gives weightage for method).
4. Substitute step by step, showing units.
5. End with a "Check" sentence: e.g., "Since $T_1 = 30 > 17$, the pinion is free from undercutting."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tooth Profiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tooth Profiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tooth Profile**: The shape of the tooth flank as observed in the transverse section (plane of rotation) of a gear. It is the curve described by the gear tooth from the root circle to the addendum circle, along which contact with the mating tooth occurs during meshing.

In automotive component design, two conjugate (continuously rolling-without-slipping) tooth profiles dominate engineering practice:

1. **Involute Tooth Profile** — generated by unwinding a taut string from a base circle.
2. **Cycloidal Tooth Profile** — formed by the rolling of a circle (epicycloid on the outside, hypocycloid on the inside) on the pitch circles of the mating gears.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conjugate Action**: Two profiles are said to be conjugate when they maintain a *constant angular velocity ratio* between the driving and driven gears throughout the entire period of meshing. The Fundamental Law of Gearing governs this requirement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine two rolling wheels in contact. If you wrap a string tightly around one wheel and pull it taut to wrap around a second wheel, the straight portion of that string between the wheels is always tangent to both — and any point on that string travels in a perfectly straight line. **That straight-line portion, traced along the wheel, generates an involute curve.** Similarly, when a small coin rolls around the outside of a larger coin without slipping, a point on the smaller coin traces an **epicycloid** — the basic building block of the cycloidal profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters in automobiles**: Every gear in the transmission, differential, starter motor, and timing drive must mesh smoothly at varying loads and small misalignments. The involute profile's *self-correcting* nature under center-distance variation is the principal reason it is the universal automotive industry standard.

### 1.3 Key Geometric Terms

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Circle** | $r_b$ | The circle from which the involute is generated. Pressure angle is measured here. |
| **Pitch Circle** | $r$ | The imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll. |
| **Addendum Circle** | $r_a$ | The outermost circle of the tooth tip. |
| **Dedendum Circle** | $r_f$ | The root circle at the bottom of the tooth space. |
| **Pressure Angle** | $\phi$ | Angle between the line of action and the common tangent to the pitch circles. **Standard values: 14.5°, 20°, 25°.** |
| **Module** | $m$ | Ratio of pitch diameter to number of teeth. Standard series: **1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm**. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Involute Tooth Profile — The Industry Standard

The involute is generated by **unwinding a string** from a base circle. As the string is unwound, its free end traces the involute curve. The unwound length of the string at any instant equals the arc of the base circle from the starting point to the tangent point.

**Geometric Construction Logic (Step-by-step):**

- A circle of radius $r_b$ (the base circle) is drawn.
- A tangent line is drawn at any point B on the base circle.
- The straight portion of this tangent (the line of action) is the locus of contact between mating involutes.
- A point P on this tangent, at a distance equal to the arc BP from B, traces the involute as the tangent rotates about B.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Property of Involutes**: The normal to an involute at any point of contact is **tangent to the base circle**. Consequently, the line of action is the *common internal tangent* to the two base circles of meshing gears.

### 2.2 Cycloidal Tooth Profile

The cycloidal profile is formed by:
- **Epicycloid**: A small circle (radius $r_p$) rolling *outside* a fixed circle (the pitch circle of the gear) — forms the **addendum flank** (above the pitch circle).
- **Hypocycloid**: The same small circle rolling *inside* the fixed pitch circle — forms the **dedendum flank** (below the pitch circle).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are exhaustively used in KTU university examination problems. Memorize the units and standard pressure angle conventions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Standard Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Module | $m = \dfrac{d}{T}$ | mm (standard series per IS 2532) |
| 2 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $d = m \cdot T$ | mm |
| 3 | Base Circle Diameter | $d_b = d \cos\phi$ | mm |
| 4 | Addendum | $a = m$ | mm (standard full-depth) |
| 5 | Dedendum (full-depth) | $b = 1.25\,m$ | mm |
| 6 | Addendum Circle Diameter | $d_a = d + 2m$ | mm |
| 7 | Dedendum Circle Diameter | $d_f = d - 2.5\,m$ | mm |
| 8 | Circular Pitch | $p_c = \pi m$ | mm |
| 9 | Involute Function | $\text{inv}(\phi) = \tan\phi - \phi$ | radians |
| 10 | Arc of Approach | $\text{inv}(\phi) = \tan\phi_a - \tan\phi + \phi - \phi_a$ | radians |
| 11 | Length of Path of Contact | $L = \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}} - (r_1+r_2)\sin\phi$ | mm |
| 12 | Contact Ratio | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L}{p_b}$ where $p_b = \pi m \cos\phi$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Minimum Teeth (no undercut) | $T_{\min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}$ | for $\phi=20°$, $T_{\min}=17$ |

> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas drive the entire automotive drivetrain — from the helical gearbox in electric vehicles to the planetary gear sets in automatic transmissions. The contact ratio $\varepsilon$ must always exceed **1.4** for automotive applications to ensure smooth, quiet power transmission.

### 2.4 Comparison: Involute vs. Cycloidal

| Property | Involute | Cycloidal |
|---|---|---|
| Generation | Unwinding a string | Rolling circles |
| Sensitivity to center-distance error | Low (self-correcting) | High |
| Manufacturing ease | Easy (rack cutter) | Difficult |
| Interference | Possible with low tooth count | Almost none |
| Standardization | Universal (ISO, AGMA, IS) | Rarely used |
| Pressure during meshing | Constant | Variable |
| Automotive use | **Overwhelming majority** | Almost obsolete |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Involute Curve (Parametric Form)

Let a string be wound around a base circle of radius $r_b$. The string is unwound from point B. At any instant, let the tangent point on the base circle be at angle $\theta$ from the initial point of unwinding (measured at the center O of the base circle). The free end of the string, point P, lies on the involute.

**Step 1: Geometry of the unwound string.**

The arc length from the starting point A to the tangent point B on the base circle is:

$$\widehat{AB} = r_b \cdot \theta$$

By the property of unwinding, the unwound length of the string BP equals this arc length:

$$BP = r_b \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2: Resolve the position vector OP into Cartesian components.**

Place O at the origin. The point B lies on the base circle at angle $\theta$ from the x-axis. The tangent at B makes an angle $\theta$ with the radius OB (tangent is perpendicular to radius).

The vector from O to P is the vector sum of OB and BP:
- $\overrightarrow{OB}$ has magnitude $r_b$, directed at angle $\theta$.
- $\overrightarrow{BP}$ has magnitude $r_b \theta$, directed at angle $\theta - 90°$ (perpendicular to OB, pointing outward along the unwinding direction).

**Step 3: Express the components.**

The x-component of OP is:

$$x = r_b \cos\theta + r_b\theta \cdot \cos(\theta - 90°)$$

Since $\cos(\theta - 90°) = \sin\theta$:

$$x = r_b(\cos\theta + \theta\sin\theta)$$

The y-component of OP is:

$$y = r_b \sin\theta + r_b\theta \cdot \sin(\theta - 90°)$$

Since $\sin(\theta - 90°) = -\cos\theta$:

$$y = r_b(\sin\theta - \theta\cos\theta)$$

**Step 4: Final parametric equations of the involute.**

$$\boxed{\;x = r_b(\cos\theta + \theta\sin\theta) \qquad y = r_b(\sin\theta - \theta\cos\theta)\;}$$

where $\theta$ is measured in **radians** and is called the **involute polar angle**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Involute Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favorite KTU question** and routinely carries 7 marks. Practice the algebra carefully.

Consider a point P on the involute. The pressure angle at P (angle between the radius OP and the tangent at P) is $\phi$. The angle between OB and OP is $\theta$ (the involute polar angle). In right triangle OBP:

- Hypotenuse OP makes angle $\theta$ with x-axis.
- OB has length $r_b$.
- The angle $\angle BOP = \theta$, and $\angle OPB = \phi$ (alternate segment property).

Therefore $\angle OBP = 90°$, and:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{BP}{OB} = \frac{r_b\theta}{r_b} = \theta$$

Hence:

$$\theta = \tan\phi$$

**Substituting back** into the definition, the involute function is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{inv}(\phi) = \tan\phi - \phi\;}$$

where $\phi$ is expressed in radians. This function represents the polar angle $\theta$ of the involute measured **from the point where the involute begins on the base circle** (where $\phi = 0$) to any point of pressure angle $\phi$.

---

### 3.3 Proof of the Fundamental Law of Gearing

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement**: *The common normal at the point of contact between two meshing gear teeth must always pass through the pitch point (the instantaneous center of rotation of the driven gear relative to the driving gear).*

**Proof:**

Let two gears with centers $O_1$ and $O_2$ rotate with angular velocities $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$. Let P be the instantaneous point of contact. The velocities of the material points on the two gears that coincide at P at this instant must be equal in magnitude and direction (otherwise the teeth would either separate or inter-penetrate).

Let $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ be the angles that the common normal at P makes with the line joining $O_1$ and $O_2$. For point on gear 1 at P:

$$v_1 = \omega_1 \cdot O_1P \cdot \sin\alpha_1$$

Similarly for gear 2:

$$v_2 = \omega_2 \cdot O_2P \cdot \sin\alpha_2$$

Equating the velocities (no slip):

$$\omega_1 \cdot O_1P \cdot \sin\alpha_1 = \omega_2 \cdot O_2P \cdot \sin\alpha_2$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{O_2P \cdot \sin\alpha_2}{O_1P \cdot \sin\alpha_1}$$

For the velocity ratio to be **constant** (independent of the position of P during meshing), the right-hand side must remain constant. By the sine rule in triangle $O_1PO_2$:

$$\frac{O_2P}{\sin\alpha_1} = \frac{O_1P}{\sin\alpha_2} = \frac{O_1O_2}{\sin(180° - (\alpha_1+\alpha_2))}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{O_1O_2 \sin\alpha_2 / \sin(180°-(\alpha_1+\alpha_2))}{O_1O_2 \sin\alpha_1 / \sin(180°-(\alpha_1+\alpha_2))} \cdot \frac{\sin\alpha_2}{\sin\alpha_1}$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{O_2P}{O_1P}$$

This is **constant** only when the common normal intersects the line of centers at a **fixed point** — the pitch point P. Thus, the law is proved. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Why involute satisfies this automatically**: Since the common normal to two meshing involutes is the common internal tangent to their base circles, and the base circles are fixed, this tangent always passes through a *fixed point* on the line of centers — the pitch point. The involute profile is therefore **inherently conjugate**.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem**: A pinion with 18 teeth meshes with a gear of 45 teeth. Module = 4 mm, pressure angle = 20°. Determine: (i) pitch circle diameters, (ii) base circle diameters, (iii) addendum circle diameters, (iv) contact ratio.

**Solution:**

(i) Pitch circle diameters:

$$d_1 = m \cdot T_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_2 = m \cdot T_2 = 4 \times 45 = 180 \text{ mm}$$

(ii) Base circle diameters ($d_b = d\cos\phi$, with $\cos 20° = 0.9397$):

$$d_{b1} = 72 \times 0.9397 = 67.66 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_{b2} = 180 \times 0.9397 = 169.14 \text{ mm}$$

(iii) Addendum circle diameters ($d_a = d + 2m$):

$$d_{a1} = 72 + 8 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_{a2} = 180 + 8 = 188 \text{ mm}$$

(iv) Contact ratio — first compute the base pitch $p_b$:

$$p_b = \pi m \cos\phi = \pi \times 4 \times 0.9397 = 11.81 \text{ mm}$$

Length of path of contact:

$$L = \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}} - (r_1 + r_2)\sin\phi$$

Computing each term:

$$\sqrt{40^2 - 33.83^2} = \sqrt{1600 - 1144.47} = \sqrt{455.53} = 21.34 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sqrt{94^2 - 84.57^2} = \sqrt{8836 - 7151.78} = \sqrt{1684.22} = 41.04 \text{ mm}$$

$$(36 + 90)\sin 20° = 126 \times 0.3420 = 43.09 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$L = 21.34 + 41.04 - 43.09 = 19.29 \text{ mm}$$

Contact ratio:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{L}{p_b} = \frac{19.29}{11.81} = 1.633$$

Since $\varepsilon > 1.4$, the design is **acceptable** for automotive service.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Gear Tooth Meshing System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DRIVING GEAR O1"] --> B["PITCH POINT P"]
    B --> C["DRIVEN GEAR O2"]
    A --> D["BASE CIRCLE DB1 INVOLUTE FLANK"]
    C --> E["BASE CIRCLE DB2 INVOLUTE FLANK"]
    D --> F["COMMON INTERNAL TANGENT LINE OF ACTION"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["POINT OF CONTACT MOVES ALONG LINE OF ACTION"]
    B --> H["CONSTANT ANGULAR VELOCITY RATIO"]
    H --> I["SMOOTH POWER TRANSMISSION IN AUTOMOTIVE DRIVETRAIN"]
    A --> J["PRESSURE ANGLE PHI AT MESH"]
    J --> F
    C --> J
    G --> K["PATH OF APPROACH AND RECESS"]
    K --> L["CONTACT RATIO EPSILON"]
    L --> M["CONTINUITY OF CONTACT CRITERIA"]
```

### 4.2 Generation Mechanism: Involute Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STRING WOUND ON BASE CIRCLE RADIUS RB"] --> B["UNWIND STRING FROM POINT A"]
    B --> C["STRING STAYS TAUT BETWEEN BASE CIRCLE AND FREE END"]
    C --> D["FREE END TRACES INVOLUTE CURVE"]
    D --> E["PARAMETRIC POINT P AT RADIUS RP"]
    E --> F["ANGLE BETWEEN OP AND X AXIS EQUALS THETA"]
    E --> G["PRESSURE ANGLE PHI EQUALS TAN INVERSE OF THETA"]
    F --> H["GEAR TOOTH FLANK SHAPE COMPLETE"]
```

### 4.3 Cycloidal Profile Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PITCH CIRCLE OF GEAR FIXED"] --> B["SMALL ROLLING CIRCLE RP ROLLS OUTSIDE"]
    A --> C["SMALL ROLLING CIRCLE RP ROLLS INSIDE"]
    B --> D["EPICYCLOID FORMS ADDENDUM FLANK"]
    C --> E["HYPOCYCLOID FORMS DEDENDUM FLANK"]
    D --> F["COMPLETE CYCLODAL TOOTH PROFILE"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["USED IN CLOCKS AND WORMS RARELY AUTOMOTIVE"]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["TOOTH PROFILE SELECTION"] --> B["INVOLUTE"]
    A --> C["CYCLODAL"]
    B --> B1["SELF CENTERING"]
    B --> B2["TOLERATES CENTER DISTANCE VARIATION"]
    B --> B3["STANDARD RACK CUTTER"]
    B --> B4["INDUSTRY STANDARD ISO AGMA"]
    C --> C1["NO INTERFERENCE"]
    C --> C2["COMPLEX MANUFACTURING"]
    C --> C3["SENSITIVE TO CENTER DISTANCE"]
    C --> C4["OBSOLETE IN AUTOMOTIVES"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define an involute tooth profile. List any two advantages of involute gears over cycloidal gears.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> An **involute tooth profile** is the curve traced by the end of a taut string as it is unwound from a base circle. Mathematically, it is defined by the parametric equations $x = r_b(\cos\theta + \theta\sin\theta)$ and $y = r_b(\sin\theta - \theta\cos\theta)$, where $r_b$ is the base circle radius and $\theta$ is the involute polar angle.

**Advantages over cycloidal gears:**
1. The involute profile satisfies the fundamental law of gearing *automatically* because the common normal to meshing involutes is the common internal tangent of the two base circles, which always passes through a fixed pitch point.
2. Small variations in the center distance do not affect the *constancy* of the angular velocity ratio — only the pressure angle changes slightly. This is the **self-centering** property.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State the fundamental law of gearing. Why is it important for smooth power transmission?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement**: *The common normal at the point of contact between two meshing gear teeth must always pass through the pitch point on the line joining the centers of the two gears.*

**Importance**:
- It guarantees that the angular velocity ratio between the driving and driven gears remains **constant** throughout the entire period of meshing.
- Without this law, gears would produce vibration, impact, noise, and rapid wear — unacceptable in an automotive transmission operating at high RPM.
- Any pair of conjugate profiles satisfying this law can mesh smoothly; the involute is the most popular such profile because it satisfies the law *without* requiring precision in the cutting tool.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

## ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

### (a) Derive the parametric equations of an involute curve. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> **Given**: A base circle of radius $r_b$, with a string initially wound around it. The string is unwound from point A on the base circle; at any instant, the tangent point on the base circle is at angle $\theta$ from A.

**Step 1 — Arc length relation**:
The arc length from A to the tangent point B is $\widehat{AB} = r_b \theta$. By the unwinding property:

$$BP = r_b \theta$$

**Step 2 — Geometry of the construction**:
The radius OB makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. The tangent BP is perpendicular to OB. Therefore BP makes an angle $(\theta - 90°)$ with the x-axis.

**Step 3 — Position vector of P**:
The vector OP is the sum of OB and BP:

$$\overrightarrow{OP} = \overrightarrow{OB} + \overrightarrow{BP}$$

**Step 4 — Component resolution**:

The x-component is:

$$x = r_b \cos\theta + (r_b\theta)\cos(\theta - 90°) = r_b\cos\theta + r_b\theta\sin\theta$$

The y-component is:

$$y = r_b \sin\theta + (r_b\theta)\sin(\theta - 90°) = r_b\sin\theta - r_b\theta\cos\theta$$

**Step 5 — Final result**:

$$\boxed{x = r_b(\cos\theta + \theta\sin\theta) \qquad y = r_b(\sin\theta - \theta\cos\theta)}$$

---

### (b) Two gears in external mesh have 20 and 40 teeth with module 5 mm and pressure angle 20°. Find the contact ratio. Take standard full-depth teeth. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pitch circle diameters**:

$$d_1 = mT_1 = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_2 = mT_2 = 5 \times 40 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Base circle diameters** ($\cos 20° = 0.9397$):

$$d_{b1} = 100 \times 0.9397 = 93.97 \text{ mm}, \quad r_{b1} = 46.985 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_{b2} = 200 \times 0.9397 = 187.94 \text{ mm}, \quad r_{b2} = 93.97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Addendum circle radii** (addendum $a = m = 5$ mm):

$$r_{a1} = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ mm}, \quad r_{a2} = 100 + 5 = 105 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Base pitch**:

$$p_b = \pi m \cos\phi = \pi \times 5 \times 0.9397 = 14.76 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Length of path of contact**:

$$L = \sqrt{r_{a1}^2 - r_{b1}^2} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^2 - r_{b2}^2} - (r_1 + r_2)\sin\phi$$

Computing each term:

$$\sqrt{55^2 - 46.985^2} = \sqrt{3025 - 2207.59} = \sqrt{817.41} = 28.59 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sqrt{105^2 - 93.97^2} = \sqrt{11025 - 8830.36} = \sqrt{2194.64} = 46.85 \text{ mm}$$

$$(50 + 100)\sin 20° = 150 \times 0.3420 = 51.30 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$L = 28.59 + 46.85 - 51.30 = 24.14 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Contact ratio**:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{L}{p_b} = \frac{24.14}{14.76} = 1.636$$

Since $\varepsilon > 1.4$, **the design is satisfactory** for automotive service.

---

## ✅ Question B (14 Marks)

### (a) State and prove the fundamental law of gearing. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Statement**: *The common normal at the point of contact between two meshing gear teeth must always pass through the pitch point on the line joining the gear centers.*

**Proof**:

Let two gears with centers $O_1$ and $O_2$ have angular velocities $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$. Let $P$ be the point of contact, and let $\alpha_1$, $\alpha_2$ be the angles the common normal at $P$ makes with $O_1O_2$.

Velocity of point P on gear 1:

$$v_1 = \omega_1 \cdot O_1P \cdot \sin\alpha_1$$

Velocity of point P on gear 2:

$$v_2 = \omega_2 \cdot O_2P \cdot \sin\alpha_2$$

For no slip at the contact point: $v_1 = v_2$.

$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{O_2P \sin\alpha_2}{O_1P \sin\alpha_1}$$

By the sine rule in triangle $O_1PO_2$:

$$\frac{O_1P}{\sin\alpha_2} = \frac{O_2P}{\sin\alpha_1}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{O_2P}{O_1P} = \text{constant}$$

This ratio remains constant only if the common normal always passes through a **fixed point** on $O_1O_2$ — the pitch point. Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

### (b) Compare involute and cycloidal tooth profiles in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| S.No. | Aspect | Involute Profile | Cycloidal Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generation | Unwinding a taut string from a base circle. | Rolling of a circle (epicycloid + hypocycloid) on the pitch circle. |
| 2 | Center-distance sensitivity | Self-centering; tolerates small variations. | Highly sensitive; small errors cause unequal velocity ratios. |
| 3 | Manufacturing | Easy with standard rack cutters. | Complex; requires special generating tools. |
| 4 | Pressure variation | Remains constant throughout meshing. | Varies with tooth position. |
| 5 | Interference | May occur if teeth < 17 (at 20°). | Almost no interference. |
| 6 | Sliding velocity | Lower sliding at tip. | Higher sliding at pitch point reversal. |
| 7 | Standardization | Universal (ISO, AGMA, BIS, DIN). | No standard series. |
| 8 | Modern automotive use | **Universal**. | Obsolete. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: When comparing profiles, students often list only one-line statements without referencing *why* one property matters. Always link each property to its **engineering consequence** (e.g., "low sensitivity to center-distance variation → robust assembly tolerance in mass-produced vehicle gearboxes"). Marks are awarded for *engineering reasoning*, not mere listing.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Involute profile** is generated by *unwinding a taut string* from a base circle.
- [ ] **Cycloidal profile** is generated by *rolling circles* (epicycloid + hypocycloid).
- [ ] **Fundamental Law of Gearing**: common normal at contact must pass through the *pitch point*.
- [ ] Standard pressure angles: **14.5°** (old), **20°** (current standard), **25°** (high-load).
- [ ] Standard full-depth system: addendum = **$m$**, dedendum = **$1.25\,m$**.
- [ ] Pitch diameter $d = mT$; base diameter $d_b = d\cos\phi$.
- [ ] Involute function: $\text{inv}(\phi) = \tan\phi - \phi$ (in radians).
- [ ] Minimum teeth without undercut (at 20°): **17 teeth**.
- [ ] Contact ratio $\varepsilon = L / p_b$ must exceed **1.4** for automotive service.
- [ ] Involute gears are **self-centering** — the dominant reason they dominate automotive applications.
- [ ] Always remember **units**: module in mm, angles in radians for involute function.
- [ ] Cycloidal gears are essentially obsolete in modern automotive transmissions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials of Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Materials of Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gear Materials** are the carefully selected engineering substances (metals, alloys, and non-metallic composites) that are shaped via machining, forging, or powder metallurgy to manufacture gear teeth capable of transmitting motion, torque, and power between shafts without slip, while resisting wear, pitting, scuffing, tooth breakage, and surface fatigue under cyclic loading.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBAUT604 – Automotive Component Design**, the topic "Materials of Gears" demands a holistic understanding of **metallurgical selection**, **heat treatment methodology**, and **service-condition mapping** for automotive transmission gears.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Comparison)

Think of a **bicycle chain and sprocket system** versus a **car gearbox**. The bicycle chain rides on cheap stamped steel sprockets because the loads are low and noise tolerance is high. However, in an automotive gearbox, gears mesh at high speeds (often > 6000 rpm) and transmit hundreds of Newton-metres of torque. If the gear material is "soft" (like the bicycle sprocket), it would crush, wear out, or shatter in hours.

Therefore, **gear materials are like the tires of a Formula 1 car** — they must simultaneously grip (resist wear), flex slightly under shock loads (toughness), and survive thousands of laps (endurance). Choosing the wrong material is like fitting scooter tires on a Formula 1 car — they will fail catastrophically.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Metrics

The following **standard engineering metrics** govern every gear material selection:

| Property (Metric) | Symbol | Typical Range (Automotive Gears) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Surface Hardness** | $HB$ or $HRC$ | 50–62 HRC (case-hardened); 200–400 HB (through-hardened) |
| **Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_u$ | **600 MPa – 2000 MPa** |
| **Endurance Limit** | $\sigma_e$ | **0.45 × $\sigma_u$ to 0.55 × $\sigma_u$** |
| **Young's Modulus** | $E$ | **2.0 × 10⁵ MPa – 2.1 × 10⁵ MPa** (for steels) |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu$ | **0.28 – 0.30** (for steels) |
| **Wear Coefficient** | $K$ | **10⁻⁸ – 10⁻⁶ (dimensionless)** |
| **Density** | $\rho$ | **7.8 g/cm³ (steels), 2.7 g/cm³ (aluminium)** |
| **Tooth Surface Speed Limit** | $v$ | **up to 200 m/s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module specifically stresses the link between **material selection** and the **Lewis Equation / Buckingham Equation** design methodology. A correct material choice is incomplete without verifying it against the bending and wear stress equations.

## 1.4 Classification of Gear Materials (Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Metallic vs. Non-Metallic Gear Materials:**
> - **Metallic gear materials** are ferrous (steel-based) and non-ferrous (brass, bronze, aluminium) substances that primarily transmit power in heavy-duty automotive gearboxes, differentials, and transfer cases.
> - **Non-metallic gear materials** are polymers and composites (nylon, acetal, phenolic resin, fibre-reinforced plastics) used for low-load, low-noise, and lubrication-free applications such as instrument drives, printers, and small appliance gears.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Desired Properties of Gear Materials — The "Why" Behind Selection

A gear tooth in service is subjected to:
1. **Bending fatigue** at the root fillet (reversing tensile stress every mesh).
2. **Contact (Hertzian) stress** on the flank surface (compressive, near yield).
3. **Scuffing / adhesive wear** due to metal-to-metal contact under load.
4. **Impact / shock loading** during sudden acceleration or gear engagement.
5. **Thermal cycling** from frictional heating at the pitch point.

Consequently, an ideal gear material must satisfy the following layered property pyramid:

### Layered Property Requirements
- **Surface Layer (Case):** Extreme hardness ($HRC \ge 58$), high residual compressive stress, wear resistance.
- **Subsurface / Core:** High toughness, ductility, fatigue strength (endurance limit).
- **Bulk Material:** Dimensional stability, machinability, heat-treatability, affordability.

## 2.2 Major Categories of Gear Materials

### 2.2.1 Plain Carbon Steels
- Examples: **AISI 1040, AISI 1050, C40, C45, C50**.
- Composition: Fe + 0.4–0.5% C + minor Mn.
- Typical Use: Low/medium duty industrial gears, crane drives.
- Heat Treatment: Through-hardening by quenching + tempering.
- Limitation: Poor core toughness; not suited for highly loaded automotive gears.

### 2.2.2 Alloy Steels
- Examples: **AISI 4140, 4340, EN19, EN24, 16MnCr5, 20MnCr5**.
- Composition: Fe + C + Cr / Ni / Mo / Mn additions.
- Typical Use: **Automotive transmission gears, helical gears, hypoid ring gears.**
- Heat Treatment: Carburizing → Quenching → Tempering.

### 2.2.3 Case-Hardening Steels (The Workhorse of Automotive Gears)
- Examples: **AISI 8620, 9310, 20NiCrMo2, 16MnCr5.**
- Process Sequence:
  - **Carburizing (Pack / Gas / Plasma):** Diffuses carbon into the surface layer (depth = 0.8–2.0 mm).
  - **Quenching:** Produces hard martensitic case.
  - **Tempering (low temp, 150–200°C):** Reduces brittleness.
  - Result: A hard, wear-resistant "case" (60–62 HRC) over a tough core (30–45 HRC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Case-hardened gears are the **default choice for automotive transmission** because they combine a wear-resistant surface with a shock-absorbing core. About **70% of all automobile gears** are made from case-hardened steels.

### 2.2.4 Through-Hardening Steels
- Examples: **AISI 4140, 4340** after quenching.
- Hardness: Uniform throughout the section (35–45 HRC).
- Use: Large-diameter gears where case-hardening depth cannot be achieved uniformly.

### 2.2.5 Nitriding Steels
- Examples: **AISI 4140, AISI 4340** with Aluminium addition (e.g., **Nitralloy 135**).
- Process: Heat in NH₃ atmosphere at 500–550°C → N diffuses into surface.
- Advantage: Minimal distortion (lower temperature than carburizing).
- Limitation: Thin case depth (~0.5 mm), high cost.

### 2.2.6 Stainless Steels
- Examples: **AISI 303, 304, 316.**
- Use: Food-grade, marine, and chemical industry gears (corrosion resistance priority over strength).
- Rare in automotive power transmission.

### 2.2.7 Cast Irons
- Types: **Gray cast iron (FC250), Ductile (nodular) cast iron (FCD450), Malleable cast iron.**
- Use: Large, slow-speed gears (mill gears, mining), where damping and low cost dominate.
- Automotive relevance: Limited to timing gear covers and large bull gears.

### 2.2.8 Non-Ferrous Metals
- **Brass (Cu-Zn):** Worm gears, instrument gears (low friction, good machinability).
- **Bronze (Cu-Sn / Cu-Al):** Worm wheels, heavy sliding contact.
- **Aluminium Alloys:** Lightweight gears in aircraft & EVs for inertia reduction.

### 2.2.9 Non-Metallic (Polymer) Gears
- **Nylon (PA66, PA12):** Quiet, self-lubricating, low cost.
- **Acetal (POM / Delrin):** Low friction, high dimensional stability.
- **Phenolic resins (Bakelite):** High strength, moisture-resistant.
- **Polycarbonate, PEEK, Fibre-Reinforced Plastics:** High-performance automotive gears (e.g., electric vehicle reduction gears).

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Trend:** Electric vehicles increasingly adopt **polymer composite gears** to reduce NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) and improve efficiency.

## 2.3 Heat Treatment Roadmap for Automotive Gears

| Heat Treatment | Temperature Range | Outcome | Typical Application |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Annealing** | 550–650°C | Softens, relieves stress | Pre-machining |
| **Normalizing** | 850–900°C | Refines grain, uniform hardness | Forged blanks |
| **Hardening (Quench)** | 800–850°C → Oil | Martensitic structure | Through-hardened steels |
| **Tempering** | 150–650°C | Reduces brittleness, restores toughness | After quenching |
| **Carburizing** | 850–950°C in C-rich atm. | High C surface layer | Low-C steels (16MnCr5) |
| **Nitriding** | 500–550°C in NH₃ | Hard nitride surface (no quench) | Nitralloy |
| **Induction Hardening** | Surface only (1.5–4 mm) | Localized hardening | Spur gears, shafts |
| **Shot Peening** | Cold work surface | Compressive residual stress | All critical gears |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the backbone of gear design and material selection in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y}$ | **Lewis Bending Stress Equation** | $F_t$ = tangential load (N); $b$ = face width (mm); $m$ = module (mm); $y$ = Lewis form factor |
| 2 | $F_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot m \cdot y$ | Beam strength of gear tooth | (N) |
| 3 | $\sigma_c = C_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot d_p \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \cos\alpha}}$ | **Buckingham Contact Stress** | $C_p$ = elastic coefficient; $W_t$ = tangential load (N); $b$ = face width (mm); $d_p$ = pitch diameter (mm); $\alpha$ = pressure angle |
| 4 | $\sigma_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot \sigma_e'$ | Endurance limit with modifying factors | $k_a$ = surface factor; $k_b$ = size factor; $k_c$ = reliability factor; $\sigma_e'$ = basic endurance limit |
| 5 | $\sigma_e' = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_u$ | Empirical relation for steels | $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength |
| 6 | $C_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}\right)}}$ | Elastic coefficient for Hertzian contact | $E_1, E_2$ = Young's moduli; $\nu_1, \nu_2$ = Poisson's ratios |
| 7 | $SH = \dfrac{F_t \cdot \cos\phi \cdot K_s \cdot K_v}{b \cdot d_p \cdot y}$ | **AGMA Bending Stress (Lewis-modified)** | $K_s$ = size factor; $K_v$ = dynamic factor; $\phi$ = helix angle |
| 8 | $b = \dfrac{F_t}{\sigma_b \cdot m \cdot y}$ | Minimum face width from bending | (mm) |
| 9 | $SH_d = \dfrac{W_t \cdot K_o \cdot K_v \cdot K_s}{b \cdot d_p} \cdot \dfrac{K_m \cdot K_H}{C_p}$ | AGMA Surface Durability (pitting resistance) | $K_o, K_m, K_H$ = overload, load-distribution, hardness ratio factors |
| 10 | $HRC_{min} \approx 50 + \dfrac{HB}{10}$ | Hardness inter-conversion (approx.) | Empirical correlation |
| 11 | $S_F = \dfrac{\sigma_{FP} \cdot Y_N}{SH}$ | AGMA bending safety factor | $\sigma_{FP}$ = allowable bending stress; $Y_N$ = stress-cycle factor |
| 12 | $S_H = \dfrac{\sigma_{HP}}{SH_d}$ | AGMA pitting safety factor | $\sigma_{HP}$ = allowable contact stress |

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Automotive Engineering

The choice of gear material directly determines:
- **Power density (kW/kg)** of the transmission — lower weight gears allow higher power throughput.
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** — softer polymer gears run quieter.
- **Durability / warranty cost** — case-hardened gears can exceed 1 million fatigue cycles.
- **Manufacturability** — choice between forging, machining, hobbing, or injection moulding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** A single gear failure in a manual transmission synchronizer can disable a vehicle. Hence, OEMs over-specify gear materials using the **AGMA / DIN / ISO 6336** safety factor framework, typically demanding $S_F \ge 1.5$ and $S_H \ge 1.2$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lewis Bending Stress Equation

The Lewis Equation is the most fundamental relation linking **gear geometry**, **applied load**, and **material strength**. Its derivation assumes the gear tooth behaves as a **cantilever beam** of parabolic profile.

### Step 1: Model the gear tooth as a cantilever beam
Treat the gear tooth as a tapered cantilever fixed at the root, with the tangential load $F_t$ applied at the pitch circle.

### Step 2: Bending moment at the tooth root
The bending moment at the tooth root section is:

$$M = F_t \cdot h$$

where $h$ is the **lever arm** from the point of load application to the critical root section.

### Step 3: Section modulus at the tooth root
For a single tooth, the section modulus is:

$$Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^2}{6}$$

where $b$ is the face width and $t$ is the tooth thickness at the root (critical section).

### Step 4: Bending stress
Substituting into the flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \cdot F_t \cdot h}{b \cdot t^2}$$

### Step 5: Introduce the Lewis Form Factor
Lewis observed that for a given tooth shape, the ratio $\dfrac{6h}{t^2}$ is purely a **geometric constant**. He defined:

$$y = \dfrac{6h}{m^2}$$

where $m$ is the module. This makes $y$ dimensionless and tabulated (e.g., $y = 0.32$ for $20^\circ$ full-depth teeth with $Z = 30$).

### Step 6: Substitute to obtain the final form
Combining:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y}$$

This is the **Lewis Equation** — the foundational formula for gear design in the **KTU PBAUT604 syllabus**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Buckingham's Contact Stress Equation

Buckingham extended the **Hertzian contact theory** to cylindrical gear flanks. Two cylinders (gear teeth) in line contact are equivalent to a cylinder on a plane, with an effective radius of curvature $R_{eq}$.

### Step 1: Define equivalent radius
For two meshing cylinders (pinion and gear):

$$\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}$$

For external meshing spur gears at the pitch point:

$$R_{eq} = \dfrac{d_1 \cdot d_2}{2(d_1 + d_2)} \cdot \sin\alpha$$

### Step 2: Hertzian contact pressure
For two cylinders in contact under load $W$, the maximum contact stress (Hertz, 1881) is:

$$p_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{W \cdot E_{eq}}{\pi \cdot L \cdot R_{eq}}}$$

where $L$ is the contact length (face width $b$ for gears) and $E_{eq}$ combines the two elastic moduli.

### Step 3: Introduce the elastic coefficient $C_p$
Define:

$$C_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}\right)}}$$

Hence:

$$p_{max} = C_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{b \cdot R_{eq}}}$$

### Step 4: Apply to gear geometry
Substituting $R_{eq} = \dfrac{d_p \sin\alpha}{2 \cos\alpha}$ for the pitch cylinder (where $d_p$ is the pitch diameter of the pinion) and using the load $W_t$:

$$\sigma_c = C_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot d_p \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \cos\alpha}}$$

This is **Buckingham's Contact Stress Equation**, governing **pitting resistance** in gear materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Matters for Materials:** Materials with **higher surface hardness** (case-hardened steels: $HRC \approx 60$) tolerate much greater $\sigma_c$ than through-hardened or nitrided steels, hence their dominance in automotive applications.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Endurance Limit from Material Data

The basic endurance limit for a steel rotating beam specimen is empirically related to its ultimate tensile strength:

$$\sigma_e' = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_u \quad \text{for } \sigma_u \le 1400 \text{ MPa}$$

For higher-strength steels, the relation flattens:

$$\sigma_e' = 700 \text{ MPa (capped value for steels)}$$

### Step-by-step derivation of the modified endurance limit:
For a real gear tooth (notched, surface-finished, scaled in size, operating at elevated temperature), modify using **modifying factors**:

$$\sigma_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot \sigma_e'$$

- $k_a$ = surface factor (accounts for surface finish; $k_a \le 1$).
- $k_b$ = size factor (for $d > 50$ mm, $k_b < 1$).
- $k_c$ = load factor (bending vs. torsion vs. combined).
- $k_d$ = temperature factor (for $T > 100°C$).

### Worked Example: Calculate Modified Endurance Limit
**Given:** AISI 4140 steel gear, $\sigma_u = 1100$ MPa, ground surface, diameter $d = 80$ mm, bending load.

**Step 1:** Compute basic endurance limit.

$$\sigma_e' = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_u = 0.5 \cdot 1100 = 550 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply surface factor. For ground surface ($\sigma_u = 1100$ MPa):

$$k_a \approx 0.88$$

**Step 3:** Apply size factor (from Marin / AGMA tables, $d = 80$ mm):

$$k_b \approx 0.85$$

**Step 4:** Apply load factor for bending:

$$k_c = 1.0$$

**Step 5:** Compute modified endurance limit.

$$\sigma_e = 0.88 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 550 = 411 \text{ MPa}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The actual gear tooth endurance strength is **411 MPa**, significantly lower than the ideal 550 MPa due to size and surface effects — this is why KTU design problems require modifying factors.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Material Selection for Automotive Gears

| Service Condition | Recommended Material | Heat Treatment | Surface Hardness | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **High-speed, heavy-load transmission** | 16MnCr5 / 20MnCr5 | Case-hardened | 58–62 HRC | Passenger car gearbox |
| **Hypoid ring & pinion (rear axle)** | 16MnCr5 / 9310 | Case-hardened | 60–63 HRC | Differential gears |
| **Synchronizer hubs & sleeves** | Brass / Bronze | None | 80–120 HB | Manual gearbox synchromesh |
| **Low-speed, high-torque (truck gearbox)** | EN24 / AISI 4340 | Quenched + tempered | 35–45 HRC | Heavy commercial gears |
| **Timing gears (engine)** | Sintered steel / Cast iron | Induction / normalized | 40–50 HRC | Cam-shaft drive |
| **Worm & wheel pair** | Worm: hardened steel; Wheel: Bronze (Cu-Sn) | Worm: induction; wheel: as-cast | 250 HB | Steering, lifts |
| **Electric vehicle reduction (NVH)** | PEEK, PA66 + glass fibre | None | 95 Shore D | EV single-speed reducer |
| **Light-duty instrument gears** | Acetal (POM) / Nylon 66 | None | 80–95 Shore D | Speedometer, instrument cluster |

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation — Module-to-Strength Link

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The module $m$ is a geometric property, but the strength it conveys is purely **material-dependent** through the Lewis form factor $y$ and allowable stress $\sigma_b$.

For two gears of the **same module** but different materials:

$$\dfrac{F_{t,1}}{F_{t,2}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{b,1}}{\sigma_{b,2}}$$

Hence, replacing a **carbon-steel gear** ($\sigma_b = 150$ MPa) with an **alloy-steel gear** ($\sigma_b = 250$ MPa) increases the load capacity by:

$$\dfrac{250}{150} = 1.667 \implies \textbf{66.7\% increase in load capacity}$$

without changing the gear dimensions. This is why **material selection** alone can dramatically shrink the transmission envelope.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Gear Materials

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["GEAR MATERIALS"] --> B["METALLIC"]
    A --> C["NON-METALLIC"]

    B --> B1["FERROUS STEELS"]
    B --> B2["CAST IRONS"]
    B --> B3["NON FERROUS METALS"]

    B1 --> D1["PLAIN CARBON STEELS"]
    B1 --> D2["ALLOY STEELS"]
    B1 --> D3["STAINLESS STEELS"]
    B1 --> D4["CASE HARDENING STEELS"]
    B1 --> D5["NITRIDING STEELS"]

    D1 --> E1["AISI 1040 C45 C50"]
    D2 --> E2["AISI 4140 4340 EN19 EN24"]
    D3 --> E3["AISI 303 304 316"]
    D4 --> E4["16MnCr5 20MnCr5 AISI 8620 9310"]
    D5 --> E5["AISI 4140 Nitralloy 135"]

    B2 --> F1["GRAY CAST IRON FC250"]
    B2 --> F2["DUCTILE IRON FCD450"]
    B2 --> F3["MALLEABLE IRON"]

    B3 --> G1["BRASS Cu Zn"]
    B3 --> G2["BRONZE Cu Sn"]
    B3 --> G3["ALUMINIUM ALLOYS"]

    C --> H1["THERMOPLASTICS"]
    C --> H2["THERMOSETS"]
    C --> H3["COMPOSITES"]

    H1 --> I1["NYLON PA66 PA12"]
    H1 --> I2["ACETAL POM DELRIN"]
    H1 --> I3["POLYCARBONATE PEEK"]

```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Material Selection for Automotive Gears

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START: Define Operating Conditions"] --> S2["Identify Service Loads"]
    S2 --> S3["Determine Tooth Bending Stress via Lewis Equation"]
    S2 --> S4["Determine Contact Stress via Buckingham Equation"]
    S3 --> S5["Compute Required Surface Hardness HRC"]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6["Select Material Family"]
    S6 --> A1["Case Hardening Steel"]
    S6 --> A2["Through Hardening Steel"]
    S6 --> A3["Nitriding Steel"]
    S6 --> A4["Bronze or Brass"]
    S6 --> A5["Polymer Composite"]
    A1 --> B1["16MnCr5 20MnCr5"]
    A2 --> B2["AISI 4140 AISI 4340 EN24"]
    A3 --> B3["Nitralloy 135"]
    A4 --> B4["Cu Sn12 Cu Sn10"]
    A5 --> B5["PA66 GF POM PEEK"]
    B1 --> C1["Carburize Quench Temper"]
    B2 --> C2["Quench Temper"]
    B3 --> C3["Nitride at 520C"]
    B4 --> C4["As Cast No Heat Treatment"]
    B5 --> C5["Injection Mould No Heat Treatment"]
    C1 --> D1["Verify via AGMA Safety Factors SF and SH"]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["END: Final Material Specified"]
```

## 4.3 Case-Hardening Process Sequence (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Raw Material Low C Steel 16MnCr5"] --> P2["Pre Machining Rough Tooth Profile"]
    P2 --> P3["Carburizing 900C in CO CO2 H2 Atmosphere"]
    P3 --> P4["Diffusion Carbon Enters Surface to 1 to 2 mm Depth"]
    P4 --> P5["Quenching in Oil Direct from Furnace"]
    P5 --> P6["Tempering at 180C for Toughness"]
    P6 --> P7["Final Grinding to AGMA ISO Quality Class"]
    P7 --> P8["Finished Gear 60 to 62 HRC Surface 35 to 40 HRC Core"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Linking Gear Stresses to Material Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["LOAD F_t Torque RPM"] --> L1["Bending Stress at Tooth Root"]
    L --> L2["Contact Stress on Tooth Flank"]
    L1 --> M1["Material Property: Endurance Limit sigma_e"]
    L2 --> M2["Material Property: Surface Hardness HRC"]
    M1 --> N1["Lewis Equation Verifies Tooth Strength"]
    M2 --> N2["Buckingham Equation Verifies Pitting Resistance"]
    N1 --> O["Material Selection Decision"]
    N2 --> O
    O --> P["Choose Case Hardened Steel for High Loads"]
    O --> Q["Choose Bronze for Worm Wheels"]
    O --> R["Choose Polymer for NVH Reduction"]
```
