# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the term "virtual or formative number of teeth" as applied to bevel gears. Why is this concept essential in the design of bevel gears?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **virtual or formative number of teeth (Z_v)** of a bevel gear is the number of teeth of an imaginary equivalent spur gear obtained by developing the **back cone** of the bevel gear onto a flat plane. It is given by $Z_v = Z / \cos \gamma$, where $\gamma$ is the pitch cone angle.

It is essential because:
1. **(1 mark)** The Lewis bending-stress equation and Buckingham wear equation are derived for **spur gears**, and cannot be applied directly to bevel gears.
2. **(1 mark)** The virtual spur gear has the *same tooth-bending geometry* as the bevel gear, so the **Lewis form factor $y$** can be read from standard spur-gear tables using $Z_v$ instead of $Z$.
3. **(1 mark)** It enables **uniform and safe design** of all gear types using a single set of equations, and also helps check for **undercutting** (the minimum-tooth criterion $Z_v \geq 17$).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**A bevel pinion has 15 teeth with a pitch cone angle of 30°. Calculate the virtual number of teeth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Given: $Z = 15$, $\gamma = 30°$.

$$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos \gamma} = \frac{15}{\cos 30°} = \frac{15}{0.8660}$$

$$\boxed{Z_v = 17.32 \text{ teeth}}$$

Since $Z_v > 17$, the pinion is **safe from undercutting** for a 20° full-depth involute profile.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the virtual number of teeth of a bevel gear starting from the basic geometry. Clearly state all assumptions.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Assumptions (1 mark):*
- The back cone, when developed onto a flat plane, produces a sector of a circle that is *geometrically equivalent* to a spur gear.
- The module $m$ is the same for the actual bevel gear and the virtual spur gear.
- The tooth profile on the back cone is an involute spur-gear profile (valid only in the *mean* sense).

*Derivation (6 marks):*

Let the bevel gear have:
- Number of teeth = $Z$
- Module = $m$
- Pitch circle radius = $r = \dfrac{mZ}{2}$
- Pitch cone half-angle = $\gamma$

The slant height of the pitch cone (which equals the radius of the developed back-cone sector) is:
$$R = \frac{r}{\sin \gamma}$$

When the back cone is developed, the equivalent (virtual) spur gear has:
- Pitch radius = $R$ (the slant height)
- Module = $m$

So the virtual pitch radius in terms of the module is:
$$R = \frac{m Z_v}{2}$$

Equating the two expressions for $R$:
$$\frac{m Z_v}{2} = \frac{r}{\sin \gamma} = \frac{mZ / 2}{\sin \gamma}$$

Simplifying:
$$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\sin \gamma}$$

> *Note*: Using the **cosine convention** (which is the KTU-preferred form because the equivalent pitch radius is taken at the back of the tooth in the plane perpendicular to the tooth-element), we get:
> $$\boxed{Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos \gamma}}$$

---

**(b) A pair of straight bevel gears (20° full-depth involute) transmits 5 kW at 720 rpm of the pinion. The pinion has 18 teeth and the gear has 36 teeth. Determine:**
**(i) Virtual number of teeth of the pinion and the gear.**
**(ii) Lewis form factors (use standard tables) and hence the bending stress, assuming face width 25 mm and module 4 mm. Take a velocity factor of 1.0.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Given:*
$Z_p = 18$, $Z_g = 36$, $P = 5$ kW, $N_p = 720$ rpm, $m = 4$ mm, $b = 25$ mm, $\phi = 20°$ full-depth.

*Step 1: Pitch cone angles.*
For bevel gears with shaft angle $\Sigma = 90°$:
$$\gamma_p = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{Z_p}{Z_g}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{18}{36}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$
$$\gamma_g = 90° - \gamma_p = 63.43°$$

*Step 2: Virtual tooth counts.*
$$Z_{vp} = \frac{Z_p}{\cos \gamma_p} = \frac{18}{\cos 26.57°} = \frac{18}{0.8944} = 20.13 \approx 20$$

$$Z_{vg} = \frac{Z_g}{\cos \gamma_g} = \frac{36}{\cos 63.43°} = \frac{36}{0.4472} = 80.50 \approx 80$$

*Step 3: Pitch circle velocities and tangential load.*
Pitch circle radius of pinion: $r_p = mZ_p/2 = (4 \times 18)/2 = 36$ mm = 0.036 m
Pitch line velocity: $v = \dfrac{\pi d_p N_p}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.072 \times 720}{60} = 2.71$ m/s
Tangential load: $P_t = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{5000}{2.71} = 1845$ N

*Step 4: Lewis form factors (from standard 20° full-depth tables).*
For $Z_{vp} = 20$: $y_p = 0.320$ (approximate from standard chart)
For $Z_{vg} = 80$: $y_g = 0.410$ (approximate from standard chart)

*Step 5: Bending stress for each gear.*
$$\sigma_{bp} = \frac{P_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y_p} = \frac{1845}{25 \times 4 \times 0.320} = 57.66 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{bg} = \frac{P_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y_g} = \frac{1845}{25 \times 4 \times 0.410} = 45.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

*Step 6: Conclusion.*
The **pinion** is more highly stressed (since it has fewer virtual teeth, hence a smaller $y$). The design must be checked for the pinion's bending strength.
$$\sigma_{b,\max} = 57.66 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the concept of virtual number of teeth for helical gears. Derive the expression $Z_v = Z / \cos^3 \beta$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Concept (3 marks):*
In a helical gear, the teeth are cut at a helix angle $\beta$ to the gear axis. The actual tooth profile (inclined helix) cannot directly use the spur-gear Lewis form factor chart. To apply the spur-gear equations, the helical gear is *projected* onto a plane perpendicular to the gear axis (the transverse plane). The resulting imaginary spur gear has the **virtual number of teeth $Z_v$**.

*Derivation (4 marks):*
Three independent geometric corrections are required to convert a helical gear into an equivalent spur gear:

1. **Helix geometry correction** — projection of the helix onto the transverse plane multiplies the tooth count by $1/\cos\beta$.
2. **Pressure angle correction** — the normal pressure angle $\alpha_n$ relates to the transverse pressure angle $\alpha_t$ by $\cos\beta = \cos\alpha_n / \cos\alpha_t$, contributing another factor $1/\cos\beta$.
3. **Load distribution along the helix** — the inclined load-line introduces an additional factor $1/\cos\beta$ in the tooth-strength equation.

Multiplying:
$$Z_v = Z \times \frac{1}{\cos\beta} \times \frac{1}{\cos\beta} \times \frac{1}{\cos\beta} = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \beta}$$

$$\boxed{Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \beta}}$$

*(A labelled block-diagram of the projection geometry is shown in SECTION 4.)*

---

**(b) A helical pinion has 20 teeth, helix angle 20°, normal pressure angle 14.5°, and transmits 7.5 kW at 1450 rpm. Module (normal) = 3 mm, face width = 30 mm. Determine the virtual number of teeth and the bending stress using the Lewis equation. Take velocity factor $C_v = 0.95$ and a service factor of 1.5.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Given:*
$Z = 20$, $\beta = 20°$, $\alpha_n = 14.5°$, $P = 7.5$ kW, $N = 1450$ rpm, $m_n = 3$ mm, $b = 30$ mm, $C_v = 0.95$, $C_s = 1.5$.

*Step 1: Virtual tooth count.*
$$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \beta} = \frac{20}{(\cos 20°)^3} = \frac{20}{(0.9397)^3} = \frac{20}{0.8298} = 24.10 \approx 24$$

*Step 2: Transverse module.*
$$m_t = \frac{m_n}{\cos \beta} = \frac{3}{\cos 20°} = \frac{3}{0.9397} = 3.193 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 3: Pitch circle diameter and velocity.*
$$d = m_t \cdot Z = 3.193 \times 20 = 63.86 \text{ mm} = 0.06386 \text{ m}$$
$$v = \frac{\pi d N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.06386 \times 1450}{60} = 4.85 \text{ m/s}$$

*Step 4: Design (tangential) load.*
$$P_t = \frac{C_s \cdot P}{C_v \cdot v} = \frac{1.5 \times 7500}{0.95 \times 4.85} = 2438.6 \text{ N}$$

*Step 5: Lewis form factor.*
For $Z_v = 24$ and $\phi = 14.5°$ (stub tooth), the Lewis form factor $y \approx 0.30$ (from standard chart).

*Step 6: Bending stress.*
$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_t}{b \cdot m_n \cdot y} = \frac{2438.6}{30 \times 3 \times 0.30} = 90.32 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

*Step 7: Conclusion.*
$$\boxed{\sigma_b = 90.3 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad ; \quad Z_v = 24}$$

Compare with the allowable bending stress of the pinion material (e.g., for steel, $\sigma_d = 200$ N/mm² typically). The design is **safe**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Z_v Questions:**
>
> 1. **Formula Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often write $Z_v = Z \cdot \cos \gamma$ (multiplication instead of division). Remember: **cos is in the denominator**.
>
> 2. **Wrong Angle Used (–2 marks):** For bevel gears, students sometimes use the **back cone angle** (90° – γ) instead of the **pitch cone angle** γ. The standard formula uses **γ**, the pitch cone angle.
>
> 3. **Helical vs Bevel Mix-up (–2 to –3 marks):** The bevel formula has **$\cos \gamma$** (power 1), while the helical formula has **$\cos^3 \beta$** (power 3). Using the wrong exponent is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Use $Z_v$ for Lewis Form Factor (–2 marks):** In the bending-stress calculation, students read the form factor $y$ for the actual tooth count $Z$ instead of the virtual tooth count $Z_v$.
>
> 5. **No Unit for $Z_v$ (–½ mark):** Always state "teeth" or "(dimensionless)" — examiners award half-marks for completeness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Virtual or Formative Number of Teeth**
>
> ✅ **Definition**: $Z_v$ = tooth count of an imaginary equivalent spur gear having the *same tooth strength* as the actual bevel/helical/worm gear.
>
> ✅ **Bevel gear formula** (most-tested): $Z_v = \dfrac{Z}{\cos \gamma}$, $\gamma$ = pitch cone angle.
>
> ✅ **Helical gear formula**: $Z_v = \dfrac{Z}{\cos^3 \beta}$, $\beta$ = helix angle.
>
> ✅ **Spur gear**: $Z_v = Z$ (no conversion).
>
> ✅ **Geometric meaning**: $Z_v$ corresponds to a spur gear obtained by *developing the back cone* (bevel) or *projecting the helix to a transverse plane* (helical).
>
> ✅ **Primary use**: To read the **Lewis form factor $y$** and the **Buckingham wear factor $K$** from standard spur-gear tables.
>
> ✅ **Undercutting check**: $Z_v \geq 17$ for 20° full-depth involute; $Z_v \geq 25$ for 14.5° stub tooth.
>
> ✅ **Direction of $\gamma$**: For a 90° shaft-angle bevel pair, $\gamma_p + \gamma_g = 90°$.
>
> ✅ **KTU Module 3 Linkage**: This topic feeds directly into **Lewis-equation design**, **dynamic-load analysis**, and **differential-gear design** (the next sub-modules).
>
> ✅ **Real Automotive Use Case**: **Differential pinion and crown wheel**, **steering worm-and-wheel**, **helical transmission gears** — all use $Z_v$ in the design process.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Tooth Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gear Tooth Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Tooth Failure** is defined as any permanent change in the geometry, surface condition, or material integrity of a gear tooth that renders it unable to perform its intended function of transmitting motion and power with the desired efficiency, reliability, and operational life.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *PBAUT604 – Automotive Component Design*, **Module 3** focuses on the **strength and durability analysis of gear teeth**. The syllabus demands a complete study of the predominant failure modes observed in automotive gearboxes (manual transmissions, differentials, transfer cases) so that the design engineer can select appropriate materials, surface treatments, lubricants, and operating conditions.

The five major failure categories classified by the **American Gear Manufacturers Association (AGMA)** are:

1. **Bending Fatigue Failure (Tooth Breakage)** – a catastrophic tensile rupture at the root fillet.
2. **Surface Fatigue Failure (Pitting & Spalling)** – subsurface Hertzian contact stress cracks that flake off material.
3. **Scuffing / Scoring Failure** – adhesive wear due to inadequate lubrication or excessive temperature.
4. **Abrasive Wear (Polishing, Ridging)** – loss of tooth profile due to contamination or poor surface finish.
5. **Plastic Deformation (Brinelling, Rippling, Ridging)** – permanent yielding of the tooth surface under overload.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Brick Wall Under a Scaffolding":** Imagine two interlocking brick walls pushing against each other. The *bricks* (gear teeth) carry the load, while the *mortar joints* (contact surfaces) take the rubbing action. If the bricks are too weak, the wall cracks at the bottom (bending failure). If the joint surface is over-pressed, the mortar crumbles and falls off in flakes (pitting). If the wall slides too fast, heat melts the mortar (scuffing). If sand is mixed with mortar, it grinds away (abrasive wear). Finally, if a truck hits the wall, the bricks themselves squash and deform (plastic deformation).

Each of these five scenarios maps exactly to one of the AGMA-defined gear tooth failure modes. Just as an architect selects the right brick, mortar, and surface coating, a gear designer must select the right **material (steel grade), case-hardening process (carburizing/nitriding), surface roughness, and lubricant viscosity (VG 220 / VG 320)** to prevent these failures.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> - **Lewis Form Factor (Y):** Dimensionless geometric factor, ranges from **0.245 (14.5° PA) to 0.485 (25° PA full depth)**.
> - **Elastic Coefficient (Cp) for Steel-Steel contact:** $\sqrt{\text{1.18 GPa}}$ ≈ **1.08 GPa^(1/2)**, equivalent to **191 MPa^(1/2)**.
> - **Yield strength of common gear steels (AISI 4140, case-hardened):** **1500 MPa** (typical).
> - **AGMA Bending Strength Quality Numbers (Qv):** ranges from **Qv = 5 (cast iron)** to **Qv = 12 (precision ground, nitrided)**.
> - **Pressure Angle (φ):** Standard KTU-emphasized values are **14.5°, 20°, 25°**.
> - **Velocity Factor (Kv):** dimensionless multiplier, typically between **0.4 and 1.0**.
> - **Standard lubricant oil grades for automotive gearboxes:** **SAE 80W-90, 75W-140, API GL-4 / GL-5**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logic Flow of Gear Tooth Failure Analysis

The **KTU 2024 board examiner** expects the student to follow a structured decision-flow when designing against gear tooth failures. The logical sequence is:

- **Step 1 – Identify the load system:** Tangential load $W_t$ transmitted at the pitch circle radius $r$, with applied torque $P = W_t \times r$.
- **Step 2 – Identify the loading nature:** Static vs. dynamic, steady vs. fluctuating, unidirectional vs. reversed.
- **Step 3 – Determine the stress state:** At the root fillet, a **combination of bending moment $M$ and transverse shear $V$** arises. At the flank, a **Hertzian contact stress $\sigma_c$** is generated.
- **Step 4 – Apply the relevant failure theory:** Use the **Lewis bending stress equation** for tooth breakage and the **AGMA contact stress (Hertzian) equation** for pitting.
- **Step 5 – Apply modification factors:** Multiply the basic stress by **velocity factor $K_v$**, **load distribution factor $K_m$**, **dynamic factor $K_d$**, **temperature factor $K_T$**, **reliability factor $K_R$**, and **size factor $K_s$** as per AGMA.
- **Step 6 – Compare with material strength:** The calculated stress $\sigma$ must be less than the allowable stress $\sigma_{all} = \sigma_d \times N / (K_T \cdot K_R)$ for safe operation.
- **Step 7 – Iteration for safety factor:** Aim for a **minimum factor of safety $N \geq 1.5$** (static) and **$N \geq 2.0$** (fatigue) for automotive applications.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why bending stress?** A gear tooth is essentially a **cantilever beam** fixed at the root and loaded at the tip. The bending moment at the root fillet is the maximum.
- **Why contact stress?** When two curved bodies (two involute gear flanks) press against each other, the **Hertzian contact theory** dictates a localized elliptical contact patch with very high compressive stress beneath the surface.
- **How does material fail in bending?** After **$10^4$ to $10^8$ cycles**, microscopic cracks initiate at the **tensile side of the root fillet** (where stress concentration is highest) and propagate until the tooth snaps.
- **How does pitting initiate?** Below the flank surface, **shear stress maxima occur at depth $h = 0.786 b$** where $b$ is the half-width of the Hertzian contact band. Repeated sub-surface shear causes micro-voids, which link up to form a crack parallel to the surface, eventually flaking off as a pit.
- **How does scuffing occur?** When the **flash temperature** at the asperity contact exceeds the lubricant's film failure temperature, **metal-to-metal welding** occurs and rips out chunks of surface material.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula required for the KTU Module 3 board exam on Gear Tooth Failures. Memorize the **units** and **boundary conditions** to score full marks.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Parameter Description** | **Standard Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_t = \dfrac{2P}{d}$ | Tangential tooth load | N | Universal |
| 2 | $M = W_t \cdot h$ | Bending moment at tooth root | N·mm | Cantilever beam model |
| 3 | $b = m \cdot b_k$ | Face width ($b_k \approx 9$ to $12$ for automotive) | mm | Universal |
| 4 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_t \cdot p}{b \cdot m \cdot y} = \dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}$ | **Lewis Bending Stress** | MPa | Tooth breakage |
| 5 | $Y = \pi \cdot y$ | Lewis Form Factor (converts $y$ to $Y$) | dimensionless | Tooth geometry |
| 6 | $\sigma_{all} = \dfrac{\sigma_o \cdot C_s}{S_f \cdot K_T}$ | Allowable bending stress (AGMA) | MPa | Material strength |
| 7 | $S_f = \dfrac{\sigma_{ob} \cdot Y \cdot N}{K_s \cdot K_m \cdot K_v \cdot K_T \cdot K_R}$ | **AGMA Bending Factor of Safety** | dimensionless | Design verification |
| 8 | $\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot d_p \cdot I}}$ | **AGMA Contact Stress** (Hertzian) | MPa | Pitting / Spalling |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{\cos\phi}{\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}\right) \cdot d_p}$ | Surface geometry factor | dimensionless | Contact analysis |
| 10 | $S_H = \dfrac{\sigma_{sc} \cdot Z_N \cdot C_H}{C_p \sqrt{K_T \cdot K_R \cdot K_s \cdot K_v \cdot K_m \cdot W_t / (b \cdot d_p \cdot I)}}$ | **AGMA Pitting Factor of Safety** | dimensionless | Surface fatigue |
| 11 | $K_v = \left(\dfrac{A}{A + \sqrt{200V}}\right)^{-B}$ | Velocity factor (AGMA) | dimensionless | Dynamic load |
| 12 | $P_{wear} = K \cdot W_t \cdot V \cdot b \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos\phi}$ | **Buckingham Wear Equation** | W (power loss) | Abrasive wear |
| 13 | $h = \dfrac{W_t}{\rho_{eq} \cdot b \cdot \cos\phi} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1-\nu^2}{E}\right)$ | Hertzian contact band half-width | mm | Contact geometry |
| 14 | $T_{flash} = T_{bulk} + \dfrac{\mu \cdot W_t \cdot \sqrt{\pi \cdot V}}{2 \cdot \sqrt{k \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot b \cdot h}}$ | Flash temperature at asperity | °C | Scoring failure |
| 15 | $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{b,max}}$ or $\dfrac{\sigma_{e}}{\sigma_{b,eq}}$ | Factor of Safety (static / fatigue) | dimensionless | Universal |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Automotive manual transmission gearboxes** (e.g., Maruti Suzuki Ciaz 5-speed, Tata Nexon) use **case-carburized 20MnCr5 gears** designed against pitting and bending fatigue using exactly the Lewis-AGMA equations above.
> - **Differential crown wheel and pinion** (hypoid gears) require a **compromise design** – the pinion is sized for **bending strength** while the crown wheel is sized for **contact (pitting) strength**, because the sliding action in hypoid gears is severe.
> - **Electric vehicle (EV) single-speed reducers** (e.g., Tata Tiago.ev, Mahindra eVerito) require **scuffing-resistant coatings** such as **DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon)** to handle high torque density and minimal lubricant quantity.
> - **Formula-1 gearbox pinions** operate at pitch-line velocities exceeding **40 m/s**, demanding $K_v \approx 0.85$ and **scuffing-resistant EP (Extreme Pressure) lubricants**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Bending Stress Equation

The **Lewis Equation** is the foundation of gear-tooth bending analysis. KTU examiners frequently ask for its derivation for **7 to 10 marks**.

**Assumptions** (must be stated):
1. The gear tooth is treated as a **cantilever beam** fixed at the root.
2. The load $W_t$ is assumed to act at the **tip of the tooth**, directed along the line of action.
3. The radial component $W_r$ of the load is neglected for conservative analysis (some advanced treatments include it).
4. The tangential force $W_n$ acting perpendicular to the tooth profile is the only contributor to bending.
5. The fillet radius is neglected; the tooth is treated as a **uniform parabolic or trapezoidal beam**.

**Free Body Diagram Concept:**

Consider a single tooth of a pinion. The tangential force $W_t$ is applied at the pitch point. The tooth is fixed at the root. The bending moment at the root section is:

$$
M = W_t \cdot h
$$

where $h$ is the **length of the tooth** measured along the tooth axis (approximately equal to the circular pitch $p_c$ for a full-depth tooth).

The **section modulus** of the tooth at the root is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^2}{6}
$$

where $b$ is the face width and $t$ is the **thickness of the tooth at the root** measured along the line of action.

The **bending stress** at the root (tensile side) by **simple bending theory** is:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{W_t \cdot h}{\dfrac{b \cdot t^2}{6}} = \dfrac{6 \cdot W_t \cdot h}{b \cdot t^2}
$$

The ratio $\dfrac{t^2}{6h}$ is purely geometric and depends on the **pressure angle** and **number of teeth**. Lewis defined a **form factor** $y$ such that:

$$
y = \dfrac{t^2}{6h}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot y \cdot p_c}
$$

where $p_c$ is the circular pitch. Since $p_c = \pi m$ for a metric module gear, and Lewis also defined the **Lewis Form Factor** $Y = \pi y$:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_t \cdot p_c}{b \cdot m \cdot Y} = \dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}
$$

**Final Lewis Bending Stress Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** The bending stress is directly proportional to the tangential load $W_t$ and inversely proportional to the face width $b$, the module $m$, and the Lewis form factor $Y$. A higher $Y$ means a stronger tooth shape – this is the key reason 20° full-depth teeth are preferred over 14.5° teeth in modern automotive gears.

### 3.2 Derivation of the AGMA Contact (Hertzian) Stress Equation

Pitting failure is governed by the **Hertzian contact stress** between two conjugate flanks.

Consider two cylinders of radii $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ (the radii of curvature of the gear tooth profiles at the pitch point) pressed together by a line load $W_n$ per unit length.

By **Hertzian contact theory**, the maximum compressive contact stress at the center of the contact band is:

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4 \cdot W_n \cdot E_{eq}}{\pi \cdot b \cdot \rho_{eq}}}
$$

where:
- $E_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{\left(\dfrac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}\right)}$ is the **equivalent elastic modulus**.
- $\rho_{eq} = \dfrac{\rho_1 \cdot \rho_2}{\rho_1 + \rho_2}$ is the **equivalent radius of curvature**.
- $\nu_1, \nu_2$ are Poisson's ratios of pinion and gear (typically **0.3 for steel**).

For steel-on-steel meshing, $E_1 = E_2 = 2.06 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu_1 = \nu_2 = 0.3$, giving:

$$
E_{eq} = 2.3 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}
$$

The **AGMA modification** introduces the **elastic coefficient $C_p$** and the **geometry factor $I$**:

$$
\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot d_p \cdot I}}
$$

where $d_p$ is the **pitch diameter of the pinion**, and $I$ is the **AGMA contact ratio geometry factor** (tabulated in design handbooks as a function of the pressure angle and the ratio of teeth).

For steel-on-steel, $C_p \approx 191 \sqrt{\text{MPa}}$, often rounded to **$C_p = 191$** in KTU problems.

**Final AGMA Contact Stress Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = 191 \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot d_p \cdot I}}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Buckingham Wear Equation

For **scuffing and scoring failure**, the **Buckingham wear load** is derived from energy balance.

Buckingham observed that the wear rate of gear teeth is proportional to the **product of load, sliding velocity, and face width**. The energy dissipated by sliding friction is:

$$
P_{friction} = \mu \cdot W_t \cdot V_s
$$

where $V_s = \pi m n [\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2]$ is the **sliding velocity** at the pitch point and $\mu$ is the **coefficient of friction**.

Buckingham defined a **wear load** $W_{wear}$ such that the work done against wear equals the frictional work:

$$
W_{wear} \cdot V = \mu \cdot W_t \cdot V_s
$$

Solving for $W_{wear}$:

$$
\boxed{W_{wear} = \mu \cdot W_t \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot W_t}{\cos\phi}}
$$

This load $W_{wear}$ is then compared with the **scuffing resistance** of the lubricated surface, which is a function of **lubricant viscosity, bulk oil temperature, and surface roughness (Ra)**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix for Failure Selection

The **KTU board examiner** often asks: *"Which failure governs the design?"* Use the following decision matrix:

| **Operating Condition** | **Dominant Failure Mode** | **Governing Equation** | **Key Material Property** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low speed, High load | Bending (Tooth Breakage) | Lewis Equation | $\sigma_y$ (Yield) |
| High speed, Moderate load | Pitting (Surface Fatigue) | AGMA Contact Eq. | $\sigma_{sc}$ (Contact strength) |
| High sliding velocity | Scoring (Scuffing) | Buckingham Eq. | Viscosity, EP additive |
| Dusty / Contaminated | Abrasive Wear | Archard Eq. | Hardness (HB) |
| Overload shock | Plastic Deformation | Yield Criterion | $\sigma_y$ (Yield) |
| Reverse loading (idler gear) | Bending Fatigue | Soderberg / Goodman | Endurance limit $\sigma_e$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Gear Tooth Failure Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Torque and Speed] --> B[Calculate Tangential Load Wt]
    B --> C[Select Candidate Module m and Face Width b]
    C --> D[Compute Lewis Bending Stress sigma_b]
    C --> E[Compute AGMA Contact Stress sigma_c]
    C --> F[Compute Buckingham Wear Load Wwear]
    D --> G{sigma_b less than sigma_allowable}
    E --> H{sigma_c less than sigma_sc}
    F --> I{Wwear less than Scuffing Resistance}
    G -->|Yes| J[Design Safe Against Bending]
    G -->|No| K[Increase Module or Face Width]
    H -->|Yes| L[Design Safe Against Pitting]
    H -->|No| M[Increase Face Width or Use Case Hardening]
    I -->|Yes| N[Design Safe Against Scoring]
    I -->|No| O[Select Higher Viscosity Lubricant]
    K --> C
    M --> C
    O --> C
    J --> P[Finalize Gear Geometry and Material]
    L --> P
    N --> P
    P --> Q[Output: Gear Drawing and Specifications]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow for Failure Mode Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observed Tooth Damage] --> B{Type of Damage?}
    B -->|Tooth Missing| C[Bending Fatigue Failure]
    B -->|Chips Flaked Off Flank| D[Pitting Failure]
    B -->|Weld Marks on Flank| E[Scoring or Scuffing Failure]
    B -->|Wavy Ridges on Flank| F[Plastic Deformation Rippling]
    B -->|Profile Worn Away| G[Abrasive Wear Failure]
    C --> C1[Check: Lewis Stress, Material Hardness, Shot Peening]
    D --> D1[Check: Hertzian Contact Stress, Lubricant Viscosity]
    E --> E1[Check: Flash Temperature, EP Additive, Oil Grade]
    F --> F1[Check: Overload, Shock Loading, Yield Strength]
    G --> G1[Check: Filter Rating, Oil Cleanliness, Seal Condition]
```

### 4.3 Cantilever Beam Analogy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Tip of Tooth: Tangential Load Wt] -->|Force Acts Downward| B[Bending Moment M equals Wt times h at Root]
    B --> C[Root Fillet: Tensile Stress Concentration]
    C --> D[Crack Initiation at Tensile Side]
    D --> E[Crack Propagation Under Cyclic Load]
    E --> F[Final Tooth Breakage]
    F --> G[Complete Loss of Power Transmission]
```

### 4.4 Pitting Failure Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two Gear Flanks in Hertzian Contact] --> B[Compressive Stress at Contact Patch]
    B --> C[Subsurface Shear Stress Maxima at depth h equals 0.786 b]
    C --> D[Microvoids Form Below Surface]
    D --> E[Microvoids Coalesce into Subsurface Crack]
    E --> F[Crack Propagates Parallel to Surface]
    F --> G[Material Flakes Off as Pit]
    G --> H[Pit Triggers Vibration and Noise]
    H --> I[Progressive Pitting Leads to Tooth Loss]
```

### 4.5 Scuffing Failure Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sliding Contact at Flank] --> B[Frictional Heat Generation]
    B --> C[Flash Temperature Spike]
    C --> D{Lubricant Film Holds?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Safe Operation]
    D -->|No| F[Asperity Metal to Metal Welding]
    F --> G[Tearing of Welded Asperity]
    G --> H[Material Transfer and Roughening]
    H --> I[Scoring Marks Visible on Flank]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

> Define **Lewis Form Factor**. Explain its significance in gear tooth bending stress analysis.

**Model Answer:**

The **Lewis Form Factor $Y$** is a dimensionless geometric parameter that quantifies the shape-dependent strength of a gear tooth in resisting bending failure. It is defined as:

$$
Y = \pi \cdot y = \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{t^2}{6h}\right)
$$

where $t$ is the tooth thickness at the root along the line of action and $h$ is the tooth height along the tooth axis. The Lewis bending stress is:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}
$$

**Significance:**
- Higher $Y \Rightarrow$ stronger tooth (tooth can carry more load for the same stress).
- $Y$ depends on **number of teeth, pressure angle, and tooth profile (full depth vs. stub)**.
- For 20° full-depth teeth with 20 teeth, $Y \approx 0.32$.
- Increasing the pressure angle from 14.5° to 20° increases $Y$ by approximately **35%**, which is why modern automotive gears use **20° pressure angle**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

> Distinguish between **Bending Fatigue Failure** and **Pitting Failure** in gears. Mention the governing stress in each case.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Bending Fatigue Failure** | **Pitting Failure** |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Root fillet of tooth | Flank surface of tooth |
| Nature of stress | **Tensile bending stress** at root | **Hertzian contact (compressive) stress** at flank |
| Governing equation | Lewis Equation: $\sigma_b = W_t / (b m Y)$ | AGMA Contact Equation: $\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{W_t / (b d_p I)}$ |
| Failure mechanism | Crack initiation at root → propagation → tooth breakage | Subsurface crack → material flaking off → pit formation |
| Material property | Endurance limit $\sigma_e$ | Contact strength $\sigma_{sc}$ |
| Visual appearance | Tooth completely missing | Crater-like depressions on flank |
| Prevention | Increase module, shot peening, case hardening | Increase face width, improve surface finish, better lubricant |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Modified]**

> **(a)** Derive the **Lewis Bending Stress Equation** for a gear tooth, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **spur gear pinion** has the following specifications: Module $m = 4$ mm, Number of teeth $Z = 20$, Face width $b = 40$ mm, Pressure angle $\phi = 20°$ (full depth), Power transmitted $P = 7.5$ kW at $N = 1440$ rpm. The material is **steel with allowable bending stress $\sigma_{allowable} = 200$ MPa**. Determine whether the gear is safe against bending failure. Use Lewis Form Factor $Y = 0.32$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Derivation:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. Key steps to write in the answer sheet:

1. State all five assumptions clearly.
2. Draw the free-body diagram of the cantilever tooth.
3. Express the bending moment: $M = W_t \cdot h$.
4. Express the section modulus: $Z = b t^2 / 6$.
5. Derive the basic stress: $\sigma_b = 6 W_t h / (b t^2)$.
6. Introduce the Lewis form factor: $y = t^2 / (6h)$ and $Y = \pi y$.
7. Substitute to get the final form: $\sigma_b = W_t / (b m Y)$.
8. Mention the **velocity factor $K_v$** modification for dynamic loads.

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 – Calculate the pitch diameter:**

$$
d = m \cdot Z = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 – Calculate the pitch circle velocity:**

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.080 \times 1440}{60} = 6.032 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 – Calculate the tangential load:**

$$
W_t = \dfrac{2P}{d} = \dfrac{2 \times 7500}{0.080} = 187500 \text{ N} = 187.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 – Apply the Lewis Bending Stress Equation:**

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y} = \dfrac{187500}{40 \times 4 \times 0.32}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{187500}{51.2} = 3662.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 – Apply the velocity factor (AGMA Barth equation):**

For $V = 6.032$ m/s and ordinary cut gears, use $A = 0.25$ and $B = 0.25$:

$$
K_v = \left(\dfrac{A}{A + \sqrt{200 V}}\right)^{B} = \left(\dfrac{0.25}{0.25 + \sqrt{200 \times 6.032}}\right)^{0.25}
$$

$$
\sqrt{200 \times 6.032} = \sqrt{1206.4} = 34.73
$$

$$
K_v = \left(\dfrac{0.25}{0.25 + 34.73}\right)^{0.25} = (0.007147)^{0.25}
$$

$$
K_v = 0.2910
$$

**Step 6 – Calculate the effective bending stress:**

$$
\sigma_{b,eff} = \sigma_b \times K_v = 3662.1 \times 0.2910 = 1065.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 7 – Compare with allowable stress and calculate factor of safety:**

$$
FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_{allowable}}{\sigma_{b,eff}} = \dfrac{200}{1065.7} = 0.1877
$$

Since $FOS = 0.1877 \ll 1.5$, the **gear is NOT safe** against bending failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to apply the velocity factor $K_v$ and directly compare 3662 MPa with 200 MPa, getting $FOS = 0.0546$. This gives the same conclusion (unsafe) but the working marks are deducted. Always show $K_v$ explicitly. Also note that the dynamic load is small (0.291), so the effective stress is still vastly higher than the allowable – meaning the **module is too small**. Recommend increasing $m$ to at least 12 mm.

**Recommendation:** The minimum module required for $FOS = 1.5$ can be back-calculated. The student should specify that the design must be revised with a larger module or wider face width.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper] – Internal Choice Alternative**

> **(a)** Explain the **AGMA Contact Stress Equation** for pitting failure. Define all terms. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **helical gear pair** made of steel has: Pinion $Z_p = 18$ teeth, Gear $Z_g = 36$ teeth, Module (normal) $m_n = 3$ mm, Helix angle $\beta = 15°$, Face width $b = 50$ mm, Power $P = 12$ kW at $N_p = 1800$ rpm, Pressure angle $\phi_n = 20°$. Determine the **AGMA contact stress** and check for pitting safety. Use elastic coefficient $C_p = 191 \sqrt{\text{MPa}}$ and geometry factor $I = 0.105$. Allowable contact stress for the material is $\sigma_{sc} = 1100$ MPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Explanation:**

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes. The AGMA contact stress equation is:

$$
\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t}{b \cdot d_p \cdot I}}
$$

Define each term:
- $\sigma_c$ = Hertzian contact stress at the pitch point (MPa).
- $C_p$ = Elastic coefficient (function of material pair; for steel-steel, $C_p = 191 \sqrt{\text{MPa}}$).
- $W_t$ = Tangential load on the pinion (N).
- $b$ = Face width (mm).
- $d_p$ = Pitch diameter of the pinion (mm).
- $I$ = AGMA contact ratio geometry factor (tabulated; depends on pressure angle and teeth numbers).

The pitting factor of safety is:

$$
S_H = \dfrac{\sigma_{sc} \cdot Z_N \cdot C_H}{\sigma_c}
$$

where $Z_N$ is the stress cycle factor and $C_H$ is the hardness ratio factor.

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 – Compute the transverse (normal) module and pitch diameter:**

For helical gears, the **virtual (transverse) form factor** requires careful use of normal module:

$$
m_t = \dfrac{m_n}{\cos\beta} = \dfrac{3}{\cos 15°} = \dfrac{3}{0.9659} = 3.106 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_p = m_t \cdot Z_p = 3.106 \times 18 = 55.91 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the pitch line velocity:**

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi d_p N_p}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.05591 \times 1800}{60} = 5.27 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute the tangential load:**

$$
W_t = \dfrac{2P}{d_p} = \dfrac{2 \times 12000}{0.05591} = 429258 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 – Apply the AGMA Contact Stress Equation:**

$$
\sigma_c = 191 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{429258}{50 \times 55.91 \times 0.105}}
$$

Denominator inside the square root:

$$
50 \times 55.91 \times 0.105 = 293.53
$$

$$
\dfrac{429258}{293.53} = 1462.55
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 191 \times \sqrt{1462.55} = 191 \times 38.244 = 7304.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 – Apply the velocity factor (for helical gears, $K_v$ is typically higher):**

For helical gears, use $A = 0.25$ and $B = 0.25$ (precision hobbed cut):

$$
K_v = \left(\dfrac{0.25}{0.25 + \sqrt{200 \times 5.27}}\right)^{0.25}
$$

$$
\sqrt{200 \times 5.27} = \sqrt{1054} = 32.46
$$

$$
K_v = \left(\dfrac{0.25}{32.71}\right)^{0.25} = (0.00764)^{0.25} = 0.2955
$$

For **preliminary design**, we conservatively ignore $K_v$ scaling and use the calculated $\sigma_c$ directly.

**Step 6 – Compute the factor of safety against pitting:**

$$
FOS_{pitting} = \dfrac{\sigma_{sc}}{\sigma_c} = \dfrac{1100}{7304.6} = 0.1506
$$

**Step 7 – Conclusion:**

Since $FOS = 0.1506 \ll 1.0$, the **gear is NOT safe against pitting failure**. The contact stress (7304 MPa) is grossly higher than the allowable (1100 MPa), which is a typical result when the problem parameters are kept at "exam-extreme" values. In a real exam, if $FOS < 1.0$, the student must **recommend corrective actions**.

**Corrective Recommendations:**
- Increase face width $b$ to **80 mm** → reduces $\sigma_c$ by $\sqrt{50/80} = 0.79$ times.
- Use a larger module, e.g., $m_n = 5$ mm → increases $d_p$ proportionally.
- Use **through-hardened steel with $\sigma_{sc} = 1500$ MPa** (e.g., AISI 4340).
- Apply **shot peening** to introduce compressive residual stress at the surface, which delays pitting crack initiation.
- Use **higher viscosity lubricant (VG 320 instead of VG 220)** to increase film thickness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall on Pitting Problems:**
> 1. **Do not** forget the **square root** in the AGMA contact stress equation – this is a very common error worth 2 marks.
> 2. **Do not** confuse $C_p$ units. For SI (MPa), $C_p = 191$; for SI (GPa), $C_p = 1.08$.
> 3. **Always** verify that the unit of $W_t$ is N and the unit of $b \cdot d_p \cdot I$ is mm², so that the inside of the square root has units of N/mm² = MPa, making $C_p$ have units of $\sqrt{\text{MPa}}$.
> 4. For helical gears, remember to use **$m_t = m_n / \cos\beta$**, not $m_n$ directly.
> 5. Final answer must include a **factor of safety** statement (e.g., $FOS = 0.15$, NOT safe) for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Gear Tooth Failures (KTU Module 3)**

- **Lewis Bending Stress:** $\sigma_b = W_t / (b m Y)$. Always show $K_v$. $Y$ depends on pressure angle and tooth count.
- **AGMA Contact Stress (Pitting):** $\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{W_t / (b d_p I)}$. For steel-steel, $C_p = 191 \sqrt{\text{MPa}}$.
- **Buckingham Wear Load:** $W_{wear} = \mu W_t / \cos\phi$. Compare with scuffing resistance from lubricant data.
- **Five Failure Modes (AGMA):** (1) Bending fatigue, (2) Pitting, (3) Scuffing, (4) Abrasive wear, (5) Plastic deformation.
- **Dynamic Load Multiplier (Velocity Factor):** $K_v$ is always $\le 1$ for cut gears; it scales the effective load up by $1/K_v$ when computing stress.
- **Lewis Form Factor $Y$ values (must memorize):** 14.5° full depth, 20 teeth → $Y = 0.245$; 20° full depth, 20 teeth → $Y = 0.32$; 20° stub, 20 teeth → $Y = 0.175$.
- **Pressure Angle Choice:** 20° full depth is **standard for automotive** (more strength than 14.5°). 25° is used for high-stress aerospace gears.
- **Material Selection:** Carburized steel (AISI 8620, 20MnCr5) for **automotive transmission gears**. Nitrided steel (AISI 4140) for **heavily loaded hypoid gears**.
- **Lubricant Grade:** API GL-4 for manual gearboxes; API GL-5 for hypoid differentials. Viscosity grade 75W-90 is **standard in Indian automotive applications**.
- **Common Pitfalls in Board Exam:**
  - Forgetting to convert $d$ from mm to m in $W_t = 2P / d$ (or vice versa) — a single unit slip costs 2 marks.
  - Using $Y$ for the wrong pressure angle.
  - Forgetting to state all assumptions of the Lewis model.
  - Mixing up **virtual (transverse)** and **normal** module for helical gears.
  - Not stating the factor of safety explicitly at the end of the solution.
- **Sample KTU Question Pattern:** "Design a spur gear pair to transmit X kW at Y rpm with a service factor of Z. Use a 20° full-depth involute system and a Lewis Form Factor of 0.32. Check the design for both bending and pitting failures." (This is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU 2024 scheme.)
- **Industrial Context:** Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, and Mahindra use **AGMA 2001 / ISO 6336** combined with **Indian standard IS 7403** for gear design. Always cite at least one standard in your answer.
- **Latest Trend (KTU 2024 NEP Alignment):** Modern EV reducers are designed for **higher pitch-line velocities (up to 30 m/s) and lower lubricant volumes**. The dominant failure mode is **scuffing**, making Buckingham's analysis more critical than Lewis's.

> **End of KTU Module 3 – Gear Tooth Failures Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beam Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Beam Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Beam Strength** in the context of Automotive Component Design refers to the ability of a structural beam-type component (such as vehicle chassis side rails, cross members, axles, leaf springs, and bumper reinforcements) to resist bending failure when subjected to transverse loading during vehicle operation. Mathematically, it is governed by the **flexure formula** (also called the **Euler–Bernoulli beam equation**):

$$\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

where every term has a precise engineering meaning that we will explore exhaustively in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBAUT604 – Module 3):**
> Beam Strength analysis is essential for the design and validation of automotive load-bearing structural members. It directly applies to chassis design, suspension linkages, bumper beams, propeller shaft supports, and ladder-frame rails. The KTU examiner expects students to compute **bending stress**, **section modulus**, and **deflection** for standard automotive cross-sections (solid round, hollow tubular, rectangular, I-section, and channel sections).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a wooden stick (a ruler) horizontally with both hands, palms up, and you press down hard in the middle with your thumb. The stick bends downward. The top surface of the stick tries to *shorten* (it is in **compression**), while the bottom surface tries to *stretch* (it is in **tension**). The middle of the stick — a horizontal plane that does not change length — is called the **neutral axis**.

Now imagine a vehicle driving over a pothole. The chassis side rail acts exactly like that ruler. The road pushes up at the wheel, the weight of the vehicle pushes down at the body mounting points. The rail bends. The "Beam Strength" tells us:
1. **How much stress** the rail experiences (will it crack?).
2. **How much it deflects** (will it sag into the floor pan?).
3. **What cross-sectional shape** gives the strongest, lightest rail (tube vs. solid rod vs. C-channel).

**Real automotive example:** A ladder-frame truck side rail is essentially a long **simply supported beam** carrying distributed load from the payload and concentrated loads at suspension pickup points. Its strength depends on its **section modulus (Z)**, not just its cross-sectional area.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Automotive Material Properties to Memorize for KTU:**
> - **Young's Modulus (E)** for mild steel: **200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ N/mm²)**
> - **Yield Strength (σ_y)** for mild steel: **250 MPa**
> - **Yield Strength** for high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) automotive steel: **350–550 MPa**
> - **Poisson's ratio (ν)** for steel: **0.30**
> - **Density of steel**: **7850 kg/m³**
> - **Factor of Safety (FoS)** for static chassis components: **1.5 – 2.5**
> - **FoS for fatigue-loaded components** (axles, springs): **2.0 – 3.5**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bending Equation — Theory Decomposed

The fundamental relationship governing beam strength is derived from three simultaneous physical conditions:

1. **Geometric Compatibility** — Plane sections remain plane after bending (kinematic assumption of Euler–Bernoulli theory).
2. **Static Equilibrium** — The internal moment (M) must balance the external bending forces.
3. **Hooke's Law** — Stress is proportional to strain within the elastic limit.

Combining these gives the master equation:

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}}$$

Where:
- **M** = Bending moment at the section (N·mm)
- **I** = Second moment of area about the neutral axis (mm⁴)
- **σ** = Bending stress at distance y from neutral axis (N/mm² = MPa)
- **y** = Distance from neutral axis to the fibre in question (mm)
- **E** = Young's modulus of elasticity (N/mm²)
- **R** = Radius of curvature of the deflected beam (mm)

### 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Derivation Logic

**Step 1 — Pure bending assumption:**
When a beam is loaded purely by moments at its ends (no shear), every cross-section experiences only a normal stress that varies linearly across the depth.

**Step 2 — Strain variation:**
A fibre at distance y from the neutral axis has a strain:

$$\epsilon = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law (σ = E·ε):**
Stress at that fibre is:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Static moment equilibrium:**
The internal bending moment is the integrated first moment of stress about the neutral axis:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A E \cdot \frac{y^2}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get the flexure formula:**
Rearranging:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

Substituting into the stress expression:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

The maximum stress occurs at the extreme fibre (y = y_max), giving:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}}$$

where **Z = I / y_max** is the **section modulus** (units: mm³).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Beam Strength

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application in Automotive Design |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Bending stress | σ = M·y / I | N/mm² (MPa) | General beam bending |
| 2 | Max bending stress | σ_max = M / Z | N/mm² (MPa) | Design check at critical section |
| 3 | Section modulus | Z = I / y_max | mm³ | Comparative strength of cross-sections |
| 4 | Solid round shaft | Z = π·d³ / 32 | mm³ | Drive shafts, solid axles |
| 5 | Hollow round shaft | Z = π·(D⁴ − d⁴) / (32·D) | mm³ | Tubular axles, bumper beams |
| 6 | Solid rectangle (b × d) | Z = b·d² / 6 | mm³ | Leaf springs, flat rails |
| 7 | Hollow rectangle | Z = (B·D³ − b·d³) / (6·D) | mm³ | Box-section chassis rails |
| 8 | Standard I-section | Z = I_xx / (D/2) | mm³ | Cross members, sub-frames |
| 9 | Standard channel | Z = I_xx / y_max | mm³ | Ladder frame rails |
| 10 | Deflection (cantilever, point load at end) | δ = P·L³ / (3·E·I) | mm | Leaf spring, trailing arm |
| 11 | Deflection (simply supported, centre load) | δ = P·L³ / (48·E·I) | mm | Cross member, bumper beam |
| 12 | Deflection (simply supported, UDL) | δ = 5·w·L⁴ / (384·E·I) | mm | Chassis rail with payload |
| 13 | Slope (simply supported, centre load) | θ = P·L² / (16·E·I) | rad | Twist/rotation check |
| 14 | Transverse shear stress | τ = V·Q / (I·b) | N/mm² | Web of I-beam, thin sections |
| 15 | Equivalent bending moment (combined loading) | M_eq = √(M² + T²) | N·mm | Shafts with bending + torsion |
| 16 | Maximum shear stress (rectangular beam) | τ_max = 1.5·V / A | N/mm² | Quick estimate, h \gg b |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-T-U Examiner Tip:** The unit **N/mm² is identical to MPa**. Always quote in **MPa** in the answer script, never N/mm², for the KTU board. Use the conversion **1 GPa = 1000 MPa = 1000 N/mm²**.

### 2.4 Real-World Automotive Engineering Utility

Beam strength calculations drive every critical decision in vehicle structural design:

- **Chassis frame rails:** A pickup truck side rail is essentially a long beam. Designers choose a **closed box section** over a C-channel because it has a much higher section modulus per kilogram of material, improving the strength-to-weight ratio — a vital metric for fuel efficiency.
- **Bumper beams:** Modern car bumper reinforcements are hollow rectangular aluminium or boron-steel beams designed to absorb crash energy through controlled bending.
- **Solid axles:** Live axles (used in trucks and rear-wheel-drive cars) act as simply supported beams loaded at the spring pad locations.
- **Leaf springs:** A leaf spring is a tapered or constant-width beam (actually a stack of beams) — its length, width, thickness, and number of leaves are all chosen using beam theory.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Section Modulus of a Hollow Circular Tube

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 3, so we work through it in full.

**Problem Setup:** Determine the section modulus Z of a hollow circular cross-section with outer diameter D and inner diameter d.

**Step 1 — Define the second moment of area about the neutral axis (centroidal axis):**

For a solid circular section of diameter x:

$$I_{solid} = \frac{\pi \cdot x^4}{64}$$

**Step 2 — Treat the hollow tube as a solid disc minus the inner bore:**

By the additive–subtractive method:

$$I = I_{outer} - I_{inner} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64} - \frac{\pi \cdot d^4}{64}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right)$$

**Step 3 — Identify y_max, the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme outer fibre:**

For a circular section, y_max = D/2.

**Step 4 — Compute section modulus Z = I / y_max:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right)}{\dfrac{D}{2}}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right) \cdot \frac{2}{D}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi \cdot \left(D^4 - d^4\right)}{32 \cdot D}$$

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{\pi \cdot \left(D^4 - d^4\right)}{32 \cdot D}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** for hollow round sections such as tubular drive shafts, bumper reinforcements, and chassis tubes.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]:**
> A simply supported chassis side rail of a light commercial vehicle has an effective span of **L = 2.4 m**. It carries a uniformly distributed load (payload + self-weight) of **w = 18 kN/m** over the entire span. The cross-section is a **hollow rectangular tube** with outer dimensions B = 80 mm, D = 120 mm and uniform wall thickness t = 6 mm.
> Material: structural steel with **E = 200 GPa**, **σ_allowable = 165 MPa**.
> Determine:
> 1. Maximum bending moment.
> 2. Section modulus of the cross-section.
> 3. Maximum bending stress and check against allowable.
> 4. Maximum deflection.

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment for simply supported beam with UDL:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$$

Substituting (in consistent units: w in N/mm, L in mm):

$$w = 18 \text{ kN/m} = 18 \text{ N/mm}, \quad L = 2400 \text{ mm}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{18 \times (2400)^2}{8} = \frac{18 \times 5{,}760{,}000}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{103{,}680{,}000}{8} = 12{,}960{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 12.96 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2 — Inner dimensions of the hollow tube:**

$$b = B - 2t = 80 - 12 = 68 \text{ mm}$$

$$d = D - 2t = 120 - 12 = 108 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Second moment of area for hollow rectangular section:**

$$I = \frac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{80 \times (120)^3 - 68 \times (108)^3}{12}$$

Computing each cube:

$$(120)^3 = 1{,}728{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$(108)^3 = 1{,}259{,}712 \text{ mm}^3$$

Therefore:

$$80 \times 1{,}728{,}000 = 138{,}240{,}000$$

$$68 \times 1{,}259{,}712 = 85{,}660{,}416$$

$$I = \frac{138{,}240{,}000 - 85{,}660{,}416}{12} = \frac{52{,}579{,}584}{12}$$

$$I = 4{,}381{,}632 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4 — Section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{4{,}381{,}632}{60} = 73{,}027.2 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$Z \approx 7.30 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Maximum bending stress (flexure formula):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{12{,}960{,}000}{73{,}027.2}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 177.49 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with allowable (165 MPa):**

$$\sigma_{max} = 177.49 \text{ MPa} \;>\; \sigma_{allow} = 165 \text{ MPa}$$

The design **FAILS** by a margin of:

$$\frac{177.49 - 165}{165} \times 100\% \approx 7.6\%$$

**Step 7 — Maximum deflection (simply supported beam, UDL):**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

$$w = 18 \text{ N/mm}, \quad L = 2400 \text{ mm}, \quad E = 200{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad I = 4{,}381{,}632 \text{ mm}^4$$

Computing L⁴:

$$L^4 = (2400)^4 = 3.31776 \times 10^{13} \text{ mm}^4$$

Numerator:

$$5 \cdot w \cdot L^4 = 5 \times 18 \times 3.31776 \times 10^{13} = 2.985984 \times 10^{15}$$

Denominator:

$$384 \cdot E \cdot I = 384 \times 200{,}000 \times 4{,}381{,}632 = 3.36655 \times 10^{14}$$

Therefore:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2.985984 \times 10^{15}}{3.36655 \times 10^{14}} \approx 8.87 \text{ mm}$$

**Permissible deflection check (L/250 is a typical automotive limit for chassis rails):**

$$\delta_{perm} = \frac{L}{250} = \frac{2400}{250} = 9.6 \text{ mm}$$

Since 8.87 mm < 9.6 mm, the **deflection criterion is satisfied**.

**Final Design Verdict:** Increase the wall thickness from 6 mm to **8 mm** (or use higher-grade steel with σ_allow = 200 MPa) to satisfy the strength criterion while keeping deflection within limits.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Beam Strength Failure Modes in Automotive Components

| Component | Loading Type | Critical Stress | Typical FoS | KTU-Required Calculation |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Chassis side rail | Bending + shear | Bending (top/bottom flange) | 1.5 – 2.0 | σ = M/Z, τ = VQ/Ib |
| Live rear axle | Bending | Bending at spring seat | 2.0 – 3.0 | σ = 32·M/(π·d³) |
| Leaf spring (master leaf) | Bending | Bending stress | 2.5 – 3.5 | σ = 6·M/(b·t²) |
| Bumper beam | Bending + axial | Bending at centre | 1.2 – 1.5 (crash) | σ = M/Z (dynamic) |
| Propeller shaft | Torsion + bending | Combined equivalent | 2.0 – 2.5 | σ_eq = (16/πd³)·√(M²+T²) |
| Cross member | Bending | Bending at mid-span | 1.5 – 2.0 | σ = M/Z |
| Torsion bar | Torsion | Shear stress | 2.0 – 3.0 | τ = 16T/(πd³) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Beam Strength Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify the automotive beam component e.g. chassis rail"] --> B["Determine loading type UDL, point load, moment"]
    B --> C["Calculate reactions and internal forces SFD and BMD"]
    C --> D["Identify the critical section location of max bending moment"]
    D --> E["Determine cross sectional geometry section modulus Z"]
    E --> F["Compute maximum bending stress using sigma equals M over Z"]
    F --> G{"Stress less than allowable?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Design is SAFE proceed to deflection check"]
    G -- No --> I["Revise cross section increase Z or change material"]
    I --> E
    H --> J["Compute maximum deflection using standard beam equation"]
    J --> K{"Deflection within limit L over n?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["Design ACCEPTED"]
    K -- No --> I
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Selection of Section Modulus for Different Beam Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Solid circular shaft Z equals pi d cubed over 32"] --> Q1["Application solid drive shaft solid axle"]
    S2["Hollow circular tube Z equals pi D4 minus d4 over 32 D"] --> Q2["Application bumper beam propeller shaft"]
    S3["Solid rectangle Z equals b d squared over 6"] --> Q3["Application leaf spring flat rail"]
    S4["Hollow rectangle Z equals BD cubed minus bd cubed over 6 D"] --> Q4["Application chassis side rail SUV frame"]
    S5["I section Z equals I xx over D by 2"] --> Q5["Application cross member sub frame"]
    S6["C channel Z equals I xx over y max"] --> Q6["Application ladder frame rail truck chassis"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Stress Distribution Across a Rectangular Beam Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NA["Neutral Axis zero stress at centroid"] --> T["Top fibre COMPRESSION maximum negative stress"]
    NA --> B["Bottom fibre TENSION maximum positive stress"]
    T --> STR["Stress varies LINEARLY from NA to extreme fibre"]
    B --> STR
    STR --> EQ["sigma max equals M over Z at y equals D by 2"]
```

### 4.4 Free-Body Diagram — Simply Supported Beam with UDL (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L["Left support Reaction R_A equals wL over 2"] --> BM["Bending moment diagram parabolic"]
    R["Right support Reaction R_B equals wL over 2"] --> BM
    BM --> MID["Maximum bending moment at mid span M_max equals wL squared over 8"]
    MID --> SF["Shear force diagram linear from plus wL over 2 to minus wL over 2"]
    SF --> CHK["Critical section located at centre for bending, at supports for shear"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid instead of physical drawings?** KTU notes delivered digitally benefit more from clearly readable block diagrams than from crude ASCII sketches. Each block represents a critical concept the student must internalize. For hand-drawn diagrams in the exam, students should practise drawing neat **BMD (Bending Moment Diagrams)** and **SFD (Shear Force Diagrams)** with proper sign conventions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define section modulus. State its physical significance and write the section modulus formula for (i) a solid circular cross-section and (ii) a hollow rectangular cross-section.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Section modulus (Z) is the ratio of the second moment of area (I) of a cross-section to the distance (y_max) from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre. It is a geometric property of the cross-section that quantifies its ability to resist bending.

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} \quad [\text{units: mm}^3]$$

**Physical significance:** A higher Z means the beam can carry a higher bending moment for the same maximum bending stress. It is purely a function of geometry, not of material.

**(i) Solid circular cross-section** of diameter d:

$$Z = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3}{32}$$

**(ii) Hollow rectangular cross-section** with outer dimensions B × D and inner dimensions b × d:

$$Z = \frac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{6 \cdot D}$$

---

#### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the simple bending theory (Euler–Bernoulli beam theory).**

**Model Answer (any five of the following are expected for full marks):**

1. The beam material is **homogeneous and isotropic** (same elastic properties in all directions).
2. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** (stress is proportional to strain, σ = E·ε).
3. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.**
4. The beam is initially **straight** and has a uniform cross-section along its length.
5. The bending stress is **purely flexural** — there is no shear deformation (deflection due to shear is neglected).
6. The radius of curvature is **large** compared with beam dimensions, so that small-deflection theory applies.
7. The self-weight of the beam is negligible compared with applied loads (unless otherwise stated).
8. The neutral axis passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section and the modulus of elasticity is the same in tension and compression.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Beam Strength Problems:**
> Students commonly lose marks on the following mistakes:
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing N/m and mm in the same equation. **Always convert all quantities to N and mm** before substituting into formulas (yielding σ in N/mm² = MPa).
> 2. **Forgetting to find critical section:** Simply plugging in maximum bending moment without confirming it is at the correct location. Always sketch or compute BMD.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** Using negative stress values in the FoS check. Take absolute value when comparing with allowable stress.
> 4. **Omitting allowable deflection check:** A design may pass stress but fail deflection. KTU expects both checks.
> 5. **Writing Z = π·d³/16** instead of **π·d³/32** — a recurring silly mistake. Memorize: **32** for circular, **6** for rectangular.

---

#### **Question B1 – Option A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A hollow steel drive shaft of a light truck has an outer diameter of **60 mm** and inner diameter of **45 mm**. It transmits a torque of **2.5 kN·m** while simultaneously subjected to a bending moment of **1.8 kN·m** at its critical section. Using the maximum shear stress theory, determine whether the shaft is safe. Take allowable shear stress as **75 MPa** for the shaft material.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A simply supported leaf spring of length **1.0 m** carries a central point load of **12 kN**. The spring has a rectangular cross-section of width **60 mm** and is built up of **8 leaves**, each of thickness **8 mm**. Calculate the maximum bending stress and the deflection of the spring. Take E = **200 GPa**.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Section modulus of the hollow circular shaft:**

$$Z = \frac{\pi \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}{32 \cdot D}$$

$$D^4 = (60)^4 = 12{,}960{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$d^4 = (45)^4 = 4{,}100{,}625 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi \times (12{,}960{,}000 - 4{,}100{,}625)}{32 \times 60}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi \times 8{,}859{,}375}{1920} = \frac{27{,}832{,}847.7}{1920}$$

$$Z \approx 14{,}496.3 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent bending moment (combined bending and torsion using maximum shear stress theory):**

For a shaft subjected to bending moment M and torque T, the equivalent bending moment is:

$$M_{eq} = \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right) \quad \text{(max shear stress theory)}$$

Substituting (M = 1.8 kN·m = 1.8 × 10⁶ N·mm, T = 2.5 kN·m = 2.5 × 10⁶ N·mm):

$$M^2 = (1.8 \times 10^6)^2 = 3.24 \times 10^{12}$$

$$T^2 = (2.5 \times 10^6)^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{12}$$

$$\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{9.49 \times 10^{12}} = 3.0804 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$M_{eq} = \frac{1}{2}(1.8 \times 10^6 + 3.0804 \times 10^6)$$

$$M_{eq} = \frac{1}{2}(4.8804 \times 10^6) = 2.4402 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum equivalent bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{M_{eq}}{Z} = \frac{2.4402 \times 10^6}{14{,}496.3}$$

$$\sigma_{eq} \approx 168.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum shear stress in the shaft:**

By maximum shear stress theory, the maximum shear stress is half of the equivalent bending stress:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{eq}}{2} = \frac{168.3}{2} \approx 84.2 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Safety check:**

$$\tau_{max} = 84.2 \text{ MPa} \;>\; \tau_{allow} = 75 \text{ MPa}$$

The shaft is **NOT safe**. The factor of safety is:

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\tau_{allow}}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{75}{84.2} \approx 0.89$$

The shaft will fail. **Recommendation:** Increase the outer diameter to **D = 65 mm** (keeping inner diameter 45 mm) and re-check.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Effective moment of inertia of the leaf spring (n leaves act as a composite beam):**

For a leaf spring of n leaves, each of width b and thickness t, the moment of inertia of the entire spring stack at the central (full-length) leaves is:

$$I = \frac{n \cdot b \cdot t^3}{12}$$

where n is the number of full-length leaves. Here n = 8 (assuming all leaves are full length):

$$I = \frac{8 \times 60 \times (8)^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times 60 \times 512}{12} = \frac{245{,}760}{12} = 20{,}480 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{t/2} = \frac{20{,}480}{4} = 5120 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending moment (simply supported, centre point load):**

$$M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L}{4} = \frac{12{,}000 \times 1000}{4} = 3{,}000{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 3 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{5120} \approx 585.9 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This stress is extremely high — typical leaf spring steel has σ_allow ≈ 500 MPa. In practice, a leaf spring is **tapered** (master leaf full width, graduated leaves progressively shorter), so the simple analysis above overestimates stress. The tapered leaf analysis is covered in Module 4 (Springs).

**Step 5 — Maximum deflection (simply supported, central point load):**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

$$L^3 = (1000)^3 = 10^9 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{12{,}000 \times 10^9}{48 \times 200{,}000 \times 20{,}480}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{13}}{1.966 \times 10^{11}} \approx 61.04 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### **Question B1 – Option B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A **cantilever bumper beam** of length **L = 800 mm** is subjected to a point load **P = 5 kN** at its free end. The beam has a rectangular cross-section, **width = 40 mm** and **height = 80 mm**. Calculate (i) maximum bending stress, (ii) maximum deflection, and (iii) the slope at the free end. Take E = **200 GPa**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A simply supported I-section cross-member of a car chassis has a span of **1.5 m** and carries a uniformly distributed load of **10 kN/m**. The I-section has flange width **100 mm**, flange thickness **12 mm**, web thickness **8 mm**, and overall depth **150 mm**. Determine the maximum bending stress in the member.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia of rectangular cross-section:**

$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times (80)^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times 512{,}000}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{20{,}480{,}000}{12} \approx 1{,}706{,}667 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{d/2} = \frac{1{,}706{,}667}{40} = 42{,}666.7 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending moment at the fixed end (cantilever with point load at free end):**

$$M_{max} = P \cdot L = 5000 \times 800 = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{42{,}666.7} \approx 93.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum deflection at free end (cantilever, point load at free end):**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

$$L^3 = (800)^3 = 5.12 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5000 \times 5.12 \times 10^8}{3 \times 200{,}000 \times 1{,}706{,}667}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{12}}{1.024 \times 10^{12}} = 2.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Slope at free end (cantilever, point load at free end):**

$$\theta = \frac{P \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

$$L^2 = (800)^2 = 640{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\theta = \frac{5000 \times 640{,}000}{2 \times 200{,}000 \times 1{,}706{,}667}$$

$$\theta = \frac{3.2 \times 10^9}{6.8267 \times 10^{11}} \approx 4.687 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta_{deg} = 4.687 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.269°$$

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment for simply supported beam with UDL:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{10 \times (1.5)^2}{8} = \frac{22.5}{8} = 2.8125 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{max} = 2.8125 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia of I-section:**

Split the I-section into three rectangles:

**Top flange:** b₁ = 100 mm, t₁ = 12 mm

**Web:** b₂ = 8 mm, h = 150 − 2 × 12 = 126 mm

**Bottom flange:** b₃ = 100 mm, t₃ = 12 mm

Using parallel axis theorem about the centroidal axis (which is the horizontal axis by symmetry):

$$I = I_{top\,flange} + I_{web} + I_{bot\,flange}$$

For top flange:

$$I_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot t_1^3}{12} + A_1 \cdot \bar{y}_1^2$$

$$I_1 = \frac{100 \times (12)^3}{12} + (100 \times 12) \times \left(\frac{150 - 12}{2}\right)^2$$

$$I_1 = \frac{100 \times 1728}{12} + 1200 \times (69)^2$$

$$I_1 = 14{,}400 + 1200 \times 4761 = 14{,}400 + 5{,}713{,}200 = 5{,}727{,}600 \text{ mm}^4$$

For web (centred on the neutral axis, so no parallel axis term):

$$I_2 = \frac{b_2 \cdot h^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times (126)^3}{12}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{8 \times 2{,}000{,}376}{12} = \frac{16{,}003{,}008}{12} \approx 1{,}333{,}584 \text{ mm}^4$$

For bottom flange (by symmetry, identical to top flange):

$$I_3 = I_1 = 5{,}727{,}600 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Total moment of inertia:**

$$I = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 = 5{,}727{,}600 + 1{,}333{,}584 + 5{,}727{,}600$$

$$I = 12{,}788{,}784 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 1.279 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{12{,}788{,}784}{75} \approx 170{,}517 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{2.8125 \times 10^6}{170{,}517} \approx 16.49 \text{ MPa}$$

This is comfortably below σ_allowable ≈ 165 MPa for structural steel, giving a FoS ≈ 10 (acceptable for a chassis cross-member in steady-state loading).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Beam Strength (Module 3):**
>
> ✅ **Master Equation:** σ/y = M/I = E/R — must be memorized verbatim.
> ✅ **Maximum stress formula:** σ_max = M/Z, where Z = I/y_max.
> ✅ **Five essential section moduli** to memorize:
>    - Solid round: π·d³/32
>    - Hollow round: π·(D⁴−d⁴)/(32·D)
>    - Solid rectangle: b·d²/6
>    - Hollow rectangle: (B·D³−b·d³)/(6·D)
>    - I-section: use parallel axis theorem and I/c
> ✅ **Standard bending moment formulas:**
>    - SS beam + centre load: M_max = PL/4
>    - SS beam + UDL: M_max = wL²/8
>    - Cantilever + end load: M_max = PL
>    - Cantilever + UDL: M_max = wL²/2
> ✅ **Standard deflection formulas** (must memorize all four):
>    - SS beam + centre load: PL³/(48EI)
>    - SS beam + UDL: 5wL⁴/(384EI)
>    - Cantilever + end load: PL³/(3EI)
>    - Cantilever + UDL: wL⁴/(8EI)
> ✅ **Units discipline:** Always work in **N, mm, MPa, N·mm**. Never mix SI and mm units in the same calculation.
> ✅ **Two design checks are mandatory:** Strength (σ_max ≤ σ_allow) AND Stiffness (δ_max ≤ δ_permissible, typically L/250 to L/360 for automotive chassis).
> ✅ **Automotive design philosophy:** Strength-to-weight ratio is paramount — hollow sections always preferred over solid for the same Z.
> ✅ **Failure theories for combined loading:** Use either maximum shear stress theory (Tresca) or distortion energy theory (von Mises) when both bending and torsion act on a shaft.
> ✅ **Permissible deflection limits** (typical automotive values):
>    - Chassis side rail: L/250 to L/300
>    - Bumper beam: L/200
>    - Cross member: L/300
>    - Leaf spring: L/100 (ride height)
> ✅ **Common examiner traps:** Wrong I-section formula, missing parallel axis term, mixed unit system, no FoS check, no deflection check.
> ✅ **Material constants to commit to memory:**
>    - Steel: E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.30, ρ = 7850 kg/m³
>    - Aluminium: E = 70 GPa, ν = 0.33, ρ = 2700 kg/m³
>    - Cast iron: E = 100–150 GPa, brittle under tension

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lewis Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lewis Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Lewis Equation** (formulated by **Wilfred Lewis in 1893**) is a classical analytical expression used in the design of **spur gears** to determine the **bending (flexural) stress** induced in a gear tooth when it is subjected to a tangential load at the pitch circle. The equation models the gear tooth as a **cantilever beam of varying cross-section**, fixed at the root and loaded at the tip, thereby providing the foundation for calculating the **beam strength** of a gear tooth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Lewis Equation is the fundamental equation used for the **design of gear teeth against bending failure**. It is the cornerstone of gear design taught under the module dealing with **Design of Transmission Elements (Gears)**.

The standard form of the Lewis Equation is expressed as:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_T \times C_s}{b \times y \times m}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_b$ = **Bending stress in the gear tooth** (N/mm² or MPa)
- $W_T$ = **Tangential tooth load** at the pitch circle (N)
- $C_s$ = **Service factor / Factor of safety** (dimensionless)
- $b$ = **Face width** of the gear (mm)
- $y$ = **Lewis Form Factor** (dimensionless)
- $m$ = **Module** of the gear (mm)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant tree (the gear tooth) growing out of a solid base (the gear rim)**. The tree is anchored firmly at its roots. When a strong wind (the tangential load) blows against the tree at its top, the tree tends to bend or snap at its weakest cross-section — which is usually somewhere near the base. 

Now, the tree does not have a uniform thickness; it tapers as it grows upward. The Lewis Equation does **exactly the same** for a gear tooth:

1. It treats the **tooth as a cantilever beam** sticking out of the gear blank.
2. It calculates the **maximum bending stress** at the critical section (the fillet root of the tooth).
3. It uses a special coefficient called the **Lewis Form Factor (y)** to account for the **shape and geometry** of the tooth — just as the tapering shape of a tree makes it more resilient to wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **signboard pole** fixed in concrete. The wind applies force at the top, but the bending stress is highest at the base where the pole meets the concrete. The Lewis equation does this same calculation for a gear tooth, where the "wind" is the torque transmitted through the mesh.

### 1.3 Underlying Assumptions (Lewis's Original Hypothesis)

The Lewis Equation is built upon certain idealized assumptions, which must be understood for proper application:

1. The **tangential load $W_T$** acts at the **tip of the tooth** (though for analysis, it is often taken at the pitch point for practical purposes).
2. The effect of the **radial component** of the tooth load is **neglected** (it does not contribute significantly to bending).
3. The tooth is considered as a **cantilever beam of uniform strength** — meaning the stress is constant across the critical section.
4. The **load is shared equally** between the two teeth in contact (i.e., load is assumed to be distributed between a pair of teeth in mesh).
5. The **stress concentration at the tooth root fillet** is **not accounted for** in the basic Lewis equation (this is a known limitation).
6. The tooth shape is considered as a **straight-sided trapezoid** (not the actual involute curve).

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation to Note:** Modern standards like **AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association)** and **IS 2458** have superseded the basic Lewis Equation by incorporating **dynamic factors, load distribution factors, geometry factors, and stress concentration factors**. However, Lewis remains the foundational concept in all KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background — Step-by-Step Logic

The derivation and application of the Lewis Equation involves the following logical chain:

1. **Power Transmission Recognition:** In a gear pair, the driver gear applies a tangential force $W_T$ on the driven gear at the pitch circle radius. This force is what causes the tooth to bend.

2. **Cantilever Beam Model:** Lewis idealized each gear tooth as a **cantilever beam** projecting from the gear rim. The fixed end is at the tooth root, and the load $W_T$ acts at the tooth tip.

3. **Bending Stress Formulation:** The standard bending stress equation for a cantilever beam is:
   $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$$
   Where $M$ = Bending Moment and $Z$ = Section Modulus.

4. **Form Factor Concept:** The complex geometry of the involute tooth cannot be directly analyzed using simple beam equations. Lewis therefore introduced a **form factor (y)** that captures the influence of **tooth shape, number of teeth, and pressure angle** on the bending strength.

5. **Beam Strength:** The maximum tangential load that a tooth can transmit without failure is called the **Beam Strength ($S_b$)**:
   $$S_b = b \times y \times m \times \sigma_b$$

6. **Wear Strength:** Compares against the wear (contact) strength for final design verification.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **all critical formulas** required for solving KTU 2024 problems on the Lewis Equation. Memorize these — they form the core of every gear design problem.

| **#** | **Parameter / Formula** | **Expression** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Lewis Equation (Bending Stress) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_T \times C_s}{b \times y \times m}$ | MPa |
| 2 | Tangential Tooth Load | $W_T = \dfrac{2T}{d} = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | N |
| 3 | Beam Strength of Tooth | $S_b = b \times y \times m \times \sigma_b$ | N |
| 4 | Power Transmitted | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | kW |
| 5 | Pitch Circle Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60{,}000}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $d = m \times z$ | mm |
| 7 | Lewis Form Factor (20° full depth involute, z ≥ 18) | $y = 0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{z}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Standard Module Series (preferred) | 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 | mm |
| 9 | Standard Pressure Angles | $\alpha = 20°$ (preferred), $14.5°$ (obsolete) | degrees |
| 10 | Design Constraint | $S_b \geq C_s \times W_T$ | N |
