# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define wear load. State Archard's equation of adhesive wear and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Wear load is the engineering parameter representing the gradual removal of material from a contacting surface subjected to repeated sliding under a normal load, leading to dimensional change and eventual loss of function.

> **Equation (1 mark):**
> $$V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}$$

> **Significance of terms (1 mark):**
> - $V$ = volume of worn material in $\text{mm}^3$
> - $K$ = wear coefficient (dimensionless), typically $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-4}$
> - $W$ = normal load in N
> - $L$ = sliding distance in mm
> - $H$ = hardness of the softer contacting surface in MPa

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the PV product in bearing design? Why is it a critical design parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The PV product is the product of the bearing pressure $P$ (in MPa) and the sliding velocity $V$ (in m/s) at the bearing interface. It quantifies the **frictional heat-generation rate per unit area** at the contact.

> **Significance (1.5 marks):** Each bearing material has a maximum permissible $(PV)_{limit}$. If the operating $(PV)_{actual}$ exceeds this limit, the lubricant film breaks down, temperature rises sharply, and catastrophic seizure or scuffing occurs. Hence PV product governs the safe operating envelope of journal bearings, bushings, and thrust bearings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-3 Internal-Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse wear coefficient $K$ (dimensionless) with specific wear rate $k_s$ (has units $\text{mm}^3/\text{Nm}$). Examiners deduct **half a mark** for every slip.
> - **Always** convert hardness to consistent units with load and area. Mixing MPa and $\text{N/mm}^2$ is fine (they are equal), but mixing MPa with $\text{kgf/mm}^2$ will lose 1 mark.
> - **Always** state the assumption "$W$, $H$, $K$ are constant over the sliding interval" before integrating. Skipping this assumption costs 1 mark.
> - **Always** close with an engineering recommendation; a numerical answer without design commentary is capped at **10/14 marks** by typical KTU valuation keys.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 marks)

A pin joint in a suspension linkage operates under the following conditions:
- Normal load on pin: $W = 5000 \, \text{N}$
- Oscillating angular velocity equivalent sliding velocity: $v = 1.5 \, \text{m/s}$
- Pin outer diameter: $D = 25 \, \text{mm}$, length: $L_{pin} = 40 \, \text{mm}$
- Material: steel-on-bronze bushing, bushing hardness $H = 350 \, \text{MPa}$
- Wear coefficient for steel-on-bronze lubricated: $K = 5 \times 10^{-7}$
- Allowable linear wear on bushing bore: $h_{allow} = 50 \, \mu\text{m}$

#### Sub-part (a) — Compute the volumetric wear rate and the expected service life in hours. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute apparent contact area of the bushing bore.**

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L_{pin} = \pi \times 25 \times 40 = 3141.6 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute bearing pressure.**

$$P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{5000}{3141.6} = 1.5915 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Archard's wear-rate equation.**

$$\dot{V} = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot v}{H} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{5000 \times 1.5}{350}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$W \cdot v = 7500 \, \text{N·m/s}$$

Compute the fraction:

$$\frac{7500}{350} = 21.4286 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Multiply by $K$:

$$\dot{V} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 21.4286 = 1.0714 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

> **Volumetric wear rate = $1.0714 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$. (3 marks awarded for steps 1–3)**

**Step 4 — Compute allowable sliding distance before $h_{allow}$ is reached.**

Using $h = K P L / H$, solve for $L_{max}$:

$$L_{max} = \frac{h_{allow} \cdot H}{K \cdot P} = \frac{0.050 \times 350}{5 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.5915}$$

Numerator:

$$0.050 \times 350 = 17.5$$

Denominator:

$$5 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.5915 = 7.9575 \times 10^{-7}$$

Divide:

$$L_{max} = \frac{17.5}{7.9575 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.199 \times 10^{7} \, \text{mm} = 21{,}990 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Convert sliding distance to operating time.**

$$t_{life} = \frac{L_{max}}{v} = \frac{21{,}990}{1.5} = 14{,}660 \, \text{s}$$

Convert to hours:

$$t_{life} = \frac{14{,}660}{3600} \approx 4.07 \, \text{hr}$$

> **Service life $\approx 4.07 \, \text{hr}$. (4 marks awarded for steps 4–5)**

#### Sub-part (b) — Is the design adequate for a target service life of 500 hours? If not, recommend two engineering modifications. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Comparison.**

Required life = 500 hr; achievable life = 4.07 hr. The design is grossly inadequate. **The design is NOT adequate.** (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Modification 1: Increase bushing hardness.**

If we replace the bronze bushing with a heat-treated steel bushing of $H_{new} = 1100 \, \text{MPa}$, life scales as $H_{new}/H_{old}$:

$$t_{life,new} = 4.07 \times \frac{1100}{350} = 12.79 \, \text{hr}$$

Still inadequate, so we need a stronger modification.

**Step 3 — Modification 2: Change wear coefficient via lubrication regime.**

Switching from boundary to hydrodynamic lubrication drops $K$ from $5 \times 10^{-7}$ to roughly $10^{-9}$. Combined with the harder material:

$$t_{life,new} = 4.07 \times \frac{1100}{350} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-9}} = 4.07 \times 3.143 \times 500 \approx 6395 \, \text{hr}$$

This comfortably exceeds the 500-hr target. (3 marks)

**Step 4 — Closing recommendation.**

> Therefore the design can be made adequate by **(i)** upgrading the bushing material to through-hardened steel (BHN ≈ 1100 MPa) **and (ii)** ensuring hydrodynamic lubrication regime through proper oil-feed grooves and viscosity-grade selection. (2 marks for recommendation)

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 marks)

A hydrodynamic journal bearing in a gearbox has the following specifications:
- Journal diameter: $D = 80 \, \text{mm}$
- Bearing length: $L_{b} = 60 \, \text{mm}$
- Rotational speed: $N = 1500 \, \text{rpm}$
- Radial load on bearing: $W = 12{,}000 \, \text{N}$
- Candidate material: leaded bronze with $(PV)_{limit} = 200 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s}$

#### Sub-part (a) — Verify whether the candidate material is suitable using the PV-product criterion. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute bearing projected area.**

$$A_{proj} = D \times L_{b} = 80 \times 60 = 4800 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute bearing pressure.**

$$P = \frac{W}{A_{proj}} = \frac{12{,}000}{4800} = 2.5 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute sliding velocity at the journal surface.**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.080 \times 1500}{60}$$

Compute numerator:

$$\pi \times 0.080 \times 1500 = 376.99$$

Divide:

$$v = \frac{376.99}{60} = 6.283 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute PV product.**

$$(PV)_{actual} = 2.5 \times 6.283 = 15.71 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with material limit.**

$$(PV)_{actual} = 15.71 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s} \quad \ll \quad (PV)_{limit} = 200 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s}$$

Factor of safety:

$$FoS = \frac{(PV)_{limit}}{(PV)_{actual}} = \frac{200}{15.71} \approx 12.7$$

> **Conclusion (a):** The candidate material is **comfortably suitable** with a very high factor of safety of ~12.7 against PV-limit failure. (7 marks)

#### Sub-part (b) — If the rotational speed is increased to 4500 rpm for an uprated gearbox, recompute the PV product. If the new PV exceeds the limit, suggest two alternative materials with higher PV limits. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute new sliding velocity.**

$$v_{new} = \frac{\pi \times 0.080 \times 4500}{60} = \frac{1130.97}{60} = 18.85 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute new PV product (pressure unchanged).**

$$(PV)_{new} = 2.5 \times 18.85 = 47.12 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with material limit.**

$$(PV)_{new} = 47.12 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s} \quad < \quad (PV)_{limit} = 200 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s}$$

The material is **still safe** even at 4500 rpm. The factor of safety is:

$$FoS_{new} = \frac{200}{47.12} \approx 4.24$$

(5 marks for steps 1–3)

**Step 4 — However, the bearing designer notes that at 4500 rpm the bearing enters mixed lubrication regime.**

To future-proof the design, two alternative materials with higher $(PV)_{limit}$ are recommended:

- **Tin-based Babbitt (Babbitt metal):** $(PV)_{limit} \approx 600 \text{ to } 1000 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s}$, excellent conformability, used in high-speed turbomachinery.
- **PTFE-lined composite bushing:** $(PV)_{limit} \approx 1000 \text{ to } 5000 \, \text{MPa \cdot m/s}$, self-lubricating, used in aircraft control linkages.

(2 marks for alternative-material recommendations)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Wear Load" — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3**

- **Governing equation to memorise verbatim:**
  $$V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}$$
- **Five wear modes to memorise in order:** Abrasive → Adhesive → Surface fatigue → Corrosive → Erosive.
- **Wear coefficient $K$ typical values:**
  - Mild wear (good lubrication): $K \approx 10^{-8} \text{ to } 10^{-6}$
  - Severe wear (boundary): $K \approx 10^{-5} \text{ to } 10^{-4}$
- **Specific wear rate relation:** $k_s = K/H$ (units $\text{mm}^3/\text{Nm}$). Always state this when comparing materials.
- **Bearing pressure formula:** $P = W/A$ where $A$ is the **projected area** ($D \times L$ for journal bearings).
- **Sliding velocity for a rotating journal:** $v = \pi D N / 60$ — always convert $D$ to metres.
- **PV-product criterion:** Compare $P \times v$ with material $(PV)_{limit}$. Common limits: Babbitt 100–600, Bronze 200, PTFE 1000+, Carbon-graphite 500+ (all in $\text{MPa \cdot m/s}$).
- **Service-life formula (linear form):**
  $$t_{life} = \frac{h_{allow} \cdot H}{K \cdot P \cdot v}$$
- **Hardness trend:** Wear life is **directly proportional** to hardness $H$. Doubling hardness doubles life (all else equal).
- **Lubrication trend:** Wear life is **inversely proportional** to $K$. Better lubrication lowers $K$ dramatically.
- **Always close a wear-design Part-B answer** with a quantified material/lubrication upgrade recommendation.
- **Typical exam trap:** $K$ is **dimensionless**, but $k_s$ has units. Mixing them up is the #1 mark-loss point.
- **Conversion traps:** $1 \, \text{MPa} = 1 \, \text{N/mm}^2$; $1 \, \text{m/s} = 1000 \, \text{mm/s}$. Watch unit cancellation in every equation.
- **Engineering judgement callout:** Even when $(PV)_{actual} < (PV)_{limit}$, if $K > 10^{-5}$, the design is operating in severe-wear territory and a lubrication review is mandatory before commissioning.

> **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — PBAUT604 / Module 3 / Wear Load.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Endurance Strength of Tooth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Endurance Strength of Tooth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Endurance Strength (σ_e or S_e):** It is the maximum value of completely reversed stress that a material (or a machine component, such as a gear tooth) can withstand for an **infinite number of stress cycles** (typically N = 10⁶ to 10⁸ cycles) without exhibiting fatigue failure.

In the context of **Automotive Component Design (Gear Design Module)**, the **endurance strength of a gear tooth** is defined as the maximum repetitive **bending stress** that the tooth can sustain over its entire service life (number of load cycles) without initiating a fatigue crack at the root fillet — the most critical, stress-concentrated region of the tooth.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$

where each $k$ represents a **Marin factor** that modifies the ideal rotating-beam endurance limit $S_e'$ to account for real-world operating conditions of the gear.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Paperclip Analogy**
> Imagine bending a steel paperclip back and forth. The first few bends are easy, but eventually it snaps — even though the bending force you applied each time was far below the force needed to break it in a single pull. This is **fatigue failure**.
> The **endurance strength** is the bending force below which you could bend the paperclip **forever** without it ever snapping.

For a **gear tooth**, every single revolution creates a load pulse on that tooth:
- **0 N** → when the tooth is not in mesh
- **F_max N** → when it is in full mesh

This **pulsating stress cycle** (0 → σ_max → 0) repeats millions of times. Endurance strength tells us the maximum value of $\sigma_{max}$ below which the tooth will survive the design life of the gearbox.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **For steels:** $S_e' \approx 0.506 \cdot S_{ut}$ (when $S_{ut}$ is in **kpsi**)
> - **For steels (SI units):** $S_e' \approx 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}$ (when $S_{ut}$ is in **MPa**)
> - **Design cycle baseline (gears):** N = 10⁶ cycles for automotive gear applications
> - **Standard reference diameter for rotating beam test:** d = 7.62 mm (0.3 in)

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Scheme)

- Endurance strength forms the **foundation of wear strength (pitting resistance) and bending strength** calculations in gear design.
- It is **directly applied** in the **Buckingham's equation** for surface durability.
- It is a **high-weightage topic** for Part-B (14-mark) questions involving design of spur/helical gears.
- It is closely linked to **Course Outcome CO2**: *Design automotive power transmission components for static and fatigue loads.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fatigue Cycle in a Gear Tooth

A gear tooth does **not** experience fully reversed stress (–σ to +σ). Instead, it experiences a **pulsating stress cycle** from 0 to σ_max as shown below:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{2}, \quad \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{2}$$

where:
- $\sigma_a$ = **alternating (amplitude) stress**
- $\sigma_m$ = **mean (steady) stress**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pulsating and not reversed?**
> When a tooth is out of mesh, the bending stress is **zero** (no load). When it engages, the full tangential force $F_t$ is applied. Hence the stress oscillates between 0 and $\sigma_{max}$, never becoming compressive.

## 2.2 Failure Criteria (Modified Goodman, Soderberg, Gerber)

For a gear tooth under pulsating bending, the **most widely used criterion** is the **Modified Goodman Equation**:

$$\frac{1}{N} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$$

Rearranged to find the **allowable working stress** $\sigma_d$:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{S_e}{\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} + 1} = \frac{S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{\sigma_m \cdot (1 + S_{ut}/S_e)}$$

For pure pulsating load (where $\sigma_m = \sigma_a$):

$$\sigma_d = \frac{S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} + S_e}$$

## 2.3 Marin Factors — Detailed Breakdown

| Factor | Symbol | Function | Governing Condition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Surface Factor | $k_a$ | Accounts for surface finish (grinding, polishing, forging) | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ |
| Size Factor | $k_b$ | Accounts for component size (probability of flaws) | $k_b = 1.24 \cdot d^{-0.107}$ for $2.79 < d \leq 51$ mm |
| Load Factor | $k_c$ | Pure bending = 1, Axial = 0.85, Torsion = 0.59 | $k_c = 1.0$ for gear teeth |
| Temperature Factor | $k_d$ | Reduces strength at elevated T | $k_d = 1.0$ for T ≤ 450 °C |
| Reliability Factor | $k_e$ | Statistical scatter in test data | $k_e = 0.814$ (R=99%), $0.897$ (R=90%), $1.0$ (R=50%) |
| Miscellaneous Factor | $k_f$ | Notch, residual stress, corrosion, fretting | $k_f = 1.0$ if not specified |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-grade** and must be memorized for Part-B derivations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S_e' = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}$ | Uncorrected endurance limit (steel) | MPa |
| 2 | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \cdot S_e'$ | Marin-modified endurance limit | MPa |
| 3 | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ | Surface factor (a, b from table) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $k_b = 1.24 \cdot d^{-0.107}$ | Size factor (for $2.79 \leq d \leq 51$ mm) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\frac{1}{N} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$ | Modified Goodman criterion | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma_d = \frac{S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} + S_e}$ | Allowable stress for pulsating load | MPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{b} = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y}$ | Lewis bending stress in gear tooth | MPa |
| 8 | $F_{eff} = \frac{\sigma_d \cdot b \cdot m \cdot y}{C_s}$ | Effective (design) load on tooth | N |

> **Note on Pipe Symbol:** In all math expressions above, the absolute-value notation $\vert x \vert$ has been written explicitly to avoid markdown table breakage. Use $\lvert \sigma \rvert$ or $\mid \sigma \mid$ in handwritten scripts.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive gearboxes:** Determines service life before tooth root crack initiation.
- **Differentials and transfer cases:** Critical for off-road and heavy-load vehicles.
- **Transmission design:** Endurance limit forms the basis of **AGMA** and **BS** standard gear rating charts.
- **Failure analysis:** Fatigue fracture at the tooth root fillet is the **#1 mode of gear failure** in service, making this topic essential for condition monitoring and reliability engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Modified Goodman Equation for a Gear Tooth

The **Modified Goodman Line** is constructed on a $\sigma_m$–$\sigma_a$ coordinate system, joining two experimentally verified points:
- **Point A (Y-axis):** $(0, S_e)$ — fully reversed stress with amplitude $S_e$
- **Point B (X-axis):** $(S_{ut}, 0)$ — static failure stress $S_{ut}$

**Step 1 — Equation of a straight line passing through A and B:**

Using two-point form:

$$\frac{\sigma_a - S_e}{0 - S_e} = \frac{\sigma_m - 0}{S_{ut} - 0}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiplying the denominators:**

$$S_{ut} \cdot (\sigma_a - S_e) = -S_e \cdot \sigma_m$$

**Step 3 — Expanding and rearranging:**

$$S_{ut} \cdot \sigma_a - S_{ut} \cdot S_e = -S_e \cdot \sigma_m$$

$$S_{ut} \cdot \sigma_a + S_e \cdot \sigma_m = S_{ut} \cdot S_e$$

**Step 4 — Dividing throughout by $S_{ut} \cdot S_e$:**

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Introducing the Factor of Safety $N$:**

For a design where the working stresses $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ are scaled by $N$:

$$\frac{N \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{N \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$

**Step 6 — Final Modified Goodman Form:**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{N} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The Goodman line is **conservative** (lies below Gerber parabola and Soderberg line), making it the **safest** choice for gear design under pulsating loads.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Allowable Stress for a Gear Tooth in Pure Pulsating Bending

For a gear tooth, the bending stress varies from **0 to σ_max** (no compressive component).

**Step 1 — Identify mean and alternating components:**

$$\sigma_m = \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Modified Goodman equation with $N$ as the design factor of safety:**

$$\frac{1}{N} = \frac{\sigma_{max}/2}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_{max}/2}{S_{ut}}$$

**Step 3 — Take $\sigma_{max}/2$ as a common factor:**

$$\frac{1}{N} = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{2} \left( \frac{1}{S_e} + \frac{1}{S_{ut}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Take the common denominator inside the bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{N} = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{2} \cdot \frac{S_{ut} + S_e}{S_e \cdot S_{ut}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\sigma_{max}$ and define it as the allowable design stress $\sigma_d$:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_d = \frac{2 \cdot N \cdot S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} + S_e}$$

**Step 6 — For a design factor of safety $N = 1$ (no safety factor applied), the endurance strength equals:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_d = \frac{2 \cdot S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} + S_e}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derived $\sigma_d$ is the **maximum safe pulsating bending stress** that the gear tooth can endure. It is directly compared to the **Lewis bending stress** $\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y}$ to determine the design safety margin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Factor Affects the Endurance Limit

| Operating Condition | $S_{ut}$ (MPa) | $k_a$ | $k_b$ (d=20 mm) | $k_e$ (R=90%) | $S_e$ (MPa) | Effect on Life |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Polished steel tooth, room T | 600 | 1.00 | 0.91 | 0.897 | 244.7 | High life |
| Ground steel tooth, 100 °C | 600 | 0.94 | 0.91 | 0.897 | 229.7 | Slight reduction |
| Hot-rolled steel tooth, 200 °C | 600 | 0.72 | 0.91 | 0.897 | 176.4 | Moderate reduction |
| Machined forged tooth, T=400 °C | 600 | 0.81 | 0.91 | 0.85 | 187.7 | Significant reduction |

> This matrix illustrates that **surface finish, size, reliability, and temperature** all combine multiplicatively — a poor surface finish alone can reduce endurance life by **30%** for the same material.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A gear tooth in an automotive transmission is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa. The tooth is ground, operates at 80 °C, has a critical section diameter equivalent of 25 mm, and requires 99% reliability. Compute the endurance limit of the tooth.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit:**

$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 700 = 350 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor $k_a$ for ground finish (Shigley table):**

For ground surface, $a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$:

$$k_a = 1.58 \times 700^{-0.085} = 1.58 \times 0.561 = 0.886$$

**Step 3 — Size factor $k_b$ for d = 25 mm:**

$$k_b = 1.24 \times 25^{-0.107} = 1.24 \times 0.644 = 0.799$$

**Step 4 — Load factor $k_c = 1.0$ (pure bending)**

**Step 5 — Temperature factor $k_d = 1.0$ (T = 80 °C < 450 °C)**

**Step 6 — Reliability factor $k_e$ for 99% reliability:**

$$k_e = 0.814$$

**Step 7 — Miscellaneous factor $k_f = 1.0$**

**Step 8 — Apply the Marin equation:**

$$S_e = 0.886 \times 0.799 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.814 \times 1.0 \times 350$$

$$S_e = 0.5765 \times 350 = 201.8 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The corrected endurance limit is **only ~28.7%** of the ultimate tensile strength — highlighting why fatigue is the **design-limiting criterion** in gears, not static strength.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Modified Goodman Diagram for a Gear Tooth

The following Mermaid block renders the **stress-cycle architecture** mapping the relationship between alternating stress, mean stress, endurance limit, and ultimate tensile strength — replacing the physical drawing with a clean functional block flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Load on Tooth] --> B{Load Type?}
    B -->|Fully Reversed| C[Sigma_a only, Sigma_m = 0]
    B -->|Pulsating| D[Sigma_a = Sigma_m = Sigma_max / 2]
    B -->|Fluctuating| E[Custom Sigma_a and Sigma_m]

    C --> F[Modified Goodman Line Check]
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G{Point lies below Goodman line?}
    G -->|Yes, Safe| H[Infinite Life Region, N greater than 10 to power 6]
    G -->|No, Unsafe| I[Finite Life Region, Apply Soderberg or Gerber]

    H --> J[Allowable Design Stress Sigma_d computed]
    I --> J

    J --> K[Compare with Lewis Bending Stress Sigma_b]
    K --> L{Is Sigma_b less than Sigma_d?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Design is SAFE for Bending Fatigue]
    L -->|No| N[REDESIGN: Increase module or face width]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Marin Factor Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Se0[Uncorrected Endurance Limit S sub e prime equals 0.5 times S ut] --> Ka[Surface Factor k sub a]
    Se0 --> Kb[Size Factor k sub b]
    Se0 --> Kc[Load Factor k sub c]
    Se0 --> Kd[Temperature Factor k sub d]
    Se0 --> Ke[Reliability Factor k sub e]
    Se0 --> Kf[Miscellaneous Factor k sub f]

    Ka --> Combine[Multiplicative Combination]
    Kb --> Combine
    Kc --> Combine
    Kd --> Combine
    Ke --> Combine
    Kf --> Combine

    Combine --> SeFinal[Corrected Endurance Limit S sub e used in Goodman Equation]

    style Se0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SeFinal fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Combine fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Is the Gear Tooth Safe?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Compute Design Bending Stress Sigma_b] --> Q1{Is Sigma_b less than Sigma_d?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Pass[Design Passes Bending Fatigue Check]
    Q1 -->|No| Action[Required Action]
    Action --> A1[Increase module m]
    Action --> A2[Increase face width b]
    Action --> A3[Use superior material, higher S ut]
    Action --> A4[Improve surface finish, higher k sub a]

    A1 --> Q1
    A2 --> Q1
    A3 --> Q1
    A4 --> Q1

    style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Pass fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Action fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are designed to be **rendered as concept maps** in your answer scripts. Drawing the Goodman diagram manually on graph paper with a clear $\sigma_m$ on X-axis and $\sigma_a$ on Y-axis is a guaranteed scoring technique for 14-mark Part-B answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**Define endurance strength of a gear tooth. Why is it important in gear design?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Endurance strength ($S_e$) is the maximum value of completely reversed stress that a gear tooth can withstand for an **infinite number of stress cycles (N = 10⁶)** without fatigue failure.
> **Importance (1.5 Marks):** In automotive gearboxes, every tooth undergoes a **pulsating stress cycle** (0 → σ_max) for millions of revolutions. The endurance strength provides the upper safe stress limit, ensuring the gear survives the design life without root crack initiation. It forms the basis of the **Lewis equation** for design against **bending fatigue**, which is the most common failure mode in automotive gears.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
**List any four Marin factors used in the modification of the endurance limit.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Surface factor ($k_a$)** – accounts for surface finish (grinding, polishing, forging).
> 2. **Size factor ($k_b$)** – accounts for the probability of internal flaws in larger sections.
> 3. **Load factor ($k_c$)** – accounts for the type of loading (bending, axial, torsion).
> 4. **Temperature factor ($k_d$)** – reduces strength at elevated operating temperatures.
> *(Bonus point 0.5 marks for mentioning Reliability and Miscellaneous factors.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often mix $S_{ut}$ in kpsi and $S_e$ in MPa — this leads to a **2-mark penalty**. Always convert to a single unit system first.
> 2. **Mean vs Alternating Confusion:** For pulsating load, students forget that $\sigma_m = \sigma_a$ — this is a **favourite trap question** in KTU papers.
> 3. **Factor Omission:** Leaving out $k_e$ (reliability) or $k_b$ (size) in the Marin equation often costs **1.5 to 2 marks** despite arriving at a "correct-looking" answer.
> 4. **Static vs Fatigue:** Using $S_{ut}/N$ (static design) instead of the **Goodman-based $\sigma_d$** is a guaranteed **3-mark loss** in gear-specific problems.

---

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**A gear tooth in an automobile transmission is made of alloy steel with $S_{ut} = 750$ MPa and $S_{yt} = 550$ MPa. The tooth is ground, has an equivalent critical-section diameter of 30 mm, operates at 60 °C, and the design requires 90% reliability. The tooth carries a tangential load of 4.5 kN, has a face width of 40 mm, a module of 4 mm, and a Lewis form factor of 0.32.**

**(a)** Compute the corrected endurance limit of the gear tooth material using all relevant Marin factors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Modified Goodman criterion, determine the **allowable design bending stress** for the tooth and check whether the **Lewis bending stress** is within the safe limit. Comment on the design. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Endure Strength Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit for steel (1 Mark):**

$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 750 = 375 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor $k_a$ for ground finish (1 Mark):**

From standard Shigley table, for ground surface: $a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$:

$$k_a = 1.58 \times 750^{-0.085} = 1.58 \times 0.553 = 0.874$$

**Step 3 — Size factor $k_b$ for d = 30 mm (1 Mark):**

$$k_b = 1.24 \times 30^{-0.107} = 1.24 \times 0.628 = 0.779$$

**Step 4 — Load factor $k_c$ for pure bending (0.5 Mark):**

$$k_c = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Temperature factor $k_d$ for 60 °C (0.5 Mark):**

$$k_d = 1.0 \text{ (since } T \leq 450 \text{ °C)}$$

**Step 6 — Reliability factor $k_e$ for 90% reliability (1 Mark):**

$$k_e = 0.897$$

**Step 7 — Miscellaneous factor $k_f$ (0.5 Mark):**

$$k_f = 1.0 \text{ (assumed no additional effects)}$$

**Step 8 — Final Marin-modified endurance limit (1.5 Marks):**

$$S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \cdot S_e'$$

$$S_e = 0.874 \times 0.779 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 375$$

$$S_e = 0.611 \times 375 = 229.1 \text{ MPa}$$

---

**Part (b) — Allowable Stress and Design Check (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify mean and alternating stresses for pulsating load (1 Mark):**

Since the tooth is unloaded when out of mesh:

$$\sigma_m = \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Lewis bending stress (1.5 Marks):**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y} = \frac{4500}{40 \times 4 \times 0.32} = \frac{4500}{51.2} = 87.89 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Modified Goodman for design factor of safety $N = 1$ (1.5 Marks):**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{2 \cdot S_e \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} + S_e}$$

$$\sigma_d = \frac{2 \times 229.1 \times 750}{750 + 229.1} = \frac{343650}{979.1} = 350.99 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compare Lewis stress with allowable stress (1.5 Marks):**

$$\sigma_b = 87.89 \text{ MPa} \ll \sigma_d = 350.99 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{Factor of Safety} = \frac{\sigma_d}{\sigma_b} = \frac{350.99}{87.89} = 3.99 \approx 4$$

**Step 5 — Design comment (1.5 Marks):**

The actual factor of safety is **4**, which is well above the typical automotive gear design requirement of $N = 1.5$ to $2$. The design is **highly conservative**; the gear module or face width could be reduced to optimize weight and cost, OR a lower-grade material could be used.

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]

**The pinion of an automotive gearbox is machined from a forged steel having $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa. The tooth is hot-rolled finished, has a critical section diameter of 18 mm, operates at a temperature of 120 °C, and must perform reliably with 99% confidence for an infinite number of cycles.**

**(a)** Using the Marin equation, determine the corrected endurance limit of the pinion tooth and compare it with the static yield strength. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the pinion is subjected to a fluctuating bending stress that varies from **0 MPa to 120 MPa**, determine the factor of safety against fatigue failure using the **Modified Goodman** and **Soderberg** criteria. State which criterion is more conservative. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Corrected Endurance Limit (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit (1 Mark):**

$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 620 = 310 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor for hot-rolled finish (1 Mark):**

For hot-rolled, $a = 4.51$, $b = -0.265$:

$$k_a = 4.51 \times 620^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.182 = 0.821$$

**Step 3 — Size factor for d = 18 mm (1 Mark):**

$$k_b = 1.24 \times 18^{-0.107} = 1.24 \times 0.681 = 0.844$$

**Step 4 — Temperature factor for 120 °C (1 Mark):**

$$k_d = 1.0 \text{ (since } T = 120 \text{ °C} < 450 \text{ °C)}$$

**Step 5 — Reliability factor for 99% (1 Mark):**

$$k_e = 0.814$$

**Step 6 — Other factors (0.5 Mark):**

$$k_c = 1.0, \quad k_f = 1.0$$

**Step 7 — Final corrected endurance limit (1.5 Marks):**

$$S_e = 0.821 \times 0.844 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.814 \times 1.0 \times 310$$

$$S_e = 0.564 \times 310 = 174.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comparison:** $S_e = 174.8$ MPa is significantly lower than $S_{yt} \approx 380$ MPa (typical for this $S_{ut}$) — confirming that **fatigue, not yielding, is the design-limiting criterion**.

---

**Part (b) — Factor of Safety Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify mean and alternating stresses (1.5 Marks):**

$$\sigma_{max} = 120 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{min} = 0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_m = \frac{120 + 0}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_a = \frac{120 - 0}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Modified Goodman factor of safety (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{1}{N_G} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$$

$$\frac{1}{N_G} = \frac{60}{174.8} + \frac{60}{620} = 0.3433 + 0.0968 = 0.4401$$

$$N_G = 2.27$$

**Step 3 — Soderberg factor of safety (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{1}{N_S} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}}$$

Assuming $S_{yt} \approx 0.65 \times S_{ut} = 403$ MPa:

$$\frac{1}{N_S} = \frac{60}{174.8} + \frac{60}{403} = 0.3433 + 0.1489 = 0.4922$$

$$N_S = 2.03$$

**Step 4 — Comparison comment (1.5 Marks):**

Since $N_S (2.03) < N_G (2.27)$, the **Soderberg criterion is more conservative**. The choice depends on the failure consequence:
- Soderberg: No yielding **or** fatigue failure
- Goodman: No **catastrophic** failure (allows local yielding)

For automotive gear applications, **Modified Goodman is preferred** as it balances safety with material economy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Endurance Strength of Tooth**

- [ ] **Endurance limit** is defined for **infinite life (N = 10⁶ cycles)**.
- [ ] For steels, the **uncorrected** endurance limit is **0.5 × S_ut**.
- [ ] The **Marin equation** is **multiplicative** — never add the factors.
- [ ] The **surface factor $k_a$** depends on finish: ground > polished > machined > hot-rolled > as-forged.
- [ ] The **size factor $k_b$** reduces strength for **larger** sections (more flaws).
- [ ] The **reliability factor $k_e$** is always **≤ 1.0** and decreases as required reliability increases.
- [ ] A gear tooth experiences **pulsating bending** — $\sigma_m = \sigma_a = \sigma_{max}/2$.
- [ ] The **Modified Goodman line** is the **most commonly used** failure criterion in KTU papers.
- [ ] The **Soderberg criterion** is the **most conservative** (replaces $S_{ut}$ with $S_{yt}$ in the denominator).
- [ ] The **Gerber parabola** is the **least conservative** (rarely used in KTU papers).
- [ ] **Static strength (S_yt)** is almost never the limiting factor in gear design — **fatigue always governs**.
- [ ] Always convert units **before** substituting in formulas (avoid kpsi/MPa mixing).
- [ ] The **factor of safety** for an automotive gear tooth is typically between **1.5 and 3.0**.
- [ ] **Drawing the Goodman diagram** in the answer script guarantees full marks in Part-B problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Durability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surface Durability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Durability** is defined as the ability of the working surfaces of meshing gear teeth (or any rolling/sliding contact pair) to resist failure caused by **surface fatigue**, specifically **pitting, spalling, and micro-spalling**, under repeated Hertzian contact stress cycles over the design life of the component.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Automotive Component Design, surface durability is treated as a **mode-of-failure criterion** distinct from bending fatigue (Lewis equation) and wear. It is governed by the **Hertzian Contact Stress Theory** combined with **Buckingham's Surface Durability Equation**, which predicts whether the contact pressure at the gear tooth flank will exceed the allowable pitting resistance of the material.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon Under a Finger" Model

Imagine pressing your fingertip against an inflated rubber balloon. The pressure at the contact point is enormous, but it is distributed over a tiny elliptical (or rectangular, in the case of gear teeth) "footprint." Now imagine doing this **one million times a day** at slightly different angles.

| Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pressing a finger on a balloon | Hertzian line contact between two gear teeth |
| Tiny rectangular imprint | Contact patch of width **b** |
| Pressure felt on the skin | Maximum contact pressure **$\sigma_c$** |
| Repeated pressing → skin fatigue → cracks | Pitting & spalling on the tooth flank |
| Bone-deep bruise | Sub-surface shear fatigue (Maxwell-Hertz criterion) |

The balloon will eventually develop micro-cracks **not at the surface**, but at a small depth below it, where shear stress is maximum. This is the **birthplace of a pit**. Surface durability is, therefore, the science of ensuring that these sub-surface shear stresses never exceed the endurance limit of the material over the gear's service life.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **Line Contact** — Two cylinders in contact (gear teeth, cam-roller)
> - **Point Contact** — Ball bearing, roller thrust bearing
> - **Formative Radius ($\rho$)** — The effective radius of curvature at the pitch point
> - **Pitting Resistance** — Material property opposing surface fatigue
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Hertzian Line Contact

When two cylindrical bodies (e.g., two gear teeth) are pressed together by a normal force **$F$**, the contact is not a mathematical line but a **narrow rectangular patch** of width **$2b$** and length **$L$** (along the axis of the cylinders).

### Step-by-Step Logic of Contact Formation:
1. **Initial Line Contact** — Without load, two perfect cylinders touch along a single line.
2. **Elastic Deformation** — Under load **$F$**, the material deforms, flattening a small region.
3. **Pressure Distribution** — A semi-elliptical pressure distribution develops across the contact width **$2b$**, peaking at the centerline.
4. **Maximum Contact Stress ($\sigma_c$)** — Occurs at the centerline of the contact patch on the surface.
5. **Maximum Shear Stress ($\tau_{max}$)** — Occurs at a depth of approximately **$0.786 \cdot b$** below the surface. **This is where pitting initiates.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pitting starts BELOW the surface:**
> At the surface, the shear stress is zero (traction-free boundary). As you go deeper, shear stress rises to a maximum at $\approx 0.786b$ and then decays. Repeated cyclic loading causes **sub-surface fatigue cracks** that eventually propagate to the surface, ejecting a small piece of metal — forming a **pit**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}$ | Formative radius of curvature (external contact) | mm |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_1} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}$ | Formative radius (internal contact, e.g., ring gear) | mm |
| 3 | $b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F \rho}{\pi L E}}$ | Half-width of Hertzian line contact | mm |
| 4 | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{F E}{\pi L \rho}}$ | Maximum Hertzian contact pressure | N/mm² |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{2 E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$ | Effective modulus of elasticity | N/mm² |
| 6 | $\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t K_o K_v K_s K_m}{F d_p b \, I}}$ | AGMA Surface Durability Equation (gear tooth) | N/mm² |
| 7 | $\sigma_{c,all} = \dfrac{C_{HR} \, C_p}{C_s \, C_R}$ | Allowable contact stress (AGMA) | N/mm² |
| 8 | $S_F = \dfrac{\sigma_{c,all}}{\sigma_c}$ | Factor of Safety against pitting | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\tau_{max} \approx 0.30 \, \sigma_c$ | Peak ortho-shear stress at depth $0.786b$ | N/mm² |
| 10 | $C_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi \left(\dfrac{1-\mu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\mu_2^2}{E_2}\right)}}$ | Elastic coefficient (surface) | $\sqrt{\text{N/mm}^2}$ |
| 11 | $\mu \approx 0.30$ (steel) | Poisson's ratio for steel | — |
| 12 | $E_{steel} = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm² | Modulus of elasticity (steel) | N/mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In most numerical problems, you can use the **combined steel value** of $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm² for both pinion and gear when both are steel. The elastic coefficient $C_p$ for **steel-on-steel = 189.8 $\sqrt{\text{N/mm}^2}$**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Surface Durability |
|---|---|
| **Manual/Auto Transmission Gears** | Determines pitting life of helical and spur gears under high torque |
| **Differential Crown Wheel & Pinion** | Hypoid gears suffer sliding; surface fatigue limits vehicle life |
| **Wheel Bearings (Tapered Roller)** | Line contact between rollers and races — line-contact Hertzian analysis |
| **Cam-Follower Systems (DOHC engines)** | High cyclic contact loads → pitting on cam lobe |
| **CV Joints & Drive Shafts** | Rolling-element fatigue in tripod/Rzeppa joints |
| **Electric Vehicle Reducers** | High-RPM helical gears demand superior pitting resistance |

> [!TIP]
> **Modern Industry Trend:** With EVs demanding 10,000+ hour gear life, **case-carburized steel (SCM420, 16MnCr5)** and **shot peening** have become industry standards — both are direct consequences of surface durability theory.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Hertzian Contact Pressure (Line Contact)

### Assumptions:
- Two cylinders in **line contact** (parallel axes, no end effects)
- Materials are **homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic**
- Load **$F$** is normal to the contact line
- Contact length along axis = **$L$**
- No friction in the direction perpendicular to load

### Step 1 — Setting Up the Half-Space Stress Equations

Using Boussinesq's solution for a normal point load on an elastic half-space, the stress at depth **$z$** and lateral distance **$x$** from the line of load is given by classical elasticity theory. For a line load per unit length **$p = F/L$**, the stress components in the **$xz$** plane are derived by integrating Boussinesq's 2D Flamant solution.

The contact width **$2b$** emerges as the region where normal pressure $p(x)$ is positive (compressive).

### Step 2 — Hertz's Assumption of Semi-Elliptical Pressure Distribution

Hertz proposed that the pressure distribution is **semi-elliptical**:

$$
p(x) = p_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x^2}{b^2}}
$$

where $p_0$ is the peak pressure and $b$ is the half-width.

### Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition

The total load must equal the integral of pressure over the contact area:

$$
F = \int_{-b}^{+b} L \cdot p(x) \, dx = L \int_{-b}^{+b} p_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x^2}{b^2}} \, dx
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluating the Integral

The standard result of the integral is:

$$
\int_{-b}^{+b} \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x^2}{b^2}} \, dx = \dfrac{\pi b}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
F = L \cdot p_0 \cdot \dfrac{\pi b}{2}
$$

Solving for the peak pressure:

$$
p_0 = \dfrac{2F}{\pi L b}
$$

### Step 5 — Compatibility: Displacement Must Match Geometry

The approach of the two surfaces at the contact is governed by the **elastic deflection** of each cylinder. For a cylinder of radius **$R_i$** and modulus **$E_i$**, the surface deflection at distance **$x$** is:

$$
w_i(x) = \dfrac{2(1-\mu_i^2)}{\pi E_i} \int_{-b}^{+b} \dfrac{p(s) \ln \vert 2b \vert}{} \, ds
$$

The relative approach (sum of deflections of both cylinders) must equal the original geometric gap $\delta_0 - x^2 / (2\rho)$, where $\rho$ is the **formative radius**.

After lengthy but standard Hertzian manipulation, the **compatibility condition** gives:

$$
b^2 = \dfrac{4 F \rho}{\pi L E}
$$

### Step 6 — Solving for Contact Pressure $\sigma_c$

Taking the square root of Step 5 and substituting into Step 4:

$$
\sigma_c = p_0 = \dfrac{2F}{\pi L \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F \rho}{\pi L E}}}
$$

Simplifying algebraically:

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{F E}{\pi L \rho}}
$$

**Final Buckingham/Hertz Result:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{F E}{\pi L \rho}}}
$$

### Step 7 — Sub-Surface Shear Stress (Pitting Initiation Criterion)

The maximum shear stress occurs at depth $z = 0.786b$ on the centerline ($x = 0$):

$$
\tau_{max} = 0.30 \, \sigma_c
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The factor **0.30** is derived from the principal stress difference $\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ at depth $z = 0.786b$. Examiners often ask: *"At what depth does pitting initiate and why?"* — The answer is **0.786b below the surface**, where $\tau_{max} = 0.30 \sigma_c$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Surface Durability of a Spur Gear Pair

**Problem:** A steel spur pinion drives a steel gear. Determine the factor of safety against pitting.

| Parameter | Pinion | Gear |
|---|---|---|
| Pitch circle diameter, $d$ | 100 mm | 250 mm |
| Face width, $F$ | 50 mm | 50 mm |
| Teeth, $Z$ | 20 | 50 |
| Module, $m$ | 5 mm | 5 mm |
| Transmitted power | 10 kW @ 1000 rpm | — |
| Material | Case-hardened steel | EN36 case-hardened |
| Allowable contact stress, $\sigma_{c,all}$ | 1100 N/mm² | 850 N/mm² |

### Step 1 — Tangential Load

$$
W_t = \dfrac{60 P}{2 \pi N \cdot d_p / 1000} = \dfrac{60 \times 10000}{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 0.1} = 954.93 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Pitch Line Velocity

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi d_p N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.1 \times 1000}{60} = 5.236 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Application Factor (assumed)

For uniform power source: $K_a = 1.25$ (moderate shock from pinion drive).

### Step 4 — Dynamic Factor (Velocity Factor)

$$
K_v = \dfrac{A}{A + \sqrt{v}} = \dfrac{50}{50 + \sqrt{5.236}} = \dfrac{50}{52.288} = 0.956
$$

### Step 5 — Size & Load Distribution Factor

Assume $K_s = 1.05$ (size), $K_m = 1.10$ (load distribution).

### Step 6 — Effective Tangential Load

$$
W_{t,eff} = W_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_v \cdot K_s \cdot K_m = 954.93 \times 1.25 \times 0.956 \times 1.05 \times 1.10
$$

$$
W_{t,eff} = 1378.65 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 7 — Formative Radius of Curvature at Pitch Point

For involute teeth, the radius of curvature of pinion tooth at pitch point is:

$$
\rho_1 = \dfrac{d_p \sin\phi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\cos\phi}{\cos\phi - \mu}
$$

Using standard pressure angle $\phi = 20°$ and $\mu = 0$ (simplified):

$$
\rho_1 = \dfrac{100 \times \sin 20°}{2} = 17.10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\rho_2 = \dfrac{250 \times \sin 20°}{2} = 42.75 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 8 — Formative Radius

$$
\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_2} = \dfrac{1}{17.10} + \dfrac{1}{42.75}
$$

$$
\rho = \dfrac{1}{0.0585 + 0.0234} = \dfrac{1}{0.0819} = 12.21 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 9 — Calculate Contact Stress Using Buckingham's Equation

For steel-on-steel: $C_p = 189.8$, $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm².

$$
\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_{t,eff}}{F \cdot d_p \cdot b \cdot I}}
$$

Assuming geometry factor $I = 0.115$ (typical for 20° full-depth spur gear):

$$
\sigma_c = 189.8 \sqrt{\dfrac{1378.65}{50 \times 100 \times 12.21 \times 0.115}}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 189.8 \sqrt{\dfrac{1378.65}{7020.75}} = 189.8 \sqrt{0.1964} = 189.8 \times 0.4431
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 84.10 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 10 — Factor of Safety

The lower of the two materials governs:

$$
S_{pinion} = \dfrac{1100}{84.10} = 13.08
$$

$$
S_{gear} = \dfrac{850}{84.10} = 10.10
$$

$$
\boxed{S_F = \min(S_{pinion}, S_{gear}) = 10.10}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Factor of safety ≈ 10 is excessively high. This indicates **over-design**; the gear can be made smaller, or a lower-grade material can be selected. KTU students should comment on this in their design reports.
