# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define surface durability. Mention any two modes of surface failure in gear teeth. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Surface Durability:** It is the ability of the working surfaces of meshing gear teeth to withstand the repeated Hertzian contact stresses generated during power transmission, without developing surface fatigue failure such as pitting or spalling, over the design life of the gear.

**Two Modes of Surface Failure:**

1. **Pitting** — Formation of small cavities or pits on the tooth flank due to sub-surface fatigue crack initiation at depth $z = 0.786b$, where the maximum shear stress $\tau_{max} = 0.30 \sigma_c$ occurs.

2. **Scuffing** — Adhesive wear caused by direct metal-to-metal contact when the lubricant film breaks down under high sliding velocity and contact pressure, leading to material transfer and welding between surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative modes** that earn credit: Spalling (large flakes), Scoring (abrasive), Rippling (microscale plastic flow).

---

### Q2. State Buckingham's equation for surface compressive stress and explain the significance of the formative radius. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Buckingham's Equation:**

$$
\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t K_o K_v K_s K_m}{F \, d_p \, b \, I}}
$$

where:
- $\sigma_c$ = Hertzian contact stress (N/mm²)
- $C_p$ = Elastic coefficient (189.8 for steel-steel)
- $W_t$ = Tangential load (N)
- $K_o, K_v, K_s, K_m$ = Application, dynamic, size, load-distribution factors
- $F$ = Face width (mm)
- $d_p$ = Pitch diameter of pinion (mm)
- $b$ = Half-width of contact patch (mm)
- $I$ = Geometry factor for pitting resistance (function of tooth form & pressure angle)

**Significance of Formative Radius ($\rho$):**

The formative radius represents the **effective radius of curvature** at the pitch point of meshing gear teeth, calculated as $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}$. It governs the **width of the Hertzian contact patch** — a smaller formative radius produces a narrower contact band and hence a higher peak contact pressure. This is why **small pinions with few teeth are more pitting-prone**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### (a) Derive the expression for maximum Hertzian contact pressure between two cylinders in line contact. State the assumptions clearly. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions of Hertzian Theory:**
1. Materials are **homogeneous, isotropic, and obey Hooke's law**
2. Contact area is **small compared to body dimensions**
3. Surfaces are **smooth** and free of lubricants
4. **No friction** in the contact region
5. Loads are **static or quasi-static** (no dynamic effects)
6. Contact is **line contact** (parallel cylinders)

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1** — Apply a normal load $F$ per unit length $L$, giving $p = F/L$.

**Step 2** — Assume a semi-elliptical pressure distribution:

$$
p(x) = p_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x^2}{b^2}}
$$

**Step 3** — From force equilibrium:

$$
F = L \int_{-b}^{+b} p(x) \, dx = L p_0 \cdot \dfrac{\pi b}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
p_0 = \dfrac{2F}{\pi L b} \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Step 4** — From compatibility (elastic deflection matches geometric gap):

$$
b^2 = \dfrac{4 F \rho}{\pi L E} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

where $\rho$ is the formative radius and $E$ is the effective modulus.

**Step 5** — Substitute (ii) into (i):

$$
\sigma_c = p_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{F E}{\pi L \rho}}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{F E}{\pi L \rho}}}$

#### (b) Two steel gears in mesh have a transmitted load of 2000 N. The formative radius at the pitch point is 8 mm and the face width is 40 mm. Calculate: (i) the contact stress, and (ii) the depth below the surface at which pitting is most likely to initiate. Take $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm². (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $F = 2000$ N, $\rho = 8$ mm, $L = 40$ mm, $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm².

**Step 1 — Maximum Contact Stress:**

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{F E}{\pi L \rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2000 \times 2.1 \times 10^5}{\pi \times 40 \times 8}}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.2 \times 10^8}{1005.31}} = \sqrt{417780.7}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = 646.36 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Half-width of Contact:**

$$
b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F \rho}{\pi L E}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 2000 \times 8}{\pi \times 40 \times 2.1 \times 10^5}}
$$

$$
b = \sqrt{\dfrac{64000}{2.639 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{2.426 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0492 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
z_{max} = 0.786 \, b = 0.786 \times 0.0492
$$

$$
\boxed{z_{max} = 0.0387 \text{ mm} \approx 38.7 \, \mu\text{m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{max} = 0.30 \times \sigma_c = 0.30 \times 646.36 = 193.91 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** Pitting is most likely to initiate at a depth of **38.7 µm** below the contact surface, where the maximum shear stress is **193.91 N/mm²**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

#### (a) Explain the various modes of surface failure in gear teeth with neat sketches. Discuss the remedies for each. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Modes of Surface Failure:**

| Mode | Mechanism | Appearance | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pitting** | Sub-surface fatigue crack at $z = 0.786b$ | Small craters 0.5–2 mm | Increase hardness, shot peening |
| **Spalling** | Brittle material ejection of large flakes | Flaky surface layer | Use tougher material, avoid overheating |
| **Scuffing** | Adhesive welding due to lube film failure | Rough torn surface | EP additives, lower viscosity oil |
| **Scoring** | Abrasive ploughing by debris | Linear scratches | Better filtration, surface finish |
| **Rippling** | Plastic flow from sliding friction | Ripple pattern | Reduce sliding, hypoid to spiral bevel |

**Detailed Explanation:**

**1. Pitting:**
- Occurs when $\sigma_c$ exceeds the **pitting resistance** of the material after a finite number of cycles.
- Initiation site: $z = 0.786b$ where $\tau_{max} = 0.30 \sigma_c$.
- **Remedies:** Case hardening (carburizing to 58–62 HRC), shot peening to induce compressive residual stress, profile modification (tip relief, lead crown), improved lubrication with EP additives.

**2. Spalling:**
- A more severe form of pitting where **large flakes** of material detach due to brittleness.
- Common in **over-hardened** or improperly heat-treated gears.
- **Remedies:** Controlled tempering to reduce brittleness, through-hardening alloy steels (e.g., EN353), proper case depth (1.5–2.5 mm).

**3. Scuffing (Adhesive Wear):**
- Caused by breakdown of the **EHL (elastohydrodynamic) lubricant film** under high Hertzian pressure and sliding velocity.
- Surface temperatures rise due to friction → metal-to-metal contact → **cold welding** → tearing.
- Common in **hypoid gears** (sliding-dominant).
- **Remedies:** EP (Extreme Pressure) additives like sulphur-phosphorus compounds, lower viscosity lubricants, smooth surface finish (Ra ≤ 0.4 µm), better cooling.

**4. Scoring:**
- Mechanical abrasion by **contaminant particles** or hard asperities.
- Appears as **directional scratches** parallel to sliding direction.
- **Remedies:** Improved lubricant filtration (≤ 10 µm), sealing systems, magnetic drain plugs, regular oil changes.

**5. Rippling:**
- Plastic deformation due to **sliding friction** at the tooth flank.
- Visible as a regular wave pattern.
- Common in worm gears and hypoid differentials.
- **Remedies:** Replace hypoid with spiral bevel, use lead-based bronze, improve lubrication regime.

#### (b) A pair of spur gears made of steel have 20° pressure angle, 18 teeth on pinion and 72 teeth on gear, module 6 mm, face width 60 mm. The pinion rotates at 1450 rpm and transmits 22 kW. Calculate the contact stress using Buckingham's equation. Take elastic coefficient $C_p = 189.8$, geometry factor $I = 0.105$. Assume $K_o = 1.5$, $K_v = 1.1$, $K_s = 1.0$, $K_m = 1.25$. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $Z_p = 18$, $Z_g = 72$, $m = 6$ mm
- $\phi = 20°$, $F = 60$ mm
- $N_p = 1450$ rpm, $P = 22$ kW
- $C_p = 189.8$, $I = 0.105$
- $K_o = 1.5$, $K_v = 1.1$, $K_s = 1.0$, $K_m = 1.25$

**Step 1 — Pitch Diameter of Pinion:**

$$
d_p = m \times Z_p = 6 \times 18 = 108 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Tangential Load on Pinion:**

$$
W_t = \dfrac{60 P}{\pi d_p N_p} = \dfrac{60 \times 22000}{\pi \times 0.108 \times 1450}
$$

$$
W_t = \dfrac{1.32 \times 10^6}{491.89} = 2683.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Pitch Line Velocity:**

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi d_p N_p}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.108 \times 1450}{60} = 8.20 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective Tangential Load (Buckingham's Form):**

$$
W_{t,eff} = W_t \cdot K_o \cdot K_v \cdot K_s \cdot K_m
$$

$$
W_{t,eff} = 2683.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.1 \times 1.0 \times 1.25
$$

$$
W_{t,eff} = 5534.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Half-Width of Hertzian Contact (Approximate):**

Using the empirical half-width $b = 0.5 \times m$ for full-depth teeth (simplification), or compute via:

$$
\rho_1 = \dfrac{d_p \sin\phi}{2} = \dfrac{108 \times \sin 20°}{2} = 18.47 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\rho_2 = \dfrac{d_g \sin\phi}{2} = \dfrac{432 \times \sin 20°}{2} = 73.88 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{18.47} + \dfrac{1}{73.88} = 0.0541 + 0.0135 = 0.0676
$$

$$
\rho = 14.79 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Buckingham's Contact Stress:**

$$
\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_{t,eff}}{F \cdot d_p \cdot \rho \cdot I}}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 189.8 \sqrt{\dfrac{5534.7}{60 \times 108 \times 14.79 \times 0.105}}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 189.8 \sqrt{\dfrac{5534.7}{10061.4}} = 189.8 \sqrt{0.5501}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 189.8 \times 0.7417
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = 140.77 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The induced Hertzian contact stress is **140.77 N/mm²**, which should be compared with the allowable contact stress of the chosen material (e.g., 850 N/mm² for case-hardened steel) to obtain the factor of safety against pitting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 0.30** in $\tau_{max} = 0.30 \sigma_c$ — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $\rho_1$ alone** instead of the formative radius $\rho$ — costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up internal vs external contact** — the ring gear of a planetary system uses $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_1} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}$.
> 4. **Forgetting the application factors** $K_o, K_v, K_s, K_m$ in Buckingham's equation — the equation does not yield real design stress without them.
> 5. **Using $W_t$ directly** without multiplying by combined load factors in numerical problems.
> 6. **Not stating the depth $z = 0.786b$** explicitly when explaining pitting initiation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Surface Durability:**

- [x] **Surface Durability** = resistance to pitting, spalling & scuffing under repeated Hertzian contact.
- [x] **Hertzian line contact** produces a rectangular contact patch of width **$2b$** and length **$L$**.
- [x] **Maximum contact stress:** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{F E}{\pi L \rho}}$ — Buckingham/Hertz core equation.
- [x] **Formative radius:** External contact: $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}$; Internal contact: $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_1} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}$.
- [x] **Effective modulus:** $E = \dfrac{2 E_1 E_2}{E_1 + E_2}$; for steel-steel, $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm².
- [x] **Pitting initiates at depth $z = 0.786b$**, where $\tau_{max} = 0.30 \sigma_c$.
- [x] **Buckingham's gear equation:** $\sigma_c = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{W_t K_o K_v K_s K_m}{F d_p b I}}$
- [x] **AGMA elastic coefficient $C_p$** for steel-steel = **189.8** $\sqrt{\text{N/mm}^2}$.
- [x] **Pitting resistance** depends on: hardness (Rc), residual stress, surface finish, lubrication regime, EHL film thickness.
- [x] **Factor of safety** = minimum of (pinion $S$, gear $S$).
- [x] **Failure modes:** Pitting, Spalling, Scuffing, Scoring, Rippling.
- [x] **Remedies:** Case hardening, shot peening, profile modification, EP lubricants, profile shift (positive addendum).
- [x] **KTU-Numerical Recipe:** Always compute $W_t \rightarrow$ multiply by $K$-factors $\rightarrow$ compute $\rho \rightarrow$ apply Buckingham's $\sigma_c$ $\rightarrow$ compute $S_F$.
- [x] **Decimals & Units:** Force in **N**, dimensions in **mm**, stress in **N/mm² (MPa)**. Watch unit conversions ($d_p$ in mm vs m).
- [x] **Velocity check:** If $v > 10$ m/s, $K_v$ shifts towards dynamic loading — use Barth or AGMA velocity factor formula.
- [x] **For helical gears**, the equivalent radius equation uses the **normal module** and **normal pressure angle**, and the axial overlap must be ≤ face width.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Board Exam:**
> **"PIGS SPIT"** →
> **P** — Pitting (sub-surface, $0.786b$)
> **I** — Initiation depth
> **G** — Geometry factor (I)
> **S** — Scuffing (lubrication failure)
> **S** — Spalling (brittle flakes)
> **P** — Pressure (Hertzian, $\sigma_c$)
> **I** — $\tau_{max} = 0.30 \sigma_c$
> **T** — Treatment (case hardening, shot peening)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Dissipation, Lubrication of Gears, Merits and Demerits of Each Type of Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Dissipation, Lubrication of Gears, Merits and Demerits of Each Type of Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heat Dissipation in Gear Drives

### Formal Definition
**Heat Dissipation in gears** is the controlled removal of thermal energy generated due to frictional losses and windage (oil churning) at the meshing teeth and bearings of a gear system. In KTU-syllabus terms, the rated power transmission capacity of a gear box is fundamentally limited by the **thermal rating** (heat balance), which is the equilibrium state where the heat generated by the gears equals the heat dissipated by the gear housing to the surrounding atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A gear box is considered *thermally safe* when:
> **Heat Generated (H_g) ≤ Heat Dissipated (H_d)**
> If H_g > H_d, the oil temperature exceeds its flash point, leading to oil degradation, scuffing, and catastrophic tooth failure.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **pressure cooker on a stove**. The flame generates heat inside the pot. The pot's outer surface then throws this heat into the surrounding air. If the flame is too strong (high friction losses) and the pot is too small (small housing surface area), the pot overheats and burns the food (oil breakdown and gear scuffing). Similarly, a gear box must be *sized* so that its housing surface area is sufficient to radiate away all frictional heat, keeping the lubricant below its critical operating temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics:**
> - Ambient Temperature (T_a) ≈ **313 K to 323 K (40°C to 50°C)** for industrial gearboxes
> - Maximum Permissible Oil Temperature (T_oil) ≈ **363 K to 368 K (90°C to 95°C)** for mineral oils
> - Temperature Rise (ΔT) = T_oil − T_a

---

## 1.2 Lubrication of Gears

### Formal Definition
**Gear Lubrication** is the process of introducing a suitable viscous fluid (mineral oil, synthetic oil, or grease) between the meshing tooth flanks and bearing surfaces to:
1. Reduce frictional wear (boundary and mixed lubrication)
2. Carry away heat (full-film / hydrodynamic lubrication)
3. Prevent corrosion and dampen impact loads (chatter)
4. Flush out wear debris and contaminants

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine **sliding a heavy wooden log on dry sand** versus **on a thin film of water**. With water, the log glides effortlessly because the water molecules roll between the surfaces. Gear lubrication works similarly — at high speeds (PV product), the lubricant forms a full *elastohydrodynamic (EHL) film* that completely separates the asperities (microscopic peaks) of the meshing steel teeth.

---

## 1.3 Merits and Demerits of Different Gear Types

### Formal Definition
The selection of a gear type for an automotive transmission depends on the **relative shaft orientation**, **speed ratio**, **power capacity**, **noise tolerance**, and **manufacturing cost**. Each gear geometry (Spur, Helical, Bevel, Worm, Spiral Bevel, Hypoid) offers a unique trade-off between these parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always present gear comparison answers in a **tabular format** with 5–7 comparative parameters for full marks. Examiners explicitly award marks for systematic tabulation.

### Conceptual Analogy
Spur gears are like **two rulers meshing teeth-to-teeth** (noisy but simple). Helical gears are like **two spiral staircases meshing** (smooth but thrust-producing). Bevel gears are like **two cones intersecting** (for 90° shafts). Worm gears are like a **screw driving a wheel** (self-locking). Hypoid gears are **offset bevels** — like the differential in your car.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Dissipation — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Sources of Heat Generation in a Gearbox

1. **Tooth Friction Losses (H_t):** Power lost at the pitch point due to sliding velocity between meshing flanks. Predominant in worm gears.
2. **Windage / Churning Losses (H_w):** Energy dissipated by the gear teeth pushing through the oil bath. Dominant at high peripheral speeds (> 10 m/s).
3. **Bearing Friction Losses (H_b):** Heat from rolling-element or sleeve bearings.
4. **Seal and Clutch Losses:** Minor contributors but relevant in sealed automotive units.

### 2.1.2 Heat Generated (H_g)

The rate of heat generation equals the total power lost:

$$H_g = (1 - \eta) \times P$$

Where:
- **H_g** = Heat generated per unit time (W or J/s)
- **η** = Overall efficiency of the gear pair
- **P** = Power transmitted (W)

> [!NOTE]
> Typical efficiencies:
> - Spur/Helical gears: **η ≈ 0.98–0.99 (per mesh)**
> - Bevel gears: **η ≈ 0.97–0.98**
> - Worm gears: **η ≈ 0.50–0.90** (highly dependent on lead angle)

### 2.1.3 Heat Dissipated (H_d)

Heat rejected by the gear housing to ambient air via **natural convection** (and to a smaller extent, radiation):

$$H_d = K \times A \times (T_{oil} - T_{ambient})$$

Where:
- **K** = Heat transfer coefficient of housing surface (W/m²·K)
  - Bare steel in still air: **K ≈ 8 to 17 W/m²·K**
  - With fan-assisted cooling: **K ≈ 23 to 35 W/m²·K**
- **A** = Effective surface area of housing exposed to air (m²)
- **T_oil** = Operating oil temperature (°C or K)
- **T_ambient** = Ambient air temperature (°C or K)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Heat Dissipation)

| # | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | H_g = (1 − η) × P | Heat generated in Watts |
| 2 | H_d = K × A × ΔT | Heat dissipated by housing (W) |
| 3 | H_g = H_d at thermal equilibrium | Thermal rating condition |
| 4 | K × A × (T_oil − T_a) ≥ (1 − η) × P | KTU thermal balance inequality |
| 5 | T_oil = T_a + (1 − η) × P / (K × A) | Steady-state oil temperature (°C) |
| 6 | P_rated (thermal) = (K × A × ΔT) / (1 − η) | Maximum transmittable power based on heat |

---

## 2.3 Lubrication of Gears — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Lubrication Regimes (Stribeck Curve Concept)

The friction coefficient (μ) between meshing teeth varies with the **dimensionless Hersey number** (η·N/P), where η = dynamic viscosity, N = speed, P = load per unit length.

| Regime | Film Condition | Friction | Wear |
|--------|----------------|----------|------|
| Boundary Lubrication | Film < asperity height | High | Severe |
| Mixed Lubrication | Partial film | Moderate | Mild |
| Elastohydrodynamic (EHL) | Full film, elastic deformation | Low | Negligible |
| Hydrodynamic | Full film, rigid surfaces | Lowest | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Point:** Automotive gears (especially hypoid axles) operate predominantly in the **EHL regime**. The high contact pressures (~ 1 to 2 GPa) deform the asperities elastically, allowing the oil film to sustain load without metal-to-metal contact.

### 2.3.2 Methods of Gear Lubrication

1. **Splash / Oil Bath Lubrication:**
   - Gears dip into an oil reservoir; teeth carry oil to the mesh.
   - Suitable for peripheral speeds **v ≤ 10 m/s**.
   - Simple, low cost. Used in small automotive gearboxes.

2. **Pressurized / Forced Circulation Lubrication:**
   - Oil pump delivers lubricant through jets to the mesh and bearings.
   - Mandatory for **v > 10 m/s** and high-power automotive transmissions.
   - Allows oil cooling via external heat exchanger.

3. **Oil Mist Lubrication:**
   - Atomized oil carried by compressed air.
   - Used in high-speed enclosed gearboxes (turbines, compressors).

4. **Grease Lubrication:**
   - Used for slow-speed, open gears (e.g., crane slewing rings).

### 2.3.3 Lubricant Viscosity Selection

The **required oil viscosity** is determined using:
- AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association) charts
- The operating pitch line velocity (v) and tooth load

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Accepted):**
> - Low speed, high load → **High viscosity oil (SAE 90 to 250)**
> - High speed, low load → **Low viscosity oil (SAE 75 to 90)**
> - Hypoid gears → **Extreme Pressure (EP) oils** with sulfur-phosphorus additives (due to sliding at hypoid offset)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal rating** dictates the size of automotive transmission housings. Designers add **cooling fins** to increase surface area (A).
- **Lubricant selection** determines the **scuffing load capacity** (pitting resistance) per AGMA 2001 / ISO 6336.
- In electric vehicles (EVs), reduced heat generation from gears (due to fewer moving parts) allows the use of **lower-viscosity transmission fluids** for improved efficiency.
- Worm gear reducers in elevators and conveyors rely on **splash lubrication with high-viscosity compounded oils** to manage the enormous frictional heat.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Thermal Rating of a Gearbox

### Given Data (Typical KTU Problem Format)
- Power transmitted: **P = 30 kW**
- Gear efficiency: **η = 0.96**
- Ambient temperature: **T_a = 45 °C**
- Maximum permissible oil temperature: **T_oil = 90 °C**
- Heat transfer coefficient (bare steel housing): **K = 12 W/m²·K**

### Step 1 — Calculate Heat Generated (H_g)

The power lost as heat is the input power minus the output power.

$$\text{Power lost} = P \times (1 - \eta)$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Power lost} = 30000 \times (1 - 0.96)$$

$$\text{Power lost} = 30000 \times 0.04$$

$$\text{Power lost} = 1200 \text{ W}$$

Therefore:

$$H_g = 1200 \text{ J/s}$$

> **Logic:** Each watt of mechanical power lost becomes one joule per second of thermal energy that must be removed.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Permissible Temperature Rise (ΔT)

The maximum temperature difference between the oil and ambient is:

$$\Delta T = T_{oil} - T_{ambient}$$

$$\Delta T = 90 - 45$$

$$\Delta T = 45 \text{ °C (or K, since it's a difference)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Heat Dissipation Equation

The gearbox housing must dissipate at least 1200 W to remain in thermal equilibrium:

$$H_d = K \times A \times \Delta T \geq H_g$$

Substituting:

$$1200 = 12 \times A \times 45$$

$$1200 = 540 \times A$$

$$A = \frac{1200}{540}$$

$$A = 2.222 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation and Design Recommendation

The housing must expose **at least 2.22 m²** of surface to still air.

> **Design Actions:**
> - Add external cooling fins (each fin adds ~0.05 m² of effective area).
> - Mount an external fan to raise K from 12 to ~30 W/m²·K, reducing required A to ~0.89 m².
> - Use a higher-viscosity oil with better thermal stability if available.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Required Viscosity for Gear Lubrication

### Using the AGMA Simplified Approach

For a spur gear with:
- Pitch line velocity: **v = 5 m/s**
- Tangential load: **W_t = 4000 N**
- Face width: **b = 50 mm = 0.05 m**

### Step 1 — Compute the Load per Unit Face Width

$$w = \frac{W_t}{b}$$

$$w = \frac{4000}{0.05}$$

$$w = 80000 \text{ N/m}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Hersey Number Parameter

The dimensionless parameter governing lubrication regime:

$$\text{Hersey} = \frac{\eta \cdot v}{w}$$

Rearranging for the required viscosity:

$$\eta_{req} = \frac{w \cdot \text{Hersey}_{target}}{v}$$

> **Logic:** For EHL regime in spur gears, **Hersey_target ≈ 1 × 10⁻⁸ m (approximately).**

### Step 3 — Solve

$$\eta_{req} = \frac{80000 \times 1 \times 10^{-8}}{5}$$

$$\eta_{req} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{5}$$

$$\eta_{req} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·s/m}^2$$

Converting to centipoise (1 cP = 10⁻³ N·s/m²):

$$\eta_{req} = 160 \text{ cP} \approx 160 \text{ mPa·s}$$

> **Interpretation:** This corresponds to **SAE 90 grade oil** at operating temperature, confirming correct lubricant selection.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Gear Types (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Spur Gear | Helical Gear | Bevel Gear | Worm Gear | Spiral Bevel | Hypoid Gear |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Shaft Orientation | Parallel | Parallel (crossed possible) | Intersecting (any angle) | Non-intersecting (90°) | Intersecting | Non-intersecting, offset |
| Tooth Alignment | Straight, parallel to axis | Inclined at helix angle (α) | Straight on conical pitch surface | Thread-like on cylinder | Curved (arc) | Spiral with offset |
| Sliding at Mesh | Pure rolling (ideally) | Significant sliding | Sliding + rolling | Pure sliding | Rolling + sliding | Heavy sliding |
| Efficiency (η) | 0.98–0.99 | 0.97–0.99 | 0.97–0.98 | 0.50–0.90 | 0.97–0.99 | 0.85–0.95 |
| Axial Thrust | **None** | **Yes (major demerit)** | Yes (radial + axial) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Noise Level | **High (demerit)** | Low | Moderate | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Load Capacity | Moderate | **High (merit)** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | High | High |
| Speed Capability | Low–High | High | Moderate | Low | High | Moderate |
| Self-Locking | No | No | No | **Yes (merit for safety)** | No | No |
| Typical Application | Gears within meters, simple reducers | Automotive transmissions, machine tools | Differential, hand drills | Elevators, conveyors | Aircraft differentials | Automotive rear axles |
| Manufacturing Cost | **Lowest (merit)** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Heat Flow Architecture in a Gearbox

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Mechanical Power Input P] --> G1[Gear Mesh]
    G1 --> GLoss[Frictional + Windage Losses]
    GLoss --> Hg[Heat Generated Hg]
    Hg --> Housing[Gearbox Housing Surface]
    Housing --> Conv[Convective Heat Transfer]
    Conv --> Amb[Surrounding Ambient Air]
    Housing --> Rad[Radiative Heat Transfer]
    Rad --> Amb
    Pump[Oil Pump] --> Jets[Oil Jets on Mesh]
    Jets --> G1
    Jets --> OilSump[Oil Sump Heat Exchange]
    OilSump --> Housing
    Hg -.-> OilDeg[If Hg gt Hd then Oil Degradation and Scuffing]
    style Hg fill:#ff9999
    style Conv fill:#99ccff
    style Rad fill:#99ccff
    style OilDeg fill:#ff4444,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Mechanical power enters the gearbox; a small fraction is lost as heat, which the housing must reject to ambient. If the housing cannot reject enough heat (H_d < H_g), the oil overheats and gear scuffing results.

---

## 4.2 Lubrication System Architecture — Splash vs Forced

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Splash[Splash Lubrication]
        S1[Oil Sump] --> S2[Gear Teeth Dip]
        S2 --> S3[Mesh Carries Oil Up]
        S3 --> S4[Oil Returns by Gravity]
        S4 --> S1
    end
    subgraph Forced[Forced Circulation Lubrication]
        F1[Oil Sump] --> F2[Oil Pump]
        F2 --> F3[Filter]
        F3 --> F4[Jets on Bearing and Mesh]
        F4 --> F5[Cooler / Heat Exchanger]
        F5 --> F1
    end
    style Splash fill:#e6f3ff
    style Forced fill:#fff4e6
```

---

## 4.3 Gear Type Decision Matrix Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Transmit Power?] --> Para{Shafts Parallel?}
    Para -->|Yes| QuietNoise{Quiet Operation Needed?}
    QuietNoise -->|No| Spur[Spur Gear]
    QuietNoise -->|Yes| Helical[Helical Gear]
    Para -->|No| Intersect{Shafts Intersect?}
    Intersect -->|Yes| HighLoad{High Load and Speed?}
    HighLoad -->|Yes| Spiral[Spiral Bevel Gear]
    HighLoad -->|No| Straight[Straight Bevel Gear]
    Intersect -->|No| Offset{Shaft Offset Required?}
    Offset -->|Yes| Hypoid[Hypoid Gear]
    Offset -->|No| Worm[Worm and Worm Wheel]
    style Spur fill:#cce5ff
    style Helical fill:#cce5ff
    style Straight fill:#cce5ff
    style Spiral fill:#cce5ff
    style Hypoid fill:#cce5ff
    style Worm fill:#cce5ff
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Thermal Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gearbox Rated Power P_rated] --> B{Mechanical Limit}
    B --> C[Tooth Bending and Wear Strength]
    A --> D{Thermal Limit}
    D --> E[Heat Dissipated Hd]
    C --> F[Safe Operation Zone]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Continuous Duty Transmission]
    style D fill:#ffe6cc
    style E fill:#ffe6cc
    style F fill:#ccffcc
```

> **Interpretation:** A gearbox's actual rated power is the **smaller** of its *mechanical rating* (tooth strength) and *thermal rating* (heat dissipation). Designers must verify both.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define *thermal rating* of a gearbox. Why is it often the limiting factor in continuous-duty applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermal rating of a gearbox is the maximum power it can transmit continuously without the lubricant temperature exceeding its permissible limit. It is governed by the heat balance equation:

$$H_g = (1 - \eta) \cdot P \leq K \cdot A \cdot (T_{oil} - T_a)$$

In continuous-duty applications (e.g., conveyor drives, elevators), the gearbox runs for hours without rest, allowing oil temperature to reach steady state. Because heat removal by convection is slow, the housing surface area becomes the bottleneck, making thermal rating smaller than the mechanical (tooth strength) rating. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why are *Extreme Pressure (EP) additives* mandatory in hypoid gear lubricants?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hypoid gears have a large **offset between the pinion and gear axes**, causing substantial **sliding velocity** at the mesh in addition to rolling. This generates very high flash temperatures at tooth contacts, which can break down ordinary mineral oils. EP additives (sulfur-phosphorus compounds) form a sacrificial chemical film on the metal surface that prevents metal-to-metal welding and scuffing under boundary and mixed lubrication conditions. Hence, EP oils are mandatory for hypoid axles. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B and answer both sub-parts.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A helical gear reducer transmits **20 kW** at an overall efficiency of **96%**. The housing has an effective cooling surface area of **1.5 m²** and a heat transfer coefficient of **14 W/m²·K** when mounted in still air. Ambient temperature is **40 °C**, and maximum permissible oil temperature is **85 °C**.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Check whether the gearbox is thermally safe. If not, recommend two design modifications.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Heat Generated**

$$H_g = (1 - \eta) \cdot P = (1 - 0.96) \cdot 20000$$

$$H_g = 0.04 \cdot 20000 = 800 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Heat Dissipated by Housing**

$$\Delta T = T_{oil} - T_a = 85 - 40 = 45 \text{ °C}$$

$$H_d = K \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 14 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 45$$

$$H_d = 945 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Comparison**

Since **H_g = 800 W < H_d = 945 W**, the gearbox **is thermally safe** under the given conditions. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
If the same unit is to be relocated to an environment with **ambient temperature of 55 °C**, determine the **new maximum transmittable power** keeping the same oil temperature limit.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: New Temperature Difference**

$$\Delta T_{new} = 85 - 55 = 30 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Heat that Can Be Dissipated**

$$H_{d,max} = 14 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 30 = 630 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Solve for New Power**

Setting H_g = H_d,max:

$$(1 - \eta) \cdot P_{new} = 630$$

$$0.04 \cdot P_{new} = 630$$

$$P_{new} = \frac{630}{0.04}$$

$$P_{new} = 15750 \text{ W} = 15.75 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** The gearbox can transmit only **15.75 kW** in the hotter environment, a **21.25% derating** from its original 20 kW rating. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Compare the following gear types with respect to **shaft orientation, axial thrust, efficiency, noise, and typical automotive application**: (i) Spur, (ii) Helical, (iii) Bevel, (iv) Worm, (v) Hypoid. State **two merits and two demerits** of each.

**Model Solution (Tabulated — 14 marks):**

| # | Gear Type | Shaft Orientation | Axial Thrust | Efficiency | Noise | Automotive Application |
|---|-----------|------------------|--------------|------------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | Spur | Parallel | **None** | 98–99% | **High** | Tachometers, simple gear trains |
| 2 | Helical | Parallel or crossed | **Yes (large)** | 97–99% | Low | Manual transmission, machine tools |
| 3 | Bevel | Intersecting (any) | Yes (radial+axial) | 97–98% | Moderate | Hand tools, differentials |
| 4 | Worm | Non-intersecting (90°) | Yes | 50–90% | Low | Steering gear, winches |
| 5 | Hypoid | Non-intersecting, offset | Yes | 85–95% | Moderate | Rear-axle final drive (cars) |

**Merits & Demerits:**

**Spur Gear:**
- Merits: (i) Simple to manufacture, (ii) No axial thrust, (iii) Highest efficiency. (1 + 1 + 0.5)
- Demerits: (i) Noisy at high speeds, (ii) Limited to parallel shafts, (iii) Unsuitable for very high speeds.

**Helical Gear:**
- Merits: (i) Quiet operation, (ii) Higher load capacity due to multiple teeth in mesh.
- Demerits: (i) Axial thrust requires thrust bearings, (ii) Lower efficiency due to sliding.

**Bevel Gear:**
- Merits: (i) Transmits power between intersecting shafts, (ii) Compact design.
- Demerits: (i) Difficult to manufacture, (ii) Requires precise mounting.

**Worm Gear:**
- Merits: (i) Self-locking (safety in lifts), (ii) Large speed reduction in single stage.
- Demerits: (i) Low efficiency, (ii) High heat generation — needs special lubrication.

**Hypoid Gear:**
- Merits: (i) Allows low driveshaft height in cars, (ii) High torque capacity.
- Demerits: (i) Sliding causes high friction, (ii) Needs EP lubricants.

(Marks distributed as: Table = 7 marks; Merits & Demerits = 7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students frequently write W/m²·°C but then plug in K (Kelvin) — losing 1–2 marks. *Always remember that a *temperature difference* (ΔT) is the same in °C and K.*
> 2. **Mixing η:** In multi-stage gearboxes, η_total = η₁ × η₂ × ... — students sometimes add efficiencies, which is **wrong**.
> 3. **Forgetting the (1 − η) factor:** Many students compute H_g = P × η, which is the *output* power, not the heat lost. *Heat lost = P × (1 − η).*
> 4. **Merits/Demerits formatting:** Presenting as a single paragraph instead of a table. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unstructured answers.
> 5. **Forgetting to state units** in numerical answers (W, m², °C). Always box your final numerical answer with units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3):**

### 🔥 Heat Dissipation
- Thermal rating equation: **H_g ≤ H_d** where H_g = (1 − η)·P and H_d = K·A·ΔT
- Typical **K values**: bare steel ~ 12 W/m²·K; with fins ~ 17; with fan ~ 30+
- ΔT = T_oil − T_ambient; T_oil max ≈ 363 K (90 °C)
- **Thermal rating is the bottleneck** in continuous-duty gearboxes.

### 🛢️ Lubrication of Gears
- Three regimes: **Boundary → Mixed → EHL** (Stribeck curve)
- Lubrication methods: **Splash (v ≤ 10 m/s), Forced (v > 10 m/s), Mist, Grease**
- **EP additives** mandatory for hypoid and worm gears.
- Viscosity selection depends on **speed × load** (high speed-low load → low viscosity).
- High-speed gears require **pressurized lubrication**.

### ⚙️ Merits & Demerits — Quick Recall
- **Spur:** Simple, no thrust, noisy.
- **Helical:** Quiet, high capacity, **axial thrust is the bane**.
- **Bevel:** Intersecting shafts, moderate efficiency.
- **Worm:** Self-locking, low efficiency, **high heat**.
- **Hypoid:** Offset shafts, used in **car rear axles**, needs EP oil.

### 🎯 KTU Exam Strategy
- Always answer merits/demerits questions using a **comparison table**.
- For numerical problems, show **all four steps**: Given → Formula → Substitution → Answer with units.
- For thermal rating problems, **explicitly state whether the gearbox is safe or not** — never leave it ambiguous.
- Mention **real-world applications** wherever possible — examiners reward contextual awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Spur Gear and Helical Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Spur Gear and Helical Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Spur Gear — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Spur Gear (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A **spur gear** is a cylindrical gear with straight teeth cut parallel to the axis of rotation. It is the simplest and most commonly used gear type, transmitting motion and power between parallel shafts through a positive, non-slip rolling contact.

Key geometric features of a standard involute spur gear (proportions as per standard full-depth system, $\phi = 20°$ pressure angle):

| Parameter | Symbol | Value (Full depth, 20°) |
|---|---|---|
| Addendum | $a$ | $1 \cdot m$ |
| Dedendum | $b$ | $1.25 \cdot m$ |
| Working depth | $h_w$ | $2 \cdot m$ |
| Whole depth | $h$ | $2.25 \cdot m$ |
| Tooth thickness on P.C. | $t$ | $\frac{\pi \cdot m}{2}$ |
| Clearance | $c$ | $0.25 \cdot m$ |

where **$m$** is the **module** (in mm) — the KTU standard parameter that replaces diametral pitch in SI units.

### 1.2 Helical Gear — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Helical Gear (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A **helical gear** is a cylindrical gear whose teeth are cut at an angle (helix angle $\beta$) to the axis of rotation. Because the contact between meshing teeth is progressive (along a line that grows gradually), helical gears run more quietly and carry higher loads than spur gears of the same size.

The **helix angle $\beta$** is measured on the pitch cylinder, and the relation between the normal module $m_n$ (in the normal plane) and the transverse module $m_t$ (in the plane of rotation) is:

$$m_t = \frac{m_n}{\cos \beta}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Rack & Pinion Car Park

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Picture for Spur Gears:** Imagine two combs placed side-by-side, with the teeth of one sliding into the gaps of the other. That is a spur gear in slow motion. Each "click" of a tooth engaging a gap is one rotation step. Spur gears transfer power in sudden, sharp impulses — which is why they hum at high speed.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Picture for Helical Gears:** Now imagine the same two combs, but their teeth are sliced at a slant (like the slash of a fountain-pen nib). As one tooth starts to enter, the previous one is still leaving — so engagement is **continuous and smooth**. This is the helix angle $\beta$ doing its job. The price you pay: an axial thrust force, which must be taken by a thrust bearing.

### 1.4 Why These Two Gears Matter in Automotive Applications

| Application | Gear Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Manual gearbox (1st–4th gear) | **Helical** | Smooth, quiet operation under high torque |
| Reverse gear in many cars | **Spur** | Simple, cheap, low load |
| Starter motor (some) | **Spur / Planetary** | High reduction ratio, intermittent use |
| Differential side gears | **Spur (straight bevel concept)** | Load capacity |
| Timing drives (older) | **Spur or Helical** | Compactness |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Whenever a problem statement gives shaft centre distance, speed ratio, and power — your first job is to **choose module from standard table** (preferred series: 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm) and **proportion the gear**. Don't compute a "custom" module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design Philosophy — The Two Failure Criteria

A gear tooth can fail by **two** primary mechanisms, and the design must satisfy **both**:

1. **Bending failure** of the tooth (treated as a cantilever beam) — predicted by **Lewis Equation**.
2. **Pitting / Wear failure** of the tooth flank surface — predicted by **Buckingham's Wear Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Practice:** The **design (allowable) load** on the gear is the **smaller** of the beam strength and the wear strength. The selected module is the **larger** of the modules required by these two criteria.

### 2.2 Lewis Beam-Strength Equation (Spur Gear)

Lewis (1892) treated the gear tooth as a **cantilever beam** of parabolic shape, fixed at the root and loaded at the tip by the tangential force $W_T$. The tooth is considered safe if the **maximum bending tensile stress** at the root does not exceed the allowable bending stress $\sigma_b$.

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y \quad [N]$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|
| $F_b$ | **Beam strength** (safe tangential load) | N |
| $\sigma_b$ | Allowable bending stress for the gear material | Cast iron: 56 MPa, Cast steel: 84 MPa, Forged steel: 105–210 MPa |
| $b$ | Face width of gear (axial length of tooth) | Usually $b = 6m$ to $10m$ |
| $m$ | Module (mm) | Selected from preferred series |
| $y$ | **Lewis form factor** (depends on number of teeth) | Tabulated; decreases as teeth get fewer |

> [!TIP]
> **Why $\pi \cdot m$?** Because the tooth acts as a cantilever whose weakest section is approximated as a rectangle of width $\pi \cdot m / 2$ (i.e., the tooth thickness at the pitch circle).

#### Lewis Form Factor $y$ — KTU Standard Table (20° full-depth involute, stub teeth excluded)

| Number of teeth $z$ | 12 | 14 | 16 | 17 | 18 | 19 | 20 | 21 | 22 | 24 | 26 | 28 | 30 | 34 | 38 | 43 | 50 | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ (20° full depth) | 0.245 | 0.276 | 0.296 | 0.303 | 0.309 | 0.314 | 0.320 | 0.327 | 0.330 | 0.341 | 0.349 | 0.355 | 0.359 | 0.371 | 0.384 | 0.397 | 0.409 | 0.422 |

For $z > 50$ (a common KTU assumption for a pinion mating with a gear), the KTU-typical conservative value used is **$y = 0.484 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{0.3}{z_v}\right)$** style; in the most-common numerical exam, $y$ is simply **read off the table**.

### 2.3 Buckingham's Wear-Strength Equation (Spur Gear)

When two meshing teeth press against each other, the contact is essentially that of two cylinders in compression (Hertzian contact). Buckingham proposed an empirical formula giving the safe tangential load before pitting (surface fatigue) occurs:

$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K \quad [N]$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $F_w$ | **Wear strength** (safe tangential load) | N |
| $d_p$ | Pitch circle diameter of the **pinion** (the smaller gear) | $z_p \cdot m$ |
| $b$ | Face width | mm |
| $Q$ | **Ratio factor** (depends on the gear material pair) | Steel-on-steel: 0.484; Cast iron on cast iron: 0.353 |
| $K$ | Load stress factor (allowable bearing pressure for the softer material) | Cast iron: 0.55 N/mm²; Cast steel: 0.84 N/mm²; Phosphor bronze: 0.69 N/mm²; Forged steel: 1.05 N/mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **$Q$ and $K$ are *not the same thing* — common KTU trap.** $Q$ is dimensionless (depends on the material pair combination), $K$ has units of N/mm² and represents the **load-stress value** (sometimes written as $C_b$).

#### Form of the Ratio Factor $Q$

For a standard $20°$ full-depth involute tooth, $Q$ is given by:

$$Q = \frac{2 \sin \phi}{\sin \phi + 1}$$

With $\phi = 20°$ : $Q = 0.484$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often gives the **material constant $K$ and ratio factor $Q$ as a single combined product** $K \cdot Q$ to make the formula simpler: $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot (K \cdot Q)$. Always check the problem statement.

### 2.4 Module Determination from the Two Criteria

The beam strength and wear strength can each be **rearranged** to give a *required* module:

**From beam strength:**

$$m_b = \sqrt[3]{\frac{2 \cdot P}{\sigma_b \cdot \pi \cdot C \cdot y \cdot z_p \cdot n_p}} \cdot \text{(with a KTU-standard face-width factor)}$$

In the KTU-prescribed compact form (where $b = k \cdot m$ and $W_T = \frac{2P}{v}$, with $v = \frac{\pi d_p n_p}{60}$), the result simplifies to:

$$m_b = \frac{W_T}{\sigma_b \cdot \pi \cdot y} \cdot \frac{1}{b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{iterative selection}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Smart Move:** In KTU Part B, you don't iterate. The KTU standard procedure is:
> 1. Compute design load $W_T = P / v$ (where $v$ is given or computed).
> 2. Assume a face-width-to-module ratio, typically $b = 10m$ for spur gears.
> 3. Rearrange $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ to get $m$.
> 4. Do the same with the wear equation to get another $m$.
> 5. Choose the **larger** $m$ and round **up** to the next preferred size.

### 2.5 Helical Gear — Geometry Conversion to an "Equivalent Spur Gear"

The simplest design method (recommended by KTU) is to **convert the helical gear into an equivalent spur gear** in the normal plane and apply the same Lewis / Buckingham equations.

| Parameter | Helical | Equivalent Spur |
|---|---|---|
| Module | Normal $m_n$ | $m_n$ |
| Number of teeth | $z$ | $z_v = \frac{z}{\cos^3 \beta}$ (**virtual / formative number of teeth**) |
| Pitch circle dia. | $d = m_t \cdot z$ | $d_v = m_n \cdot z_v$ |
| Face width | $b$ | $b \cdot \cos \beta$ (effective) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $z_v = z / \cos^3 \beta$?** The tooth of a helical gear is slanted by $\beta$. The standard derivation (textbook) shows that the tooth-thickness-to-pitch relationship along the helix, combined with the normal plane projection, gives this cubic $\cos$ factor.

The **beam strength** of a helical gear (per KTU design data book):

$$F_{b,helical} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y \quad \text{with } y \text{ based on } z_v$$

(Note: in some KTU references, the helical beam strength is **multiplied by $\cos \beta$** to account for the projection; in others, the $\cos \beta$ is absorbed into the face width. **Always check your data book.** The conservative KTU practice is to **omit** the extra $\cos \beta$ factor and use the equivalent-spur method.)

The **wear strength** of a helical gear is:

$$F_{w,helical} = d_v \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K \quad \text{with } d_v = m_n \cdot z_v$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in almost every KTU Part B problem on gear design.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60}$ | Pitch-line velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $W_T = \frac{2P}{v} = \frac{60000 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}$ | Tangential (design) load | N |
| 3 | $d = m \cdot z$ | Pitch diameter | mm |
| 4 | $a = \frac{m(z_1 + z_2)}{2}$ | Centre distance | mm |
| 5 | $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | Lewis beam strength (spur) | N |
| 6 | $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | Buckingham wear strength (spur) | N |
| 7 | $z_v = \frac{z}{\cos^3 \beta}$ | Virtual no. of teeth (helical) | – |
| 8 | $m_t = \frac{m_n}{\cos \beta}$ | Transverse module (helical) | mm |
| 9 | $F_{b,helical} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y$ (with $z_v$) | Helical beam strength | N |
| 10 | $F_{w,helical} = d_v \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | Helical wear strength | N |
| 11 | $C = \frac{z_2}{z_1}$ | Speed ratio | – |
| 12 | $b = k \cdot m$ (with $k = 6$ to $10$) | Face-width rule of thumb | mm |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive gearbox design** uses the helical beam + wear criterion to size the module for 1st gear (highest torque, ~3× engine torque).
- **AGMA refinement**: In real production, the Lewis/Buckingham equations are replaced by AGMA equations that use stress-correction factors $J$, elastic coefficient $C_p$, and contact ratio $m_N$. KTU 2024 module-3 syllabus keeps the **classical (Lewis + Buckingham) approach** as the primary method.
- The **virtual-tooth concept** for helical gears is a beautiful example of how 3-D problems are reduced to 2-D by an elegant geometric substitution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Beam-Strength Equation

**Step 1 — Model the gear tooth as a cantilever.**
The tooth is treated as a tapered cantilever beam fixed at the base (root) and loaded at the tip by the tangential force $W_T$ acting at the pitch circle.

**Step 2 — Express the bending moment at the weakest section (pitch circle).**
If $h$ is the height of the cantilever from the pitch circle to the root (≈ addendum $a$ for full-depth teeth), the bending moment at the root is:

$$M = W_T \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Section modulus of the tooth at the pitch circle.**
The tooth is approximated as a rectangular section of width $t$ (tooth thickness at P.C.) and depth $h$. Section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{t \cdot h^2}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress.**
Bending stress $\sigma = M / Z$. Substituting:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{W_T \cdot h \cdot 6}{t \cdot h^2} = \frac{6 \cdot W_T}{t \cdot h}$$

**Step 5 — Lewis's modification.**
Lewis observed that the actual stress is **lower** than this because the tooth is not exactly rectangular, it is parabola-shaped, and the stress concentration at the fillet is offset by load sharing. He introduced the **Lewis form factor $y$** such that:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{W_T}{b \cdot m \cdot y}$$

The geometric derivation (textbook, Shigley / V.B. Bhandari) substitutes $t = \pi m / 2$ and a parabolic tooth-shape factor, which collapses to:

$$\boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{W_T}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y} \cdot (\text{with appropriate substitution } W_T = F_b \text{ at design load})}$$

Rearranging for the **safe tangential load (beam strength)**:

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

This is the **Lewis beam-strength equation** you will use in every KTU problem.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Spur Gear Beam Module (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- Power $P = 7.5$ kW
- Speed of pinion $n_p = 1200$ rpm
- Speed ratio $C = 3$
- Pressure angle $\phi = 20°$, full-depth teeth
- Material: Cast steel — $\sigma_b = 84$ N/mm²
- Lewis form factor for $z_p = 20$: $y = 0.320$
- Face width assumed $b = 10 m$

**Find:** Required module $m$ from bending criterion.

**Step 1 — Number of teeth.**
Assume $z_p = 20$ (pinion); then $z_g = 3 \cdot 20 = 60$.

**Step 2 — Pitch circle diameter of pinion.**
$$d_p = m \cdot z_p = 20 m$$

**Step 3 — Pitch line velocity.**
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot n_p}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot 20m \cdot 1200}{60} = 400 \pi \cdot m \quad [m/s]$$
$$v = 1256.64 \cdot m$$

**Step 4 — Design tangential load.**
$$W_T = \frac{2P}{v} = \frac{2 \cdot 7500}{1256.64 \cdot m} = \frac{15000}{1256.64 \cdot m} = \frac{11.936}{m} \quad [kN] = \frac{11936}{m} \quad [N]$$

**Step 5 — Apply Lewis equation.**
$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

The design condition is $W_T = F_b$:

$$\frac{11936}{m} = 84 \cdot (10m) \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot 0.320$$

$$\frac{11936}{m} = 84 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.320 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2 = 268.08 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2$$

$$\frac{11936}{m} = 842.23 \cdot m^2$$

$$11936 = 842.23 \cdot m^3$$

$$m^3 = \frac{11936}{842.23} = 14.171$$

$$m = \sqrt[3]{14.171} = 2.416 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Standard module selection.**
Select next preferred size: **$m = 2.5$ mm** ✅

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Spur Gear Wear Module

**Given:** Same example, with material pair **steel-on-steel**, $Q = 0.484$, $K = 0.84$ N/mm².

**Step 1 — Write Buckingham equation with $d_p = 20m$ and $b = 10m$.**
$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K = (20m) \cdot (10m) \cdot 0.484 \cdot 0.84$$

$$F_w = 200 \cdot m^2 \cdot 0.4066 = 81.31 \cdot m^2 \quad [N]$$

**Step 2 — Set $W_T = F_w$.**
$$\frac{11936}{m} = 81.31 \cdot m^2$$

$$m^3 = \frac{11936}{81.31} = 146.8$$

$$m = \sqrt[3]{146.8} = 5.28 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Select $m = 6$ mm** (next preferred size) for the wear criterion.

**Step 4 — Design Decision.**
The design module is the **larger** of the two: **$m = 6$ mm**. The gear teeth are safe against *both* bending and wear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the dominance of the wear criterion** in this example — for medium-power, steel-on-steel gears, wear usually governs. The opposite (bending governs) is true for very low-speed, high-power gears with hardened pinions.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Helical Gear Module

**Given:**
- $P = 15$ kW
- $n_p = 1450$ rpm
- $z_p = 20$, $z_g = 60$, helix angle $\beta = 15°$
- Material: Forged steel, $\sigma_b = 105$ N/mm²
- Steel-on-steel: $Q = 0.484$, $K = 1.05$ N/mm²
- $y$ evaluated at $z_v$, but conservatively $y = 0.320$ (assuming $z_v \ge 18$)
- Face width $b = 10 m_n$

**Step 1 — Virtual number of teeth.**
$$z_v = \frac{z_p}{\cos^3 \beta} = \frac{20}{\cos^3 15°} = \frac{20}{0.9659^3} = \frac{20}{0.9010} \approx 22.2$$

For $z_v \approx 22$, the KTU table gives $y \approx 0.330$. Use **$y = 0.330$**.

**Step 2 — Pitch circle diameter of pinion.**
$$d_p = m_t \cdot z_p = \frac{m_n}{\cos \beta} \cdot z_p = \frac{m_n \cdot 20}{0.9659} = 20.7 \cdot m_n$$

**Step 3 — Velocity.**
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot n_p}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot 20.7 m_n \cdot 1450}{60} = 1571.4 \cdot m_n \quad [m/s]$$

**Step 4 — Design tangential load.**
$$W_T = \frac{2P}{v} = \frac{2 \cdot 15000}{1571.4 \cdot m_n} = \frac{30000}{1571.4 \cdot m_n} = \frac{19.09}{m_n} \quad [kN] = \frac{19090}{m_n} \quad [N]$$

**Step 5 — Beam strength check.**
$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y = 105 \cdot (10 m_n) \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot 0.330$$
$$F_b = 105 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.330 \cdot \pi \cdot m_n^2 = 1088.5 \cdot m_n^2 \quad [N]$$

Setting $W_T = F_b$:

$$\frac{19090}{m_n} = 1088.5 \cdot m_n^2$$

$$m_n^3 = \frac{19090}{1088.5} = 17.54$$

$$m_n = \sqrt[3]{17.54} = 2.60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Wear strength check.**
$$F_w = d_v \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K = (m_n \cdot z_v) \cdot (10 m_n) \cdot 0.484 \cdot 1.05$$
$$F_w = 22.2 \cdot m_n \cdot 10 m_n \cdot 0.508 = 112.78 \cdot m_n^2$$

Setting $W_T = F_w$:

$$\frac{19090}{m_n} = 112.78 \cdot m_n^2$$

$$m_n^3 = \frac{19090}{112.78} = 169.3$$

$$m_n = \sqrt[3]{169.3} = 5.53 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Final design.**
The **wear criterion** governs. Choose next preferred size: **$m_n = 6$ mm**.

Transverse module: $m_t = \frac{6}{\cos 15°} = \frac{6}{0.9659} = 6.21$ mm.

> [!NOTE]
> The helical gear can transmit about **2× the load** of an equivalent spur gear in the same envelope — this is the engineering reason every modern manual gearbox uses helical gears.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — When Does Each Criterion Govern?

| Scenario | Governs | Why |
|---|---|---|
| High speed, small module, steel pinion | **Wear** | Hertzian contact stresses scale with $1/m$ |
| Low speed, large module, soft pinion | **Bending** | Tooth root is the weak link |
| Hardened & ground gears | **Bending (endurance)** | Surface is no longer the limit |
| Helical gear (vs spur) | **Wear shifts, but the design margin reduces** | Higher contact ratio smooths loading |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Gear Tooth Cantilever-Loading Schematic (Lewis Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Tangential Force W_T applied at Pitch Circle"] --> nodeB["Tooth acts as Cantilever Beam"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Bending Moment M = W_T x h at the root"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Maximum tensile bending stress at the root fillet"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Is sigma_calc > sigma_allowable?"}
    nodeE -- "Yes" --> nodeF["FAILURE: Tooth breaks at root"]
    nodeE -- "No" --> nodeG["SAFE: Tooth survives beam criterion"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Check Wear (Buckingham) next"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Is W_T > F_w?"}
    nodeI -- "Yes" --> nodeJ["FAILURE: Pitting on flank"]
    nodeI -- "No" --> nodeK["SAFE: Design complete"]

    style nodeA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeI fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeK fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Spur Gear — Forces at the Pitch Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Pinion"] -- "W_T = Tangential" --> nodeC["Pitch Point Contact"]
    nodeC -- "W_R = Radial" --> nodeP
    nodeC -- "W_T (reaction)" --> nodeG["Gear"]
    nodeG -- "W_R (reaction)" --> nodeC
    nodeC -- "W_A = 0 (no axial thrust)" --> nodeAnchor["Anchored in pure rotation"]

    style nodeP fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Helical Gear — Added Axial Thrust

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePH["Pinion Helical"] -- "W_T = Tangential" --> nodeCH["Pitch Point"]
    nodeCH -- "W_R = Radial" --> nodePH
    nodeCH -- "W_T (reaction)" --> nodeGH["Gear Helical"]
    nodeCH -- "W_R" --> nodeGH
    nodeCH -- "W_A = W_T x tan beta (AXIAL)" --> nodePH
    nodeCH -- "W_A reaction" --> nodeGH
    nodePH -- "Thrust bearing required" --> nodeTB["Thrust Bearing"]

    style nodePH fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeGH fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeCH fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeTB fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 KTU Module-Design Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["Start: Given P, n, C, materials"] --> nodeA["Assume z_p and find z_g = C x z_p"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["Assume b = 10m (face width)"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compute v in terms of m"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Compute W_T in terms of m"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Apply Lewis: m_b"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Apply Buckingham: m_w"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["m_b"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["m_w"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["m_design = max of m_b, m_w"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Round up to next preferred module"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Recompute v, W_T, b with chosen m"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Verify both criteria are satisfied"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["END: Final m, z_p, z_g, d_p, d_g, a, b"]

    style nodeS fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeI fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeM fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Spur vs. Helical Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["SPUR GEAR"]
        SP_A["Teeth parallel to axis"]
        SP_B["No axial thrust"]
        SP_C["Simple, cheap, noisy"]
        SP_D["Low load capacity"]
    end

    subgraph HE["HELICAL GEAR"]
        HE_A["Teeth at helix angle beta"]
        HE_B["Axial thrust W_T tan beta"]
        HE_C["Smooth, quiet operation"]
        HE_D["High load capacity"]
    end

    SP --> DECIDE{"Need quiet high-load transmission?"}
    DECIDE -- "No" --> SP_USE["Use Spur"]
    DECIDE -- "Yes" --> HE_USE["Use Helical + Thrust Bearing"]

    style SP fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    style HE fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define the term "Lewis Form Factor" for a gear tooth. Why does it depend on the number of teeth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Lewis form factor $y$** is a dimensionless geometric ratio that accounts for the actual shape of the gear tooth (parabolic-like) compared to a rectangular cantilever, and also includes a stress-concentration correction at the root fillet. Mathematically it represents the ratio of the **bending moment capacity** of the actual tooth to that of a rectangular reference tooth, divided by the face width and module.
- $y$ **decreases as the number of teeth decreases** because fewer teeth mean a more slender (undercut, pointed) tooth, with higher stress concentration at the root.
- For example: at 20° full depth, $y$ varies from **0.245 for 12 teeth** to **0.422 for 60 teeth**.
- It is used in the **Lewis equation** $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **State Buckingham's equation for wear strength of a spur gear and explain the role of the ratio factor $Q$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Buckingham's equation for the wear (pitting) strength of a spur gear is:

$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$$

where $F_w$ is the safe tangential load before pitting, $d_p$ is the pitch circle diameter of the pinion, $b$ is the face width, and $K$ is the allowable bearing stress for the **softer** material of the pair.

The **ratio factor $Q$** is a **dimensionless** factor that accounts for the geometry of the meshing tooth pair and the material combination. For standard 20° full-depth involute teeth:
$$Q = \frac{2 \sin \phi}{\sin \phi + 1} = 0.484 \text{ (for steel on steel)}$$

It is **different from $K$**: $Q$ is a *geometry + material-pair* correction, $K$ is the *material's allowable surface load stress*. Together $Q \cdot K$ gives the effective bearing pressure. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**A pair of spur gears is to transmit 11.25 kW from an electric motor running at 1440 rpm to a machine shaft running at 480 rpm. The gears are made of cast steel with allowable bending stress 84 N/mm². The pinion has 20 teeth of 20° full-depth involute form. Take Lewis form factor for 20 teeth as 0.32, ratio factor $Q = 0.484$, and material constant $K = 0.84$ N/mm². Design the gear pair based on both beam and wear strengths.**

**(a) Determine the required module from beam strength.** **(7 marks)**
**(b) Determine the required module from wear strength and finalize the design.** **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Q3A:

**Given Data:**
- $P = 11.25$ kW
- $n_p = 1440$ rpm
- $n_g = 480$ rpm
- $\sigma_b = 84$ N/mm²
- $z_p = 20$, $\phi = 20°$, full depth
- $y = 0.32$
- $Q = 0.484$, $K = 0.84$ N/mm²
- Assume $b = 10m$

**Step 1 — Number of teeth of gear.**
$$C = \frac{n_p}{n_g} = \frac{1440}{480} = 3$$
$$z_g = 3 \cdot 20 = 60$$

**Step 2 — Pitch circle diameter of pinion.**
$$d_p = m \cdot z_p = 20m \text{ mm}$$

---

#### Part (a) — Beam Strength Module **(7 marks)**

**Step 3 — Pitch-line velocity.**
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot n_p}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot 20m \cdot 1440}{60} = 1507.96 \cdot m \quad [m/s]$$

**Step 4 — Design tangential load.**
$$W_T = \frac{2P}{v} = \frac{2 \cdot 11250}{1507.96 \cdot m} = \frac{22500}{1507.96 \cdot m} = \frac{14.92}{m} \text{ kN} = \frac{14920}{m} \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Lewis equation.**
$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

Setting $W_T = F_b$:
$$\frac{14920}{m} = 84 \cdot (10m) \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot 0.32$$
$$\frac{14920}{m} = 84 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.32 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2$$
$$\frac{14920}{m} = 844.65 \cdot m^2$$
$$m^3 = \frac{14920}{844.65} = 17.665$$
$$m_b = \sqrt[3]{17.665} = 2.61 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_b \approx 2.61 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Wear Strength Module and Final Design **(7 marks)**

**Step 6 — Apply Buckingham's equation.**
$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K = (20m)(10m)(0.484)(0.84)$$
$$F_w = 200 \cdot m^2 \cdot 0.4066 = 81.31 \cdot m^2 \quad [N]$$

**Step 7 — Set $W_T = F_w$.**
$$\frac{14920}{m} = 81.31 \cdot m^2$$
$$m^3 = \frac{14920}{81.31} = 183.5$$
$$m_w = \sqrt[3]{183.5} = 5.68 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8 — Final design module.**
$$m_{design} = \max(m_b, m_w) = \max(2.61, 5.68) = 5.68 \text{ mm}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Select next preferred module: } m = 6 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 9 — Final gear proportions with $m = 6$ mm:**
- $d_p = 20 \cdot 6 = 120$ mm
- $d_g = 60 \cdot 6 = 360$ mm
- Centre distance $a = \frac{120 + 360}{2} = 240$ mm
- Face width $b = 10m = 60$ mm
- Addendum $a = m = 6$ mm
- Dedendum $b = 1.25m = 7.5$ mm
- Tooth thickness on P.C. $t = \pi m / 2 = 9.42$ mm

**Step 10 — Verification (with chosen $m = 6$):**
$$v = 1507.96 \cdot 0.006 = 9.05 \text{ m/s}$$
$$W_T = 14920/6 = 2486.7 \text{ N}$$
$$F_b = 84 \cdot 60 \cdot \pi \cdot 6 \cdot 0.32 = 30409 \text{ N} \quad \text{✅}$$
$$F_w = 81.31 \cdot 36 = 2927 \text{ N} \quad \text{✅ (just above $W_T$)}$$

**The design is safe.** **(14 marks total)**

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**A helical gear pair is to transmit 20 kW at 1500 rpm of the pinion. The speed ratio is 4. The helix angle is 20°. Material is forged steel with $\sigma_b = 105$ N/mm². The pinion has 25 teeth. Take $y$ for the virtual number of teeth (assume as 28 teeth in the table, $y = 0.355$), $Q = 0.484$, $K = 1.05$ N/mm², and face width $b = 10 m_n$.**

**(a) Determine the module from beam strength.** **(7 marks)**
**(b) Determine the module from wear strength and finalize the design.** **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Q3B:

**Given:** $P = 20$ kW, $n_p = 1500$ rpm, $C = 4$, $\beta = 20°$, $\sigma_b = 105$ N/mm², $z_p = 25$, $z_g = 100$, $y = 0.355$, $Q = 0.484$, $K = 1.05$ N/mm², $b = 10 m_n$.

**Step 1 — Virtual number of teeth.**
$$z_v = \frac{z_p}{\cos^3 \beta} = \frac{25}{\cos^3 20°} = \frac{25}{0.9397^3} = \frac{25}{0.8298} = 30.13$$

Use $z_v = 30$, $y = 0.359$ (slight refinement). We'll keep $y = 0.355$ as per the problem.

**Step 2 — Pitch diameter of pinion (transverse).**
$$d_p = m_t \cdot z_p = \frac{m_n}{\cos \beta} \cdot z_p = \frac{m_n \cdot 25}{0.9397} = 26.60 \cdot m_n$$

**Step 3 — Velocity.**
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot n_p}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot 26.60 \cdot m_n \cdot 1500}{60} = 2089.5 \cdot m_n \quad [m/s]$$

**Step 4 — Design load.**
$$W_T = \frac{2P}{v} = \frac{2 \cdot 20000}{2089.5 \cdot m_n} = \frac{40000}{2089.5 \cdot m_n} = \frac{19.144}{m_n} \text{ kN} = \frac{19144}{m_n} \text{ N}$$

---

#### Part (a) — Beam Strength **(7 marks)**

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y = 105 \cdot (10 m_n) \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot 0.355$$
$$F_b = 1171.0 \cdot m_n^2 \quad [N]$$

Setting $W_T = F_b$:
$$\frac{19144}{m_n} = 1171.0 \cdot m_n^2$$
$$m_n^3 = \frac{19144}{1171.0} = 16.35$$
$$m_n = \sqrt[3]{16.35} = 2.54 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Wear Strength and Final Design **(7 marks)**

**Step 5 — Virtual pitch diameter.**
$$d_v = m_n \cdot z_v = m_n \cdot 30 = 30 \cdot m_n$$

**Step 6 — Buckingham equation.**
$$F_w = d_v \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K = (30 m_n)(10 m_n)(0.484)(1.05)$$
$$F_w = 300 \cdot m_n^2 \cdot 0.5082 = 152.46 \cdot m_n^2 \quad [N]$$

**Step 7 — Set $W_T = F_w$.**
$$\frac{19144}{m_n} = 152.46 \cdot m_n^2$$
$$m_n^3 = \frac{19144}{152.46} = 125.57$$
$$m_n = \sqrt[3]{125.57} = 5.01 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8 — Final design.**
$$m_n = \max(2.54, 5.01) = 5.01 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \boxed{m_n = 5 \text{ mm} \text{ (or next preferred size, 5 mm)}}$$

Actually 5 mm **is** a preferred module, so $m_n = 5$ mm is the design choice. (Or $m_n = 5$ mm exactly; or step up to 6 mm for safety margin.)

**Step 9 — Final proportions with $m_n = 5$ mm:**
- $m_t = 5 / \cos 20° = 5 / 0.9397 = 5.32$ mm
- $d_p = m_t \cdot z_p = 5.32 \cdot 25 = 133.0$ mm
- $d_g = 5.32 \cdot 100 = 532.0$ mm
- Centre distance $a = (d_p + d_g)/2 = 332.5$ mm
- Face width $b = 10 \cdot 5 = 50$ mm
- Axial thrust on pinion: $W_A = W_T \tan \beta = (19144/5) \tan 20° = 3828.8 \cdot 0.364 = 1393.7$ N (must be carried by a thrust bearing). **(14 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mixing $Q$ and $K$:** Students often write $F_w = d \cdot b \cdot K$ and forget $Q$ entirely. The Buckingham equation has **TWO** material-related factors. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Using $z_p$ in place of $z_v$ for helical gears:** The Lewis form factor must be read for the **virtual** number of teeth, not the actual. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting to round up the module:** A computed module of 2.61 mm must be rounded up to 3 mm (or 2.5 if available), **never** rounded down. Down-rounding makes the gear weaker than designed. **(–2 marks)**
> 4. **Wrong pitch-line velocity formula:** $v = \frac{\pi d n}{60}$ gives **m/s** when $d$ is in **metres**. If you use $d$ in mm, divide by 60000. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Forgetting to recompute $W_T$ after choosing the standard module:** Always re-verify $W_T$ vs $F_b$ and $F_w$ with the **final** module. **(–2 marks)**
> 6. **For helical gears, forgetting axial thrust $W_T \tan \beta$ on the bearing.** Examiners love to test this! **(–2 marks)**

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Spur & Helical Gear Design**

- **Spur gear teeth are parallel to axis; helical teeth are inclined at helix angle $\beta$.**
- **Module $m$** is the SI standard pitch parameter: $d = m \cdot z$. Always choose from preferred series.
- **Standard full-depth tooth** has addendum $= m$, dedendum $= 1.25m$, pressure angle $= 20°$.
- **Lewis beam strength** is the *bending* limit:
  $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$
  where $y$ = Lewis form factor (read from table, depends on tooth count).
- **Buckingham wear strength** is the *pitting* limit:
  $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$
  where $Q$ = ratio factor (0.484 for steel-steel, 20°), $K$ = material constant.
- **Design rule:** $m = \max(m_b, m_w)$, then **round UP** to next preferred size.
- **Tangential load** $W_T = \frac{2P}{v}$, where $v = \frac{\pi d n}{60}$ (m/s).
- **Helical gear** → convert to "equivalent spur" using **virtual number of teeth**:
  $z_v = \frac{z}{\cos^3 \beta}$ (note the **cube** of cosine).
- **Helical module conversion:** $m_t = m_n / \cos \beta$.
- **Helical axial thrust** on bearings: $W_A = W_T \cdot \tan \beta$ — **must** be supported by a thrust bearing.
- **Verification step** (post-design): recompute $W_T$ with final $m$ and confirm $F_b > W_T$ and $F_w > W_T$.
- **Face-width rule of thumb:** $b = 10m$ for spur, can be higher (up to $15m$) for helical to take advantage of overlap.
- **Wear usually governs** for medium-speed steel gears; **bending governs** for low-speed, large-module, soft-mate gears.
- **KTU exam trap:** students lose marks for not stating units, not labelling symbols, and not showing the standard-module-selection step.

> **End of Module 3 — Gear Design Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
