**A centrifugal clutch has shoes pivoted on the driving shaft. The following data is given: Number of shoes n = 3; drum radius R = 100 mm; shoe CG radius r_cg = 60 mm; pivot angle α = 35°; coefficient of friction μ = 0.25; mass per shoe m = 0.5 kg. The clutch is required to engage at 1200 rpm.**

**Solve:**
- **(a)** Determine the **engagement speed** considering weight effects, and verify if the design speed matches the requirement. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** Determine the **spring force** required at the shoe pivot such that engagement occurs at exactly 1200 rpm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Apply the engagement condition (no spring, with weight):**

$$\omega_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{g \cot\alpha}{r_{cg}}}$$

**Step 2: Compute cot α:**

$$\cot 35° = \dfrac{\cos 35°}{\sin 35°} = \dfrac{0.8192}{0.5736} = 1.428$$

**Step 3: Substitute values:**

$$\omega_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{9.81 \times 1.428}{0.060}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{14.01}{0.060}} = \sqrt{233.5} = 15.28 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Convert to rpm:**

$$N_e = \dfrac{60 \times 15.28}{2\pi} = \dfrac{916.8}{6.283} = 145.9 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5: Compare with required engagement speed:**

$$\boxed{N_e \approx 146 \text{ rpm, which is far below the required 1200 rpm.}}$$

This means the shoes will engage at 146 rpm — much earlier than needed. **Without a spring, the clutch cannot be tuned to engage at 1200 rpm**; we need a restraining spring.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Required engagement angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Centrifugal force at required engagement speed:**

$$F_c = m \cdot r_{cg} \cdot \omega_{\text{req}}^2 = 0.5 \times 0.060 \times (125.66)^2$$

$$F_c = 0.030 \times 15791 = 473.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Weight component opposing engagement:**

$$W = m g = 0.5 \times 9.81 = 4.905 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Required spring force from moment balance about pivot A:**

At engagement, the spring force $F_s$ provides additional moment opposing engagement. With the spring assumed horizontal and perpendicular to the lever:

$$F_c \sin\alpha = W \cos\alpha + F_s$$

Solving for $F_s$:
$$F_s = F_c \sin\alpha - W \cos\alpha$$

**Step 5: Compute:**

$$F_s = 473.7 \times \sin 35° - 4.905 \times \cos 35°$$

$$F_s = 473.7 \times 0.5736 - 4.905 \times 0.8192$$

$$F_s = 271.7 - 4.02 = 267.7 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_s \approx 268 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 6: Spring stiffness (if deflection x is given):**

If the problem additionally gives spring deflection $x$, then:
$$k = \dfrac{F_s}{x}$$

For example, if $x = 5$ mm = 0.005 m:
$$k = \dfrac{267.7}{0.005} = 53{,}540 \text{ N/m} \approx 53.5 \text{ N/mm}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to convert rpm → rad/s before substituting into $\omega^2$ terms. Always remember: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
>
> 2. **Angle Convention Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Using degrees instead of radians in the trigonometric arguments of transcendental functions (rare here, but fatal in some problems). The cot and sin of α always use **degrees** in KTU machine design problems — but state it explicitly.
>
> 3. **Sign Error in Friction Term (–2 marks):** In the denominator $\sin\alpha \pm \mu\cos\alpha$, using the wrong sign based on direction of rotation. The convention is: **'+' sign when friction opposes engagement** (most common KTU scenario). Always draw the FBD and verify.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Shoe Weight W (–1 mark):** At low engagement speeds, W is small; at 3000+ rpm, W becomes negligible compared to Fc. Students often drop W incorrectly at low speeds. KTU expects you to **show the assumption explicitly** ("W is neglected as Fc >> W").
>
> 5. **Single-shoe vs Total Torque Confusion (–3 marks):** Computing $T_1 = \mu NR$ and forgetting to multiply by $n$ (number of shoes). Always end with $T = n\mu NR$.
>
> 6. **Forgetting CG Radius Distinction (–2 marks):** Mixing up $r_{cg}$ (radius of center of gravity) with $R$ (drum contact radius). These are **different geometric quantities** — never substitute one for the other.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Centrifugal clutch = automatic friction clutch engaged by centrifugal force on shoes.
- **Engagement Speed Formula:** $\omega_e = \sqrt{g \cot\alpha / r_{cg}}$ (no friction), or with friction: $\omega_e = \sqrt{g(\cot\alpha \pm \mu)/r_{cg}}$.
- **Normal Force:** $N = (F_c \sin\alpha - W \cos\alpha) / (\sin\alpha \pm \mu \cos\alpha)$ — derived from moment equilibrium about shoe pivot.
- **Torque Capacity:** $T = n \cdot \mu \cdot N \cdot R$ — multiply by number of shoes.
- **Power:** $P = T \cdot \omega = 2\pi NT/60$.
- **Components:** Driving shaft + shoes + friction lining + springs + drum.
- **Materials:** Cast iron drum + leather/asbestos friction lining on shoes.
- **Friction Coefficient:** μ ≈ 0.20–0.25 (leather), 0.30–0.40 (asbestos).
- **Applications:** Two-wheelers, go-karts, mowers, mixers, small pumps.
- **Advantages:** Automatic, no pedal, self-protecting against overload, simple.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited torque capacity, slip during engagement causes heat, not suitable for high-power vehicles.
- **Number of Shoes:** Typically n = 2 or 4 (must be even for dynamic balance).
- **Design Rule of Thumb:** Allowable surface velocity at drum ≤ 25 m/s (heat limit).
- **Self-energizing vs Non-self-energizing:** Depends on direction of rotation relative to shoe pivot geometry — always draw the FBD and verify the sign of the μN moment.
- **Exam Tip:** KTU typically gives all geometric parameters (α, β, r_cg, R, μ) — your job is to apply the correct formula and arrive at numerical answer in **3 significant figures**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brakes - Block Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Brakes - Block Brakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Block Brake** is a simple, rugged, and cost-effective friction device used extensively in heavy-duty industrial machinery, hoists, cranes, trucks, and railway rolling stock. It operates on the principle of **kinetic friction**, where a rigid block (also called a *shoe* or *pad*) lined with a high-friction material (such as asbestos-based compounds, sintered metal, or composite ceramics) is forced into mechanical contact with the periphery of a rotating cylindrical drum or wheel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> *"A block brake consists of a brake drum rotating about a fixed axis and a shoe or block which presses against the drum. The brake is actuated by means of a lever pivoted at one end and connected to the block at the other end."*

### 1.2 Components of a Typical Block Brake Assembly

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Brake Drum** | Rotating cylindrical member (usually cast iron) carrying kinetic energy |
| 2 | **Brake Block / Shoe** | Stationary friction element pressed against the drum |
| 3 | **Friction Lining** | High-μ material bonded to the block surface |
| 4 | **Lever / Shoe Arm** | Mechanical linkage transmitting actuating force |
| 5 | **Pivot / Fulcrum** | Fixed point about which the lever rotates |
| 6 | **Actuating Force (P)** | Input effort applied by hydraulic/pneumatic/mechanical means |
| 7 | **Normal Reaction (N)** | Force perpendicular to the friction surface |
| 8 | **Friction Force (μN)** | Tangential retarding force at the contact interface |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> Imagine a playground spinning merry-go-round (the drum). To stop it, instead of grabbing the central pole, you press your rubber-soled shoe sideways against the rim. The harder you press (Normal Force, $N$), the more friction your sole generates ($\mu N$), and the faster it stops. Now, wrap a long stick around the rim and pull — that's the lever amplifying your force. The pivot is your hand position, and the block brake is exactly this system, formalized with engineering precision.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** Typically **0.3 to 0.5** for cast iron drum with asbestos lining
- **Maximum Pressure (p):** Limited to **0.35 to 1.0 MPa** to prevent lining failure
- **Drum Temperature Limit:** Usually kept below **250°C to 300°C** to avoid fade
- **Wear Coefficient (K):** Empirical constant governing lining life

### 1.5 Classification of Block Brakes

```
BLOCK BRAKES
├── Single Block Brake
│   ├── Simple (Pivot on same side as block)
│   └── Pivoted (Pivot offset from block)
├── Double Block Brake
│   ├── Symmetric (Both blocks identical geometry)
│   └── Unsymmetric (Different lever lengths)
└── Based on Theory
    ├── Uniform Pressure Theory
    └── Uniform Wear Theory
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle

When the actuating force $P$ is applied at the end of a pivoted lever, it generates a normal reaction $N$ at the block-drum interface. The product of this reaction and the coefficient of friction ($\mu N$) produces a tangential retarding force. This tangential force, multiplied by the effective drum radius $r$, gives the **braking torque** $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Master Equation:**
> $$\boxed{T = \mu \cdot N \cdot r}$$

The efficiency and self-locking behavior of the block brake depend critically on:
1. The **direction of rotation** of the drum (clockwise vs. counter-clockwise).
2. The **relative position** of the pivot with respect to the normal line of contact.
3. The **sense of the friction force** (which opposes relative motion).

## 2.2 Single Block Brake — Analytical Setup

Consider a lever pivoted at point $O$. The block contacts the drum at the top (12 o'clock position for simplicity). Let:
- $a$ = perpendicular distance from pivot $O$ to the line of action of $P$
- $x$ = perpendicular distance from pivot $O$ to the normal reaction line $N$
- $r$ = radius of the brake drum
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction

### 2.3 Effect of Friction — Direction-Sensitive Analysis

The friction force $\mu N$ acts **tangentially** at the point of contact. Its line of action lies at a perpendicular distance $\mu r$ from the pivot (lever-arm effect). Depending on the direction of drum rotation, $\mu N$ either **aids** or **opposes** the actuating force $P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Energizing vs. Self-De-energizing:**
> - If $\mu N$ *assists* $P$ → brake is **Self-Energizing** (requires *less* force to operate).
> - If $\mu N$ *opposes* $P$ → brake is **Self-De-energizing** (requires *more* force to operate).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| S.No | Brake Type / Condition | Equation for N (Normal Reaction) | Required Actuating Force P |
|------|------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Single Block, No Friction (Ideal) | $N = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x}$ | $P = \dfrac{N \cdot x}{a}$ |
| 2 | Single Block, Friction aiding (Self-energizing) | $N = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x - \mu r}$ | $P = \dfrac{N(x - \mu r)}{a}$ |
| 3 | Single Block, Friction opposing (Self-de-energizing) | $N = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x + \mu r}$ | $P = \dfrac{N(x + \mu r)}{a}$ |
| 4 | Double Block (Symmetric) | $N_1 = N_2 = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{x}$ | $P = \dfrac{N \cdot x}{a}$ |
| 5 | Double Block (Unsymmetric) | Solved via simultaneous equations | Depends on geometry |
| 6 | Braking Torque | $T = \mu N r$ | $T = F \cdot r$ where $F = \mu N$ |
| 7 | Power Dissipated | $W = T \cdot \omega = 2 \pi N_{rpm} T / 60$ | kW rating |
| 8 | Energy Absorbed per stop | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ | Joules |
| 9 | Self-Locking Condition | $x \leq \mu r$ | Brake locks (P = 0 needed) |
| 10 | Maximum Braking Torque | $T_{max} = \mu N_{max} \cdot r$ | Limited by p_max and A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Locking Limitation:** When $x = \mu r$, the denominator becomes zero, and theoretically no force is required — this is the **self-locking** condition. KTU examiners *love* testing this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heavy Cranes & Winches:** Where sudden stops under full load are required
- **Railway Wagons:** As parking/emergency brakes using double block configuration
- **Elevators & Hoists:** For precise holding at static positions
- **Press Machines & Rolling Mills:** Where massive torques must be absorbed rapidly
- **Automotive Drum Brakes (Older Vehicles):** Internal expanding shoe brakes are a related descendant

## 2.6 Block Brake with Long Shoe (Uniform Pressure Theory)

For a long shoe subtending an angle $2\theta$ at the drum center:
- Total normal force: $N = p \cdot b \cdot r \cdot \theta$ (for half shoe)
- Total friction force: $F = \mu \cdot p \cdot b \cdot r \cdot \theta$
- Where $p$ = intensity of normal pressure, $b$ = width of shoe

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single Block Brake (With Friction)

### Setup
Let the brake drum rotate **clockwise**. Let the lever be pivoted at point $O$. The block presses on the drum from below the pivot, with normal reaction $N$ acting radially outward at the contact point.

### Step 1: Identify All Forces Acting on the Lever
1. Applied force $P$ (downward or as per geometry)
2. Normal reaction $N$ from drum on block (radially inward toward drum center)
3. Friction force $F = \mu N$ (tangential, opposing drum motion)
4. Reaction at pivot $O$ (does not enter moment equation)

### Step 2: Apply Moment Equilibrium about Pivot O

Taking moments about $O$ (so pivot reaction drops out):

$$P \cdot a = N \cdot x + (\mu N) \cdot (\pm r)$$

The sign of the $\mu N \cdot r$ term depends on whether friction assists or opposes the moment due to $P$.

### Case A: Friction Assists P (Self-Energizing)

The friction force creates a moment in the **same sense** as $P$, so it appears with a *negative* sign on the RHS:

$$P \cdot a = N \cdot x - \mu N \cdot r$$

$$P \cdot a = N(x - \mu r)$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{P \cdot a}{x - \mu r}}$$

For finite $N$, we require $x > \mu r$.

### Case B: Friction Opposes P (Self-De-energizing)

The friction force creates a moment **opposite** to $P$, so it appears with a *positive* sign on the RHS:

$$P \cdot a = N \cdot x + \mu N \cdot r$$

$$P \cdot a = N(x + \mu r)$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{P \cdot a}{x + \mu r}}$$

### Step 3: Braking Torque Calculation

Once $N$ is determined, the friction force is $F = \mu N$, and the braking torque is:

$$T_b = F \cdot r = \mu N \cdot r$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Double Block Symmetric Brake

### Setup
Two identical blocks press on opposite sides of the drum (180° apart). Both levers are pivoted and connected to a common actuating mechanism.

### Step 1: Symmetry Condition
By symmetry: $N_1 = N_2 = N$, and the lever geometry is identical.

### Step 2: Moment Equilibrium for Each Block

For one block (treating as single block):

$$P_1 \cdot a = N \cdot x \quad \text{(per block)}$$

If a single force $P$ actuates both blocks simultaneously through a common linkage, each block receives $P/2$:

$$\frac{P}{2} \cdot a = N \cdot x$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{P \cdot a}{2x}}$$

### Step 3: Total Braking Torque

$$T_{total} = 2 \cdot \mu N \cdot r = 2 \mu r \cdot \frac{P \cdot a}{2x}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} = \frac{\mu \cdot P \cdot a \cdot r}{x}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Double Block Unsymmetric Brake

### Setup
Two blocks with **different** lever geometries, actuated by a common force $P$ through a differential linkage. Let:
- Block 1: lever arm $a_1$, pivot distance $x_1$, produces $N_1$
- Block 2: lever arm $a_2$, pivot distance $x_2$, produces $N_2$
- Friction effects: $\mu_1 r_1$ and $\mu_2 r_2$ respectively

### Step 1: Free Body Equations

For Block 1 (self-energizing direction):

$$P_1 \cdot a_1 = N_1(x_1 - \mu_1 r_1)$$

For Block 2 (self-de-energizing direction):

$$P_2 \cdot a_2 = N_2(x_2 + \mu_2 r_2)$$

### Step 2: Common Actuation Constraint
Assuming both levers share the total applied force $P$ via a horizontal link:

$$P = P_1 + P_2$$

### Step 3: Solve Simultaneously

$$P = \frac{N_1(x_1 - \mu_1 r_1)}{a_1} + \frac{N_2(x_2 + \mu_2 r_2)}{a_2}$$

For equal normal reactions ($N_1 = N_2 = N$) — common design assumption:

$$\boxed{P = N \left[ \frac{x_1 - \mu_1 r_1}{a_1} + \frac{x_2 + \mu_2 r_2}{a_2} \right]}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Braking Torque (Uniform Wear Theory)

For a pivoted block brake, the maximum tangential friction force is achieved when the resultant friction force $F$ passes through the pivot. This gives the condition:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{\mu N \cdot r}{N \cdot x} = \frac{\mu r}{x}$$

where $\phi$ is the angle of the resultant reaction from the radial line.

The maximum friction force:

$$F_{max} = \sqrt{N^2 + (\mu N)^2} = N\sqrt{1 + \mu^2}$$

Applied at an angle $\phi$ where $\tan \phi = \mu r / x$.

The maximum braking torque then becomes:

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \mu N \cdot r \text{ (still valid, but limited by wear/pivot geometry)}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A single block brake has a drum of diameter $300$ mm and is operated by a lever of length $400$ mm. The block is at a distance of $50$ mm from the pivot. The actuating force is applied at $350$ mm from the pivot. If $\mu = 0.4$ and the drum rotates clockwise such that the friction aids the effort, find:
(i) Maximum actuating force if the maximum pressure on the lining is $1.0$ MPa and block area is $25$ cm².
(ii) Braking torque.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Drum diameter $d = 300$ mm → $r = 150$ mm = $0.15$ m
- Total lever length $L = 400$ mm (where $P$ is applied) → $a = 350$ mm = $0.35$ m
- Block distance from pivot $x = 50$ mm = $0.05$ m
- $\mu = 0.4$, $p_{max} = 1.0$ MPa = $1.0 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- Block area $A = 25$ cm² = $25 \times 10^{-4}$ m² = $0.0025$ m²

**Step 1: Maximum Normal Reaction**

$$N_{max} = p_{max} \cdot A = 1.0 \times 10^6 \times 0.0025 = 2500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Actuating Force (Self-Energizing Case)**

$$P_{max} = \frac{N(x - \mu r)}{a}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{2500 \times (0.05 - 0.4 \times 0.15)}{0.35}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{2500 \times (0.05 - 0.06)}{0.35}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{2500 \times (-0.01)}{0.35} = -71.43 \text{ N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative value indicates self-locking has already occurred!** Since $x = 0.05 < \mu r = 0.06$, the brake is inherently locked regardless of applied force. The KTU examiner will *penalize* students who do not flag this condition.

**Step 3: Braking Torque at Maximum Reaction**

$$T_b = \mu N_{max} \cdot r = 0.4 \times 2500 \times 0.15 = 150 \text{ N·m}$$

**Conclusion:** The current geometry causes unwanted self-locking. The design must be modified so that $x > \mu r$ to allow release.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Single Block Brake

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Self-Energizing Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Brake Design Parameters Given"]) --> Q1{"Direction of Rotation?"}

    Q1 -->|CCW| Q3{"Pivot position relative to friction line?"}
    Q2 -->|"Pivot on side where friction assists"| SE1["SELF-ENERGIZING"]
    Q2 -->|"Pivot on side where friction opposes"| SD1["SELF-DE-ENERGIZING"]
    Q3 -->|"Pivot on side where friction assists"| SE2["SELF-ENERGIZING"]
    Q3 -->|"Pivot on side where friction opposes"| SD2["SELF-DE-ENERGIZING"]
    SE1 --> Check{"Check x versus mu*r"}
    SD1 --> Check
    SE2 --> Check
    SD2 --> Check
    Check -->|"x is greater than mu*r"| Safe["SAFE DESIGN"]
    Check -->|"x equals mu*r"| Lock["SELF-LOCKING BOUNDARY"]
    Check -->|"x is less than mu*r"| Fail["SELF-LOCKED — NO RELEASE POSSIBLE"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a block brake. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a single block brake.**

**Model Answer:**
A block brake is a friction device consisting of a stationary brake block (shoe) which is pressed against a rotating drum to retard or stop its motion by means of friction. A single block brake consists of a lever pivoted at one end. The actuating force $P$ is applied at the free end of the lever, and the brake block is attached at the other end, which presses against the rotating drum. The normal reaction $N$ acts at the contact point, and the friction force $\mu N$ acts tangentially. The braking torque developed is $T = \mu N r$, where $r$ is the radius of the drum. *(Sketch showing lever, pivot, block, drum with $P$, $N$, and $\mu N$ labeled should be drawn.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between self-energizing and self-de-energizing brakes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Self-Energizing | Self-De-energizing |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Friction Direction** | Aids the applied force $P$ | Opposes the applied force $P$ |
| **Required Effort** | Less force needed for same torque | More force needed for same torque |
| **Condition** | $\mu N$ creates moment *with* $P$ | $\mu N$ creates moment *against* $P$ |
| **Formula** | $P = N(x - \mu r)/a$ | $P = N(x + \mu r)/a$ |
| **Risk** | May lead to self-locking if $x \leq \mu r$ | Always safe to release |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A pivoted single block brake has the following specifications:**
- Drum diameter $d = 400$ mm
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.35$
- Lever arm for applied force $a = 500$ mm
- Distance of block from pivot $x = 75$ mm
- Angle of contact of block = 60°
- Permissible lining pressure $p = 0.8$ MPa
- Width of block $b = 60$ mm
- Drum rotates such that the friction aids the effort.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the maximum actuating force $P$ that can be safely applied.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the braking torque and the power that can be dissipated if the drum rotates at $200$ rpm.

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**Given Data:**
- $d = 400$ mm → $r = 200$ mm = $0.2$ m
- $\mu = 0.35$
- $a = 500$ mm = $0.5$ m
- $x = 75$ mm = $0.075$ m
- Contact angle $\theta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad
- $p = 0.8$ MPa = $0.8 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- $b = 60$ mm = $0.06$ m
- $N_{rpm} = 200$ rpm

**Part (a): Maximum Actuating Force**

**Step 1: Calculate the contact area of the block**

The block contacts the drum along an arc. For a curved block:

$$A = b \cdot r \cdot \theta$$

$$A = 0.06 \times 0.2 \times \frac{\pi}{3}$$

$$A = 0.06 \times 0.2 \times 1.0472$$

$$A = 0.01257 \text{ m}^2 = 125.66 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the maximum normal reaction**

$$N_{max} = p \cdot A$$

$$N_{max} = 0.8 \times 10^6 \times 0.01257$$

$$N_{max} = 10053 \text{ N} \approx 10.05 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Verify Self-Locking Condition**

$$\mu \cdot r = 0.35 \times 0.2 = 0.07 \text{ m} = 70 \text{ mm}$$

$$x = 75 \text{ mm} > \mu r = 70 \text{ mm}$$

Since $x > \mu r$, the brake is **safe** (no self-locking).

**Step 4: Calculate Maximum Actuating Force (Self-Energizing)**

$$P_{max} = \frac{N_{max}(x - \mu r)}{a}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{10053 \times (0.075 - 0.07)}{0.5}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{10053 \times 0.005}{0.5}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 100.53 \text{ N}}$$

---

**Part (b): Braking Torque and Power**

**Step 1: Braking Torque**

$$T_b = \mu \cdot N_{max} \cdot r$$

$$T_b = 0.35 \times 10053 \times 0.2$$

$$T_b = 703.71 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_b \approx 703.7 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 2: Angular Velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N_{rpm}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = \frac{400\pi}{60}$$

$$\omega = 20.944 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Power Dissipated**

$$P_{power} = T_b \cdot \omega$$

$$P_{power} = 703.71 \times 20.944$$

$$P_{power} = 14740 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{power} \approx 14.74 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A double block symmetric brake is used on a crane. The specifications are:**
- Drum diameter = $500$ mm
- Each block width = $80$ mm
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$
- Lever length (force arm) = $600$ mm
- Distance of block from pivot = $100$ mm
- Maximum allowable pressure = $0.7$ MPa
- Each block subtends an angle of $45°$ at the drum center.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the maximum actuating force that the system can handle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the braking torque and verify whether the brake can hold a load of $20$ kN suspended from a drum of effective radius $250$ mm.

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Given Data:**
- $d = 500$ mm → $r = 250$ mm = $0.25$ m
- $b = 80$ mm = $0.08$ m
- $\mu = 0.3$
- $a = 600$ mm = $0.6$ m
- $x = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m
- $p_{max} = 0.7$ MPa = $0.7 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- $\theta = 45° = \pi/4$ rad

**Part (a): Maximum Actuating Force**

**Step 1: Area of contact per block**

$$A = b \cdot r \cdot \theta$$

$$A = 0.08 \times 0.25 \times \frac{\pi}{4}$$

$$A = 0.08 \times 0.25 \times 0.7854$$

$$A = 0.01571 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Maximum normal reaction per block**

$$N_{max} = p_{max} \cdot A = 0.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.01571 = 10996 \text{ N} \approx 11 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Check Self-Locking for Each Block**

$$\mu r = 0.3 \times 0.25 = 0.075 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ mm}$$

$$x = 100 \text{ mm} > \mu r = 75 \text{ mm}$$

Safe design (no self-locking).

**Step 4: Symmetric Configuration Force Balance**

For symmetric double block, each block shares the actuating force equally. Considering both blocks simultaneously, the moment equilibrium gives:

$$P \cdot a = 2 N \cdot x$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 N_{max} \cdot x}{a}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 \times 10996 \times 0.1}{0.6}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 3665.3 \text{ N} \approx 3.67 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**Part (b): Braking Torque and Load Verification**

**Step 1: Total Braking Torque (both blocks)**

$$T_{total} = 2 \times \mu \times N_{max} \times r$$

$$T_{total} = 2 \times 0.3 \times 10996 \times 0.25$$

$$T_{total} = 1649.4 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} \approx 1649.4 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 2: Load Torque to be Resisted**

$$T_{load} = W \times R_{eff}$$

$$T_{load} = 20000 \times 0.25$$

$$T_{load} = 5000 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Verification**

$$T_{total} = 1649.4 \text{ N·m} < T_{load} = 5000 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Brake FAILS to hold the load!** $T_{total}$ is only about **33%** of the required $T_{load}$. The brake design is **inadequate** for the specified load. Students *must* clearly state "FAIL" or "Not Safe" with numerical justification to secure full marks.

**Step 4: Required Upgrades**
- Increase $N_{max}$ via larger block area (increase $b$ or $\theta$)
- Increase $\mu$ via better lining material
- Decrease load via counter-weighting
- Use a higher gear reduction to lower effective drum torque

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in $\mu r$ Term:** Always check whether friction *aids* or *opposes* $P$ before choosing $+$ or $-$. Wrong sign = wrong answer = **0 marks** for that part.
> 2. **Forgetting the Self-Locking Check:** If you compute $P$ without verifying $x > \mu r$, the examiner will deduct **2 marks minimum**.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mix of mm and m in the same equation is the #1 silent killer. Convert *everything* to SI units at the start.
> 4. **Block Area for Curved Contact:** Students often use $A = b \times L$ (rectangular area). For curved shoes, use $A = b \cdot r \cdot \theta$. This error costs **3 marks**.
> 5. **Not Labeling Direction of Rotation:** Without specifying CW or CCW, you cannot determine self-energizing vs. self-de-energizing. Always state it explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Master Equation:** $T = \mu N r$ — the foundation of all brake torque problems.
- [x] **Single Block Brake:** $P = N(x \pm \mu r)/a$ — sign depends on friction direction.
- [x] **Self-Locking Condition:** Occurs when $x \leq \mu r$ → brake cannot release.
- [x] **Double Block Symmetric:** Total braking torque is **twice** that of single block for the same $N$.
- [x] **Double Block Unsymmetric:** Solve simultaneously using both lever moment equations.
- [x] **Contact Area:** For curved shoes, $A = b \cdot r \cdot \theta$ — not $b \times L$.
- [x] **Pressure Check:** Always verify $p_{actual} = N/A \leq p_{allowable}$.
- [x] **Power Dissipation:** $W = T \omega$ where $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
- [x] **Energy Absorption:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ for deceleration analysis.
- [x] **KTU Exam Weight:** Expect 1 Part-A (3 marks) and 1 Part-B (14 marks) question from this module.
- [x] **Always mention direction of rotation** before writing any equation.
- [x] **Lever arm $\mu r$** is the perpendicular distance from pivot to the friction force line of action.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Band Brake

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Band Brake** is a friction brake in which a flexible band (fabric, steel, or composite) lined with friction material is wrapped partially around a rotating drum or pulley to create a braking torque through frictional contact. The radial inward force is generated by tightening the band against the drum, leveraging the **principle of belt friction** between the band lining and the drum surface.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBAUT604 – Module 4: Braking Systems)**, band brakes are categorized into:
- **Simple Band Brake** (single-lever actuation)
- **Differential Band Brake** (two-lever / compound lever actuation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Band brakes fall under the broader category of **mechanically-actuated friction brakes** used in automotive subsystems such as parking brakes, winch systems, conveyor drives, and chain hoist mechanisms. KTU specifically tests the *derivation of braking torque*, *self-energizing conditions*, and *differential band brake analysis* in board exams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bucket Handle Principle

Imagine gripping the handle of a heavy **plastic bucket full of water** and twisting it. Your hand applies a squeezing force perpendicular to the handle, but the *bucket* experiences a tangential *retarding torque* due to the grip friction. The harder you squeeze, the more torque is transmitted. Now, instead of your hand, imagine a flexible steel strap wrapped around the bucket — when you pull the strap's ends together, it tightens and grips the bucket. This strap is the **band**, the bucket is the **drum**, and your pulling force is the **actuation force (P)**.

**Self-Energy Analogy:** Now imagine tying one end of the strap to a fixed peg. When the drum rotates, friction drags the band along, which *automatically increases* the wrap tightness — this is called a **self-energizing band brake**, where motion does part of the work for you. Conversely, a **self-de-energizing** brake loosens itself when the drum rotates, requiring greater actuation force.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of Friction | $\mu$ | 0.25 – 0.40 (asbestos lining on cast iron) |
| Band Wrap Angle | $\theta$ | Radians (commonly $180°\text{ to } 360°$) |
| Width of Lining | $b$ | $30\text{ to }100\text{ mm}$ |
| Drum Diameter | $D$ | $100\text{ to }600\text{ mm}$ |
| Safe Pressure | $p$ | $0.3\text{ to }0.6\text{ N/mm}^2$ |
| Allowable Lining Stress | $\sigma$ | $0.7\text{ N/mm}^2$ (asbestos) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Reference:** The empirical relation $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ (Eytelwein's equation) is the **single most-tested formula** in this module. Memorize it in both forms: $T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}$ and $T_2 = T_1 \cdot e^{-\mu\theta}$.

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The working of a band brake follows a strict chain of mechanical events:

1. **Actuation:** The driver/operator applies a force $P$ at the free end of the lever (or pinion-driven mechanism pulls the band taut).
2. **Tension Generation:** The lever pivots about a fulcrum, transforming $P$ into two unequal tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ on the band.
3. **Frictional Engagement:** The difference $(T_1 - T_2)$ creates a **net normal pressure** distributed along the band-drum contact arc.
4. **Belt Friction Activation:** Per Eytelwein's theorem, $T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}$, so the ratio is exponential in the wrap angle.
5. **Braking Torque Output:** The drum experiences a resisting torque $W \cdot R = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R$, where $W$ is the tangential friction force at the drum surface.
6. **Heat Dissipation:** Kinetic energy converts to heat at the lining — **design must ensure the lining can dissipate heat** without fade.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why unequal tensions?** The drum rotation direction determines which end of the band is *tight side* ($T_1$) and *slack side* ($T_2$). Without rotation, $T_1 = T_2$ (static equilibrium).
- **Why exponential?** As the band wraps further around an angle $d\theta$, an infinitesimal friction element $dT = \mu \cdot T \cdot d\theta$ is added, leading to the first-order ODE $\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \, d\theta$ whose solution is the exponential form.
- **Why is self-energization desired?** It reduces the operator's effort (or hydraulic cylinder size) — critical for parking brakes where mechanical advantage matters.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$ | Eytelwein's belt friction equation | N |
| 2 | $\theta = \text{wrap angle in radians}$ | Angle of contact between band & drum | rad |
| 3 | $T_1 - T_2 = W$ | Net effective tangential friction force | N |
| 4 | $\text{Braking Torque} \, M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R$ | Torque resisting drum rotation | N·m |
| 5 | $P \cdot a = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot b$ | Simple band brake lever equation | N·m |
| 6 | $P \cdot a = (T_1 \cdot b_1 - T_2 \cdot b_2)$ | General lever torque balance | N·m |
| 7 | $P \cdot a = (T_1 + T_2) \cdot b$ | Differential band brake (one direction) | N·m |
| 8 | $P \cdot a = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot b$ | Differential band brake (other direction) | N·m |
| 9 | $P_1 = P_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$ | Self-energizing condition ($T_1 > T_2$ side) | N |
| 10 | $p = \dfrac{2 \, W}{b \cdot D}$ | Maximum lining pressure (uniform wear theory) | N/mm² |
| 11 | $p = \dfrac{W}{b \cdot R \cdot \theta}$ | Maximum lining pressure (uniform pressure theory) | N/mm² |
| 12 | $\sigma = \dfrac{T_1}{b \cdot t}$ | Maximum tensile stress in band (thickness $t$) | N/mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Reference:** All formulas above use **$\vert$ symbol replaced with bare brackets**. For absolute-value notations in working problems (e.g., $\vert T_1 - T_2 \vert$), use the LaTeX `\vert` command inside math mode.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Band brakes dominate applications requiring:
- **Compact braking in confined spaces** (e.g., elevator drums, crane winches, chain hoists).
- **Bidirectional braking** — flipping the lever re-energizes the brake (differential type).
- **High torque-to-size ratio** — exponential gain from wrap angle allows small actuation forces.
- **Cost-effective** solution for low-duty-cycle equipment where drum-disc brakes are overkill.
- In **automotive parking brakes**, a band brake acts on the propeller shaft or transmission drum.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Eytelwein's Belt Friction Equation

Consider a flexible band wrapped around a fixed cylindrical drum over an elementary arc $d\theta$ at radius $R$. Let the tensions in the band on either side of the element be $T$ and $T + dT$ (with $T + dT > T$).

**Free-Body Diagram of Elementary Element:**

Forces acting on the element:
- Tangential tensions: $T$ and $T + dT$ at the two ends of the arc.
- Normal reaction: $dN$ radially inward.
- Frictional force: $\mu \, dN$ tangentially opposing impending motion.

**Step 1: Tangential Equilibrium (along drum surface)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(T + dT) \cos\!\left(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) - T \cos\!\left(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) &= \mu \, dN
\end{aligned}
$$

For a small angle, $\cos(d\theta/2) \approx 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dT &= \mu \, dN \quad \text{(Equation A)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Radial Equilibrium (perpendicular to drum surface)**

The two tangential tensions each have a radial component pulling the band away from the drum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dN &= T \sin\!\left(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) + (T + dT) \sin\!\left(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a small arc, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$, and the $dT \cdot d\theta$ term is second-order (negligible):

$$
\begin{aligned}
dN &= 2T \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = T \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Equation B into Equation A**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dT &= \mu \, T \, d\theta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Integrate Both Sides from Slack Side to Tight Side**

Integrating from $\theta = 0$ (where $T = T_2$) to $\theta = \theta$ (where $T = T_1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} &= \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta \\
\ln(T_1) - \ln(T_2) &= \mu \theta \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) &= \mu \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Exponential Form**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Used:** This derivation assumes **impending slip** (limiting friction, $\mu$ constant) and **small wrap element**. The drum is assumed to rotate in the direction that *increases* $T_1$. If rotation is reversed, swap the indices.

---

## 3.2 Simple Band Brake — Braking Torque Derivation

**Setup:** A single lever of length $L$ pivots at fulcrum $C$. The band is attached at point $A$ (distance $a$ from pivot) on the tight side and point $B$ (distance $b$ from pivot) on the slack side. Force $P$ is applied at the lever end.

**Free-Body Diagram of the Lever (Equilibrium):**

Taking moments about the pivot $C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot a &= T_1 \cdot b - T_2 \cdot b \\
P \cdot a &= (T_1 - T_2) \cdot b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solving for Actuation Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot b}{a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting Eytelwein's Relation ($T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot a &= (T_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} - T_2) \cdot b \\
P \cdot a &= T_2 \cdot b \cdot (e^{\mu\theta} - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Braking Torque on the Drum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R &= T_2 \cdot (e^{\mu\theta} - 1) \cdot R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style):**

Given: $D = 300\text{ mm}$, $\mu = 0.3$, $\theta = 270° = \frac{3\pi}{2}\text{ rad}$, $a = 100\text{ mm}$, $b = 50\text{ mm}$, $P = 200\text{ N}$. Find $M_t$.

**Step 1: Compute wrap angle in radians.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2} = 4.7124 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Eytelwein's exponent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu\theta = 0.3 \times 4.7124 = 1.4137 \\
e^{\mu\theta} = e^{1.4137} = 4.1117
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_2$ from lever equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot a &= T_2 \cdot b \cdot (e^{\mu\theta} - 1) \\
200 \times 100 &= T_2 \times 50 \times (4.1117 - 1) \\
20{,}000 &= T_2 \times 50 \times 3.1117 \\
T_2 &= \frac{20{,}000}{155.585} = 128.55 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $T_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= T_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} = 128.55 \times 4.1117 = 528.55 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Braking Torque.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_t &= (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R = (528.55 - 128.55) \times 0.15 \\
M_t &= 400 \times 0.15 = 60 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Verification:** Alternatively, $M_t = \frac{P \cdot a \cdot R}{b} = \frac{200 \times 100 \times 0.15}{50} = 60 \text{ N·m}$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Differential Band Brake — Full Analysis

**Setup:** Two levers (or a forked lever) actuate the band. Both tight and slack sides are attached to the lever at different distances from the pivot.

**Configuration A (Drum rotates CW — tight side attached at distance $b_1$ from pivot):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot a &= (T_1 \cdot b_1) - (T_2 \cdot b_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Configuration B (Drum rotates CCW — tight side attached at distance $b_2$ from pivot):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot a &= (T_1 \cdot b_2) - (T_2 \cdot b_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special Case — Equal Lever Arms ($b_1 = b_2 = b$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direction A:} \quad P \cdot a &= (T_1 - T_2) \cdot b \\
\text{Direction B:} \quad P \cdot a &= (T_2 - T_1) \cdot b = -(T_1 - T_2) \cdot b
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign in Direction B means the actuation force must **reverse direction** to brake the drum spinning the opposite way.

**Self-Energizing Condition:**

If $P$ acts in the same direction in both brake applications, the brake is **self-energizing in one direction** and **self-de-energizing in the other**. For full bidirectional symmetry, a unique relationship must hold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 = b_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof of Self-Energizing Condition:**

Substituting $T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}$ into Direction A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot a &= (T_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}) \cdot b_1 - T_2 \cdot b_2 \\
P \cdot a &= T_2 \cdot (b_1 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} - b_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $P > 0$ (i.e., self-energizing in Direction A): $\quad b_1 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} > b_2$

For Direction B: $\quad P \cdot a = T_2 \cdot (b_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} - b_1)$

For $P > 0$ in Direction B: $\quad b_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} > b_1$

**Symmetric Bidirectional Condition:**

Both conditions hold simultaneously when:
$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} > b_2 \quad \text{AND} \quad b_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} > b_1
\end{aligned}
$$

This implies $b_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} > b_1 > \frac{b_2}{e^{\mu\theta}} = b_2 \cdot e^{-\mu\theta}$.

Combining: $b_2 \cdot e^{-\mu\theta} < b_1 < b_2 \cdot e^{\mu\theta}$.

A widely cited **optimum symmetric condition** is $b_1 = b_2$ paired with $P$ reversing sign per direction (i.e., **mechanically assisted bidirectional**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question:** *"When does the differential band brake become fully self-locking?"* Answer: When $P = 0$ but the brake still resists — i.e., when $\mu \theta \to \infty$ or when the geometry satisfies $b_1 \cdot e^{\mu\theta} = b_2$ exactly. Practically, this is avoided as it prevents drum release.

---

## 3.4 Lining Pressure Analysis

For **uniform wear theory** (commonly assumed in KTU problems):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum pressure } p_{max} &= \frac{2 \, W}{b \cdot D} = \frac{2 (T_1 - T_2)}{b \cdot D}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **uniform pressure theory** (rarely used):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{max} &= \frac{W}{b \cdot R \cdot \theta} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{b \cdot R \cdot \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Default:** The board typically uses **uniform wear theory** unless otherwise specified. The value $p$ must satisfy $p_{max} \leq 0.6 \text{ N/mm}^2$ for asbestos lining.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Simple Band Brake

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Input Force P] --> B[Lever Mechanical Advantage a/b]
    B --> C[Pivot Fulcrum C]
    C --> D1[Tension Generation T1]
    C --> D2[Tension Generation T2]
    D1 --> E[Belt Friction Activation]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F["Eytelwein: T1 = T2 x e^(mu x theta)"]
    F --> G[Braking Torque Computation]
    G --> H["Mt = (T1 - T2) x R"]
    H --> I[Drum Deceleration]
    I --> J[Kinetic Energy to Heat Dissipation]
    J --> K[Lining Thermal Limit Check]
    K --> L{Heat Capacity OK?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Brake Holds]
    L -- No --> N[WARNING: Lining Fade Risk]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Differential Band Brake

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actuation Force P at Lever End] --> B[Compound Forked Lever]
    B --> C{Pivot Fulcrum C}
    C --> D1[Left Arm at distance b1]
    C --> D2[Right Arm at distance b2]
    D1 --> E[Connects to Band Side 1]
    D2 --> F[Connects to Band Side 2]
    E --> G[Band Wraps Drum]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Drum Rotation Direction Detection]
    H --> I{Drum CW or CCW?}
    I -- CW --> J[Tight Side T1 at b1]
    I -- CCW --> K[Tight Side T1 at b2]
    J --> L["P x a = T1 x b1 - T2 x b2"]
    K --> M["P x a = T1 x b2 - T2 x b1"]
    L --> N[Self-Energizing Check]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Symmetric Bidirectional?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Optimum Design Achieved]
    O -- No --> Q[Asymmetric Brake Behavior Noted]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Begin: Design a Band Brake] --> Q1[Step 1: Identify Drum Diameter D]
    Q1 --> Q2[Step 2: Estimate Wrap Angle theta]
    Q2 --> Q3[Step 3: Choose Friction Material mu]
    Q3 --> Q4[Step 4: Compute T1/T2 Ratio via e to the mu theta]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step 5: Apply Lever Equation P a equals T1 b1 minus T2 b2]
    Q5 --> Q6[Step 6: Compute Braking Torque Mt equals T1 minus T2 times R]
    Q6 --> Q7[Step 7: Compute Lining Pressure p equals 2W over bD]
    Q7 --> Check{p max within limit}
    Check -- Yes --> Done[Design Acceptable]
    Check -- No --> Revise[Revise Band Width b or Material mu]
    Revise --> Q3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams are **functional architecture flowcharts** that represent the operational logic and design decision tree of band brakes. Physical schematic drawings (drum, band, lever geometry) are best drawn in hand-sketched form by students during exams, as KTU provides scribble sheets for this purpose.
